%% ── S1: Graph Algorithms ──────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{S1 --- Graph Algorithms}
\label{sec:S1}
\markboth{S1 --- Graph Algorithms}{}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cA}{Shortest Paths}
\label{sub:s1-shortestpaths}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item Let $S$ be a source vertex in a directed graph with non-negative edge weights. Suppose that after applying Dijkstra's algorithm, the following shortest path distances from $S$ to all other vertices are obtained:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{l|cccccccc}
\textbf{Vertex}   & $S$ & $A$ & $B$ & $C$ & $D$ & $E$ & $F$ & $G$ \\ \hline
\textbf{Distance} & 0   & 2   & 4   & 5   & 7   & 8   & 9   & 11  
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
Construct a directed weighted graph with non-negative integer edge weights such that running Dijkstra's algorithm from source $S$ produces exactly the given distances, and justify that Dijkstra's algorithm produces those distances. The graph must satisfy: 

\begin{enumerate}
\item Every vertex must be reachable from $S$.
\item The graph must contain at least two edges that are not part of any shortest path from $S$.
\item The shortest path to each vertex must be uniquely determined.
\end{enumerate}

\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Graph Construction}

To construct a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ that satisfies all the given conditions, we first establish a backbone path (a shortest path tree spanning all vertices) that yields the exact target distances. Then, we introduce auxiliary edges that do not alter these shortest paths but fulfill the remaining structural constraints.

Let the vertex set be $V = \{S, A, B, C, D, E, F, G\}$. We define the edge set $E$ with non-negative integer weights $w(u, v)$ as follows:

\subsubsection*{Shortest Path Tree Edges (Main Chain):}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $w(S, A) = 2 \implies d(A) = 2$
    \item $w(A, B) = 2 \implies d(B) = 2 + 2 = 4$
    \item $w(B, C) = 1 \implies d(C) = 4 + 1 = 5$
    \item $w(C, D) = 2 \implies d(D) = 5 + 2 = 7$
    \item $w(D, E) = 1 \implies d(E) = 7 + 1 = 8$
    \item $w(E, F) = 1 \implies d(F) = 8 + 1 = 9$
    \item $w(F, G) = 2 \implies d(G) = 9 + 2 = 11$
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Auxiliary (Non-Shortest Path) Edges:}
To satisfy the requirement of having at least two edges not belonging to any shortest path, we add:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $(S, B)$ with weight $w(S, B) = 5$
    \item $(B, D)$ with weight $w(B, D) = 4$
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5cm}

\subsection*{2. Graph Diagram (TikZ)}

Below is the visual representation of the constructed directed graph. The solid lines denote the unique shortest path tree edges, and the dashed lines denote the auxiliary edges that are not part of any shortest path.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    vertex/.style={circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, minimum size=24pt, font=\bfseries, text=cA}, % changed \color{cA} to text=cA for node text
    spedge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, color=black},
    extraedge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, dashed, color=red!80!black},
    every node/.style={font=\small}
]
    % Place vertices
    \node[vertex] (S) at (0,0) {S};
    \node[vertex] (A) at (1.8,0) {A};
    \node[vertex] (B) at (3.6,0) {B};
    \node[vertex] (C) at (5.4,0) {C};
    \node[vertex] (D) at (7.2,0) {D};
    \node[vertex] (E) at (9.0,0) {E};
    \node[vertex] (F) at (10.8,0) {F};
    \node[vertex] (G) at (12.6,0) {G};

    % Draw shortest path tree edges
    \draw[spedge] (S) -- node[above] {2} (A);
    \draw[spedge] (A) -- node[above] {2} (B);
    \draw[spedge] (B) -- node[above] {1} (C);
    \draw[spedge] (C) -- node[above] {2} (D);
    \draw[spedge] (D) -- node[above] {1} (E);
    \draw[spedge] (E) -- node[above] {1} (F);
    \draw[spedge] (F) -- node[above] {2} (G);

    % Draw non-shortest path edges
    \draw[extraedge] (S) to[bend right=45] node[below] {5} (B);
    \draw[extraedge] (B) to[bend right=45] node[below] {4} (D);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5cm}

\subsection*{3. Trace and Justification of Dijkstra's Algorithm}

We formally execute Dijkstra's algorithm from source $S$ to prove it yields the exact distance profile and to confirm the shortest paths are uniquely determined. Let $dist[v]$ denote the tentative distance to vertex $v$, initialized to $dist[S] = 0$ and $dist[v] = \infty$ for all $v \neq S$.

% FIXED TABLE SECTION: Removed \begin{table}[H] and used \captionof
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{c|c|c|ccccccc}
\textbf{Step} & \textbf{Extract Min} & \textbf{Settled Distance} & \multicolumn{7}{c}{\textbf{Tentative Distances ($dist$ array)}} \\
& $u$ & $dist[u]$ & $A$ & $B$ & $C$ & $D$ & $E$ & $F$ & $G$ \\ \hline
0 & — & — & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ \\
1 & $S$ & 0 & \textbf{2} & \textbf{5} & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ \\
2 & $A$ & 2 & 2 & \textbf{4} & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ \\
3 & $B$ & 4 & 2 & 4 & \textbf{5} & \textbf{8} & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ \\
4 & $C$ & 5 & 2 & 4 & 5 & \textbf{7} & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ \\
5 & $D$ & 7 & 2 & 4 & 5 & 7 & \textbf{8} & $\infty$ & $\infty$ \\
6 & $E$ & 8 & 2 & 4 & 5 & 7 & 8 & \textbf{9} & $\infty$ \\
7 & $F$ & 9 & 2 & 4 & 5 & 7 & 8 & 9 & \textbf{11} \\
8 & $G$ & 11 & 2 & 4 & 5 & 7 & 8 & 9 & 11 \\
\end{tabular}
\captionof{table}{Step-by-step trace of Dijkstra's algorithm execution.}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Detailed Step Explanation:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Extract $S$ ($dist[S]=0$):} Relaxing neighbors of $S$:
    \[ dist[A] = \min(\infty, 0 + 2) = 2, \quad dist[B] = \min(\infty, 0 + 5) = 5 \]
    \item \textbf{Extract $A$ ($dist[A]=2$):} Relaxing neighbors of $A$:
    \[ dist[B] = \min(5, 2 + 2) = 4 \]
    \item \textbf{Extract $B$ ($dist[B]=4$):} Relaxing neighbors of $B$:
    \[ dist[C] = \min(\infty, 4 + 1) = 5, \quad dist[D] = \min(\infty, 4 + 4) = 8 \]
    \item \textbf{Extract $C$ ($dist[C]=5$):} Relaxing neighbors of $C$:
    \[ dist[D] = \min(8, 5 + 2) = 7 \]
    \item \textbf{Extract $D$ ($dist[D]=7$):} Relaxing neighbors of $D$:
    \[ dist[E] = \min(\infty, 7 + 1) = 8 \]
    \item \textbf{Extract $E$ ($dist[E]=8$):} Relaxing neighbors of $E$:
    \[ dist[F] = \min(\infty, 8 + 1) = 9 \]
    \item \textbf{Extract $F$ ($dist[F]=9$):} Relaxing neighbors of $F$:
    \[ dist[G] = \min(\infty, 9 + 2) = 11 \]
    \item \textbf{Extract $G$ ($dist[G]=11$):} No remaining unvisited neighbors.
\end{enumerate}

The final values matching the target table exactly:
\[ [0, 2, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9, 11] \]

\vspace{0.5cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5cm}

\subsection*{4. Verification of Constraints}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Every vertex must be reachable from $S$:} \\
    As established by the primary chain, there exists a valid directed path from $S$ to every vertex:
    \[ S \to A \to B \to C \to D \to E \to F \to G \]
    Hence, reachability is completely guaranteed.

    \item \textbf{The graph must contain at least two edges that are not part of any shortest path from $S$:} \\
    We evaluate our two auxiliary edges:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Edge $(S, B)$ with $w(S,B) = 5$:} The unique shortest path distance to $B$ is $d(B) = 4$. Any path utilizing the edge $(S, B)$ as its initial segment has a minimum length of $5$, which is strictly greater than $d(B)$. Thus, $(S, B)$ is never part of any shortest path.
        \item \textbf{Edge $(B, D)$ with $w(B,D) = 4$:} The unique shortest path distance to $D$ is $d(D) = 7$. Any path arriving at $D$ via the edge $(B, D)$ must have a length of at least $d(B) + w(B, D) = 4 + 4 = 8$. Since $8 > 7$, this edge cannot be part of any shortest path.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{The shortest path to each vertex must be uniquely determined:} \\
    We inspect the incoming options for each vertex to guarantee that no ties in path length exist:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Vertex $A$:} Only has one incoming edge $(S, A)$, so the path $S \to A$ (length 2) is unique.
        \item \textbf{Vertex $B$:} Incoming paths are $S \to A \to B$ (length 4) and $S \to B$ (length 5). Since $4 < 5$, the path $S \to A \to B$ is uniquely minimal.
        \item \textbf{Vertex $C$:} Only has one incoming edge $(B, C)$, so it must extend the unique shortest path of $B$. Path $S \to A \to B \to C$ (length 5) is unique.
        \item \textbf{Vertex $D$:} Incoming paths can arrive via $C$ or $B$:
        \begin{itemize}
            \item Via $C$: $S \to A \to B \to C \to D$ with length $5 + 2 = 7$.
            \item Via $B$: $S \to A \to B \to D$ with length $4 + 4 = 8$, or $S \to B \to D$ with length $5 + 4 = 9$.
        \end{itemize}
        Since $7 < 8 < 9$, the path $S \to A \to B \to C \to D$ is uniquely minimal.
        \item \textbf{Vertices $E, F, G$:} Each of these nodes possesses exactly one incoming edge within the graph topology ($(D, E)$, $(E, F)$, and $(F, G)$ respectively). Consequently, their shortest paths are bound to uniquely extend the unique shortest path found for $D$. 
    \end{itemize}
    As all alternative paths yield strictly larger values, the shortest path to every vertex is unambiguously \textbf{uniquely determined}.
\end{enumerate}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item Consider a directed weighted graph with vertices $\{A, B, C, D\}$ and edges $A \to B\ (3)$, $B \to C\ (2)$, $C \to D\ (2)$, $D \to B\ (-8)$, $A \to D\ (10)$, $B \to D\ (5)$. Determine whether the graph contains a negative weight cycle by applying the Floyd-Warshall algorithm. If a negative cycle exists, identify the vertex (or vertices) involved in the cycle.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25}
\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Graph Representation and TikZ Diagram}

To find out whether the directed graph contains a negative weight cycle, we define the vertex mapping to matrix indices as follows:
\[ A \mapsto 1, \quad B \mapsto 2, \quad C \mapsto 3, \quad D \mapsto 4 \]

The structural representation of the input graph is visualized below:

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    vertex/.style={circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, minimum size=26pt, font=\bfseries, text=cA}, % \color{cA} পরিবর্তন করে text=cA করা হয়েছে
    edge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, color=black},
    loopedge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, color=red!80!black}
]
    % Vertices positioned in a diamond layout to avoid overlaps
    \node[vertex] (A) at (0, 2) {A};
    \node[vertex] (B) at (3, 4) {B};
    \node[vertex] (C) at (6, 2) {C};
    \node[vertex] (D) at (3, 0) {D};

    % Graph edges
    \draw[edge] (A) -- node[above left] {3} (B);
    \draw[edge] (B) -- node[above right] {2} (C);
    \draw[edge] (C) -- node[below right] {2} (D);
    \draw[edge] (A) -- node[below left] {10} (D);
    
    % Bi-directional paths with distinct curves to preserve visibility
    \draw[edge] (B) to[bend left=20] node[right] {5} (D);
    \draw[edge] (D) to[bend left=20] node[left] {-8} (B);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsection*{2. Floyd-Warshall Algorithm Foundation}

The Floyd-Warshall algorithm is a dynamic programming approach that computes all-pairs shortest paths. Let $D^{(k)}[i][j]$ represent the weight of the shortest path from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$ such that all intermediate vertices are chosen from the set $\{1, 2, \dots, k\}$. 

The fundamental dynamic programming recurrence relation is given by:
\[
D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min\left(D^{(k-1)}[i][j], \; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]\right)
\]

\subsubsection*{Intuition Behind Negative Cycle Detection}
\textit{In a graph without negative cycles, the shortest path from any vertex $i$ to itself ($D[i][i]$) must always be $0$. If there exists a negative cycle reachable from vertex $i$, the algorithm will discover a path through the cycle that improves the self-distance, rendering $D^{(n)}[i][i] < 0$. Therefore, a negative diagonal entry at the end of the execution serves as a definitive proof of a negative weight cycle.}

\vspace{0.3cm}
% সংশোধন: ফ্লট বাদ দিয়ে সরাসরি ডিজাইন করা অ্যালগরিদম ব্লক
\begin{tcolorbox}[colback=cA, colback=white, colframe=cA!50, arc=3pt, title=\textbf{Algorithm: Floyd-Warshall with Cycle Validation}]
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State Initialize $D^{(0)}$ of size $n \times n$ using given edge weights
\For{$k = 1$ \to $n$}
    \For{$i = 1$ \to $n$}
        \For{$j = 1$ \to $n$}
            \State $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min\left(D^{(k-1)}[i][j], \; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]\right)$
        \EndFor
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\end{algorithmic}
\end{tcolorbox}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsection*{3. Step-by-Step Matrix Execution Tracing}

\subsubsection*{Step 0: Initial Base Matrix $D^{(0)}$}
The base matrix constructed directly from the graph edges where $D^{(0)}[i][i] = 0$ and non-existent edges are initialized to $\infty$:
\[
D^{(0)} = \begin{pmatrix}
0 & 3 & \infty & 10 \\
\infty & 0 & 2 & 5 \\
\infty & \infty & 0 & 2 \\
\infty & -8 & \infty & 0
\end{pmatrix}
\]

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Intermediate Vertex $k = 1$ (Vertex $A$)}
We evaluate paths passing through intermediate node $A$. Since no other vertices can reach $A$ (Column 1 contains only $\infty$ except for $D[1][1]$), no shorter paths can be formed.
\[
D^{(1)} = D^{(0)} = \begin{pmatrix}
0 & 3 & \infty & 10 \\
\infty & 0 & 2 & 5 \\
\infty & \infty & 0 & 2 \\
\infty & -8 & \infty & 0
\end{pmatrix}
\]

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Intermediate Vertex $k = 2$ (Vertex $B$)}
We evaluate paths passing through intermediate node $B$. The updating computations are:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $D^{(2)}[1][3] = \min(\infty, D^{(1)}[1][2] + D^{(1)}[2][3]) = \min(\infty, 3 + 2) = 5$
    \item $D^{(2)}[1][4] = \min(10, D^{(1)}[1][2] + D^{(1)}[2][4]) = \min(10, 3 + 5) = 8$
    \item $D^{(2)}[4][3] = \min(\infty, D^{(1)}[4][2] + D^{(1)}[2][3]) = \min(\infty, -8 + 2) = -6$
    \item $D^{(2)}[4][4] = \min(0, D^{(1)}[4][2] + D^{(1)}[2][4]) = \min(0, -8 + 5) = -3$
\end{itemize}
\[
D^{(2)} = \begin{pmatrix}
0 & 3 & 5 & 8 \\
\infty & 0 & 2 & 5 \\
\infty & \infty & 0 & 2 \\
\infty & -8 & -6 & -3
\end{pmatrix}
\]

\subsubsection*{Step 3: Intermediate Vertex $k = 3$ (Vertex $C$)}
We evaluate paths passing through intermediate node $C$. The updating computations are:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $D^{(3)}[1][4] = \min(8, D^{(2)}[1][3] + D^{(2)}[3][4]) = \min(8, 5 + 2) = 7$
    \item $D^{(3)}[2][4] = \min(5, D^{(2)}[2][3] + D^{(2)}[3][4]) = \min(5, 2 + 2) = 4$
    \item $D^{(3)}[4][4] = \min(-3, D^{(2)}[4][3] + D^{(2)}[3][4]) = \min(-3, -6 + 2) = -4$
\end{itemize}
\[
D^{(3)} = \begin{pmatrix}
0 & 3 & 5 & 7 \\
\infty & 0 & 2 & 4 \\
\infty & \infty & 0 & 2 \\
\infty & -8 & -6 & -4
\end{pmatrix}
\]

\subsubsection*{Step 4: Intermediate Vertex $k = 4$ (Vertex $D$)}
We evaluate paths passing through intermediate node $D$. The updating computations are:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $D^{(4)}[1][2] = \min(3, D^{(3)}[1][4] + D^{(3)}[4][2]) = \min(3, 7 + (-8)) = -1$
    \item $D^{(4)}[1][3] = \min(5, D^{(3)}[1][4] + D^{(3)}[4][3]) = \min(5, 7 + (-6)) = 1$
    \item $D^{(4)}[2][2] = \min(0, D^{(3)}[2][4] + D^{(3)}[4][2]) = \min(0, 4 + (-8)) = -4$
    \item $D^{(4)}[2][3] = \min(2, D^{(3)}[2][4] + D^{(3)}[4][3]) = \min(2, 4 + (-6)) = -2$
    \item $D^{(4)}[3][2] = \min(\infty, D^{(3)}[3][4] + D^{(3)}[4][2]) = \min(\infty, 2 + (-8)) = -6$
    \item $D^{(4)}[3][3] = \min(0, D^{(3)}[3][4] + D^{(3)}[4][3]) = \min(0, 2 + (-6)) = -4$
    \item $D^{(4)}[4][2] = \min(-8, D^{(3)}[4][4] + D^{(3)}[4][2]) = \min(-8, -4 + (-8)) = -12$
    \item $D^{(4)}[4][3] = \min(-6, D^{(3)}[4][4] + D^{(3)}[4][3]) = \min(-6, -4 + (-6)) = -10$
    \item $D^{(4)}[4][4] = \min(-4, D^{(3)}[4][4] + D^{(3)}[4][4]) = \min(-4, -4 + (-4)) = -8$
\end{itemize}
\[
D^{(4)} = \begin{pmatrix}
0 & -1 & 1 & 7 \\
\infty & -4 & -2 & 4 \\
\infty & -6 & -4 & 2 \\
\infty & -12 & -10 & -8
\end{pmatrix}
\]

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsection*{4. Conclusion and Identification of Cycle Vertices}

By scanning the primary main diagonal of the final matrix $D^{(4)}$, we observe the following values:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $D^{(4)}[1][1] = D^{(4)}[A][A] = 0$
    \item $D^{(4)}[2][2] = D^{(4)}[B][B] = -4 \quad \mathbf{( < 0 )}$
    \item $D^{(4)}[3][3] = D^{(4)}[C][C] = -4 \quad \mathbf{( < 0 )}$
    \item $D^{(4)}[4][4] = D^{(4)}[D][D] = -8 \quad \mathbf{( < 0 )}$
\end{itemize}

Since multiple diagonal elements contain negative values, \textbf{the graph definitively contains a negative weight cycle}.

\subsubsection*{Vertices Involved in the Negative Cycle:}
The vertices whose diagonal values dropped below zero are \textbf{$B$, $C$, and $D$}. 

We can track and cross-verify the specific sequence of this negative cycle directly from our graph structure:
\[ B \xrightarrow{2} C \xrightarrow{2} D \xrightarrow{-8} B \]
The cumulative total weight of this directed cycle loop is:
\[ \text{Total Cycle Weight} = 2 + 2 + (-8) = -4 \]
As the net path distance sum is $-4 < 0$, it forms an infinite looping optimization vector, which mathematically confirms our automated Floyd-Warshall matrix outcome.

\subsection*{5. Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Worst-Case Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(V^3)$ due to the three nested structural loops iterating over the four vertices.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(V^2)$ needed to maintain state tables across steps.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Let $c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n$ be various currencies. For any two currencies $c_i$ and $c_j$, there is an 
exchange rate $r_{ij}$ which means that you can purchase $r_{ij}$ units of currency $c_j$ in 
exchange for one unit of $c_i$. These exchange rates usually satisfy the condition that 
$r_{ij} \cdot r_{ji} < 1$, so that if you start with a unit of currency $c_i$, change it into currency $c_j$ and 
then convert back to currency $c_i$, you end up with less than one unit of currency $c_i$.
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Design an efficient algorithm for the following problem: Given a set of exchange rates $r_{ij}$, and two currencies $s$ and $t$, find the most advantageous sequence of currency exchanges for converting currency $s$ into currency $t$.
  \item Occasionally, there is a sequence of currencies $c_{i_1}, c_{i_2}, \ldots, c_{i_k}$ such that $r_{i_1 i_2} \cdot r_{i_2 i_3} \cdots r_{i_{k-1} i_k} \cdot r_{i_k i_1} > 1$. Give an efficient algorithm for detecting the presence of such an anomaly. (Hint: try formulating the problem using a graph.)
\end{enumerate}
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Core Intuition and Graph Formulation}

To solve both parts of the problem, we model the currencies and their exchange rates as a directed graph $G = (V, E)$, where each vertex $v \in V$ represents a currency $c_i$, and each directed edge $(u, v) \in E$ has a weight associated with the exchange rate $r_{uv}$. 

The fundamental challenge is that we want to maximize the \textit{product} of exchange rates along a path, but standard graph algorithms (like Dijkstra's or Bellman-Ford) are designed to minimize the \textit{sum} of edge weights. We can bridge this gap using the properties of logarithms.

For any sequence of currency exchanges $c_{i_1} \to c_{i_2} \to \dots \to c_{i_k}$, the final yield is:
\[ \text{Yield} = r_{i_1 i_2} \cdot r_{i_2 i_3} \cdots r_{i_{k-1} i_k} \]
Taking the logarithm (base $e$ or 2) of both sides converts the product into a sum:
\[ \log(\text{Yield}) = \log(r_{i_1 i_2}) + \log(r_{i_2 i_3}) + \dots + \log(r_{i_{k-1} i_k}) \]
Because the logarithm is a strictly increasing function, maximizing the yield is mathematically equivalent to maximizing $\log(\text{Yield})$. To transform this into a standard minimization problem, we multiply by $-1$:
\[ -\log(\text{Yield}) = \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} -\log(r_{i_j i_{j+1}}) \]

\textbf{Transformation:} We define the weight of edge $(u,v)$ as $w(u, v) = -\log(r_{uv})$. Now, finding the path that maximizes the exchange rate product is exactly equivalent to finding the \textbf{shortest path} (minimum sum of weights) in the transformed graph. Note that if an exchange rate $r_{uv} > 1$, then $w(u,v) = -\log(r_{uv}) < 0$. Because the graph may contain negative edge weights, we must use the \textbf{Bellman-Ford algorithm}.

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Part (a): Most Advantageous Sequence of Exchanges}

\textbf{Algorithm Design:}
We are given a starting currency $s$ and a target currency $t$. We want to find the simple path from $s$ to $t$ that minimizes the sum of modified edge weights $w(u,v) = -\log(r_{uv})$. 

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Construct graph $G=(V,E)$ where $w(u,v) = -\log(r_{uv})$ for all given rates.
    \item Initialize distance from $s$ to all other vertices as $\infty$, and $dist[s] = 0$.
    \item Run the Bellman-Ford algorithm, relaxing all edges $|V| - 1$ times.
    \item To extract the actual sequence of exchanges, maintain a `parent` array during the relaxation step.
    \item Trace back from $t$ using the `parent` array to construct the optimal path.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, auto, node distance=3.5cm, thick]
    \node[circle, draw=cA, text=cA, minimum size=1.2cm] (s) {$s$};
    \node[circle, draw=cA, text=cA, minimum size=1.2cm] (u) [right of=s, yshift=1.5cm] {$u$};
    \node[circle, draw=cA, text=cA, minimum size=1.2cm] (v) [right of=s, yshift=-1.5cm] {$v$};
    \node[circle, draw=cA, text=cA, minimum size=1.2cm] (t) [right of=u, yshift=-1.5cm] {$t$};

    \path[->, draw=black!70]
        (s) edge node[above left, text=black] {$-\log(r_{su})$} (u)
        (s) edge node[below left, text=black] {$-\log(r_{sv})$} (v)
        (u) edge node[above right, text=black] {$-\log(r_{ut})$} (t)
        (v) edge node[below right, text=black] {$-\log(r_{vt})$} (t)
        (u) edge node[right, text=black] {$-\log(r_{uv})$} (v);
\end{tikzpicture}
\captionof{figure}{Visualizing the transformed graph for Shortest Path finding.}
\end{center}

\textbf{Complexity Analysis:} Let $n = |V|$ be the number of currencies and $m = |E|$ be the number of exchange rates. Transforming the weights takes $O(m)$ time. The Bellman-Ford algorithm takes $O(n \cdot m)$ time. Tracing the path takes $O(n)$ time. The overall Time Complexity is $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot m)$ and Space Complexity is $\mathcal{O}(n)$ for the distance and parent arrays.

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Part (b): Detecting Anomalies (Arbitrage)}

\textbf{Intuition:}
The problem asks us to find a sequence of currencies where $r_{i_1 i_2} \cdot r_{i_2 i_3} \cdots r_{i_k i_1} > 1$. Applying our logarithmic transformation, this translates to:
\[ -\log(r_{i_1 i_2}) - \log(r_{i_2 i_3}) - \dots - \log(r_{i_k i_1}) < -\log(1) = 0 \]
This is the exact definition of a \textbf{negative-weight cycle} in our transformed graph.

\textbf{Algorithm Design:}
We must modify standard Bellman-Ford slightly. A negative cycle might exist in an isolated component of the graph not reachable from a single specific source node. Therefore, we conceptually introduce a "super-source" connected to all vertices with weight $0$, which is equivalent to initializing $dist[v] = 0$ for all $v \in V$.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Transform edge weights to $w(u,v) = -\log(r_{uv})$.
    \item Initialize $dist[v] = 0$ for all $v \in V$.
    \item Relax all edges $|V| - 1$ times.
    \item Perform one final $|V|$-th relaxation pass over all edges.
    \item If any edge can \textit{still} be relaxed (i.e., $dist[u] + w(u,v) < dist[v]$), then a negative-weight cycle (an arbitrage opportunity) exists.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{tcolorbox}[coltitle=cA, colback=white, colframe=cA!50, arc=3pt, title=\textbf{Algorithm: Detect Arbitrage Anomaly}]
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Graph $V$ (currencies), $E$ (exchange rates $r_{uv}$)
\State \textbf{Output:} \texttt{TRUE} if anomaly exists, \texttt{FALSE} otherwise
\For{each edge $(u, v) \in E$}
    \State $w(u, v) \gets -\log(r_{uv})$
\EndFor
\For{each vertex $v \in V$}
    \State $dist[v] \gets 0$ \Comment{Initialize to 0 to detect disconnected cycles}
\EndFor
\For{$i = 1$ \textbf{to} $|V| - 1$}
    \For{each edge $(u, v) \in E$}
        \If{$dist[u] + w(u, v) < dist[v]$}
            \State $dist[v] \gets dist[u] + w(u, v)$
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\For{each edge $(u, v) \in E$} \Comment{The $|V|$-th verification pass}
    \If{$dist[u] + w(u, v) < dist[v]$}
        \State \Return \texttt{TRUE} \Comment{Negative weight cycle detected}
    \EndIf
\EndFor
\State \Return \texttt{FALSE}
\end{algorithmic}
\end{tcolorbox}

\textbf{Complexity Analysis:} 
Initializing distances takes $O(n)$ time. The $|V|-1$ relaxation steps take $O(n \cdot m)$ time, and the final verification step takes $O(m)$ time. Overall Time Complexity is $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot m)$. Space Complexity is $\mathcal{O}(n)$ for the distance array. This perfectly constitutes an efficient, polynomial-time algorithm for both detection and pathfinding.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item Explain the Floyd-Warshall algorithm for the all-pairs shortest paths problem. In what context is Johnson's algorithm preferable to the Floyd-Warshall algorithm?
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. The Floyd-Warshall Algorithm}

The Floyd-Warshall algorithm is a classic dynamic programming solution for the \textbf{All-Pairs Shortest Paths (APSP)} problem. Given a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with edge weights $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$, the goal is to find the shortest path distances between every pair of vertices in $V$. The graph may contain negative edge weights, but it must not contain any negative-weight cycles.

\textbf{Dynamic Programming Formulation:} \\
The core intuition relies on restricting the set of \textit{intermediate} vertices allowed on a path. We label the vertices of $G$ as $1, 2, \dots, |V|$. Let $D^{(k)}[i, j]$ be the length of the shortest path from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$ such that all intermediate vertices on the path belong exclusively to the set $\{1, 2, \dots, k\}$.

\textbf{Recurrence Relation:} \\
When determining $D^{(k)}[i, j]$, there are two possibilities for the shortest path restricted to intermediate nodes $\{1, \dots, k\}$:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Vertex $k$ is NOT on the shortest path:} The shortest path only uses intermediate vertices from $\{1, \dots, k-1\}$. The cost remains $D^{(k-1)}[i, j]$.
    \item \textbf{Vertex $k$ IS on the shortest path:} The path can be broken down into two subpaths: $i \leadsto k$ and $k \leadsto j$. Both of these subpaths use intermediate vertices strictly from $\{1, \dots, k-1\}$. The cost is $D^{(k-1)}[i, k] + D^{(k-1)}[k, j]$.
\end{enumerate}
This gives us the state transition:
\[ D^{(k)}[i, j] = \min \left( D^{(k-1)}[i, j], \; D^{(k-1)}[i, k] + D^{(k-1)}[k, j] \right) \]

\textbf{Base Case:} \\
For $k=0$ (no intermediate vertices allowed, only direct edges):
\[ 
D^{(0)}[i, j] = 
\begin{cases} 
0 & \text{if } i = j \\
w(i, j) & \text{if } (i, j) \in E \\
\infty & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\]

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, auto, node distance=2.5cm, thick]
    \node[circle, draw=cA, text=cA, minimum size=1cm] (i) {$i$};
    \node[circle, draw=cA, text=cA, minimum size=1cm] (k) [above right of=i, xshift=1.5cm, yshift=0.5cm] {$k$};
    \node[circle, draw=cA, text=cA, minimum size=1cm] (j) [below right of=k, xshift=1.5cm, yshift=-0.5cm] {$j$};

    \path[->, draw=black!80, dashed] (i) edge[bend right=20] node[below] {$D^{(k-1)}[i, j]$} (j);
    \path[->, draw=cA, dashed] (i) edge[bend left=10] node[above left] {$D^{(k-1)}[i, k]$} (k);
    \path[->, draw=cA, dashed] (k) edge[bend left=10] node[above right] {$D^{(k-1)}[k, j]$} (j);
\end{tikzpicture}
\captionof{figure}{Visualizing the $k$-th relaxation step. The direct path is compared against the path passing through the new intermediate vertex $k$.}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.2cm}

\begin{tcolorbox}[colframe=cA, colback=white, colframe=cA!50, arc=3pt, title=\textbf{Algorithm: Floyd-Warshall}]
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Weight matrix $W$ of size $V \times V$
\State Let $D$ be a new $|V| \times |V|$ matrix initialized to $W$
\For{$k = 1$ \textbf{to} $|V|$}
    \For{$i = 1$ \textbf{to} $|V|$}
        \For{$j = 1$ \textbf{to} $|V|$}
            \State $D[i][j] = \min(D[i][j], D[i][k] + D[k][j])$
        \EndFor
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\State \textbf{Return} $D$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{tcolorbox}

\textbf{Complexity Analysis:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} The algorithm consists of three tightly nested loops, each iterating $|V|$ times. Thus, the exact time complexity is $\mathcal{O}(|V|^3)$, which is extremely consistent regardless of graph density.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} A naive implementation maintaining all $k$ matrices requires $\mathcal{O}(|V|^3)$ space. However, because iteration $k$ only relies on row $k$ and column $k$ from iteration $k-1$, it can be computed completely in-place, reducing the space complexity to $\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Comparison: When is Johnson's Algorithm Preferable?}

Johnson's algorithm is another technique for solving the APSP problem. It works by exploiting the fact that Dijkstra's algorithm is highly efficient but cannot handle negative weights. Johnson's algorithm bridges this gap via \textbf{reweighting}.

\textbf{How Johnson's works (briefly):}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Add a dummy node $s$ connected to all vertices with weight $0$.
    \item Run Bellman-Ford from $s$ to find the shortest path $h(v)$ to every vertex $v$.
    \item Reweight the original graph edges: $\hat{w}(u, v) = w(u, v) + h(u) - h(v)$. Now, all $\hat{w}(u, v) \ge 0$.
    \item Run Dijkstra's algorithm from \textit{every} vertex $u \in V$ using the non-negative weights $\hat{w}$.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Complexity \& Preference Context:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Johnson's Time Complexity:} Bellman-Ford takes $\mathcal{O}(|V||E|)$. Running Dijkstra $|V|$ times using a Fibonacci Heap takes $\mathcal{O}(|V|(|V| \log |V| + |E|)) = \mathcal{O}(|V|^2 \log |V| + |V||E|)$.
    \item \textbf{Floyd-Warshall Time Complexity:} Always $\mathcal{O}(|V|^3)$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Conclusion: The Sparsity Threshold} \\
Johnson's algorithm is strictly preferable in the context of \textbf{sparse graphs}. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item If the graph is sparse, i.e., $|E| = \mathcal{O}(|V|)$, Johnson's algorithm completes in $\mathcal{O}(|V|^2 \log |V|)$, which is asymptotically much faster than Floyd-Warshall's $\mathcal{O}(|V|^3)$.
    \item If the graph is dense, i.e., $|E| = \mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$, both algorithms are $\mathcal{O}(|V|^3)$. In dense scenarios, Floyd-Warshall is generally preferred in practice due to its remarkably small hidden constant factors (tight loops, excellent cache locality, no complex data structures like Fibonacci heaps).
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item Formulate a proof on the correctness of Dijkstra's algorithm.
\mk{13} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Theorem:} Dijkstra's algorithm correctly computes the shortest path distances from a source vertex $s$ to all other reachable vertices in a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative edge weights $w(u, v) \ge 0$.

\textbf{Preliminaries \& Notation:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Let $\delta(s, v)$ be the \textbf{true} shortest path distance from $s$ to $v$.
    \item Let $d[v]$ be the algorithm's \textbf{current computed estimate} of the distance from $s$ to $v$.
    \item Let $S$ be the set of "settled" vertices (vertices for which the algorithm has permanently locked in the shortest path).
    \item Let $Q = V \setminus S$ be the set of "unsettled" vertices currently in the priority queue.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Loop Invariant:} 
At the start of each iteration of the algorithm's main loop, for every vertex $v \in S$, the computed distance matches the true distance: $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$.

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Proof by Mathematical Induction}

\textbf{Base Case:}
Initially, $S = \emptyset$, so the invariant holds vacuously. When the first vertex $s$ is extracted and added to $S$, its distance is $d[s] = 0$. Since there are no negative cycles (edge weights are non-negative), the true shortest distance $\delta(s, s)$ is indeed $0$. The invariant holds for $|S| = 1$.

\textbf{Inductive Hypothesis:}
Assume the loop invariant holds for some step where $|S| = k$. That is, for all vertices $v \in S$, $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$.

\textbf{Inductive Step:}
Let $u$ be the next vertex extracted from $Q$ (the unsettled vertex with the minimum $d[u]$ value) and added to $S$. We must prove that $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$. 

We will prove this by \textbf{contradiction}. 
Suppose, for the sake of contradiction, that $d[u] > \delta(s, u)$. This implies there exists some hypothetical optimal path $P$ from $s$ to $u$ whose true cost is strictly less than our estimate $d[u]$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick]
    % Set S (Settled) - Height increased to 3.2 to prevent clipping
    \draw[fill=cA!8, draw=cA, dashed, rounded corners=12pt] (-1, -2) rectangle (3, 3.2);
    \node[text=cA, anchor=north] at (1, 2.9) {\textbf{Set $S$ (Settled)}};
    
    % Set Q (Unsettled) - Height increased to 3.2 to prevent clipping
    \draw[fill=black!4, draw=black!40, dashed, rounded corners=12pt] (4, -2) rectangle (9, 3.2);
    \node[text=black!70, anchor=north] at (6.5, 2.9) {\textbf{Set $Q = V \setminus S$}};

    % Vertices (with explicit white fills to crisp up line intersections)
    \node[circle, draw=cA, fill=white, text=cA, minimum size=0.8cm, inner sep=0pt] (s) at (0, 0) {$s$};
    \node[circle, draw=cA, fill=white, text=cA, minimum size=0.8cm, inner sep=0pt] (x) at (2, -0.5) {$x$};
    \node[circle, draw=black!70, fill=white, text=black, minimum size=0.8cm, inner sep=0pt] (y) at (5, -0.5) {$y$};
    \node[circle, draw=black!70, fill=white, text=black, minimum size=0.8cm, inner sep=0pt] (u) at (8, 0) {$u$};

    % Paths
    \path[->, draw=cA, decorate, decoration={snake, amplitude=1.5pt, segment length=5pt}] 
        (s) -- node[midway, above=2pt, text=cA] {$p_1$} (x);
    \path[->, draw=black!80] 
        (x) -- node[midway, above=2pt] {$w(x,y)$} (y);
    \path[->, draw=black!80, decorate, decoration={snake, amplitude=1.5pt, segment length=5pt}] 
        (y) -- node[midway, above=2pt] {$p_2$} (u);
    
    % Algorithm's chosen path - Reduced bend to 30 to sit cleanly below titles
    \path[->, draw=cA, dashed] 
        (s) edge[bend left=30] node[midway, above=3pt, text=cA] {Algorithm's estimate $d[u]$} (u);
\end{tikzpicture}
\captionof{figure}{The contradiction setup: The true shortest path $P$ must cross the cut from $S$ to $Q$ at some edge $(x, y)$.}
\end{center}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Because $s \in S$ and $u \notin S$, the hypothetical optimal path $P$ must at some point cross the boundary from $S$ to $Q$. 
    \item Let $y$ be the \textit{first} vertex on path $P$ that is in $Q$, and let $x$ be its immediate predecessor on $P$. Therefore, $x \in S$.
    \item The path $P$ can be decomposed into: $s \stackrel{p_1}{\leadsto} x \to y \stackrel{p_2}{\leadsto} u$.
    \item Since $x \in S$, by our inductive hypothesis, the algorithm has already found the true shortest path to $x$: $d[x] = \delta(s, x)$.
    \item When $x$ was added to $S$, the algorithm relaxed all its outgoing edges, including $(x, y)$. Thus, the current estimate for $y$ must be bounded by the path through $x$:
    \[ d[y] \le d[x] + w(x, y) = \delta(s, x) + w(x, y) = \delta(s, y) \]
    Since an estimate can never be smaller than the true shortest path, we must have $d[y] = \delta(s, y)$.
    \item Since all edge weights in $G$ are non-negative ($w \ge 0$), the subpath $p_2$ from $y$ to $u$ must have a weight $\ge 0$. Therefore, the true distance to $u$ is at least the true distance to $y$:
    \[ \delta(s, u) = \delta(s, y) + w(p_2) \ge \delta(s, y) = d[y] \]
    \item Recall that the algorithm chose $u$ from $Q$ because it had the minimum estimated distance among all unsettled vertices. Since $y$ is also in $Q$, it must be true that:
    \[ d[u] \le d[y] \]
    \item Combining these inequalities, we get:
    \[ d[u] \le d[y] = \delta(s, y) \le \delta(s, u) \]
\end{enumerate}

This shows that $d[u] \le \delta(s, u)$. However, our contradiction assumption stated $d[u] > \delta(s, u)$, which is impossible. Furthermore, an algorithm's computed path can never be shorter than the mathematical shortest path, so $d[u] \ge \delta(s, u)$. 

Therefore, it must be exactly that $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$. The inductive step holds, and by mathematical induction, the algorithm is correct for all vertices. \hfill $\blacksquare$

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Crucial Observation: Why Negative Weights Break Dijkstra}
The step in the proof where we state $\delta(s, u) \ge \delta(s, y)$ \textbf{strictly relies} on the assumption that the remaining path $p_2$ has a cost $w(p_2) \ge 0$. If negative edge weights exist, $w(p_2)$ could be negative, meaning $\delta(s, u)$ could be \textit{less} than $\delta(s, y)$. In such a case, the algorithm's greedy choice to lock in $u$ before $y$ would be premature and incorrect.

\subsubsection*{Complexity Analysis Summary}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Using a Binary Min-Heap:} Extracting the minimum takes $\mathcal{O}(\log |V|)$ and relaxing edges takes $\mathcal{O}(\log |V|)$. Total Time Complexity: $\mathcal{O}((|V| + |E|) \log |V|)$.
    \item \textbf{Using a Fibonacci Heap:} Extracting the minimum takes $\mathcal{O}(\log |V|)$ amortized, but decreasing a key during relaxation takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amortized. Total Time Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(|V| \log |V| + |E|)$.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ to store the distance array, parent pointers, and priority queue structures.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item Analyze the complexity of Johnson's algorithm for the all-pairs shortest paths problem.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Complexity Analysis of Johnson's Algorithm}

To rigorously analyze the time and space complexity of Johnson's algorithm for the All-Pairs Shortest Paths (APSP) problem, we must break down the algorithm into its constituent phases. Johnson's algorithm acts as a pipeline, combining Bellman-Ford and Dijkstra's algorithms through a mathematical reweighting process.

Let $G = (V, E)$ be the input directed graph, where $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$ represents the edge weights. Let $n = |V|$ be the number of vertices and $m = |E|$ be the number of edges.

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{1. Phase-by-Phase Time Complexity Analysis}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Graph Augmentation (Super-source addition):}
    The algorithm begins by adding a new vertex $s$ to $V$ and adding directed edges from $s$ to every vertex $v \in V$ with a weight of $0$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Cost:} Creating one vertex and $n$ edges takes \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(n)$ time.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Bellman-Ford Algorithm (Potential Calculation):}
    Run the Bellman-Ford algorithm from the super-source $s$ to find the shortest path $h(v) = \delta(s, v)$ for all $v \in V$. If Bellman-Ford detects a negative-weight cycle, the algorithm terminates.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Cost:} Bellman-Ford processes $n+1$ vertices and $m+n$ edges. The complexity is $\mathcal{O}(|V_{\text{new}}| \cdot |E_{\text{new}}|) = \mathcal{O}((n+1)(m+n))$. Assuming $m \ge n$ (a connected graph), this simplifies to \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(n \cdot m)$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Edge Reweighting:}
    Using the potentials $h(v)$ computed in Phase 2, compute the new, non-negative edge weights $\hat{w}(u, v) = w(u, v) + h(u) - h(v)$ for all $(u, v) \in E$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Cost:} Iterating over all edges takes \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(m)$ time.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{All-Pairs Dijkstra's Algorithm:}
    Since all $\hat{w}(u, v) \ge 0$, we can safely run Dijkstra's algorithm from \textit{every} vertex $u \in V$ to compute the transformed shortest paths $\hat{\delta}(u, v)$ to all other vertices $v \in V$. The time complexity of this phase strictly depends on the priority queue data structure used for Dijkstra's algorithm:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Using a standard Binary Min-Heap:} A single run of Dijkstra takes $\mathcal{O}((n + m) \log n)$. Running it $n$ times yields \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(n \cdot m \log n)$.
        \item \textbf{Using a Fibonacci Heap:} A single run takes $\mathcal{O}(n \log n + m)$ amortized time. Running it $n$ times yields \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n + n \cdot m)$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Distance Reversal:}
    Finally, map the computed distances back to the original graph's scale using the formula $\delta(u, v) = \hat{\delta}(u, v) + h(v) - h(u)$ for all pairs $(u, v)$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Cost:} Updating an $n \times n$ matrix takes \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ time.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, auto, node distance=3cm, thick]
    \node[circle, draw=cA, text=cA, minimum size=1cm] (u) {$u$};
    \node[circle, draw=cA, text=cA, minimum size=1cm] (v) [right of=u, xshift=2cm] {$v$};
    
    \path[->, draw=black!50] (u) edge[bend left=20] node[above] {$w(u, v) \quad \text{(may be } < 0\text{)}$} (v);
    \path[->, draw=cA] (u) edge[bend right=20] node[below, text=cA] {$\hat{w}(u, v) = w(u, v) + h(u) - h(v) \ge 0$} (v);
\end{tikzpicture}
\captionof{figure}{Visualizing the reweighting step that safely bridges the gap between negative weights and Dijkstra's non-negativity constraint. Time taken: $\mathcal{O}(m)$.}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.2cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Total Time Complexity}

Summing the phases, the dominant term comes from Phase 4 (running Dijkstra $n$ times). 
\[ T(n, m) = \mathcal{O}(n) + \mathcal{O}(n \cdot m) + \mathcal{O}(m) + \mathcal{O}(\text{Dijkstra} \times n) + \mathcal{O}(n^2) \]

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Best theoretical time (Fibonacci Heap):} \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(|V|^2 \log |V| + |V||E|)$
    \item \textbf{Standard practical time (Binary Heap):} \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(|V||E| \log |V|)$
\end{itemize}
\textit{Pedagogical Note:} For a highly dense graph where $|E| = \mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$, both complexities simplify to $\mathcal{O}(|V|^3 \log |V|)$ or $\mathcal{O}(|V|^3)$, in which case the much simpler \textbf{Floyd-Warshall algorithm} ($\mathcal{O}(|V|^3)$) is preferred due to smaller hidden constants. Johnson's algorithm shines strictly in \textbf{sparse graphs} where $|E| \approx |V|$.

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Space Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Graph Representation:} Storing the augmented graph as an adjacency list requires $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ space.
    \item \textbf{Distance Output:} Storing the final all-pairs shortest paths requires an $n \times n$ matrix, taking \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$ space.
    \item \textbf{Auxiliary Structures:} The Bellman-Ford potential array $h$, Dijkstra's distance arrays, and the priority queue each require $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ space.
\end{itemize}

Since the output matrix is mandatory for the APSP problem, the dominating factor is the $n \times n$ grid. Therefore, the \textbf{Total Space Complexity is \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$}.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2022-23
\item Develop an algorithm for finding the shortest path in a graph where all edge weights are equal.
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{Core Intuition: The Power of Breadth-First Search}

When finding the shortest path in a graph where all edge weights are strictly equal (let's say weight $W > 0$), utilizing a heavy algorithm like Dijkstra's or Bellman-Ford is fundamentally unnecessary. Because every edge contributes identically to the path length, the shortest path between a source vertex $s$ and any target vertex $t$ is simply the path with the \textbf{fewest number of edges}.

This transforms our shortest-path problem into an unweighted shortest-path problem, for which \textbf{Breadth-First Search (BFS)} is the optimal approach. 

\textbf{Why BFS works:} BFS explores the graph uniformly outward in concentric "ripples" or levels. It discovers all vertices at a distance of 1 edge away, then all vertices at a distance of 2 edges away, and so on. Because it operates strictly level-by-level using a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) queue, the very first time BFS encounters a vertex, it is mathematically guaranteed to have reached it via the minimum number of edges.

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Algorithm Design \& Execution}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Initialization:} Create a distance array \texttt{dist[]} initialized to $\infty$ for all vertices to indicate they are unvisited. Set \texttt{dist[s] = 0} for the source vertex $s$.
    \item \textbf{Queue Setup:} Initialize a standard FIFO queue $Q$ and enqueue the source vertex $s$.
    \item \textbf{Exploration:} While $Q$ is not empty:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Dequeue the front vertex $u$.
        \item Iterate through all adjacent vertices $v$ of $u$.
        \item If $v$ has not been visited (\texttt{dist[v] == $\infty$}), we have found the shortest path to $v$. Set \texttt{dist[v] = dist[u] + W} (where $W$ is the uniform edge weight), and enqueue $v$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path Reconstruction (Optional):} To output the actual path, maintain a \texttt{parent[]} array, assigning \texttt{parent[v] = u} when $v$ is discovered. Trace backwards from the target to the source.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, auto, node distance=2cm, thick]
    % Level 0
    \node[circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!20, text=cA, minimum size=0.8cm] (s) {$s$};
    \node[above=0.2cm of s, text=cA, font=\bfseries] {Level 0};
    
    % Level 1
    \node[circle, draw=black!70, fill=white, text=black, minimum size=0.8cm] (u1) [below left of=s, xshift=-1cm, yshift=-1cm] {$u_1$};
    \node[circle, draw=black!70, fill=white, text=black, minimum size=0.8cm] (u2) [below right of=s, xshift=1cm, yshift=-1cm] {$u_2$};
    \node[left=0.8cm of u1, text=black!70, font=\bfseries] {Level 1};

    % Level 2
    \node[circle, draw=black!70, fill=white, text=black, minimum size=0.8cm] (v1) [below left of=u1, xshift=-0.5cm, yshift=-1cm] {$v_1$};
    \node[circle, draw=black!70, fill=white, text=black, minimum size=0.8cm] (v2) [below right of=u1, xshift=0.5cm, yshift=-1cm] {$v_2$};
    \node[circle, draw=black!70, fill=white, text=black, minimum size=0.8cm] (v3) [below right of=u2, xshift=0.5cm, yshift=-1cm] {$v_3$};
    \node[left=0.5cm of v1, text=black!70, font=\bfseries] {Level 2};

    % Edges
    \path[->, draw=black!80] (s) edge node[above left] {$W$} (u1);
    \path[->, draw=black!80] (s) edge node[above right] {$W$} (u2);
    \path[->, draw=black!80] (u1) edge node[above left] {$W$} (v1);
    \path[->, draw=black!80] (u1) edge node[above right] {$W$} (v2);
    \path[->, draw=black!80] (u2) edge node[above right] {$W$} (v3);
    
    % Cross-level edge (already visited)
    \path[->, draw=black!30, dashed] (u2) edge node[below] {$W$} (v2);
    
    % Level dividers
    \draw[dashed, draw=cA!50] (-4, -1.5) -- (4, -1.5);
    \draw[dashed, draw=cA!50] (-4, -4.5) -- (4, -4.5);
\end{tikzpicture}
\captionof{figure}{BFS explores strictly by depth. The dashed edge $(u_2, v_2)$ is ignored because $v_2$ is already reached via $u_1$ at the same or lesser depth.}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Pseudocode Implementation}

\begin{tcolorbox}[coltitle=cA, colback=white, colframe=cA!50, arc=3pt, title=\textbf{Algorithm: BFS Shortest Path (Uniform Weights)}]
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Graph $G = (V, E)$, source $s \in V$, uniform weight $W$
\State \textbf{Output:} Array $dist$ mapping each vertex to its shortest path distance from $s$
\For{each vertex $v \in V$}
    \State $dist[v] \gets \infty$
\EndFor
\State $dist[s] \gets 0$
\State Let $Q$ be a new FIFO queue
\State $Q.\text{enqueue}(s)$
\While{$Q$ is not empty}
    \State $u \gets Q.\text{dequeue}()$
    \For{each neighbor $v$ of $u$}
        \If{$dist[v] == \infty$} \Comment{If unvisited, we found the optimal path}
            \State $dist[v] \gets dist[u] + W$
            \State $Q.\text{enqueue}(v)$
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndWhile
\State \Return $dist$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{tcolorbox}

\subsubsection*{Proof of Correctness \& Edge Cases}

\textbf{Correctness:} Dijkstra's algorithm fundamentally relies on extracting the unvisited node with the minimum distance. In a graph with uniform weights $W$, the distances of vertices currently in the queue can differ by at most $W$, and they are naturally ordered by distance simply based on the time they were inserted. Therefore, a standard FIFO queue perfectly simulates the behavior of a priority queue (Min-Heap) used in Dijkstra's, making BFS functionally equivalent to Dijkstra's algorithm for uniform weights, but without the logarithmic overhead of heap operations.

\textbf{Edge Cases Handled Perfectly:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Disconnected Target:} If a target node $t$ is unreachable from $s$, it will never be enqueued. Its distance will remain $\infty$, correctly indicating no path exists.
    \item \textbf{Source equals Target:} If $s = t$, the algorithm immediately assigns $dist[s] = 0$. Since it is marked visited, it won't be redundantly processed, correctly yielding a distance of $0$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} In the worst case, BFS visits every vertex exactly once and iterates over every edge exactly once. Thus, the time complexity is tightly bounded by \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$, which is linear with respect to the size of the graph.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} The algorithm maintains a queue $Q$, which in the worst case (a star graph or very wide tree) could contain up to $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ vertices. The distance array also requires $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ space. Thus, the total Space Complexity is \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(|V|)$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2022-23
\item Devise an efficient algorithm for finding the shortest path where edge weights of the graph are non-equal but positive.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]
\subsubsection*{Core Intuition: The Greedy Choice Property}

When a graph contains directed or undirected edges with non-equal but strictly positive weights ($w(u, v) > 0$), a simple Breadth-First Search (BFS) is no longer sufficient because the path with the fewest edges is not necessarily the path with the minimum total weight. 

The optimal strategy for this scenario is \textbf{Dijkstra's Algorithm}. Dijkstra's algorithm uses a greedy approach based on a fundamental structural observation: \textit{if all edge weights are positive, any subpath of a shortest path must also be a shortest path, and extending a path can only increase (or keep equal) its total weight.} Therefore, if we maintain a set of "settled" vertices $S$ for which the shortest path from the source $s$ is already known, we can greedily look at the remaining "unsettled" vertices and permanently select the one with the smallest tentative distance estimate. Because all weights are positive, no alternative path winding through other unsettled vertices could ever circle back and yield a shorter distance to this chosen node.

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Algorithm Design: Dijkstra with a Priority Queue}

To implement this efficiently, we utilize a \textbf{Min-Priority Queue} ($Q$) to instantly retrieve the unsettled vertex with the minimum distance estimate.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Initialization:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Set $d[s] = 0$ for the source vertex $s$, and $d[v] = \infty$ for all other vertices $v \in V \setminus \{s\}$.
        \item Insert all vertices into the priority queue $Q$, keyed by their $d[\cdot]$ values.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Greedy Extraction:} Extract the vertex $u$ with the minimum $d[u]$ from $Q$. At this exact moment, $d[u]$ shifts from a tentative estimate to the permanently locked true shortest distance $\delta(s, u)$.
    \item \textbf{Edge Relaxation:} For each outgoing neighbor $v$ of $u$, check if passing through $u$ offers a shorter path to $v$ than currently known. If $d[u] + w(u, v) < d[v]$, we update $d[v] = d[u] + w(u, v)$. This operational step is known as \textit{relaxation}.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, auto, thick]
    % Vertices positioned using explicit coordinates to eliminate positioning library bugs
    \node[circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, text=cA, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries] (s) at (0,0) {$s$};
    \node[circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, text=cA, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries] (u) at (3,0) {$u$};
    \node[circle, draw=black!70, fill=white, text=black, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries] (v) at (6,1.3) {$v$};
    \node[circle, draw=black!70, fill=white, text=black, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries] (w) at (6,-1.3) {$w$};

    % Connections
    \path[->, draw=cA, line width=1.5pt] (s) -- node[above, text=cA] {\small Extracted Min: $d[u]=4$} (u);
    \path[->, draw=black!80] (u) -- node[above left, text=black] {\small w(u,v)=2} (v);
    \path[->, draw=black!80] (u) -- node[below left, text=black] {\small w(u,w)=7} (w);
    
    % Prior tentative distance path
    \path[->, draw=black!30, dashed] (s) to[bend left=25] node[above, text=black!50] {\small Old $d[v]=9$} (v);
    
    % Highlight updated values with absolute positions
    \node[right, text=cA, font=\small\bfseries] at (6.6, 1.3) {New $d[v] = \min(9, 4+2) = 6$};
    \node[right, text=black!60, font=\small] at (6.6, -1.3) {$d[w] = 4+7 = 11$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\captionof{figure}{Visualizing Edge Relaxation. Vertex $u$ is extracted with a locked-in distance of $4$. Its neighbors $v$ and $w$ are relaxed, updating $d[v]$ down from $9$ to $6$.}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Pseudocode Implementation}

\begin{tcolorbox}[
    colback=white,
    colframe=cA!50,
    arc=3pt,
    title=\textbf{Algorithm: Dijkstra's Shortest Path}
]
\begin{algorithmic}[1]

\State \textbf{Input:} Graph $G=(V,E)$, weight function $w$, source vertex $s \in V$
\State \textbf{Output:} Array $d$ filled with optimal single-source shortest path weights

\For{each vertex $v \in V$}
    \State $d[v] \gets \infty$
    \State $parent[v] \gets \text{NIL}$
\EndFor

\State $d[s] \gets 0$

\State Let $Q$ be a min-priority queue containing all $v \in V$ keyed by $d[v]$

\While{$Q$ is not empty}
    \State $u \gets \mathrm{ExtractMin}(Q)$

    \For{each outgoing neighbor $v$ of $u$}

        \If{$d[u] + w(u,v) < d[v]$}

            \State $d[v] \gets d[u] + w(u,v)$
            \State $parent[v] \gets u$
            \State $\mathrm{DecreaseKey}(Q,v,d[v])$

        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndWhile

\State \Return $d$

\end{algorithmic}
\end{tcolorbox}

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Complexity Analysis}

The efficiency of Dijkstra's algorithm depends directly on how the Min-Priority Queue $Q$ is implemented because it dictates the performance of the $|V|$ $\text{extractMin}$ operations and up to $|E|$ $\text{decreaseKey}$ operations.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Binary Min-Heap Implementation:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Initializing the heap takes $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ time.
        \item $\text{extractMin}$ takes $\mathcal{O}(\log |V|)$ time. For $|V|$ vertices, total is $\mathcal{O}(|V| \log |V|)$.
        \item $\text{decreaseKey}$ takes $\mathcal{O}(\log |V|)$ time. In the worst case, this occurs for every edge, totaling $\mathcal{O}(|E| \log |V|)$.
        \item \textbf{Total Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}((|V| + |E|) \log |V|)$. For a connected graph ($|E| \ge |V|$), this simplifies smoothly to $\mathcal{O}(|E| \log |V|)$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Fibonacci Heap Implementation (Asymptotically Optimal):}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $\text{extractMin}$ takes $\mathcal{O}(\log |V|)$ amortized time. Total: $\mathcal{O}(|V| \log |V|)$.
        \item $\text{decreaseKey}$ takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amortized constant time. Total for all edges: $\mathcal{O}(|E|)$.
        \item \textbf{Total Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V| \log |V| + |E|)$. This is exceptionally efficient for dense graphs where $|E| \approx |V|^2$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ to store the tracking arrays ($d$, $parent$) and the heap element states inside the priority queue.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2022-23
\item Develop an efficient algorithm for finding the shortest path when the edge weights can be negative and the graph can have negative edge-weight cycles.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{Core Intuition: Overcoming Negative Weights}

When edge weights can be negative, Dijkstra's greedy strategy fails because a path that currently appears suboptimal might later traverse a severely negative edge, making it the true optimal path. To handle this, we use the \textbf{Bellman-Ford Algorithm}, which relies on the principle of dynamic programming and \textit{edge relaxation}.

The fundamental observation of Bellman-Ford is: \textbf{If a graph has no negative-weight cycles, the shortest path from a source $s$ to any destination $v$ can have at most $|V| - 1$ edges.} 
Therefore, if we repeatedly "relax" all edges in the graph $|V| - 1$ times, we are guaranteed to find the absolute shortest path to every reachable node, regardless of the order in which we process the edges.

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{The Anomaly: Negative Edge-Weight Cycles}

If the graph contains a \textbf{negative-weight cycle} (a cycle whose edges sum to a negative value) reachable from the source, the concept of a "shortest path" mathematically breaks down. You could simply loop around this cycle infinitely, decreasing the path cost towards $-\infty$. 

Bellman-Ford provides an elegant way to \textit{detect} these cycles. After performing the requisite $|V| - 1$ relaxation passes, the shortest paths should be strictly finalized. If we perform \textbf{one more pass} (the $|V|$-th pass) and find that we can \textit{still} relax an edge, it mathematically proves that a negative cycle exists.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, auto, node distance=2.5cm, thick]
    % Nodes
    \node[circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, text=cA, minimum size=0.8cm] (s) {$s$};
    \node[circle, draw=black!70, fill=white, text=black, minimum size=0.8cm] (u) [right of=s, xshift=1cm] {$u$};
    \node[circle, draw=black!70, fill=white, text=black, minimum size=0.8cm] (v) [below right of=u, xshift=0.5cm, yshift=0.5cm] {$v$};
    \node[circle, draw=black!70, fill=white, text=black, minimum size=0.8cm] (w) [above right of=v, xshift=-0.5cm, yshift=0.5cm] {$w$};
    \node[circle, draw=black!70, fill=white, text=black, minimum size=0.8cm] (t) [right of=w, xshift=0.5cm] {$t$};

    % Edges
    \path[->, draw=black!70] (s) edge node[above] {$5$} (u);
    \path[->, draw=cA, line width=1.2pt] (u) edge node[above right, text=cA] {$2$} (v);
    \path[->, draw=cA, line width=1.2pt] (v) edge node[right, text=cA] {$-6$} (w);
    \path[->, draw=cA, line width=1.2pt] (w) edge node[above left, text=cA] {$1$} (u);
    \path[->, draw=black!70] (w) edge node[above] {$3$} (t);
\end{tikzpicture}
\captionof{figure}{A graph with a negative cycle $(u \to v \to w \to u)$ where weight is $2 - 6 + 1 = -3$. The shortest path to $t$ is $-\infty$ because we can loop the cycle indefinitely before heading to $t$.}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Algorithm Design \& Pseudocode}

\begin{tcolorbox}[coltitle=cA, colback=white, colframe=cA!50, arc=3pt, title=\textbf{Algorithm: Bellman-Ford Shortest Path}]
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Graph $G = (V, E)$, weight function $w(u, v)$, source $s \in V$
\State \textbf{Output:} Array $dist$ if no negative cycles, or \texttt{ERROR} if a cycle exists
\For{each vertex $v \in V$}
    \State $dist[v] \gets \infty$
    \State $parent[v] \gets \text{NIL}$
\EndFor
\State $dist[s] \gets 0$

\State \Comment{Phase 1: Relax all edges $|V| - 1$ times}
\For{$i = 1$ \textbf{to} $|V| - 1$}
    \For{each edge $(u, v) \in E$}
        \If{$dist[u] + w(u, v) < dist[v]$}
            \State $dist[v] \gets dist[u] + w(u, v)$
            \State $parent[v] \gets u$
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndFor

\State \Comment{Phase 2: Check for negative-weight cycles (the $|V|$-th pass)}
\For{each edge $(u, v) \in E$}
    \If{$dist[u] + w(u, v) < dist[v]$}
        \State \Return \texttt{ERROR: Negative Edge-Weight Cycle Detected}
    \EndIf
\EndFor

\State \Return $dist$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{tcolorbox}

\subsubsection*{Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Initialization takes $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ time.
        \item Phase 1 consists of $|V| - 1$ iterations. Inside each iteration, we process every edge $(u, v) \in E$ exactly once. This takes $\mathcal{O}(|V| \cdot |E|)$ time.
        \item Phase 2 iterates over all edges one final time, taking $\mathcal{O}(|E|)$ time.
        \item \textbf{Overall Worst-Case Time Complexity:} \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(|V| \cdot |E|)$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} The algorithm strictly maintains two arrays of size $|V|$ (the $dist$ array and the $parent$ array) alongside the graph representation. Thus, the \textbf{Auxiliary Space Complexity is \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(|V|)$}.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item The Bangladesh government is deeply concerned about addressing the severe traffic congestion issues in Dhaka. Despite numerous attempts to alleviate the problem, it remains a persistent challenge. Recognizing the need for fresh perspectives and innovative solutions, the government has turned to experts like you in the field of computer science, seeking guidance on a novel approach.

Your proposed solution is straightforward: the construction of new roads. However, the key challenge lies in ensuring the effective utilization of these newly built roads. This can only be achieved by reducing travel costs along specific routes. By doing so, the traffic will naturally redistribute throughout the network, alleviating congestion in critical areas.

In this problem, a map of Dhaka is represented as an undirected graph, where each node represents a station and each edge represents a road between two stations. These nodes are depicted as simple 2-D geometric points, and the cost of traveling along a road is directly proportional to the Euclidean distance between two nodes.

The proposed strategy involves temporarily constructing a single road, with a specific criterion for selecting the two nodes between which this new road will be built. To make the decision, whether a new road will be built between node $u$ and node $v$, the following formula is employed:
\[
C_{uv} = \sum \left( \text{PreCost}_{ij} - \text{CurCost}_{ij} \right)
\]
Here $\text{CurCost}_{ij}$ represents the shortest path cost from any node $i$ to any node $j$ if a road is built between node $u$ and node $v$. On the other hand, $\text{PreCost}_{ij}$ represents the shortest path cost before the road between $u$ and $v$ is constructed. The pair $(u,v)$ with the highest $C_{uv}$ value is selected as the optimal choice.

In essence, this approach aims to identify the pair of nodes where the construction of a new road will result in the most significant improvement in the overall transportation network, effectively reducing travel costs and mitigating traffic congestion in Dhaka.

From the above problem description, answer the following:


\begin{enumerate}
\item  Define the input and output of the program for finding the most efficient route.
\mk{8} 
%% Q — 2021-22
\item Sketch the pseudo-code of the solution for finding the most efficient route.
 
\mk{12} 
%% Q — 2021-22
\item Develop the most efficient solution of the underlying algorithm that serves as the backbone of the solution.
\mk{15} 
\end{enumerate}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{Mathematical Modeling of the Problem}

Let the existing transportation network of Dhaka be represented as an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$, where $V$ is the set of stations (modeled as 2-D coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$) and $E$ is the set of existing roads. The weight of an existing edge $(u, v) \in E$ is the Euclidean distance $d(u, v) = \sqrt{(x_u - x_v)^2 + (y_u - y_v)^2}$. If no direct road exists between two stations, $w(u,v) = \infty$.

The problem requires us to evaluate all possible pairs of nodes $(u, v) \notin E$ where a \textit{new} road could be built. The cost of this proposed road will also be its Euclidean distance $d(u, v)$. We need to select the pair $(u, v)$ that maximizes the total cost reduction across all pairs of nodes $(i, j)$ in the network:
\[ C_{uv} = \sum_{i \in V} \sum_{j \in V} \left( \text{PreCost}_{ij} - \text{CurCost}_{ij} \right) \]

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Part 1: Input and Output Specification}

To solve the optimization problem of finding the most efficient new route to construct, we must precisely define the operational inputs and outputs of our program.

\textbf{Input Specs:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Vertices ($V$):} An integer $n = |V|$ representing the number of stations, followed by an array of $n$ pairs of coordinates $\text{Loc}[1 \dots n] = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \dots, (x_n, y_n)\}$ representing their physical 2-D geometric positions.
    \item \textbf{Edges ($E$):} An integer $m = |E|$ representing the number of existing roads, followed by an adjacency list or edge list where each entry specifies an existing link $(u, v)$ between station $u$ and station $v$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Output Specs:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Optimal Pair:} A pair of vertex identifiers $(u^*, v^*)$ such that constructing a road between them yields the absolute maximum total network cost reduction ($C_{u^*v^*} = \max_{(u,v) \notin E} C_{uv}$).
    \item \textbf{Maximized Utility Value:} The scalar floating-point value $C_{u^*v^*}$ representing the net reduction in global travel costs.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Part 2: High-Level Pseudocode of the Optimization Strategy}

The naive approach would require recalculating all-pairs shortest paths from scratch for every potential new edge, leading to a massive $\mathcal{O}(n^4)$ runtime. We optimize this substantially. 

First, we precompute the baseline all-pairs shortest paths matrix $D$ (where $D[i][j] = \text{PreCost}_{ij}$) using an all-pairs shortest path framework. When a new edge $(u, v)$ with weight $w = d(u, v)$ is temporarily added, the new shortest path between any node $i$ and $j$ can either remain $D[i][j]$ or it can change by taking advantage of the new road. If it uses the new road, it can cross it in two directions: $i \leadsto u \to v \leadsto j$ or $i \leadsto v \to u \leadsto j$. Thus, the updated cost can be derived in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time as:
\[ \text{CurCost}_{ij} = \min\big(D[i][j], \; D[i][u] + w + D[v][j], \; D[i][v] + w + D[u][j]\big) \]

\begin{tcolorbox}[colback=white, colframe=cA!50, arc=3pt, title=\textbf{Algorithm: Dhaka Traffic Congestion Optimizer}]
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Vertex locations $\text{Loc}[1 \dots n]$, existing edges $E$
\State \textbf{Output:} Optimal edge pair $(u^*, v^*)$, maximum savings $C_{\max}$
\State Initialize an $n \times n$ weight matrix $W$ where $W[i][j] = \infty$ if $i \neq j$ else $0$
\For{each existing edge $(i, j) \in E$}
    \State $W[i][j] \gets \text{EuclideanDistance}(\text{Loc}[i], \text{Loc}[j])$
    \State $W[j][i] \gets W[i][j]$ \Comment{Graph is undirected}
\EndFor
\State Matrix $D \gets \text{ComputeAllPairsShortestPaths}(W, n)$ \Comment{Backbone Call}
\State $C_{\max} \gets -1$, \quad $u^* \gets \text{NIL}$, \quad $v^* \gets \text{NIL}$
\For{$u = 1$ \textbf{to} $n$}
    \For{$v = u + 1$ \textbf{to} $n$}
        \If{$(u, v) \notin E$}
            \State $w \gets \text{EuclideanDistance}(\text{Loc}[u], \text{Loc}[v])$
            \State $\text{CurrentCostReduction} \gets 0$
            \For{$i = 1$ \textbf{to} $n$}
                \For{$j = 1$ \textbf{to} $n$}
                    \State $\text{CurCost} \gets \min(D[i][j], D[i][u] + w + D[v][j], D[i][v] + w + D[u][j])$
                    \State $\text{CurrentCostReduction} \gets \text{CurrentCostReduction} + (D[i][j] - \text{CurCost})$
                \EndFor
            \EndFor
            \If{$\text{CurrentCostReduction} > C_{\max}$}
                \State $C_{\max} \gets \text{CurrentCostReduction}$
                \State $u^* \gets u, \quad v^* \gets v$
            \EndIf
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\State \Return $(u^*, v^*), C_{\max}$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{tcolorbox}

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Part 3: The Backbone Algorithm (All-Pairs Shortest Paths)}

The structural backbone of this entire architecture is computing the initial all-pairs shortest paths matrix $D$. Since our weights are derived from physical Euclidean distances, they are \textbf{strictly positive} ($w(u, v) > 0$). 

For a graph with purely positive edge weights, the most efficient way to compute all-pairs shortest paths is to run \textbf{Dijkstra's Algorithm optimized with a Binary Min-Heap exactly $n$ times} (once from each vertex as a root source). This is asymptotically much faster than the classic Floyd-Warshall algorithm ($\mathcal{O}(n^3)$) for any graph that is not totally dense.

\begin{tcolorbox}[colback=white, colframe=cA!50, arc=3pt, title=\textbf{Backbone Algorithm: Multi-Source Dijkstra (All-Pairs)}]
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Function} \text{ComputeAllPairsShortestPaths}($W$, $n$)
\State Initialize an empty $n \times n$ matrix $D$
\For{$s = 1$ \textbf{to} $n$} \Comment{Run Dijkstra independently from each source $s$}
    \State Initialize an array $dist$ of size $n$ with $\infty$, and $dist[s] \gets 0$
    \State Let $Q$ be an empty Binary Min-Priority Queue
    \State $Q.\text{insert}(s, 0)$
    \While{$Q$ is not empty}
        \State $(u, \text{curr\_d}) \gets Q.\text{extractMin}()$
        \If{$\text{curr\_d} > dist[u]$} \textbf{continue} \EndIf \Comment{Skip stale entries}
        \For{$v = 1$ \textbf{to} $n$}
            \If{$W[u][v] \neq \infty$} \Comment{Valid neighbor verification}
                \If{$dist[u] + W[u][v] < dist[v]$}
                    \State $dist[v] \gets dist[u] + W[u][v]$
                    \State $Q.\text{insert}(v, dist[v])$
                \EndIf
            \EndIf
        \EndFor
    \EndWhile
    \For{$t = 1$ \textbf{to} $n$}
        \State $D[s][t] \gets dist[t]$ \Comment{Save calculated distances into matrix row}
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\State \Return $D$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{tcolorbox}

\textbf{Asymptotic Complexity Analysis of Backbone + Optimization Pipeline:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Backbone Phase Cost:} Running a Min-Heap Dijkstra from a single node takes $\mathcal{O}(m \log n)$. Running it $n$ times to populate matrix $D$ takes $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot m \log n)$. Since Dhaka's road network is historically planer/sparse ($m = \mathcal{O}(n)$), this phase runs in a clean $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n)$.
    \item \textbf{Evaluation Phase Cost:} There are $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ potential new edge configurations. For each candidate edge, evaluating the updated cost reduction using our formula takes $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ time via nested loops over $i$ and $j$. Thus, evaluating all combinations takes $\mathcal{O}(n^4)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Total Time Complexity:} The entire program scales as $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n + n^4) = \boldmath\mathcal{O}(n^4)$, which is exceptionally efficient compared to the baseline brute-force execution time of $\mathcal{O}(n^5)$.
    \item \textbf{Total Space Complexity:} \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ space to maintain the foundational matrix grids ($W$ and $D$).
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}
%% Q — 2022-23
\item Design an efficient algorithm for finding the length of the longest path in a dag (directed acyclic graph).
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{Core Intuition: The Power of Topological Ordering}

In a general graph, finding the longest simple path is an NP-hard problem because it cannot easily avoid looping through cycles. However, in a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**, the absence of cycles allows us to sort the vertices linearly such that for every directed edge $(u, v)$, vertex $u$ comes before vertex $v$ in the ordering. This is known as a **Topological Sort**.

Once the DAG is topologically sorted, we can solve the longest path problem efficiently using **Dynamic Programming (DP)**. Because all dependencies point in one direction, when we process a vertex $u$, we are guaranteed that the maximum path lengths to all its ancestors have already been completely finalized. We can then cleanly "push" or "pull" path lengths to compute the longest path in a single linear pass.



\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Algorithm Design}

We will design the algorithm to find the longest path in a weighted DAG (which naturally covers the unweighted case by treating every edge weight as $1$). 

Let $dist[v]$ represent the length of the longest path *ending* at vertex $v$.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Topological Sort:} Compute a topological ordering of the graph using Kahn's algorithm (indegree-based) or a Depth-First Search (DFS) post-order reversal.
    \item \textbf{DP Initialization:} Initialize an array $dist$ of size $|V|$. If we want to find the longest path starting from *any* arbitrary vertex, we initialize $dist[v] = 0$ for all $v \in V$. 
    \item \textbf{Linear Relaxation:} Iterate through the vertices in their topological order. For each vertex $u$, look at all its outgoing edges $(u, v)$. Update the neighbor's maximum distance:
    \[ dist[v] = \max(dist[v], \; dist[u] + w(u, v)) \]
    \item \textbf{Max Extraction:} The length of the longest path in the entire DAG will be the maximum value found in the $dist$ array after processing all nodes.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Pseudocode Implementation}

\begin{tcolorbox}[colback=white, colframe=cA!50, arc=3pt, title=\textbf{Algorithm: Longest Path in a DAG}]
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Graph $G = (V, E)$ as an adjacency list, edge weights $w(u, v)$
\State \textbf{Output:} The scalar length of the absolute longest path in $G$
\State $\text{order} \gets \text{TopologicalSort}(G)$ 
\For{each vertex $v \in V$}
    \State $dist[v] \gets 0$ \Comment{Base case: path of length 0 ending at $v$}
\EndFor
\State $\text{max\_path} \gets 0$
\For{each vertex $u$ \textbf{in} $\text{order}$}
    \For{each outgoing neighbor $v$ of $u$}
        \If{$dist[u] + w(u, v) > dist[v]$}
            \State $dist[v] \gets dist[u] + w(u, v)$
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\For{each vertex $v \in V$}
    \State $\text{max\_path} \gets \max(\text{max\_path}, dist[v])$
\EndFor
\State \Return $\text{max\_path}$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{tcolorbox}

\vspace{0.3cm}\hrule\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Finding the topological sort takes $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ time using standard DFS or Kahn's algorithm.
        \item The dynamic programming phase iterates through each vertex exactly once, and for each vertex, it examines all of its outgoing edges. This processes every edge in the graph exactly once, taking $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ time.
        \item Finding the maximum value in the array takes $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ time.
        \item \textbf{Total Time Complexity:} \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$, which is strictly linear and optimal.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Storing the topological order list requires $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ space.
        \item The tracking array $dist$ requires $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ space.
        \item Auxiliary structures for the topological sort (such as an indegree array or recursion stack) require at most $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ space.
        \item \textbf{Total Space Complexity:} \boldmath$\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ auxiliary space (excluding the space required to store the input graph itself).
\end{itemize}
\end{itemize}
\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2020-21
\item \textbf{Minimum-sum descent:} Some positive integers are arranged in an equilateral triangle with $n$ numbers in its base. The problem is to find the smallest sum in a descent from the triangle apex to its base through a sequence of adjacent numbers. Explain how the Minimum-sum descent problem can be solved by Dijkstra's algorithm.
\begin{center}
   \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.2, % Grid spacing scale
        % Style for circled numbers
        circled/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\large, inner sep=0pt},
        % Style for plain numbers
        plain/.style={minimum size=0.7cm, font=\large}
    ]

        % ==========================================
        % TRIANGULAR GRID NODES (Row by Row)
        % ==========================================
        
        % Row 1 (Top)
        \node[circled] (r1_1) at (1.5, 3.6) {2};

        % Row 2
        \node[circled] (r2_1) at (1.0, 2.4) {5};
        \node[plain]   (r2_2) at (2.0, 2.4) {4};

        % Row 3
        \node[circled] (r3_1) at (0.5, 1.2) {1};
        \node[plain]   (r3_2) at (1.5, 1.2) {4};
        \node[plain]   (r3_3) at (2.5, 1.2) {7};
