        % Row 4 (Bottom)
        \node[plain]   (r4_1) at (0.0, 0.0) {8};
        \node[circled] (r4_2) at (1.0, 0.0) {6};
        \node[plain]   (r4_3) at (2.0, 0.0) {9};
        \node[plain]   (r4_4) at (3.0, 0.0) {6};

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{1. Graph Modeling}

To solve the Minimum-sum descent problem using Dijkstra's algorithm, we must map the triangular arrangement of numbers into a directed graph $G = (V, E)$. Standard Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path based on \emph{edge weights}, but the cost in this problem is determined by the values on the \emph{nodes}. 

We resolve this by pushing the node values onto the incoming edges. The formal construction of the graph is as follows:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Vertices ($V$):} Create a vertex $v_{i,j}$ for each number in the triangle, where $1 \le i \le n$ (row number) and $1 \le j \le i$ (column number). Additionally, introduce two dummy vertices: a source node $S$ and a target node $T$.
    \item \textbf{Edges ($E$) and Weights ($W$):}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{Source Edge:} Add a directed edge from the dummy source $S$ to the apex $v_{1,1}$ with a weight equal to the value of the apex. \\
        $S \to v_{1,1}$ with weight $w(S, v_{1,1}) = \text{value}(v_{1,1})$.
        \item \textbf{Descent Edges:} From any node $v_{i,j}$ (where $i < n$), add directed edges to its two adjacent nodes in the row below ($v_{i+1,j}$ and $v_{i+1,j+1}$). The weight of the edge is the value of the destination node. \\
        $w(v_{i,j}, v_{i+1,j}) = \text{value}(v_{i+1,j})$ \\
        $w(v_{i,j}, v_{i+1,j+1}) = \text{value}(v_{i+1,j+1})$
        \item \textbf{Target Edges:} Add directed edges from all nodes in the bottom row ($v_{n,j}$ for $1 \le j \le n$) to the dummy target $T$ with a weight of 0. \\
        $w(v_{n,j}, T) = 0$.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{itemize}

By finding the shortest path from $S$ to $T$ using Dijkstra's algorithm, we naturally accumulate the exact sum of the numbers in the optimal descent path. Since all given numbers are positive integers, all edge weights are strictly non-negative, satisfying Dijkstra's requirement.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{2. Visualizing the Graph Transformation}

Below is the graph representation corresponding to the provided example. The edges show the weights derived from the node values.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.4,
        node distance=1.5cm,
        vertex/.style={circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large},
        dummy/.style={circle, draw=black, dashed, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large},
        edge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, draw=black!70},
        weight/.style={midway, fill=white, inner sep=1pt, font=\small\color{cA}}
    ]

        % Dummy Source
        \node[dummy] (S) at (1.5, 5.0) {S};

        % Row 1
        \node[vertex] (r1_1) at (1.5, 3.6) {2};

        % Row 2
        \node[vertex] (r2_1) at (0.7, 2.4) {5};
        \node[vertex] (r2_2) at (2.3, 2.4) {4};

        % Row 3
        \node[vertex] (r3_1) at (-0.1, 1.2) {1};
        \node[vertex] (r3_2) at (1.5, 1.2) {4};
        \node[vertex] (r3_3) at (3.1, 1.2) {7};

        % Row 4
        \node[vertex] (r4_1) at (-0.9, 0.0) {8};
        \node[vertex] (r4_2) at (0.7, 0.0) {6};
        \node[vertex] (r4_3) at (2.3, 0.0) {9};
        \node[vertex] (r4_4) at (3.9, 0.0) {6};

        % Dummy Target
        \node[dummy] (T) at (1.5, -1.2) {T};

        % Source Edge
        \draw[edge] (S) -- (r1_1) node[weight] {2};

        % Row 1 to Row 2
        \draw[edge] (r1_1) -- (r2_1) node[weight] {5};
        \draw[edge] (r1_1) -- (r2_2) node[weight] {4};

        % Row 2 to Row 3
        \draw[edge] (r2_1) -- (r3_1) node[weight, left=2pt] {1};
        \draw[edge] (r2_1) -- (r3_2) node[weight] {4};
        \draw[edge] (r2_2) -- (r3_2) node[weight] {4};
        \draw[edge] (r2_2) -- (r3_3) node[weight, right=2pt] {7};

        % Row 3 to Row 4
        \draw[edge] (r3_1) -- (r4_1) node[weight, left=2pt] {8};
        \draw[edge] (r3_1) -- (r4_2) node[weight] {6};
        \draw[edge] (r3_2) -- (r4_2) node[weight] {6};
        \draw[edge] (r3_2) -- (r4_3) node[weight] {9};
        \draw[edge] (r3_3) -- (r4_3) node[weight] {9};
        \draw[edge] (r3_3) -- (r4_4) node[weight, right=2pt] {6};

        % Row 4 to Target (Weight 0)
        \draw[edge, dashed] (r4_1) -- (T) node[weight] {0};
        \draw[edge, dashed] (r4_2) -- (T) node[weight] {0};
        \draw[edge, dashed] (r4_3) -- (T) node[weight] {0};
        \draw[edge, dashed] (r4_4) -- (T) node[weight] {0};
        
        % Highlight the optimal path
        \draw[->, >=stealth, line width=2pt, draw=red, opacity=0.5] (S) -- (r1_1);
        \draw[->, >=stealth, line width=2pt, draw=red, opacity=0.5] (r1_1) -- (r2_1);
        \draw[->, >=stealth, line width=2pt, draw=red, opacity=0.5] (r2_1) -- (r3_1);
        \draw[->, >=stealth, line width=2pt, draw=red, opacity=0.5] (r3_1) -- (r4_2);
        \draw[->, >=stealth, line width=2pt, draw=red, opacity=0.5] (r4_2) -- (T);

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\textit{Note: The minimum path evaluates to $2 + 5 + 1 + 6 = 14$ (highlighted in red).}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{3. Algorithm}

Once the graph is formulated, standard Dijkstra's algorithm is applied. Let $V$ be the set of all mapped vertices, and $E$ be the set of mapped edges.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Minimum-Sum Descent via Dijkstra}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Matrix $M[1 \dots n][1 \dots n]$ representing the lower triangle
\State Construct Graph $G=(V,E)$ with dummy nodes $S, T$ as described above.
\State Initialize $dist[v] \gets \infty$ for all $v \in V$
\State $dist[S] \gets 0$
\State Initialize an empty min-priority queue $Q$
\State $Q.\text{insert}(S, 0)$
\While{$Q$ is not empty}
    \State $u \gets Q.\text{extract\_min}()$
    \If{$u == T$}
        \State \Return $dist[T]$ \Comment{Optimal path sum found}
    \EndIf
    \For{each neighbor $v$ of $u$ in $G$}
        \State $alt \gets dist[u] + w(u, v)$
        \If{$alt < dist[v]$}
            \State $dist[v] \gets alt$
            \State $Q.\text{decrease\_key}(v, dist[v])$ or $Q.\text{insert}(v, dist[v])$
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndWhile
\State \Return $\infty$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{4. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Number of Vertices ($|V|$):} The triangle has $\frac{n(n+1)}{2}$ nodes. Adding $S$ and $T$, $|V| = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} + 2 = O(n^2)$.
    \item \textbf{Number of Edges ($|E|$):} Each node $v_{i,j}$ ($i<n$) has exactly 2 outgoing edges. The number of such nodes is $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$. Adding 1 edge from $S$ and $n$ edges to $T$, $|E| = 2 \cdot \frac{n(n-1)}{2} + n + 1 = n^2 + 1 = O(n^2)$.
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} Using a Min-Heap based priority queue, Dijkstra's algorithm runs in $O((|V| + |E|) \log |V|)$. Substituting $|V|$ and $|E|$ yields $O(n^2 \log(n^2))$, which simplifies to $\mathbf{O(n^2 \log n)}$. 
    
    \textit{Academic Note:} Since the constructed graph is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG), the problem can actually be solved in strict $O(V+E) = O(n^2)$ time using topological sorting and dynamic programming, which avoids the $O(\log n)$ priority queue overhead. However, Dijkstra's is perfectly correct and meets the explicit constraint of the problem prompt.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} The algorithm requires $O(n^2)$ space to store the distance array $dist$, the priority queue $Q$, and optionally the explicitly constructed graph. Therefore, Space Complexity is $\mathbf{O(n^2)}$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2020-21
\item The shortest paths to a given vertex from each other vertex of a weighted digraph is called the single-destination shortest-paths problem. Solve the single-destination shortest-path problem in a graph with nonnegative numbers assigned to its vertices in addition to the edge weights.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{1. Problem Analysis}

The problem requires us to find the shortest path from every vertex $u \in V$ to a single, specific destination vertex $d \in V$. We are given a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where each edge $(u, v) \in E$ has a non-negative weight $w(u, v) \ge 0$, and additionally, each vertex $v \in V$ has a non-negative weight $c(v) \ge 0$.

The cost of a path $P = \langle v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k \rangle$ where $v_k = d$ is defined as the sum of both its edge weights and its vertex weights:
$$\text{Cost}(P) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c(v_i) + \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} w(v_i, v_{i+1})$$

We can solve this efficiently by transforming it into a standard Single-Source Shortest Path (SSSP) problem and applying Dijkstra's algorithm. The transformation involves two key steps: \textbf{Edge Reversal} and \textbf{Vertex Weight Absorption}.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{2. Graph Transformation}

To utilize Dijkstra's algorithm, we map the single-destination problem on $G$ to a single-source problem on a modified graph $G' = (V, E')$. 

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Edge Reversal:} To find paths \emph{to} $d$, we reverse the direction of every edge. Finding paths from $u$ to $d$ in $G$ is equivalent to finding paths from $d$ to $u$ in $G'$. Thus, $E' = \{ (v, u) \mid (u, v) \in E \}$, and the destination $d$ becomes our new source vertex.
    \item \textbf{Vertex Weight Absorption:} Dijkstra's algorithm operates on edge weights, not vertex weights. We can "absorb" the cost of entering a reversed edge. 
    In the original graph $G$, traversing an edge $u \to v$ adds both $c(u)$ and $w(u, v)$ to the path cost. In the reversed graph $G'$, we traverse $v \to u$. We assign the new edge weight as:
    $$W(v, u) = w(u, v) + c(u)$$
    \item \textbf{Base Condition:} The destination vertex $d$ contributes $c(d)$ to every valid path. We account for this by initializing the shortest path distance to $d$ as $c(d)$ instead of $0$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{3. Visualizing the Transformation}

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.0,
        vertex/.style={circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large},
        edge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, draw=black!80},
        weight/.style={midway, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, font=\small},
        vweight/.style={above, font=\footnotesize\color{red!70!black}}
    ]

        % --- ORIGINAL GRAPH ---
        \node[font=\bfseries] at (1.5, 3.5) {Original Graph $G$};
        
        \node[vertex, label={[vweight]above:$c=2$}] (u) at (0, 0) {$u$};
        \node[vertex, label={[vweight]above:$c=1$}] (v) at (1.5, 2) {$v$};
        \node[vertex, label={[vweight]above:$c=3$}] (d) at (3, 0) {$d$};

        \draw[edge] (u) -- (v) node[weight] {$4$};
        \draw[edge] (v) -- (d) node[weight] {$2$};
        \draw[edge] (u) -- (d) node[weight, below] {$10$};

        % --- MODIFIED GRAPH ---
        \node[font=\bfseries] at (8.5, 3.5) {Transformed Graph $G'$};

        \node[vertex] (u2) at (7, 0) {$u$};
        \node[vertex] (v2) at (8.5, 2) {$v$};
        \node[vertex, fill=red!20, draw=red, thick] (d2) at (10, 0) {$d$};

        \draw[edge] (v2) -- (u2) node[weight,below] {$4+2=6$};
        \draw[edge] (d2) -- (v2) node[weight, above] {$2+1=3$};
        \draw[edge] (d2) -- (u2) node[weight, below] {$10+2=12$};
        
        \node[font=\footnotesize, text=red!70!black] at (10, -0.6) {Initial dist: $3$};

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\textit{In $G$: Cost of $u \to v \to d$ is $c(u) + w(u,v) + c(v) + w(v,d) + c(d) = 2+4+1+2+3 = 12$. \\
In $G'$: Cost of $d \to v \to u$ is $\text{dist}[d] + W(d,v) + W(v,u) = 3 + 3 + 6 = 12$. The transformation is perfectly equivalent.}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{4. Algorithm Steps}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Single-Destination Shortest Path with Vertex Weights}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Directed Graph $G=(V, E)$, edge weights $w$, vertex weights $c$, destination $d$
\Ensure Array $dist$ containing minimum path costs from every $u \in V$ to $d$
\State Initialize $G' = (V, E')$ as an empty graph
\For{each edge $(u, v) \in E$}
    \State Add directed edge $(v, u)$ to $E'$
    \State $W(v, u) \gets w(u, v) + c(u)$ \Comment{Absorb origin vertex weight}
\EndFor
\State Initialize $dist[v] \gets \infty$ for all $v \in V$
\State $dist[d] \gets c(d)$ \Comment{Base cost for reaching destination}
\State Initialize Min-Priority Queue $Q$ and insert $(d, dist[d])$
\While{$Q$ is not empty}
    \State $x \gets Q.\text{extract\_min}()$
    \For{each outgoing edge $(x, y) \in E'$ in $G'$}
        \State $alt \gets dist[x] + W(x, y)$
        \If{$alt < dist[y]$}
            \State $dist[y] \gets alt$
            \State $Q.\text{decrease\_key}(y, dist[y])$ or $Q.\text{insert}(y, dist[y])$
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndWhile
\State \Return $dist$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{5. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} Constructing the reversed graph $G'$ takes $O(|V| + |E|)$ time since we iterate through all vertices and edges once. Running Dijkstra's algorithm using a Fibonacci heap takes $O(|E| + |V| \log |V|)$, or $O((|V| + |E|) \log |V|)$ with a standard Binary Min-Heap. The total time complexity is identical to standard Dijkstra: $\mathbf{O((|V| + |E|) \log |V|)}$.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} The algorithm requires storing the new graph $G'$ which has the same number of vertices and edges as $G$, taking $O(|V| + |E|)$ space. The priority queue and distance array require $O(|V|)$ space. Thus, the overall space complexity is $\mathbf{O(|V| + |E|)}$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2020-21
\item Explain how to implement Warshall's algorithm without using extra memory for storing elements of the algorithm's intermediate matrices.
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{1. The In-Place Concept}

Warshall's algorithm computes the transitive closure of a directed graph. The standard mathematical formulation constructs a sequence of boolean matrices $R^{(0)}, R^{(1)}, \dots, R^{(n)}$, where $R^{(k)}[i, j] = 1$ if there is a path from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$ using only intermediate vertices from the set $\{1, 2, \dots, k\}$. 

The standard recurrence relation is:
$$R^{(k)}[i, j] = R^{(k-1)}[i, j] \lor \left( R^{(k-1)}[i, k] \land R^{(k-1)}[k, j] \right)$$

A naive implementation might use an extra matrix to read from $R^{(k-1)}$ and write to $R^{(k)}$, taking $O(n^2)$ auxiliary memory. However, we can drop the superscripts and compute the updates \emph{in-place} using a single $n \times n$ matrix $R$:
$$R[i, j] \gets R[i, j] \lor \left( R[i, k] \land R[k, j] \right)$$

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{2. Mathematical Proof of Correctness (Why In-Place Works)}

To prove that removing the superscripts is safe, we must guarantee that overwriting $R[i, j]$ during the $k$-th iteration does not corrupt the calculation for subsequent elements in the \emph{same} iteration. 

In the $k$-th iteration, computing $R[i, j]$ strictly depends on two other elements:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $R[i, k]$ (an element in the $k$-th column)
    \item $R[k, j]$ (an element in the $k$-th row)
\end{enumerate}

Let us evaluate how the $k$-th column is updated during the $k$-th iteration:
\begin{align*}
R^{(k)}[i, k] &= R^{(k-1)}[i, k] \lor \left( R^{(k-1)}[i, k] \land R^{(k-1)}[k, k] \right) \\
&= R^{(k-1)}[i, k] \quad \text{(by the Boolean absorption law: } A \lor (A \land B) = A)
\end{align*}

Similarly, for the $k$-th row:
\begin{align*}
R^{(k)}[k, j] &= R^{(k-1)}[k, j] \lor \left( R^{(k-1)}[k, k] \land R^{(k-1)}[k, j] \right) \\
&= R^{(k-1)}[k, j] \quad \text{(by the Boolean absorption law)}
\end{align*}

\textbf{Conclusion:} The elements in the $k$-th row and $k$-th column \textbf{do not change} during the $k$-th iteration. Therefore, whether we read them before or after they are "updated" in the $k$-th pass, their values remain identical. It is perfectly safe to perform all updates in-place.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{3. Visualizing the Dependencies}

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9, transform shape]
        % Draw grid representing the matrix R
        \draw[step=1cm, black!30, very thin] (0,0) grid (6,6);
        \draw[thick, black] (0,0) rectangle (6,6);

        % Define Kth row and column (Let k=3)
        % Note: Y-axis is inverted for matrix representation (Top to Bottom)
        \fill[cA!30, opacity=0.7] (0,3) rectangle (6,4); % 3rd Row (k=3)
        \fill[cA!30, opacity=0.7] (2,0) rectangle (3,6); % 3rd Column (k=3)

        % Intersection (k,k)
        \fill[cA!70, opacity=0.8] (2,3) rectangle (3,4);

        % Highlighted elements
        % Let i=2 (row 2), j=5 (col 5)
        \fill[red!40] (4,4) rectangle (5,5); % R[i,j]
        \fill[blue!40] (2,4) rectangle (3,5); % R[i,k]
        \fill[blue!40] (4,3) rectangle (5,4); % R[k,j]

        % Labels
        \node at (4.5, 4.5) {\textbf{$R_{i,j}$}};
        \node at (2.5, 4.5) {\textbf{$R_{i,k}$}};
        \node at (4.5, 3.5) {\textbf{$R_{k,j}$}};
        \node at (2.5, 3.5) {\textbf{$R_{k,k}$}};

        % Axes Labels
        \node at (-0.5, 4.5) {Row $i$};
        \node at (-0.5, 3.5) {Row $k$};
        
        \node at (2.5, 6.3) {Col $k$};
        \node at (4.5, 6.3) {Col $j$};

        % Arrow showing dependency
        \draw[->, >=stealth, thick, red!80!black, dashed] (3.05, 4.5) -- (3.95, 4.5);
        \draw[->, >=stealth, thick, red!80!black, dashed] (4.5, 3.95) -- (4.5, 4.05);

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\textit{During the $k$-th phase (shaded grey), computing $R[i,j]$ (red) depends exclusively on $R[i,k]$ and $R[k,j]$ (blue). Because the grey row and column are invariant in phase $k$, the order of updates within the matrix is completely irrelevant.}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{4. In-Place Algorithm}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{In-Place Warshall's Algorithm}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Adjacency matrix $A[1 \dots n][1 \dots n]$ representing a directed graph
\Ensure Matrix $R[1 \dots n][1 \dots n]$ representing the transitive closure
\State Initialize $R \gets A$ \Comment{Copy adjacency matrix to $R$}
\For{$k \gets 1$ \textbf{to} $n$}
    \For{$i \gets 1$ \textbf{to} $n$}
        \If{$R[i,k] == 1$} \Comment{Optimization: Only check $j$ if path to $k$ exists}
            \For{$j \gets 1$ \textbf{to} $n$}
                \State $R[i,j] \gets R[i,j] \lor R[k,j]$ \Comment{In-place update}
            \EndFor
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\State \Return $R$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{5. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} The algorithm utilizes three nested loops (over $k$, $i$, and $j$), each running exactly $n$ times. The operations inside the inner loop take constant time $O(1)$. Thus, the time complexity is strictly $\mathbf{O(n^3)}$. \textit{(Note: The `if` statement on line 4 is a practical optimization that speeds up sparse graphs by skipping the innermost loop, but the worst-case asymptotic time remains $O(n^3)$).}
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} By overwriting the initial matrix $R$, we do not allocate any additional $n \times n$ matrices for the intermediate steps $R^{(1)} \dots R^{(n-1)}$. Therefore, the auxiliary space complexity is $\mathbf{O(1)}$ beyond the $O(n^2)$ space required to store the input/output matrix itself.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2021-22
\item Given a graph with integer edge weights between 1 and 5 (inclusive), you want to find the shortest weighted path between a pair of vertices. How would you reduce this problem to the shortest unweighted path problem, which can be solved using a Breadth First Search?
\mk{13} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{1. Reduction Strategy (Edge Splitting)}

Breadth-First Search (BFS) computes the shortest path in an unweighted graph because it explores vertices layer by layer in terms of edge count. To adapt BFS for a graph with integer edge weights between $1$ and $5$, we can transform the weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ into an equivalent unweighted graph $G' = (V', E')$ by replacing each heavily weighted edge with a chain of directed edges of weight $1$, separated by new dummy vertices.

Specifically, for any directed edge $(u, v) \in E$ with an integer weight $w(u, v) = k$ (where $1 \le k \le 5$):
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $k = 1$, we retain the edge $(u, v)$ as an unweighted edge in $G'$.
    \item If $k > 1$, we introduce $k-1$ dummy vertices, say $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{k-1}$, and replace the single edge $(u, v)$ with a directed path of $k$ unweighted edges:
    $$u \to x_1 \to x_2 \to \dots \to x_{k-1} \to v$$
\end{itemize}

Since every individual edge in the newly constructed graph $G'$ has an implicit weight of $1$, the unweighted shortest path length (number of edges traversed) between any pair of original vertices in $G'$ corresponds exactly to the minimum weight sum in $G$.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{2. Visualizing the Reduction}

The diagram below illustrates how an edge of weight $4$ is transformed into a sequence of $4$ unweighted edges using $3$ dummy vertices.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.2,
        vertex/.style={circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large},
        dummy/.style={circle, draw=black!60, fill=black!5, dashed, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small},
        edge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, draw=black!80},
        weight/.style={midway, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, font=\small}
    ]

        % Original Edge
        \node[font=\bfseries] at (0, 1.2) {Original Edge in $G$};
        \node[vertex] (u) at (-1.5, 0) {$u$};
        \node[vertex] (v) at (1.5, 0) {$v$};
        \draw[edge] (u) -- (v) node[weight, color=cA] {weight = 4};

        % Conversion Indicator
        \node[font=\LARGE, color=black!50] at (3.5, 0) {$\Longrightarrow$};

        % Transformed Chain
        \node[font=\bfseries] at (8.0, 1.2) {Transformed Chain in $G'$};
        \node[vertex] (u2) at (5.0, 0) {$u$};
        \node[dummy] (x1) at (6.5, 0) {$x_1$};
        \node[dummy] (x2) at (8.0, 0) {$x_2$};
        \node[dummy] (x3) at (9.5, 0) {$x_3$};
        \node[vertex] (v2) at (11.0, 0) {$v$};

        \draw[edge] (u2) -- (x1);
        \draw[edge] (x1) -- (x2);
        \draw[edge] (x2) -- (x3);
        \draw[edge] (x3) -- (v2);

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{3. Algorithm}

The overall algorithm consists of a graph construction phase followed by a standard BFS. As requested, we utilize \texttt{\textbackslash Require} and \texttt{\textbackslash Ensure} for specifying inputs and outputs cleanly in accordance with standard \texttt{algorithmic} syntax.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Shortest Path via Edge Splitting and BFS}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Directed Graph $G = (V, E)$ with integer weights $w(e) \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$, source $s$, target $t$
\Ensure Shortest distance from $s$ to $t$
\State Initialize $V' \gets V$
\State Initialize $E' \gets \emptyset$
\State $\text{dummy\_counter} \gets 0$
\For{each edge $(u, v) \in E$} \Comment{Graph Reduction Phase}
    \State $k \gets w(u, v)$
    \If{$k == 1$}
        \State Add $(u, v)$ to $E'$
    \Else
        \State $prev \gets u$
        \For{$i \gets 1$ \textbf{to} $k-1$}
            \State $\text{dummy\_counter} \gets \text{dummy\_counter} + 1$
            \State Create a new vertex $x_{\text{dummy\_counter}}$
            \State Add $x_{\text{dummy\_counter}}$ to $V'$
            \State Add edge $(prev, x_{\text{dummy\_counter}})$ to $E'$
            \State $prev \gets x_{\text{dummy\_counter}}$
        \EndFor
        \State Add edge $(prev, v)$ to $E'$
    \EndIf
\EndFor
\State \Return $\text{BFS}(G', s, t)$ \Comment{Standard unweighted BFS phase}
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{4. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Graph Size Expansion:} 
    Let $W_{\max} = 5$ be the maximum edge weight. For each edge in $E$, we introduce at most $W_{\max} - 1 = 4$ dummy vertices and at most $W_{\max} = 5$ unweighted edges.
    Therefore, the size of the transformed graph $G' = (V', E')$ is bounded by:
    $$|V'| \le |V| + (W_{\max} - 1)|E| = |V| + 4|E|$$
    $$|E'| \le W_{\max}|E| = 5|E|$$
    
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \emph{Reduction Phase:} Constructing $G'$ takes $O(|V'| + |E'|)$ time since we process each original edge and generate a small constant number of dummy elements.
        \item \emph{BFS Phase:} Running BFS on $G'$ takes linear time with respect to the size of $G'$, which is $O(|V'| + |E'|)$.
    \end{enumerate}
    Substituting the bounds for $|V'|$ and $|E'|$, the total time complexity becomes $O(|V| + 4|E| + 5|E|) = \mathbf{O(|V| + |E|)}$. Because $W_{\max}$ is a small fixed constant ($5$), the asymptotic execution time remains perfectly linear, matching the efficiency of an unweighted graph.
    
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} 
    The storage requirement for the extra vertices and edges in $G'$ is directly proportional to the size of $G'$. Since $|V'| = O(|V| + |E|)$ and $|E'| = O(|E|)$, the total auxiliary space complexity is $\mathbf{O(|V| + |E|)}$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2019-20
\item A new startup FastRoute wants to route information along a path in a network. Each vertex represents a router and each edge in the network has a bandwidth. Design an algorithm to find the path with maximum bandwidth from any source $s$ to any destination $t$. The bandwidth of a path is the minimum of the bandwidths of the edges on that path; the minimum edge is the bottleneck. Explain how to modify Dijkstra's algorithm to do this. Justify your answer.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{1. Algorithmic Strategy (Max-Min Modification)}

The problem asks us to find a path from a source $s$ to a destination $t$ that maximizes the bottleneck (the minimum edge weight along the path). This is known as the \emph{Max-Min Path Problem} or the \emph{Widest Path Problem}. 

Standard Dijkstra's algorithm minimizes the \emph{sum} of edge weights along a path using a Min-Priority Queue. To find the path with the maximum bottleneck bandwidth, we modify Dijkstra's algorithm in three core ways:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Metric Transformation:} Instead of accumulating a sum ($\text{dist}[u] + w(u,v)$), the bandwidth of a path extended from $u$ to $v$ is determined by the minimum of the bandwidth already secured up to $u$ and the capacity of the edge $(u,v)$. That is: $\min(\mathit{bw}[u], b(u,v))$.
    \item \textbf{Optimization Direction:} We want to \emph{maximize} this path metric. Therefore, the relaxation step updates a neighbor $v$ if a strictly larger bottleneck capacity can be achieved through $u$.
    \item \textbf{Priority Queue Inversion:} We utilize a \textbf{Max-Priority Queue} instead of a Min-Priority Queue to greedily extract the vertex with the maximum possible bottleneck bandwidth at each step.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{2. Visualizing the Bottleneck Selection}

The diagram below highlights why a standard shortest-path approach fails and why tracking the bottleneck capacity yields the correct path.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.2,
        vertex/.style={circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large},
        edge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, draw=black!80},
        path_edge/.style={->, >=stealth, line width=2.5pt, draw=red!60},
        weight/.style={midway, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, font=\small\bfseries}
    ]

        % Define nodes
        \node[vertex] (s) at (0, 1) {$s$};
        \node[vertex] (A) at (2.5, 2) {$A$};
        \node[vertex] (B) at (2.5, 0) {$B$};
        \node[vertex] (t) at (5, 1) {$t$};

        % Draw non-optimal path with a high individual weight but lower bottleneck
        \draw[edge] (s) -- (A) node[weight, color=blue] {10};
        \draw[edge] (A) -- (t) node[weight, color=blue] {5};

        % Draw optimal path (Bottleneck = 7)
        \draw[path_edge] (s) -- (B);
        \draw[path_edge] (B) -- (t);
        \draw[edge] (s) -- (B) node[weight, color=red!90!black] {8};
        \draw[edge] (B) -- (t) node[weight, color=red!90!black] {7};

        % Context Annotations
        \node[font=\footnotesize, align=center] at (2.5, 3) {Path $s \to A \to t$: \\ Individual capacities: 10, 5 \\ Bottleneck = $\min(10, 5) = \mathbf{5}$};
        \node[font=\footnotesize, align=center] at (2.5, -1) {Path $s \to B \to t$: \\ Individual capacities: 8, 7 \\ Bottleneck = $\min(8, 7) = \mathbf{7}$ (Optimal)};

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\textit{Note: The upper path starts strong with an edge of $10$, but is restricted by the bottleneck of $5$. The algorithm correctly chooses the lower path because its bottleneck ($\mathbf{7}$) is superior.}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{3. Modified Dijkstra Algorithm}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Max-Min Dijkstra for Widest Path}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Directed Graph $G = (V, E)$, edge bandwidths $b(u, v) \ge 0$, source $s$, destination $t$
\Ensure The maximum bottleneck bandwidth from $s$ to $t$
\State Initialize $\mathit{bw}[v] \gets 0$ for all $v \in V$
\State Initialize $\mathit{parent}[v] \gets \text{NIL}$ for all $v \in V$
\State $\mathit{bw}[s] \gets \infty$ \Comment{Source has infinite initial capacity}
\State Initialize a Max-Priority Queue $Q$
\For{each vertex $v \in V$}
    \State $Q.\text{insert}(v, \mathit{bw}[v])$
\EndFor
\While{$Q$ is not empty}
    \State $u \gets Q.\text{extract\_max}()$
    \If{$u == t$ or $\mathit{bw}[u] == 0$}
        \State \textbf{break} \Comment{Target reached or remaining nodes unreachable}
    \EndIf
    \For{each neighbor $v$ of $u$ such that $(u, v) \in E$}
        \State $\mathit{local\_bottleneck} \gets \min(\mathit{bw}[u], b(u, v))$
        \If{$\mathit{local\_bottleneck} > \mathit{bw}[v]$}
            \State $\mathit{bw}[v] \gets \mathit{local\_bottleneck}$
            \State $\mathit{parent}[v] \gets u$
            \State $Q.\text{change\_priority}(v, \mathit{bw}[v])$ \Comment{Increase key operation}
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndWhile
\State \Return $\mathit{bw}[t]$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.3em}
\hrule height 0.8pt % নিচের মোটা দাগ
\bigskip

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{4. Mathematical Justification and Correctness Proof}

To prove that this modified algorithm is correct, we show that when a vertex $u$ is extracted from the Max-Priority Queue, its assigned value $\mathit{bw}[u]$ is the exact maximum bottleneck capacity from $s$ to $u$. 

Let $S$ be the set of vertices already extracted from $Q$. We use induction on the size of $S$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Base Case:} When $S = \{s\}$, $\mathit{bw}[s] = \infty$, which is trivially correct because the path from $s$ to itself has no limiting edges.
    \item \textbf{Inductive Step:} Suppose the hypothesis holds for the current set $S$. The algorithm now selects a vertex $u \notin S$ that maximizes $\mathit{bw}[u]$ among all elements in $Q$. Let $P$ be the path discovered by the algorithm to $u$. By definition, its bandwidth is $\mathit{bw}[u]$.
    
    Assume for contradiction that there exists another path $P'$ from $s$ to $u$ with a strictly higher bottleneck bandwidth, meaning $B(P') > \mathit{bw}[u]$.
    
    Since $s \in S$ and $u \notin S$, the path $P'$ must cross the boundary of $S$. Let $(x, y)$ be the first edge in $P'$ such that $x \in S$ and $y \notin S$. 
    
    Since $x \in S$, its optimal bottleneck value was already correctly computed as $\mathit{bw}[x]$ by our inductive hypothesis. The bottleneck bandwidth of $P'$ up to node $y$ is given by $\min(\mathit{bw}[x], b(x,y))$. When $x$ was extracted, the edge $(x,y)$ was relaxed, meaning:
    $$\mathit{bw}[y] \ge \min(\mathit{bw}[x], b(x, y)) \ge B(P')$$
    
    Because the bottleneck capacity of a path can only decrease or stay the same as more edges are added, the final bottleneck bandwidth of $P'$ at destination $u$ cannot exceed its bottleneck bandwidth at $y$:
    $$B(P') \le \mathit{bw}[y]$$
    
    However, our algorithm selected $u$ from the Max-Priority Queue instead of $y$, which implies:
    $$\mathit{bw}[u] \ge \mathit{bw}[y]$$
    
    Combining these inequalities yields:
    $$\mathit{bw}[u] \ge \mathit{bw}[y] \ge B(P')$$
    
    This directly contradicts our assumption that $B(P') > \mathit{bw}[u]$. Thus, no path can offer a strictly greater bottleneck than the one identified by our algorithm, proving its complete mathematical correctness.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{5. Complexity Analysis}

The structural design matches standard Dijkstra's algorithm, meaning its asymptotic complexity depends completely on the choice of data structure used to implement the priority queue $Q$.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{Binary Max-Heap:} There are $|V|$ executions of \texttt{extract\_max}, each taking $O(\log |V|)$ time. There are at most $|E|$ executions of the \texttt{change\_priority} (increase key) operation, each taking $O(\log |V|)$ time. This yields an overall time complexity of $\mathbf{O((|V| + |E|) \log |V|)}$.
        \item \textbf{Fibonacci Heap:} Using a Fibonacci Heap, \texttt{extract\_max} takes $O(\log |V|)$ amortized time, while the \texttt{change\_priority} operation runs in $O(1)$ amortized time. This optimizes the performance to $\mathbf{O(|E| + |V| \log |V|)}$, which is ideal for dense networks.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} The storage overhead includes the priority queue, the $\mathit{bw}$ array, and the $\mathit{parent}$ pointers. Each array requires a size proportional to the number of vertices. Thus, the total auxiliary space complexity is linear with respect to the graph size: $\mathbf{O(|V| + |E|)}$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2019-20
\item Show how to express the single-source shortest-paths problem as a product of matrices and a vector. Describe how evaluating this product corresponds to a Bellman-Ford-like algorithm.
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}



%% Q — 2019-20
\item Give an efficient algorithm to find the length (number of edges) of a minimum-length negative-weight cycle in a graph.
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{1. Algorithmic Strategy}

The problem requires finding the minimum number of edges (the minimum length) of a negative-weight cycle in a directed graph $G = (V, E)$. While the standard Bellman-Ford or Floyd-Warshall algorithms can detect the *existence* of a negative cycle, they do not inherently find the cycle that minimizes the edge count.

To solve this efficiently, we can leverage the **min-plus semiring matrix multiplication** framework. Let $W$ be the $n \times n$ adjacency weight matrix of the graph, with $W_{ii} = 0$ for all $i$. In the min-plus semiring, computing the $m$-th matrix power $W^m$ yields entries $W^m_{ij}$ representing the minimum weight of a path from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$ consisting of \textbf{at most} $m$ edges. A negative-weight cycle of length at most $m$ exists if and only if there is some vertex $i$ such that:
$$W^m_{ii} < 0$$

A naive sequential evaluation of $W^1, W^2, \dots, W^n$ would find the first $m$ where a negative diagonal element appears, requiring $O(n)$ matrix multiplications and yielding an $O(n^4)$ time complexity. We can optimize this to **$O(n^3 \log n)$** using **binary lifting** (order-of-magnitude decomposition). By precomputing the power-of-two matrices ($W^1, W^2, W^4, \dots, W^{2^k}$), we can greedily find the maximum number of edges $m$ that can be traversed *without* forming a negative cycle. The length of the shortest negative cycle is then exactly $m + 1$.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{2. Visualizing Cycle Elongation}

The diagram below shows a sample graph containing a negative cycle. The algorithm tracks the path weights based on edge constraints to pinpoint the exact moment the cycle closes and drops below zero.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.2,
        vertex/.style={circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large},
        edge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, draw=black!80},
        cycle_edge/.style={->, >=stealth, line width=2pt, draw=red!70},
        weight/.style={midway, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, font=\small\bfseries}
    ]
        % Vertices
        \node[vertex] (A) at (0, 2) {$A$};
        \node[vertex] (B) at (2.5, 2) {$B$};
        \node[vertex] (C) at (1.25, 0) {$C$};
        \node[vertex] (D) at (-2, 1) {$D$};

        % Edges
        \draw[cycle_edge] (A) -- (B) node[weight, color=red!80!black] {2};
        \draw[cycle_edge] (B) -- (C) node[weight, color=red!80!black] {3};
        \draw[cycle_edge] (C) -- (A) node[weight, color=red!80!black] {-6};
        \draw[edge] (D) -- (A) node[weight] {1};
        
        % Labels
        \node[font=\footnotesize, align=center] at (1.25, -1.2) {
            \textbf{Negative Cycle Exploration:} \\
            Path length 1 edge: $\min(W_{ii}) = 0 \ge 0$ \\
            Path length 2 edges: $\min(W^2_{ii}) = 0 \ge 0$ \\
            Path length 3 edges: $W^3_{AA} = 2 + 3 - 6 = \mathbf{-1} < 0 \implies \text{Minimum Length} = 3$
        };
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{3. The Binary Lifting Cycle-Detection Algorithm}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Binary Lifting for Minimum-Length Negative Cycle}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Directed graph representation as an $n \times n$ matrix $W$, where $W_{ii} = 0$, $W_{ij} = w(i,j)$ if $(i,j) \in E$, and $W_{ij} = \infty$ otherwise.
\Ensure The exact number of edges in a minimum-length negative cycle, or $\infty$ if no negative cycle exists.
\State $k \gets \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$
\State Initialize an array of matrices $\mathit{P}[0 \dots k]$ of size $n \times n$
\State $\mathit{P}[0] \gets W$
\For{$j \gets 1$ \textbf{to} $k$}
    \State $\mathit{P}[j] \gets \mathit{P}[j-1] \otimes \mathit{P}[j-1]$ \Comment{Precompute $W^{2^j}$ under the min-plus semiring}
\EndFor
\State Initialize matrix $D$ of size $n \times n$ where $D_{ii} = 0$ and $D_{ij} = \infty$ for $i \neq j$ \Comment{Identity Matrix $I$}
\State $m \gets 0$
\For{$j \gets k$ \textbf{down to} $0$}
    \State $T \gets D \otimes \mathit{P}[j]$ \Comment{Candidate distance matrix for $m + 2^j$ edges}
    \State $\text{has\_neg\_cycle} \gets \mathbf{false}$
    \For{$i \gets 1$ \textbf{to} $n$}
        \If{$T[i][i] < 0$}
            \State $\text{has\_neg\_cycle} \gets \mathbf{true}$
            \textbf{break}
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
    \If{\textbf{not} $\text{has\_neg\_cycle}$}
        \State $D \gets T$ \Comment{Accept the edge accumulation}
        \State $m \gets m + 2^j$
    \EndIf
\EndFor
\If{$m \ge n$}
    \State \Return $\infty$ \Comment{No negative cycle exists within the simple path boundary}
\Else
    \State \Return $m + 1$
\EndIf
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}
\vspace{1em}
\textbf{4. Mathematical Justification and Correctness}

To prove the correctness of this approach, we must guarantee that:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $W^p_{ii} < 0$ implies the existence of a negative cycle of length $\le p$.
    \item Binary lifting successfully identifies the exact boundary without skipping the optimal value.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{Proof of Property 1 (Matrix Property):} By the properties of min-plus matrix multiplication, the cell value is defined as:
$$W^p_{ij} = \min_{1 \le v_1, v_2, \dots, v_{p-1} \le n} \left( W_{i v_1} + W_{v_1 v_2} + \dots + W_{v_{p-1} j} \right)$$
When observing a diagonal element $W^p_{ii}$, this represents the minimum weight of a closed walk starting at $i$ and ending at $i$ containing at most $p$ edges. Because the diagonal identity components $W_{vv} = 0$ are available, any walk of length shorter than $p$ is automatically preserved in the minimization pool. If $W^p_{ii} < 0$, a negative walk exists, meaning a structural negative cycle must exist within those $p$ edges.
\end{quote}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{Proof of Property 2 (Lifting Convergence):} Let $L^*$ be the true minimum length of a negative-weight cycle in $G$. Any simple cycle can contain at most $n$ edges, meaning $1 \le L^* \le n$. 
By definition, for any length $p < L^*$, no negative cycle can be formed, so $W^p_{ii} \ge 0$ for all $i \in V$. Conversely, for any length $p \ge L^*$, the optimal negative cycle is available, meaning $\min_{i} W^p_{ii} < 0$. 

The binary lifting loop processes bits from most significant ($k$) down to least significant ($0$). It maintains a running count $m$ representing a path length known to contain \textbf{no} negative cycles. At each bit stage $j$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $m + 2^j < L^*$, then $T_{ii} = W^{m+2^j}_{ii} \ge 0$. The algorithm sets $m \gets m + 2^j$.
    \item If $m + 2^j \ge L^*$, then $T_{ii} = W^{m+2^j}_{ii} < 0$. The algorithm rejects the step, keeping $m$ unchanged.
\end{itemize}
By the termination of the loop, $m$ will have converged precisely to the largest integer matching $m = L^* - 1$. Adding $1$ yields $m + 1 = L^*$, which matches the exact length of the minimum negative cycle. If $m$ finishes at $\ge n$, it guarantees no negative cycle exists in the entire graph.
\end{quote}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{5. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{Precomputation Phase:} The algorithm performs $k = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ matrix multiplications to compute the matrix powers $\mathit{P}[j]$. Each min-plus matrix multiplication takes $O(n^3)$ time. This phase takes $O(n^3 \log n)$ time.
        \item \textbf{Binary Lifting Phase:} The outer loop runs $k+1$ times. In each iteration, it executes one matrix multiplication ($D \otimes \mathit{P}[j]$) taking $O(n^3)$ time, followed by a diagonal check taking $O(n)$ time. This phase also takes $O(n^3 \log n)$ time.
    \end{enumerate}
    Combining these phases gives a total time complexity of $\mathbf{O(n^3 \log n)}$, which is significantly faster than the sequential $O(n^4)$ approach.

    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} The algorithm stores $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil + 1$ matrices of dimensions $n \times n$ within the matrix power array $\mathit{P}$. This results in a total auxiliary space complexity of $\mathbf{O(n^2 \log n)}$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2019-20
\item You are given a strongly connected directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with positive edge weights along with a particular node $v_0 \in V$. Give an efficient algorithm for finding shortest paths between all pairs of nodes, with the one restriction that these paths must all pass through $v_0$.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{1. Algorithmic Strategy (Path Decomposition)}

The problem requires us to find the shortest path between every pair of nodes $(u, w) \in V \times V$, subject to the strict constraint that the path must visit a specific intermediate node $v_0$. 

Because edge weights are strictly positive, the shortest path from $u$ to $w$ passing through $v_0$ can be cleanly decomposed into two independent optimal sub-paths:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item The shortest path from the source $u$ to the intermediate node $v_0$.
    \item The shortest path from the intermediate node $v_0$ to the destination $w$.
\end{enumerate}

Therefore, the minimum constrained distance $D(u, w)$ can be mathematically expressed as:
$$D(u, w) = \text{dist}(u, v_0) + \text{dist}(v_0, w)$$

Instead of running an All-Pairs Shortest Path (APSP) algorithm like Floyd-Warshall, which takes $O(|V|^3)$ time, we only need to compute distances relative to the single hub node $v_0$. We can achieve this efficiently using \textbf{Dijkstra's Algorithm} in two phases.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{2. Two-Phase Dijkstra Approach}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Phase 1: Distances FROM $v_0$ (Single-Source)} \\
    We run standard Dijkstra's algorithm on the original graph $G$ starting from $v_0$. This yields an array $\mathit{dist\_from}[w]$, representing the shortest path from $v_0$ to every node $w \in V$.
    
    \item \textbf{Phase 2: Distances TO $v_0$ (Single-Destination)} \\
    To find the shortest paths from every node $u$ \emph{to} $v_0$, we construct the transposed (reversed) graph $G^R = (V, E^R)$, where every directed edge $(x, y) \in E$ is reversed to $(y, x) \in E^R$. We then run Dijkstra's algorithm on $G^R$ starting from $v_0$. This yields an array $\mathit{dist\_to}[u]$, representing the shortest path from every node $u \in V$ to $v_0$ in the original graph.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{3. Visualizing the Decomposition}

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.2,
        vertex/.style={circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large},
        hub/.style={circle, draw=red, fill=red!15, thick, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\large\bfseries},
        edge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, draw=black!80},
        path/.style={->, >=stealth, line width=2pt, draw=cA!80, dashed}
    ]

        \node[vertex] (u) at (0, 0) {$u$};
        \node[hub] (v0) at (4, 0) {$v_0$};
        \node[vertex] (w) at (8, 0) {$w$};
        
        \node[font=\small, align=center] at (2, 1.2) {Phase 2: Dijkstra on $G^R$ \\ Solves $\text{dist}(u, v_0)$};
        \node[font=\small, align=center] at (6, 1.2) {Phase 1: Dijkstra on $G$ \\ Solves $\text{dist}(v_0, w)$};

        \draw[path] (u) to[bend left=20] node[midway, below=2pt] {$\mathit{dist\_to}[u]$} (v0);
        \draw[path] (v0) to[bend left=20] node[midway, below=2pt] {$\mathit{dist\_from}[w]$} (w);

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{4. Algorithm}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{All-Pairs Shortest Path via Hub $v_0$}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Strongly connected directed Graph $G=(V, E)$ with positive weights $w$, hub node $v_0$
\Ensure A $|V| \times |V|$ matrix $D$ where $D[u, w]$ is the shortest path from $u$ to $w$ through $v_0$

\State \textbf{Function} $\text{Dijkstra}(Graph, \mathit{source})$:
\State \quad Initialize $\mathit{dist}[v] \gets \infty$ for all $v \in V$; $\mathit{dist}[\mathit{source}] \gets 0$
\State \quad Initialize Min-Priority Queue $Q$ with $(\mathit{source}, 0)$
\State \quad \textbf{While} $Q$ is not empty:
\State \quad \quad $x \gets Q.\text{extract\_min}()$
\State \quad \quad \textbf{For} each neighbor $y$ of $x$:
\State \quad \quad \quad \textbf{If} $\mathit{dist}[x] + weight(x, y) < \mathit{dist}[y]$:
\State \quad \quad \quad \quad $\mathit{dist}[y] \gets \mathit{dist}[x] + weight(x, y)$
\State \quad \quad \quad \quad $Q.\text{insert\_or\_decrease\_key}(y, \mathit{dist}[y])$
\State \quad \textbf{Return} $\mathit{dist}$

\State $\mathit{dist\_from} \gets \text{Dijkstra}(G, v_0)$ \Comment{Phase 1: Distances from $v_0$}
\State Construct $G^R = (V, E^R)$ by reversing all edges in $E$
\State $\mathit{dist\_to} \gets \text{Dijkstra}(G^R, v_0)$ \Comment{Phase 2: Distances to $v_0$}

\State Initialize $|V| \times |V|$ matrix $D$
\For{each $u \in V$}
    \For{each $w \in V$}
        \State $D[u, w] \gets \mathit{dist\_to}[u] + \mathit{dist\_from}[w]$ \Comment{Combine the sub-paths}
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\State \Return $D$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{5. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Constructing the reversed graph $G^R$ takes $O(|V| + |E|)$ time.
        \item Running Dijkstra's algorithm twice (once on $G$ and once on $G^R$) using a Fibonacci heap takes $2 \times O(|E| + |V| \log |V|) = O(|E| + |V| \log |V|)$ time. 
        \item Constructing the final $|V| \times |V|$ distance matrix requires computing the sum for every pair, taking exactly $O(|V|^2)$ time.
    \end{enumerate}
    Because $|E| \le |V|^2$, the time taken to build the explicit $|V| \times |V|$ output matrix is the dominating factor. Thus, the total time complexity is strictly $\mathbf{O(|V|^2)}$. \textit{(Note: If only an implicit representation or an $O(1)$ query mechanism is needed instead of generating the full matrix, the time complexity drops to $O(|E| + |V| \log |V|)$).}
    
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} The algorithm stores the graph $G$, the reversed graph $G^R$, the priority queue, and the two distance arrays ($\mathit{dist\_from}$ and $\mathit{dist\_to}$). This takes $O(|V| + |E|)$ auxiliary space. Storing the final output matrix $D$ takes $\mathbf{O(|V|^2)}$ space.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2020-21
\item Dijkstra's algorithm for the shortest path problem is shown below. What would be the time-complexity of the algorithm (show line by line analysis) for an input graph $G$ if adjacency list is used for graph representation and an ordinary array is used for storing $d[\,]$ values.
 
\begin{lstlisting}
Dijkstra (G)
1  Q = V[G];
2  for each u in Q
3     d[v] = INF;
4  d[s] = 0; S = {};
5  while (Q != {})
6     u = ExtractMin(Q);
7     S = S U {u};
8     for each v in u->Adj[]
9        if (v in Q and d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v))
10          d[v] = d[u] + w(u,v);
\end{lstlisting}
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{1. Algorithmic Context}

The requested analysis depends heavily on the data structures chosen. Because the problem specifies that an \textbf{adjacency list} is used for the graph $G=(V, E)$ and an \textbf{ordinary array} is used to store the shortest-path estimates $d[\,]$, the operations behave differently compared to a standard Min-Heap implementation. 

Specifically, finding the minimum value in an unsorted array takes linear time, but updating a distance value takes constant time.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{2. Line-by-Line Complexity Analysis}

Let $|V|$ be the number of vertices and $|E|$ be the number of edges.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Line 1: \texttt{Q = V[G];}} \\
    Initializing the set or array of unvisited vertices $Q$ takes $\mathbf{O(|V|)}$ time.
    
    \item \textbf{Lines 2 \& 3: \texttt{for each u in Q \{ d[v] = INF; \}}} \\
    This loop iterates over all vertices to initialize their distances to infinity. \textit{(Note: Assuming \texttt{d[v]} is a typo in the provided pseudocode and means \texttt{d[u]}).} This takes $\mathbf{O(|V|)}$ time.
    
    \item \textbf{Line 4: \texttt{d[s] = 0; S = \{\};}} \\
    Setting the source distance and initializing the visited set $S$ takes $\mathbf{O(1)}$ time.
    
    \item \textbf{Line 5: \texttt{while (Q != \{\})}} \\
    Because exactly one vertex is extracted from $Q$ in each iteration, this \texttt{while} loop executes exactly $\mathbf{|V|}$ times. 
    
    \item \textbf{Line 6: \texttt{u = ExtractMin(Q);}} \\
    Since $d[\,]$ is an ordinary unsorted array, finding the vertex with the minimum distance requires scanning the remaining vertices in $Q$. This scan takes $O(|V|)$ time per iteration. Over all $|V|$ iterations of the \texttt{while} loop, the total time spent here is $\mathbf{O(|V|^2)}$.
    
    \item \textbf{Line 7: \texttt{S = S U \{u\};}} \\
    Adding the extracted vertex $u$ to the visited set $S$ (which can be implemented as a boolean array) takes $O(1)$ time per iteration. Total time over the whole algorithm is $\mathbf{O(|V|)}$.
    
    \item \textbf{Line 8: \texttt{for each v in u->Adj[]}} \\
    Because the graph uses an adjacency list, this \texttt{for} loop iterates over the neighbors of $u$. Across all $|V|$ iterations of the outer \texttt{while} loop, every adjacency list is traversed exactly once. The sum of the lengths of all adjacency lists is proportional to the number of edges. Thus, the code strictly inside this loop executes $\mathbf{O(|E|)}$ times \emph{in total} across the entire algorithm.
    
    \item \textbf{Lines 9: \texttt{if (v in Q and d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v))}} \\
    Checking membership in $Q$ takes $O(1)$ time (using a boolean array mapping), and checking the distance condition is basic arithmetic, also $O(1)$. Executed $O(|E|)$ times globally, taking $\mathbf{O(|E|)}$ total time.
    
    \item \textbf{Line 10: \texttt{d[v] = d[u] + w(u,v);}} \\
    Updating the distance array is a direct index assignment. Because $d[\,]$ is an ordinary array, this update takes $O(1)$ time (there is no \texttt{decrease-key} overhead like there would be in a priority queue). Executed at most $O(|E|)$ times, this takes $\mathbf{O(|E|)}$ total time.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{3. Overall Time Complexity}

To find the final time complexity, we sum the aggregate costs of all operations:
$$T(|V|, |E|) = O(|V|) + O(|V|) + O(1) + O(|V|^2) + O(|V|) + O(|E|) + O(|E|) + O(|E|)$$

This simplifies to:
$$T(|V|, |E|) = \mathbf{O(|V|^2 + |E|)}$$

Since the number of edges $|E|$ in a simple graph is bounded by $O(|V|^2)$ (i.e., $|E| \le |V|(|V|-1)$), the $|E|$ term is asymptotically dominated by $|V|^2$. 

\textbf{Final Conclusion:} The overall time complexity of Dijkstra's algorithm implemented with an ordinary array is tightly bounded at $\mathbf{O(|V|^2)}$. This implementation is highly efficient and actually optimal for dense graphs where $|E| \approx |V|^2$.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item Johnson's Algorithm makes clever use of Bellman-Ford and Dijkstra's Algorithms to do All-Pairs-Shortest-Paths efficiently on sparse graphs. Explain the basic clever idea of Johnson's Algorithm.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{1. The Core Challenge}

If a graph has only non-negative edge weights, we can find the shortest paths between all pairs of nodes by running \textbf{Dijkstra's Algorithm} $|V|$ times (once from each vertex). On a sparse graph, this takes $O(|V||E| \log |V|)$ time, which is significantly faster than Floyd-Warshall's $O(|V|^3)$.

However, Dijkstra's algorithm fails if the graph contains even a single negative edge weight. A naive guess to fix this might be to find the most negative edge weight $-W$, and add $W$ to every edge to make them all non-negative. \textbf{This does not work.} 
\begin{itemize}
    \item If Path A uses 2 edges and Path B uses 5 edges, adding $W$ to every edge penalizes Path B by $5W$ and Path A by only $2W$. 
    \item This structurally alters the graph's topology and changes which path is actually the shortest.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{2. The Clever Idea: Vertex Potential Reweighting}

Johnson's algorithm overcomes this by assigning a \textbf{potential} $h(u)$ to every vertex $u$. Instead of altering edges uniformly, it alters each edge $(u, v)$ based on the potentials of its endpoints. 

The new reweighted edge cost $\hat{w}(u, v)$ is calculated as:
$$\hat{w}(u, v) = w(u, v) + h(u) - h(v)$$

For this scheme to work, the potential function must satisfy two magical properties:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{No Negative Edges:} The new weights must be non-negative ($\hat{w}(u, v) \ge 0$) so we can use Dijkstra.
    \item \textbf{Preservation of Shortest Paths:} The relative order of path lengths between any two nodes must remain identical to the original graph.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{3. Visualizing the Reweighting Mechanism}

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.2,
        vertex/.style={circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large},
        dummy/.style={circle, draw=red!80, fill=red!10, dashed, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large},
        edge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, draw=black!80},
        weight/.style={midway, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, font=\small}
    ]

        \node[dummy] (s) at (0, 1.5) {$s$};
        \node[vertex, label=left:{$h(u)$}] (u) at (-2, 0) {$u$};
        \node[vertex, label=right:{$h(v)$}] (v) at (2, 0) {$v$};

        \draw[edge, dashed, color=red!80] (s) -- (u) node[weight, color=red!80] {0};
        \draw[edge, dashed, color=red!80] (s) -- (v) node[weight, color=red!80] {0};
        
        \draw[edge] (u) -- (v) node[weight, yshift=-10pt] {$w(u,v) = -2$};
        
        \node[font=\footnotesize, align=center] at (0, -1.2) {
            $\hat{w}(u, v) = w(u, v) + h(u) - h(v)$ \\
            By triangle inequality from $s$: $h(v) \le h(u) + w(u, v)$ \\
            $\implies \hat{w}(u, v) \ge 0$
        };

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{4. The Algorithm}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Johnson's Algorithm for APSP}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Directed graph $G=(V, E)$ with edge weights $w(u,v)$
\Ensure Matrix $D$ of shortest path distances for all pairs, or indication of a negative cycle
\State Add a dummy vertex $s$ to $V$
\State Add directed edges $(s, v)$ with $w(s, v) = 0$ for all $v \in V$
\State Run Bellman-Ford on $G$ from source $s$
\If{Bellman-Ford detects a negative-weight cycle}
    \State \Return "Graph contains a negative-weight cycle"
\EndIf
\For{each $v \in V$}
    \State $h(v) \gets \text{shortest path distance from } s \text{ to } v \text{ computed by Bellman-Ford}$
\EndFor
\For{each edge $(u, v) \in E$}
    \State $\hat{w}(u, v) \gets w(u, v) + h(u) - h(v)$ \Comment{Reweight edges}
\EndFor
\State Initialize $|V| \times |V|$ matrix $D$
\State Remove dummy vertex $s$ and its incident edges
\For{each vertex $u \in V$}
    \State Run Dijkstra's algorithm from $u$ using the new weights $\hat{w}$ to compute $\hat{\delta}(u, v)$
    \For{each vertex $v \in V$}
        \State $D[u][v] \gets \hat{\delta}(u, v) - h(u) + h(v)$ \Comment{Revert to original distances}
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\State \Return $D$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{5. Why Path Lengths Are Preserved (Mathematical Proof)}

Consider any path $P$ from vertex $v_1$ to vertex $v_k$ consisting of the vertices $v_1 \to v_2 \to \dots \to v_k$. 

If we sum up the reweighted edges along this path, we get:
$$\hat{w}(P) = (w(v_1, v_2) + h(v_1) - h(v_2)) + (w(v_2, v_3) + h(v_2) - h(v_3)) + \dots + (w(v_{k-1}, v_k) + h(v_{k-1}) - h(v_k))$$

Notice how the intermediate potentials completely cancel out (a telescoping sum):
$$\hat{w}(P) = w(P) + h(v_1) - h(v_k)$$

Because $h(v_1)$ and $h(v_k)$ depend \emph{only} on the start and end vertices of the path, \textbf{every single path between $v_1$ and $v_k$ is shifted by the exact same constant value}. Therefore, whichever path was the shortest in the original graph remains the shortest in the reweighted graph.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{6. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Bellman-Ford Phase:} Running Bellman-Ford once from the dummy node takes $O(|V||E|)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Reweighting Phase:} Updating all edge weights takes $O(|E|)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Dijkstra Phase:} Running Dijkstra's algorithm $|V|$ times using a Fibonacci Heap takes $|V| \times O(|E| + |V| \log |V|) = O(|V||E| + |V|^2 \log |V|)$ time. Using a standard Binary Min-Heap takes $O(|V||E| \log |V|)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Total Time Complexity:} The Dijkstra phase dominates, making the overall time complexity $\mathbf{O(|V||E| + |V|^2 \log |V|)}$ with a Fibonacci heap, which is vastly superior to Floyd-Warshall's $O(|V|^3)$ for sparse graphs (where $|E| \ll |V|^2$).
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathbf{O(|V|^2)}$ to store the final All-Pairs Shortest Path matrix.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item How can BFS be adapted to solve the single-source shortest path problem in an edge-weighted graph with unequal weights? Explain. 
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{1. The Core Limitation of Standard BFS}

Standard Breadth-First Search (BFS) inherently assumes an unweighted graph, meaning every traversed edge costs exactly $1$. Because of this uniform structure, a standard BFS guarantees that the first time a vertex is discovered, the path taken to reach it is the absolute shortest in terms of edge count (and thus total weight). 

When edges have unequal weights, this structural guarantee breaks down. A path with fewer edges might have a significantly higher cumulative weight than a path with more edges. To adapt BFS to solve the Single-Source Shortest Path (SSSP) problem under unequal weights, we can choose between two primary paradigms depending on our performance constraints and edge attributes.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{2. Method 1: Edge Splitting (The Dummy Node Approach)}

If all edge weights are \textbf{small, positive integers}, we can conceptually transform the weighted graph back into an unweighted graph by decomposing heavy edges. For any directed edge $(u, v)$ with an integer weight $w > 1$, we replace it with a chain containing $w - 1$ temporary dummy vertices interconnected by edges of weight $1$.

\vspace{0.5em}
\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.1,
        vertex/.style={circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small},
        dummy/.style={circle, draw=black!50, fill=black!5, dashed, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small},
        edge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, draw=black!80},
        weight/.style={midway, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, font=\footnotesize}
    ]
        % Top: Original
        \node[vertex] (A1) at (0, 1.2) {$A$};
        \node[vertex] (B1) at (4, 1.2) {$B$};
        \draw[edge] (A1) -- (B1) node[weight] {$w = 3$};
        \node[font=\footnotesize\bfseries, anchor=west] at (4.5, 1.2) {Original Weighted Edge};

        % Bottom: Transformed
        \node[vertex] (A2) at (0, 0) {$A$};
        \node[dummy] (d1) at (1.33, 0) {$d_1$};
        \node[dummy] (d2) at (2.66, 0) {$d_2$};
        \node[vertex] (B2) at (4, 0) {$B$};
        
        \draw[edge] (A2) -- (d1) node[weight] {1};
        \draw[edge] (d1) -- (d2) node[weight] {1};
        \draw[edge] (d2) -- (B2) node[weight] {1};
        \node[font=\footnotesize\bfseries, anchor=west] at (4.5, 0) {Decomposed Unweighted Path};
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.5em}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{How it works:} This graph transformation runs during input processing. Once the graph consists exclusively of unit-weight edges, we run standard BFS using a standard First-In-First-Out (FIFO) queue. The shortest path distances are accurately found because the dummy chains correctly delay the arrival of the wavefront at node $B$.
    \item \textbf{Complexity:} The time complexity shifts from $O(|V| + |E|)$ to $O(|V| + \sum_{(u,v) \in E} w(u,v))$.
    \item \textbf{Limitations:} This approach is highly inefficient or entirely unusable if the graph contains huge integer weights (e.g., a weight of $10^6$ generates $999,999$ dummy nodes, exhausting memory) or real-valued (floating-point) capacities.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{3. Method 2: Upgrading the Queue (Dijkstra's Algorithm)}

If weights are arbitrary positive real numbers or large integers, adding structural dummy elements becomes impossible. Instead of modifying the graph topology to fit the algorithm, we \textbf{modify the algorithm's tracking queue to fit the graph}. This adaptation is formally recognized as \textbf{Dijkstra's Algorithm}.

In standard BFS, the FIFO queue keeps nodes sorted by distance because every traversal step increments the distance uniformly by $+1$. To handle non-uniform steps, we replace the basic FIFO queue with a \textbf{Min-Priority Queue}.

\vspace{0.5em}
\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.1,
        vertex/.style={circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\small},
        current/.style={circle, draw=red, fill=red!10, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\small},
        edge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, draw=black!80},
        weight/.style={midway, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, font=\footnotesize}
    ]
        \node[current, label=above:{\footnotesize ExtractMin}] (u) at (0,0) {$u$};
        \node[vertex] (v1) at (2.5, 0.8) {$v_1$};
        \node[vertex] (v2) at (2.5, -0.8) {$v_2$};

        \draw[edge] (u) -- (v1) node[weight] {$w=7$};
        \draw[edge] (u) -- (v2) node[weight] {$w=2$};

        \node[draw, rectangle, rounded corners, fill=gray!10, thick, font=\footnotesize, align=left] at (6, 0) {
            \textbf{Priority Queue Operations:} \\
            $\bullet$ Extracts minimum element dynamically \\
            $\bullet$ Updates key if: $d[u] + w(u,v) < d[v]$ \\
            $\bullet$ Maintains correct traversal wave front
        };
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.5em}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{How it works:} Instead of pulling the oldest vertex, the priority queue always extracts the vertex $u \in Q$ possessing the minimum tentative distance $d[u]$. When $u$ is processed, we relax all its outgoing edges. If a shorter path to a neighbor $v$ is discovered through $u$, we update $d[v]$ and adjust its placement in the priority queue (\texttt{Decrease-Key}).
    \item \textbf{Complexity:} Using a binary min-heap implementation, the execution time is bounded at $O((|V| + |E|) \log |V|)$. 
    \item \textbf{Limitations:} While handles real numbers effectively, this priority queue adaptation relies on the property that paths grow monotonically. It cannot handle edges with \textbf{negative weights} without structural alterations (such as Johnson's reweighting potentials).
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{4. Algorithmic Synthesis Comparison}

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Metric} & \textbf{Standard BFS} & \textbf{Edge-Splitting BFS} & \textbf{Priority Queue BFS (Dijkstra)} \\ \hline
\textbf{Weight Constraint} & Edges must be exactly $1$ & Small Positive Integers & Non-Negative Real Numbers \\ \hline
\textbf{Core Data Structure} & FIFO Queue & FIFO Queue & Min-Priority Queue (Heap) \\ \hline
\textbf{Graph Transformations} & None Required & Generates $w-1$ Dummy Nodes & None Required \\ \hline
\textbf{Time Complexity} & $O(|V| + |E|)$ & $O(|V| + \sum w)$ & $O((|V| + |E|) \log |V|)$ \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Explain the intuitive idea behind the Bellman-Ford algorithm. How can you detect a negative cycle using Bellman-Ford? \mk{6+2}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{1. The Intuitive Idea Behind Bellman-Ford}

The Bellman-Ford algorithm is fundamentally rooted in dynamic programming and the principle of \textbf{edge relaxation}. Its core intuition revolves around the concept of \emph{iterative path expansion} or information "rippling."

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Edge Relaxation:} The primary mechanism is checking whether an edge $(u, v)$ offers a shortcut to $v$ via $u$:
    $$d[v] = \min(d[v], d[u] + w(u, v))$$
    \item \textbf{The Ripple Effect:} Instead of selectively tracing single paths like Depth-First Search (DFS) or expanding greedily by immediate distance like Dijkstra's, Bellman-Ford simply sweeps through \textbf{every single edge} in the graph globally. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item After \textbf{1 pass} of relaxing all edges, the algorithm guarantees it has discovered the absolute shortest paths for all vertices that are exactly $1$ edge away from the source.
        \item After \textbf{2 passes}, it guarantees the shortest paths for all vertices at most $2$ edges away.
        \item After \textbf{k passes}, it guarantees the optimal paths restricted to at most $k$ edges.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{The Strict Upper Bound:} In any valid graph containing $|V|$ vertices, a simple path (a path that does not revisit any vertex) can contain at most $|V| - 1$ edges. Therefore, looping through the relaxation step exactly $|V| - 1$ times guarantees that the shortest-path information has rippled across the longest possible simple path in the graph.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{2. Visualizing a Negative Cycle Contraction}

The diagram below shows how a negative-weight cycle causes path distances to continually drop on successive passes, preventing stabilization.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.2,
        vertex/.style={circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!10, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large},
        edge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, draw=black!80},
        cycle_edge/.style={->, >=stealth, line width=1.5pt, draw=red!70},
        weight/.style={midway, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, font=\small\bfseries}
    ]
        \node[vertex] (A) at (0, 1.5) {$A$};
        \node[vertex] (B) at (1.7, 0) {$B$};
        \node[vertex] (C) at (-1.7, 0) {$C$};

        \draw[edge] (A) -- (B) node[weight] {4};
        \draw[cycle_edge] (B) -- (C) node[weight, color=red!80!black] {-6};
        \draw[cycle_edge] (C) -- (A) node[weight, color=red!80!black] {1};
        
        \node[font=\footnotesize, align=center] at (0, -0.9) {
            \textbf{Negative Cycle:} $A \to B \to C \to A$ \\
            Total Weight: $4 + (-6) + 1 = -1$ \\
            Each full traversal reduces all vertex distances by $-1$ indefinitely.
        };
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{3. Detecting a Negative Cycle}

A \textbf{negative-weight cycle} is a reachable loop whose aggregate edge weight sum is less than zero. If a graph contains such a cycle, a true "shortest path" cannot exist because an algorithm could loop infinitely to drive the path cost down toward $-\infty$. 

Bellman-Ford exploits the Pigeonhole Principle to detect these cycles using a simple mathematical check:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{The Stability Property:} If the graph is free of negative cycles, all shortest paths must stabilize by the end of the $(|V|-1)$-th edge relaxation pass.
    \item \textbf{The Extra Verification Pass:} The algorithm executes exactly \textbf{one extra pass} (the $|V|$-th iteration) over all edges.
    \item \textbf{The Condition:} If any edge $(u, v)$ can still be relaxed such that:
    $$d[u] + w(u, v) < d[v]$$
    \item \textbf{The Conclusion:} A successful relaxation on the $|V|$-th pass means there exists a path containing $|V|$ edges that is shorter than any path containing $|V|-1$ edges. By the Pigeonhole Principle, a path with $|V|$ edges traversing $|V|$ unique vertices must contain a cycle. Because traversing this cycle lowered the overall path cost, the cycle's total weight \textbf{must be negative}.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{4. The Complete Bellman-Ford Algorithm with Detection}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Bellman-Ford Shortest Paths and Negative Cycle Detection}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Graph $G=(V, E)$ with edge weights $w$, source vertex $s$
\Ensure Distance array $d$, or an error if a negative cycle is detected
\State Initialize $d[v] \gets \infty$ for all $v \in V$; $d[s] \gets 0$
\For{$i \gets 1$ \textbf{to} $|V| - 1$} \Comment{Core $|V|-1$ relaxation loops}
    \For{each edge $(u, v) \in E$}
        \If{$d[u] + w(u, v) < d[v]$}
            \State $d[v] \gets d[u] + w(u, v)$
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\For{each edge $(u, v) \in E$} \Comment{$|V|$-th verification loop}
    \If{$d[u] + w(u, v) < d[v]$}
        \State \Return "Error: Graph contains a negative-weight cycle"
    \EndIf
\EndFor
\State \Return $d$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write Dijkstra's algorithm for the single-source shortest path problem. Prove its correctness. Analyze the time complexity of the algorithm. What would be the running time if a Fibonacci heap is used instead of a binary heap? \mk{5+5+7+5}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{1. Dijkstra's Algorithm}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Dijkstra's Single-Source Shortest Path}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Graph $G=(V, E)$ with non-negative edge weights $w$, source $s$
\Ensure Array $d$ where $d[v]$ is the shortest distance from $s$ to $v$
\State \textbf{Function} $\text{Dijkstra}(G, w, s)$:
\State \quad Initialize $d[v] \gets \infty$ for all $v \in V$
\State \quad $d[s] \gets 0$
\State \quad $S \gets \emptyset$ \Comment{Set of explored vertices}
\State \quad Initialize Min-Priority Queue $Q \gets V$ keyed by $d$ values
\State \quad \textbf{While} $Q$ is not empty:
\State \quad \quad $u \gets Q.\text{Extract-Min}()$
\State \quad \quad $S \gets S \cup \{u\}$
\State \quad \quad \textbf{For} each vertex $v \in G.\text{Adj}[u]$: \Comment{Relaxation step}
\State \quad \quad \quad \textbf{If} $v \in Q$ \textbf{and} $d[u] + w(u, v) < d[v]$:
\State \quad \quad \quad \quad $d[v] \gets d[u] + w(u, v)$
\State \quad \quad \quad \quad $Q.\text{Decrease-Key}(v, d[v])$
\State \quad \textbf{Return} $d$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{2. Proof of Correctness}

We prove correctness by contradiction, showing that whenever a vertex $u$ is extracted from $Q$ and added to the set $S$, its distance estimate $d[u]$ is exactly the true shortest path distance $\delta(s, u)$.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Hypothesis:} For all $v \in S$, $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$.
    \item \textbf{Base Case:} Initially, $S = \emptyset$. When $s$ is extracted, $d[s] = 0 = \delta(s, s)$, which is trivially correct.
    \item \textbf{Inductive Step:} Suppose there exists a first vertex $u$ such that $d[u] > \delta(s, u)$ at the moment it is extracted from $Q$ and added to $S$.
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Let $P$ be the true shortest path from $s$ to $u$.
        \item Since $s \in S$ and $u \notin S$ just before $u$ is extracted, path $P$ must cross the boundary from $S$ to $V-S$. Let $(x, y)$ be the first edge on $P$ where $x \in S$ and $y \notin S$.
        \item Because $x \in S$, by our hypothesis, $d[x] = \delta(s, x)$. 
        \item When $x$ was added to $S$, its outgoing edges were relaxed. Therefore, $d[y] \le d[x] + w(x, y) = \delta(s, y)$.
        \item Because edge weights are strictly non-negative ($w \ge 0$), the shortest path distance cannot decrease as we move further along $P$. Thus, $\delta(s, y) \le \delta(s, u)$.
        \item Combining steps 4 and 5: $d[y] \le \delta(s, u)$.
        \item However, the algorithm chose to extract $u$ from $Q$ instead of $y$, meaning $d[u] \le d[y]$.
        \item Therefore, $d[u] \le d[y] \le \delta(s, u) \implies d[u] \le \delta(s, u)$.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Contradiction:} We assumed $d[u] > \delta(s, u)$, but logically derived $d[u] \le \delta(s, u)$. Thus, $d[u]$ must equal $\delta(s, u)$ when $u$ is added to $S$. The algorithm is correct.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{3. Time Complexity Analysis (Binary Heap)}

Assuming the Min-Priority Queue $Q$ is implemented using a standard \textbf{Binary Min-Heap}:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Initialization:} Setting initial distances and populating the heap takes $O(|V|)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Extract-Min Operations:} The \texttt{while} loop executes exactly $|V|$ times. Each \texttt{Extract-Min} operation on a binary heap takes $O(\log |V|)$ time. Total time for extractions: $\mathbf{O(|V| \log |V|)}$.
    \item \textbf{Relaxation (Decrease-Key):} The inner \texttt{for} loop traverses the adjacency list of every vertex exactly once, meaning it runs $|E|$ times overall. If a distance is updated, \texttt{Decrease-Key} takes $O(\log |V|)$ time. Total maximum time for all updates: $\mathbf{O(|E| \log |V|)}$.
\end{itemize}
Summing these up, the overall time complexity is $O(|V| \log |V| + |E| \log |V|) = \mathbf{O((|V| + |E|) \log |V|)}$.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{4. Time Complexity using a Fibonacci Heap}

If a \textbf{Fibonacci Heap} is used for the priority queue, the time complexities of the fundamental operations change:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \texttt{Extract-Min} remains $\mathbf{O(\log |V|)}$ amortized time. Across $|V|$ operations, this takes $O(|V| \log |V|)$.
    \item \texttt{Decrease-Key} drops significantly to $\mathbf{O(1)}$ amortized time. Across up to $|E|$ edge relaxations, this takes just $O(|E|)$ time.
\end{itemize}
Therefore, replacing the binary heap with a Fibonacci heap improves the overall time complexity to strictly \textbf{$\mathbf{O(|V| \log |V| + |E|)}$}. This makes a massive theoretical difference for dense graphs where $|E|$ is much larger than $|V|$.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write the properties satisfied by shortest paths of a graph. Show by example that Dijkstra's algorithm gives wrong results for a graph with negative-weight edges. Write an algorithm that finds shortest paths in a DAG and analyze its time complexity. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2016--17}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Properties of Shortest Paths}
Let $G = (V, E)$ be a weighted directed graph with weight function $w : E \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $s \in V$ be the source vertex. The shortest path weight from $s$ to $v$ is denoted as $\delta(s, v)$, and the algorithm's current estimate is $d[v]$. The shortest paths satisfy the following fundamental properties:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Optimal Substructure Property:} If $p = \langle v_0, v_1, \dots, v_k \rangle$ is a shortest path from $v_0$ to $v_k$, then for any $0 \le i \le j \le k$, the subpath $p_{ij} = \langle v_i, v_i+1, \dots, v_j \rangle$ is a shortest path from $v_i$ to $v_j$.
    \item \textbf{Triangle Inequality:} For any edge $(u, v) \in E$, we have $\delta(s, v) \le \delta(s, u) + w(u, v)$.
    \item \textbf{Upper-Bound Property:} We always have $d[v] \ge \delta(s, v)$ for all vertices $v \in V$. Once $d[v]$ achieves the value $\delta(s, v)$, it never changes.
    \item \textbf{No-Path Property:} If there is no path from $s$ to $v$, then $d[v] = \delta(s, v) = \infty$ always holds.
    \item \textbf{Convergence Property:} If $s \leadsto u \to v$ is a shortest path, and if $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$ at any time prior to relaxing edge $(u, v)$, then $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$ at all times afterward.
    \item \textbf{Path-Relaxation Property:} If $p = \langle v_0, v_1, \dots, v_k \rangle$ is a shortest path from $s = v_0$ to $v_k$, and we relax the edges of $p$ in the order $(v_0, v_1), (v_1, v_2), \dots, (v_{k-1}, v_k)$, then $d[v_k] = \delta(s, v_k)$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5cm}
\subsection*{2. Dijkstra's Algorithm Failure with Negative-Weight Edges}
