Dijkstra's algorithm is a greedy algorithm that assumes once a vertex $u$ is extracted from the priority queue (i.e., added to the set of finalized vertices $S$), its shortest path distance $d[u]$ is strictly optimal ($\delta(s, u)$). Negative weight edges break this greedy choice property.

Consider the following graph with source vertex $S$:

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick]
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, fill=white] (S) at (0, 0) {S};
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, fill=white] (A) at (3, 1.5) {A};
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, fill=white] (B) at (3, -1.5) {B};

    \draw[->] (S) -- node[above left] {3} (A);
    \draw[->] (S) -- node[below left] {4} (B);
    \draw[->] (B) -- node[right] {-2} (A);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\noindent
\textbf{Execution Trace of Dijkstra's Algorithm:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Initialization:} $d[S] = 0, d[A] = \infty, d[B] = \infty$. Priority Queue $Q = \{(S, 0), (A, \infty), (B, \infty)\}$.
    \item \textbf{Extract Min (S):} $S$ is permanently finalized. Relax outgoing edges $(S, A)$ and $(S, B)$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $d[A] = \min(\infty, 0 + 3) = 3$.
        \item $d[B] = \min(\infty, 0 + 4) = 4$.
    \end{itemize}
    $Q$ is now $\{(A, 3), (B, 4)\}$.
    \item \textbf{Extract Min (A):} $A$ is permanently finalized with distance $d[A] = 3$. There are no outgoing edges from $A$.
    \item \textbf{Extract Min (B):} $B$ is permanently finalized with distance $d[B] = 4$. Relax edge $(B, A)$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Attempt to update $A$: $d[A] = \min(3, 4 - 2) = 2$.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}
Since standard Dijkstra permanently finalizes a vertex upon extraction, the final distance to $A$ reported by the algorithm is $3$. However, the true shortest path is $S \to B \to A$ with a total cost of $4 + (-2) = 2$. The greedy assumption failed.

\vspace{0.5cm}
\subsection*{3. Shortest Paths in a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)}
For a Directed Acyclic Graph, we can compute the single-source shortest paths in linear time by processing the vertices in topologically sorted order. Since all edges point from left to right in a topological sort, processing vertices in this order guarantees that by the time we process vertex $v$, all possible paths to $v$ have already been explored.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{DAG-Shortest-Paths$(G, w, s)$}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
    \State \textbf{Input:} A DAG $G = (V, E)$, weight function $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$, source vertex $s \in V$
    \State \textbf{Output:} Array $d$ containing shortest path weights, and array $\pi$ for predecessor tree
    \State
    \State \textbf{Phase 1: Topological Sort}
    \State Let $L$ be an empty list
    \State Perform Depth-First Search (DFS) on $G$ to compute finishing times
    \State As each vertex finishes, prepend it to $L$
    \State
    \State \textbf{Phase 2: Initialization}
    \For{each vertex $v \in V$}
        \State $d[v] \gets \infty$
        \State $\pi[v] \gets \text{NIL}$
    \EndFor
    \State $d[s] \gets 0$
    \State
    \State \textbf{Phase 3: Relaxation}
    \For{each vertex $u$ in topological order $L$}
        \If{$d[u] \ne \infty$}
            \For{each vertex $v \in \text{Adj}[u]$}
                \If{$d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v)$}
                    \State $d[v] \gets d[u] + w(u, v)$ \Comment{Relax the edge $(u, v)$}
                    \State $\pi[v] \gets u$
                \EndIf
            \EndFor
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
    \State \Return $d, \pi$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\noindent
\textbf{Complexity Analysis:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Topological sorting (using DFS) takes $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$ time.
        \item Initializing the distance array $d$ and predecessor array $\pi$ takes $\mathcal{O}(V)$ time.
        \item The outer relaxation loop runs exactly once per vertex ($\mathcal{O}(V)$). The inner loop iterates over all outgoing edges of $u$. Across the entire execution, each edge is relaxed exactly once. Thus, the relaxation phase takes $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$ time.
        \item \textbf{Total Time:} $\mathcal{O}(V + E) + \mathcal{O}(V) + \mathcal{O}(V + E) = \mathcal{O}(V + E)$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Adjacency list representation of the graph takes $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$ space.
        \item Arrays $d$ and $\pi$, and the list $L$ for topological ordering take $\mathcal{O}(V)$ space.
        \item Recursion stack for DFS takes up to $\mathcal{O}(V)$ space.
        \item \textbf{Total Space:} $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Prove that the Bellman-Ford algorithm returns TRUE if the input graph contains no negative-weight cycles reachable from the source, and FALSE otherwise. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2016--17}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{Theorem: Correctness of the Bellman-Ford Algorithm's Boolean Return Value}
Let $G = (V, E)$ be a weighted directed graph with source $s \in V$ and weight function $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$. The Bellman-Ford algorithm relaxes all edges $|V| - 1$ times and then performs a final verification pass over all edges. The algorithm returns \texttt{FALSE} if there exists an edge $(u, v) \in E$ such that $d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v)$, and \texttt{TRUE} otherwise. 

We must prove two claims:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Claim 1:} If $G$ contains no negative-weight cycles reachable from $s$, the algorithm returns \texttt{TRUE}.
    \item \textbf{Claim 2:} If $G$ contains a negative-weight cycle reachable from $s$, the algorithm returns \texttt{FALSE}.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\subsubsection*{Proof of Claim 1: No negative-weight cycle reachable from $s \implies$ Returns \texttt{TRUE}}
Assume that graph $G$ contains no negative-weight cycles reachable from the source vertex $s$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item By the \textbf{Path-Relaxation Property}, after relaxing all edges $|V| - 1$ times, the shortest-path estimate $d[v]$ correctly computes the true shortest-path distance $\delta(s, v)$ for all vertices $v$ reachable from $s$.
    \item For any vertex $v$ that is not reachable from $s$, $d[v] = \delta(s, v) = \infty$.
    \item Because there are no negative-weight cycles, the shortest paths are well-defined, and the true distances satisfy the \textbf{Triangle Inequality}. That is, for every edge $(u, v) \in E$:
    $$ \delta(s, v) \le \delta(s, u) + w(u, v) $$
    \item Substituting the computed estimates $d$ for $\delta$, we get:
    $$ d[v] \le d[u] + w(u, v) \quad \text{for all } (u, v) \in E $$
    \item Since this inequality holds for every edge, the condition $d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v)$ will evaluate to false during the final verification pass. Thus, the algorithm correctly returns \texttt{TRUE}.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\subsubsection*{Proof of Claim 2: A negative-weight cycle reachable from $s \implies$ Returns \texttt{FALSE}}
Assume that graph $G$ contains a negative-weight cycle $c = \langle v_0, v_1, \dots, v_k \rangle$ reachable from $s$, where $v_0 = v_k$. 
By the definition of a negative-weight cycle, the sum of the edge weights along the cycle is strictly less than zero:
\begin{equation}
    \sum_{i=1}^{k} w(v_{i-1}, v_i) < 0
\end{equation}

We will prove this claim using a \textbf{Proof by Contradiction}. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item Assume, for the sake of contradiction, that the algorithm returns \texttt{TRUE}. This implies that for all edges in $E$, and specifically for all edges in the cycle $c$, the following inequality holds:
    $$ d[v_i] \le d[v_{i-1}] + w(v_{i-1}, v_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, k $$
    \item Summing these inequalities around the cycle yields:
    \begin{equation}
        \sum_{i=1}^{k} d[v_i] \le \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left( d[v_{i-1}] + w(v_{i-1}, v_i) \right)
    \end{equation}
    \item Because the cycle $c$ is reachable from $s$, every vertex $v_i$ in the cycle has a finite distance estimate $d[v_i] < \infty$ after $|V|-1$ iterations.
    \item Since $v_0 = v_k$, the sum of the distance estimates $\sum_{i=1}^{k} d[v_i]$ is exactly the same as $\sum_{i=1}^{k} d[v_{i-1}]$.
    \item Because these sums are finite, we can algebraically subtract $\sum_{i=1}^{k} d[v_i]$ from both sides of the inequality, leaving:
    \begin{equation}
        0 \le \sum_{i=1}^{k} w(v_{i-1}, v_i)
    \end{equation}
\end{itemize}

\noindent
\textbf{Contradiction:} Inequality (3) states that the sum of the edge weights in cycle $c$ is non-negative, which directly contradicts our initial premise in Equation (1) that $c$ is a negative-weight cycle. 

\vspace{0.2cm}
\noindent
Therefore, our assumption that the algorithm returns \texttt{TRUE} must be false. There must be at least one edge $(u, v)$ in the cycle $c$ for which $d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v)$. Consequently, the algorithm correctly detects this and returns \texttt{FALSE}. 

\hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item State and prove the convergence property of edge relaxation in the context of shortest paths. \mk{8}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{Convergence Property of Edge Relaxation}

\textbf{Statement:} \\
Let $G = (V, E)$ be a weighted directed graph with source vertex $s \in V$ and weight function $w : E \to \mathbb{R}$. Let $s \leadsto u \to v$ be a shortest path in $G$ for some vertices $u, v \in V$. 
If $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$ at any time prior to relaxing the edge $(u, v)$, then $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$ at all times after the relaxation.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick]
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, fill=white] (s) at (0, 0) {$s$};
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, fill=white] (u) at (4, 0) {$u$};
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, fill=white] (v) at (7, 0) {$v$};

    \draw[->, decorate, decoration={snake, amplitude=1.5pt, segment length=5pt}] (s) -- node[above=2pt] {shortest path} node[below=2pt] {$\delta(s, u)$} (u);
    \draw[->] (u) -- node[above] {$w(u, v)$} (v);
    
    \node at (3.5, -1) {Prior to relaxation: $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.3cm}

\textbf{Proof:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Optimal Substructure:} Because $s \leadsto u \to v$ is a shortest path from $s$ to $v$, it must be that the subpath $s \leadsto u$ is a shortest path from $s$ to $u$. Therefore, the shortest path weight to $v$ can be expressed as:
    \begin{equation}
        \delta(s, v) = \delta(s, u) + w(u, v)
    \end{equation}

    \item \textbf{Pre-condition:} By the hypothesis of the property, at some point before the edge $(u, v)$ is relaxed, we have achieved the true shortest path distance for $u$:
    \begin{equation}
        d[u] = \delta(s, u)
    \end{equation}

    \item \textbf{Relaxation Step:} The operation of relaxing the edge $(u, v)$ performs the following update:
    \begin{equation}
        d[v] = \min(d[v], d[u] + w(u, v))
    \end{equation}

    \item \textbf{Substitution:} Substituting Equation (2) into the relaxation step yields:
    \begin{equation}
        d[v] = \min(d[v], \delta(s, u) + w(u, v))
    \end{equation}
    Using Equation (1), this simplifies directly to:
    \begin{equation}
        d[v] = \min(d[v], \delta(s, v))
    \end{equation}

    \item \textbf{Upper-Bound Property:} By the fundamental Upper-Bound Property of shortest paths, the current estimate $d[v]$ is always strictly bounded below by the true shortest path distance. That is, $d[v] \ge \delta(s, v)$ holds at all times during any valid shortest path algorithm.

    \item \textbf{Conclusion:} Because $d[v] \ge \delta(s, v)$, the minimum in Equation (5) will always evaluate to exactly $\delta(s, v)$. Therefore, immediately after the relaxation step, we have:
    $$ d[v] = \delta(s, v) $$
    Furthermore, because of the Upper-Bound Property, once $d[v]$ reaches the true lower bound $\delta(s, v)$, it can never be reduced further. Thus, $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$ remains true at all times thereafter.
\end{enumerate}

\hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Provide a correctness proof of Dijkstra's algorithm. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{Correctness Proof of Dijkstra's Algorithm}

Dijkstra's algorithm finds the single-source shortest paths on a directed or undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative edge weights ($w(u, v) \ge 0$ for all $(u, v) \in E$). 

Let $s$ be the source vertex. The algorithm maintains a set $S$ of vertices whose final shortest-path weights from the source have already been determined. At each step, it greedily selects a vertex $u \in V \setminus S$ with the minimum shortest-path estimate $d[u]$, adds $u$ to $S$, and relaxes all edges leaving $u$.

\subsubsection*{Theorem (Loop Invariant)}
At the start of each iteration of the \texttt{while} loop, $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$ for all vertices $v \in S$.

\subsubsection*{Proof by Mathematical Induction}
We prove the correctness of the loop invariant by induction on the size of the set $S$.

\noindent
\textbf{Initialization:} 
Initially, $S = \emptyset$. The invariant holds vacuously since there are no vertices in $S$. (Alternatively, after the very first step, $S = \{s\}$ and $d[s] = 0 = \delta(s, s)$, which is correct because edge weights are non-negative, so no path can have a total weight less than 0).

\noindent
\textbf{Maintenance:} 
Suppose the invariant holds for the current set $S$. Let $u$ be the next vertex chosen from $V \setminus S$ to be added to $S$, meaning $d[u] = \min_{z \in V \setminus S} \{d[z]\}$. To prove the invariant continues to hold, we must show that $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$.

We prove this by contradiction. Assume that $d[u] \neq \delta(s, u)$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item By the Upper-Bound Property of shortest paths, $d[u] \ge \delta(s, u)$ always. 
    \item Therefore, our assumption implies that $d[u] > \delta(s, u)$.
\end{itemize}

Consider a true shortest path $p$ from $s$ to $u$. Since $s \in S$ and $u \in V \setminus S$, there must be a first edge on path $p$ that connects a vertex in $S$ to a vertex in $V \setminus S$. Let this edge be $(x, y)$, such that $x \in S$ and $y \in V \setminus S$. Note that $x$ could potentially be $s$, and $y$ could potentially be $u$. 

The path $p$ can be decomposed as:
$$ s \xrightarrow{p_1} x \to y \xrightarrow{p_2} u $$

\vspace{0.3cm}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick, scale=0.9]
    % Set S
    \draw[fill=cA!5, draw=cA, dashed] (0,0) ellipse (2cm and 2.5cm);
    \node[color=cA] at (0, 2.1) {\textbf{Set $S$}};
    
    % Set V \ S
    \draw[fill=gray!5, draw=gray, dashed] (5,0) ellipse (2cm and 2.5cm);
    \node[color=gray] at (5, 2.1) {\textbf{Set $V \setminus S$}};
    
    % Vertices
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.7cm, fill=white] (s) at (-0.8, 0) {$s$};
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.7cm, fill=white] (x) at (1, -0.5) {$x$};
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.7cm, fill=white] (y) at (4, -0.5) {$y$};
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.7cm, fill=white] (u) at (5.8, 0) {$u$};
    
    % Paths and Edges
    \draw[->, decorate, decoration={snake, amplitude=1pt, segment length=4pt}] (s) -- (x);
    \draw[->, red, ultra thick] (x) -- node[above, color=black] {$(x,y)$} (y);
    \draw[->, decorate, decoration={snake, amplitude=1pt, segment length=4pt}] (y) -- (u);
    
    % Labels
    \node[below=3pt] at (x) {$d[x]=\delta(s,x)$};
    \node[below=3pt] at (y) {$d[y]=\delta(s,y)$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.3cm}

Let us analyze the relationships along this shortest path:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Since $x \in S$, by the inductive hypothesis, its distance estimate is already correct:
    \begin{equation}
        d[x] = \delta(s, x)
    \end{equation}
    
    \item When vertex $x$ was added to $S$, the edge $(x, y)$ was relaxed. By the \textbf{Convergence Property}, immediately after relaxing $(x, y)$, the distance estimate to $y$ became optimal:
    \begin{equation}
        d[y] = \delta(s, y)
    \end{equation}
    Since distance estimates are monotonically non-increasing, $d[y]$ remains equal to $\delta(s, y)$ at the current step.

    \item Because $y$ occurs before $u$ on the optimal shortest path $p$, and given that all edge weights are non-negative ($w(e) \ge 0$), the shortest-path distance to $y$ cannot exceed the shortest-path distance to $u$:
    \begin{equation}
        \delta(s, y) \le \delta(s, u)
    \end{equation}

    \item Combining our mathematical statements yields the following chain of inequalities:
    \begin{align*}
        d[y] &= \delta(s, y) && \text{(from Equation 2)} \\
             &\le \delta(s, u) && \text{(from Equation 3)} \\
             &< d[u] && \text{(by our initial contradiction assumption } \delta(s,u) < d[u]\text{)}
    \end{align*}
    Therefore, we conclude that:
    \begin{equation}
        d[y] < d[u]
    \end{equation}
\end{enumerate}

\noindent
\textbf{Contradiction:} \\
Equation (4) explicitly states that $d[y] < d[u]$. However, both $y$ and $u$ belong to $V \setminus S$, and the algorithm selected $u$ because it possessed the \textit{minimum} $d$ value in $V \setminus S$ ($d[u] \le d[z]$ for all $z \in V \setminus S$). 

This is a direct contradiction. Thus, our initial assumption must be false, meaning $d[u]$ cannot be strictly greater than $\delta(s, u)$. We conclude that $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$, which completes the inductive maintenance step.

\subsubsection*{Termination}
The algorithm terminates when $Q = V \setminus S = \emptyset$, meaning $S = V$. Since the loop invariant holds at the beginning of every iteration, it holds upon termination. Thus, for all vertices $v \in V$, $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$.

\hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Apply the Bellman-Ford algorithm to find the shortest paths. Show all relaxation steps. 

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small},
  every path/.style={->, line width=1pt},
  ]
\tikzset{>={Stealth[length=12pt, width=8pt, inset=2pt]}}

  \node (A) at (0, 2)    {A};
  \node (B) at (1, 0)    {B};
  \node (C) at (3.5, -1) {C};
  \node (D) at (3.5, 1)  {D};
  \node (E) at (3.5, 3)  {E};
  \node (F) at (6.5, 3)  {F};
  \node (G) at (6, 1)    {G};
  \node (H) at (6.5, -1) {H};

  \draw (A) -> node[edge label, above] {6} (E);
  \draw (A) -> node[edge label, left]  {8} (B);
  \draw (E) -> node[edge label, above] {3} (F);
  \draw (E) -> node[edge label, above] {2} (G);
  \draw (F) -> node[edge label, right] {6} (G);
  \draw (G) -> node[edge label, above] {1} (D);
  \draw (H) -> node[edge label, right] {-2}(G);
  \draw (G) -> node[edge label, right] {-1}(C);
  \draw (D) -> node[edge label, above] {2} (B);
  \draw (B) -> node[edge label, below] {6} (C);
  \draw (C) -> node[edge label, below] {4} (H);

  \end{tikzpicture}

\end{center}

\mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Initialization and Assumptions}
To apply the Bellman-Ford algorithm, we must designate a source vertex. In this graph, vertex \textbf{A} is the natural source as it has an in-degree of 0. We initialize the distance to the source as $d[A] = 0$, and all other vertices as $d[v] = \infty$. 

Let $V$ be the set of vertices and $E$ be the set of edges. The graph has $|V| = 8$ vertices, so the algorithm will perform at most $|V| - 1 = 7$ relaxation passes over all edges to find the shortest paths. For deterministic and traceable updates, we will relax the edges in lexicographical order:
$$ E = \{ (A, B), (A, E), (B, C), (C, H), (D, B), (E, F), (E, G), (F, G), (G, C), (G, D), (H, G) \} $$

\subsection*{2. Edge Relaxation Steps}
During each iteration $k$, we update $d[v] = \min(d[v], d[u] + w(u, v))$ sequentially for every edge $(u, v) \in E$.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Iteration ($k$)} & \textbf{A} & \textbf{B} & \textbf{C} & \textbf{D} & \textbf{E} & \textbf{F} & \textbf{G} & \textbf{H} \\
\hline
\textbf{0 (Init)} & 0 & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ \\
\hline
\textbf{1} & 0 & 8 & 7 & 9 & 6 & 9 & 8 & 18 \\
\hline
\textbf{2} & 0 & 8 & 7 & 9 & 6 & 9 & 8 & 11 \\
\hline
\textbf{3} & 0 & 8 & 7 & 9 & 6 & 9 & 8 & 11 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.3cm}

\textbf{Detailed Trace of Updates:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Iteration 1:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $(A, B, 8) \implies d[B] = \min(\infty, 0+8) = \mathbf{8}$
        \item $(A, E, 6) \implies d[E] = \min(\infty, 0+6) = \mathbf{6}$
        \item $(B, C, 6) \implies d[C] = \min(\infty, 8+6) = \mathbf{14}$
        \item $(C, H, 4) \implies d[H] = \min(\infty, 14+4) = \mathbf{18}$
        \item $(E, F, 3) \implies d[F] = \min(\infty, 6+3) = \mathbf{9}$
        \item $(E, G, 2) \implies d[G] = \min(\infty, 6+2) = \mathbf{8}$
        \item $(G, C, -1) \implies d[C] = \min(14, 8-1) = \mathbf{7}$ \textit{(Overwrites previous C value)}
        \item $(G, D, 1) \implies d[D] = \min(\infty, 8+1) = \mathbf{9}$
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Iteration 2:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $(C, H, 4) \implies d[H] = \min(18, 7+4) = \mathbf{11}$ \textit{(Updated because C changed in Pass 1)}
        \item All other edges fail to find a strictly shorter path.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Iteration 3:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item No distance values change during this pass. Because a full pass resulted in zero updates, the algorithm guarantees convergence and can safely \textbf{terminate early}.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

Since we completed a full pass without any changes, we also verified that the graph contains \textbf{no negative-weight cycles} reachable from A.

\subsection*{3. Final Shortest Path Tree}
The final shortest path distances from source $A$ are:
$$ d[A]=0, \quad d[B]=8, \quad d[C]=7, \quad d[D]=9, \quad d[E]=6, \quad d[F]=9, \quad d[G]=8, \quad d[H]=11 $$

The Shortest Path Tree (SPT) is visualized below. The active edges forming the tree are highlighted in solid blue, while the unused edges are grayed out.

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries, fill=white},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, inner sep=1pt},
  every path/.style={->, line width=1pt},
  ]
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta}
  \tikzset{>={Stealth[length=12pt, width=8pt, inset=2pt]}}

  % Nodes with distances
  \node (A) at (0, 2)    {A};
  \node (B) at (1, 0)    {B};
  \node (C) at (3.5, -1) {C};
  \node (D) at (3.5, 1)  {D};
  \node (E) at (3.5, 3)  {E};
  \node (F) at (6.5, 3)  {F};
  \node (G) at (6, 1)    {G};
  \node (H) at (6.5, -1) {H};

  % Distance labels
  \node[draw=none, fill=none, font=\small, color=cA] at (0, 2.7) {$d=0$};
  \node[draw=none, fill=none, font=\small, color=cA] at (0, 0) {$d=8$};
  \node[draw=none, fill=none, font=\small, color=cA] at (3.5, -1.7) {$d=7$};
  \node[draw=none, fill=none, font=\small, color=cA] at (3.5, 1.7) {$d=9$};
  \node[draw=none, fill=none, font=\small, color=cA] at (3.5, 3.7) {$d=6$};
  \node[draw=none, fill=none, font=\small, color=cA] at (6.5, 3.7) {$d=9$};
  \node[draw=none, fill=none, font=\small, color=cA] at (7, 1) {$d=8$};
  \node[draw=none, fill=none, font=\small, color=cA] at (7.2, -1) {$d=11$};

  % Non-SPT edges (grayed out)
  \draw[gray!30] (F) -> node[edge label, right, text=gray!50] {6} (G);
  \draw[gray!30] (H) -> node[edge label, right, text=gray!50] {-2}(G);
  \draw[gray!30] (D) -> node[edge label, above, pos=0.3, text=gray!50] {2} (B);
  \draw[gray!30] (B) -> node[edge label, below, text=gray!50] {6} (C);

  % SPT edges (highlighted)
  \draw[cA, line width=1.5pt] (A) -> node[edge label, above, text=black] {6} (E);
  \draw[cA, line width=1.5pt] (A) -> node[edge label, left, text=black]  {8} (B);
  \draw[cA, line width=1.5pt] (E) -> node[edge label, above, text=black] {3} (F);
  \draw[cA, line width=1.5pt] (E) -> node[edge label, above, text=black] {2} (G);
  \draw[cA, line width=1.5pt] (G) -> node[edge label, above, text=black] {1} (D);
  \draw[cA, line width=1.5pt] (G) -> node[edge label, right, text=black] {-1}(C);
  \draw[cA, line width=1.5pt] (C) -> node[edge label, below, text=black] {4} (H);

  \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsection*{4. Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V| \cdot |E|)$. In the worst case, the algorithm iterates over all $|E|$ edges $|V|-1$ times. Since $|V| = 8$ and $|E| = 11$, the maximum number of relaxations is $(8-1) \times 11 = 77$. Early termination optimizes this in practice, but the asymptotic bound remains $\mathcal{O}(|V||E|)$.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$. The algorithm requires space to maintain the distance array $d$ and predecessor array $\pi$, both of size $|V|$. Representing the graph requires $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ space.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Prove that the component graph of a directed graph is a DAG. \mk{6}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Formal Definitions}

Let $G = (V, E)$ be a directed graph (digraph). 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Strongly Connected Component (SCC):} A strongly connected component of $G$ is a maximal subset of vertices $V_i \subseteq V$ such that for every pair of vertices $u, v \in V_i$, there exists a directed path from $u$ to $v$ and a directed path from $v$ to $u$. In other words, mutual reachability is an equivalence relation that partitions $V$ into disjoint sets $V_1, V_2, \dots, V_k$.
    \item \textbf{Component Graph (Condensation Graph):} The component graph of $G$, denoted by $G^{\text{SCC}} = (V^{\text{SCC}}, E^{\text{SCC}})$, is a directed graph defined as follows:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $V^{\text{SCC}} = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$, where each $C_i$ corresponds to a strongly connected component of $G$.
        \item A directed edge $(C_i, C_j) \in E^{\text{SCC}}$ exists if and only if $i \neq j$ and there exists at least one directed edge $(u, v) \in E$ in the original graph such that $u \in C_i$ and $v \in C_j$.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{2. Visualization: Graph Condensation}
Below is an illustration of a directed graph $G$ and its corresponding component graph $G^{\text{SCC}}$, demonstrating how mutually reachable cycles collapse into a single macro-node, eliminating cyclical paths.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick, scale=0.85]
    % Original Graph G
    \begin{scope}[local bounding box=GBox]
        % Component 1 nodes
        \node[circle, draw, fill=cA!10, minimum size=0.6cm] (A) at (0, 2) {A};
        \node[circle, draw, fill=cA!10, minimum size=0.6cm] (B) at (1.5, 3) {B};
        \node[circle, draw, fill=cA!10, minimum size=0.6cm] (C) at (1.5, 1) {C};
        \draw[dashed, draw=cA!60] (-0.4,0.5) rectangle (2.0,3.5);
        \node[color=cA] at (0.8, 0.2) {$C_1$};
        
        % Component 2 nodes
        \node[circle, draw, fill=gray!10, minimum size=0.6cm] (D) at (4.5, 2) {D};
        \node[circle, draw, fill=gray!10, minimum size=0.6cm] (E) at (6, 2) {E};
        \draw[dashed, draw=gray!60] (4.0,1.2) rectangle (6.5,2.8);
        \node[color=gray] at (5.25, 0.9) {$C_2$};

        % Component 3 node
        \node[circle, draw, fill=blue!10, minimum size=0.6cm] (F) at (3, -1.5) {F};
        \draw[dashed, draw=blue!60] (2.4,-2.1) rectangle (3.6,-0.9);
        \node[color=blue] at (3, -2.4) {$C_3$};

        % Edges within C1
        \draw[->] (A) -- (B);
        \draw[->] (B) -- (C);
        \draw[->] (C) -- (A);
        
        % Edges within C2
        \draw[->] (D) [bend left] to (E);
        \draw[->] (E) [bend left] to (D);
        
        % Inter-component edges
        \draw[->, ultra thick, cA] (B) -- (D);
        \draw[->, ultra thick, cA] (C) -- (F);
        \draw[->, ultra thick, gray] (D) -- (F);
    \end{scope}
    
    \node[font=\bfseries\large] at (3, 4.2) {Original Graph $G$};

    % Arrow to Component Graph
    \draw[->, line width=2pt, gray!70] (7.2, 0.5) -- node[above, text=black] {Condense} (9.3, 0.5);

    % Component Graph G_SCC
    \begin{scope}[shift={(10,0)}]
        \node[circle, draw, fill=cA!10, minimum size=1.2cm, font=\bfseries] (C1) at (0, 1.5) {$C_1$};
        \node[circle, draw, fill=gray!10, minimum size=1.2cm, font=\bfseries] (C2) at (2.5, 1.5) {$C_2$};
        \node[circle, draw, fill=blue!10, minimum size=1.2cm, font=\bfseries] (C3) at (1.25, -1) {$C_3$};
        
        \draw[->, line width=1.2pt] (C1) -- (C2);
        \draw[->, line width=1.2pt] (C1) -- (C3);
        \draw[->, line width=1.2pt] (C2) -- (C3);
        
        \node[font=\bfseries\large] at (1.25, 4.2) {Component Graph $G^{\text{SCC}}$};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsection*{3. Theorem and Formal Proof}

\textbf{Theorem:} The component graph $G^{\text{SCC}} = (V^{\text{SCC}}, E^{\text{SCC}})$ of any directed graph $G$ is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG).

\vspace{0.2cm}
\textbf{Proof (By Contradiction):} \\
Assume, for the sake of contradiction, that the component graph $G^{\text{SCC}}$ is \textit{not} acyclic. This implies that $G^{\text{SCC}}$ contains at least one directed cycle.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Let this directed cycle in $G^{\text{SCC}}$ consists of $m$ distinct macro-nodes ($m \ge 2$, since by definition, $G^{\text{SCC}}$ has no self-loops):
    \begin{equation}
        C_1 \to C_2 \to C_3 \to \dots \to C_m \to C_1
    \end{equation}
    
    \item By the definition of edges in a component graph, the existence of the edge $C_i \to C_{i+1}$ means there exists a directed path in the original graph $G$ from some vertex in $C_i$ to some vertex in $C_{i+1}$. Let us denote this path in $G$ as $P(C_i, C_{i+1})$.
    
    \item Similarly, the final back-edge $C_m \to C_1$ in the cycle implies there exists a directed path in $G$ from some vertex in $C_m$ to some vertex in $C_1$. Let us denote this path as $P(C_m, C_1)$.
    
    \item Within any single strongly connected component $C_k$, \textit{all} internal vertices are mutually reachable by definition. Therefore, we can stitch these internal paths together with the inter-component paths $P(C_i, C_{i+1})$ to construct a valid path between any two vertices belonging to the union of these components.
    
    \item Specifically, let $u$ be any arbitrary vertex in $C_1$ and $v$ be any arbitrary vertex in $C_2$:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item We can travel from $u$ to the exit vertex of $C_1$, follow $P(C_1, C_2)$ to enter $C_2$, and then reach $v$. Thus, there is a path $u \leadsto v$ in $G$.
        \item Conversely, to go from $v$ to $u$, we can travel through $C_2 \to C_3 \to \dots \to C_m \to C_1$ using the path segments guaranteed by the cycle. Thus, there is a path $v \leadsto u$ in $G$.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item Because $u \leadsto v$ and $v \leadsto u$ both exist in $G$, the vertices $u$ and $v$ are mutually reachable in the original graph. By definition, they must belong to the \textbf{same} strongly connected component.
    
    \item This logic applies uniformly to all vertices across all components $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_m$ in the cycle. Therefore, all these vertices belong to a single, larger strongly connected component.
\end{enumerate}

\noindent
\textbf{Contradiction:} \\
This conclusion directly contradicts the maximality property of strongly connected components, which states that $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_m$ are distinct, disjoint maximal subsets partitioned by the equivalence relation. 

Since our assumption that a cycle exists leads to a direct mathematical contradiction, $G^{\text{SCC}}$ cannot contain any directed cycles. Thus, the component graph $G^{\text{SCC}}$ must be a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG).

\hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Assume that a breadth-first tree has already been computed with BFS on a graph $G$ with source vertex $s$. Write an algorithm to print the vertices on a shortest path from $s$ to $v$. \mk{8}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Algorithmic Strategy}
When a Breadth-First Search (BFS) is executed on a graph $G$ from a source vertex $s$, it computes a shortest-path distance $d[v]$ and a predecessor pointer $\pi[v]$ for each vertex $v \in V$. The predecessor pointers implicitly form a \textbf{Breadth-First Tree} rooted at $s$. 

To print the vertices along the shortest path from $s$ to $v$, we can follow these predecessor pointers backward from $v$ to $s$. Since this gives the path in reverse order ($v \leadsto s$), we can use a recursive approach to leverage the call stack, printing the vertices only after the recursive call returns. This naturally reverses the traversal order, ensuring the path prints correctly from $s$ to $v$.

\subsection*{2. Algorithm Specification}
Below is the standard, elegant recursive algorithm to print the shortest path.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Print-BFS-Path$(G, s, v)$}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Graph $G$, source vertex $s$, target vertex $v$, and a precomputed predecessor array $\pi$
\State \textbf{Output:} Prints the vertices on the shortest path from $s$ to $v$
\State
\If{$v = s$}
    \State \textbf{print} $s$ \Comment{Base Case: Source vertex reached}
\Else
    \If{$\pi[v] = \text{NIL}$}
        \State \textbf{print} ``No path from '' $s$ `` to '' $v$ `` exists.'' \Comment{Target is unreachable}
    \Else
        \State \Call{Print-BFS-Path}{$G, s, \pi[v]$} \Comment{Recurse toward the source}
        \State \textbf{print} $v$ \Comment{Print vertex during unwind phase}
    \EndIf
\EndIf
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsection*{3. Visual Trace via Tree Diagram}
Consider a precomputed Breadth-First Tree where we wish to print the path from source $\mathbf{s}$ to target $\mathbf{v}$. The algorithm moves up the tree via $\pi$ pointers until it hits $s$, then prints while unwinding.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level distance=1.5cm,
  level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm},
  level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
  path_edge/.style={->, ultra thick, cA},
  normal_edge/.style={->, thick, gray!40}]

  % Tree structure
  \node (S) at (0,0) {s}
    child {node (A) {A} edge from parent[normal_edge]}
    child {node (B) {B}
      child {node (C) {C} edge from parent[normal_edge]}
      child {node (V) [fill=cA!10] {v} edge from parent[path_edge]}
      edge from parent[path_edge]
    };

  % Labels and annotations
  \node[draw=none, fill=none, right=10pt, font=\small] at (S) {1. Base case reached $\to$ Print $s$};
  \node[draw=none, fill=none, right=10pt, font=\small] at (B) {2. Unwind step $\to$ Print $B$};
  \node[draw=none, fill=none, right=10pt, font=\small] at (V) {3. Final step $\to$ Print $v$};
  
  % Step execution arrows (going up)
  \draw[->, bend left, dashed, thick, gray] (V) to node[draw=none, right, font=\scriptsize] {$\pi[v]$} (B);
  \draw[->, bend left, dashed, thick, gray] (B) to node[draw=none, right, font=\scriptsize] {$\pi[B]$} (S);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\noindent
\textbf{Output Sequence for the example above:} $s \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow v$

\subsection*{4. Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(V)$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item In the worst-case scenario, the shortest path could be a simple linear chain containing all vertices in the graph (i.e., a path length of $|V| - 1$).
        \item Each recursive step performs a constant number of operations $\mathcal{O}(1)$ before making exactly one recursive call. 
        \item Therefore, the total time spent is directly proportional to the number of vertices on the path, yielding a tight upper bound of $\mathcal{O}(V)$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(V)$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item The algorithm does not allocate additional dynamic structural memory, running in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ auxiliary space.
        \item However, because it is implemented recursively, it uses the system call stack. In the worst case (a single linear path of length $|V|-1$), the maximum depth of the recursion tree is $|V|$, requiring $\mathcal{O}(V)$ stack frame space.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item State and prove the convergence property of shortest paths. \mk{12}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{Convergence Property of Shortest Paths}

\textbf{Formal Statement:}\\
Let $G = (V, E)$ be a weighted directed graph with weight function $w : E \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $s \in V$ be the source vertex. Let $s \leadsto u \to v$ be a shortest path in $G$ for some vertices $u, v \in V$. 

If the shortest-path estimate for $u$ has converged, meaning $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$, at any time prior to relaxing the edge $(u, v)$, then the shortest-path estimate for $v$ will converge to its true optimum, meaning $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$, at all times after the relaxation of $(u, v)$.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick]
    % Nodes
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, fill=white] (s) at (0, 0) {$s$};
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, fill=white] (u) at (4, 0) {$u$};
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, fill=white] (v) at (8, 0) {$v$};

    % Path s to u
    \draw[->, decorate, decoration={snake, amplitude=1.2pt, segment length=6pt}, cA] (s) -- node[above=2pt, text=black] {Shortest path $p'$} node[below=2pt, text=black] {Weight: $\delta(s, u)$} (u);
    
    % Edge u to v
    \draw[->, ultra thick, cA] (u) -- node[above, text=black] {$w(u, v)$} (v);
    
    % Annotations
    \node[align=center, text=black, font=\small, draw, fill=gray!10, rounded corners] at (4, -1.5) {\textbf{Precondition}\\ $d[u] = \delta(s, u)$};
    \node[align=center, text=black, font=\small, draw, fill=cA!10, rounded corners] at (8, -1.5) {\textbf{Post-Relaxation}\\ $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.3cm}

\textbf{Formal Proof:}

To prove this property rigorously, we rely on two fundamental axioms of shortest paths:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Optimal Substructure Lemma:} Subpaths of shortest paths are shortest paths.
    \item \textbf{Upper-Bound Property:} For all $v \in V$, $d[v] \ge \delta(s, v)$ always. Once $d[v]$ achieves $\delta(s, v)$, it never changes.
\end{enumerate}

\noindent
\textbf{Step-by-Step Derivation:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Optimal Substructure Application:} \\
    By the hypothesis, the path $s \leadsto u \to v$ is a true shortest path from $s$ to $v$. By the Optimal Substructure Lemma, the subpath $s \leadsto u$ must also be a true shortest path from $s$ to $u$. Therefore, the total shortest-path distance to $v$ can be exactly decomposed as:
    \begin{equation}
        \delta(s, v) = \delta(s, u) + w(u, v)
    \end{equation}

    \item \textbf{The Precondition:} \\
    The property assumes that at some moment before edge $(u, v)$ is relaxed, the algorithm has successfully found the optimal distance to $u$. Thus:
    \begin{equation}
        d[u] = \delta(s, u)
    \end{equation}

    \item \textbf{The Relaxation Operation:} \\
    The definition of relaxing the directed edge $(u, v)$ is executing the following assignment:
    \begin{equation}
        d[v] \gets \min(d[v], d[u] + w(u, v))
    \end{equation}

    \item \textbf{Substitution:} \\
    Substitute the exact value of $d[u]$ from Equation (2) into the relaxation step (Equation 3):
    \begin{equation}
        d[v] \gets \min(d[v], \delta(s, u) + w(u, v))
    \end{equation}
    Now, apply the Optimal Substructure decomposition from Equation (1) into Equation (4):
    \begin{equation}
        d[v] \gets \min(d[v], \delta(s, v))
    \end{equation}

    \item \textbf{Applying the Upper-Bound Property:} \\
    The Upper-Bound Property guarantees that the algorithm's estimate $d[v]$ never falls below the true shortest-path distance $\delta(s, v)$. That means at the moment of relaxation, $d[v]$ is either strictly greater than $\delta(s, v)$ or exactly equal to it:
    $$ d[v] \ge \delta(s, v) $$
    Because $d[v] \ge \delta(s, v)$, the minimum evaluated in Equation (5) will trivially resolve to $\delta(s, v)$. 
    
    \item \textbf{Conclusion:} \\
    Therefore, immediately after executing the relaxation of edge $(u, v)$, we have:
    $$ d[v] = \delta(s, v) $$
    Furthermore, the second clause of the Upper-Bound Property dictates that once $d[v]$ reaches the absolute lower bound $\delta(s, v)$, no future edge relaxations can possibly reduce it further. Thus, the condition $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$ will persist at all times afterward, completing the proof.
\end{enumerate}

\hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write the Bellman-Ford algorithm. Analyze the time complexity and correctness of the Bellman-Ford algorithm. \mk{20}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. The Bellman-Ford Algorithm}

The Bellman-Ford algorithm solves the single-source shortest-paths problem in the general case where edge weights may be negative. Unlike Dijkstra's algorithm, it can handle graphs containing negative-weight edges, provided there are no negative-weight cycles reachable from the source. It returns a boolean value indicating whether a negative-weight cycle was detected.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Bellman-Ford$(G, w, s)$}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
    \State \textbf{Input:} A weighted directed graph $G = (V, E)$, weight function $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$, source vertex $s \in V$
    \State \textbf{Output:} Boolean (\texttt{TRUE} if no negative cycles, \texttt{FALSE} otherwise), updates $d$ and $\pi$ arrays
    \State
    \State \textbf{Step 1: Initialize Single Source}
    \For{each vertex $v \in V$}
        \State $d[v] \gets \infty$
        \State $\pi[v] \gets \text{NIL}$
    \EndFor
    \State $d[s] \gets 0$
    \State
    \State \textbf{Step 2: Relax Edges Repeatedly}
    \For{$i \gets 1$ \textbf{to} $|V| - 1$}
        \For{each edge $(u, v) \in E$}
            \If{$d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v)$}
                \State $d[v] \gets d[u] + w(u, v)$
                \State $\pi[v] \gets u$
            \EndIf
        \EndFor
    \EndFor
    \State
    \State \textbf{Step 3: Check for Negative-Weight Cycles}
    \For{each edge $(u, v) \in E$}
        \If{$d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v)$}
            \State \Return \texttt{FALSE} \Comment{A negative-weight cycle is reachable}
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
    \State \Return \texttt{TRUE} \Comment{Shortest paths are correctly computed}
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsection*{2. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(V \cdot E)$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Initialization (Step 1):} Takes $\mathcal{O}(V)$ time to set up the initial distance estimates and predecessors.
        \item \textbf{Relaxation Loop (Step 2):} The outer loop runs exactly $|V| - 1$ times. The inner loop iterates over all edges $|E|$ in the graph. Thus, the relaxation phase dominates the algorithm, taking $(|V| - 1) \times \mathcal{O}(E) = \mathcal{O}(V \cdot E)$ time.
        \item \textbf{Negative Cycle Check (Step 3):} Iterates through all edges in the graph exactly once, which takes $\mathcal{O}(E)$ time.
        \item \textbf{Total Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(V) + \mathcal{O}(V \cdot E) + \mathcal{O}(E) = \mathcal{O}(V \cdot E)$. On a dense graph where $E = \mathcal{O}(V^2)$, this translates to a worst-case time complexity of $\mathcal{O}(V^3)$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(V)$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item The algorithm requires space to maintain two primary arrays of size $|V|$: the distance estimates array $d$ and the predecessor tracking array $\pi$. 
        \item Since the graph structure itself takes $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$ space using an adjacency list format, the auxiliary runtime storage overhead remains tightly bounded at $\mathcal{O}(V)$.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{3. Correctness Proof}

To prove the correctness of the Bellman-Ford algorithm, we must demonstrate that it achieves two things: correctly computing shortest-path weights when no negative-weight cycles exist, and identifying negative-weight cycles when they do.

\subsubsection*{Part 1: In the absence of negative-weight cycles, $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$ for all reachable $v$}

\textbf{Lemma (Path-Relaxation Property):} \\
Let $G=(V,E)$ be a weighted directed graph with source $s$. Suppose $G$ contains no negative-weight cycles reachable from $s$. Let $p = \langle v_0, v_1, \dots, v_k \rangle$ be a shortest path from $s = v_0$ to $v_k$. If we relax the edges of $p$ in the order $(v_0, v_1), (v_1, v_2), \dots, (v_{k-1}, v_k)$, then $d[v_k] = \delta(s, v_k)$ upon completion, and this equality holds regardless of any interleaved relaxation steps on other edges.

\noindent
\textbf{Proof by Mathematical Induction on Path Length:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Base Case:} Before the loop begins, Step 1 sets $d[v_0] = d[s] = 0 = \delta(s, s)$. By the upper-bound property, this value can never change.
    \item \textbf{Inductive Step:} Assume that after the $(i-1)$-th pass of the relaxation loop, the algorithm has achieved the optimal value for vertex $v_{i-1}$, meaning $d[v_{i-1}] = \delta(s, v_{i-1})$.
    \item During the $i$-th iteration of the outer loop, \textit{every} edge in the graph is relaxed, which includes the specific shortest-path edge $(v_{i-1}, v_i)$.
    \item By the \textbf{Convergence Property}, since $d[v_{i-1}] = \delta(s, v_{i-1})$ held prior to this relaxation pass, once the edge $(v_{i-1}, v_i)$ is processed, we are guaranteed that $d[v_i] = \delta(s, v_i)$.
    \item Since a simple path in a graph with no negative-weight cycles contains at most $|V|-1$ edges ($k \le |V|-1$), the $|V|-1$ passes performed in Step 2 are mathematically sufficient to ensure every edge along every simple shortest path is relaxed in its correct structural sequence.
\end{itemize}
Thus, after $|V|-1$ iterations, $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$ for all reachable vertices $v \in V$.

\subsubsection*{Part 2: Correctness of the Boolean Return Value}

We complete the proof by establishing that Step 3 acts as a perfect classifier for the presence of a reachable negative-weight cycle.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Case A (No Negative Cycles Exist):} \\
    If the graph contains no reachable negative cycles, Part 1 guarantees that $d[v] = \delta(s, v)$ for all reachable vertices upon entering Step 3. By the \textbf{Triangle Inequality}, the true shortest paths must obey $\delta(s, v) \le \delta(s, u) + w(u, v)$ for every edge $(u, v) \in E$. Substituting the optimal $d$ estimates, we obtain $d[v] \le d[u] + w(u, v)$. Therefore, the condition in Step 3 ($d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v)$) will evaluate to false for all edges, and the algorithm correctly returns \texttt{TRUE}.
    
    \item \textbf{Case B (A Negative Cycle is Reachable):} \\
    Suppose there exists a negative-weight cycle $c = \langle v_0, v_1, \dots, v_m \rangle$ where $v_0 = v_m$, reachable from $s$. Assume for contradiction that the algorithm returns \texttt{TRUE}. This implies that Step 3 found no edge violating the triangle inequality, meaning for all edges in the cycle:
    $$ d[v_j] \le d[v_{j-1}] + w(v_{j-1}, v_j) \quad \text{for } j=1, \dots, m $$
    Summing these inequalities around the cycle yields:
    $$ \sum_{j=1}^{m} d[v_j] \le \sum_{j=1}^{m} d[v_{j-1}] + \sum_{j=1}^{m} w(v_{j-1}, v_j) $$
    Because the cycle is reachable, all $d[v_j]$ values are finite. Since $v_0 = v_m$, the vertex sum on the left and right sides are identical ($\sum d[v_j] = \sum d[v_{j-1}]$). Subtracting this finite sum from both sides leaves:
    $$ 0 \le \sum_{j=1}^{m} w(v_{j-1}, v_j) $$
    This states that the cycle has a non-negative total weight, which directly contradicts our premise that $c$ is a negative-weight cycle. Thus, the condition in Step 3 must be triggered by at least one edge in the cycle, and the algorithm correctly returns \texttt{FALSE}.
\end{itemize}

\hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Does Dijkstra's algorithm terminate if it is applied on a graph $G$ that contains a negative-weight cycle? Why or why not? Justify your answer. \mk{6}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{Termination of Dijkstra's Algorithm on Negative-Weight Cycles}

\textbf{Direct Answer:} \\
Yes, the standard textbook implementation of Dijkstra's algorithm \textbf{is guaranteed to terminate} when applied to a graph containing a negative-weight cycle. However, while it terminates successfully, the computed shortest-path distances will be \textbf{incorrect} for any vertices reachable from or trapped within that negative cycle.

\subsubsection*{1. Why It Terminates (Control Flow Analysis)}
The termination of Dijkstra's algorithm is governed entirely by its structural control flow and data structures, completely independent of edge weight values:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Finite Queue Capacity:} Upon initialization, the algorithm inserts exactly $|V|$ vertices of the graph $G$ into the priority queue $Q$.
    \item \textbf{Strict Extraction Policy:} In every single iteration of the main \texttt{while} loop, the algorithm performs exactly one \texttt{Extract-Min} operation to remove the vertex $u$ with the smallest distance estimate $d[u]$ from $Q$. 
    \item \textbf{No Re-enqueuing:} Crucially, once a vertex is extracted from $Q$, it is considered "settled." The standard implementation of Dijkstra's algorithm \textbf{never re-inserts} a settled vertex back into the priority queue. Edge relaxation only modifies the keys (\texttt{Decrease-Key}) of vertices that are \textit{currently} still waiting inside $Q$.
\end{itemize}
Because the priority queue starts with exactly $|V|$ elements and loses exactly one element per iteration, the main loop will execute exactly $|V|$ times before $Q$ becomes empty, forcing the algorithm to terminate.

\subsubsection*{2. Why It Fails (Correctness Breakdown \& Counterexample)}
Dijkstra’s algorithm relies heavily on a \textbf{Greedy Choice Property}: it assumes that the moment a vertex $u$ is pulled from the priority queue, its current distance estimate $d[u]$ has successfully converged to the true shortest-path distance $\delta(s, u)$. When a negative-weight cycle is introduced, this assumption shatters.

Consider the following graph with source $s$ and a negative cycle between vertices $A$ and $B$:

\vspace{0.3cm}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick, scale=1.2]
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm] (s) at (0,0) {$s$};
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm] (A) at (2.5,0) {$A$};
    \node[circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm] (B) at (5,0) {$B$};

    \draw[->] (s) -- node[above] {2} (A);
    \draw[->] (A) [bend left] to node[above] {3} (B);
    \draw[->, red, line width=1.2pt] (B) [bend left] to node[below] {-5} (A);

    \node[draw=none, fill=none, color=cA, font=\small] at (0,-0.6) {$d[s]=0$};
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, color=cA, font=\small] at (2.5,-0.6) {$d[A]=0$ \textit{(Incorrect)}};
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, color=cA, font=\small] at (5,-0.6) {$d[B]=5$ \textit{(Incorrect)}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.3cm}

\textbf{Execution Trace:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Initialization:} $d[s]=0, d[A]=\infty, d[B]=\infty$. Queue $Q = \{s, A, B\}$.
    \item \textbf{Iteration 1:} Extract $s$ ($d[s]=0$). Relax edge $(s, A) \implies d[A] = \min(\infty, 0+2) = 2$. Remaining $Q = \{A, B\}$.
    \item \textbf{Iteration 2:} Extract $A$ ($d[A]=2$). Vertex $A$ is now permanently popped and settled. Relax edge $(A, B) \implies d[B] = \min(\infty, 2+3) = 5$. Remaining $Q = \{B\}$.
    \item \textbf{Iteration 3:} Extract $B$ ($d[B]=5$). Vertex $B$ is settled. Relax edge $(B, A) \implies$ the updated path weight to $A$ would be $d[B] + w(B, A) = 5 - 5 = 0$. Since $0 < d[A]$ (current $d[A]=2$), a shorter path to $A$ is discovered! However, because $A$ is no longer in $Q$, the algorithm cannot re-evaluate $A$ or propagate this improvement to downstream paths. Queue $Q = \emptyset$, loop terminates.
\end{enumerate}
The mathematical shortest-path distance to $A$ and $B$ is $-\infty$ due to the infinitely repeating negative cycle $\langle A, B, A \rangle$, but Dijkstra's algorithm exits prematurely with the incorrect finite metrics $d[A]=0$ and $d[B]=5$.

\subsubsection*{3. Summary of Behavior}
\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Algorithmic Metric} & \textbf{Behavior on Graphs with Negative Cycles} \\ \hline
\textbf{Termination} & \textbf{Guaranteed.} Stops cleanly after exactly $|V|$ loop iterations. \\ \hline
\textbf{Correctness} & \textbf{Fails.} Distances for vertices affected by the cycle will be incorrect. \\ \hline
\textbf{Infinite Loop Risk} & Only occurs in non-standard variants that explicitly re-enqueue settled vertices. \\ \hline
\textbf{Correct Alternative} & \textbf{Bellman-Ford Algorithm}, which safely catches and handles these cycles. \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What does $d[v]$ represent at the end of the execution of Algorithm 1, if the algorithm is applied on an unweighted directed acyclic graph (DAG) $G$ with a source $s$? What is the runtime complexity in terms of $|V|$ and $|E|$ of Algorithm 1?
\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{\textsc{LongestPath-DAG}($G$)}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State Compute a topological sorting of $G$
\For{each $v \in V[G]$}
    \State $d[v] \gets 0$
\EndFor
\For{each $u$ in topologically sorted order}
    \For{each $v \in Adj[u]$}
        \If{$d[v] < d[u] + 1$}
            \State $d[v] \gets d[u] + 1$
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

 \mk{6+6}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Representation of $d[v]$ at Execution End}

At the end of the execution of Algorithm 1, $d[v]$ represents the **length of the longest path (measured by the total number of edges) from the source vertex $s$ to the vertex $v$**.

\subsubsection*{Mathematical Justification:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Initialization:} The algorithm initially sets $d[v] = 0$ for all $v \in V$. Since $G$ is a DAG with a single source $s$ (meaning $s$ is the unique vertex with an in-degree of 0 and all other vertices are reachable from it), the absolute longest path to any vertex must originate at $s$.
    \item \textbf{Topological Order Invariant:} Processing the vertices in a topologically sorted order ensures a strict dependency guarantee: when the outer loop reaches a vertex $u$, all possible incoming paths from preceding vertices to $u$ have already been completely evaluated. Thus, $d[u]$ is guaranteed to have settled to its final maximum value.
    \item \textbf{Relaxation Step:} The conditional update:
    $$ d[v] = \max(d[v], d[u] + 1) $$
    correctly propagates and accumulates the maximum edge count along any valid directed sequence from $s$ to $v$.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{2. Runtime Complexity Analysis}

The total runtime complexity of the algorithm is \textbf{$\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$}, which is linear with respect to the size of the graph.

\subsubsection*{Step-by-Step Breakdown:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Topological Sorting (Line 1):} Computing a topological sort of a directed acyclic graph using either Kahn's algorithm (indegree-based) or a Depth-First Search (DFS) tracking finish times takes exactly \textbf{$\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$} time.
    
    \item \textbf{Initialization Loop (Lines 2--4):} This loop iterates exactly once for each vertex in the graph to initialize the distance estimates to zero, contributing \textbf{$\mathcal{O}(|V|)$} time.
    
    \item \textbf{Nested Relaxation Loops (Lines 5--11):}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item The outer loop (Line 5) visits every vertex $u \in V$ exactly once.
        \item The inner loop (Line 6) iterates over all outgoing edges in the adjacency list of the current vertex $u$, denoted as $\text{Adj}[u]$.
        \item Summing the work done across all vertices, the inner loop processes exactly the total number of edges in the graph:
        $$ \sum_{u \in V} \text{out-degree}(u) = |E| $$
        \item Since the comparison and assignment operations inside the conditional block (Lines 7--9) take constant time $\mathcal{O}(1)$, the total time spent in these nested loops is tightly bounded at \textbf{$\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$}.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Total Asymptotic Time Complexity:}
Combining all the sequential steps of the algorithm yields:
$$ \mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|) \text{ [Sorting]} + \mathcal{O}(|V|) \text{ [Init]} + \mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|) \text{ [Relaxation]} = \mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|) $$

\hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}
 
\item In the figure below, the shortest path estimate of each vertex appears within the vertex and the distance to traverse from a vertex to another appears as the weight of the corresponding directed edge. Show the updated shortest path estimates of vertices after relaxing the following edges.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=2cm, every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm}]
  \node (4)  {4};
  \node (7)  [right of=4] {7};
  \node (12) [below of=4] {12};
  \node (17) [right of=12] {17};
  \node (9)  [below of=12] {9};
  \node (11) [right of=9] {11};
  \draw[->] (4)  -- node[draw=none, above] {3} (7);
  \draw[->] (12) -- node[draw=none, above] {4} (17);
  \draw[->] (9)  -- node[draw=none, above] {5} (11);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\mk{6} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Edge Relaxation Principle}
Relaxing a directed edge $(u, v)$ with a given weight $w(u, v)$ checks whether a shorter path to vertex $v$ can be found by traveling through vertex $u$. The mathematical operation is defined as:
$$ d[v] \gets \min(d[v], d[u] + w(u, v)) $$
An update occurs only if the new path estimate is strictly less than the existing estimate ($d[u] + w(u, v) < d[v]$).

\subsection*{2. Step-by-Step Relaxation Trace}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Relaxing edge $(4) \to (7)$ with weight $3$:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Source estimate $d[u] = 4$, Target estimate $d[v] = 7$.
        \item New path candidate: $d[4] + w(4,7) = 4 + 3 = 7$.
        \item Since $7 \not< 7$, no update occurs. \textbf{Final value:} $d[7] = 7$.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Relaxing edge $(12) \to (17)$ with weight $4$:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Source estimate $d[u] = 12$, Target estimate $d[v] = 17$.
        \item New path candidate: $d[12] + w(12,17) = 12 + 4 = 16$.
        \item Since $16 < 17$, the shortest path estimate drops. \textbf{Final value:} $d[17] = 16$.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Relaxing edge $(9) \to (11)$ with weight $5$:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Source estimate $d[u] = 9$, Target estimate $d[v] = 11$.
        \item New path candidate: $d[9] + w(9,11) = 9 + 5 = 14$.
        \item Since $14 \not< 11$, no update occurs. \textbf{Final value:} $d[11] = 11$.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{3. Updated Graph State Diagram}
Below is the updated configuration of the graph. The successfully relaxed edge and its updated destination estimate node are highlighted.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=2.2cm, every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries}, >=stealth, thick]
  % Vertices
  \node (4)  {4};
  \node (7)  [right of=4] {7};
  \node (12) [below of=4] {12};
  \node (16) [right of=12, fill=cA!10, draw=cA, ultra thick] {16}; % Highlighted updated node
  \node (9)  [below of=12] {9};
  \node (11) [right of=9] {11};
  
  % Edges
  \draw[->] (4)  -- node[draw=none, above] {3} (7);
  \draw[->, cA, ultra thick] (12) -- node[draw=none, above, text=black] {4} (16); % Highlighted edge
  \draw[->] (9)  -- node[draw=none, above] {5} (11);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Explain how Dijkstra's algorithm can be implemented using a priority queue. Find the running time for this algorithm. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2011--12}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Implementing Dijkstra's Algorithm with a Priority Queue}

Dijkstra’s algorithm relies on a greedy approach: at every step, it extracts the unvisited vertex with the smallest tentative shortest-path distance. A \textbf{Priority Queue} ($Q$) is the ideal data structure to support this operation efficiently, storing vertices as elements and their current distance estimates ($d[v]$) as keys.

The algorithm maps directly to three primary priority queue operations:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Insert(Q, v, key)}}: Executed during initialization to populate the queue with all vertices $|V|$.
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Extract-Min(Q)}}: Executed at the beginning of each iteration of the main loop to extract the vertex $u$ with the absolute lowest $d[u]$ value.
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Decrease-Key(Q, v, new\_key)}}: Executed during edge relaxation when a shorter path to a neighbor $v$ is discovered, forcing its key to be lowered and its position re-balanced within the queue.
\end{itemize}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Dijkstra-PQ$(G, w, s)$}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
    \State \textbf{Input:} Graph $G = (V, E)$, edge weight function $w$, source vertex $s$
    \State
    \For{each vertex $v \in V$}
        \State $d[v] \gets \infty$
        \State $\pi[v] \gets \text{NIL}$
    \EndFor
    \State $d[s] \gets 0$
    \State
    \State Initialize an empty priority queue $Q$
    \For{each vertex $v \in V$}
        \State \Call{Insert}{$Q, v, d[v]$}
    \EndFor
    \State
    \While{$Q \text{ is not empty}$}
        \State $u \gets$ \Call{Extract-Min}{$Q$} \Comment{Greedily pick closest vertex}
        \For{each vertex $v \in \text{Adj}[u]$}
            \If{$d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v)$}
                \State $d[v] \gets d[u] + w(u, v)$
                \State $\pi[v] \gets u$
                \State \Call{Decrease-Key}{$Q, v, d[v]$} \Comment{Update priority in $Q$}
            \EndIf
        \EndFor
    \EndWhile
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsection*{2. Time Complexity Analysis}

The total execution time of Dijkstra's algorithm is bounded by the operations performed on the priority queue. Let $|V|$ be the number of vertices and $|E|$ be the number of edges. The control flow guarantees exactly:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{$|V|$} \texttt{Insert} operations to build the initial queue structure.
    \item \textbf{$|V|$} \texttt{Extract-Min} operations, since each vertex is extracted exactly once.
    \item At most \textbf{$|E|$} \texttt{Decrease-Key} operations, since every edge is relaxed at most once during the execution.
\end{itemize}

The running time varies significantly based on the structural choice of the priority queue:

\subsubsection*{Case A: Unsorted Array implementation}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \texttt{Extract-Min} takes $\mathcal{O}(V)$ via a linear scan. Total for all extractions: $|V| \cdot \mathcal{O}(V) = \mathcal{O}(V^2)$.
    \item \texttt{Decrease-Key} takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ via direct array index accessing. Total for all relaxations: $|E| \cdot \mathcal{O}(1) = \mathcal{O}(E)$.
    \item \textbf{Total Running Time:} $\mathcal{O}(V^2 + E) = \mathbf{\mathcal{O}(V^2)}$. This implementation is highly efficient for \textit{dense graphs} where $|E| \approx |V|^2$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Case B: Binary Min-Heap implementation}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \texttt{Extract-Min} takes $\mathcal{O}(\log V)$ to restore heap order after root eviction. Total for all extractions: $\mathcal{O}(V \log V)$.
    \item \texttt{Decrease-Key} takes $\mathcal{O}(\log V)$ to bubble up an element. Total for all relaxations: $\mathcal{O}(E \log V)$.
    \item \textbf{Total Running Time:} $\mathcal{O}(V \log V + E \log V) = \mathbf{\mathcal{O}((V + E) \log V)}$. This is the standard, practical implementation choice for general or \textit{sparse graphs} ($|E| \ll |V|^2$).
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Case C: Fibonacci Heap implementation}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \texttt{Extract-Min} takes $\mathcal{O}(\log V)$ amortized time. Total for all extractions: $\mathcal{O}(V \log V)$.
    \item \texttt{Decrease-Key} takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amortized time via structural pointer updates. Total for all relaxations: $\mathcal{O}(E)$.
    \item \textbf{Total Running Time:} $\mathbf{\mathcal{O}(V \log V + E)}$. This represents the theoretical optimal asymptotic upper bound for large graphs.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{Summary Comparison}
\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{PQ Implementation} & \textbf{\texttt{Extract-Min}} & \textbf{\texttt{Decrease-Key}} & \textbf{Total Dijkstra Runtime} \\ \hline
Unsorted Array & $\mathcal{O}(V)$ & $\mathcal{O}(1)$ & $\mathcal{O}(V^2)$ \\ \hline
Binary Heap & $\mathcal{O}(\log V)$ & $\mathcal{O}(\log V)$ & $\mathcal{O}((V + E) \log V)$ \\ \hline
Fibonacci Heap & $\mathcal{O}(\log V)$ & $\mathcal{O}(1)$ & $\mathcal{O}(V \log V + E)$ \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Find the topological order of courses for this given prerequisite graph.
\mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2006--07}

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={rectangle, draw, minimum width=1.8cm,
                       minimum height=0.7cm, font=\small\bfseries},
    every path/.style={->, line width=0.8pt},
  ]
\tikzset{>={Stealth[length=12pt, width=8pt, inset=2pt]}}
  \node (cse100) at (3, 8)    {CSE 100};
  \node (cse102) at (2.5, 6.5){CSE 102};
  \node (cse205) at (5.5, 6.5){CSE 205};
  \node (cse105) at (1.5, 5)  {CSE 105};
  \node (cse207) at (5, 5)    {CSE 207};
  \node (cse311) at (4, 3.5)  {CSE 311};
  \node (cse209) at (1, 2)    {CSE 209};
  \node (cse211) at (1, 0.5)  {CSE 211};
  \node (cse401) at (6, 1)    {CSE 401};
  \node (cse215) at (3.5, -1) {CSE 215};

  \draw (cse100) -> (cse102);
  \draw (cse102) -> (cse205);
  \draw (cse102) -> (cse105);
  \draw (cse205) -> (cse207);
  \draw (cse105) -> (cse207);
  \draw (cse105) -> (cse311);
  \draw (cse207) -> (cse311);
  \draw (cse311) -> (cse401);
  \draw (cse311) -> (cse209);
  \draw (cse209) -> (cse211);
  \draw (cse211) -> (cse215);
  \draw (cse215) -> (cse401);
  \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,
  boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,
  title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]
\textbf{Algorithm:} Run DFS; upon finishing each vertex prepend it to the
output list (or equivalently, push onto a stack and pop at the end).

\textbf{In-degree method (Kahn's):}

\begin{enumerate}[nosep,itemsep=3pt]
  \item Compute in-degree of every vertex.
  \item Enqueue all vertices with in-degree $0$.
  \item While queue non-empty: dequeue $u$, append to order, decrement
        in-degree of each neighbor; enqueue neighbor if in-degree becomes $0$.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{In-degrees:}

\begin{center}
\small\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{lc}
\toprule
\textbf{Course} & \textbf{In-degree} \\
\midrule
CSE 100 & 0 \\
CSE 102 & 1 \\
CSE 205 & 1 \\
CSE 105 & 1 \\
CSE 207 & 2 \\
CSE 311 & 2 \\
CSE 209 & 1 \\
CSE 211 & 1 \\
CSE 215 & 1 \\
CSE 401 & 2 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\textbf{Execution trace:}

\begin{center}
\small\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\toprule
\textbf{Step} & \textbf{Dequeued} & \textbf{Queue after} \\
\midrule
1 & CSE 100 & [CSE 102] \\
2 & CSE 102 & [CSE 205, CSE 105] \\
3 & CSE 205 & [CSE 105] \quad \textit{(CSE 207 in-deg $\to 1$)} \\
4 & CSE 105 & [CSE 207] \quad \textit{(CSE 207 in-deg $\to 0$, CSE 311 in-deg $\to 1$)} \\
5 & CSE 207 & [CSE 311] \quad \textit{(CSE 311 in-deg $\to 0$)} \\
6 & CSE 311 & [CSE 209] \quad \textit{(CSE 209 in-deg $\to 0$, CSE 401 in-deg $\to 1$)} \\
7 & CSE 209 & [CSE 211] \quad \textit{(CSE 211 in-deg $\to 0$)} \\
8 & CSE 211 & [CSE 215] \quad \textit{(CSE 215 in-deg $\to 0$)} \\
9 & CSE 215 & [CSE 401] \quad \textit{(CSE 401 in-deg $\to 0$)} \\
10 & CSE 401 & [] \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\textbf{Topological Order:}
\[
\text{CSE\,100} \to \text{CSE\,102} \to \text{CSE\,205} \to \text{CSE\,105} \to \text{CSE\,207} \to \text{CSE\,311} \to \text{CSE\,209}
\]
\[
\to \text{CSE\,211} \to \text{CSE\,215} \to \text{CSE\,401}
\]

\textbf{Time complexity:} $O(V + E)$.
\end{tcolorbox}


\end{enumerate}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cA}{Minimum Spanning Trees}
\label{sub:s1-mst}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item Consider the following undirected weighted graph. Vertices: $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$. Edges: $A$--$B\ (1)$, $A$--$C\ (3)$, $A$--$D\ (4)$, $B$--$C\ (2)$, $B$--$D\ (6)$, $C$--$D\ (5)$, $C$--$E\ (7)$, $D$--$E\ (8)$, $E$--$F\ (9)$, $D$--$F\ (10)$, $C$--$F\ (11)$. Determine whether the minimum spanning tree (MST) of this graph is unique. Justify your answer rigorously using MST properties such as the cut property or cycle property. If the MST is not unique, construct at least two distinct MSTs with equal total cost. If it is unique, provide a formal justification explaining why no alternative MST with the same total weight can exist.
\mk{20} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Prim's Algorithm} can be used to construct the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) and rigorously determine its uniqueness. Since the cut property states that the strictly lightest edge crossing any cut must belong to every MST, finding a unique minimum edge at every step of Prim's execution proves that the resulting MST is entirely unique.

\vspace{0.2cm}
Let's initialize the algorithm starting from vertex \textbf{A}.
\begin{itemize}[nosep]
    \item \textbf{Visited Set ($V_{vis}$):} Graph vertices currently included in the growing tree.
    \item \textbf{MST Edges ($E_{mst}$):} Edges selected for the final minimum spanning tree.
\end{itemize}

%% Define TikZ styles and common graph globally for this block
\tikzset{
  nd/.style={circle, draw=black!70, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries, fill=white},
  visited/.style={circle, draw=blue, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries, fill=blue!10, thick},
  edge/.style={-, color=black!20, line width=0.8pt},
  treeedge/.style={-, color=blue, line width=2pt},
  lbl/.style={draw=none, fill=white, font=\scriptsize, inner sep=1pt, text=black!50},
  treelbl/.style={draw=none, fill=white, font=\scriptsize\bfseries, inner sep=1pt, text=blue}
}

\def\drawBaseGraph{
  \node[nd] (A) at (0, 2) {A};
  \node[nd] (B) at (2, 4) {B};
  \node[nd] (C) at (2, 0) {C};
  \node[nd] (D) at (5, 4) {D};
  \node[nd] (E) at (7, 2) {E};
  \node[nd] (F) at (5, 0) {F};

  \draw[edge] (A) -- node[lbl, above left] {1} (B);
  \draw[edge] (B) -- node[lbl, left]       {2} (C);
  \draw[edge] (A) -- node[lbl, below left] {3} (C);
  \draw[edge] (A) -- node[lbl, pos=0.4, above] {4} (D);
  \draw[edge] (C) -- node[lbl, pos=0.3, above] {5} (D);
  \draw[edge] (B) -- node[lbl, above]      {6} (D);
  \draw[edge] (C) -- node[lbl, pos=0.4, below] {7} (E);
  \draw[edge] (D) -- node[lbl, above right] {8} (E);
  \draw[edge] (E) -- node[lbl, below right] {9} (F);
  \draw[edge] (D) -- node[lbl, right]      {10} (F);
  \draw[edge] (C) -- node[lbl, below]      {11} (F);
}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\textbf{Step 1: Initialization} \\
Start at vertex \textbf{A}. $V_{vis} = \{A\}$. \\
Available crossing edges from visited nodes: $(A,B)=1, (A,C)=3, (A,D)=4$. \\
\textbf{Action:} The unique minimum weight is \textbf{1}. Add edge \textbf{(A, B)} and vertex \textbf{B}.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1, transform shape]
  \drawBaseGraph
  \node[visited] (A) at (0, 2) {A};
  \node[visited] (B) at (2, 4) {B};
  \draw[treeedge] (A) -- node[treelbl, above left] {1} (B);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\textbf{Step 2:} \\
$V_{vis} = \{A, B\}$. \\
Available crossing edges: $(B,C)=2, (A,C)=3, (A,D)=4, (B,D)=6$. \\
\textbf{Action:} The unique minimum weight is \textbf{2}. Add edge \textbf{(B, C)} and vertex \textbf{C}.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1, transform shape]
  \drawBaseGraph
  \node[visited] (A) at (0, 2) {A};
  \node[visited] (B) at (2, 4) {B};
  \node[visited] (C) at (2, 0) {C};
  \draw[treeedge] (A) -- node[treelbl, above left] {1} (B);
  \draw[treeedge] (B) -- node[treelbl, left]       {2} (C);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\textbf{Step 3:} \\
$V_{vis} = \{A, B, C\}$. \\
Available crossing edges: $(A,D)=4, (C,D)=5, (B,D)=6, (C,E)=7, (C,F)=11$. \\
\textbf{Action:} The unique minimum weight is \textbf{4}. Add edge \textbf{(A, D)} and vertex \textbf{D}.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1, transform shape]
  \drawBaseGraph
  \node[visited] (A) at (0, 2) {A};
  \node[visited] (B) at (2, 4) {B};
  \node[visited] (C) at (2, 0) {C};
  \node[visited] (D) at (5, 4) {D};
  \draw[treeedge] (A) -- node[treelbl, above left] {1} (B);
  \draw[treeedge] (B) -- node[treelbl, left]       {2} (C);
  \draw[treeedge] (A) -- node[treelbl, pos=0.4, above] {4} (D);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\textbf{Step 4:} \\
$V_{vis} = \{A, B, C, D\}$. \\
Available crossing edges: $(C,E)=7, (D,E)=8, (D,F)=10, (C,F)=11$. \\
\textbf{Action:} The unique minimum weight is \textbf{7}. Add edge \textbf{(C, E)} and vertex \textbf{E}.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1, transform shape]
  \drawBaseGraph
  \node[visited] (A) at (0, 2) {A};
  \node[visited] (B) at (2, 4) {B};
  \node[visited] (C) at (2, 0) {C};
  \node[visited] (D) at (5, 4) {D};
  \node[visited] (E) at (7, 2) {E};
  \draw[treeedge] (A) -- node[treelbl, above left] {1} (B);
  \draw[treeedge] (B) -- node[treelbl, left]       {2} (C);
  \draw[treeedge] (A) -- node[treelbl, pos=0.4, above] {4} (D);
  \draw[treeedge] (C) -- node[treelbl, pos=0.4, below] {7} (E);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\textbf{Step 5:} \\
$V_{vis} = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$. \\
Available crossing edges to unvisited node `F`: $(E,F)=9, (D,F)=10, (C,F)=11$. \\
\textbf{Action:} The unique minimum weight is \textbf{9}. Add edge \textbf{(E, F)} and vertex \textbf{F}.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1, transform shape]
  \drawBaseGraph
  \node[visited] (A) at (0, 2) {A};
  \node[visited] (B) at (2, 4) {B};
  \node[visited] (C) at (2, 0) {C};
  \node[visited] (D) at (5, 4) {D};
  \node[visited] (E) at (7, 2) {E};
  \node[visited] (F) at (5, 0) {F};
  \draw[treeedge] (A) -- node[treelbl, above left] {1} (B);
  \draw[treeedge] (B) -- node[treelbl, left]       {2} (C);
  \draw[treeedge] (A) -- node[treelbl, pos=0.4, above] {4} (D);
  \draw[treeedge] (C) -- node[treelbl, pos=0.4, below] {7} (E);
  \draw[treeedge] (E) -- node[treelbl, below right] {9} (F);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\textbf{Rigorous Uniqueness Justification:} \\
According to the \textbf{Cut Property of MSTs}, for any cut separating a subset of vertices from the rest of the graph, if there exists a single edge crossing that cut whose weight is strictly less than all other crossing edges, that edge \textit{must} be included in any and all valid MSTs. 

As tracked across Steps 1 through 5, the minimum crossing edge choice at every structural iteration was strictly unique (there were never any weight ties for the minimum boundary edge). Because each edge addition was mathematically mandatory and left no alternative options, **the minimum spanning tree of this graph is completely unique**.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\textbf{Conclusion:} All vertices are securely spanned ($|V| - 1 = 5$ edges selected). \\
\textbf{Unique MST Edges:} $\{ (A,B), (B,C), (A,D), (C,E), (E,F) \}$ \\
\textbf{Total MST Cost:} $1 + 2 + 4 + 7 + 9 = \mathbf{23}$. \checkmark

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item Draw a minimum spanning tree of the graph shown in the figure using (i) Prim's algorithm, and (ii) Kruskal's algorithm. Write the order of the edges in each case (whenever there is a choice of vertices, always use alphabetical ordering).
\begin{center}
      \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.5,
        every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries}
    ]
        % Nodes positioning
        \node (a) at (0,1) {a};
        \node (b) at (1,2) {b};
        \node (c) at (1,0) {c};
        \node (d) at (2.5,1) {d};
        \node (e) at (4,2) {e};
        \node (f) at (4,0) {f};
        \node (g) at (5,1) {g};

        % Edges with weights
        \path[-] (a) edge node[above left, draw=none] {2} (b);
        \path[-] (a) edge node[below left, draw=none] {8} (c);
        
        \path[-] (b) edge node[left, draw=none] {9} (c);
        \path[-] (b) edge node[above, draw=none] {5} (d);
        \path[-] (b) edge node[above, draw=none] {6} (e);
        
        \path[-] (c) edge node[below, draw=none] {4} (d);
        \path[-] (c) edge node[below, draw=none] {3} (f);
        
        \path[-] (d) edge node[above, draw=none] {4} (e);
        \path[-] (d) edge node[below, draw=none] {4} (f);
        
        \path[-] (e) edge node[right, draw=none] {3} (f);
        \path[-] (e) edge node[above right, draw=none] {6} (g);
        
        \path[-] (f) edge node[below right, draw=none] {5} (g);

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{(i) Prim's Algorithm}
