Assuming we start at vertex \textbf{a} (alphabetically the first vertex). At each step, we select the minimum weight edge connecting a visited vertex to an unvisited vertex. If there is a tie in edge weights, we choose the edge that connects to the alphabetically earlier unvisited vertex.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Start at \textbf{a}. Available: (a,b)=2, (a,c)=8. \textbf{Select (a,b).}
    \item Visited: \{a,b\}. Available: (a,c)=8, (b,c)=9, (b,d)=5, (b,e)=6. \textbf{Select (b,d).}
    \item Visited: \{a,b,d\}. Available: (a,c)=8, (b,c)=9, (b,e)=6, (c,d)=4, (d,e)=4, (d,f)=4. 
    \begin{itemize}[nosep]
        \item Minimum weight is 4. Choices: (c,d), (d,e), (d,f).
        \item Target unvisited vertices are c, e, f. Alphabetically, \textbf{c} comes first. \textbf{Select (c,d).}
    \end{itemize}
    \item Visited: \{a,b,c,d\}. Available crossing edges: (c,f)=3, (d,e)=4, (d,f)=4, (b,e)=6, etc. \textbf{Select (c,f).}
    \item Visited: \{a,b,c,d,f\}. Available crossing edges: (e,f)=3, (d,e)=4, (f,g)=5, etc. \textbf{Select (e,f).}
    \item Visited: \{a,b,c,d,e,f\}. Available crossing edges: (f,g)=5, (b,e)=6, (e,g)=6. \textbf{Select (f,g).}
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Order of edges added (Prim's):} $\mathbf{(a, b), (b, d), (c, d), (c, f), (e, f), (f, g)}$

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\subsection*{(ii) Kruskal's Algorithm}
Kruskal's algorithm sorts all edges by weight and adds them one by one, provided they do not form a cycle. If weights are tied, we evaluate the edges in alphabetical order (e.g., (c,d) before (d,e)).

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Weight 2: \textbf{Select (a,b)}. (No cycle)
    \item Weight 3: Choices are (c,f) and (e,f). Alphabetically evaluate (c,f) first. \textbf{Select (c,f)}.
    \item Weight 3: \textbf{Select (e,f)}. (No cycle)
    \item Weight 4: Choices are (c,d), (d,e), (d,f). Alphabetically evaluate (c,d) first. \textbf{Select (c,d)}.
    \begin{itemize}[nosep]
        \item Evaluate (d,e): Reject, forms cycle (c-d-e-f).
        \item Evaluate (d,f): Reject, forms cycle (c-d-f).
    \end{itemize}
    \item Weight 5: Choices are (b,d) and (f,g). Alphabetically evaluate (b,d) first. \textbf{Select (b,d)}.
    \item Weight 5: \textbf{Select (f,g)}. (No cycle)
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Order of edges added (Kruskal's):} $\mathbf{(a, b), (c, f), (e, f), (c, d), (b, d), (f, g)}$

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\subsection*{Final Minimum Spanning Tree}
Both algorithms produce the same unique Minimum Spanning Tree with a total cost of \textbf{22}.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.5,
        every node/.style={circle, draw=black, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
        edge/.style={-, color=black!20, line width=1pt},
        treeedge/.style={-, color=blue, line width=2.5pt}
    ]
        % Nodes
        \node (a) at (0,1) {a};
        \node (b) at (1,2) {b};
        \node (c) at (1,0) {c};
        \node (d) at (2.5,1) {d};
        \node (e) at (4,2) {e};
        \node (f) at (4,0) {f};
        \node (g) at (5,1) {g};

        % MST Edges (Highlighted)
        \draw[treeedge] (a) -- node[above left, text=blue, draw=none] {2} (b);
        \draw[treeedge] (b) -- node[above, text=blue, draw=none] {5} (d);
        \draw[treeedge] (c) -- node[below, text=blue, draw=none] {4} (d);
        \draw[treeedge] (c) -- node[below, text=blue, draw=none] {3} (f);
        \draw[treeedge] (e) -- node[right, text=blue, draw=none] {3} (f);
        \draw[treeedge] (f) -- node[below right, text=blue, draw=none] {5} (g);

        % Rejected Edges (Faded)
        \draw[edge] (a) -- node[below left, text=black!40, draw=none] {8} (c);
        \draw[edge] (b) -- node[left, text=black!40, draw=none] {9} (c);
        \draw[edge] (b) -- node[above, text=black!40, draw=none] {6} (e);
        \draw[edge] (d) -- node[above, text=black!40, draw=none] {4} (e);
        \draw[edge] (d) -- node[below, text=black!40, draw=none] {4} (f);
        \draw[edge] (e) -- node[above right, text=black!40, draw=none] {6} (g);

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Prove (i) the optimal sub-structure property and (ii) the greedy choice property of the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) problem.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{Introduction}
To prove that an optimization problem can be solved correctly using a greedy strategy, we must establish two fundamental properties: \textbf{Optimal Substructure} and the \textbf{Greedy Choice Property}. Below are the formal proofs for both properties in the context of the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) problem.

\subsection*{(i) Proof of the Optimal Substructure Property}

\textbf{Theorem Statement:} \\
Let $G = (V, E)$ be a connected, undirected graph with weight function $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $T$ be an MST of $G$. If an edge $e = (u, v) \in T$ is removed, the tree $T$ decomposes into two disjoint subtrees, $T_1$ and $T_2$. Let $V_1 \subset V$ be the vertices spanned by $T_1$, and let $V_2 = V \setminus V_1$ be the vertices spanned by $T_2$. Let $G_1$ and $G_2$ be the subgraphs of $G$ induced by $V_1$ and $V_2$, respectively. Then, $T_1$ is an MST for $G_1$, and $T_2$ is an MST for $G_2$.

\textbf{Proof (by Contradiction):} \\
The total weight of the minimum spanning tree $T$ can be expressed as the sum of its constituent parts:
$$ w(T) = w(T_1) + w(T_2) + w(e) $$

Suppose for the sake of contradiction that the optimal substructure property does not hold. Without loss of generality, assume that $T_1$ is \textit{not} an MST for the induced subgraph $G_1$. 

Since $T_1$ is sub-optimal for $G_1$, there must exist an alternative valid spanning tree $T_1'$ for $G_1$ that yields a strictly lower total cost:
$$ w(T_1') < w(T_1) $$

Now, let us construct a new spanning tree $T^*$ for the original graph $G$ by substituting $T_1$ with the superior alternative $T_1'$, while keeping $T_2$ and the edge $e$ exactly as they were:
$$ T^* = T_1' \cup T_2 \cup \{e\} $$

Since $T_1'$ spans all vertices in $V_1$, $T_2$ spans all vertices in $V_2$, and the single edge $e = (u, v)$ reconnects a vertex from $V_1$ to a vertex in $V_2$, $T^*$ constitutes a valid spanning tree for the entire graph $G$. Let us evaluate its total weight:
$$ w(T^*) = w(T_1') + w(T_2) + w(e) $$

By substituting our assumption $w(T_1') < w(T_1)$ into the equation, we find:
$$ w(T^*) < w(T_1) + w(T_2) + w(e) = w(T) $$
$$ w(T^*) < w(T) $$

This result states that $T^*$ is a spanning tree of $G$ with a total cost strictly less than that of $T$. This directly contradicts our initial condition that $T$ is a \textbf{Minimum} Spanning Tree of $G$. 

Consequently, our assumption must be false. $T_1$ must be a valid MST for $G_1$, and by symmetric logic, $T_2$ must be a valid MST for $G_2$.

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\subsection*{(ii) Proof of the Greedy Choice Property (The Cut Property)}

\textbf{Theorem Statement:} \\
Let $G = (V, E)$ be a connected, undirected graph with weight function $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$. Let $S \subset V$ define a cut $(S, V \setminus S)$ of the graph. If $e = (u, v) \in E$ is a minimum-weight edge crossing the cut $(S, V \setminus S)$ (where $u \in S$ and $v \in V \setminus S$), then there exists an MST $T$ of $G$ that contains the edge $e$.

\textbf{Proof (by Exchange Argument):} \\
Let $T'$ be an arbitrary MST of $G$. We evaluate two possible scenarios:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Case 1: $e \in T'$} \\
    If the minimum-weight crossing edge $e$ is already included within $T'$, the greedy choice property is trivially satisfied, and we set $T = T'$.
    
    \item \textbf{Case 2: $e \notin T'$} \\
    If $e$ is not present in $T'$, we will construct an alternative spanning tree $T$ that contains $e$ and matches the optimal weight of $T'$.
    
    Because $T'$ is a spanning tree, a unique path $P$ must exist within $T'$ connecting vertex $u$ to vertex $v$. Since $u \in S$ and $v \in V \setminus S$, this path $P$ must cross the boundary of the cut $(S, V \setminus S)$ at least once. 
    
    Let $e' = (u', v')$ be an edge on this path $P$ in $T'$ that crosses the cut $(S, V \setminus S)$ such that $u' \in S$ and $v' \in V \setminus S$.
    
    If we temporarily insert our greedy edge $e$ into $T'$, we create a unique simple cycle $C = P \cup \{e\}$. This cycle contains both $e$ and $e'$. To break the cycle while maintaining connectivity, we remove the edge $e'$, producing a new edge set:
    $$ T = T' \cup \{e\} \setminus \{e'\} $$
    Since we broke the single cycle created by adding an edge to a tree, $T$ is guaranteed to be a valid, connected spanning tree of $G$.
\end{itemize}

Let us calculate the total weight of this newly constructed spanning tree $T$:
$$ w(T) = w(T') + w(e) - w(e') $$

By the problem definition, $e$ is a \textit{minimum-weight edge} crossing the cut $(S, V \setminus S)$. Since $e'$ is an edge crossing the exact same cut, its weight must be greater than or equal to that of $e$:
$$ w(e) \le w(e') \implies w(e) - w(e') \le 0 $$

Substituting this inequality back into the weight equation for $T$ yields:
$$ w(T) \le w(T') $$

However, because $T'$ is a Minimum Spanning Tree, its weight is already minimal by definition, meaning $w(T)$ cannot be strictly less than $w(T')$. Therefore, we must have:
$$ w(T) = w(T') $$

This equality demonstrates that the newly constructed spanning tree $T$ is also an MST of $G$. Since $T$ explicitly includes the greedy edge $e$, we have proven that making the local greedy choice is safe and always preserves the path to a globally optimal solution.

\hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2021-22
\item Develop a solution of the MST problem using disjoint set / union-find data structure. Use the array implementation to maintain the union-find data structure.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Array-Based Union-Find Data Structure}

To develop a solution for the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) problem using a disjoint-set data structure, we implement \textbf{Kruskal's Algorithm}. The disjoint-set (Union-Find) efficiently tracks which vertices belong to which connected components, preventing cycle formation when adding edges.

The array implementation utilizes two primary arrays of size $|V|$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \texttt{parent[i]}: Stores the representative (or parent) of vertex $i$. If \texttt{parent[i] == i}, vertex $i$ is the root of its set.
    \item \texttt{rank[i]}: Stores an upper bound on the height of the tree rooted at $i$, used to keep trees shallow during union operations (\textit{Union by Rank}).
\end{itemize}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Union-Find Array Operations}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Procedure{Make-Set}{$v$}
    \State $\text{parent}[v] \gets v$
    \State $\text{rank}[v] \gets 0$
\EndProcedure
\State
\Procedure{Find}{$v$} \Comment{With Path Compression}
    \If{$\text{parent}[v] \neq v$}
        \State $\text{parent}[v] \gets$ \Call{Find}{$\text{parent}[v]$} \Comment{Attach directly to root}
    \EndIf
    \State \Return $\text{parent}[v]$
\EndProcedure
\State
\Procedure{Union}{$u, v$} \Comment{With Union by Rank}
    \State $\text{root\_u} \gets$ \Call{Find}{$u$}
    \State $\text{root\_v} \gets$ \Call{Find}{$v$}
    \If{$\text{root\_u} \neq \text{root\_v}$}
        \If{$\text{rank}[\text{root\_u}] > \text{rank}[\text{root\_v}]$}
            \State $\text{parent}[\text{root\_v}] \gets \text{root\_u}$
        \ElsIf{$\text{rank}[\text{root\_u}] < \text{rank}[\text{root\_v}]$}
            \State $\text{parent}[\text{root\_u}] \gets \text{root\_v}$
        \Else
            \State $\text{parent}[\text{root\_v}] \gets \text{root\_u}$
            \State $\text{rank}[\text{root\_u}] \gets \text{rank}[\text{root\_u}] + 1$
        \EndIf
    \EndIf
\EndProcedure
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}
\subsection*{2. Kruskal's MST Algorithm}

Using the array-based Union-Find structure defined above, Kruskal's algorithm systematically processes edges in increasing order of weight, adding an edge to the MST only if it connects two previously disconnected components.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Kruskal-MST$(G, w)$}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
    \State \textbf{Input:} Graph $G = (V, E)$, edge weight function $w$
    \State $A \gets \emptyset$ \Comment{Initialize the set of MST edges}
    \State
    \For{each vertex $v \in V$}
        \State \Call{Make-Set}{$v$}
    \EndFor
    \State
    \State Sort the edges of $E$ into non-decreasing order by weight $w$
    \State
    \For{each edge $(u, v) \in E$, taken in sorted order}
        \If{\Call{Find}{$u$} $\neq$ \Call{Find}{$v$}} \Comment{If they are in different sets (no cycle)}
            \State $A \gets A \cup \{(u, v)\}$
            \State \Call{Union}{$u, v$} \Comment{Merge the two sets}
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
    \State
    \State \Return $A$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsection*{3. Time Complexity Analysis}

The efficiency of this solution is determined by the sorting step and the union-find operations:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Sorting:} Sorting the $|E|$ edges requires $\mathcal{O}(E \log E)$ time. Since $|E| \le |V|^2$, we know that $\log E \le 2 \log V$, so this is equivalently $\mathcal{O}(E \log V)$.
    \item \textbf{Union-Find Operations:} We perform exactly $|V|$ \texttt{Make-Set} operations and at most $2|E|$ \texttt{Find} and $|V|-1$ \texttt{Union} operations. Thanks to \textit{Path Compression} and \textit{Union by Rank}, a sequence of $m$ disjoint-set operations takes $\mathcal{O}(m \cdot \alpha(V))$ time, where $\alpha$ is the extremely slow-growing inverse Ackermann function. Thus, the disjoint-set operations take $\mathcal{O}(E \cdot \alpha(V))$ time.
\end{itemize}
Because $\alpha(V)$ is practically a constant $\le 4$ for any reasonably sized graph, the sorting step dominates the overall running time. 
\textbf{Total Time Complexity:} $\mathbf{\mathcal{O}(E \log E)}$ or $\mathbf{\mathcal{O}(E \log V)}$.

\hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item Let $T$ be a minimum spanning tree of graph $G$ obtained by Prim's algorithm. Let $G_{\text{new}}$ be a graph obtained by adding to $G$ a new vertex and some edges, with weights, connecting the new vertex to some vertices in $G$. Can you construct a minimum spanning tree of $G_{\text{new}}$ by adding one of the new edges to $T$? If your answer is yes, explain how; if your answer is no, explain why not.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Answer: No.}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\textbf{Explanation:} \\
You cannot simply construct the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) of $G_{\text{new}}$ by adding exactly one of the new edges to the existing MST $T$. 

Adding a single new edge $e_{\text{new}}$ to $T$ forces you to keep \textit{all} the original edges of $T$. While this will successfully create a valid spanning tree for $G_{\text{new}}$ (since it connects the new vertex without forming cycles), it does not guarantee a \textit{minimum} spanning tree. 

The introduction of the new vertex and its associated edges might provide a much cheaper "shortcut" between two vertices that already exist in $G$. To take advantage of this shortcut, the optimal strategy for $G_{\text{new}}$ might require including \textbf{multiple} new edges and removing one or more heavy edges that were previously part of $T$.

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\textbf{Counterexample:} \\
Consider a very simple graph $G$ with just two vertices, $A$ and $B$, connected by an edge of weight 10. 
\begin{itemize}[nosep]
    \item The MST $T$ of $G$ is just the edge $(A, B)$. Total cost = \textbf{10}.
\end{itemize}

Now, let's create $G_{\text{new}}$ by adding a new vertex $N$ along with two new edges: $(N, A)$ with weight 1, and $(N, B)$ with weight 1.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    scale=1.2,
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black!70, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge/.style={-, color=black!80, line width=1pt},
    newedge/.style={-, color=blue, line width=1pt, dashed},
    treeedge/.style={-, color=red, line width=2pt}
]
    \node (A) at (0,0) {A};
    \node (B) at (3,0) {B};
    \node[fill=blue!10, draw=blue, text=blue] (N) at (1.5,2) {N};

    \draw[edge] (A) -- node[below, draw=none] {10} (B);
    \draw[newedge] (N) -- node[above left, text=blue, draw=none] {1} (A);
    \draw[newedge] (N) -- node[above right, text=blue, draw=none] {1} (B);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{Case 1: Adding exactly one new edge to $T$ (The proposed method)} \\
If we keep $T$ (the edge $(A,B)$) and add exactly one new edge, say $(N, A)$, the resulting spanning tree consists of $\{(A,B), (N,A)\}$.
\begin{itemize}[nosep]
    \item Total cost = $10 + 1 = \mathbf{11}$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Case 2: The actual MST of $G_{\text{new}}$} \\
The true Minimum Spanning Tree of $G_{\text{new}}$ should connect $A$, $B$, and $N$ as cheaply as possible. We can do this by using both of the new edges and discarding the original edge $(A,B)$ from $T$. The actual MST consists of $\{(N,A), (N,B)\}$.
\begin{itemize}[nosep]
    \item Total cost = $1 + 1 = \mathbf{2}$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\textbf{Conclusion:} Since $11 \neq 2$, adding exactly one new edge to the original MST $T$ fails to yield the MST of $G_{\text{new}}$.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2021-22
\item Prove that if all edge weights of a graph are positive, then any subset of edges that connects all vertices and has minimum total weight must be a tree. Give an example to show that the same conclusion does not follow if we allow some weights to be nonpositive.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{Part (i): Proof for Positive Edge Weights}

\textbf{Theorem:} If all edge weights of a connected graph $G = (V, E)$ are strictly positive ($w(e) > 0$ for all $e \in E$), then any subset of edges $E' \subseteq E$ that connects all vertices and minimizes the total weight $\sum_{e \in E'} w(e)$ must be a tree.

\textbf{Proof (by Contradiction):}
Let $E'$ be a subset of edges that connects all vertices in $V$ and has the absolute minimum total weight among all such connecting subsets. By definition, the subgraph $G' = (V, E')$ is connected. 

Suppose for the sake of contradiction that $G'$ is \textit{not} a tree. 
Since $G'$ is connected and is not a tree, it must contain at least one simple cycle, which we will call $C$. 

Let $e_c$ be any edge belonging to this cycle $C$. Consider a new edge subset $E''$ formed by removing $e_c$ from $E'$:
$$ E'' = E' \setminus \{e_c\} $$

We now evaluate two essential properties of this new subset $E''$:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Connectivity:} In any graph, removing an edge from a cycle does not disconnect the graph. Any path in $G'$ that originally utilized the edge $e_c = (u, v)$ can bypass it by traveling along the remaining path of the cycle $C \setminus \{e_c\}$ to get from $u$ to $v$. Therefore, the subgraph $G'' = (V, E'')$ remains fully connected and still spans all vertices in $V$.
    
    \item \textbf{Total Weight:} Let us calculate the total weight of the new connecting subset $E''$:
    $$ w(E'') = \sum_{e \in E''} w(e) = \left( \sum_{e \in E'} w(e) \right) - w(e_c) = w(E') - w(e_c) $$
    
    By the initial premise of the problem, all edge weights in the graph are strictly positive, meaning $w(e_c) > 0$. Substituting this into our weight equation yields:
    $$ w(E'') < w(E') $$
\end{enumerate}

This inequality states that $E''$ is a subset of edges that completely connects all vertices in $V$ and has a total weight strictly less than $w(E')$. This directly contradicts our initial assumption that $E'$ was a connecting subset of \textbf{minimum} total weight.

Thus, our assumption must be false. The minimum-weight connecting subset $E'$ cannot contain any cycles. A connected graph with no cycles is, by definition, a tree. 

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\subsection*{Part (ii): Counterexample for Nonpositive Edge Weights}

If we allow edge weights to be nonpositive ($w(e) \le 0$), a minimum-weight connecting subset does not have to be a tree. It will greedily include extra edges forming cycles because adding those edges either decreases or maintains the total weight while preserving connectivity.

\textbf{Example (Negative Weights):}
Consider a graph $G$ with 3 vertices $\{u, v, w\}$ forming a triangle where every edge has a negative weight of $-5$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    scale=1.2,
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black!70, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge/.style={-, color=blue, line width=2pt}
]
    \node (u) at (0,0) {u};
    \node (v) at (2,0) {v};
    \node (w) at (1,1.5) {w};

    \draw[edge] (u) -- node[below, text=blue, draw=none] {-5} (v);
    \draw[edge] (v) -- node[above right, text=blue, draw=none] {-5} (w);
    \draw[edge] (w) -- node[above left, text=blue, draw=none] {-5} (u);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

Let us compare the possible subsets of edges that connect all three vertices:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Option 1 (Spanning Trees):} Any valid spanning tree must select exactly $|V| - 1 = 2$ edges to avoid cycles. For example, selecting edges $\{(u,v), (v,w)\}$ yields a total weight of:
    $$ (-5) + (-5) = -10 $$
    
    \item \textbf{Option 2 (The Entire Cycle):} If we select all 3 edges $E' = \{(u,v), (v,w), (w,u)\}$, the vertices remain perfectly connected, and the total weight becomes:
    $$ (-5) + (-5) + (-5) = -15 $$
\end{itemize}

Since $-15 < -10$, the unique subset of edges that connects all vertices and achieves the absolute minimum total weight is the entire cycle. Because this optimal subset contains a cycle, it is \textbf{not a tree}.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\textbf{Conclusion:} The requirement that edge weights must be strictly positive is necessary to ensure that the minimum connecting subgraph is structurally a tree.

\hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2019-20
\item Show that for each minimum spanning tree $T$ of $G$, there is a way to sort the edges of $G$ in Kruskal's algorithm so that the algorithm returns $T$. (Kruskal's algorithm can return different spanning trees for the same input graph $G$, depending on how it breaks ties when the edges are sorted.)
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Proof Strategy:}
To prove that Kruskal's algorithm can always return a specific Minimum Spanning Tree $T$, we must define a tie-breaking rule for sorting the edges. By strategically ordering edges of the same weight, we can force Kruskal's algorithm to favor the edges of $T$, thereby reconstructing $T$ perfectly.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\textbf{The Sorting Rule:}
Sort all edges in the graph $G$ in non-decreasing order of their weights. If two edges have the exact same weight, \textbf{break the tie by placing the edge that belongs to $T$ before the edge that does not belong to $T$}. (If both edges belong to $T$ or neither belongs to $T$, their relative order does not matter).

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\textbf{Proof by Contradiction:}

Let us run Kruskal's algorithm on the graph $G$ using this specific sorted list of edges. Suppose, for the sake of contradiction, that the algorithm outputs a spanning tree $T_K$ that is different from $T$ ($T_K \neq T$).

Since both $T$ and $T_K$ are valid spanning trees (each containing $|V| - 1$ edges), there must exist at least one edge in $T$ that Kruskal's algorithm rejected. 

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Let $e = (u,v)$ be the \textit{first} edge in our sorted list that belongs to $T$ but is rejected by Kruskal's algorithm.
    \item Kruskal's algorithm only rejects an edge if adding it creates a cycle with the edges it has \textit{already accepted}. Let $A$ be the set of edges Kruskal's has accepted just before evaluating $e$. Adding $e$ to $A$ forms a cycle $C$.
    \item Because $T$ is a tree, it contains no cycles. Therefore, not all edges of the cycle $C$ can belong to $T$. There must be at least one edge in $C$ that is not in $T$.
    \item Now, consider the original MST $T$. If we remove the edge $e$ from $T$, it splits $T$ into two disconnected components, $V_1$ and $V_2$. This forms a cut $(V_1, V_2)$, and $e$ is the \textit{only} edge in $T$ that crosses this cut.
    \item Because $C$ is a cycle that contains $e$ (which connects $V_1$ and $V_2$), the cycle must cross the cut back over to close the loop. Therefore, there must be another edge $e'' \in C$ (where $e'' \neq e$) that also crosses the cut $(V_1, V_2)$.
    \item Since $e''$ crosses the cut but $e$ is the only edge in $T$ that crosses this cut, it is strictly true that $\mathbf{e'' \notin T}$.
    \item Furthermore, since $e'' \in C \setminus \{e\}$, it must be part of the already-accepted set $A$. This means Kruskal's algorithm evaluated and accepted $e''$ \textit{before} it even reached $e$. Thus, in our sorted list, $e''$ appears before $e$, implying:
    $$ w(e'') \le w(e) $$
    \item Let us evaluate a new spanning tree formed by replacing $e$ with $e''$ in the optimal tree $T$: 
    $$ T^* = T \setminus \{e\} \cup \{e''\} $$
    The weight of this new tree is $w(T^*) = w(T) - w(e) + w(e'')$. 
    \item Since $w(e'') \le w(e)$, it must be that $w(T^*) \le w(T)$. However, $T$ is a \textit{Minimum} Spanning Tree, meaning its weight cannot be strictly beaten. Therefore, $w(T^*)$ must equal $w(T)$, which forces:
    $$ w(e'') = w(e) $$
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{The Contradiction:}
We have established three facts:
\begin{itemize}[nosep]
    \item $w(e) = w(e'')$ (The edges have identical weights).
    \item $e \in T$ and $e'' \notin T$.
    \item $e''$ was evaluated by Kruskal's before $e$ (meaning $e''$ appeared before $e$ in our sorted list).
\end{itemize}

This is a direct contradiction! Our explicit tie-breaking rule states that if two edges have the same weight, the edge belonging to $T$ ($e$) \textbf{must} appear before the edge not in $T$ ($e''$). 

Because our assumption leads to a logical impossibility, the assumption must be false. Kruskal's algorithm, using this tie-breaking sort, will \textbf{never} reject an edge of $T$. Since it will accept all $|V| - 1$ edges of $T$, it must perfectly reconstruct $T$.

\hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2020-21
\item Prove that an optimal minimum spanning tree is composed of optimal minimum spanning subtrees.
 Draw a minimum spanning tree of the graph $G$ shown below such that the minimum spanning tree contains the edges with weights $x$ and $z$.
\begin{center}
  
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small},
  ]

  % Top row
  \node (A) at (0, 4)  {A};
  \node (B) at (3, 4)  {B};
  \node (C) at (6, 4)  {C};
  \node (D) at (9, 4)  {D};
  \node (E) at (12, 4)  {E};

  % Bottom row
  \node (F) at (0, 0)  {F};
  \node (G) at (3, 0)  {G};
  \node (H) at (6, 0)  {H};
  \node (I) at (9, 0)  {I};
  \node (J) at (12, 0)  {J};

  % Top row edges
  \draw (A) -- node[edge label, above] {13} (B);
  \draw (B) -- node[edge label, above] {z}  (C);
  \draw (C) -- node[edge label, above] {2}  (D);
  \draw (D) -- node[edge label, above] {6}  (E);

  % Bottom row edges
  \draw (F) -- node[edge label, below] {x}  (G);
  \draw (G) -- node[edge label, below] {4}  (H);
  \draw (H) -- node[edge label, below] {3}  (I);
  \draw (I) -- node[edge label, below] {1}  (J);

  % Vertical & diagonal edges
  \draw (A) -- node[edge label, left]        {11} (F);
  \draw (E) -- node[edge label, right]       {5}  (J);
  \draw (A) -- node[edge label, right]       {1}  (G);
  \draw (B) -- node[edge label, left]        {14} (G);
  \draw (B) -- node[edge label, right]       {8}  (H);
  \draw (C) -- node[edge label, right]       {12} (H);
  \draw (D) -- node[edge label, right]       {9}  (H);
  \draw (D) -- node[edge label, left]        {6}  (I);
  \draw (D) -- node[edge label, right]       {7}  (J);

  \end{tikzpicture}

\end{center}
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{Part 1: Proof of Optimal Substructure Property}

\textbf{Theorem:} An optimal Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) is composed of optimal minimum spanning subtrees.

\textbf{Proof (by Contradiction):} \\
Let $G = (V, E)$ be a connected, undirected graph with weight function $w$. Let $T$ be an optimal MST of $G$.

Suppose we remove an arbitrary edge $e = (u, v)$ from $T$. This removal splits $T$ into two disjoint subtrees, $T_1$ and $T_2$, which span two disjoint sets of vertices, $V_1$ and $V_2$, respectively. 

We must prove that $T_1$ is an optimal MST for the subgraph $G_1$ (induced by $V_1$) and $T_2$ is an optimal MST for $G_2$ (induced by $V_2$).

Assume, for the sake of contradiction, that $T_1$ is \textbf{not} an optimal minimum spanning tree for $G_1$. This means there must exist some alternative spanning tree $T_1'$ for $G_1$ that successfully connects all vertices in $V_1$ but has a strictly smaller total weight:
$$w(T_1') < w(T_1)$$

Now, let us construct a new spanning tree $T^*$ for the entire graph $G$ by replacing $T_1$ with the superior subtree $T_1'$ and reconnecting it to $T_2$ using the original edge $e$:
$$T^* = T_1' \cup T_2 \cup \{e\}$$

The total weight of this newly constructed tree $T^*$ is:
$$w(T^*) = w(T_1') + w(T_2) + w(e)$$

Since we established $w(T_1') < w(T_1)$ in our assumption, we can substitute this inequality:
$$w(T^*) < w(T_1) + w(T_2) + w(e)$$

Because the right side of the inequality evaluates to the exact weight of our original MST $T$, we get:
$$w(T^*) < w(T)$$

This result states that $T^*$ is a valid spanning tree for $G$ with a strictly lower total weight than $T$. However, this directly contradicts our initial premise that $T$ is the \textit{Minimum} Spanning Tree of $G$. 

Therefore, our assumption must be false. $T_1$ is guaranteed to be an optimal minimum spanning tree for $G_1$, and by symmetric logic, $T_2$ is optimal for $G_2$.

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\subsection*{Part 2: Minimum Spanning Tree Construction}

We need to construct an MST of $G$ that guarantees the inclusion of the edges with weights $x$ (edge \textbf{F-G}) and $z$ (edge \textbf{B-C}). We can achieve this by initializing our MST with these two edges as forced inclusions, and then proceeding with Kruskal's algorithm for the remaining edges.

\textbf{Step-by-Step Construction:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Force inclusion:} Add edge \textbf{(F,G)} [weight $x$] and \textbf{(B,C)} [weight $z$].
    \item \textbf{Sort remaining edges:} Sort by weight. If weights tie, the tie is broken using alphabetical ordering of the vertices.
    \item \textbf{Apply Kruskal's:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Weight 1:} Add \textbf{(A,G)} and \textbf{(I,J)}. (Neither forms a cycle).
        \item \textbf{Weight 2:} Add \textbf{(C,D)}.
        \item \textbf{Weight 3:} Add \textbf{(H,I)}.
        \item \textbf{Weight 4:} Add \textbf{(G,H)}.
        \item \textbf{Weight 5:} Add \textbf{(E,J)}.
        \item \textbf{Weight 6:} The candidate edges are \textbf{(D,E)} and \textbf{(D,I)}. 
        \begin{itemize}
            \item Following alphabetical ordering, we evaluate \textbf{(D,E)} first. 
            \item Adding \textbf{(D,E)} successfully connects the disconnected upper sub-component $\{B,C,D\}$ with the larger component $\{A,F,G,H,I,J,E\}$. No cycle is formed, so we add it.
            \item At this exact point, all 10 vertices are connected by 9 edges. The spanning tree is complete, and any subsequent edge evaluations (like \textbf{(D,I)}) are bypassed or rejected as they would form cycles.
        \end{itemize}
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Final MST Edges \& Weights:} \\
\textbf{(A,G)} = 1, \textbf{(I,J)} = 1, \textbf{(C,D)} = 2, \textbf{(H,I)} = 3, \textbf{(G,H)} = 4, \textbf{(E,J)} = 5, \textbf{(D,E)} = 6, \textbf{(F,G)} = $x$, \textbf{(B,C)} = $z$.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\textbf{Visual Diagram of the MST:}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    scale=1.0,
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black!80, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small},
    mst edge/.style={-, color=blue!70!black, line width=1.5pt}
]

    % Top row
    \node (A) at (0, 4)  {A};
    \node (B) at (3, 4)  {B};
    \node (C) at (6, 4)  {C};
    \node (D) at (9, 4)  {D};
    \node (E) at (12, 4) {E};

    % Bottom row
    \node (F) at (0, 0)  {F};
    \node (G) at (3, 0)  {G};
    \node (H) at (6, 0)  {H};
    \node (I) at (9, 0)  {I};
    \node (J) at (12, 0) {J};

    % Draw the MST edges
    \draw[mst edge] (F) -- node[edge label, below] {$x$} (G);
    \draw[mst edge] (B) -- node[edge label, above] {$z$} (C);
    
    \draw[mst edge] (A) -- node[edge label, right] {1} (G);
    \draw[mst edge] (I) -- node[edge label, below] {1} (J);
    
    \draw[mst edge] (C) -- node[edge label, above] {2} (D);
    \draw[mst edge] (H) -- node[edge label, below] {3} (I);
    
    \draw[mst edge] (G) -- node[edge label, below] {4} (H);
    \draw[mst edge] (E) -- node[edge label, right] {5} (J);
    \draw[mst edge] (D) -- node[edge label, above] {6} (E);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write down an algorithm for constructing a mincost arborescence in a digraph from a given root vertex $r$. Apply the algorithm to the following digraph, showing every step in separate diagraphs: $ra(7), rx(2), ax(1), xy(5), ya(3), xc(6), cb(4), bx(2), yb(2)$ (weights in parentheses). \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Algorithm Description}

The standard university-level method for finding a minimum-cost arborescence in a directed graph is \textbf{Edmonds' Algorithm} (also known as the \textbf{Chu-Liu-Edmonds Algorithm}). 

An arborescence rooted at a specified vertex $r$ is a directed spanning tree in which every vertex $v \neq r$ has exactly one incoming edge, and there is a unique directed path from $r$ to $v$.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Chu-Liu-Edmonds Algorithm}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Statex \textbf{Input:} A directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with edge weights $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$, and a root vertex $r \in V$.
\Statex \textbf{Output:} A minimum-cost arborescence $A$ rooted at $r$.
\State Remove all self-loops from $E$ and all incoming edges to the root $r$.
\State For each vertex $v \in V \setminus \{r\}$, select its minimum-weight incoming edge:
$$\pi(v) = \operatorname{argmin}_{(u, v) \in E} w(u, v)$$
\State Let $P = \{ \pi(v) \mid v \in V \setminus \{r\} \}$ be the set of selected edges.
\If{$P$ does not contain any directed cycles}
    \State \Return $A = P$ \Comment{$P$ is already the optimal arborescence}
\Else
    \State Select a directed cycle $C$ in $P$.
    \State Contract the vertices of $C$ into a single pseudo-vertex $v_C$, creating a new graph $G' = (V', E')$.
    \State Modify the weights of all edges entering the cycle: For each edge $e = (u, v) \in E$ with $u \notin C$ and $v \in C$, define its new weight as:
    $$w'(u, v_C) = w(u, v) - w(\pi(v))$$
    \State For edges leaving the cycle $(u, v)$ where $u \in C, v \notin C$, retain their original weight: $w'(v_C, v) = w(u, v)$.
    \State For edges entirely inside or between vertices of $C$, discard them.
    \State $A' \gets \text{Chu-Liu-Edmonds}(G', w', r)$ \Comment{Recursive call on the contracted graph}
    \State Find the unique edge $(u, v_C) \in A'$ that enters the pseudo-vertex $v_C$ (corresponding to some original edge $(u, v)$ where $v \in C$).
    \State Construct $A$ by expanding $v_C$: include all edges from $A'$, replace $(u, v_C)$ with $(u, v)$, and include all edges of the cycle $C$ except the unique edge $\pi(v)$ that enters $v$.
    \State \Return $A$
\EndIf
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsection*{2. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Naive Implementation:} In each contraction step, we spend $O(|E|)$ time to find minimum incoming edges and reconstruct the graph. Since each contraction reduces the number of vertices by at least one, there can be at most $O(|V|)$ contractions. Thus, the total time complexity is $\mathcal{O}(|V| \cdot |E|)$.
        \item \textbf{Optimized Implementation:} By utilizing specialized priority queues (such as pairing heaps or Fibonacci heaps) along with a Disjoint-Set (Union-Find) data structure to maintain components, the time complexity can be optimized to $\mathcal{O}(|E| \log |V|)$ or $\mathcal{O}(|E| + |V| \log |V|)$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ to store the graph representations, edge weight modifications, and the recursive contraction history tree required for the expansion phase.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{3. Step-by-Step Application to the Given Digraph}

The given digraph has the vertex set $V = \{r, a, x, y, c, b\}$ with $r$ as the root. The edges and weights are:
$$
(r,a):7, \quad (r,x):2, \quad (a,x):1, \quad (x,y):5, \quad (y,a):3, \quad (x,c):6, \quad (c,b):4, \quad (b,x):2, \quad (y,b):2
$$

---

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Original Graph $G_0$ and Selection of Minimum Incoming Edges}

First, we visualize the original graph $G_0$. For every vertex except the root $r$, we identify and select the incoming edge with the lowest weight.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.3, auto, >=stealth, thick,
  node/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=gray!10, minimum size=8mm, font=\bfseries},
  chosen/.style={->, >=stealth, line width=1.8pt, blue}]
  
  % Nodes
  \node[node] (r) at (0,2) {r};
  \node[node] (a) at (2.5,4) {a};
  \node[node] (x) at (2.5,1) {x};
  \node[node] (y) at (5.5,4) {y};
  \node[node] (b) at (5.5,1) {b};
  \node[node] (c) at (4, -0.8) {c};

  % Edges
  \draw[->] (r) -- node {7} (a);
  \draw[->] (r) -- node [swap] {2} (x);
  \draw[->] (a) -- node {1} (x);
  \draw[->] (x) -- node [pos=0.4] {5} (y);
  \draw[->] (y) -- node [swap] {3} (a);
  \draw[->] (x) -- node [swap] {6} (c);
  \draw[->] (c) -- node [swap] {4} (b);
  \draw[->] (b) -- node {2} (x);
  \draw[->] (y) -- node {2} (b);
\end{tikzpicture}
\\ \textit{Figure 1: Original Digraph $G_0$}
\end{center}

Let us tabulate the minimum incoming edges for each non-root vertex:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|c|l|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Vertex ($v$)} & \textbf{All Incoming Edges with Weights} & \textbf{Minimum Edge $\pi(v)$} & \textbf{Weight $w(\pi(v))$} \\ \hline
$a$ & $(r, a): 7, \ \mathbf{(y, a): 3}$ & $(y, a)$ & 3 \\ \hline
$x$ & $(r, x): 2, \ \mathbf{(a, x): 1}, \ (b, x): 2$ & $(a, x)$ & 1 \\ \hline
$y$ & $\mathbf{(x, y): 5}$ & $(x, y)$ & 5 \\ \hline
$c$ & $\mathbf{(x, c): 6}$ & $(x, c)$ & 6 \\ \hline
$b$ & $(c, b): 4, \ \mathbf{(y, b): 2}$ & $(y, b)$ & 2 \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Let $P_0$ be the set of these chosen minimum incoming edges:
$$P_0 = \{ (y, a), (a, x), (x, y), (x, c), (y, b) \}$$



Let us plot the subgraph containing only the edges in $P_0$ to inspect for cycles:

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.3, auto, >=stealth, thick,
  node/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=gray!10, minimum size=8mm, font=\bfseries},
  chosen/.style={->, >=stealth, line width=1.8pt, blue},
  cycle/.style={->, >=stealth, line width=1.8pt, red}]
  
  % Nodes
  \node[node] (r) at (0,2) {r};
  \node[node] (a) at (2.5,4) {a};
  \node[node] (x) at (2.5,1) {x};
  \node[node] (y) at (5.5,4) {y};
  \node[node] (b) at (5.5,1) {b};
  \node[node] (c) at (4, -0.8) {c};

  % Chosen Edges highlighting the cycle
  \draw[cycle] (a) -- node {\textbf{1}} (x);
  \draw[cycle] (x) -- node [pos=0.4] {\textbf{5}} (y);
  \draw[cycle] (y) -- node [swap] {\textbf{3}} (a);
  \draw[chosen] (x) -- node [swap] {6} (c);
  \draw[chosen] (y) -- node {2} (b);
\end{tikzpicture}
\\ \textit{Figure 2: Selected Subgraph $P_0$ showcasing the critical cycle $C_1$ in red}
\end{center}

As explicitly highlighted above, the edge set contains a directed cycle $C_1 = \{x \to y \to a \to x\}$. 

---

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Contraction of Cycle $C_1$ and Edge Weight Modification}

We contract the cycle $C_1 = \{x, y, a\}$ into a single pseudo-vertex denoted as $v_{xay}$. Now, we recompute the modified weights $w'$ for all incoming edges entering this pseudo-vertex from external nodes:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Edges from $r$ to $C_1$:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Original edge $(r, a)$ with $w(r, a) = 7$. The cycle edge entering $a$ is $\pi(a) = (y, a)$ with weight 3.
        $$w'(r, v_{xay}) = w(r, a) - w(y, a) = 7 - 3 = 4$$
        \item Original edge $(r, x)$ with $w(r, x) = 2$. The cycle edge entering $x$ is $\pi(x) = (a, x)$ with weight 1.
        $$w'(r, v_{xay}) = w(r, x) - w(a, x) = 2 - 1 = 1$$
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Edges from $b$ to $C_1$:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Original edge $(b, x)$ with $w(b, x) = 2$. The cycle edge entering $x$ is $\pi(x) = (a, x)$ with weight 1.
        $$w'(b, v_{xay}) = w(b, x) - w(a, x) = 2 - 1 = 1$$
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Edges leaving $C_1$:} Retain their original minimum values.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Edge from $x \to c$ becomes $v_{xay} \to c$ with weight 6.
        \item Edge from $y \to b$ becomes $v_{xay} \to b$ with weight 2.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Other remaining edges:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Edge $c \to b$ remains unchanged with weight 4.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

Let us plot the newly constructed contracted graph $G_1$:

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.3, auto, >=stealth, thick,
  node/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=gray!10, minimum size=8mm, font=\bfseries},
  pseudo/.style={ellipse, draw=black, fill=blue!10, dashed, minimum width=18mm, minimum height=10mm, font=\bfseries}]
  
  % Nodes
  \node[node] (r) at (0,2) {r};
  \node[pseudo] (v) at (3,2) {$v_{xay}$};
  \node[node] (b) at (6,2) {b};
  \node[node] (c) at (4.5, 0) {c};

  % Edges with modified weights
  \draw[->] (r) edge [bend left=30] node {4 (via a)} (v);
  \draw[->] (r) edge [bend right=10] node [swap] {1 (via x)} (v);
  \draw[->] (v) edge [bend left=15] node {2 (via y)} (b);
  \draw[->] (b) edge [bend left=15] node {1 (via x)} (v);
  \draw[->] (v) -- node [swap] {6 (via x)} (c);
  \draw[->] (c) -- node [swap] {4} (b);
\end{tikzpicture}
\\ \textit{Figure 3: Contracted Graph $G_1$ with Modified Weights}
\end{center}

Now, we perform the selection of the minimum incoming edges for each non-root vertex in $G_1$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item For $c$: The only incoming edge is $v_{xay} \to c$ with weight 6.
    \item For $b$: Incoming edges are $v_{xay} \to b$ (weight 2) and $c \to b$ (weight 4). The minimum is $v_{xay} \to b$ with weight 2.
    \item For $v_{xay}$: Incoming edges are from $r$ (weight 1 via $x$), from $r$ (weight 4 via $a$), and from $b$ (weight 1 via $x$). 
    
    There is a tie for the minimum incoming edge to $v_{xay}$ between $(r, v_{xay})$ and $(b, v_{xay})$, both having an adjusted weight of 1. Following standard greedy optimization rules, we select the edge coming from the root $r$, i.e., \textbf{$(r, v_{xay})$ via $x$}. This selection prevents any further cycling and resolves the graph directly.
\end{itemize}

Let us graph the chosen edge set $P_1$ in $G_1$:

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.3, auto, >=stealth, thick,
  node/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=gray!10, minimum size=8mm, font=\bfseries},
  pseudo/.style={ellipse, draw=black, fill=blue!10, dashed, minimum width=18mm, minimum height=10mm, font=\bfseries},
  chosen/.style={->, >=stealth, line width=1.8pt, blue}]
  
  % Nodes
  \node[node] (r) at (0,2) {r};
  \node[pseudo] (v) at (3,2) {$v_{xay}$};
  \node[node] (b) at (6,2) {b};
  \node[node] (c) at (4.5, 0) {c};

  % Chosen edges
  \draw[chosen] (r) edge [bend right=10] node [swap] {1} (v);
  \draw[chosen] (v) edge [bend left=15] node {2} (b);
  \draw[chosen] (v) -- node [swap] {6} (c);
\end{tikzpicture}
\\ \textit{Figure 4: Acyclic Selected Arborescence $A'$ in $G_1$}
\end{center}

Since $A' = \{ (r, v_{xay}) \text{ via } x, \ (v_{xay}, c) \text{ via } x, \ (v_{xay}, b) \text{ via } y \}$ contains absolutely no cycles, it forms the optimal spanning arborescence for the contracted graph $G_1$.

---

\subsubsection*{Step 3: Expansion Phase and Final Arborescence Construction}

We expand the pseudo-vertex $v_{xay}$ back to its original components $\{x, y, a\}$ to reconstruct the optimal solution for $G_0$:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item The incoming edge chosen for $v_{xay}$ in $A'$ is $(r, v_{xay})$ which originally enters vertex \textbf{$x$}. Therefore, we add the edge \textbf{$(r, x)$} to our final arborescence.
    \item Since the entry point into the cycle is $x$, we must break the cycle $C_1$ by discarding the internal cycle edge that enters $x$. The edge entering $x$ in the cycle is $(a, x)$. Thus, $(a, x)$ is eliminated.
    \item The remaining cycle edges, \textbf{$(x, y)$} and \textbf{$(y, a)$}, are safely broken out and included in our final edge set.
    \item The external outgoing structural edges chosen in $A'$ are preserved: $(v_{xay}, c)$ which corresponds to \textbf{$(x, c)$}, and $(v_{xay}, b)$ which corresponds to \textbf{$(y, b)$}.
\end{enumerate}

Compiling these components yields the final minimum-cost arborescence edge set $A$:
$$A = \{ (r, x), (x, y), (y, a), (x, c), (y, b) \}$$

Let us visualize the final structural configuration of the minimum cost arborescence:

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.3, auto, >=stealth, thick,
  node/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=gray!10, minimum size=8mm, font=\bfseries},
  sol/.style={->, >=stealth, line width=2pt, blue}]
  
  % Nodes
  \node[node] (r) at (0,2) {r};
  \node[node] (a) at (2.5,4) {a};
  \node[node] (x) at (2.5,1) {x};
  \node[node] (y) at (5.5,4) {y};
  \node[node] (b) at (5.5,1) {b};
  \node[node] (c) at (4, -0.8) {c};

  % Final Arborescence Edges
  \draw[sol] (r) -- node [swap] {2} (x);
  \draw[sol] (x) -- node [pos=0.4] {5} (y);
  \draw[sol] (y) -- node [swap] {3} (a);
  \draw[sol] (x) -- node [swap] {6} (c);
  \draw[sol] (y) -- node {2} (b);
\end{tikzpicture}
\\ \textit{Figure 5: Final Minimum Cost Arborescence $A$}
\end{center}

\subsection*{4. Final Cost Verification}

Let us compute the total exact cost of the resulting minimum-cost arborescence:
$$
\text{Total Cost} = w(r, x) + w(x, y) + w(y, a) + w(x, c) + w(y, b)
$$
$$
\text{Total Cost} = 2 + 5 + 3 + 6 + 2 = \mathbf{18}
$$

All vertices are uniquely and optimally reachable from the root node $r$ with a total minimum cost of \textbf{18}.
\end{tcolorbox}

\item What is a spanning tree of a graph? Write Kruskal's algorithm for finding a minimum spanning tree of an edge-weighted graph. Analyze the time complexity of the algorithm by suggesting appropriate data structures. \mk{3+5+7}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Definition of a Spanning Tree}

Let $G = (V, E)$ be a connected, undirected graph. A \textbf{spanning tree} of $G$ is a subgraph $T = (V, E')$ such that:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Spanning:} It includes all vertices of the original graph $G$ (the vertex set $V$ is identical).
    \item \textbf{Acyclic:} It contains no cycles.
    \item \textbf{Connected:} There is exactly one simple path between any pair of vertices in $T$.
\end{itemize}
Mathematically, a spanning tree always contains exactly $|V| - 1$ edges. If the graph $G$ has edge weights, a \textbf{Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)} is a spanning tree whose sum of edge weights is minimized.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.5, auto, >=stealth, thick,
  node/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=gray!10, minimum size=8mm, font=\bfseries},
  edge/.style={draw=black!70, line width=1pt},
  mst/.style={draw=cA, line width=2.5pt}]
  
  % Original Graph G
  \begin{scope}[xshift=0cm]
      \node at (1, 2.5) {\textbf{Original Graph $G$}};
      \node[node] (A) at (0,1.5) {A};
      \node[node] (B) at (2,1.5) {B};
      \node[node] (C) at (2,0) {C};
      \node[node] (D) at (0,0) {D};
      
      \draw[edge] (A) -- node {1} (B);
      \draw[edge] (B) -- node {2} (C);
      \draw[edge] (C) -- node {3} (D);
      \draw[edge] (D) -- node {4} (A);
      \draw[edge] (A) -- node [pos=0.3] {5} (C);
  \end{scope}

  % Minimum Spanning Tree
  \begin{scope}[xshift=5cm]
      \node at (1, 2.5) {\textbf{Minimum Spanning Tree}};
      \node[node] (A2) at (0,1.5) {A};
      \node[node] (B2) at (2,1.5) {B};
      \node[node] (C2) at (2,0) {C};
      \node[node] (D2) at (0,0) {D};
      
      \draw[mst] (A2) -- node {1} (B2);
      \draw[mst] (B2) -- node {2} (C2);
      \draw[mst] (C2) -- node {3} (D2);
      \draw[edge, dashed, draw=black!30] (D2) -- node {4} (A2);
      \draw[edge, dashed, draw=black!30] (A2) -- node [pos=0.3] {5} (C2);
  \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsection*{2. Kruskal's Algorithm}

Kruskal's Algorithm is a greedy approach. It builds the MST forest by considering edges in increasing order of their weights and adding an edge to the forest if and only if it does not form a cycle. Cycle detection is efficiently handled using a Disjoint-Set data structure.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Kruskal's Algorithm}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Statex \textbf{Input:} A connected, undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with edge weights $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$.
\Statex \textbf{Output:} A set of edges $A$ forming a Minimum Spanning Tree.
\State $A \gets \emptyset$ \Comment{Initialize an empty set to store MST edges}
\For{each vertex $v \in V$}
    \State \Call{Make-Set}{$v$} \Comment{Create $|V|$ disjoint sets, one for each vertex}
\EndFor
\State Sort all edges in $E$ into non-decreasing order by their weight $w$.
\For{each edge $(u, v) \in E$, taken in sorted order}
    \If{\Call{Find-Set}{$u$} $\neq$ \Call{Find-Set}{$v$}} \Comment{Check if $u$ and $v$ are in different components}
        \State $A \gets A \cup \{(u, v)\}$ \Comment{Add edge to the MST}
        \State \Call{Union}{$u, v$} \Comment{Merge the two components}
    \EndIf
\EndFor
\State \Return $A$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsection*{3. Time Complexity Analysis and Data Structures}

To achieve optimal time complexity, we must choose appropriate data structures:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Sorting Data Structure:} We can use an array of edges and sort them using an efficient comparison-based sorting algorithm like \textbf{Merge Sort} or \textbf{Quick Sort}.
    \item \textbf{Disjoint-Set (Union-Find) Data Structure:} We maintain the connected components using a \textbf{Disjoint-Set Forest} implemented with two crucial heuristics:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textit{Union by Rank:} Attaches the shorter tree under the root of the taller tree during a union operation to keep the trees flat.
        \item \textit{Path Compression:} Makes every visited node point directly to the root during a find operation.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Step-by-Step Complexity Breakdown:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Initialization (Make-Set):} Creating a disjoint set for each of the $|V|$ vertices takes $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Sorting Edges:} Sorting the $|E|$ edges takes $\mathcal{O}(|E| \log |E|)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Cycle Checking and Merging (Find-Set and Union):} The algorithm iterates over $|E|$ edges. In each iteration, it performs two \textproc{Find-Set} operations, and if the condition is met, one \textproc{Union} operation. For $|E|$ operations on $|V|$ elements using Union by Rank and Path Compression, the total time is $\mathcal{O}(|E| \alpha(|V|))$, where $\alpha$ is the inverse Ackermann function. Since $\alpha(|V|) \le 4$ for all practical values of $|V|$, this phase runs in virtually linear time $\mathcal{O}(|E|)$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Overall Time Complexity:}
The total time complexity is the sum of these phases:
$$ T(|V|, |E|) = \mathcal{O}(|V| + |E| \log |E| + |E| \alpha(|V|)) $$
Since the graph is a simple graph, the number of edges is bounded by $|V|^2$, implying that $\log |E| \le \log(|V|^2) = 2 \log |V|$. Thus, $\mathcal{O}(\log |E|)$ is equivalent to $\mathcal{O}(\log |V|)$. 
The sorting step asymptotically dominates the runtime.

\textbf{Final Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|E| \log |V|)$

\subsubsection*{Space Complexity:}
The space complexity requires storing the graph structure (edges) and the Disjoint-Set forest.
\begin{itemize}
    \item Storing the edge list takes $\mathcal{O}(|E|)$ space.
    \item The parent and rank arrays for the Disjoint-Set take $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ space.
\end{itemize}
\textbf{Final Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Find a minimum spanning tree using Prim's algorithm, showing every step.

\begin{center}

  ]

  \node (e) at (0, 0.5) {e};
  \node (f) at (4, 2)   {f};

  \draw (a) -- node[edge label, above]       {5} (b);
  \draw (a) -- node[edge label, below right] {2} (f);
  \draw (a) -- node[edge label, left]        {6} (e);
  \draw (a) -- node[edge label, pos=0.3]       {6} (c);
  \draw (b) -- node[edge label, right]       {3} (c);
  \draw (b) -- node[edge label, above right] {7} (f);
  \draw (f) -- node[edge label, below]       {4} (c);

\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,
  boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,
  title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Prim's Algorithm} builds the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) by starting from an arbitrary node and repeatedly adding the cheapest edge that connects a visited node to an unvisited node.

\vspace{0.2cm}
Let's define the nodes and edges. We will start the algorithm from node \textbf{a}.
\begin{itemize}[nosep]
    \item \textbf{Visited Set ($V_{vis}$):} Keeps track of nodes already in the MST.
    \item \textbf{MST Edges ($E_{mst}$):} Keeps track of edges added to the MST.
\end{itemize}

%% Define TikZ styles and common graph globally for this block
\tikzset{
  nd/.style={circle, draw=black!70, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries, fill=white},

}

\def\drawBaseGraph{
  \node[nd] (a) at (2, 4)   {a};
  \node[nd] (b) at (6, 4.5) {b};
  \node[nd] (c) at (8, 2)   {c};
  \node[nd] (d) at (5, 0)   {d};
  \node[nd] (e) at (0, 0.5) {e};
  \node[nd] (f) at (4, 2)   {f};

  \draw[edge] (a) -- node[lbl, above]       {5} (b);
  \draw[edge] (a) -- node[lbl, below right] {2} (f);
  \draw[edge] (a) -- node[lbl, left]        {6} (e);
  \draw[edge] (a) -- node[lbl, pos=0.3]     {6} (c);
  \draw[edge] (b) -- node[lbl, right]       {3} (c);
  \draw[edge] (b) -- node[lbl, above right] {7} (f);
  \draw[edge] (f) -- node[lbl, below]       {4} (c);
  \draw[edge] (f) -- node[lbl, right]       {3} (d);
  \draw[edge] (c) -- node[lbl, right]       {2} (d);
  \draw[edge] (e) -- node[lbl, above]       {5} (f);
  \draw[edge] (e) -- node[lbl, below]       {5} (d);
  \draw[edge] (c) to[bend left=100] node[lbl, below] {9} (e);
}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\textbf{Step 1: Initialization} \\
Start at vertex \textbf{a}. $V_{vis} = \{a\}$. \\
Available edges from visited nodes: $(a,f)=2, (a,b)=5, (a,e)=6, (a,c)=6$. \\
\textbf{Action:} Minimum weight is \textbf{2}. Add edge \textbf{(a, f)} and vertex \textbf{f}.

  \drawBaseGraph
  \node[visited] (a) at (2, 4) {a};
  \node[visited] (f) at (4, 2) {f};
  \draw[treeedge] (a) -- node[treelbl, below right] {2} (f);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\textbf{Step 2:} \\
$V_{vis} = \{a, f\}$. \\
Available crossing edges: $(f,d)=3, (f,c)=4, (a,b)=5, (e,f)=5, (a,e)=6, (a,c)=6, (b,f)=7$. \\
\textbf{Action:} Minimum weight is \textbf{3}. Add edge \textbf{(f, d)} and vertex \textbf{d}.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1, transform shape]
  \drawBaseGraph
  \node[visited] (a) at (2, 4) {a};
  \node[visited] (f) at (4, 2) {f};
  \node[visited] (d) at (5, 0) {d};
  \draw[treeedge] (a) -- node[treelbl, below right] {2} (f);
  \draw[treeedge] (f) -- node[treelbl, right] {3} (d);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\textbf{Step 3:} \\
$V_{vis} = \{a, f, d\}$. \\
Available crossing edges: $(c,d)=2, (f,c)=4, (a,b)=5, (e,f)=5, (e,d)=5, \dots$ \\
\textbf{Action:} Minimum weight is \textbf{2}. Add edge \textbf{(d, c)} and vertex \textbf{c}.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1, transform shape]
  \drawBaseGraph
  \node[visited] (a) at (2, 4) {a};
  \node[visited] (f) at (4, 2) {f};
  \node[visited] (d) at (5, 0) {d};
  \node[visited] (c) at (8, 2) {c};
  \draw[treeedge] (a) -- node[treelbl, below right] {2} (f);
  \draw[treeedge] (f) -- node[treelbl, right] {3} (d);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\textbf{Step 4:} \\
$V_{vis} = \{a, f, d, c\}$. \\
Available crossing edges: $(b,c)=3, (a,b)=5, (e,f)=5, (e,d)=5, (c,e)=9, \dots$ \\
\textbf{Action:} Minimum weight is \textbf{3}. Add edge \textbf{(c, b)} and vertex \textbf{b}.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1, transform shape]
  \drawBaseGraph
  \node[visited] (a) at (2, 4) {a};
  \node[visited] (f) at (4, 2) {f};
  \node[visited] (d) at (5, 0) {d};
  \node[visited] (c) at (8, 2) {c};
  \node[visited] (b) at (6, 4.5) {b};
