  \draw[treeedge] (f) -- node[treelbl, right] {3} (d);
  \draw[treeedge] (c) -- node[treelbl, right] {2} (d);
  \draw[treeedge] (b) -- node[treelbl, right] {3} (c);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\textbf{Step 5:} \\
$V_{vis} = \{a, f, d, c, b\}$. \\
Available crossing edges to unvisited node `e`: $(e,f)=5, (e,d)=5, (a,e)=6, (c,e)=9$. \\
\textbf{Action:} Minimum weight is \textbf{5}. We can pick either $(e,f)$ or $(e,d)$. Let's pick \textbf{(e, f)}. Add vertex \textbf{e}. 

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1, transform shape]
  \drawBaseGraph
  \node[visited] (a) at (2, 4) {a};
  \node[visited] (f) at (4, 2) {f};
  \node[visited] (d) at (5, 0) {d};
  \node[visited] (c) at (8, 2) {c};
  \node[visited] (b) at (6, 4.5) {b};
  \node[visited] (e) at (0, 0.5) {e};
  \draw[treeedge] (a) -- node[treelbl, below right] {2} (f);
  \draw[treeedge] (f) -- node[treelbl, right] {3} (d);
  \draw[treeedge] (c) -- node[treelbl, right] {2} (d);
  \draw[treeedge] (b) -- node[treelbl, right] {3} (c);
  \draw[treeedge] (e) -- node[treelbl, above] {5} (f);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\textbf{Conclusion:} All vertices are now visited ($|V| - 1 = 5$ edges added). \\
\textbf{Final MST Edges:} $\{ (a,f), (f,d), (d,c), (c,b), (e,f) \}$ \\
\textbf{Total MST Cost:} $2 + 3 + 2 + 3 + 5 = \mathbf{15}$. \checkmark

\end{tcolorbox}


\item Let $G$ be a weighted connected graph and let $V_1, V_2$ be a partition of its vertices into two disjoint non-empty sets. Let $e$ be an edge in $G$ with minimum weight among those with one endpoint in $V_1$ and the other in $V_2$. Show that there is a minimum spanning tree $T$ of $G$ containing $e$. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Theorem Statement (The Cut Property of MSTs)}

Let $G = (V, E)$ be a connected, undirected graph with an edge-weight function $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$. Let $(V_1, V_2)$ be a partition of $V$ (i.e., $V_1 \cap V_2 = \emptyset$, $V_1 \cup V_2 = V$, and $V_1, V_2 \neq \emptyset$). Let $e = (u, v)$ be an edge in $G$ such that $u \in V_1$ and $v \in V_2$, and for all edges $e' = (u', v')$ crossing the cut (where $u' \in V_1, v' \in V_2$), we have $w(e) \le w(e')$. Then, there exists a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) $T$ of $G$ that contains $e$.

---

\subsection*{2. Formal Proof (The Cut-Exchange Argument)}

We will prove this property using a standard university-level \textbf{cut-exchange argument}.

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Assumption of an Arbitrary MST}
Let $T^*$ be any Minimum Spanning Tree of $G$. We consider two mutually exclusive cases:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Case 1:} $e \in T^*$. If the chosen MST already contains the minimum-weight cut edge $e$, then the claim holds immediately by setting $T = T^*$.
    \item \textbf{Case 2:} $e \notin T^*$. We proceed to show that we can construct an alternative spanning tree $T$ from $T^*$ such that $e \in T$ and $w(T) = w(T^*)$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Cycle Creation via Edge Insertion}
Suppose $e = (u, v) \notin T^*$. Because $T^*$ is a spanning tree, there already exists a unique, simple path $P$ in $T^*$ connecting vertex $u$ to vertex $v$. If we temporarily add the edge $e$ to $T^*$, it creates a subgraph $T^* \cup \{e\}$ containing exactly one unique simple cycle $C$, consisting of the edge $e$ combined with the path $P$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.2, auto, >=stealth, thick,
  node/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=gray!10, minimum size=7mm, font=\bfseries}]
  
  % Cut Partition V1
  \draw[fill=blue!5, dashed, draw=gray!60, line width=1pt] (-0.5,1.5) ellipse (1.5cm and 2.2cm);
  \node[text=blue!70!black] at (-0.5, 3.4) {\textbf{Partition $V_1$}};
  
  % Cut Partition V2
  \draw[fill=red!5, dashed, draw=gray!60, line width=1pt] (5.5,1.5) ellipse (1.5cm and 2.2cm);
  \node[text=red!70!black] at (5.5, 3.4) {\textbf{Partition $V_2$}};
  
  % Nodes
  \node[node] (u) at (0.5, 2.3) {u};
  \node[node] (v) at (4.5, 2.3) {v};
  \node[node] (u_prime) at (0.5, 0.7) {u'};
  \node[node] (v_prime) at (4.5, 0.7) {v'};
  
  % Edges
  \draw[->, line width=2.5pt, blue] (u) -- node[above, text=black] {$e$ (Min Weight)} (v);
  \draw[draw=black!40, line width=1.5pt, dashed] (u) -- (u_prime);
  \draw[->, line width=2.5pt, cA] (u_prime) -- node[below, text=black] {$e'$} (v_prime);
  \draw[draw=black!40, line width=1.5pt, dashed] (v_prime) -- (v);
  
  % Labels
  \node[align=center, text=black!60, font=\small] at (2.5, -1) {Figure 1: The unique cycle $C$ formed by adding $e$ to $T^*$. \\ The alternative cut-crossing edge is $e'$.};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 3: Finding an Alternative Cut-Crossing Edge}
The vertex $u$ lies in $V_1$ and the vertex $v$ lies in $V_2$. Therefore, the path $P$ within $T^*$ must start in $V_1$ and terminate in $V_2$. This implies that the path $P$ must cross the cut boundary from $V_1$ to $V_2$ at least once. 

Let $e' = (u', v')$ be an edge on the path $P$ such that $u' \in V_1$ and $v' \in V_2$. By definition, $e' \in T^*$ and $e' \neq e$.

\subsubsection*{Step 4: The Exchange and Construction of $T$}
We now construct a new subgraph $T$ by adding $e$ to $T^*$ and removing $e'$:
$$T = T^* \cup \{e\} \setminus \{e'\}$$

Let us verify that $T$ satisfies all necessary conditions of a Spanning Tree:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Spanning Property:} No vertices were removed; thus, $T$ contains all vertices $V$.
    \item \textbf{Acyclicity:} The insertion of $e$ created exactly one cycle $C$. Removing $e'$ (which belongs to $C$) breaks that cycle, leaving the graph completely acyclic.
    \item \textbf{Connectivity:} Removing an edge from a cycle does not disconnect a graph. Since $T^*$ was connected, $T$ remains connected.
\end{enumerate}
With $|V|-1$ edges, no cycles, and complete connectivity, $T$ is mathematically proven to be a valid spanning tree of $G$.

\subsubsection*{Step 5: Minimality Analysis}
We now calculate and compare the total edge weight of our newly constructed spanning tree $T$:
$$w(T) = w(T^*) + w(e) - w(e')$$

Recall the core structural hypothesis of the theorem: $e$ is an edge of \textbf{minimum weight} crossing the cut $(V_1, V_2)$. Since $e'$ is also an edge crossing this exact same cut, it must satisfy:
$$w(e) \le w(e') \implies w(e) - w(e') \le 0$$

Substituting this inequality back into our weight equation yields:
$$w(T) \le w(T^*)$$

However, our initial assumption designated $T^*$ as a \textbf{Minimum} Spanning Tree, meaning no spanning tree in $G$ can have a strictly lower total weight than $T^*$. Therefore, it must be true that:
$$w(T) = w(T^*)$$

\subsection*{3. Conclusion}

This proves that $T$ is also a Minimum Spanning Tree of $G$. Since $e \in T$ by construction, we have successfully demonstrated that there is always an MST of $G$ containing the minimum-weight cut edge $e$. \hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write Prim's algorithm that computes a minimum spanning tree of a graph. Analyze its time complexity. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2016--17}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Introduction and Algorithm Description}

\textbf{Prim's Algorithm} is a greedy minimum spanning tree (MST) algorithm that constructs the tree vertex by vertex. Starting from an arbitrary root vertex $r$, the algorithm maintains a set of vertices $S$ that have already been included in the growing MST. At each step, it identifies the minimum-weight edge crossing the cut $(S, V \setminus S)$—meaning one endpoint is in $S$ and the other is in $V \setminus S$—and adds the corresponding vertex to $S$.

To implement this efficiently, a min-priority queue $Q$ is utilized to store all vertices currently in $V \setminus S$. The priority (or $key$) of a vertex $v \in Q$ corresponds to the minimum weight of any edge connecting $v$ to an existing vertex in $S$.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Prim's Algorithm}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Statex \textbf{Input:} A connected, undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with an edge-weight function $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$, and a starting root vertex $r \in V$.
\Statex \textbf{Output:} A Minimum Spanning Tree defined by predecessor/parent pointers $\pi$.
\For{each vertex $u \in V$}
    \State $key[u] \gets \infty$ \Comment{Initialize all cuts to infinity}
    \State $\pi[u] \gets \text{NIL}$ \Comment{Set predecessor of each vertex to null}
\EndFor
\State $key[r] \gets 0$ \Comment{Set the root's key to 0 so it is extracted first}
\State Initialize a min-priority queue $Q$ containing all vertices $V$, keyed by $key$ values.
\While{$Q$ is not empty}
    \State $u \gets \Call{Extract-Min}{Q}$ \Comment{Add vertex $u$ to the growing MST component $S$}
    \For{each vertex $v \in \text{Adj}[u]$}
        \If{$v \in Q$ \textbf{and} $w(u, v) < key[v]$}
            \State $\pi[v] \gets u$ \Comment{Update the parent pointer}
            \State $key[v] \gets w(u, v)$ \Comment{Update the priority key value}
            \State \Call{Decrease-Key}{$Q, v, key[v]$} \Comment{Re-heapify the priority queue}
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndWhile
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

---

\subsection*{2. Visualizing the Cut Property in Prim's Algorithm}

The foundational correctness of Prim's algorithm rests on the \textit{Cut Property}. At any point during execution, the vertices are split into the tree vertices $S$ and the remaining vertices in the priority queue $Q = V \setminus S$.



\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.2, auto, >=stealth, thick,
  node/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=gray!10, minimum size=8mm, font=\bfseries},
  mstnode/.style={circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!20, minimum size=8mm, font=\bfseries}]
  
  % Set S
  \draw[fill=blue!5, dashed, draw=blue!40, line width=1.2pt] (0,1) ellipse (1.5cm and 2cm);
  \node[text=blue!80!black] at (0, 3.2) {\textbf{Tree Vertices ($S$)}};
  \node[mstnode] (r) at (-0.5, 1.8) {r};
  \node[mstnode] (a) at (0.3, 0.5) {a};
  \draw[draw=cA, line width=2pt] (r) -- (a);

  % Set V \ S
  \draw[fill=red!5, dashed, draw=red!40, line width=1.2pt] (5,1) ellipse (1.5cm and 2cm);
  \node[text=red!80!black] at (5, 3.2) {\textbf{Queue Vertices ($V \setminus S$)}};
  \node[node] (b) at (4.5, 1.8) {b};
  \node[node] (c) at (5.2, 0.5) {c};
  \draw[draw=black!30, dashed] (b) -- (c);

  % Cut-crossing edges
  \draw[->, line width=2pt, cA] (a) -- node[above, text=black] {min = 2} (b);
  \draw[draw=black!40] (r) edge [bend left=20] node[above, text=black] {6} (b);
  \draw[draw=black!40] (a) -- node[below, text=black] {5} (c);
  
\end{tikzpicture}
\\ \textit{Figure 1: The Cut Boundary during Prim's Algorithm execution.}
\end{center}

---

\subsection*{3. Detailed Time Complexity Analysis}

The total execution runtime depends completely on the choice of the underlying data structure used to implement the min-priority queue $Q$. The algorithm's structural runtime consists of three primary components:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Initialization:} Executed exactly $|V|$ times, running in $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ time.
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Extract-Min}} operations: Executed exactly once for every vertex, totaling $|V|$ invocations.
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Decrease-Key}} operations: Triggered inside the adjacency loop. In an undirected graph, each edge $(u,v)$ is examined at most twice (once from $u$ and once from $v$). Thus, \texttt{Decrease-Key} is invoked at most $2|E| = \mathcal{O}(|E|)$ times.
\end{enumerate}

Let us analyze the overall asymptotic bounds across different heap variations:

\subsubsection*{Case A: Binary Heap Implementation}
If the priority queue $Q$ is implemented via a standard standard Binary Min-Heap (e.g., array-based binary tree):
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Building the Heap:} Constructing the initial structure takes $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$.
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Extract-Min}:} Each extraction takes $\mathcal{O}(\log |V|)$ time. For $|V|$ vertices, this costs $\mathcal{O}(|V| \log |V|)$.
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Decrease-Key}:} Each key modification requires bubbling up elements, costing $\mathcal{O}(\log |V|)$ time. For $|E|$ updates, this costs $\mathcal{O}(|E| \log |V|)$.
\end{itemize}
Summing these operations up:
$$ T(|V|, |E|) = \mathcal{O}(|V|) + \mathcal{O}(|V| \log |V|) + \mathcal{O}(|E| \log |V|) = \mathcal{O}((|V| + |E|) \log |V|) $$
For any simple connected graph, $|E| \ge |V| - 1$, meaning that $|E|$ asymptotically dominates $|V|$. 
$$\textbf{Total Binary Heap Complexity: } \mathcal{O}(|E| \log |V|)$$

\subsubsection*{Case B: Fibonacci Heap Implementation}
If the priority queue $Q$ is optimized via a Fibonacci Heap:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Extract-Min}:} Takes $\mathcal{O}(\log |V|)$ amortized time. For $|V|$ vertices, the total cost is $\mathcal{O}(|V| \log |V|)$.
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Decrease-Key}:} Takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ constant amortized time. For $|E|$ edge modifications, the total cost drops to $\mathcal{O}(|E|)$.
\end{itemize}
Summing these operations up:
$$\textbf{Total Fibonacci Heap Complexity: } \mathcal{O}(|E| + |V| \log |V|)$$
This is highly advantageous for \textbf{dense graphs} where $|E| \approx |V|^2$, dropping the time complexity down to a virtually linear $\mathcal{O}(|E|)$.

---

\subsection*{4. Space Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Graph Storage:} Storing the graph using an adjacency list requires $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ space.
    \item \textbf{Auxiliary Arrays:} Storing the tracking arrays ($key$, $\pi$) requires $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ space.
    \item \textbf{Priority Queue Space:} The priority queue holds at most $|V|$ vertices at any time, consuming $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ space.
\end{itemize}
$$\textbf{Total Space Complexity: } \mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write the correctness proof of Kruskal's algorithm for finding a minimum spanning tree. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2016--17}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Introduction to the Proof}

To prove the correctness of Kruskal's Algorithm, we must establish two properties for a given connected, undirected, edge-weighted graph $G = (V, E)$:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Spanning Tree Property:} The algorithm terminates and produces a valid spanning tree (a connected and acyclic subgraph covering all vertices).
    \item \textbf{Minimality Property:} The produced spanning tree has the minimum possible total edge weight.
\end{enumerate}

---

\subsection*{2. Proof of the Spanning Tree Property}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Acyclicity:} The algorithm explicitly utilizes a Disjoint-Set (Union-Find) data structure to only add an edge $(u, v)$ if $u$ and $v$ belong to different connected components. Thus, it is impossible for the algorithm to form a cycle.
    \item \textbf{Connectivity and Spanning:} Initially, the graph is a forest of $|V|$ isolated vertices. Every time an edge is added, two distinct components are merged into one, reducing the total number of components by exactly 1. For a connected graph, there are enough edges to continue this process until only 1 component remains. This requires exactly $|V| - 1$ edges. An acyclic graph with $|V|$ vertices and $|V| - 1$ edges is, by definition, a connected spanning tree.
\end{itemize}

---

\subsection*{3. Proof of Minimality (via Mathematical Induction)}

We will prove that the spanning tree generated by Kruskal's algorithm is a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) using induction on the sequence of edges added to the tree.

Let the edges added by Kruskal's algorithm be $e_1, e_2, \dots, e_{n-1}$ (where $n = |V|$), ordered by the time of their insertion. 
Let $T_k = \{e_1, e_2, \dots, e_k\}$ be the set of the first $k$ edges chosen by the algorithm.

\textbf{Inductive Hypothesis:} For every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n-1\}$, the forest $T_k$ is a subset of some Minimum Spanning Tree $T^*$ of $G$.

\subsubsection*{Base Case ($k = 0$):}
$T_0 = \emptyset$. The empty set is trivially a subset of any Minimum Spanning Tree of $G$. The base case holds.

\subsubsection*{Inductive Step:}
Assume that the hypothesis holds for $k-1$. That is, there exists some MST $T^*$ such that $T_{k-1} \subseteq T^*$. We must show that there exists an MST (either $T^*$ or a different one) containing $T_k = T_{k-1} \cup \{e_k\}$.

Let $e_k = (u, v)$ be the $k$-th edge added by Kruskal's algorithm. We consider two possible cases:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Case 1: $e_k \in T^*$} \\
    If the $k$-th edge is already in $T^*$, then $T_k = T_{k-1} \cup \{e_k\} \subseteq T^*$. The induction step immediately holds.
    
    \item \textbf{Case 2: $e_k \notin T^*$} \\
    If $e_k \notin T^*$, adding $e_k$ to the tree $T^*$ creates exactly one simple cycle, $C$, because $T^*$ is a spanning tree.
    
    Because $e_k$ was chosen by Kruskal's algorithm, it must connect two different components in the forest $T_{k-1}$. Let $S$ be the set of vertices in the component containing $u$ within $T_{k-1}$. The vertex $v$ lies in a different component, so $v \notin S$.
    
    Since the cycle $C$ goes from $u \in S$ to $v \notin S$ and back, there must be some other edge $e' = (x, y)$ in the cycle $C$ such that $e'$ crosses the cut $(S, V \setminus S)$.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.2, auto, >=stealth, thick,
  node/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=gray!10, minimum size=7mm, font=\bfseries}]
  
  % Cut Partition S
  \draw[fill=blue!5, dashed, draw=blue!40, line width=1.2pt] (0, 1.5) ellipse (1.8cm and 2.2cm);
  \node[text=blue!80!black] at (0, 3.4) {\textbf{Component in $T_{k-1}$ ($S$)}};
  
  % Cut Partition V \ S
  \draw[fill=red!5, dashed, draw=red!40, line width=1.2pt] (5, 1.5) ellipse (1.8cm and 2.2cm);
  \node[text=red!80!black] at (5, 3.4) {\textbf{Other Vertices ($V \setminus S$)}};
  
  % Nodes
  \node[node] (u) at (0.5, 2.3) {u};
  \node[node] (v) at (4.5, 2.3) {v};
  \node[node] (x) at (0.5, 0.7) {x};
  \node[node] (y) at (4.5, 0.7) {y};
  
  % Edges
  \draw[->, line width=2pt, cA] (u) -- node[above, text=black] {$e_k$ (Kruskal's choice)} (v);
  \draw[draw=black!40, line width=1.5pt, dashed] (u) -- node[left] {Path in $T_{k-1}$} (x);
  \draw[->, line width=2pt, orange] (x) -- node[below, text=black] {$e' \in T^*$} (y);
  \draw[draw=black!40, line width=1.5pt, dashed] (y) -- node[right] {Path in $T^*$} (v);

\end{tikzpicture}
\\ \textit{Figure 1: Cycle exchange mechanism illustrating $e_k$ and $e'$ crossing the cut.}
\end{center}

\textbf{Analyzing the weights:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Since $e'$ crosses the cut between components of $T_{k-1}$, adding it to $T_{k-1}$ would not form a cycle. It was a valid candidate when Kruskal's algorithm was considering edges.
    \item Kruskal's considers edges in non-decreasing order of weight. Since it chose $e_k$ instead of $e'$, it must be that $w(e_k) \le w(e')$.
    \item However, $T^*$ is assumed to be an MST. If we construct a new spanning tree $T^{**} = T^* \cup \{e_k\} \setminus \{e'\}$, its total weight is:
    $$w(T^{**}) = w(T^*) + w(e_k) - w(e')$$
    \item Since $T^*$ is an MST, no spanning tree can have a smaller weight. Thus $w(T^{**}) \ge w(T^*)$, which implies $w(e_k) \ge w(e')$.
    \item Combining the inequalities $w(e_k) \le w(e')$ and $w(e_k) \ge w(e')$, we conclude that $w(e_k) = w(e')$.
\end{enumerate}

Therefore, $w(T^{**}) = w(T^*)$, meaning $T^{**}$ is also an optimal Minimum Spanning Tree. Furthermore, since $e'$ was not in $T_{k-1}$, the removal of $e'$ does not disturb the prior edges. We now have $T_k = T_{k-1} \cup \{e_k\} \subseteq T^{**}$. 

By the principle of mathematical induction, $T_{n-1}$ (the final output of Kruskal's algorithm) is a subset of some MST. Since $T_{n-1}$ contains exactly $n-1$ edges, it is precisely that Minimum Spanning Tree. \hfill $\blacksquare$

---

\subsection*{4. Brief Complexity Summary}
\textit{As per standard algorithm analysis requirements:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|E| \log |V|)$ due to sorting the edges, followed by $\mathcal{O}(|E| \alpha(|V|))$ for the Union-Find operations, where $\alpha$ is the inverse Ackermann function. The sorting step asymptotically dominates.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ to store the graph, the edge array, and the Union-Find disjoint-set data structures.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item There is a network of roads $G = (V, E)$ connecting a set of cities $V$.
Each road in $E$ has an associated length $l_e$. There is a proposal to add one
new road to this network, and there is a list $E'$ of pairs of cities between
which the new road can be built. Each such potential road $e' \in E'$ has an
associated length. As a designer for the public works department you are asked
to determine the road $e' \in E'$ whose addition to existing network $G$ would
result in the maximum decrease in the driving distance between two fixed cities
$s$ and $t$ in the network. Give an algorithm for solving this problem. Your
algorithm should run in $O((V + E + E') \log V)$ time.
 \mk{15}\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2015--16}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Core Insight}

If we introduce a single new road $e' = (u, v) \in E'$ with length $l_{e'}$ into the existing undirected network $G = (V, E)$, the new shortest path from city $s$ to city $t$ can only take one of two structural forms:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Scenario A:} It does not use the new road $e'$ at all. The driving distance remains the original baseline distance in $G$, denoted as $\text{dist}_G(s, t)$.
    \item \textbf{Scenario B:} It uses the new road $e'$ exactly once. Since the road is bidirectional, the path will either traverse the new road from $u$ to $v$ or from $v$ to $u$. 
\end{enumerate}

Thus, the minimized driving distance after incorporating a candidate road $e'$ is given mathematically by:
$$\min \Big( \text{dist}_G(s, t), \;\; \text{dist}_G(s, u) + l_{e'} + \text{dist}_G(v, t), \;\; \text{dist}_G(s, v) + l_{e'} + \text{dist}_G(u, t) \Big)$$

Instead of running an expensive Dijkstra search from scratch for each proposed road in $E'$, we can precompute the shortest distances from $s$ to all vertices, and from all vertices to $t$, using just \textbf{two independent passes} of Dijkstra's algorithm.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.2, auto, >=stealth, thick,
  node/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=gray!10, minimum size=8mm, font=\bfseries},
  special/.style={circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!20, minimum size=8mm, font=\bfseries}]

  % Fixed endpoints
  \node[special] (s) at (0,1) {s};
  \node[special] (t) at (7,1) {t};
  
  % Candidate Edge Endpoints
  \node[node] (u) at (2.5,2.2) {u};
  \node[node] (v) at (4.5,2.2) {v};

  % Paths via u -> v
  \draw[->, line width=1.2pt, dashed, draw=blue!70] (s) edge[bend left=15] node {$\text{dist}_S[u]$} (u);
  \draw[->, line width=2.5pt, cA] (u) -- node[above, text=black] {$e' \in E'$} (v);
  \draw[->, line width=1.2pt, dashed, draw=red!70] (v) edge[bend left=15] node {$\text{dist}_T[v]$} (t);

  % Alternative cross paths (dashed light)
  \draw[->, line width=1pt, dotted, draw=black!40] (s) edge[bend right=15] (v);
  \draw[->, line width=1pt, dotted, draw=black!40] (u) edge[bend right=15] (t);

  % Baseline path
  \draw[->, line width=1pt, dashdotted, draw=black!60] (s) -- node[below] {Original $\text{dist}_G(s,t)$} (t);
  
\end{tikzpicture}
\\ \textit{Figure 1: Distance decomposition using candidate road $e' = (u,v)$.}
\end{center}

---

\subsection*{2. Algorithm Steps}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Forward Dijkstra Pass:} Run a standard single-source shortest path Dijkstra algorithm on $G$ starting from the source city $s$. Store the output weights in a lookup array $\text{dist}_S$, where $\text{dist}_S[x] = \text{dist}_G(s, x)$ for all $x \in V$.
    \item \textbf{Backward Dijkstra Pass:} Run Dijkstra's algorithm on $G$ starting from the destination city $t$. Store the output weights in a lookup array $\text{dist}_T$, where $\text{dist}_T[x] = \text{dist}_G(x, t)$ for all $x \in V$.
    \item \textbf{Candidate Evaluation Loop:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Initialize the optimal distance variables: $D_{\text{best}} \gets \text{dist}_S[t]$ and $\text{best\_road} \gets \text{NIL}$.
        \item Iterate through every structural choice $e' = (u, v) \in E'$ with length $l_{e'}$. Compute:
        $$C_1 = \text{dist}_S[u] + l_{e'} + \text{dist}_T[v] \quad (\text{Path via } u \to v)$$
        $$C_2 = \text{dist}_S[v] + l_{e'} + \text{dist}_T[u] \quad (\text{Path via } v \to u)$$
        $$C_{\text{min}} = \min(C_1, C_2)$$
        \item If $C_{\text{min}} < D_{\text{best}}$, update $D_{\text{best}} \gets C_{\text{min}}$ and store $\text{best\_road} \gets e'$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Output:} Return $\text{best\_road}$ along with the maximum metric reduction value: $\text{dist}_S[t] - D_{\text{best}}$.
\end{enumerate}

---

\subsection*{3. Pseudocode}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Optimal Road Addition Optimizer}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Statex \textbf{Input:} Graph $G=(V,E)$ with lengths $l$, proposed set $E'$ with lengths $l'$, source $s$, destination $t$.
\Statex \textbf{Output:} The optimal road $e^* \in E'$ and its respective maximum distance reduction.
\State $\text{dist}_S \gets \Call{Dijkstra}{G, l, s}$ \Comment{O($(V+E)\log V$)}
\State $\text{dist}_T \gets \Call{Dijkstra}{G, l, t}$ \Comment{O($(V+E)\log V$)}
\State $D_{\text{best}} \gets \text{dist}_S[t]$
\State $\text{best\_road} \gets \text{NIL}$
\For{each road $e' = (u, v) \in E'$}
    \State $C_1 \gets \text{dist}_S[u] + l'_{e'} + \text{dist}_T[v]$
    \State $C_2 \gets \text{dist}_S[v] + l'_{e'} + \text{dist}_T[u]$
    \State $C_{\text{min}} \gets \min(C_1, C_2) $
    \If{$C_{\text{min}} < D_{\text{best}}$}
        \State $D_{\text{best}} \gets C_{\text{min}}$
        \State $\text{best\_road} \gets e'$
    \EndIf
\EndFor
\State $\text{max\_decrease} \gets \text{dist}_S[t] - D_{\text{best}}$
\State \Return $(\text{best\_road}, \text{max\_decrease})$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

---

\subsection*{4. Complexity Analysis}

\subsubsection*{Time Complexity:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Dijkstra Runs:} Executing two passes of binary-heap-based Dijkstra takes:
    $$2 \times \mathcal{O}((V + E) \log V) = \mathcal{O}((V + E) \log V)$$
    \item \textbf{Evaluation Loop:} The loop executes exactly $|E'|$ times. Within each iteration, array lookups and arithmetic comparison operations take $\mathcal{O}(1)$ static time. Thus, the evaluation overhead is bounded strictly by $\mathcal{O}(|E'|)$.
\end{itemize}
Summing these execution blocks together gives the total runtime complexity:
$$T = \mathcal{O}((V + E)\log V + E')$$
Since $|E'| \le |E'| \log |V|$, this cleanly aggregates into the required theoretical target:
$$\textbf{Total Time Complexity: } \mathcal{O}((V + E + E') \log V)$$

\subsubsection*{Space Complexity:}
The distance maps $\text{dist}_S$ and $\text{dist}_T$ each consume $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ memory space. Graph representations and input collections dictate $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E| + |E'|)$ memory overhead.
$$\textbf{Total Space Complexity: } \mathcal{O}(V + E + E')$$

\end{tcolorbox} 

\item Find a minimum spanning tree by running (i) Kruskal's algorithm and (ii) Prim's algorithm. Show the order of edges selected in each case (use alphabetical ordering when there is a tie). \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2015--16}

\begin{center}

  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none,  font=\normalfont\small},
  ]

  \node (d) at (3, 2)   {d};
  \node (g) at (7, 2)   {g};
  \node (b) at (0, -3)   {b};
  \node (c) at (3, -0.5) {c};
  \node (f) at (7, -0.5) {f};
  \node (e) at (7, -3)   {e};

  \draw (a) -- node[edge label, above]       {3} (d);
  \draw (a) -- node[edge label, left]        {2} (b);
  \draw (a) -- node[edge label, above right] {3} (c);
  \draw (d) -- node[edge label, right]       {5} (c);
  \draw (d) -- node[edge label, above right] {7} (f);
  \draw (g) -- node[edge label, right]       {9} (f);
  \draw (b) -- node[edge label, below right] {4} (c);
  \draw (b) -- node[edge label, below]       {3} (e);
  \draw (c) -- node[edge label, below right] {1} (e);
  \draw (f) -- node[edge label, right]       {8} (e);

  \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,
  boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,
  title={\textbf{Detailed Answer: Kruskal's \& Prim's Algorithm}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

}

\section*{Part 1: Kruskal's Algorithm}
\textbf{Step 1: Sort edges by weight.} When weights are equal, sort alphabetically (e.g., $ac$ before $ad$).\\
Sorted list: $ce(1), ab(2), ac(3), ad(3), be(3), bc(4), cd(5), df(7), ef(8), fg(9)$.

\section*{Part 1: Kruskal's Algorithm Step-by-Step}
\textbf{Step 1: Sort edges by weight.} When weights are equal, sort alphabetically (e.g., $ac$ before $ad$).\\
Sorted list: $ce(1), ab(2), ac(3), ad(3), be(3), bc(4), cd(5), df(7), ef(8), fg(9)$.

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{llcl}
\toprule
\textbf{Edge} & \textbf{Weight} & \textbf{Add to MST?} & \textbf{Reason} \\
\midrule
$c$--$e$ & 1 & \textbf{Yes} & No cycle \\
$a$--$b$ & 2 & \textbf{Yes} & No cycle \\
$a$--$c$ & 3 & \textbf{Yes} & No cycle \\
$a$--$d$ & 3 & \textbf{Yes} & No cycle \\
$b$--$e$ & 3 & \textcolor{red}{No}  & Forms cycle ($b$-$a$-$c$-$e$-$b$) \\
$b$--$c$ & 4 & \textcolor{red}{No}  & Forms cycle ($b$-$a$-$c$-$b$) \\
$c$--$d$ & 5 & \textcolor{red}{No}  & Forms cycle ($c$-$a$-$d$-$c$) \\
$d$--$f$ & 7 & \textbf{Yes} & No cycle \\
$e$--$f$ & 8 & \textcolor{red}{No}  & Forms cycle ($e$-$c$-$a$-$d$-$f$-$e$) \\
$f$--$g$ & 9 & \textbf{Yes} & No cycle. \textbf{Done} ($|V|-1 = 6$ edges) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\section*{Part 2: Prim's Algorithm (Using Key Values)}
Start at vertex \textbf{a}. We maintain a \textbf{Key} value for each vertex (initialized to $\infty$, meaning unreachable) and its \textbf{Parent}. In each step, we extract the unvisited vertex with the minimum key. Ties are broken alphabetically.

\midrule
0 & Initial State & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ \\
1 & \textbf{a} & $2(a)$ & $3(a)$ & $3(a)$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ \\
2 & \textbf{b} & - & $3(a)$ & $3(a)$ & $3(b)$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ \\
3 & \textbf{c} & - & - & $3(a)$ & $\mathbf{1(c)}$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ \\
4 & \textbf{e} & - & - & $3(a)$ & - & $8(e)$ & $\infty$ \\
5 & \textbf{d} & - & - & - & - & $\mathbf{7(d)}$ & $\infty$ \\
6 & \textbf{f} & - & - & - & - & - & $\mathbf{9(f)}$ \\

\item Let $G = (V, E)$ be a connected undirected graph with weight function $w$ on $E$. Let $A \subseteq E$ be included in some MST for $G$, let $(S, V-S)$ be any cut that respects $A$, and let $(u, v)$ be a light edge crossing $(S, V-S)$. Prove that $(u, v)$ is safe for $A$. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Definitions and Terminology}

Before beginning the proof, let us establish the precise mathematical definitions from the theorem statement:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Cut:} A partition of the vertex set $V$ into two disjoint, non-empty sets $S$ and $V - S$.
    \item \textbf{Crossing the Cut:} An edge $(x, y)$ crosses the cut $(S, V - S)$ if one of its endpoints is in $S$ and the other endpoint is in $V - S$.
    \item \textbf{Respects:} A cut $(S, V - S)$ respects a set of edges $A$ if no edge in $A$ crosses the cut.
    \item \textbf{Light Edge:} An edge $(u, v)$ is a light edge crossing a cut if its weight $w(u,v)$ is minimized among all edges crossing that specific cut.
    \item \textbf{Safe Edge:} An edge $(u, v)$ is safe for $A$ if the union $A \cup \{(u, v)\}$ is a subset of some valid Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) for $G$.
\end{itemize}

---

\subsection*{2. The Proof (Cut-Exchange Argument)}

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Establish the Baseline MST}
By the theorem's premise, the edge set $A$ is included in some Minimum Spanning Tree of $G$. Let us denote this baseline MST as $T$. Therefore:
$$A \subseteq T$$

We must demonstrate that there exists \textit{some} MST $T'$ (which could be $T$ itself or a newly constructed MST) such that $A \cup \{(u, v)\} \subseteq T'$. We analyze two mutually exclusive scenarios:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Case 1: $(u, v) \in T$} \\
    If the light edge $(u, v)$ is already an edge in our chosen MST $T$, then $A \cup \{(u, v)\} \subseteq T$ holds immediately. Thus, $(u, v)$ is trivially safe for $A$, and the proof concludes for this case.
    
    \item \textbf{Case 2: $(u, v) \notin T$} \\
    If $(u, v)$ is not present in $T$, we must mathematically construct an alternative minimum spanning tree $T'$ that encompasses both $A$ and $(u, v)$.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.2, auto, >=stealth, thick,
  node/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=gray!10, minimum size=7mm, font=\bfseries}]
  
  % Cut Partition S
  \draw[fill=blue!5, dashed, draw=blue!40, line width=1.2pt] (0, 1.5) ellipse (1.8cm and 2.2cm);
  \node[text=blue!80!black] at (0, 3.4) {\textbf{Set $S$}};
  
  % Cut Partition V - S
  \draw[fill=red!5, dashed, draw=red!40, line width=1.2pt] (5, 1.5) ellipse (1.8cm and 2.2cm);
  \node[text=red!80!black] at (5, 3.4) {\textbf{Set $V - S$}};
  
  % Nodes
  \node[node] (u) at (0.5, 2.3) {u};
  \node[node] (v) at (4.5, 2.3) {v};
  \node[node] (x) at (0.5, 0.7) {x};
  \node[node] (y) at (4.5, 0.7) {y};
  
  % Edges
  \draw[-, line width=2pt, cA] (u) -- node[above, text=black] {$(u, v)$ (Light Edge)} (v);
  \draw[draw=black!40, line width=1.5pt, dashed] (u) -- node[left] {Path in $T$} (x);
  \draw[-, line width=2pt, orange] (x) -- node[below, text=black] {$(x, y) \in T$} (y);
  \draw[draw=black!40, line width=1.5pt, dashed] (y) -- node[right] {Path in $T$} (v);

\end{tikzpicture}
\\ \textit{Figure 1: Cycle exchange mechanism illustrating $(u,v)$ and $(x,y)$ crossing the cut.}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Cycle Creation via Edge Insertion}
Because $T$ is a spanning tree, it provides exactly one unique, simple path between any pair of vertices. Inserting the edge $(u, v)$ into $T$ creates exactly one unique, simple cycle $C$ containing the edge $(u, v)$.

\subsubsection*{Step 3: Locating an Alternative Crossing Edge}
The edge $(u, v)$ crosses the cut $(S, V - S)$ because $u \in S$ and $v \in V - S$. A continuous cycle cannot cross a boundary exactly once. Since the cycle $C$ travels from $S$ to $V - S$ via $(u, v)$ and must eventually return to $u \in S$, there must exist at least one other edge $(x, y)$ in the cycle $C$ that crosses the cut back from $S$ to $V - S$ (where $x \in S$ and $y \in V - S$).

\subsubsection*{Step 4: Comparing Edge Properties}
We can deduce two crucial facts regarding this alternative crossing edge $(x, y)$:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{It cannot belong to $A$:} The theorem states that the cut $(S, V - S)$ \textit{respects} $A$, meaning no edge in $A$ crosses the cut. Since $(x, y)$ crosses the cut, it is mathematically impossible for $(x, y)$ to be an element of $A$. Hence, $(x, y) \notin A$.
    \item \textbf{Weight relationship:} The theorem states that $(u, v)$ is a \textit{light edge} crossing the cut. This implies its weight is less than or equal to any other edge crossing this specific cut. Because $(x, y)$ is also a crossing edge, it must be true that:
    $$w(u, v) \le w(x, y)$$
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Step 5: Constructing the New Tree $T'$}
We construct a new edge set $T'$ by executing an exchange operation: we add the light edge $(u, v)$ to $T$ and remove the alternative crossing edge $(x, y)$.
$$T' = T \setminus \{(x, y)\} \cup \{(u, v)\}$$

Let us verify the mathematical validity of $T'$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Spanning Tree Validity:} Removing $(x, y)$ breaks the unique cycle $C$ created by inserting $(u, v)$. The resulting graph remains fully connected, contains no cycles, and spans $V$. Thus, $T'$ is a valid spanning tree.
    \item \textbf{Minimality Analysis:} We calculate the total weight of $T'$ relative to $T$:
    $$w(T') = w(T) - w(x, y) + w(u, v)$$
    Since $w(u, v) \le w(x, y)$, substituting this yields:
    $$w(T') \le w(T)$$
    However, $T$ was defined as a \textit{Minimum} Spanning Tree, meaning no spanning tree can have a weight strictly less than $w(T)$. Therefore, the weights must be exactly equal:
    $$w(T') = w(T)$$
\end{itemize}
This proves that $T'$ is also a valid Minimum Spanning Tree of $G$.

---

\subsection*{3. Conclusion}

We have successfully constructed an alternative MST $T'$ such that:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $(u, v) \in T'$ by explicit insertion.
    \item $A \subseteq T'$ because $A \subseteq T$, and the only edge removed from $T$ to build $T'$ was $(x, y)$, which we proved in Step 4 was not an element of $A$.
\end{enumerate}

Consequently, $A \cup \{(u, v)\} \subseteq T'$. By definition, this conclusively proves that the light edge $(u, v)$ is \textbf{safe} for $A$. \hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Find the light and respectful crossing edges for each step of Kruskal's method for building the MST.

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small},
  ]

  \node (a) at (0,   0)    {a};
  \node (b) at (2,   3)    {b};
  \node (c) at (1.5, -2)   {c};
  \node (d) at (4,   0)    {d};
  \node (e) at (4,  -2.5)  {e};
  \node (f) at (6.5, -1)   {f};
  \node (g) at (6.5,  2.5) {g};
  \node (h) at (8.5,  1)   {h};

  \draw (a) -- node[edge label, above left]  {3}  (b);
  \draw (a) -- node[edge label, above]       {5}  (d);
  \draw (a) -- node[edge label, below left]  {2}  (c);
  \draw (b) -- node[edge label, right]       {2}  (d);
  \draw (b) -- node[edge label, above]       {13} (g);
  \draw (c) -- node[edge label, below] {2}  (d);
  \draw (c) -- node[edge label, below]       {5}  (e);
  \draw (d) -- node[edge label, above right] {6}  (g);
  \draw (d) -- node[edge label, above]       {3}  (f);
  \draw (d) -- node[edge label, right]       {4}  (e);
  \draw (e) -- node[edge label, below right] {6}  (f);
  \draw (g) -- node[edge label, right]       {2}  (f);
  \draw (g) -- node[edge label, above right] {3}  (h);
  \draw (h) -- node[edge label, right]       {6}  (f);

  \end{tikzpicture}

\end{center}

\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

To trace Kruskal's algorithm and find a corresponding **respectful** and **light** crossing edge for each successful step, we first list all the edges of the graph sorted in non-decreasing order of their weights:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{cll}
\hline
\textbf{Weight} & \textbf{Edges} \\ \hline
\textbf{2} & $(a, c)$, $(b, d)$, $(c, d)$, $(g, f)$ \\
\textbf{3} & $(a, b)$, $(d, f)$, $(g, h)$ \\
\textbf{4} & $(d, e)$ \\
\textbf{5} & $(a, d)$, $(c, e)$ \\
\textbf{6} & $(d, g)$, $(e, f)$, $(h, f)$ \\
\textbf{13} & $(b, g)$ \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Let $A$ denote the growing set of edges in the MST forest. Initially, $A = \emptyset$. For each step where an edge is accepted, we define a cut $(S, V \setminus S)$ that **respects** $A$ (no edges in $A$ cross it) and for which the chosen edge is a **light edge** (has the minimum weight among all edges crossing that cut).

---

\subsection*{Step-by-Step Execution of Kruskal's Algorithm}

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Consider edge $(a, c)$ with weight 2}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Action:} Accept (does not form a cycle). New forest $A = \{(a, c)\}$.
    \item \textbf{Respectful Cut:} Let $S = \{a\}$, so $V \setminus S = \{b, c, d, e, f, g, h\}$. This respects $A = \emptyset$.
    \item \textbf{Crossing Edges:} $\{(a, b): 3, \; (a, d): 5, \; (a, c): 2\}$.
    \item \textbf{Light Edge:} $(a, c)$ has the minimum weight (2) crossing this cut.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Consider edge $(b, d)$ with weight 2}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Action:} Accept (does not form a cycle). New forest $A = \{(a, c), (b, d)\}$.
    \item \textbf{Respectful Cut:} Let $S = \{b\}$, so $V \setminus S = \{a, c, d, e, f, g, h\}$. This respects $A$ because the only existing edge $(a, c)$ has both endpoints in $V \setminus S$.
    \item \textbf{Crossing Edges:} $\{(b, a): 3, \; (b, d): 2, \; (b, g): 13\}$.
    \item \textbf{Light Edge:} $(b, d)$ has the minimum weight (2) crossing this cut.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 3: Consider edge $(c, d)$ with weight 2}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Action:} Accept (connects components $\{a, c\}$ and $\{b, d\}$). New forest $A = \{(a, c), (b, d), (c, d)\}$.
    \item \textbf{Respectful Cut:} Let $S = \{a, c\}$, so $V \setminus S = \{b, d, e, f, g, h\}$. This respects $A$ because $(a, c)$ lies entirely within $S$, and $(b, d)$ lies entirely within $V \setminus S$.
    \item \textbf{Crossing Edges:} $\{(a, b): 3, \; (a, d): 5, \; (c, d): 2, \; (c, e): 5\}$.
    \item \textbf{Light Edge:} $(c, d)$ has the minimum weight (2) crossing this cut.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 4: Consider edge $(g, f)$ with weight 2}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Action:} Accept (does not form a cycle). New forest $A = \{(a, c), (b, d), (c, d), (g, f)\}$.
    \item \textbf{Respectful Cut:} Let $S = \{g\}$, so $V \setminus S = \{a, b, c, d, e, f, h\}$. This respects $A$ because all three existing edges lie entirely within $V \setminus S$.
    \item \textbf{Crossing Edges:} $\{(g, b): 13, \; (g, d): 6, \; (g, f): 2, \; (g, h): 3\}$.
    \item \textbf{Light Edge:} $(g, f)$ has the minimum weight (2) crossing this cut.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 5: Consider edge $(a, b)$ with weight 3}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Action:} \textbf{Reject}. Vertices $a$ and $b$ already belong to the same connected component $\{a, b, c, d\}$, so adding it would form a cycle.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 6: Consider edge $(d, f)$ with weight 3}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Action:} Accept (connects components $\{a, b, c, d\}$ and $\{f, g\}$). New forest $A = \{(a, c), (b, d), (c, d), (g, f), (d, f)\}$.
    \item \textbf{Respectful Cut:} Let $S = \{f, g\}$, so $V \setminus S = \{a, b, c, d, e, h\}$. This cut respects $A$ because prior edges are either entirely in $V \setminus S$ or entirely in $S$ [edge $(g, f)$].
    \item \textbf{Crossing Edges:} $\{(f, d): 3, \; (f, e): 6, \; (f, h): 6, \; (g, b): 13, \; (g, d): 6, \; (g, h): 3\}$.
    \item \textbf{Light Edge:} $(d, f)$ is a light edge because its weight (3) is tied for the minimum crossing weight along with $(g, h)$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 7: Consider edge $(g, h)$ with weight 3}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Action:} Accept (connects component $\{a, b, c, d, f, g\}$ to $\{h\}$). New forest $A = \dots \cup \{(g, h)\}$.
    \item \textbf{Respectful Cut:} Let $S = \{h\}$, so $V \setminus S = \{a, b, c, d, e, f, g\}$. This respects $A$ because all prior edges avoid vertex $h$ entirely and remain within $V \setminus S$.
    \item \textbf{Crossing Edges:} $\{(h, g): 3, \; (h, f): 6\}$.
    \item \textbf{Light Edge:} $(g, h)$ has the minimum weight (3) crossing this cut.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 8: Consider edge $(d, e)$ with weight 4}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Action:} Accept (connects the main component to $\{e\}$). New forest $A = \dots \cup \{(d, e)\}$.
    \item \textbf{Respectful Cut:} Let $S = \{e\}$, so $V \setminus S = \{a, b, c, d, f, g, h\}$. This respects $A$ because all previously selected edges avoid node $e$ and reside entirely inside $V \setminus S$.
    \item \textbf{Crossing Edges:} $\{(e, c): 5, \; (e, d): 4, \; (e, f): 6\}$.
    \item \textbf{Light Edge:} $(d, e)$ has the minimum weight (4) crossing this cut.
\end{itemize}

---

\subsection*{Summary of the Final MST Edge Selection Sequence}

The algorithm terminates here because we have successfully selected $|V| - 1 = 7$ edges, spanning all 8 vertices without any cycles.

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccccc}
\hline
\textbf{Kruskal Step} & \textbf{Edge Selected} & \textbf{Weight} & \textbf{Cut Used ($S$)} & \textbf{Light Edge Verification} \\ \hline
1 & $(a, c)$ & 2 & $\{a\}$ & $2 = \min(3, 5, 2)$ \\
2 & $(b, d)$ & 2 & $\{b\}$ & $2 = \min(3, 2, 13)$ \\
3 & $(c, d)$ & 2 & $\{a, c\}$ & $2 = \min(3, 5, 2, 5)$ \\
4 & $(g, f)$ & 2 & $\{g\}$ & $2 = \min(13, 6, 2, 3)$ \\
5 & $(d, f)$ & 3 & $\{f, g\}$ & $3 = \min(3, 6, 6, 13, 6, 3)$ \\
6 & $(g, h)$ & 3 & $\{h\}$ & $3 = \min(3, 6)$ \\
7 & $(d, e)$ & 4 & $\{e\}$ & $4 = \min(5, 4, 6)$ \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What is the cost of finding and joining (union) of sets in Kruskal's method? \mk{8}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. The Disjoint-Set (Union-Find) Data Structure}

In Kruskal's algorithm, the problem of detecting whether adding an edge creates a cycle is solved using a \textbf{Disjoint-Set} (or Union-Find) data structure. This structure maintains a collection of disjoint dynamic sets, where each set represents a connected component in the growing spanning forest.



The algorithm relies on three fundamental operations:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Make-Set(v)}:} Creates a new set containing the single vertex $v$.
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Find-Set(v)}:} Returns the representative (or root) element of the set containing $v$. If two vertices yield the same representative, they belong to the same component (and connecting them would form a cycle).
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Union(u, v)}:} Merges the two sets containing $u$ and $v$ into a single connected component.
\end{itemize}

---

\subsection*{2. Total Operation Counts in Kruskal's Algorithm}

Let $|V|$ be the number of vertices and $|E|$ be the number of edges in the graph. During the execution of Kruskal's algorithm, the operations are called as follows:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Make-Set}:} Executed exactly once for each vertex during initialization. Total calls: $|V|$.
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Find-Set}:} Executed twice for every edge in the graph (once for each endpoint) when iterating through the sorted edge list to check for cycles. Total calls: $2|E|$.
    \item \textbf{\texttt{Union}:} Executed only when a valid, cycle-free edge is added to the Minimum Spanning Tree. Since a spanning tree has exactly $|V| - 1$ edges, this is called at most $|V| - 1$ times.
\end{enumerate}
Thus, we are executing a sequence of $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ total operations, which simplifies to $\mathcal{O}(|E|)$ operations since the graph is connected and $|E| \ge |V| - 1$.

---

\subsection*{3. Core Optimizations}

To achieve maximum efficiency, two critical heuristics are applied to the Disjoint-Set data structure:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Union by Rank / Size:} When merging two trees, the root of the smaller tree is made a child of the root of the larger tree. This prevents the trees from becoming heavily unbalanced linear chains, keeping the maximum depth logarithmic.
    \item \textbf{Path Compression:} During a \texttt{Find-Set} traversal, every node visited along the path to the root is directly attached to the root. This flattens the tree structure, making subsequent lookups for those nodes occur in effectively constant time.
\end{itemize}

---

\subsection*{4. Exact Time Complexity Cost}

When both \textbf{Union by Rank} and \textbf{Path Compression} are implemented, the mathematical bounds become extremely tight. 

A sequence of $m$ operations on $n$ elements takes total time:
$$\mathcal{O}(m \alpha(n))$$
where $\alpha(n)$ is the \textbf{inverse Ackermann function}.

Substituting our algorithm's parameters ($m = |E|$ operations on $n = |V|$ vertices), the total cost of all finding and joining operations in Kruskal's algorithm is:
$$\textbf{Total Cost: } \mathcal{O}(|E| \alpha(|V|))$$

\subsubsection*{Practical Significance of $\alpha(|V|)$}
The inverse Ackermann function $\alpha(n)$ grows incredibly slowly. For any practical graph that could theoretically exist inside the observable universe (up to $n = 2^{2^{65536}}$ vertices), the value of $\alpha(n)$ is strictly less than or equal to $4$. 

Therefore, for all practical computing purposes, $\alpha(|V|)$ behaves as an absolute constant $\mathcal{O}(1)$. Consequently, the \textit{amortized} cost per individual \texttt{Find-Set} or \texttt{Union} operation is $\mathcal{O}(1)$, making the practical total cost of maintaining the disjoint sets directly proportional to the number of edges:
$$\textbf{Practical Total Cost: } \mathcal{O}(|E|)$$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item For the graph below, show the value of $u$, $v.key$, $Q$ for the following algorithm (show each step):

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{\textsc{MST}($G,\, w,\, r$)}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State $Q \gets V[G]$
\For{each $u \in Q$}
    \State $u.key \gets \infty$
\EndFor
\State $r.key \gets 0$;\quad $p[r] \gets \textbf{nil}$
\While{$Q \neq \emptyset$}
    \State $u \gets \textsc{Extract-Min}(Q)$
    \For{each $v \in Adj[u]$}
        \If{$v \in Q$ \textbf{and} $w(u,v) < v.key$}
            \State $p[v] \gets u$
            \State $v.key \gets w(u,v)$
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndWhile
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small},
  ]

  \node (a) at (0,   0)    {a};
  \node (b) at (2,   3)    {b};
  \node (c) at (1.5, -2)   {c};
  \node (d) at (4,   0)    {d};
  \node (e) at (4,  -2.5)  {e};
  \node (f) at (6.5, -1)   {f};
  \node (g) at (6.5,  2.5) {g};
  \node (h) at (8.5,  1)   {h};

  \draw (a) -- node[edge label, above left]  {3}  (b);
  \draw (a) -- node[edge label, above]       {5}  (d);
  \draw (a) -- node[edge label, below left]  {2}  (c);
  \draw (b) -- node[edge label, right]       {2}  (d);
  \draw (b) -- node[edge label, above]       {13} (g);
  \draw (c) -- node[edge label, below] {2}  (d);
  \draw (c) -- node[edge label, below]       {5}  (e);
  \draw (d) -- node[edge label, above right] {6}  (g);
  \draw (d) -- node[edge label, above]       {3}  (f);
  \draw (d) -- node[edge label, right]       {4}  (e);
  \draw (e) -- node[edge label, below right] {6}  (f);
  \draw (g) -- node[edge label, right]       {2}  (f);
  \draw (g) -- node[edge label, above right] {3}  (h);
  \draw (h) -- node[edge label, right]       {6}  (f);

  \end{tikzpicture}

\end{center}

\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{Algorithm Trace: Prim's Minimum Spanning Tree}

The given pseudocode executes \textbf{Prim's Algorithm}. Since the root vertex $r$ is not explicitly specified in the prompt, we will use standard convention and assume $r = a$ as the starting node.

\subsubsection*{Initialization (Lines 1-4)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $Q$ is initialized with all vertices: $Q = \{a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h\}$
    \item All keys are set to $\infty$, except $a.key = 0$.
\end{itemize}

---

\subsubsection*{Step-by-Step Execution (Lines 5-11)}

At each step, we extract the vertex $u$ with the minimum key from $Q$. Then, we look at its adjacent neighbors $v$. If $v \in Q$ and the edge weight $w(u,v) < v.key$, we update $v.key$ to $w(u,v)$.

\textbf{Step 1:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Extract-Min:} $u = a$ (key $= 0$)
    \item \textbf{Remaining $Q$:} $\{b, c, d, e, f, g, h\}$
    \item \textbf{Neighbors of $a$ in $Q$:} $b$ (weight 3), $c$ (weight 2), $d$ (weight 5).
    \item \textbf{Updates:} 
    $b.key \gets 3$, \quad $c.key \gets 2$, \quad $d.key \gets 5$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Step 2:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Extract-Min:} $u = c$ (minimum key in $Q$ is $2$)
    \item \textbf{Remaining $Q$:} $\{b, d, e, f, g, h\}$
    \item \textbf{Neighbors of $c$ in $Q$:} $d$ (weight 2), $e$ (weight 5).
    \item \textbf{Updates:} 
    $d.key \gets \min(5, 2) = 2$, \quad $e.key \gets 5$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Step 3:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Extract-Min:} $u = d$ (minimum key in $Q$ is $2$) \textit{Note: $b$ also has key 2, either can be picked; choosing $d$ first.}
    \item \textbf{Remaining $Q$:} $\{b, e, f, g, h\}$
    \item \textbf{Neighbors of $d$ in $Q$:} $b$ (weight 2), $e$ (weight 4), $f$ (weight 3), $g$ (weight 6).
    \item \textbf{Updates:}
    $b.key \gets \min(3, 2) = 2$, \quad $e.key \gets \min(5, 4) = 4$, \\
    $f.key \gets 3$, \quad $g.key \gets 6$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Step 4:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Extract-Min:} $u = b$ (minimum key in $Q$ is $2$)
    \item \textbf{Remaining $Q$:} $\{e, f, g, h\}$
    \item \textbf{Neighbors of $b$ in $Q$:} $g$ (weight 13).
    \item \textbf{Updates:} $13 \not< 6$, so $g.key$ remains $6$. No updates.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Step 5:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Extract-Min:} $u = f$ (minimum key in $Q$ is $3$)
    \item \textbf{Remaining $Q$:} $\{e, g, h\}$
    \item \textbf{Neighbors of $f$ in $Q$:} $e$ (weight 6), $g$ (weight 2), $h$ (weight 6).
    \item \textbf{Updates:} 
    $e.key \gets \min(4, 6) = 4$ (no change), \\
    $g.key \gets \min(6, 2) = 2$, \quad $h.key \gets 6$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Step 6:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Extract-Min:} $u = g$ (minimum key in $Q$ is $2$)
    \item \textbf{Remaining $Q$:} $\{e, h\}$
    \item \textbf{Neighbors of $g$ in $Q$:} $h$ (weight 3).
    \item \textbf{Updates:} $h.key \gets \min(6, 3) = 3$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Step 7:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Extract-Min:} $u = h$ (minimum key in $Q$ is $3$)
    \item \textbf{Remaining $Q$:} $\{e\}$
    \item \textbf{Neighbors of $h$ in $Q$:} None in $Q$.
    \item \textbf{Updates:} None.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Step 8:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Extract-Min:} $u = e$ (minimum key in $Q$ is $4$)
    \item \textbf{Remaining $Q$:} $\emptyset$ (Empty)
    \item \textbf{Neighbors of $e$ in $Q$:} None.
    \item \textbf{Updates:} None.
\end{itemize}

---

\subsubsection*{Summary State Table}

The table below summarizes the value of $Q$ prior to extraction, the extracted node $u$, and the final state of all $v.key$ values at the end of that step. Once a vertex is extracted from $Q$, its key is fixed (denoted with an asterisk $*$).

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{llcccccccc}
\hline
\textbf{Extracted} $\mathbf{u}$ & \textbf{Set} $\mathbf{Q}$ \textbf{after extraction} & \textbf{a} & \textbf{b} & \textbf{c} & \textbf{d} & \textbf{e} & \textbf{f} & \textbf{g} & \textbf{h} \\ \hline
\textit{(Init)} & $\{a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h\}$ & 0 & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ \\
$\mathbf{a}$ & $\{b, c, d, e, f, g, h\}$ & 0* & 3 & 2 & 5 & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ \\
$\mathbf{c}$ & $\{b, d, e, f, g, h\}$ & 0* & 3 & 2* & 2 & 5 & $\infty$ & $\infty$ & $\infty$ \\
$\mathbf{d}$ & $\{b, e, f, g, h\}$ & 0* & 2 & 2* & 2* & 4 & 3 & 6 & $\infty$ \\
$\mathbf{b}$ & $\{e, f, g, h\}$ & 0* & 2* & 2* & 2* & 4 & 3 & 6 & $\infty$ \\
$\mathbf{f}$ & $\{e, g, h\}$ & 0* & 2* & 2* & 2* & 4 & 3* & 2 & 6 \\
$\mathbf{g}$ & $\{e, h\}$ & 0* & 2* & 2* & 2* & 4 & 3* & 2* & 3 \\
$\mathbf{h}$ & $\{e\}$ & 0* & 2* & 2* & 2* & 4 & 3* & 2* & 3* \\
$\mathbf{e}$ & $\emptyset$ & 0* & 2* & 2* & 2* & 4* & 3* & 2* & 3* \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item  \textit{GreedyCell}, a mobile phone operator, wants to place cell towers 
(base stations) along a long straight country road, to provide network coverage to $n$ 
houses sparsely scattered along the road. The distance of these houses from the start 
of the road are, in miles and in increasing order, $d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_n$. Each cell 
tower has coverage of $R$ miles, i.e., if a house is within $R$ miles of a tower, it 
would get service from that tower. Now give an efficient algorithm for finding a 
placement of cell towers that uses as few cell towers as possible to provide coverage 
to all the $n$ houses.\mk{12}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Core Insight: The Greedy Strategy}

This problem is a classic application of the \textbf{1D Interval Covering} greedy algorithm. 

To minimize the number of cell towers, each tower should cover as much "new" ground as possible. If we look at the first uncovered house at distance $d_1$, a tower must be placed within $R$ miles of it to provide service. 

To maximize the coverage for the remaining houses further down the road, we should place this tower as far to the right of $d_1$ as possible. Therefore, the optimal placement for the first tower is exactly at $p_1 = d_1 + R$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item This tower will cover all houses located in the interval $[d_1, d_1 + 2R]$.
    \item We then find the next house that falls strictly outside this coverage range (i.e., $d_i > d_1 + 2R$) and repeat the exact same process by placing the next tower at $d_i + R$.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[xscale=0.9, thick, auto, >=stealth]
    % Road Axis
    \draw[->, line width=1pt] (0,0) -- (11,0) node[right, font=\bfseries] {Road (miles)};
    
    % Houses
    \fill[black] (1,0) circle (3pt) node[below=3pt, font=\bfseries] {$d_1$};
    \fill[black] (2.2,0) circle (3pt) node[below=3pt] {$d_2$};
    \fill[black] (4.5,0) circle (3pt) node[below=3pt] {$d_3$};
    
    \fill[black] (7.5,0) circle (3pt) node[below=3pt, font=\bfseries] {$d_4$};
    \fill[black] (10,0) circle (3pt) node[below=3pt] {$d_5$};

    % Tower 1 and coverage
    \draw[cA, line width=2pt] (3, 0) -- (3, 1.2) node[above, text=black, font=\bfseries] {Tower 1 at $d_1 + R$};
    \fill[cA] (3, 1.2) circle (4pt);
    
    \draw[<->, cA!60, line width=1pt] (1, 0.6) -- (3, 0.6) node[midway, above, text=black] {$R$};
    \draw[<->, cA!60, line width=1pt] (3, 0.6) -- (5, 0.6) node[midway, above, text=black] {$R$};
    
    \draw[-, dashed, cA] (1, -0.8) -- (1, 1.2);
    \draw[-, dashed, cA] (5, -0.8) -- (5, 1.2);
    
    \draw[cA, thick, decorate, decoration={brace, amplitude=6pt, mirror}] (1,-1) -- (5,-1) node[midway, below=8pt] {Coverage: $[d_1, d_1 + 2R]$};

\end{tikzpicture}
\\ \vspace{0.5cm} \textit{Figure 1: Maximizing the greedy choice by placing the tower $R$ miles ahead of the leftmost uncovered house.}
\end{center}

---

\subsection*{2. Algorithm / Pseudocode}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Optimal Cell Tower Placement}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Statex \textbf{Input:} Array of house distances $D = [d_1, d_2, \dots, d_n]$ (sorted in increasing order), coverage radius $R$.
\Statex \textbf{Output:} List of optimal tower locations $T$.
\State $T \gets \emptyset$
\State $i \gets 1$
\While{$i \le n$}
    \State $p \gets d_i + R$ \Comment{Place tower to cover the leftmost uncovered house}
    \State $T.\text{append}(p)$
    \State $i \gets i + 1$
    \While{$i \le n$ \textbf{and} $d_i \le p + R$} \Comment{Skip all houses covered by this tower}
        \State $i \gets i + 1$
    \EndWhile
\EndWhile
\State \Return $T$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

---

\subsection*{3. Complexity Analysis}

\subsubsection*{Time Complexity:}
Although there is a nested \texttt{while} loop, notice that the index $i$ only ever moves forward. Every house in the array $D$ is evaluated exactly once and skipped over once it falls within a covered range. Since the array is already given in increasing order, no sorting is required.
$$\textbf{Total Time Complexity: } \mathcal{O}(n)$$

\subsubsection*{Space Complexity:}
The algorithm only requires a few variable counters ($i$, $p$) and a list $T$ to store the output coordinates of the towers. In the worst-case scenario (where houses are so far apart that every house requires its own tower), $T$ will store $n$ elements.
$$\textbf{Total Space Complexity: } \mathcal{O}(n)$$
\textit{(If excluding the output array size from auxiliary space, the auxiliary space is strictly $\mathcal{O}(1)$).}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Given a directed graph with edge list and weights $AB(5), BC(2), AC(4), AD(6),\\ CD(3), DB(1), DE(6), EB(3), EF(3), FB(4)$, construct a minimum-cost arborescence rooted at $A$. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2013--14}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

To construct the minimum-cost arborescence (directed minimum spanning tree) rooted at $A$, we apply the \textbf{Chu-Liu/Edmonds' Algorithm}. The algorithm iteratively reduces incoming edge weights to find a zero-weight spanning arborescence. If a cycle of zero-weight edges is formed, it contracts the cycle into a super-node and recursively repeats the process.

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Find Minimum Incoming Edge Weights}
First, we find the minimum weight of an incoming edge for every node except the root $A$. Let this minimum incoming weight for a node $v$ be denoted as $min\_in(v)$.

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{|c|l|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Node} & \textbf{Incoming Edges (Weight)} & $min\_in(v)$ \\
\hline
$B$ & $AB(5), DB(1), EB(3), FB(4)$ & $1$ (from $D$) \\
$C$ & $BC(2), AC(4)$ & $2$ (from $B$) \\
$D$ & $AD(6), CD(3)$ & $3$ (from $C$) \\
$E$ & $DE(6)$ & $6$ (from $D$) \\
$F$ & $EF(3)$ & $3$ (from $E$) \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Reduce Edge Weights and Find 0-Weight Cycle}
We subtract $min\_in(v)$ from the weight of every incoming edge to node $v$. The new reduced weight is $w'(u, v) = w(u, v) - min\_in(v)$. 
We then select one 0-weight incoming edge for each non-root node.

\begin{itemize}
    \item To $B$: $AB(4), \mathbf{DB(0)}, EB(2), FB(3)$
    \item To $C$: $\mathbf{BC(0)}, AC(2)$
    \item To $D$: $AD(3), \mathbf{CD(0)}$
    \item To $E$: $\mathbf{DE(0)}$
    \item To $F$: $\mathbf{EF(0)}$
\end{itemize}

Selecting the 0-weight edges $\{DB, BC, CD, DE, EF\}$ creates a \textbf{cycle}: $B \xrightarrow{0} C \xrightarrow{0} D \xrightarrow{0} B$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, node distance=2cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=8mm, fill=cA!10, thick},
    edge/.style={->, thick},
    cycle_edge/.style={->, very thick, red}]

    \node (A) at (0, 0) {A};
    \node (B) at (3, 1.5) {B};
    \node (C) at (3, -1.5) {C};
    \node (D) at (5.5, 0) {D};
    \node (E) at (8, 0) {E};
    \node (F) at (10.5, 0) {F};

    % Cycle edges
    \draw[cycle_edge] (D) -- node[above, black, fill=white, inner sep=1pt] {0 (orig 1)} (B);
    \draw[cycle_edge] (B) -- node[left, black, fill=white, inner sep=1pt] {0 (orig 2)} (C);
    \draw[cycle_edge] (C) -- node[below, black, fill=white, inner sep=1pt] {0 (orig 3)} (D);
    
    % Other 0-weight edges
    \draw[edge] (D) -- node[above, black, fill=white, inner sep=1pt] {0} (E);
    \draw[edge] (E) -- node[above, black, fill=white, inner sep=1pt] {0} (F);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 3: Contract the Cycle}
We contract the cycle $C_1 = \{B, C, D\}$ into a single super-node $X$. We must recalculate the reduced weights of edges entering and leaving $X$. 

\textbf{Edges entering $X$:}
The cost of an edge entering $X$ at a node $v \in C_1$ is its current reduced weight. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item $A \to X$ (via $B$): $w'(AB) = 4$
    \item $A \to X$ (via $C$): $w'(AC) = 2 \quad \leftarrow \text{Minimum incoming from } A$
    \item $A \to X$ (via $D$): $w'(AD) = 3$
    \item $E \to X$ (via $B$): $w'(EB) = 2$
    \item $F \to X$ (via $B$): $w'(FB) = 3$
\end{itemize}
The minimum weight of an edge entering $X$ is $2$ (specifically, $A \to C$ and $E \to B$). We subtract this new minimum ($2$) from all edges entering $X$. The newly reduced edge $A \to X$ (via $C$) now has weight $0$.

\textbf{Edges leaving $X$:}
The cost is simply the weight of the 0-weight edge leaving the component.
\begin{itemize}
    \item $X \to E$ (via $D \to E$): $w'(DE) = 0$
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 4: Arborescence in the Contracted Graph}
In the contracted graph with nodes $\{A, X, E, F\}$, the 0-weight edges are:
$A \xrightarrow{0} X$ (via $AC$), $X \xrightarrow{0} E$ (via $DE$), and $E \xrightarrow{0} F$. 
This forms a valid arborescence without any cycles.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, node distance=2.5cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=8mm, fill=cA!10, thick},
    edge/.style={->, thick}]

    \node (A) at (0, 0) {A};
    \node (X) at (3, 0) {X};
    \node (E) at (6, 0) {E};
    \node (F) at (9, 0) {F};

    \draw[edge, blue] (A) -- node[above, black] {0 (via AC)} (X);
    \draw[edge, blue] (X) -- node[above, black] {0 (via DE)} (E);
    \draw[edge, blue] (E) -- node[above, black] {0 (via EF)} (F);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 5: Expand the Cycle}
We expand super-node $X$ back to $B, C, D$. 
The chosen incoming edge to $X$ is $A \to C$. Because this edge enters the cycle at node $C$, we \textbf{must drop the cycle edge that enters $C$}. 
The cycle was $B \to C \to D \to B$. The edge entering $C$ in the cycle is $B \to C$. We drop $B \to C$ and keep the remaining cycle edges: $C \to D$ and $D \to B$.

\subsubsection*{Step 6: Final Arborescence and Cost Calculation}
The final edges in our Minimum-Cost Arborescence are:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $A \to C$ (Weight = 4)
    \item $C \to D$ (Weight = 3)
    \item $D \to B$ (Weight = 1)
    \item $D \to E$ (Weight = 6)
    \item $E \to F$ (Weight = 3)
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Total Minimum Cost} $= 4 + 3 + 1 + 6 + 3 = \mathbf{17}$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, node distance=2cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=8mm, fill=cA!10, thick},
    edge/.style={->, very thick, blue}]

    \node (A) at (0, 0) {A};
    \node (B) at (3, 1.5) {B};
    \node (C) at (3, -1.5) {C};
    \node (D) at (5.5, 0) {D};
    \node (E) at (8, 0) {E};
    \node (F) at (10.5, 0) {F};

    \draw[edge] (A) -- node[below left, black] {4} (C);
    \draw[edge] (C) -- node[below right, black] {3} (D);
    \draw[edge] (D) -- node[above left, black] {1} (B);
    \draw[edge] (D) -- node[above, black] {6} (E);
    \draw[edge] (E) -- node[above, black] {3} (F);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(V E)$. In the worst case, we might find a cycle and contract the graph $\mathcal{O}(V)$ times. In each iteration, finding the minimum incoming edges and updating weights takes $\mathcal{O}(E)$ time. (Note: using Fibonacci heaps and disjoint-set data structures, this can be optimized to $\mathcal{O}(E + V \log V)$).
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$ to store the graph representations, reduced edge weights, and tracking the parent pointers for cycles and super-nodes.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Find a minimum spanning tree using both Kruskal's algorithm and Prim's algorithm (starting from $A$) for the graph with edges: $AB(5), BC(2), AC(4), AD(6), CD(3),\\ DB(1), DE(6), EB(3), EF(3), FB(4)$. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2013--14}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

To find the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) of the given undirected graph, we will apply both \textbf{Kruskal's Algorithm} and \textbf{Prim's Algorithm}. An MST for a graph with $V = 6$ vertices ($A, B, C, D, E, F$) will contain exactly $V - 1 = 5$ edges and will connect all vertices with the minimum possible total edge weight.

\subsubsection*{1. Kruskal's Algorithm}
Kruskal's algorithm operates by sorting all edges in ascending order of their weights and greedily adding them to the MST, provided they do not form a cycle (managed efficiently via a Disjoint-Set / Union-Find data structure).

\textbf{Step 1: Sort the edges by weight.}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $DB (1), BC (2), CD (3), EB (3), EF (3), AC (4), FB (4), AB (5), AD (6), DE (6)$
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Step 2: Iteratively select edges.}

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Edge} & \textbf{Weight} & \textbf{Action / Reason} & \textbf{Edges in MST} \\
\hline
$DB$ & $1$ & \textbf{Add} (Does not form a cycle) & $1$ \\
$BC$ & $2$ & \textbf{Add} (Does not form a cycle) & $2$ \\
$CD$ & $3$ & \textbf{Skip} (Forms cycle $D-B-C$) & $2$ \\
$EB$ & $3$ & \textbf{Add} (Does not form a cycle) & $3$ \\
$EF$ & $3$ & \textbf{Add} (Does not form a cycle) & $4$ \\
$AC$ & $4$ & \textbf{Add} (Does not form a cycle) & $5$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\textit{We stop here because the MST has reached $V - 1 = 5$ edges.}

\textbf{MST Edges:} $\{DB, BC, EB, EF, AC\}$\\
\textbf{Total Cost:} $1 + 2 + 3 + 3 + 4 = \mathbf{13}$

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Prim's Algorithm (Starting from $A$)}
Prim's algorithm builds the MST by maintaining a set of visited vertices and greedily adding the smallest edge that crosses the cut between the visited and unvisited sets.

\textbf{Initialization:} Visited set $V_{mst} = \{A\}$.

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{|c|l|l|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Step} & \textbf{Visited Nodes ($V_{mst}$)} & \textbf{Candidate Edges Crossing Cut} & \textbf{Edge Chosen} & \textbf{Cost} \\
\hline
1 & $\{A\}$ & $AB(5), \mathbf{AC(4)}, AD(6)$ & $AC$ & $4$ \\
2 & $\{A, C\}$ & $AB(5), AD(6), \mathbf{CB(2)}, CD(3)$ & $CB$ & $2$ \\
3 & $\{A, C, B\}$ & $AD(6), CD(3), \mathbf{BD(1)}, BE(3), BF(4)$ & $BD$ & $1$ \\
4 & $\{A, C, B, D\}$ & $BE(3), BF(4), DE(6)$ \quad \textit{(CD, AD form cycles)} & $BE$ & $3$ \\
5 & $\{A, C, B, D, E\}$ & $\mathbf{EF(3)}, BF(4)$ \quad \textit{(DE forms cycle)} & $EF$ & $3$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\textbf{MST Edges:} $\{AC, CB, BD, BE, EF\}$\\
\textbf{Total Cost:} $4 + 2 + 1 + 3 + 3 = \mathbf{13}$

Both algorithms successfully yield the identical MST and minimum cost.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Minimum Spanning Tree Visualization}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, node distance=2.5cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=8mm, fill=cA!10, thick},
    mst_edge/.style={-, ultra thick, cA},
    non_mst_edge/.style={-, dashed, gray!60, thick}]

    % Nodes
    \node (A) at (0, 2) {A};
    \node (C) at (0, 0) {C};
    \node (D) at (3, 2) {D};
    \node (B) at (3, 0) {B};
    \node (E) at (6, 0) {E};
    \node (F) at (9, 0) {F};

    % Non-MST Edges
    \draw[non_mst_edge] (A) -- node[above, black] {5} (B);
    \draw[non_mst_edge] (A) -- node[above, black] {6} (D);
    \draw[non_mst_edge] (C) -- node[above left, black] {3} (D);
    \draw[non_mst_edge] (D) -- node[above right, black] {6} (E);
    \draw[non_mst_edge] (B) to[bend right=30] node[below, black] {4} (F);

    % MST Edges
    \draw[mst_edge] (A) -- node[left, black] {4} (C);
    \draw[mst_edge] (C) -- node[below, black] {2} (B);
    \draw[mst_edge] (B) -- node[left, black] {1} (D);
    \draw[mst_edge] (B) -- node[below, black] {3} (E);
    \draw[mst_edge] (E) -- node[below, black] {3} (F);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{4. Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Kruskal's Algorithm Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(E \log E)$ or $\mathcal{O}(E \log V)$. Sorting the edges dominates the runtime at $\mathcal{O}(E \log E)$. The Disjoint-Set operations take near-constant time $\mathcal{O}(\alpha(V))$ per edge, bounded by $\mathcal{O}(E \log V)$. 
    \item \textbf{Prim's Algorithm Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(E + V \log V)$ when using a Fibonacci heap for the min-priority queue, or $\mathcal{O}(E \log V)$ using a standard binary min-heap. Finding the minimum edge crossing the cut efficiently is the core driver of Prim's runtime.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} Both algorithms operate in $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$ space to store the graph structures (Adjacency List/Edge List) and the priority queues/Disjoint-Set arrays.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item  If Prim's algorithm is applied on a given graph, which will be the fifth edge added to the spanning tree? Start from vertex $a$. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2012--13}

\begin{center}

  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},

\end{center}

  \draw[grayedge] (b) -- node[graylbl, right]       {11} (h);
  \draw[grayedge] (b) -- node[graylbl, above]       {8}  (c);
  \draw[grayedge] (c) -- node[graylbl, above]       {7}  (d);
  \draw[grayedge] (c) -- node[graylbl, above right] {2}  (i);
  \draw[grayedge] (d) -- node[graylbl, right]       {9}  (e);

  \draw[grayedge] (h) -- node[graylbl, above]       {7}  (i);
  \draw[grayedge] (h) -- node[graylbl, below]       {1}  (g);
  \draw[grayedge] (i) -- node[graylbl, left]        {6}  (g);
  \draw[grayedge] (c) -- node[graylbl, above]       {4}  (f);
  \draw[grayedge] (g) -- node[graylbl, below]       {2}  (f);
}
%% ======================================================

  boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,
  title={\textbf{Answer: Prim's Algorithm}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Start Node:} $a$. \\
When ties occur (e.g., between edges $a$--$h(8)$ and $b$--$c(8)$), we pick alphabetically (adding $c$ instead of $h$).
