\item State and prove the ``cut property'' and the ``cycle property''. Explain with necessary examples how these properties are useful in solving the minimum spanning tree problem. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2011--12}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

The greedy strategies used to solve the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) problem rely on two fundamental graph-theoretic properties: the \textbf{Cut Property} and the \textbf{Cycle Property}. These properties govern which edges must be included in an MST and which edges can be safely discarded.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{1. The Cut Property}

\textbf{Statement:} Let $G = (V, E)$ be a connected, undirected graph with a weight function $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$. Let $(S, V \setminus S)$ be any cut (a partition of the vertices into two disjoint sets). If $e = (u, v)$ is the strictly minimum-weight edge crossing this cut (with $u \in S$ and $v \in V \setminus S$), then $e$ must belong to \textit{every} minimum spanning tree of $G$. (If the minimum weight is not unique, $e$ belongs to \textit{some} MST).

\textbf{Proof (by contradiction/exchange argument):}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Assume there exists an MST $T$ of $G$ that \textit{does not} contain the minimum-weight crossing edge $e = (u, v)$.
    \item Because $T$ is a spanning tree, it must contain exactly one simple path $P$ connecting node $u$ to node $v$.
    \item Since $u \in S$ and $v \in V \setminus S$, the path $P$ must cross the cut at least once. Let $e' = (x, y)$ be an edge on this path that crosses the cut, with $x \in S$ and $y \in V \setminus S$.
    \item By our initial premise, $w(e) < w(e')$.
    \item If we remove $e'$ from $T$, the tree becomes disconnected into two components. If we then add $e$ back, the two components are reconnected, forming a new spanning tree $T' = T - \{e'\} \cup \{e\}$.
    \item The total weight of the new tree is:
    $$ w(T') = w(T) - w(e') + w(e) $$
    Since $w(e) < w(e')$, it follows that $w(T') < w(T)$.
    \item This contradicts the assumption that $T$ is a Minimum Spanning Tree. Therefore, $e$ must be in the MST. $\blacksquare$
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=2cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=6mm, fill=white, thick},
    edge/.style={-, thick},
    path/.style={-, dashed, thick, blue}]

    % Cut boundaries
    \draw[fill=cA!10, rounded corners=15pt, dashed, draw=cA] (-1, -2.5) rectangle (2, 2.5);
    \node[draw=none, fill=none] at (0.5, -2) {\large $S$};
    
    \draw[fill=cA!10, rounded corners=15pt, dashed, draw=cA] (4, -2.5) rectangle (7, 2.5);
    \node[draw=none, fill=none] at (5.5, -2) {\large $V \setminus S$};

    % Nodes
    \node (u) at (1, 1) {$u$};
    \node (x) at (1, -1) {$x$};
    \node (v) at (5, 1) {$v$};
    \node (y) at (5, -1) {$y$};

    % Edges crossing the cut
    \draw[edge, red, ultra thick] (u) -- node[above, black, draw=none, fill=none] {$e$ (min weight)} (v);
    \draw[edge] (x) -- node[below, black, draw=none, fill=none] {$e'$} (y);

    % Path inside sets
    \draw[path] (u) to[bend right=30] (x);
    \draw[path] (v) to[bend left=30] (y);
    \node[draw=none, fill=none] at (3, -1.8) {Path in $T$};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. The Cycle Property}

\textbf{Statement:} Let $G = (V, E)$ be a connected, undirected graph with a weight function $w$. Let $C$ be any cycle in $G$. If $e = (u, v)$ is the strictly heaviest edge in $C$, then $e$ \textit{cannot} belong to any Minimum Spanning Tree of $G$.

\textbf{Proof (by contradiction/exchange argument):}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Assume there exists an MST $T$ that \textit{does} contain the heaviest edge $e = (u, v)$ from cycle $C$.
    \item If we remove $e$ from $T$, the tree is partitioned into two disconnected components, say $T_1$ and $T_2$, with $u \in T_1$ and $v \in T_2$.
    \item The cycle $C$ forms a closed loop containing $e$. If $e$ is removed, the remaining edges of $C$ form a path connecting $u$ and $v$.
    \item This path must contain at least one other edge $e' = (x, y)$ that has one endpoint in $T_1$ and the other in $T_2$.
    \item By our premise, $e$ is the strictly heaviest edge in $C$, so $w(e') < w(e)$.
    \item If we add $e'$ to reconnect $T_1$ and $T_2$, we form a new spanning tree $T' = T - \{e\} \cup \{e'\}$.
    \item The total weight of the new tree is:
    $$ w(T') = w(T) - w(e) + w(e') $$
    Since $w(e') < w(e)$, it follows that $w(T') < w(T)$.
    \item This contradicts the assumption that $T$ is an MST. Therefore, $e$ cannot be in the MST. $\blacksquare$
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=2cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=6mm, fill=white, thick},
    edge/.style={-, thick}]

    % Nodes forming a cycle
    \node (A) at (0, 0) {$u$};
    \node (B) at (1.5, 1.5) {$x$};
    \node (C) at (3.5, 1.5) {$y$};
    \node (D) at (5, 0) {$v$};
    \node (E) at (2.5, -1.5) {};

    % Cycle Edges
    \draw[edge, red, ultra thick] (A) -- node[below, black, draw=none, fill=none] {$e$ (heaviest)} (D);
    \draw[edge] (A) -- (B);
    \draw[edge, blue, thick] (B) -- node[above, black, draw=none, fill=none] {$e'$ (alternative)} (C);
    \draw[edge] (C) -- (D);
    \draw[edge, dashed, gray] (A) -- (E);
    \draw[edge, dashed, gray] (E) -- (D);
    
    % Component boundaries to illustrate removal of e
    \draw[draw=blue, rounded corners=10pt, dashed] (-0.8, -2) rectangle (2, 2.5);
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, text=blue] at (0.5, 2.2) {$T_1$};
    
    \draw[draw=blue, rounded corners=10pt, dashed] (3, -2) rectangle (5.8, 2.5);
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, text=blue] at (4.5, 2.2) {$T_2$};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Usefulness in Solving the MST Problem}

The Cut and Cycle properties are the driving forces behind all standard greedy MST algorithms. By applying these properties, we avoid the $\mathcal{O}(2^E)$ brute-force evaluation of all spanning trees.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Prim's Algorithm (Applies the Cut Property):}
    Prim's algorithm actively maintains a cut $(S, V \setminus S)$, where $S$ is the set of vertices already added to the MST. At each step, it looks at all edges crossing the cut. By the \textbf{Cut Property}, it is guaranteed that picking the minimum-weight crossing edge will yield a valid MST edge. It greedily incorporates this edge and moves the corresponding vertex into $S$, repeating until all vertices are spanned.
    
    \item \textbf{Kruskal's Algorithm (Applies Both Properties):}
    Kruskal's algorithm sorts all edges by weight and processes them in ascending order.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Cycle Property justification:} If the next shortest edge forms a cycle with the edges already chosen, it means this edge is the heaviest edge in that newly formed cycle (because edges are processed in strictly ascending weight order). By the \textbf{Cycle Property}, we can safely discard it.
        \item \textbf{Cut Property justification:} If the edge connects two separate components (trees in the forest), we can view those two components as a cut $(S, V \setminus S)$. Since we are processing edges in ascending order, this edge is the minimum weight edge crossing that cut. By the \textbf{Cut Property}, it safely belongs to the MST.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Complexity Overview}
Relying on these properties allows us to use efficient data structures (like Priority Queues for Prim's and Union-Find for Kruskal's) to find the MST in $\mathcal{O}(E \log V)$ time and $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$ space, guaranteeing global optimality through purely local, greedy decisions.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item How is the minimum cost arborescence problem different from the minimum spanning tree problem? Construct a minimum cost arborescence for the graph with edges $AB(7), AC(3), DA(4), EA(1), BC(91), CF(9), BD(7), EB(2), FB(5), CE(4), DE(6), \\FD(3)$, with edge orientations from the first vertex to the second. Assume source vertex is $A$. \mk{5+5}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2006--07}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Key Differences: Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) vs. Minimum Cost Arborescence (MCA)}

While both problems seek a minimum-weight subgraph that spans all vertices without cycles, they differ fundamentally in graph structure, properties, and algorithmic complexity:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Graph Edge Directionality:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{MST:} Defined exclusively on \textbf{undirected graphs}. Edges have no orientation, meaning connectivity between any pair of nodes is symmetric.
        \item \textbf{MCA:} Defined on \textbf{directed graphs (digraphs)}. Edges have a strict orientation (from a source vertex to a target vertex), and connectivity is asymmetric.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Structural and In-Degree Constraints:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{MST:} Any acyclic connected subgraph spanning all $V$ vertices with $V-1$ edges qualifies. Vertices can have any degree sequence as long as global connectivity is maintained.
        \item \textbf{MCA:} Requires a designated \textbf{root vertex} $R$. The final directed tree must ensure that there is a unique directed path from $R$ to every other vertex $v \in V$. Consequently, every vertex except the root must have an \textbf{in-degree of exactly 1} ($\text{in-degree}(R) = 0$).
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Algorithmic Approach and Failure of Simple Greedy Methods:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{MST:} Solved efficiently by purely greedy choices (e.g., Prim's or Kruskal's algorithms) due to the \textit{Cut Property} and \textit{Cycle Property}. 
        \item \textbf{MCA:} Simple greedy strategies fail because choosing the cheapest incoming edge for each node can result in localized directed cycles that isolate components from the root. It requires more complex algorithms (such as the \textbf{Chu-Liu/Edmonds' Algorithm}) which systematically contract cycles and adjust edge weights dynamically.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Influence of the Root Node:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{MST:} Independent of any root node. Starting Prim's algorithm from any arbitrary vertex always yields the same minimum total weight.
        \item \textbf{MCA:} Strongly dependent on the chosen root. An arborescence might be optimal for one root but completely non-existent for another if certain nodes are unreachable.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Edmonds' Algorithm Specification}

To rigorously solve the MCA problem, we formally follow the standard steps of Edmonds' Algorithm:

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Chu-Liu/Edmonds' Algorithm}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require{A directed graph $G = (V, E)$, edge weights $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$, and root vertex $R \in V$}
\Ensure{A minimum-cost spanning arborescence rooted at $R$}
\State Discard all incoming edges to the root $R$.
\For{each vertex $v \in V \setminus \{R\}$}
    \State Select the incoming edge $e = (u, v)$ with the minimum weight.
    \State Define $\pi(v) = u$ and $min\_in(v) = w(u, v)$.
\EndFor
\If{the subgraph formed by edges $(\pi(v), v)$ contains no directed cycles}
    \State \Return the edge set $\{(\pi(v), v)\}$ as the optimal arborescence.
\Else
    \State Identify a directed cycle $C$, contract it into a super-node $v_C$.
    \State Update weights of incoming edges to $C$: $w'(x, y) = w(x, y) - min\_in(y)$ where $y \in C, x \notin C$.
    \State Recurse on the contracted graph, then expand the cycle and break it optimally.
\EndIf
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. Construction of Minimum Cost Arborescence for the Given Instance}

We are given a directed graph with vertices $V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ and root $R = A$. Let's map the full original graph to visualize its topology.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, node distance=2.5cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=9mm, fill=cA!8, thick},
    edge/.style={->, thick, -{Stealth[scale=1.2]}},
    lbl/.style={fill=white, inner sep=1.5pt, font=\small}]

    % Node Configuration
    \node (A) at (4, 4) {\textbf{A}};
    \node (C) at (4, 2) {\textbf{C}};
    \node (E) at (1.5, 0) {\textbf{E}};
    \node (F) at (6.5, 0) {\textbf{F}};
    \node (B) at (1.5, -2) {\textbf{B}};
    \node (D) at (6.5, -2) {\textbf{D}};

    % Edges mapping
    \draw[edge] (A) to[bend right=45] node[lbl, left] {7} (B);
    \draw[edge] (A) -- node[lbl, left] {3} (C);
    \draw[edge] (D) to[bend right=35] node[lbl, right] {4} (A);
    \draw[edge] (E) to[bend left=35] node[lbl, left] {1} (A);
    \draw[edge] (B) to[bend left=15] node[lbl, right] {91} (C);
    \draw[edge] (C) -- node[lbl, above right] {9} (F);
    \draw[edge] (B) -- node[lbl, above] {7} (D);
    \draw[edge] (E) -- node[lbl, left] {2} (B);
    \draw[edge] (F) -- node[lbl, above left] {5} (B);
    \draw[edge] (C) -- node[lbl, above left] {4} (E);
    \draw[edge] (D) -- node[lbl, right] {6} (E);
    \draw[edge] (F) -- node[lbl, right] {3} (D);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Identify Minimum Incoming Edges}
We evaluate all incoming directed edges for each non-root node $v \in \{B, C, D, E, F\}$ and pick the one with the minimal weight:

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{tabular}{|c|l|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Target Node ($v$)} & \textbf{All Incoming Edges (Weights)} & \textbf{Selected Edge} & $min\_in(v)$ \\
\hline
\textbf{B} & $AB(7), \ \mathbf{EB(2)}, \ FB(5)$ & $EB$ & $2$ \\
\hline
\textbf{C} & $\mathbf{AC(3)}, \ BC(91)$ & $AC$ & $3$ \\
\hline
\textbf{D} & $BD(7), \ \mathbf{FD(3)}$ & $FD$ & $3$ \\
\hline
\textbf{E} & $\mathbf{CE(4)}, \ DE(6)$ & $CE$ & $4$ \\
\hline
\textbf{F} & $\mathbf{CF(9)}$ & $CF$ & $9$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Cycle Detection Analysis}
Let us trace the tracking paths constructed exclusively by our optimal selections $\{EB, AC, FD, CE, CF\}$ starting from the root node $A$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $A \xrightarrow{3} C$
    \item From $C$, the path branches along two distinct parallel fronts:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $C \xrightarrow{4} E \xrightarrow{2} B$ (Terminates at leaf node $B$)
        \item $C \xrightarrow{9} F \xrightarrow{3} D$ (Terminates at leaf node $D$)
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

Every target vertex has an in-degree of exactly $1$. There are \textbf{no directed cycles} formed by this subset of edges, meaning no cycle contraction step is triggered. The algorithm directly terminates successfully.

\subsubsection*{Step 3: Total Cost Formulation}
The chosen collection of edges satisfies all conditions of a valid spanning arborescence rooted at $A$. 
$$ \text{Total Cost} = w(AC) + w(CE) + w(EB) + w(CF) + w(FD) $$
$$ \text{Total Cost} = 3 + 4 + 2 + 9 + 3 = \mathbf{21} $$

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, node distance=2.5cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=9mm, fill=cA!20, thick},
    edge/.style={->, ultra thick, cA, -{Stealth[scale=1.4]}},
    lbl/.style={fill=white, inner sep=1.5pt, font=\bfseries\small}]

    % Final Arborescence Tree Layout
    \node (A) at (4, 4) {A};
    \node (C) at (4, 2) {C};
    \node (E) at (1.5, 0) {E};
    \node (F) at (6.5, 0) {F};
    \node (B) at (1.5, -2) {B};
    \node (D) at (6.5, -2) {D};

    % Directed tree paths
    \draw[edge] (A) -- node[lbl, black] {3} (C);
    \draw[edge] (C) -- node[lbl, black] {4} (E);
    \draw[edge] (C) -- node[lbl, black] {9} (F);
    \draw[edge] (E) -- node[lbl, black] {2} (B);
    \draw[edge] (F) -- node[lbl, black] {3} (D);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{4. Asymptotic Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} For this specific instance, the execution takes a single phase running in $\mathcal{O}(E)$ time because no cycle contractions occur. Universally, the worst-case implementation requires $\mathcal{O}(V \cdot E)$ due to up to $\mathcal{O}(V)$ nested cycle contractions. Optimized variants utilizing Fibonacci Heaps run in $\mathcal{O}(E + V \log V)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$ auxiliary space to preserve tracked predecessor values ($\pi$), adjacency matrices, and cost reduction state variables.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\end{enumerate}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cA}{Maximum Flow}
\label{sub:s1-maxflow}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item A central warehouse supplies relief packages to three depots $D_1, D_2, D_3$. The depots distribute packages to four affected zones $Z_1, Z_2, Z_3, Z_4$. Each depot has a daily sending capacity: $D_1 = 15$, $D_2 = 20$, $D_3 = 10$. Each zone has a receiving limit: $Z_1 = 10$, $Z_2 = 15$, $Z_3 = 10$, $Z_4 = 10$. Distribution routes: $D_1 \to \{Z_1, Z_2\}$, $D_2 \to \{Z_1, Z_3, Z_4\}$, $D_3 \to \{Z_2, Z_4\}$. The warehouse supply is unlimited. Determine the maximum number of relief packages that can be delivered per day. Model the situation as a flow network by clearly defining the source, sink, intermediate nodes, and edge capacities and apply Ford-Fulkerson's algorithm.
\mk{20} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

To determine the maximum number of relief packages that can be delivered per day, we model this scenario as a \textbf{Maximum Flow Problem}. We will construct a flow network $G = (V, E)$ with a designated source node $S$ and a sink node $T$, applying the \textbf{Ford-Fulkerson Algorithm} to compute the maximum throughput.

\subsubsection*{1. Flow Network Modeling}

The physical constraints (depot sending capacities and zone receiving limits) act on the \textit{nodes} in reality. In standard flow networks, capacities belong on \textit{edges}. We handle this by modeling the system as follows:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Source ($S$):} Represents the central warehouse with unlimited supply.
    \item \textbf{Sink ($T$):} A virtual sink that aggregates all successfully delivered packages from the zones.
    \item \textbf{Intermediate Nodes:} Depots $D_1, D_2, D_3$ and Zones $Z_1, Z_2, Z_3, Z_4$.
    \item \textbf{Edges and Capacities ($C$):}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Warehouse to Depots:} Capacity represents the daily sending limit.
              $C(S \to D_1) = 15$, $C(S \to D_2) = 20$, $C(S \to D_3) = 10$.
        \item \textbf{Depots to Zones:} The routes themselves have no stated limits, so we assign infinite capacity ($\infty$).
              $C(D_1 \to Z_1, Z_2) = \infty$ \\
              $C(D_2 \to Z_1, Z_3, Z_4) = \infty$ \\
              $C(D_3 \to Z_2, Z_4) = \infty$
        \item \textbf{Zones to Sink:} Capacity represents the daily receiving limit of each zone.
              $C(Z_1 \to T) = 10$, $C(Z_2 \to T) = 15$, $C(Z_3 \to T) = 10$, $C(Z_4 \to T) = 10$.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{2. Initial Flow Network Diagram}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, node distance=2cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=8mm, fill=cA!10, thick},
    edge/.style={->, thick},
    lbl/.style={fill=cA!4, inner sep=1pt, font=\small, draw=none}]

    % Nodes
    \node (S) at (0, 0) {S};
    
    \node (D1) at (3, 2.5) {$D_1$};
    \node (D2) at (3, 0) {$D_2$};
    \node (D3) at (3, -2.5) {$D_3$};
    
    \node (Z1) at (7, 3.5) {$Z_1$};
    \node (Z2) at (7, 1.2) {$Z_2$};
    \node (Z3) at (7, -1.2) {$Z_3$};
    \node (Z4) at (7, -3.5) {$Z_4$};
    
    \node (T) at (10, 0) {T};

    % Edges S -> D
    \draw[edge] (S) -- node[lbl, above left] {15} (D1);
    \draw[edge] (S) -- node[lbl, above] {20} (D2);
    \draw[edge] (S) -- node[lbl, below left] {10} (D3);

    % Edges D -> Z
    \draw[edge] (D1) -- node[lbl, above] {$\infty$} (Z1);
    \draw[edge] (D1) -- node[lbl, left] {$\infty$} (Z2);
    
    \draw[edge] (D2) -- node[lbl, right] {$\infty$} (Z1);
    \draw[edge] (D2) -- node[lbl, above] {$\infty$} (Z3);
    \draw[edge] (D2) -- node[lbl, above] {$\infty$} (Z4);
    
    \draw[edge] (D3) -- node[lbl, left] {$\infty$} (Z2);
    \draw[edge] (D3) -- node[lbl, below] {$\infty$} (Z4);

    % Edges Z -> T
    \draw[edge] (Z1) -- node[lbl, above right] {10} (T);
    \draw[edge] (Z2) -- node[lbl, above] {15} (T);
    \draw[edge] (Z3) -- node[lbl, below] {10} (T);
    \draw[edge] (Z4) -- node[lbl, below right] {10} (T);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Ford-Fulkerson Execution Steps}

We initialize the flow $f = 0$ on all edges. We iteratively find augmenting paths from $S$ to $T$ in the residual graph until no such paths exist. The bottleneck capacity of a path is $\Delta = \min(C_{residual}(u, v))$ for all edges $(u, v)$ in the path.

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{tabular}{|c|l|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Iter.} & \textbf{Augmenting Path ($P$)} & \textbf{Bottleneck ($\Delta$)} & \textbf{Calculation} & \textbf{Total Flow} \\
\hline
1 & $S \to D_1 \to Z_1 \to T$ & $10$ & $\min(15, \infty, 10)$ & $0 + 10 = \mathbf{10}$ \\
\hline
\multicolumn{5}{|p{14cm}|}{\textit{Updates:} $C_f(S \to D_1) = 5$, $C_f(Z_1 \to T) = 0$ (Saturated). $D_1$ sent $10$ to $Z_1$.} \\
\hline
2 & $S \to D_1 \to Z_2 \to T$ & $5$ & $\min(5, \infty, 15)$ & $10 + 5 = \mathbf{15}$ \\
\hline
\multicolumn{5}{|p{14cm}|}{\textit{Updates:} $C_f(S \to D_1) = 0$ (Saturated), $C_f(Z_2 \to T) = 10$. $D_1$ maxed out.} \\
\hline
3 & $S \to D_2 \to Z_3 \to T$ & $10$ & $\min(20, \infty, 10)$ & $15 + 10 = \mathbf{25}$ \\
\hline
\multicolumn{5}{|p{14cm}|}{\textit{Updates:} $C_f(S \to D_2) = 10$, $C_f(Z_3 \to T) = 0$ (Saturated). $D_2$ sent $10$ to $Z_3$.} \\
\hline
4 & $S \to D_2 \to Z_4 \to T$ & $10$ & $\min(10, \infty, 10)$ & $25 + 10 = \mathbf{35}$ \\
\hline
\multicolumn{5}{|p{14cm}|}{\textit{Updates:} $C_f(S \to D_2) = 0$ (Saturated), $C_f(Z_4 \to T) = 0$ (Saturated). $D_2$ and $Z_4$ maxed out.} \\
\hline
5 & $S \to D_3 \to Z_2 \to T$ & $10$ & $\min(10, \infty, 10)$ & $35 + 10 = \mathbf{45}$ \\
\hline
\multicolumn{5}{|p{14cm}|}{\textit{Updates:} $C_f(S \to D_3) = 0$ (Saturated), $C_f(Z_2 \to T) = 0$ (Saturated). $D_3$ and $Z_2$ maxed out.} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

At this point, all edges leaving the source $S$ ($S \to D_1$, $S \to D_2$, $S \to D_3$) are saturated (residual capacities are 0). Thus, no more augmenting paths can be found.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{4. Final Flow Network Diagram}
The final flow representation showing $\text{Flow} / \text{Capacity}$ across the network.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, node distance=2cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=8mm, fill=cA!10, thick},
    edge/.style={->, thick, draw=blue!70!black},
    lbl/.style={fill=cA!4, inner sep=1pt, font=\small, draw=none, text=blue!80!black}]

    % Nodes
    \node (S) at (0, 0) {S};
    
    \node (D1) at (3, 2.5) {$D_1$};
    \node (D2) at (3, 0) {$D_2$};
    \node (D3) at (3, -2.5) {$D_3$};
    
    \node (Z1) at (7, 3.5) {$Z_1$};
    \node (Z2) at (7, 1.2) {$Z_2$};
    \node (Z3) at (7, -1.2) {$Z_3$};
    \node (Z4) at (7, -3.5) {$Z_4$};
    
    \node (T) at (10, 0) {T};

    % Edges S -> D
    \draw[edge] (S) -- node[lbl, above left] {\textbf{15}/15} (D1);
    \draw[edge] (S) -- node[lbl, above] {\textbf{20}/20} (D2);
    \draw[edge] (S) -- node[lbl, below left] {\textbf{10}/10} (D3);

    % Edges D -> Z (Only showing active flows > 0 to avoid clutter, else 0/\infty)
    \draw[edge] (D1) -- node[lbl, above] {\textbf{10}/$\infty$} (Z1);
    \draw[edge] (D1) -- node[lbl, left] {\textbf{5}/$\infty$} (Z2);
    
    \draw[->, thick, draw=gray!40, dashed] (D2) -- node[fill=cA!4, inner sep=1pt, font=\small, draw=none, text=gray] {0/$\infty$} (Z1);
    \draw[edge] (D2) -- node[lbl, above] {\textbf{10}/$\infty$} (Z3);
    \draw[edge] (D2) -- node[lbl, above] {\textbf{10}/$\infty$} (Z4);
    
    \draw[edge] (D3) -- node[lbl, left] {\textbf{10}/$\infty$} (Z2);
    \draw[->, thick, draw=gray!40, dashed] (D3) -- node[fill=cA!4, inner sep=1pt, font=\small, draw=none, text=gray] {0/$\infty$} (Z4);

    % Edges Z -> T
    \draw[edge] (Z1) -- node[lbl, above right] {\textbf{10}/10} (T);
    \draw[edge] (Z2) -- node[lbl, above] {\textbf{15}/15} (T);
    \draw[edge] (Z3) -- node[lbl, below] {\textbf{10}/10} (T);
    \draw[edge] (Z4) -- node[lbl, below right] {\textbf{10}/10} (T);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{5. Conclusion and Verification}
The \textbf{Maximum Flow is 45 relief packages per day}. 

We can verify this mathematically using the \textbf{Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem}:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Maximum theoretical supply based on depot capacities: $15 + 20 + 10 = \mathbf{45}$.
    \item Maximum theoretical demand based on zone capacities: $10 + 15 + 10 + 10 = \mathbf{45}$.
\end{itemize}
Since the total flow equals the minimum cut of the network (the edges originating from $S$ or edges entering $T$), $45$ is provably the maximum number of packages that can be delivered per day.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Derive a proof for the max-flow min-cut theorem.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24, 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

The \textbf{Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem} is a foundational result in combinatorial optimization and graph theory. It states that in a flow network, the maximum amount of flow passing from the source to the sink is exactly equal to the minimum capacity of a cut that separates the source from the sink.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{1. Preliminaries and Definitions}

To rigorously prove the theorem, we first establish the formal definitions:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Flow Network:} A directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with a source node $s \in V$ and a sink node $t \in V$. Each edge $(u, v) \in E$ has a non-negative capacity $c(u, v) \ge 0$. If $(u, v) \notin E$, we assume $c(u, v) = 0$.
    \item \textbf{Flow:} A function $f: V \times V \to \mathbb{R}$ satisfying:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \textit{Capacity Constraint:} For all $u, v \in V$, $0 \le f(u, v) \le c(u, v)$.
        \item \textit{Flow Conservation:} For all $u \in V \setminus \{s, t\}$, incoming flow equals outgoing flow:
        $$ \sum_{v \in V} f(v, u) = \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v) $$
    \end{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Value of Flow ($|f|$):} The net flow leaving the source:
    $$ |f| = \sum_{v \in V} f(s, v) - \sum_{v \in V} f(v, s) $$
    \item \textbf{Cut $(S, T)$:} A partition of $V$ into two disjoint subsets $S$ and $T$ such that $s \in S$ and $t \in T$.
    \item \textbf{Capacity of a Cut ($c(S, T)$):} The sum of capacities of all edges directed from $S$ to $T$:
    $$ c(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} c(u, v) $$
    \item \textbf{Net Flow Across a Cut ($f(S, T)$):} The total flow from $S$ to $T$ minus the total flow from $T$ to $S$:
    $$ f(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} f(v, u) $$
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, node distance=2cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=8mm, fill=cA!10, thick},
    edge/.style={->, thick},
    cut/.style={dashed, ultra thick, red}]

    % Nodes
    \node (s) at (0, 0) {$s$};
    \node (u) at (3, 1.5) {$u$};
    \node (u2) at (3, -1.5) {$u'$};
    \node (v) at (6, 1.5) {$v$};
    \node (v2) at (6, -1.5) {$v'$};
    \node (t) at (9, 0) {$t$};

    % Edges
    \draw[edge] (s) -- (u);
    \draw[edge] (s) -- (u2);
    \draw[edge] (u) -- node[above, draw=none, fill=none, text=black] {$c(u,v)$} (v);
    \draw[edge] (u2) -- (v2);
    \draw[edge] (u) -- (v2);
    \draw[edge] (v) -- (t);
    \draw[edge] (v2) -- (t);

    % The Cut
    \draw[cut] (4.5, 3) -- (4.5, -3) node[below, text=red, draw=none, fill=none, font=\bfseries] {Cut $(S, T)$};
    
    % Set Labels
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, text=cA, font=\Large\bfseries] at (1.5, 2.5) {S};
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, text=cA, font=\Large\bfseries] at (7.5, 2.5) {T};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Lemma: Weak Duality}

Before proving the main theorem, we establish that \textbf{the value of any flow $|f|$ is bounded above by the capacity of any cut $c(S, T)$}.

\textbf{Proof:}
By flow conservation, the net flow across any cut $(S, T)$ equals the total value of the flow $|f|$ leaving the source:
$$ |f| = f(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} f(v, u) $$
Because flow cannot be negative ($f(v, u) \ge 0$), dropping the subtracted term creates an inequality:
$$ |f| \le \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} f(u, v) $$
By the capacity constraint ($f(u, v) \le c(u, v)$):
$$ |f| \le \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} c(u, v) = c(S, T) $$
Thus, for \textit{any} flow $f$ and \textit{any} cut $(S, T)$, $|f| \le c(S, T)$. This implies the maximum flow cannot exceed the minimum cut.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Proof of the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem}

To prove the theorem, we show that the following three statements are strictly equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $f$ is a maximum flow in $G$.
    \item The residual network $G_f$ contains no augmenting paths from $s$ to $t$.
    \item $|f| = c(S, T)$ for some cut $(S, T)$ of $G$.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Step 1: Prove $(1) \implies (2)$} \\
Suppose $f$ is a maximum flow, but there \textit{is} an augmenting path $P$ in $G_f$ from $s$ to $t$. An augmenting path has bottleneck capacity $c_f(P) > 0$. We can push additional flow $c_f(P)$ along $P$ to create a new flow $f'$ with $|f'| = |f| + c_f(P) > |f|$. This contradicts $f$ being a maximum flow. Thus, no augmenting paths can exist.

\textbf{Step 2: Prove $(2) \implies (3)$} \\
Assume $G_f$ has no augmenting paths from $s$ to $t$. Let $S$ be the set of vertices reachable from $s$ in $G_f$ via paths with positive residual capacity, and $T = V \setminus S$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item $s \in S$ by definition.
    \item $t \notin S$ (otherwise an augmenting path exists), so $t \in T$. Thus, $(S, T)$ is a valid cut.
\end{itemize}
For any pair $u \in S$ and $v \in T$, there is no residual capacity from $u$ to $v$, so $c_f(u, v) = 0$. Since $c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v) = 0$, it must be that $f(u, v) = c(u, v)$. Furthermore, $f(v, u) = 0$ (otherwise there would be a backward residual edge from $u$ to $v$, contradicting $v \in T$).
Substituting these into the net flow equation:
$$ |f| = \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} f(v, u) = \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} c(u, v) - 0 = c(S, T) $$

\textbf{Step 3: Prove $(3) \implies (1)$} \\
Assume $|f| = c(S, T)$ for some cut $(S, T)$. From Weak Duality, we know $|f'| \le c(S, T)$ for \textit{any} possible flow $f'$. Since $|f| = c(S, T)$, it follows that $|f'| \le |f|$. Therefore, $f$ must be the maximum flow. 

\textbf{Conclusion:} \\
Because $(1) \implies (2) \implies (3) \implies (1)$, the statements are equivalent. The existence of a maximum flow $f$ inherently guarantees a cut $(S, T)$ where $|f| = c(S, T)$, completing the proof. $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item Define the Max-flow problem.
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Definition of the Maximum Flow Problem}

The \textbf{Maximum Flow (Max-Flow) problem} is a fundamental optimization problem in graph theory and operations research. It involves finding the maximum possible rate of flow from a designated source node to a designated sink node through a flow network, subject to the capacity limits of the network's edges.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, node distance=2.5cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=8mm, fill=cA!10, thick},
    edge/.style={->, thick},
    lbl/.style={fill=cA!4, inner sep=1pt, font=\small, draw=none}]

    % Nodes
    \node (s) at (0, 0) {$s$};
    \node (v1) at (3, 1.5) {$v_1$};
    \node (v2) at (3, -1.5) {$v_2$};
    \node (t) at (6, 0) {$t$};

    % Edges with capacities
    \draw[edge] (s) -- node[lbl, above left] {10} (v1);
    \draw[edge] (s) -- node[lbl, below left] {5} (v2);
    \draw[edge] (v1) -- node[lbl, right] {15} (v2);
    \draw[edge] (v1) -- node[lbl, above right] {10} (t);
    \draw[edge] (v2) -- node[lbl, below right] {10} (t);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Formal Mathematical Model}

A flow network is defined as a directed graph $G = (V, E)$, where:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $V$ is a set of vertices, including a specific \textbf{source node} $s$ and a \textbf{sink node} $t$.
    \item $E$ is a set of directed edges.
    \item Each edge $(u, v) \in E$ has a non-negative \textbf{capacity} $c(u, v) \ge 0$, representing the maximum amount of flow that can pass through that edge.
\end{itemize}

A \textbf{flow} is a function $f: V \times V \to \mathbb{R}$ that assigns a numerical flow value to each edge. To be considered a valid flow, this function must satisfy two strict conditions:

\textbf{1. Capacity Constraint:}\\
The flow through any given edge cannot exceed the capacity of that edge, and it cannot be negative.
$$ 0 \le f(u, v) \le c(u, v) $$
\textit{(For all pairs of nodes $u, v \in V$. If an edge does not exist between $u$ and $v$, $c(u, v) = 0$.)}

\textbf{2. Flow Conservation:}\\
For every intermediate node (every node other than the source $s$ and the sink $t$), the total flow entering the node must exactly equal the total flow leaving the node. No flow can be created, destroyed, or stored at intermediate nodes.
$$ \sum_{v \in V} f(v, u) = \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v) $$
\textit{(For all $u \in V \setminus \{s, t\}$.)}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. The Objective}

The \textbf{value of the flow}, denoted as $|f|$, is the net amount of flow leaving the source node $s$ (which is mathematically guaranteed to equal the net amount of flow arriving at the sink node $t$).

$$ |f| = \sum_{v \in V} f(s, v) - \sum_{v \in V} f(v, s) $$

The objective of the Max-Flow problem is to \textbf{maximize $|f|$}. In other words, the goal is to route as much flow as possible from $s$ to $t$ without violating the capacity or conservation constraints.

\subsubsection*{4. Common Algorithms}
The problem is famously solved using several classic algorithms, the most notable being:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Ford-Fulkerson Method} (using augmenting paths)
    \item \textbf{Edmonds-Karp Algorithm} (a specific implementation of Ford-Fulkerson using Breadth-First Search)
    \item \textbf{Dinic's Algorithm} (using level graphs and blocking flows)
    \item \textbf{Push-Relabel Algorithms} (using preflows and active nodes)
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Consider a car parking problem scenario, where you need to match each of the cars with a parking spot. The cars and the available parking spots are located at different parts of the city and can only accessible through a road network graph, $G$. Let $A$ be the set of cars and $B$ be the set of parking slots. The cost of a car, $a \in A$ to reach to a parking slot, $b \in B$ is the shortest distance (on the graph $G$) between $a$ and $b$. If the spot is unreachable by a car, we can assign it an infinite cost.
\begin{enumerate}
%% Q — 2021-22
\item Formulate the car parking assignment problem using the Max-flow/min-cut framework.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}

%% Q — 2021-22
\item Develop an efficient solution of the above problem to find a match for every car so that the overall cost is minimized.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}
\end{enumerate}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Max-Flow Formulation for the Car Parking Problem}

To formulate the assignment of cars to parking spots as a maximum flow problem, we model the situation as a \textbf{Maximum Bipartite Matching} problem using a flow network.

Let $A = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n\}$ be the set of cars, and $B = \{b_1, b_2, \dots, b_m\}$ be the set of parking spots. We construct a directed flow network $G' = (V', E')$ as follows:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Vertices ($V'$):} 
    Include all cars and parking spots, plus a conceptual \textbf{supersource} $S$ and a \textbf{supersink} $T$. 
    $$V' = A \cup B \cup \{S, T\}$$
    
    \item \textbf{Edges ($E'$) and Capacities ($C$):}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Source to Cars:} Add a directed edge from $S$ to every car $a \in A$ with a capacity of $1$. This ensures each car is assigned at most once.
        $$ \forall a \in A, \ C(S \to a) = 1 $$
        \item \textbf{Cars to Spots:} Add a directed edge from a car $a \in A$ to a parking spot $b \in B$ if and only if the spot is reachable from the car (i.e., shortest distance $d(a, b) < \infty$ on the original road network $G$). Set the capacity of these edges to $1$.
        $$ \forall a \in A, b \in B \text{ where } d(a,b) < \infty, \ C(a \to b) = 1 $$
        \item \textbf{Spots to Sink:} Add a directed edge from every parking spot $b \in B$ to the sink $T$ with a capacity of $1$. This ensures each parking spot accepts at most one car.
        $$ \forall b \in B, \ C(b \to T) = 1 $$
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Conclusion:} By running a Max-Flow algorithm (like Ford-Fulkerson) on $G'$, the maximum integer flow from $S$ to $T$ will represent the maximum number of cars that can be successfully parked. If an edge $a \to b$ carries a flow of 1, it means car $a$ is assigned to parking spot $b$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, node distance=2.5cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=8mm, fill=cA!10, thick},
    edge/.style={->, thick},
    lbl/.style={fill=cA!4, inner sep=1pt, font=\small, draw=none}]

    % Nodes
    \node (S) at (0, 0) {$S$};
    
    \node (a1) at (3, 1.5) {$a_1$};
    \node (a2) at (3, 0) {$a_2$};
    \node (an) at (3, -2) {$a_n$};
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, text=black] at (3, -1) {$\vdots$};
    
    \node (b1) at (6, 2) {$b_1$};
    \node (b2) at (6, 0.5) {$b_2$};
    \node (bm) at (6, -2.5) {$b_m$};
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, text=black] at (6, -1) {$\vdots$};

    \node (T) at (9, 0) {$T$};

    % Set Labels
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, text=cA, font=\Large\bfseries] at (3, 3) {Cars ($A$)};
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, text=cA, font=\Large\bfseries] at (6, 3.5) {Spots ($B$)};

    % Edges S -> A
    \draw[edge] (S) -- node[lbl, above left] {cap: 1} (a1);
    \draw[edge] (S) -- node[lbl, above] {cap: 1} (a2);
    \draw[edge] (S) -- node[lbl, below left] {cap: 1} (an);

    % Edges A -> B
    \draw[edge] (a1) -- (b1);
    \draw[edge] (a1) -- (b2);
    \draw[edge] (a2) -- (b2);
    \draw[edge] (a2) -- (bm);
    \draw[edge] (an) -- (bm);

    % Edges B -> T
    \draw[edge] (b1) -- node[lbl, above right] {cap: 1} (T);
    \draw[edge] (b2) -- node[lbl, above] {cap: 1} (T);
    \draw[edge] (bm) -- node[lbl, below right] {cap: 1} (T);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Efficient Solution to Minimize Overall Cost}

Finding a matching that just assigns cars to spots is insufficient if we want to \textit{minimize the overall distance driven}. This elevates the problem to the \textbf{Minimum-Cost Maximum-Flow (MCMF)} problem (or equivalent Bipartite Assignment Problem). 

We can solve this efficiently in two main phases:

\paragraph{Phase 1: Compute Shortest Paths on Road Network $G$}
We must first calculate the exact cost $d(a, b)$ for every car $a \in A$ to every spot $b \in B$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item Run \textbf{Dijkstra's Algorithm} from each car node $a \in A$ on the city road graph $G$.
    \item This generates an $|A| \times |B|$ cost matrix where the entry $(a, b)$ is the shortest path distance $d(a, b)$.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Phase 2: Minimum-Cost Maximum-Flow (Successive Shortest Path Algorithm)}
We take the flow network $G'$ created in part 1 and assign a \textbf{cost per unit of flow} to each edge:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $cost(S \to a) = 0$ for all $a \in A$.
    \item $cost(a \to b) = d(a, b)$ for all valid pairs.
    \item $cost(b \to T) = 0$ for all $b \in B$.
\end{itemize}
The capacity of all edges remains $1$. We then apply the \textbf{Successive Shortest Path Algorithm}:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Initialize flow $f = 0$ on all edges.
    \item While there is a path from $S$ to $T$ in the residual graph $G'_f$:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Find the \textit{shortest} path $P$ from $S$ to $T$ in $G'_f$ with respect to the edge costs. (Since initial costs are positive, we can use Dijkstra. For subsequent iterations with negative residual edge costs, we use \textbf{Bellman-Ford} or Dijkstra with \textbf{Johnson's vertex potentials}).
        \item The bottleneck capacity of $P$ will always be $1$ because all original capacities are $1$.
        \item Augment $1$ unit of flow along $P$.
        \item Update the residual graph $G'_f$. The residual backward edges will have a capacity of $1$ and a negated cost: $cost(v \to u) = -cost(u \to v)$.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item Stop when $|A|$ units of flow have been pushed (meaning all cars are parked) or no more paths exist (meaning it's impossible to match all cars).
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Alternative: The Hungarian Algorithm}
Since the capacities are $1$, this is strictly a weighted bipartite matching problem (also known as the Assignment Problem). If $|A| \le |B|$, we can build the $|A| \times |B|$ cost matrix $M$ where $M_{i,j} = d(a_i, b_j)$ and apply the \textbf{Hungarian Algorithm}. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Efficiency:} The Hungarian algorithm runs in $\mathcal{O}(V^3)$ time (where $V = |A| + |B|$), which is highly efficient and perfectly tailored to guarantee the minimum possible sum of shortest path distances.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item You want to increase the maximum flow of a network as much as possible, but you are only allowed to increase the capacity of one edge. How do you find such an edge? (Give pseudocode). You may assume the existence of algorithms to compute max flow min cut. What's the running of your algorithm?
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Core Strategy}

To increase the maximum flow of a network, the edge whose capacity is increased \textbf{must be a bottleneck}—meaning it must be part of a minimum cut. If an edge is not currently being used to its full capacity (i.e., it is not saturated), increasing its capacity will have absolutely no effect on the overall maximum flow. 

Therefore, the strategy is to:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Compute the initial maximum flow to identify which edges are saturated.
    \item Iterate through only the saturated edges.
    \item For each saturated edge, temporarily remove its capacity constraint (set to $\infty$), and re-evaluate the maximum flow to see how much additional flow can be pushed.
    \item Keep track of the edge that yields the highest overall network flow and return it.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Pseudocode}

We assume the existence of a black-box function \texttt{MaxFlow(G, s, t)} that returns the maximum flow value of the network $G$ from source $s$ to sink $t$.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Find Optimal Edge to Increase Capacity}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require{Flow network $G=(V, E)$, source $s$, sink $t$, and edge capacities $c$}
\Ensure{The edge $e$ that provides the maximum increase in total flow}
\State $initial\_flow \gets \text{MaxFlow}(G, s, t)$
\State $best\_edge \gets \text{NULL}$
\State $max\_increase \gets 0$

\For{each edge $e \in E$}
    \State \Comment{Optimization: Only check edges that are currently bottlenecks}
    \If{flow through $e$ equals $c(e)$ in the initial max flow result}
        \State $original\_cap \gets c(e)$
        \State $c(e) \gets \infty$ \Comment{Temporarily allow infinite flow through $e$}
        
        \State $new\_flow \gets \text{MaxFlow}(G, s, t)$
        \State $increase \gets new\_flow - initial\_flow$
        
        \If{$increase > max\_increase$}
            \State $max\_increase \gets increase$
            \State $best\_edge \gets e$
        \EndIf
        
        \State $c(e) \gets original\_cap$ \Comment{Restore the edge's original capacity}
    \EndIf
\EndFor

\State \Return $best\_edge$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Running Time Analysis}

Let $T_{MF}(V, E)$ be the time complexity of the chosen Max-Flow algorithm (e.g., Edmonds-Karp, Dinic's).

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Initial Computation:} The algorithm computes the maximum flow once at the beginning, taking $O(T_{MF}(V, E))$ time.
    \item \textbf{Iteration:} The loop runs for every saturated edge. In the absolute worst-case scenario, every edge in the graph $E$ is saturated. 
    \item \textbf{Re-computation:} Inside the loop, the max flow algorithm is called again. This takes $|E| \times O(T_{MF}(V, E))$ time.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Total Time Complexity:} 
$$ \mathcal{O}(|E| \cdot T_{MF}(V, E)) $$

\textbf{Practical Optimization Note:} 
While the worst-case asymptotic time remains the same, the practical running time can be drastically reduced. Instead of running \texttt{MaxFlow(G, s, t)} from scratch inside the loop, we can run the algorithm starting from the \textbf{residual graph} $G_f$ generated by the initial flow. Setting $c(e) = \infty$ simply adds an infinite capacity forward edge in $G_f$. The search then only takes time proportional to finding the \textit{additional} augmenting paths, which is heavily pruned compared to a cold start.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2020-21
\item The edge connectivity of an undirected graph is the minimum number $k$ of edges that must be removed to disconnect the graph. For example, the edge connectivity of a tree is 1, and the edge connectivity of a cyclic chain of vertices is 2. Show how to determine the edge connectivity of an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ by running a maximum-flow algorithm on at most $|V|$ flow networks, each having $O(V)$ vertices and $O(E)$ edges.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Conceptual Foundation}

The edge connectivity of an undirected graph $G$, denoted as $\lambda(G)$, is the minimum number of edges to remove to disconnect the graph. By the \textbf{Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem}, if we set the capacity of every edge to $1$, the maximum flow between any two vertices $s$ and $t$ is exactly equal to the minimum number of edges that must be removed to disconnect $s$ from $t$ (the $s-t$ min cut).

To find the global minimum cut of the entire graph, we do not need to check all $\binom{|V|}{2}$ pairs of vertices. Instead, we can fix an \textit{arbitrary} vertex $s$. In the global minimum cut that disconnects the graph into two components $S$ and $T$, the fixed vertex $s$ must belong to one of these sets (say, $S$). Therefore, at least one other vertex $t$ must belong to the other set $T$. By computing the max flow from $s$ to \textit{every other vertex} $t \in V \setminus \{s\}$, we are guaranteed to eventually select a $t$ that lies on the opposite side of the global minimum cut.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Flow Network Construction}

For each max-flow computation, we construct a directed flow network $G' = (V', E')$ from the undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ as follows:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Vertices:} $V' = V$. The network has exactly $|V|$ vertices, satisfying the $O(V)$ constraint.
    \item \textbf{Edges \& Capacities:} For every undirected edge $\{u, v\} \in E$, create two directed edges $(u, v)$ and $(v, u)$ in $E'$, both with a capacity of $1$. The network has exactly $2|E|$ edges, satisfying the $O(E)$ constraint.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, node distance=2.5cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=8mm, fill=cA!10, thick},
    edge/.style={-, thick, draw=black},
    dedge/.style={->, thick, draw=blue!70!black},
    lbl/.style={fill=cA!4, inner sep=1pt, font=\small, draw=none, text=black}]

    % Undirected
    \node (u1) at (0, 0) {$u$};
    \node (v1) at (3, 0) {$v$};
    \draw[edge] (u1) -- node[lbl, above] {undirected} (v1);

    % Arrow
    \node[draw=none, fill=none] at (4.5, 0) {\Large $\implies$};

    % Directed
    \node (u2) at (6, 0) {$u$};
    \node (v2) at (9, 0) {$v$};
    \draw[dedge] (u2) to[bend left=15] node[lbl, above] {cap: 1} (v2);
    \draw[dedge] (v2) to[bend left=15] node[lbl, below] {cap: 1} (u2);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Algorithm}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Compute Edge Connectivity $\lambda(G)$}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require{Undirected graph $G=(V, E)$}
\Ensure{Edge connectivity $k$}
\State Construct directed flow network $G' = (V', E')$ with unit capacities as described.
\State Select an arbitrary starting vertex $s \in V'$.
\State $k \gets \infty$
\For{each vertex $t \in V' \setminus \{s\}$}
    \State $current\_flow \gets \text{MaxFlow}(G', s, t)$
    \If{$current\_flow < k$}
        \State $k \gets current\_flow$
    \EndIf
\EndFor
\State \Return $k$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsubsection*{4. Complexity and Constraint Verification}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Number of Flow Networks:} The algorithm runs the max-flow subroutine exactly $|V| - 1$ times. This strictly satisfies the condition of running it on \textit{at most $|V|$} flow networks.
    \item \textbf{Network Size:} As established, each network $G'$ maintains $O(V)$ vertices and $O(E)$ edges.
    \item \textbf{Total Running Time:} If Edmonds-Karp is used, the max flow on a unit-capacity network takes $O(|V| \cdot |E|^2)$, though specialized unit-capacity algorithms like Dinic's run in $O(|E| \min(|V|^{2/3}, \sqrt{|E|}))$. Running this $|V|-1$ times yields an overall highly efficient polynomial-time algorithm for edge connectivity.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2021-22
\item Ad-hoc networks are made up of cheap, low-powered wireless devices, which can communicate within a limited range. Consider an ad-hoc wireless network where any two devices can communicate if and only if the distance between them is at most $D$. To improve reliability, we require each device $x$ to have $k$ potential backup devices, all within distance $D$ of $x$; we call these $k$ devices the backup set of $x$. Also, we do not want any device to be in the backup set of more than $b$ devices, say; otherwise, a single failure might affect a large fraction of the network.

\medskip

\noindent Suppose we are given the communication distance $D$, parameters $b$ and $k$, and an array $d[1 \dots n, 1 \dots n]$ of distances, where $d[i, j]$ is the distance between device $i$ and device $j$. The problem is to design and analyze an algorithm that either computes a backup set of size $k$ for each of the $n$ devices, such that no device appears in more than $b$ backup sets, or correctly reports that no good collection of backup sets exists. Now answer the following questions: \textbf{(10+7+3)}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Reduce this problem to a max-flow problem. Describe a max-flow problem (a directed, capacitated graph $G$, a source and sink pair) such that a good collection of backup sets exist if and only if the max-flow in $G$ has a certain size. What is this size?
    \item Prove the correctness of your reduction. Show that a good collection of backup sets exist if and only if the max-flow in $G$ has a certain size.
    \item Give an algorithm to solve this max-flow problem. What is the running time of this algorithm as a function of $n$, $k$ and $b$?
\end{enumerate}
\mk{23} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Reduction to a Max-Flow Problem}

We can model this problem as finding a constrained assignment in a bipartite graph. We construct a directed, capacitated flow network $G = (V', E')$ as follows:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Vertices ($V'$):} 
    Create a supersource $S$ and a supersink $T$. 
    For the $n$ devices, create two sets of nodes:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $U = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_n\}$ representing the devices in their capacity as \textit{receivers} of backups.
        \item $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\}$ representing the devices in their capacity as \textit{providers} of backups.
    \end{itemize}
    Thus, $V' = \{S, T\} \cup U \cup V$.

    \item \textbf{Edges ($E'$) and Capacities ($C$):}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{Source to Receivers:} Add an edge from $S$ to every node $u_i \in U$ with a capacity of $k$. This enforces that each device requires exactly $k$ backups.
        $$ \forall i \in \{1 \dots n\}, \ C(S \to u_i) = k $$
        \item \textbf{Providers to Sink:} Add an edge from every node $v_j \in V$ to $T$ with a capacity of $b$. This enforces the constraint that no device can act as a backup for more than $b$ devices.
        $$ \forall j \in \{1 \dots n\}, \ C(v_j \to T) = b $$
        \item \textbf{Receivers to Providers:} Add an edge from $u_i$ to $v_j$ if and only if device $j$ is a valid backup for device $i$. This is true when $i \neq j$ and the distance $d[i, j] \le D$. Set the capacity of these edges to $1$, ensuring device $j$ is assigned to device $i$'s backup set at most once.
        $$ \forall i, j \in \{1 \dots n\}, \text{ if } i \neq j \text{ and } d[i, j] \le D, \ C(u_i \to v_j) = 1 $$
    \end{enumerate}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Target Flow Size:} A good collection of backup sets exists if and only if the max-flow in $G$ has a size of exactly $\mathbf{n \cdot k}$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Stealth, node distance=2.5cm, 
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=8mm, fill=cA!10, thick},
    edge/.style={->, thick},
    lbl/.style={fill=cA!4, inner sep=1pt, font=\small, draw=none}]

    % Nodes
    \node (S) at (0, 0) {$S$};
    
    \node (u1) at (3, 2) {$u_1$};
    \node (u2) at (3, 0) {$u_2$};
    \node (un) at (3, -2.5) {$u_n$};
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, text=black] at (3, -1.2) {$\vdots$};
    
    \node (v1) at (6, 2) {$v_1$};
    \node (v2) at (6, 0) {$v_2$};
    \node (vn) at (6, -2.5) {$v_n$};
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, text=black] at (6, -1.2) {$\vdots$};

    \node (T) at (9, 0) {$T$};

    % Set Labels
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, text=cA, font=\bfseries] at (3, 3) {Receivers ($U$)};
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, text=cA, font=\bfseries] at (6, 3) {Providers ($V$)};

    % Edges S -> U
    \draw[edge] (S) -- node[lbl, above left] {cap: $k$} (u1);
    \draw[edge] (S) -- node[lbl, above] {cap: $k$} (u2);
    \draw[edge] (S) -- node[lbl, below left] {cap: $k$} (un);

    % Edges U -> V (Example valid connections)
    \draw[edge] (u1) -- node[lbl, above, pos=0.3] {cap: 1} (v2);
    \draw[edge] (u1) -- (vn);
    \draw[edge] (u2) -- (v1);
    \draw[edge] (un) -- (v2);

    % Edges V -> T
    \draw[edge] (v1) -- node[lbl, above right] {cap: $b$} (T);
    \draw[edge] (v2) -- node[lbl, above] {cap: $b$} (T);
    \draw[edge] (vn) -- node[lbl, below right] {cap: $b$} (T);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Proof of Correctness}

We must prove that a valid collection of backup sets exists $\iff$ the max flow is $n \cdot k$.

\textbf{Forward Direction ($\implies$):} \\
Assume a good collection of backup sets exists. Let $B_i$ be the backup set for device $i$, where $|B_i| = k$. We construct a flow as follows: for every $j \in B_i$, send $1$ unit of flow along the path $S \to u_i \to v_j \to T$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item Because $|B_i| = k$, exactly $k$ units of flow leave $S$ for each $u_i$, perfectly saturating the $S \to u_i$ edge (capacity $k$). 
    \item Because no device acts as a backup for more than $b$ devices, node $v_j$ receives flow from at most $b$ distinct $u_i$ nodes. Thus, the flow on $v_j \to T$ is $\le b$, respecting the capacity constraint.
    \item Flow conservation is maintained.
\end{itemize}
The total flow out of $S$ is $\sum_{i=1}^n k = n \cdot k$. Thus, a flow of $n \cdot k$ is achieved.

\textbf{Reverse Direction ($\impliedby$):} \\
Assume the maximum flow in $G$ is exactly $n \cdot k$. Because all edge capacities are integers, the \textbf{Integrality Theorem} guarantees an integer max flow exists (and standard algorithms will find it). 
\begin{itemize}
    \item The total capacity of edges leaving $S$ is $\sum_{i=1}^n k = n \cdot k$. For the max flow to be $n \cdot k$, every edge $S \to u_i$ must be saturated, carrying exactly $k$ units of flow.
    \item By flow conservation, exactly $k$ units of flow must leave each $u_i$. Since the capacities of edges $u_i \to v_j$ are $1$, this means exactly $k$ distinct edges leaving $u_i$ carry $1$ unit of flow. 
    \item These $k$ edges correspond to valid backup devices ($d[i, j] \le D$, $i \neq j$), defining a backup set of size exactly $k$ for device $i$.
    \item By flow conservation at $v_j$, the total flow entering $v_j$ equals the flow leaving $v_j$ on the edge $v_j \to T$. Since $C(v_j \to T) = b$, at most $b$ units of flow enter $v_j$. This guarantees $v_j$ is selected as a backup device at most $b$ times.
\end{itemize}
Thus, a valid collection of backup sets can be extracted from the integer flow.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Algorithm and Time Complexity}

\textbf{Algorithm:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Read the distance matrix $d[n, n]$ and construct the flow network $G=(V', E')$ as described in Part 1.
    \item Run the \textbf{Ford-Fulkerson algorithm} (or Edmonds-Karp) on $G$ to find the maximum integer flow $f^*$.
    \item If $|f^*| < n \cdot k$, report "No good collection of backup sets exists."
    \item If $|f^*| == n \cdot k$, iterate through all edges $u_i \to v_j$. If the flow on the edge is $1$, add device $j$ to the backup set of device $i$. Return the sets.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Time Complexity Analysis:}
Let's analyze the time complexity using the Ford-Fulkerson method:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Graph Construction:} Parsing the $n \times n$ matrix to generate edges takes $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ time. The number of vertices $|V'| = 2n + 2 = \mathcal{O}(n)$. The number of edges $|E'| = \mathcal{O}(n^2)$ in the worst-case dense network.
    \item \textbf{Max-Flow Execution:} Ford-Fulkerson finds an augmenting path in $\mathcal{O}(E)$ time. In the worst case, each augmenting path adds $1$ unit of flow. The maximum possible flow is $f^* = n \cdot k$. Therefore, finding the max flow takes $\mathcal{O}(|E| \cdot f^*) = \mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot nk) = \mathcal{O}(kn^3)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Extracting Results:} Iterating through the bipartite edges to extract the sets takes $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ time.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Overall Running Time:} $\mathcal{O}(k \cdot n^3)$

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2020-21

    \item \textbf{Path chosen:} $S \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to T$
    \item \textbf{Bottleneck capacity:} $\min(6, 5, 6, 6) = 5$
    \item \textbf{Action:} Push 5 units of flow.
\end{itemize}

\textit{Final Residual Network (No more paths from $S$ to $T$ exist):}

    \node (4) at (5.5, -2)  {4};

    \draw (T) -> node[edge label] {10} (4);

The maximum flow is the sum of the bottleneck capacities: 
$$|f_{max}| = 4 + 6 + 4 + 5 = 19$$

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Maximum Flow Network}

This diagram illustrates the original network with the format $flow / capacity$ on each edge.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=cA!4, inner sep=2pt},
    every path/.style={->, line width=0.8pt},
]
\tikzset{>={Stealth[length=12pt, width=8pt, inset=2pt]}}
    \node (S) at (-2, 0)    {S};
    \node (1) at (1, 2)     {1};
    \node (2) at (1, -2)    {2};
    \node (3) at (5.5, 2)   {3};
    \node (4) at (5.5, -2)  {4};
    \node (T) at (9, 0)     {T};

    \draw (S) -> node[edge label, above left]  {10/10} (1);
    \draw (S) -> node[edge label, below left]  {9/10}  (2);
    \draw (1) -> node[edge label, right]       {0/2}   (2);
    \draw (1) -> node[edge label, above]       {4/4}   (3);
    \draw (1) -> node[edge label, above right] {6/8}   (4);
    \draw (2) -> node[edge label, below]       {9/9}   (4);
    \draw (4) -> node[edge label, right]       {5/6}   (3);
    \draw (3) -> node[edge label, above right] {9/10}  (T);
    \draw (4) -> node[edge label, below right] {10/10} (T);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Min-Cut Identification}

To find the minimum cut, we look at the \textbf{Final Residual Network} from Step 4 and identify all nodes that are still reachable from $S$ using edges with positive residual capacity.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item From $S$, we can reach $2$ (residual capacity of $1$).
    \item From $2$, there are no outgoing forward edges with remaining capacity ($2 \to 4$ is fully saturated). The only outgoing residual edge is the backward edge to $S$.
    \item We cannot reach any other nodes.
\end{enumerate}

Therefore, the set of reachable nodes is $A = \{S, 2\}$. 
The set of unreachable nodes is $B = \{1, 3, 4, T\}$.

The \textbf{min-cut} is the partition: \textbf{$(\{S, 2\}, \{1, 3, 4, T\})$}.

We can verify this by summing the capacities of the original edges crossing from set $A$ to set $B$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $c(S, 1) = 10$
    \item $c(2, 4) = 9$
\end{itemize}

Total Min-Cut Capacity = $10 + 9 = \mathbf{19}$. This perfectly matches our max flow value, satisfying the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Define a flow network. What is an augmenting path in a flow network? Explain with illustrative examples. \mk{3+3}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Definition of a Flow Network}

A \textbf{flow network} is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ that satisfies the following conditions:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Capacity Constraint:} Each edge $(u, v) \in E$ has a non-negative capacity $c(u, v) \ge 0$. If an edge $(u, v)$ does not exist in $E$, we assume $c(u, v) = 0$.
    \item \textbf{Source and Sink:} There are two distinguished vertices: a \textit{source} node $s$ (which produces flow) and a \textit{sink} node $t$ (which consumes flow).
    \item \textbf{Connectivity:} For every vertex $v \in V$, there exists a path from $s$ to $t$ that passes through $v$.
\end{itemize}
A \textit{flow} in $G$ is a real-valued function $f: V \times V \to \mathbb{R}$ that satisfies the capacity constraint ($0 \le f(u, v) \le c(u, v)$) and flow conservation ($\sum_{v \in V} f(v, u) = \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v)$ for all $u \in V \setminus \{s, t\}$).

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Definition of an Augmenting Path}

An \textbf{augmenting path} $p$ is a simple path from the source $s$ to the sink $t$ in the \textbf{residual network} $G_f$. 

The residual network $G_f = (V, E_f)$ consists of edges with strictly positive \textit{residual capacity} $c_f(u, v) > 0$. The residual capacity indicates how much additional flow can be pushed along an edge, defined as:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Forward Edges:} $c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v)$ (Remaining capacity on the original edge).
    \item \textbf{Backward Edges:} $c_f(v, u) = f(u, v)$ (Amount of flow that can be canceled or "pushed back").
\end{itemize}
An augmenting path represents a valid route through which we can send additional flow from $s$ to $t$. The maximum amount of flow that can be added along this path is determined by the bottleneck edge, denoted as $c_f(p) = \min_{(u, v) \in p} c_f(u, v)$.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Illustrative Example}

Consider the simple flow network below with source $S$ and sink $T$. 
The edge labels represent \textbf{flow / capacity} ($f(u, v) / c(u, v)$).

\textbf{Scenario 1: Current Flow Network} \\
Suppose we have an existing flow where $2$ units are sent along the path $S \to A \to T$.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=cA!4, inner sep=2pt},
    every path/.style={->, line width=0.8pt},
    >=Stealth
]
    \node (S) at (0, 0) {S};
    \node (A) at (3, 1.5) {A};
    \node (B) at (3, -1.5) {B};
    \node (T) at (6, 0) {T};

    \draw (S) -- node[edge label, above left] {2/5} (A);
    \draw (S) -- node[edge label, below left] {0/4} (B);
    \draw (A) -- node[edge label, right] {0/3} (B);
    \draw (A) -- node[edge label, above right] {2/2} (T);
    \draw (B) -- node[edge label, below right] {0/5} (T);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{Scenario 2: Corresponding Residual Network \& Augmenting Path} \\
We construct the residual network based on the current flow. For edge $S \to A$, the forward residual is $5 - 2 = 3$, and the backward residual is $2$. Edge $A \to T$ is fully saturated (forward $0$, backward $2$).

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=cA!4, inner sep=2pt},
    aug label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=red!10, text=red, inner sep=2pt},
    every path/.style={->, line width=0.8pt},
    >=Stealth
]
    \node (S) at (0, 0) {S};
    \node (A) at (3, 1.5) {A};
    \node (B) at (3, -1.5) {B};
    \node (T) at (6, 0) {T};

    % S to A
    \draw (S) to[bend left=15] node[edge label] {3} (A);
    \draw (A) to[bend left=15] node[edge label] {2} (S);
    
    % S to B (Augmenting path part 1)
    \draw[red, very thick] (S) -- node[aug label] {4} (B);
    
    % A to B
    \draw (A) -- node[edge label] {3} (B);
    
    % A to T
    \draw (T) -- node[edge label, above right] {2} (A); % only backward exists
    
    % B to T (Augmenting path part 2)
    \draw[red, very thick] (B) -- node[aug label] {5} (T);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{Identifying the Augmenting Path:}
In the residual network above, the path \textcolor{red}{\textbf{$S \to B \to T$}} is an \textbf{augmenting path}. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item The residual capacity of this path is the bottleneck: $\min(4, 5) = 4$.
    \item This means we can push exactly $4$ more units of flow from $S$ to $T$ through $B$, bringing the total maximum flow to $2 + 4 = 6$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Find the maximum flow and the minimum cut using the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm.The vertex s is the source and the vertex t is the sink
of the flow network. Integers represent the flow capacities of the edges.

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
  edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none,  font=\normalfont\small},
  every path/.style={->, line width=0.7pt},
]
\tikzset{>={Stealth[length=12pt, width=8pt, inset=2pt]}}
  \node (s) at (-1.5,  0)    {s};
  \node (w) at (3,  0)    {w};
  \node (x) at (5.5, 3) {x};
  \node (y) at (5.5,-3) {y};
  \node (z) at (7.5, 0)   {z};
  \node (t) at (11, 0)   {t};

  \draw (s) -> node[edge label, above left]  {4} (x);
  \draw (s) -> node[edge label, above]       {6} (w);
  \draw (s) -> node[edge label, below left]  {2} (y);
  \draw (w) -> node[edge label, above]  {3} (x);
  \draw (w) -> node[edge label, above]       {2} (z);
  \draw (w) -> node[edge label, below]  {2} (y);
  \draw (x) -> node[edge label, above] {3} (z);
  \draw (x) -> node[edge label, above right] {5} (t);
  \draw (z) -> node[edge label, below] {3} (y);
  \draw (y) -> node[edge label, below right] {7} (t);
  \draw (z) -> node[edge label, above]       {4} (t);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Finding Maximum Flow via Ford-Fulkerson Algorithm}

We will find the maximum flow by repeatedly finding augmenting paths in the residual network from the source $s$ to the sink $t$. We start with an initial flow of 0 on all edges.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Iteration 1:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Path:} $s \to x \to t$
        \item \textbf{Bottleneck Capacity:} $\min(c(s,x), c(x,t)) = \min(4, 5) = 4$
        \item \textbf{Update:} Push 4 units of flow. Flow increases to $4$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Iteration 2:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Path:} $s \to w \to y \to t$
        \item \textbf{Bottleneck Capacity:} $\min(c(s,w), c(w,y), c(y,t)) = \min(6, 2, 7) = 2$
        \item \textbf{Update:} Push 2 units of flow. Flow increases to $4 + 2 = 6$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Iteration 3:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Path:} $s \to w \to z \to t$
        \item \textbf{Bottleneck Capacity:} $\min(c_f(s,w), c(w,z), c(z,t)) = \min(6-2, 2, 4) = \min(4, 2, 4) = 2$
        \item \textbf{Update:} Push 2 units of flow. Flow increases to $6 + 2 = 8$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Iteration 4:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Path:} $s \to y \to t$
        \item \textbf{Bottleneck Capacity:} $\min(c(s,y), c_f(y,t)) = \min(2, 7-2) = \min(2, 5) = 2$
        \item \textbf{Update:} Push 2 units of flow. Flow increases to $8 + 2 = 10$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Iteration 5:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Path:} $s \to w \to x \to t$
        \item \textbf{Bottleneck Capacity:} $\min(c_f(s,w), c(w,x), c_f(x,t)) = \min(6-4, 3, 5-4) = \min(2, 3, 1) = 1$
        \item \textbf{Update:} Push 1 unit of flow. Flow increases to $10 + 1 = 11$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Iteration 6:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Path:} $s \to w \to x \to z \to t$
        \item \textbf{Bottleneck Capacity:} $\min(c_f(s,w), c_f(w,x), c(x,z), c_f(z,t)) = \min(6-5, 3-1, 3, 4-2) = \min(1, 2, 3, 2) = 1$
        \item \textbf{Update:} Push 1 unit of flow. Flow increases to $11 + 1 = 12$.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

At this point, all edges leaving the source $s$ ($s \to x, s \to w, s \to y$) are fully saturated. There are no more augmenting paths from $s$ to $t$ in the residual graph. 

\textbf{Maximum Flow} = $\mathbf{12}$

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Final Maximum Flow Network}

The following network illustrates the final state with edge labels in the format \textbf{flow/capacity}.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
  edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=cA!4, inner sep=2pt},
  every path/.style={->, line width=0.8pt},
]
\tikzset{>={Stealth[length=12pt, width=8pt, inset=2pt]}}
  \node (s) at (-1.5,  0)    {s};
  \node (w) at (3,  0)    {w};
  \node (x) at (5.5, 3)   {x};
  \node (y) at (5.5,-3)   {y};
  \node (z) at (7.5, 0)   {z};
  \node (t) at (11, 0)    {t};

  \draw (s) -> node[edge label, above left]  {4/4} (x);
  \draw (s) -> node[edge label, above]       {6/6} (w);
  \draw (s) -> node[edge label, below left]  {2/2} (y);
  \draw (w) -> node[edge label, above left]  {2/3} (x);
  \draw (w) -> node[edge label, above]       {2/2} (z);
  \draw (w) -> node[edge label, below left]  {2/2} (y);
  \draw (x) -> node[edge label, above right] {1/3} (z);
  \draw (x) -> node[edge label, above right] {5/5} (t);
  \draw (z) -> node[edge label, below right] {0/3} (y);
  \draw (y) -> node[edge label, below right] {4/7} (t);
  \draw (z) -> node[edge label, above]       {3/4} (t);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Minimum Cut}

To find the minimum cut, we look at the final residual network and determine the set of vertices reachable from the source $s$ via edges with positive residual capacity.

Since the flow on all outgoing edges from $s$ ($s \to x, s \to w, s \to y$) perfectly equals their respective capacities, their forward residual capacities are $0$. Consequently, no other nodes are reachable from $s$ in the final residual graph.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Set $S$ (Reachable from $s$):} $\{s\}$
    \item \textbf{Set $T$ (Unreachable from $s$):} $\{w, x, y, z, t\}$
\end{itemize}

The \textbf{Minimum Cut} is the partition $\mathbf{(\{s\}, \{w, x, y, z, t\})}$.

We can verify the capacity of this cut by summing the capacities of the original edges crossing from set $S$ to set $T$:
$$Capacity = c(s, x) + c(s, w) + c(s, y) = 4 + 6 + 2 = \mathbf{12}$$
This matches the maximum flow value, consistent with the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Describe the minimum path length augmentation method of Edmonds and Karp and explain how it improves the time complexity of the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. The Minimum Path Length Augmentation Method (Edmonds-Karp)}

The \textbf{Edmonds-Karp algorithm} is a specific implementation of the Ford-Fulkerson method for computing the maximum flow in a flow network. While the generic Ford-Fulkerson method allows choosing \textit{any} augmenting path in the residual network $G_f$, Edmonds and Karp introduced a strict rule for path selection:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Breadth-First Search (BFS):} The algorithm must always choose the shortest augmenting path from the source $s$ to the sink $t$ in the residual graph.
    \item \textbf{Unit Edge Weights:} "Shortest" is defined in terms of the \textit{number of edges}, not the capacities. All edges in the residual graph $G_f$ with $c_f(u, v) > 0$ are treated as having a weight of $1$.
\end{itemize}

By using BFS, the algorithm finds the path with the minimum number of edges, which is the "minimum path length augmentation" method.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. The Problem with Standard Ford-Fulkerson}

In the standard Ford-Fulkerson algorithm (often implemented using Depth-First Search), the choice of path is arbitrary. This leads to several issues:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Pseudo-Polynomial Time:} The time complexity is $O(E |f_{max}|)$, where $E$ is the number of edges and $|f_{max}|$ is the value of the maximum flow. 
    \item \textbf{Capacity Dependency:} If the edge capacities are very large, and the algorithm unfortunately picks paths that only augment a tiny amount of flow (e.g., $1$ unit) back-and-forth across a bottleneck edge, the algorithm can take a very long time to converge. 
    \item \textbf{Non-termination:} If the capacities are irrational numbers, the algorithm is not guaranteed to terminate.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. How Edmonds-Karp Improves Time Complexity}

The Edmonds-Karp method completely eliminates the dependence on edge capacities, reducing the time complexity to a strongly polynomial bound of $\mathbf{O(V E^2)}$, where $V$ is the number of vertices and $E$ is the number of edges. It achieves this through the following properties:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Monotonicity of Shortest Paths:} It can be proven that during the execution of the Edmonds-Karp algorithm, the shortest path distance (in number of edges) from the source $s$ to any vertex $v$ in the residual network \textit{never decreases}.
    
    \item \textbf{Critical Edge Bound:} In every augmenting step, at least one edge on the chosen path becomes a \textbf{critical edge} (the bottleneck edge whose residual capacity becomes zero). Once an edge $(u, v)$ becomes critical, it disappears from the residual network. It can only reappear if flow is pushed backward along $(v, u)$, which only happens if the distance to $u$ has strictly increased.
    
    \item \textbf{Maximum Number of Augmentations:} Because the distance from $s$ to any node is at most $|V|-1$, any specific edge $(u, v)$ can become critical at most $|V|/2$ times. Since there are $O(E)$ edges that can appear in the residual graph, the total number of critical edges (and thus the absolute maximum number of augmenting paths) is bounded by $\mathbf{O(V E)}$.
    
    \item \textbf{Cost per Augmentation:} Each BFS traversal to find the shortest path takes $\mathbf{O(E)}$ time.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Final Complexity Calculation:} \\
Multiplying the maximum number of augmentations by the time it takes to find each augmenting path gives the worst-case time complexity:
$$O(V E) \text{ augmentations} \times O(E) \text{ per BFS} = \mathbf{O(V E^2)}$$

This is a massive improvement because the execution time is strictly bound by the network's topology (vertices and edges) and is entirely independent of the numerical values of the flow capacities.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Find the value of the maximum flow by drawing residual networks at each step. Draw the maximum flow network and identify the min-cut.

\begin{center}

]

  \node (b) at (4, 1)   {};
  \node (c) at (7, 1)   {};
  \node (d) at (1,-2)   {};
  \node (e) at (4,-2)   {};
  \node (f) at (7,-2)   {};

  \draw (b) -> node[edge label, right] {5} (f);

\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2016--17}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Step-by-Step Residual Networks}

We will find the maximum flow by repeatedly finding augmenting paths in the residual network from the source $s$ to the sink $t$. For clarity, the empty nodes in the given problem have been labeled $a, b, c, d, e, f$.

\textbf{Step 1: First Augmenting Path}\\
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path chosen:} $s \to a \to b \to c \to t$
    \item \textbf{Bottleneck capacity:} $\min(10, 8, 7, 5) = 5$
    \item \textbf{Action:} Push 5 units of flow along this path.
\end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Bottleneck capacity:} $\min(5, 5, 3, 5) = 3$
    \item \textbf{Action:} Push 3 units of flow along this path.
\end{itemize}

\textit{Residual Network after Step 3:}
