\textbf{Step 4: Fourth Augmenting Path}\\
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path chosen:} $s \to a \to b \to f \to t$
    \item \textbf{Bottleneck capacity:} $\min(2, 3, 5, 2) = 2$
    \item \textbf{Action:} Push 2 units of flow along this path.
\end{itemize}

\textit{Final Residual Network after Step 4 (No more paths from $s$ to $t$):}

]

    \node (b) at (4, 1)     {b};
    \node (c) at (7, 1)     {c};
    \node (d) at (1,-2)     {d};
    \node (e) at (4,-2)     {e};

The maximum flow is the sum of the bottleneck capacities: 
$$|f_{max}| = 5 + 5 + 3 + 2 = \mathbf{15}$$

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Maximum Flow Network}

This diagram illustrates the original network with the final format \textbf{flow/capacity} on each edge.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=cA!4, inner sep=2pt},
    every path/.style={->, line width=0.8pt},
]
\tikzset{>={Stealth[length=12pt, width=8pt, inset=2pt]}}
    \node (s) at (-2,-0.5)  {s};
    \node (a) at (1, 1)     {a};
    \node (b) at (4, 1)     {b};
    \node (c) at (7, 1)     {c};
    \node (d) at (1,-2)     {d};
    \node (e) at (4,-2)     {e};
    \node (f) at (7,-2)     {f};
    \node (t) at (10, -0.5) {t};

    \draw (s) -> node[edge label, above left]  {10/10} (a);
    \draw (s) -> node[edge label, below left]  {5/5}   (d);
    \draw (a) -> node[edge label, above]       {7/8}   (b);
    \draw (b) -> node[edge label, above]       {5/7}   (c);
    \draw (c) -> node[edge label, above right] {5/5}   (t);
    \draw (d) -> node[edge label, below]       {5/7}   (e);
    \draw (a) -> node[edge label, right]       {3/5}   (e);
    \draw (b) -> node[edge label, right]       {2/5}   (f);
    \draw (e) -> node[edge label, right]       {8/8}   (f);
    \draw (f) -> node[edge label, right]       {10/10} (t);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Minimum Cut Identification}

To find the minimum cut, we look at the \textbf{Final Residual Network} from Step 4 and identify all nodes that are still reachable from the source $s$ using edges with positive residual capacity.

\begin{itemize}
    \item Outgoing from $s$: Both $s \to a$ and $s \to d$ are fully saturated ($10/10$ and $5/5$). Their forward residual capacity is $0$.
    \item Therefore, no other nodes in the network can be reached from $s$.
\end{itemize}

This gives us the partition sets:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Reachable Set $A$:} $\{s\}$
    \item \textbf{Unreachable Set $B$:} $\{a, b, c, d, e, f, t\}$
\end{itemize}

The \textbf{min-cut} is the partition: $\mathbf{(\{s\}, \{a, b, c, d, e, f, t\})}$.

We verify this by summing the capacities of the original edges crossing from set $A$ to set $B$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $c(s, a) = 10$
    \item $c(s, d) = 5$
\end{itemize}
Total Min-Cut Capacity = $10 + 5 = \mathbf{15}$. This perfectly matches our max flow value.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What is a flow network? Explain how network flow algorithms allow us to find the maximum bipartite matching. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2016--17}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Definition of a Flow Network}

A \textbf{flow network} is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ consisting of a set of vertices $V$ and a set of directed edges $E$, subject to the following properties:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Capacity Constraint:} Every edge $(u, v) \in E$ has a non-negative real-valued capacity $c(u, v) \ge 0$. If an edge does not exist, its capacity is considered $0$.
    \item \textbf{Source and Sink:} There are two special vertices: a \textit{source} $s$ with no incoming edges (produces flow) and a \textit{sink} $t$ with no outgoing edges (consumes flow).
    \item \textbf{Flow Function:} A flow is a function $f: V \times V \to \mathbb{R}$ that satisfies two conditions for all nodes:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \textit{Capacity Constraint:} $0 \le f(u, v) \le c(u, v)$.
        \item \textit{Flow Conservation:} For every node $u$ except $s$ and $t$, the total incoming flow equals the total outgoing flow: $\sum_{v \in V} f(v, u) = \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v)$.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Maximum Bipartite Matching using Network Flow}

A \textbf{bipartite graph} is a graph $G = (V, E)$ where the vertex set $V$ can be partitioned into two disjoint sets, $L$ and $R$, such that every edge connects a vertex in $L$ to a vertex in $R$. A \textbf{matching} is a subset of edges where no two edges share a common vertex. The \textbf{maximum bipartite matching} problem asks to find the matching with the maximum possible number of edges.

We can elegantly solve this using network flow algorithms (like Ford-Fulkerson or Edmonds-Karp) by transforming the bipartite graph into a flow network. 

\textbf{Step-by-Step Reduction:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Add Source and Sink:} Create a new super-source node $s$ and a super-sink node $t$.
    \item \textbf{Connect Source to Left Set ($L$):} Add a directed edge from $s$ to every node $u \in L$. Assign a capacity of $1$ to each of these edges. (This ensures each node in $L$ can be matched at most once).
    \item \textbf{Direct Original Edges:} Direct all the original undirected edges in $E$ from $L$ to $R$. Assign each of these edges a capacity of $1$ (or $\infty$, as the flow is bottlenecked by the $s$ and $t$ edges anyway).
    \item \textbf{Connect Right Set ($R$) to Sink:} Add a directed edge from every node $v \in R$ to $t$. Assign a capacity of $1$ to each of these edges. (This ensures each node in $R$ can be matched at most once).
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Why it works (The Integrality Theorem):} \\
Because all edge capacities in our newly constructed flow network are integers (specifically $1$), algorithms like Ford-Fulkerson are guaranteed to find an \textit{integral} maximum flow. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $1$ unit of flow passes through an edge $(u, v)$ where $u \in L$ and $v \in R$, it means $u$ and $v$ are \textbf{matched}.
    \item The value of the maximum flow $|f_{max}|$ from $s$ to $t$ will exactly equal the number of edges in the maximum bipartite matching.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Illustrative Diagram of the Transformation}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=white, inner sep=1pt},
    every path/.style={line width=0.8pt},
    >=Stealth
]

    % Original Bipartite Graph
    \node[draw=none] at (1, 3.5) {Original Bipartite Graph};
    \node (L1) at (0, 2) {L1};
    \node (L2) at (0, 0) {L2};
    \node (L3) at (0, -2) {L3};
    
    \node (R1) at (2, 2) {R1};
    \node (R2) at (2, 0) {R2};
    \node (R3) at (2, -2) {R3};
    
    \draw (L1) -- (R1);
    \draw (L1) -- (R2);
    \draw (L2) -- (R1);
    \draw (L3) -- (R2);
    \draw (L3) -- (R3);

    % Flow Network Graph
    \node[draw=none] at (8, 3.5) {Corresponding Flow Network};
    \node (S) at (4, 0) {s};
    
    \node (fL1) at (6, 2) {L1};
    \node (fL2) at (6, 0) {L2};
    \node (fL3) at (6, -2) {L3};
    
    \node (fR1) at (9, 2) {R1};
    \node (fR2) at (9, 0) {R2};
    \node (fR3) at (9, -2) {R3};
    
    \node (T) at (11, 0) {t};
    
    % Edges from S to L (Capacity 1)
    \draw[->, blue, thick] (S) -- node[edge label] {1} (fL1);
    \draw[->, blue, thick] (S) -- node[edge label] {1} (fL2);
    \draw[->, blue, thick] (S) -- node[edge label] {1} (fL3);
    
    % Directed Bipartite Edges (Capacity 1)
    \draw[->] (fL1) -- node[edge label] {1} (fR1);
    \draw[->] (fL1) -- node[edge label] {1} (fR2);
    \draw[->] (fL2) -- node[edge label] {1} (fR1);
    \draw[->] (fL3) -- node[edge label] {1} (fR2);
    \draw[->] (fL3) -- node[edge label] {1} (fR3);
    
    % Edges from R to T (Capacity 1)
    \draw[->, red, thick] (fR1) -- node[edge label] {1} (T);
    \draw[->, red, thick] (fR2) -- node[edge label] {1} (T);
    \draw[->, red, thick] (fR3) -- node[edge label] {1} (T);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write the Edmonds-Karp algorithm that solves the maximum-flow problem. Analyze its time complexity for integral capacities. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2015--16}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. The Edmonds-Karp Algorithm}

The Edmonds-Karp algorithm is a specific implementation of the Ford-Fulkerson method. Its defining characteristic is that it uses \textbf{Breadth-First Search (BFS)} to find the shortest augmenting path (in terms of the number of edges) from the source $s$ to the sink $t$ in the residual network.

\vspace{0.5em}
\noindent\textbf{Pseudocode:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Initialize Flow:} For each edge $(u, v) \in E$:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $f(u, v) = 0$
        \item $f(v, u) = 0$
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Find Augmenting Path:} \textbf{while} there exists a path $p$ from $s$ to $t$ in the residual network $G_f$, found using \textbf{BFS} (where all edge weights are considered 1):
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \textit{// Find the bottleneck capacity of path $p$}
        \item $c_f(p) = \min \{ c_f(u, v) \mid (u, v) \text{ is in } p \}$
        
        \item \textit{// Augment flow along the path}
        \item \textbf{for} each edge $(u, v)$ in $p$:
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \textbf{if} $(u, v)$ is a forward edge in the original graph:
            \begin{itemize}
                \item $f(u, v) = f(u, v) + c_f(p)$
            \end{itemize}
            \item \textbf{else} (it is a backward edge representing flow $f(v, u)$):
            \begin{itemize}
                \item $f(v, u) = f(v, u) - c_f(p)$
            \end{itemize}
        \end{itemize}
    \end{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Return} the maximum flow $f$.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Time Complexity Analysis}

For a graph with $V$ vertices and $E$ edges, we analyze the complexity by looking at the cost of finding a path and the maximum number of times we can augment the flow.

\textbf{Cost of finding a path:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item The algorithm uses Breadth-First Search (BFS) to find the shortest path in the unweighted residual network $G_f$.
    \item A standard BFS traversal takes $\mathbf{O(V + E)}$ time. Since $E \ge V - 1$ for any connected flow network, this simplifies to $\mathbf{O(E)}$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Maximum number of augmentations:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item A crucial property of using BFS is that the shortest path distance $\delta(s, v)$ from the source to any vertex $v$ in the residual graph increases monotonically over the execution of the algorithm.
    \item During each augmentation, at least one edge $(u, v)$ on the path becomes a \textbf{critical edge}. An edge is critical if it is the bottleneck ($c_f(u, v) = c_f(p)$). 
    \item Once an edge $(u, v)$ becomes critical, its residual capacity drops to $0$, and it disappears from the residual network.
    \item For the edge $(u, v)$ to reappear in the residual network, flow must be pushed backward from $v$ to $u$. This can only happen if the distance from $s$ to $u$ has increased by at least $2$.
    \item The maximum distance from $s$ to any node without cycles is $V - 1$. Therefore, a single edge can become critical at most $(V - 1) / 2 = \mathbf{O(V)}$ times.
    \item Since there are $O(E)$ possible edges that can be present in the residual graph, the absolute maximum number of critical edges (and thus the total number of augmenting paths) is bounded by $\mathbf{O(V E)}$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Total Time Complexity Calculation:}
$$ \text{Total Time} = (\text{Max Augmentations}) \times (\text{Time per BFS}) $$
$$ \text{Total Time} = O(V E) \times O(E) = \mathbf{O(V E^2)} $$

\textbf{Note on Integral Capacities:}
While the general Ford-Fulkerson algorithm has a complexity of $O(E |f_{max}|)$ (which is pseudo-polynomial and heavily relies on capacities being integers to ensure termination), the Edmonds-Karp complexity of $O(V E^2)$ is \textbf{strongly polynomial}. It relies entirely on the network's structural topology (vertices and edges) rather than the magnitude of the integral capacities. Integral capacities simply guarantee that the flow pushed in each iteration, and the final maximum flow, are exact integers, preventing floating-point precision issues.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Explain how network flow algorithms allow us to find the maximum bipartite matching. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2015--16}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. The Maximum Bipartite Matching Problem}

A \textbf{bipartite graph} is an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ in which the vertex set $V$ can be partitioned into two disjoint sets, $L$ (Left) and $R$ (Right), such that every edge connects a vertex in $L$ to a vertex in $R$. 

A \textbf{matching} is a subset of edges $M \subseteq E$ such that no two edges in $M$ share a common vertex. The \textbf{maximum bipartite matching} problem asks us to find a matching of maximum size (i.e., containing the largest possible number of edges).

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Reduction to a Network Flow Problem}

We can solve the maximum bipartite matching problem by transforming the bipartite graph $G$ into a flow network $G' = (V', E')$. This transformation is done through the following systematic steps:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Add Source and Sink:} Introduce two new vertices: a super-source node $s$ and a super-sink node $t$. The new vertex set is $V' = V \cup \{s, t\}$.
    
    \item \textbf{Connect Source to Left Set ($L$):} For every vertex $u \in L$, add a directed edge from $s$ to $u$. Assign a capacity of $c(s, u) = 1$ to each of these edges. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textit{Purpose:} This ensures that each node in the left set can supply at most 1 unit of flow, meaning it can be matched to at most one node in $R$.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Direct the Original Edges:} Replace each original undirected edge $(u, v) \in E$ (where $u \in L$ and $v \in R$) with a directed edge from $u$ to $v$. Assign a capacity of $c(u, v) = 1$ (or $\infty$) to each of these edges.
    
    \item \textbf{Connect Right Set ($R$) to Sink:} For every vertex $v \in R$, add a directed edge from $v$ to $t$. Assign a capacity of $c(v, t) = 1$ to each of these edges.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textit{Purpose:} This ensures that each node in the right set can route at most 1 unit of flow to the sink, meaning it can be matched to at most one node in $L$.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Proof of Correctness (Why this works)}

Once the flow network $G'$ is constructed, we apply a standard network flow algorithm (like Ford-Fulkerson or Edmonds-Karp) to find the maximum flow from $s$ to $t$.

\textbf{The Integrality Theorem:} Because all edge capacities in $G'$ are integers (specifically $1$), the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm is guaranteed to produce an \textbf{integral maximum flow}. This means the flow through any edge is either $0$ or $1$.

\textbf{One-to-One Correspondence:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $1$ unit of flow passes through an edge $(u, v)$ where $u \in L$ and $v \in R$, we include the original edge $(u, v)$ in our matching $M$.
    \item The capacity constraints $c(s, u) = 1$ and $c(v, t) = 1$ strictly enforce the rule that node $u$ and node $v$ can have at most $1$ unit of flow passing through them. Thus, no two selected edges can share a vertex.
    \item Therefore, the value of the maximum flow $|f_{max}|$ in $G'$ is exactly equal to the maximum number of edges in the bipartite matching $M$ of $G$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{4. Illustrative Diagram}

The following diagram illustrates the transformation from a standard bipartite graph to its corresponding flow network.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=white, inner sep=1pt},
    every path/.style={line width=0.8pt},
    >=Stealth
]

    % --- Original Bipartite Graph ---
    \node[draw=none, font=\bfseries\normalsize] at (1, 3.5) {1. Bipartite Graph $G$};
    
    \node (L1) at (0, 2) {L1};
    \node (L2) at (0, 0) {L2};
    \node (L3) at (0, -2) {L3};
    
    \node (R1) at (2, 2) {R1};
    \node (R2) at (2, 0) {R2};
    \node (R3) at (2, -2) {R3};
    
    \draw (L1) -- (R1);
    \draw (L1) -- (R2);
    \draw (L2) -- (R1);
    \draw (L3) -- (R2);
    \draw (L3) -- (R3);

    % --- Flow Network Graph ---
    \node[draw=none, font=\bfseries\normalsize] at (8, 3.5) {2. Flow Network $G'$};
    \node (S) at (4, 0) {s};
    
    \node (fL1) at (6, 2) {L1};
    \node (fL2) at (6, 0) {L2};
    \node (fL3) at (6, -2) {L3};
    
    \node (fR1) at (9, 2) {R1};
    \node (fR2) at (9, 0) {R2};
    \node (fR3) at (9, -2) {R3};
    
    \node (T) at (11, 0) {t};
    
    % Edges from S to L (Capacity 1)
    \draw[->, blue, thick] (S) -- node[edge label] {1} (fL1);
    \draw[->, blue, thick] (S) -- node[edge label] {1} (fL2);
    \draw[->, blue, thick] (S) -- node[edge label] {1} (fL3);
    
    % Directed Bipartite Edges (Capacity 1)
    \draw[->] (fL1) -- node[edge label] {1} (fR1);
    \draw[->] (fL1) -- node[edge label] {1} (fR2);
    \draw[->] (fL2) -- node[edge label] {1} (fR1);
    \draw[->] (fL3) -- node[edge label] {1} (fR2);
    \draw[->] (fL3) -- node[edge label] {1} (fR3);
    
    % Edges from R to T (Capacity 1)
    \draw[->, red, thick] (fR1) -- node[edge label] {1} (T);
    \draw[->, red, thick] (fR2) -- node[edge label] {1} (T);
    \draw[->, red, thick] (fR3) -- node[edge label] {1} (T);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item  A traffic engineer needs to decide which roads to widen to allow maximum traffic from $A$ to $G$. Which roads are to be widened?
\begin{center}

  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small},
    every path/.style={->, line width=0.8pt},
  ]
  \tikzset{>={Stealth[length=10pt, width=6pt]}}

  \node (A) at (0, 0)    {A};
  \node (B) at (2, 2)    {B};
  \node (C) at (1, -2)   {C};
  \node (D) at (4, 0)    {D};
  \node (E) at (6, 2)    {E};
  \node (F) at (5, -2)   {F};
  \node (G) at (7, 0)    {G};

  \draw (A) -> node[edge label, above left]  {5}  (B);
  \draw (A) -> node[edge label, below left]  {4}  (C);
  \draw (A) -> node[edge label, above]       {8}  (D);
  \draw (B) -> node[edge label, right]       {1}  (D);
  \draw (B) -> node[edge label, above]       {1}  (E);
  \draw (D) -> node[edge label, below right] {3}  (C);
  \draw (C) -> node[edge label, below]       {5}  (F);
  \draw (D) -> node[edge label, above left]  {4}  (E);
  \draw (D) -> node[edge label, right]       {5}  (F);
  \draw (E) -> node[edge label, right]       {10} (G);
  \draw (F) -> node[edge label, below right] {5}  (G);

  \end{tikzpicture}

\end{center}
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Identifying the Maximum Flow}

To determine which roads act as bottlenecks, we first need to find the maximum flow from the source $A$ to the sink $G$. We can do this by finding augmenting paths.

\textbf{Augmenting Paths:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path 1:} $A \to C \to F \to G$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Bottleneck: $\min(4, 5, 5) = 4$. Push 4 units.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path 2:} $A \to B \to E \to G$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Bottleneck: $\min(5, 1, 10) = 1$. Push 1 unit.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path 3:} $A \to D \to E \to G$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Bottleneck: $\min(8, 4, 9) = 4$. Push 4 units.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path 4:} $A \to D \to F \to G$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Bottleneck: $\min(4, 5, 1) = 1$. Push 1 unit.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

At this point, the total flow leaving $A$ is $4 + 1 + 4 + 1 = 10$. No more augmenting paths can be found from $A$ to $G$. The \textbf{maximum traffic flow} is $\mathbf{10}$.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Final Flow Network \& The Minimum Cut}

The network below shows the final state with labels representing \textbf{flow/capacity}. To increase the maximum flow, the traffic engineer must widen the roads that form the \textbf{minimum cut} (the absolute bottlenecks of the network).

We identify the minimum cut by finding all nodes reachable from $A$ in the final residual network (i.e., using paths where capacity is not fully saturated).
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Reachable from $A$ (Set $S$):} $\{A, B, C, D, F\}$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $A \to B$ has residual (5-1 = 4).
        \item $A \to D$ has residual (8-5 = 3).
        \item $D \to C$ has residual (3-0 = 3).
        \item $D \to F$ has residual (5-1 = 4).
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Unreachable from $A$ (Set $T$):} $\{E, G\}$
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=cA!4, inner sep=2pt},
    cut label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\bfseries\small, text=red, fill=red!10, inner sep=2pt},
    every path/.style={->, line width=0.8pt},
  ]
  \tikzset{>={Stealth[length=10pt, width=6pt]}}

  \node (A) at (0, 0)    {A};
  \node (B) at (2, 2)    {B};
  \node (C) at (1, -2)   {C};
  \node (D) at (4, 0)    {D};
  \node (E) at (6, 2)    {E};
  \node (F) at (5, -2)   {F};
  \node (G) at (7, 0)    {G};

  % Normal edges
  \draw (A) -> node[edge label, above left]  {1/5}  (B);
  \draw (A) -> node[edge label, below left]  {4/4}  (C);
  \draw (A) -> node[edge label, above]       {5/8}  (D);
  \draw (B) -> node[edge label, right]       {0/1}  (D);
  \draw (D) -> node[edge label, below right] {0/3}  (C);
  \draw (C) -> node[edge label, below]       {4/5}  (F);
  \draw (D) -> node[edge label, right]       {1/5}  (F);
  \draw (E) -> node[edge label, right]       {5/10} (G);

  % Min-cut edges (Bottlenecks)
  \draw[red, very thick] (B) -> node[cut label, above] {1/1}  (E);
  \draw[red, very thick] (D) -> node[cut label, above left] {4/4}  (E);
  \draw[red, very thick] (F) -> node[cut label, below right] {5/5}  (G);

  % Cut line visualization
  \draw[dashed, red, thick, ->] (3.5, 3) .. controls (4.5, 1) and (4.5, -1) .. (6, -3) node[right, font=\bfseries] {Min-Cut};

  \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Decision: Roads to be Widened}

According to the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem, the maximum flow of the network is restricted entirely by the edges crossing the minimum cut from $S$ to $T$. These edges are operating at $100\%$ capacity.

To allow maximum traffic from $A$ to $G$, the traffic engineer needs to widen the following roads:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Road $B \to E$}
    \item \textbf{Road $D \to E$}
    \item \textbf{Road $F \to G$}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Why?} 
Widening any of these specific roads will instantly allow more traffic because there is still unutilized capacity leading up to them and trailing away from them. For example, widening $F \to G$ will allow more traffic to flow via $A \to D \to F$, which currently has unused residual capacity.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Define and explain the following terms in a network flow model: super sink node, augmented path, residual capacity, super source node. \mk{8}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

Here are the definitions and structural explanations of the core components in a network flow model:

\subsection*{1. Super Source Node}
A \textbf{super source node} (often denoted as $s$) is a single, artificial vertex introduced into a network flow graph when there are multiple production sites or starting points. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Function:} It simplifies a multiple-source network into a single-source problem. Directed edges are drawn from this super source to every real source node in the system.
    \item \textbf{Capacity:} The capacity assigned to these new edges is usually set equal to the maximum supply capacity of each respective original source node.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{2. Super Sink Node}
A \textbf{super sink node} (often denoted as $t$) is a single, artificial vertex introduced into a network flow graph when there are multiple consumer sites or destination points.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Function:} It aggregates all the separate destinations into one unified endpoint. Directed edges are drawn from every real sink node in the system to this super sink.
    \item \textbf{Capacity:} The capacity assigned to these new edges is typically set equal to the maximum demand capacity of each respective original sink node.
\end{itemize}



\subsection*{3. Augmented Path (Augmenting Path)}
An \textbf{augmenting path} is a simple, directed path from the source $s$ to the sink $t$ in the \textit{residual network} along which additional flow can be pushed.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Structure:} It can consist of both forward edges (where current flow can be increased) and backward edges (where current flow can be decreased or redirected).
    \item \textbf{Role in Optimization:} According to the Ford-Fulkerson method, a flow network reaches its maximum capacity if and only if no more augmenting paths can be found.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{4. Residual Capacity}
The \textbf{residual capacity} ($c_f$) is the remaining, usable capacity on an edge given its current flow state. It defines how much more flow can be sent through a connection.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Forward Edges:} For an edge $(u,v)$ with capacity $c(u,v)$ and current flow $f(u,v)$, the forward residual capacity is:
    $$c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v)$$
    \item \textbf{Backward Edges:} To allow algorithms to undo bad flow decisions, the backward residual capacity equals the current flow value itself, allowing flow to be pushed back:
    $$c_f(v, u) = f(u, v)$$
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What is the pitfall of Ford-Fulkerson's max-flow min-cut method? How can it be improved? \mk{8}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. The Pitfalls of the Standard Ford-Fulkerson Method}

The core flaw of the basic Ford-Fulkerson algorithm is that it leaves the method for choosing the augmenting path completely arbitrary. If an implementation chooses poorly (for instance, by using a naïve Depth-First Search), it leads to severe performance degradation.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Pseudo-Polynomial Time Complexity:} The worst-case time complexity is $O(E |f_{max}|)$, where $E$ is the number of edges and $|f_{max}|$ is the total value of the maximum flow. The algorithm's runtime is dependent on the actual numerical capacities of the edges, not just the size of the graph.
    
    \item \textbf{The "Bad Path" Pathology (Zig-Zagging):} Consider a network with two parallel paths of huge capacity (e.g., $1,000,000$) connected by a single cross edge of capacity $1$. If the algorithm unluckily chooses an augmenting path that zig-zags across this capacity-$1$ edge, it will only push $1$ unit of flow per iteration. It would take $2,000,000$ iterations to find the max flow, whereas a smart algorithm would find it in just $2$ iterations.
    
    \item \textbf{Non-termination with Irrational Numbers:} If the edge capacities are irrational numbers, the arbitrary path selection means the algorithm is mathematically not guaranteed to terminate. Worse, the flow might converge to a limit that is strictly less than the actual maximum flow.
\end{itemize}



\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. How the Method Can Be Improved}

To eliminate these pitfalls, the algorithm must transition from an arbitrary path selection to a structured, optimized path selection. 

\textbf{Improvement 1: The Edmonds-Karp Algorithm}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{The Fix:} Instead of picking any path, use \textbf{Breadth-First Search (BFS)} to strictly find the shortest augmenting path in the residual network (shortest in terms of the \textit{number of edges}, treating all edge weights as $1$).
    \item \textbf{The Result:} This simple rule prevents the algorithm from infinitely zig-zagging across small bottlenecks. It bounds the maximum number of augmentations to $O(V E)$, resulting in a \textbf{strongly polynomial} time complexity of $\mathbf{O(V E^2)}$. The runtime becomes entirely independent of the edge capacities, ensuring fast termination even with massive or irrational numbers.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Improvement 2: Advanced Graph Algorithms}
For even larger networks, the augmenting path concept itself can be optimized or replaced:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Dinic's Algorithm:} Instead of finding one path at a time, it uses BFS to build a "Level Graph" (grouping nodes by distance from the source) and then pushes a "blocking flow" along multiple paths simultaneously. This drastically improves the time complexity to $\mathbf{O(V^2 E)}$.
    \item \textbf{Push-Relabel (Preflow-Push) Algorithm:} This abandons augmenting paths from source to sink entirely. Instead, it pushes local "preflow" across edges node-by-node based on height gradients (labels). This localizes the optimization, achieving complexities like $\mathbf{O(V^3)}$ or $\mathbf{O(V^2 \sqrt{E})}$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Find the maximum job assignment using a maximum flow algorithm.

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
  every path/.style={->, line width=0.7pt},
]
\tikzset{>={Stealth[length=20pt, width=10pt]}}

  \node (a)  at (0,  3)   {a};
  \node (b)  at (0,  1)   {b};
  \node (c)  at (0, -1)   {c};
  \node (d)  at (0, -3)   {d};
  \node (j1) at (5,  3)   {j1};
  \node (j2) at (5,  1)   {j2};
  \node (j3) at (5, -1)   {j3};
  \node (j4) at (5, -3)   {j4};

  \draw (a) -> (j2);
  \draw (a) -> (j4);
  \draw (b) -> (j1);
  \draw (c) -> (j3);
  \draw (c) -> (j2);
  \draw (c) -> (j4);
  \draw (d) -> (j1);
  \draw (d) -> (j4);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
 \mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Network Transformation}

To apply a maximum flow algorithm (like Ford-Fulkerson or Edmonds-Karp), we construct a flow network $G' = (V', E')$ from the original bipartite graph:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Add a Super Source and Super Sink:} Create a super source node $s$ and a super sink node $t$.
    \item \textbf{Connect the Source:} Add directed edges from $s$ to every applicant node $\{a, b, c, d\}$, each with a capacity of \textbf{1}.
    \item \textbf{Direct the Original Edges:} Direct all the bipartite edges from the applicants to the jobs $\{j1, j2, j3, j4\}$, each with a capacity of \textbf{1}.
    \item \textbf{Connect to the Sink:} Add directed edges from every job node $\{j1, j2, j3, j4\}$ to $t$, each with a capacity of \textbf{1}.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=white, inner sep=1pt},
    every path/.style={->, line width=0.8pt},
    >=Stealth
]
    % Nodes
    \node (s)  at (-2, 0)   {s};
    \node (a)  at (1,  3)   {a};
    \node (b)  at (1,  1)   {b};
    \node (c)  at (1, -1)   {c};
    \node (d)  at (1, -3)   {d};
    \node (j1) at (5,  3)   {j1};
    \node (j2) at (5,  1)   {j2};
    \node (j3) at (5, -1)   {j3};
    \node (j4) at (5, -3)   {j4};
    \node (t)  at (8,  0)   {t};

    % Source edges
    \draw[blue] (s) -- node[edge label] {1} (a);
    \draw[blue] (s) -- node[edge label] {1} (b);
    \draw[blue] (s) -- node[edge label] {1} (c);
    \draw[blue] (s) -- node[edge label] {1} (d);

    % Bipartite edges
    \draw (a) -- node[edge label] {1} (j2);
    \draw (a) -- node[edge label] {1} (j4);
    \draw (b) -- node[edge label] {1} (j1);
    \draw (c) -- node[edge label] {1} (j3);
    \draw (c) -- node[edge label] {1} (j2);
    \draw (c) -- node[edge label] {1} (j4);
    \draw (d) -- node[edge label] {1} (j1);
    \draw (d) -- node[edge label] {1} (j4);

    % Sink edges
    \draw[red] (j1) -- node[edge label] {1} (t);
    \draw[red] (j2) -- node[edge label] {1} (t);
    \draw[red] (j3) -- node[edge label] {1} (t);
    \draw[red] (j4) -- node[edge label] {1} (t);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Execution of the Max Flow Algorithm}

We will repeatedly find augmenting paths from $s$ to $t$ in the residual network. Because all capacities are 1, every augmenting path we find will push exactly $1$ unit of flow. 

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path 1:} $s \to a \to j2 \to t$. Push $\min(1, 1, 1) = 1$ unit. \\
    \textit{Residual updates:} Edges $(s, a)$, $(a, j2)$, and $(j2, t)$ are saturated.
    
    \item \textbf{Path 2:} $s \to b \to j1 \to t$. Push $\min(1, 1, 1) = 1$ unit. \\
    \textit{Residual updates:} Edges $(s, b)$, $(b, j1)$, and $(j1, t)$ are saturated.
    
    \item \textbf{Path 3:} $s \to c \to j3 \to t$. Push $\min(1, 1, 1) = 1$ unit. \\
    \textit{Residual updates:} Edges $(s, c)$, $(c, j3)$, and $(j3, t)$ are saturated.
    
    \item \textbf{Path 4:} $s \to d \to j4 \to t$. Push $\min(1, 1, 1) = 1$ unit. \\
    \textit{Residual updates:} Edges $(s, d)$, $(d, j4)$, and $(j4, t)$ are saturated.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Termination:} At this point, all edges leaving the super source $s$ are fully saturated. There are no more valid augmenting paths from $s$ to $t$ in the residual network. The maximum flow is \textbf{4}.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Maximum Job Assignment}

By the Integrality Theorem, the total maximum flow corresponds to the size of the maximum bipartite matching. The specific edges between the applicants and jobs that carry $1$ unit of flow represent our final assignments. 

Extracting the active edges gives us the following perfect matching:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Applicant $a$} is assigned to \textbf{Job $j2$}
    \item \textbf{Applicant $b$} is assigned to \textbf{Job $j1$}
    \item \textbf{Applicant $c$} is assigned to \textbf{Job $j3$}
    \item \textbf{Applicant $d$} is assigned to \textbf{Job $j4$}
\end{itemize}

This assignment perfectly matches all 4 workers to 4 unique jobs, achieving the maximum possible assignment.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What is a flow network? What is the maximum-flow problem? Explain how one can convert a multiple-source, multiple-sink maximum-flow problem into a problem with a single source and a single sink. \mk{4+2+4=10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2011--12}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Definition of a Flow Network}

A \textbf{flow network} is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ in which each edge $(u, v) \in E$ has a non-negative capacity $c(u, v) \ge 0$. If $(u, v) \notin E$, we assume $c(u, v) = 0$. The network contains two distinguished vertices: a \textbf{source} $s$ (which produces flow) and a \textbf{sink} $t$ (which consumes flow). 

A \textbf{flow} in $G$ is a real-valued function $f: V \times V \to \mathbb{R}$ that satisfies two fundamental properties:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Capacity Constraint:} For all $u, v \in V$, we require $0 \le f(u, v) \le c(u, v)$. The flow along an edge cannot exceed its capacity.
    \item \textbf{Flow Conservation:} For all vertices $u \in V - \{s, t\}$, the total flow entering the node must equal the total flow leaving the node:
    $$ \sum_{v \in V} f(v, u) = \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v) $$
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. The Maximum-Flow Problem}

The \textbf{maximum-flow problem} asks us to find a valid flow $f$ from the source $s$ to the sink $t$ such that the total \textbf{value} of the flow is maximized. 

The value of a flow, denoted as $|f|$, is defined as the total net flow leaving the source (which is equivalent to the total net flow arriving at the sink):
$$ |f| = \sum_{v \in V} f(s, v) - \sum_{v \in V} f(v, s) $$
The objective is to maximize $|f|$ subject to the capacity and conservation constraints.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Converting a Multiple-Source, Multiple-Sink Problem}

Many real-world networks involve multiple starting points (e.g., several factories) and multiple destinations (e.g., several warehouses). Let the set of sources be $\{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k\}$ and the set of sinks be $\{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_m\}$.

Standard max-flow algorithms require a single source and a single sink. We can elegantly convert the multiple-source, multiple-sink problem into an equivalent single-source, single-sink problem using the following steps:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Add a Super-Source:} Create a new, artificial vertex $S$ (the super-source).
    \item \textbf{Connect to Original Sources:} Add directed edges from $S$ to each of the original sources $s_i$. Assign a capacity of $\infty$ (or the maximum possible production capacity of that specific source) to each of these new edges: $c(S, s_i) = \infty$.
    \item \textbf{Add a Super-Sink:} Create another new, artificial vertex $T$ (the super-sink).
    \item \textbf{Connect from Original Sinks:} Add directed edges from each of the original sinks $t_j$ to $T$. Assign a capacity of $\infty$ (or the maximum possible consumption capacity of that specific sink) to each of these new edges: $c(t_j, T) = \infty$.
\end{enumerate}

By solving the standard maximum-flow problem from $S$ to $T$ on this newly constructed network, we perfectly model and solve the original multi-source/multi-sink problem. The infinite capacities ensure that the new artificial edges never act as bottlenecks.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Illustrative Diagram:}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=white, inner sep=1pt},
    every path/.style={->, line width=0.8pt},
    >=Stealth
]

    % Super nodes
    \node[fill=blue!20] (S) at (-2, 0) {S};
    \node[fill=red!20] (T) at (8, 0) {T};
    
    % Original Sources
    \node (s1) at (1, 1.5) {$s_1$};
    \node (s2) at (1, -1.5) {$s_2$};
    
    % Original Sinks
    \node (t1) at (5, 1.5) {$t_1$};
    \node (t2) at (5, -1.5) {$t_2$};
    
    % Intermediate Network (abstract box)
    \draw[dashed, draw=black, fill=gray!10, rounded corners] (2.5, -2.5) rectangle (3.5, 2.5);
    \node[draw=none, rotate=90] at (3, 0) {Original Network};
    
    % Edges from Super-Source
    \draw[blue, thick] (S) -- node[edge label] {$\infty$} (s1);
    \draw[blue, thick] (S) -- node[edge label] {$\infty$} (s2);
    
    % Edges to Super-Sink
    \draw[red, thick] (t1) -- node[edge label] {$\infty$} (T);
    \draw[red, thick] (t2) -- node[edge label] {$\infty$} (T);

    % Dummy connections to network box
    \draw (s1) -- (2.5, 1.5);
    \draw (s2) -- (2.5, -1.5);
    \draw (3.5, 1.5) -- (t1);
    \draw (3.5, -1.5) -- (t2);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm for solving the maximum-flow problem, and analyze the time complexity of the algorithm. Explain how the Edmonds-Karp algorithm improves the bound of the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm. \mk{10+5=15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2011--12}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. The Ford-Fulkerson Algorithm}

The Ford-Fulkerson method relies on the concept of a \textbf{residual network}. It iteratively finds an augmenting path from the source $s$ to the sink $t$ and pushes flow along this path until no more such paths exist.

\vspace{0.5em}
\noindent\textbf{Pseudocode:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Initialize:} For every edge $(u, v) \in E$, set the initial flow to zero: $f(u, v) = 0$ and $f(v, u) = 0$.
    \item \textbf{While} there exists a path $p$ from $s$ to $t$ in the residual network $G_f$ (found using any search algorithm like DFS or BFS):
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \textit{// Find the bottleneck capacity of the path $p$}
        \item $c_f(p) = \min \{ c_f(u, v) \mid (u, v) \text{ is an edge in } p \}$
        \item \textit{// Augment flow along the path}
        \item \textbf{For} each edge $(u, v)$ in path $p$:
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \textbf{If} $(u, v)$ is a forward edge: $f(u, v) = f(u, v) + c_f(p)$
            \item \textbf{Else} (it is a backward edge): $f(v, u) = f(v, u) - c_f(p)$
        \end{itemize}
    \end{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Return} the flow $f$.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Time Complexity Analysis of Ford-Fulkerson}

Let $V$ be the number of vertices, $E$ be the number of edges, and $|f^*|$ be the value of the maximum flow.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Finding a Path:} A single graph traversal (like DFS or BFS) to find an augmenting path in the residual network takes $\mathbf{O(E)}$ time.
    \item \textbf{Number of Augmentations:} Assuming all edge capacities are integers, every augmenting path pushes an integer amount of flow of at least $1$. Therefore, the absolute maximum number of iterations the `while` loop can execute is exactly $|f^*|$.
    \item \textbf{Total Worst-Case Time Complexity:} Multiplying the time per iteration by the maximum number of iterations yields $\mathbf{O(E |f^*|)}$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{The Pitfall:} This complexity is \textbf{pseudo-polynomial} because it depends on the numerical value of the capacities, not just the size of the graph ($V$ and $E$). 



If the algorithm unluckily chooses an alternating "zig-zag" path across a small bottleneck edge (e.g., an edge with capacity $1$ connecting two paths of capacity $10^6$), it could take $2 \times 10^6$ iterations to finish, adding only $1$ unit of flow each time. Furthermore, if capacities are irrational numbers, the arbitrary path selection means the algorithm is not mathematically guaranteed to terminate.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. The Edmonds-Karp Improvement}

The Edmonds-Karp algorithm is an implementation of the Ford-Fulkerson method that completely eliminates the pseudo-polynomial worst-case scenario.

\textbf{How it improves the bound:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{The Strategy:} Instead of picking \textit{any} augmenting path, Edmonds-Karp strictly uses \textbf{Breadth-First Search (BFS)} to find the \textbf{shortest augmenting path} (where "shortest" means the fewest number of edges, treating all edge weights as $1$).
    \item \textbf{Monotonicity:} By always picking the shortest path, it guarantees that the shortest-path distance from the source to any node in the residual network \textit{increases monotonically} over the algorithm's execution.
    \item \textbf{Bounding Augmentations:} Because of this monotonic property, any specific edge can become a "critical edge" (the bottleneck of a path) at most $(V - 1) / 2$ times. Since there are $O(E)$ edges in the residual network, the total number of flow augmentations is strictly bounded to $\mathbf{O(V E)}$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{New Time Complexity:} 
Since the algorithm takes $O(E)$ time for the BFS and does at most $O(V E)$ augmentations, the total time complexity becomes:
$$ O(E) \times O(V E) = \mathbf{O(V E^2)} $$

This makes the algorithm \textbf{strongly polynomial}. Its performance is bounded purely by the structural size of the graph (nodes and edges) and is entirely independent of the maximum flow value $|f^*|$ or the magnitude of the edge capacities.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item State the max-flow min-cut theorem in its simplest form. What is the main argument to prove the theorem? Find a min-cut and its capacity. Find the flow in that cut and the maximum flow in the network.

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
  edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small},
  every path/.style={->, line width=1pt},
]

\tikzset{>={Stealth[length=15pt, width=10pt, inset=3pt]}}

  \node (s)  at (0, 0)    {s};
  \node (v1) at (3, 2)    {$v_1$};
  \node (v2) at (3, -1)   {$v_2$};
  \node (v3) at (6, 2)    {$v_3$};
  \node (v4) at (6, -1)   {$v_4$};
  \node (t)  at (9, 0.5)  {t};

  \draw (s)  -> node[edge label, above left]  {16} (v1);
  \draw (s)  -> node[edge label, below left]  {13} (v2);
  \draw (v2) -> node[edge label, right, pos=0.3] {4}  (v1);
  \draw (v1) -> node[edge label, above]       {10} (v3);
  \draw (v1) -> node[edge label, below right] {9}  (v4);
  \draw (v2) -> node[edge label, below]       {14} (v4);
  \draw (v4) -> node[edge label, right]       {7}  (v3);
  \draw (v3) -> node[edge label, above right] {20} (t);
  \draw (v4) -> node[edge label, below right] {4}  (t);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\mk{17} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2009--10}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. The Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem}

\textbf{Statement:} In any flow network, the maximum value of an $s-t$ flow is exactly equal to the minimum capacity over all $s-t$ cuts in the network.

\textbf{Main Argument to Prove the Theorem:} \\
The proof hinges on the concept of the \textbf{residual network} $G_f$. 
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Assume we have found a flow $f$ using the Ford-Fulkerson method, and the algorithm has terminated because there are no more augmenting paths from the source $s$ to the sink $t$ in the residual network $G_f$.
    \item Let $S$ be the set of all nodes reachable from $s$ in $G_f$, and let $T$ be the set of all remaining nodes (which must include $t$). This defines an $s-t$ cut $(S, T)$.
    \item Because there are no paths from $S$ to $T$ in the residual network:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Every original edge going from $S$ to $T$ must be operating at \textbf{full capacity} (otherwise, it would have residual capacity and allow a path to $T$).
        \item Every original edge going from $T$ to $S$ must have \textbf{zero flow} (otherwise, it would provide a backward residual edge allowing a path from $S$).
    \end{itemize}
    \item Therefore, the net flow across this specific cut $(S, T)$ is exactly equal to its capacity. Since no flow can ever exceed the capacity of \textit{any} cut, this cut must be the minimum possible cut, and our current flow must be the maximum possible flow.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Solving the Given Network}

To find the maximum flow and the minimum cut, we push flow through the network until no augmenting paths remain.

\textbf{Flow Assignments (Flow / Capacity):}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $s \to v_1$: \textbf{10} / 16
    \item $s \to v_2$: \textbf{11} / 13
    \item $v_2 \to v_1$: \textbf{0} / 4
    \item $v_1 \to v_3$: \textbf{10} / 10 \quad \textit{(Saturated)}
    \item $v_1 \to v_4$: \textbf{0} / 9
    \item $v_2 \to v_4$: \textbf{11} / 14
    \item $v_4 \to v_3$: \textbf{7} / 7 \quad \textit{(Saturated)}
    \item $v_4 \to t$: \textbf{4} / 4 \quad \textit{(Saturated)}
    \item $v_3 \to t$: \textbf{17} / 20
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Finding the Min-Cut:}
By examining the saturated edges that bottleneck the network, we find the nodes reachable from $s$ in the residual graph are $S = \{s, v_1, v_2, v_4\}$. The unreachable nodes are $T = \{v_3, t\}$.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Min-Cut Partition:} $S = \{s, v_1, v_2, v_4\}$, $T = \{v_3, t\}$
    \item \textbf{Edges in the Min-Cut (from $S$ to $T$):} $(v_1, v_3)$, $(v_4, v_3)$, and $(v_4, t)$.
    \item \textbf{Capacity of Min-Cut:} $c(v_1, v_3) + c(v_4, v_3) + c(v_4, t) = 10 + 7 + 4 = \mathbf{21}$.
    \item \textbf{Flow in that Cut:} The flow passing through these specific edges is $10 + 7 + 4 = \mathbf{21}$.
    \item \textbf{Maximum Flow:} By the theorem (and as calculated by the flow leaving $s$: $10 + 11 = 21$), the maximum flow in the network is \textbf{21}.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Visual Representation of the Final State \& Min-Cut:}

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=cA!4, inner sep=2pt},
    cut label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\bfseries\small, text=red, fill=red!10, inner sep=2pt},
    every path/.style={->, line width=1pt},
  ]
  \tikzset{>={Stealth[length=12pt, width=8pt, inset=2pt]}}

  \node (s)  at (0, 0)    {s};
  \node (v1) at (3, 2)    {$v_1$};
  \node (v2) at (3, -1)   {$v_2$};
  \node (v3) at (6, 2)    {$v_3$};
  \node (v4) at (6, -1)   {$v_4$};
  \node (t)  at (9, 0.5)  {t};

  % Normal Edges (Flow / Capacity)
  \draw (s)  -> node[edge label, above left]  {10/16} (v1);
  \draw (s)  -> node[edge label, below left]  {11/13} (v2);
  \draw (v2) -> node[edge label, right, pos=0.3] {0/4} (v1);
  \draw (v1) -> node[edge label, below right] {0/9} (v4);
  \draw (v2) -> node[edge label, below]       {11/14} (v4);
  \draw (v3) -> node[edge label, above right] {17/20} (t);

  % Min-Cut Edges (Saturated)
  \draw[red, very thick] (v1) -> node[cut label, above] {10/10} (v3);
  \draw[red, very thick] (v4) -> node[cut label, right] {7/7} (v3);
  \draw[red, very thick] (v4) -> node[cut label, below right] {4/4} (t);

  % Cut line visualization
  \draw[dashed, red, thick, ->] (4.5, 3) .. controls (4.5, 1) and (7.5, 0.5) .. (8, -2) node[right, font=\bfseries] {Min-Cut};

  \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item  Find the maximum flow and the minimum cut. Find also the maximum flow in the minimum cut.

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
  edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small},
  every path/.style={->, line width=0.7pt},
]
\tikzset{>={Stealth[length=12pt, width=8pt, inset=2pt]}}

  \node (s)  at (0, 0)    {s};
  \node (v1) at (3, 3)    {$v_1$};
  \node (v2) at (3, -2)   {$v_2$};
  \node (v3) at (6, 3)    {$v_3$};
  \node (v4) at (6, -2)   {$v_4$};
  \node (t)  at (9, 0.5)  {t};

  \draw (s)  -> node[edge label, above left]  {16} (v1);
  \draw (s)  -> node[edge label, below left]  {13} (v2);
  \draw (v1) to [bend right=20pt] node[edge label, left, pos=0.3]  {10} (v2);
  \draw (v2) to [bend right=20pt] node[edge label, right] {4}  (v1);
  \draw (v1) -> node[edge label, above]       {12} (v3);
  \draw (v3) -> node[edge label, below] {9}  (v2);
  \draw (v2) -> node[edge label, below]       {14} (v4);
  \draw (v4) -> node[edge label, right]       {7}  (v3);
  \draw (v3) -> node[edge label, above right] {20} (t);
  \draw (v4) -> node[edge label, below right] {4}  (t);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2008--09}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Finding the Maximum Flow}

To find the maximum flow from the source $s$ to the sink $t$, we repeatedly push flow through augmenting paths until no more paths exist in the residual network.

\textbf{Augmenting Paths:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path 1:} $s \to v_1 \to v_3 \to t$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Bottleneck capacity: $\min(16, 12, 20) = 12$. 
        \item \textit{Push 12 units.}
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path 2:} $s \to v_2 \to v_4 \to t$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Bottleneck capacity: $\min(13, 14, 4) = 4$. 
        \item \textit{Push 4 units.}
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path 3:} $s \to v_2 \to v_4 \to v_3 \to t$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Residual capacities: $c_f(s, v_2) = 13 - 4 = 9$; $c_f(v_2, v_4) = 14 - 4 = 10$; $c_f(v_4, v_3) = 7$; $c_f(v_3, t) = 20 - 12 = 8$.
        \item Bottleneck capacity: $\min(9, 10, 7, 8) = 7$. 
        \item \textit{Push 7 units.}
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

At this point, the total flow leaving $s$ is $12 + 4 + 7 = \mathbf{23}$. No more augmenting paths can be found from $s$ to $t$. The maximum flow is \textbf{23}.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Finding the Minimum Cut}

The minimum cut separates the network into two sets of vertices, $S$ and $T$. We find $S$ by identifying all nodes reachable from $s$ in the final residual network.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Reachable from $s$ (Set $S$):} $\{s, v_1, v_2, v_4\}$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $s \to v_1$ has residual capacity $16 - 12 = 4$.
        \item $s \to v_2$ has residual capacity $13 - 11 = 2$.
        \item $v_2 \to v_4$ has residual capacity $14 - 11 = 3$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Unreachable from $s$ (Set $T$):} $\{v_3, t\}$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $v_1 \to v_3$, $v_4 \to v_3$, and $v_4 \to t$ are all fully saturated (0 residual capacity).
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

The edges forming the minimum cut are the forward edges crossing from $S$ to $T$:
$$ \text{Cut Edges} = \{ (v_1, v_3), (v_4, v_3), (v_4, t) \} $$
\textbf{Capacity of the Minimum Cut:} $c(v_1, v_3) + c(v_4, v_3) + c(v_4, t) = 12 + 7 + 4 = \mathbf{23}$.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Maximum Flow in the Minimum Cut}

According to the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem, the net flow across any cut in a maximized network is strictly equal to the value of the maximum flow. 

For the minimum cut specifically, all forward edges are perfectly saturated, and all backward edges carry zero flow. Therefore, the total net flow passing through the minimum cut is exactly equal to its capacity:
$$ \text{Flow in Min-Cut} = f(v_1, v_3) + f(v_4, v_3) + f(v_4, t) = 12 + 7 + 4 = \mathbf{23}. $$

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Final Flow Network Diagram:}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=cA!4, inner sep=2pt},
    cut label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\bfseries\small, text=red, fill=red!10, inner sep=2pt},
    every path/.style={->, line width=0.8pt},
]
\tikzset{>={Stealth[length=12pt, width=8pt, inset=2pt]}}

  \node (s)  at (0, 0)    {s};
  \node (v1) at (3, 3)    {$v_1$};
  \node (v2) at (3, -2)   {$v_2$};
  \node (v3) at (6, 3)    {$v_3$};
  \node (v4) at (6, -2)   {$v_4$};
  \node (t)  at (9, 0.5)  {t};

  % Normal edges (Flow/Capacity)
  \draw (s)  -> node[edge label, above left]  {12/16} (v1);
  \draw (s)  -> node[edge label, below left]  {11/13} (v2);
  \draw (v1) to [bend right=20pt] node[edge label, left, pos=0.3]  {0/10} (v2);
  \draw (v2) to [bend right=20pt] node[edge label, right] {0/4}  (v1);
  
  \draw (v3) -> node[edge label, below] {0/9}  (v2);
  \draw (v2) -> node[edge label, below]       {11/14} (v4);
  \draw (v3) -> node[edge label, above right] {19/20} (t);
  
  % Min-Cut Edges (Saturated)
  \draw[red, very thick] (v1) -> node[cut label, above] {12/12} (v3);
  \draw[red, very thick] (v4) -> node[cut label, right] {7/7}  (v3);
  \draw[red, very thick] (v4) -> node[cut label, below right] {4/4}  (t);

  % Cut line visualization
  \draw[dashed, red, thick, ->] (4.5, 4.5) .. controls (4.5, 1) and (7.5, 0.5) .. (8, -3) node[right, font=\bfseries] {Min-Cut};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Discuss how a maximum flow algorithm can be used to determine the maximum number of disjoint paths in directed and undirected graphs. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2007--08}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

Finding the maximum number of disjoint paths between a source vertex $s$ and a sink vertex $t$ is a foundational problem in graph theory, heavily tied to \textbf{Menger's Theorem}. By setting edge and node constraints to unit capacities, we can use a maximum flow algorithm (like Ford-Fulkerson or Edmonds-Karp) to solve these problems efficiently. 

Disjoint paths can be categorized into two types: \textbf{Edge-Disjoint} (paths that share no edges) and \textbf{Vertex-Disjoint} (paths that share no vertices except $s$ and $t$).

---

\subsection*{1. Directed Graphs}

\subsubsection*{Case A: Edge-Disjoint Paths in Directed Graphs}
To find the maximum number of directed paths from $s$ to $t$ that do not share any edges:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Transformation:} Take the original directed graph $G = (V, E)$ and assign a capacity of $\mathbf{c(u, v) = 1}$ to every directed edge $(u, v) \in E$.
    \item \textbf{Run Max-Flow:} Execute a maximum flow algorithm from $s$ to $t$.
    \item \textbf{Path Extraction:} Because all capacities are integers ($1$), the \textit{Integrality Theorem} guarantees that the resulting flow on every edge will be either $0$ or $1$. Each path carrying a unit of flow corresponds to one edge-disjoint path.
    \item \textbf{Correctness:} Since each edge has a capacity of $1$, no two paths can share the same directed edge, ensuring the paths are strictly edge-disjoint. Thus, $\text{Max Flow} = \text{Max Edge-Disjoint Paths}$.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Case B: Vertex-Disjoint Paths in Directed Graphs}
To ensure that paths do not share any intermediate vertices, we must enforce a capacity constraint on the \textbf{vertices} themselves. This is achieved using a technique called \textbf{Vertex Splitting}:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Transformation:} For every original vertex $v \in V - \{s, t\}$, split it into two distinct nodes: an \textbf{in-node} ($v_{in}$) and an \textbf{out-node} ($v_{out}$).
    \item \textbf{Internal Edges:} Connect them with a directed internal edge $(v_{in}, v_{out})$ and assign it a capacity of $\mathbf{1}$.
    \item \textbf{External Edges:} For every original directed edge $(u, v) \in E$:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item If $u = s$, draw an edge from $s$ to $v_{in}$ with capacity $\infty$ (or $1$).
        \item If $v = t$, draw an edge from $u_{out}$ to $t$ with capacity $\infty$ (or $1$).
        \item Otherwise, draw an edge from $u_{out}$ to $v_{in}$ with capacity $\infty$ (or $1$).
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Run Max-Flow:} Compute the maximum flow from $s$ to $t$ on this transformed graph.
\end{enumerate}



\textbf{Why this works:} Since the internal edge $(v_{in}, v_{out})$ has a maximum capacity of $1$, at most $1$ unit of flow can pass through any split node. This physically prevents more than one path from utilizing vertex $v$, guaranteeing that all extracted paths are strictly vertex-disjoint.

---

\subsection*{2. Undirected Graphs}

Undirected graphs present a challenge because flow can technically travel in either direction along an undirected edge $\{u, v\}$.

\subsubsection*{Case A: Edge-Disjoint Paths in Undirected Graphs}
To find edge-disjoint paths when edges have no inherent direction:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Transformation:} Replace each undirected edge $\{u, v\}$ with \textbf{two directed anti-parallel edges}: $(u, v)$ and $(v, u)$. 
    \item \textbf{Capacity:} Assign a capacity of $\mathbf{1}$ to both directed edges.
    \item \textbf{Run Max-Flow:} Run a standard max-flow algorithm from $s$ to $t$.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Handling the Bidirectional Conflict:} \\
You might wonder: What if the algorithm sends $1$ unit of flow from $u \to v$ and another path sends $1$ unit from $v \to u$? 
In network flow computations, these opposing flows cancel each other out ($f(u,v) - f(v,u) = 0$). The residual graph naturally eliminates such inefficient looping paths during optimization. Consequently, the final maximum flow value directly equals the maximum number of edge-disjoint paths in the undirected graph.

\subsubsection*{Case B: Vertex-Disjoint Paths in Undirected Graphs}
To find vertex-disjoint paths in an undirected graph, we seamlessly combine the vertex splitting and anti-parallel edge techniques:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Vertex Splitting:} Split every vertex $v \notin \{s, t\}$ into $v_{in}$ and $v_{out}$, connected by a directed edge $(v_{in}, v_{out})$ with a capacity of $\mathbf{1}$.
    \item \textbf{Edge Mapping:} For every undirected edge $\{u, v\}$ in the original graph:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Create a directed edge from $u_{out} \to v_{in}$ with capacity $1$.
        \item Create a directed edge from $v_{out} \to u_{in}$ with capacity $1$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Run Max-Flow:} Calculate the max flow from $s$ to $t$. The bottlenecking unit-capacity internal node edges ensure that no node is visited more than once across all discovered paths.
\end{enumerate}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Figure shows a flow network on which $s$-$t$ flow has been computed. The capacity of each edge appears as a label next to the edge, and the numbers in boxes give the amount of flow sent on each edge. Edges without boxed numbers have no flow on them.
\begin{enumerate}
  \item What is the value of this flow? Is this a maximum $(s, t)$ flow on this graph?
  \item Find a minimum $s$-$t$ cut in the flow network pictured in Figure 1, and also say what its capacity is.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}

  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small},
  every path/.style={->, line width=1pt},
  ]
  \tikzset{>={Stealth[length=15pt, width=15pt, inset=10pt]}}

  \node (s)  at (-2, 0)    {s};
  \node (v1) at (3, 3)    {};
  \node (v2) at (2, 0)    {};
  \node (d)  at (5, -3)   {d};
  \node (v3) at (6, 0)    {};
  \node (t)  at (10, 0)    {t};

  \draw (s)  -> node[edge label, above left]  {10|5} (v1);
  \draw (s)  -> node[edge label, above]       {8|8}  (v2);
  \draw (s)  -> node[edge label, below left]  {5|5}  (d);
  \draw (v1) -> node[edge label, above] {3}    (v3);
  \draw (v1) -> node[edge label, above]       {5|5}  (t);
  \draw (v2) -> (v1);
  \draw (v2) -> node[edge label, above]       {10|8} (v3);
  \draw (v2) -> node[edge label, right]       {3}    (d);
  \draw (v3) -> node[edge label, above]       {8|8}  (t);
  \draw (d)  -> (v3);
  \draw (d)  -> node[edge label, below right] {10|5} (t);

  \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
 \mk{20} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2007--08}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Value of the Current Flow \& Maximality}

First, let's determine the value of the current flow, $|f|$. We can find this by summing the flow leaving the source node $s$ (or alternatively, arriving at the sink $t$).
\begin{itemize}
    \item Flow on $s \to v_1$: \textbf{5}
    \item Flow on $s \to v_2$: \textbf{8}
    \item Flow on $s \to d$: \textbf{5}
\end{itemize}
The total current flow is $|f| = 5 + 8 + 5 = \mathbf{18}$.

\textbf{Is this a maximum $(s, t)$ flow?} \\
\textbf{No.} We can prove this by finding an augmenting path in the residual network $G_f$ from $s$ to $t$. 

If we examine the residual capacities, we can route additional flow by pushing flow \textit{backward} along an edge that already has flow. Let's trace a path:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $s \to v_1$: Forward edge. Residual capacity = $10 - 5 = \mathbf{5}$.
    \item $v_1 \to v_3$: Forward edge. Residual capacity = $3 - 0 = \mathbf{3}$.
    \item $v_3 \to v_2$: \textbf{Backward edge.} The original edge $v_2 \to v_3$ has 8 units of flow. We can "push back" or redirect this flow. Residual capacity = $\mathbf{8}$.
    \item $v_2 \to d$: Forward edge. Residual capacity = $3 - 0 = \mathbf{3}$.
    \item $d \to t$: Forward edge. Residual capacity = $10 - 5 = \mathbf{5}$.
\end{enumerate}

The bottleneck (minimum residual capacity) along this path $s \to v_1 \to v_3 \to v_2 \to d \to t$ is $\min(5, 3, 8, 3, 5) = \mathbf{3}$. 

By augmenting this path with 3 units of flow (which effectively reroutes 3 units of flow away from the $v_2 \to v_3$ edge and down through $d$), we can increase the total network flow. 
The new maximum flow is $18 + 3 = \mathbf{21}$.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Minimum $s$-$t$ Cut and its Capacity}

To find the minimum cut, we must identify the set of vertices $S$ that are reachable from the source $s$ in the \textbf{final} residual network (after the flow is maximized to 21).

After pushing the additional 3 units of flow, let's look at the edges leaving $s$ and $v_1$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $s \to v_1$: Flow is now 8/10. Residual capacity = 2. ($v_1$ is reachable from $s$)
    \item $s \to v_2$: Flow is 8/8. Saturated. ($v_2$ is not reachable from $s$)
    \item $s \to d$: Flow is 5/5. Saturated. ($d$ is not reachable from $s$)
    \item $v_1 \to v_3$: Flow is 3/3. Saturated. ($v_3$ is not reachable from $v_1$)
    \item $v_1 \to t$: Flow is 5/5. Saturated. ($t$ is not reachable from $v_1$)
\end{itemize}

Since no other forward paths exist, the reachable set of nodes from $s$ is just $S = \{s, v_1\}$. The remaining nodes form the set $T = \{v_2, v_3, d, t\}$.

\textbf{Defining the Min-Cut:}
* **Partition:** $(S, T) = (\{s, v_1\}, \{v_2, v_3, d, t\})$

\textbf{Calculating Cut Capacity:}
The capacity of a cut is the sum of the capacities of all original edges going from $S$ to $T$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item $s \to v_2$: Capacity = \textbf{8}
    \item $s \to d$: Capacity = \textbf{5}
    \item $v_1 \to v_3$: Capacity = \textbf{3}
    \item $v_1 \to t$: Capacity = \textbf{5}
\end{itemize}

Total Capacity $= 8 + 5 + 3 + 5 = \mathbf{21}$. \\
\textit{(Note: This perfectly matches our calculated maximum flow, confirming the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem).}

\end{tcolorbox} 

\item There is a wireless network of nodes $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$, $E$, $F$, $G$ and $H$. Capacity of the wireless links are given in the following format: ``-'' indicates there is no link between those nodes. What is the maximum data flow, if node $A$ sends data to node $H$? What is the capacity of the optimal cut? (Use Ford-Fulkerson method and show the steps.) \mk{10+5=15}

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccccccccc}
\toprule
  & A  & B  & C & D & E  & F  & G  & H \\
\midrule
A & -  & 10 & 5 & 15 & - & -  & -  & - \\
B & -  & -  & 4 & -  & 9 & 15 & -  & - \\
C & -  & -  & - & 4  & - & 8  & -  & - \\
D & -  & -  & - & -  & - & -  & 30 & - \\
E & -  & -  & - & -  & - & 15 & -  & 10 \\
F & -  & -  & - & -  & - & -  & 15 & 10 \\
G & -  & -  & 6 & -  & - & -  & -  & 10 \\
H & -  & -  & - & -  & - & -  & -  & - \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2006--07}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Maximum Data Flow using Ford-Fulkerson}

To find the maximum data flow from the source node $A$ to the sink node $H$, we will iteratively find \textbf{augmenting paths} in the residual network and push as much flow as possible through them until no more paths exist. 

\textbf{Initialization:} Start with an initial flow of 0 on all edges.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Iteration 1: Path $A \to B \to E \to H$}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Capacities: $A \to B$ (10), $B \to E$ (9), $E \to H$ (10)
        \item Bottleneck: $\min(10, 9, 10) = 9$
        \item \textbf{Action:} Push 9 units of flow.
        \item \textit{Total Flow = 9}
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Iteration 2: Path $A \to D \to G \to H$}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Capacities: $A \to D$ (15), $D \to G$ (30), $G \to H$ (10)
        \item Bottleneck: $\min(15, 30, 10) = 10$
        \item \textbf{Action:} Push 10 units of flow.
        \item \textit{Total Flow = 19}
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Iteration 3: Path $A \to C \to F \to H$}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Capacities: $A \to C$ (5), $C \to F$ (8), $F \to H$ (10)
        \item Bottleneck: $\min(5, 8, 10) = 5$
        \item \textbf{Action:} Push 5 units of flow.
        \item \textit{Total Flow = 24}
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Iteration 4: Path $A \to B \to F \to H$}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Residual Capacities: $A \to B$ ($10 - 9 = 1$), $B \to F$ (15), $F \to H$ ($10 - 5 = 5$)
        \item Bottleneck: $\min(1, 15, 5) = 1$
        \item \textbf{Action:} Push 1 unit of flow.
        \item \textit{Total Flow = 25}
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Iteration 5: Path $A \to D \to G \to C \to F \to H$}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Residual Capacities: 
        \begin{itemize}
            \item $A \to D$: $15 - 10 = 5$
            \item $D \to G$: $30 - 10 = 20$
            \item $G \to C$: 6 \textit{(Original capacity, no flow yet)}
            \item $C \to F$: $8 - 5 = 3$
            \item $F \to H$: $10 - 5 - 1 = 4$
        \end{itemize}
        \item Bottleneck: $\min(5, 20, 6, 3, 4) = 3$
        \item \textbf{Action:} Push 3 units of flow.
        \item \textit{Total Flow = 28}
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Termination:} If we examine the residual graph now, there are no more paths from $A$ to $H$. All forward edges out of the reachable nodes are fully saturated. 

\textbf{Maximum Data Flow = 28}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Capacity of the Optimal Cut}

By the \textbf{Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem}, the capacity of the optimal (minimum) cut is exactly equal to the maximum flow. Let's explicitly define this cut by finding the set of reachable nodes from the source in the final residual graph.

\textbf{Finding the Cut Sets ($S$ and $T$):} \\
We start at source $A$ and traverse edges that still have residual capacity (forward edges not full, or backward edges with flow):
\begin{itemize}
    \item From $A$, we can still reach $D$ (since 13/15 units are used, residual is 2).
    \item From $D$, we can reach $G$ (since 13/30 units are used, residual is 17).
    \item From $G$, we can reach $C$ (since 3/6 units are used, residual is 3).
    \item No other nodes can be reached. $A \to B$ is saturated (10/10), $A \to C$ is saturated (5/5), $G \to H$ is saturated (10/10), and $C \to F$ is saturated (8/8).
\end{itemize}

This splits our nodes into two distinct sets:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Source Set ($S$):} $\{A, C, D, G\}$
    \item \textbf{Sink Set ($T$):} $\{B, E, F, H\}$
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Calculating the Cut Capacity:} \\
The capacity of the cut is the sum of the capacities of all original forward edges going from $S$ to $T$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item $A \to B$: 10
    \item $C \to F$: 8
    \item $G \to H$: 10
\end{itemize}
\textit{(Note: Edges going backwards from $T$ to $S$, like $B \to C$, are ignored when calculating cut capacity).}

\textbf{Optimal Cut Capacity:} $10 + 8 + 10 = \mathbf{28}$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item How can the Ford--Fulkerson method be used to find a maximum matching? Explain with a simple example. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2006--07}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

The Maximum Bipartite Matching problem can be elegantly solved by reducing it to a Maximum Flow problem and applying the \textbf{Ford-Fulkerson method}. 

\subsection*{1. Algorithmic Reduction Strategy}

Given an undirected bipartite graph $G = (L \cup R, E)$, where $L$ is the set of left vertices, $R$ is the set of right vertices, and $E$ is the set of edges connecting nodes from $L$ to $R$, we construct a directed flow network $G' = (V', E')$ as follows:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Add Source and Sink:} Introduce a virtual source node $s$ and a virtual sink node $t$. Thus, $V' = L \cup R \cup \{s, t\}$.
    \item \textbf{Source to Left Set:} Direct an edge from $s$ to every vertex $u \in L$. Assign each of these edges a capacity of $\mathbf{1}$.
    \item \textbf{Left Set to Right Set:} Direct all the original edges from $L$ to $R$. That is, for every undirected edge $\{u, v\} \in E$ where $u \in L$ and $v \in R$, create a directed edge $(u, v)$ with a capacity of $\mathbf{1}$ (or $\infty$).
    \item \textbf{Right Set to Sink:} Direct an edge from every vertex $v \in R$ to the sink $t$. Assign each of these edges a capacity of $\mathbf{1}$.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Why this works (Integrality Theorem):} 
Since all edge capacities in $G'$ are integers ($1$), the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm is guaranteed to find a maximum flow where the flow value through every edge is an integer (either $0$ or $1$). 
* A flow of $1$ from $s \to u$ ensures node $u \in L$ is matched at most once.
* A flow of $1$ from $v \to t$ ensures node $v \in R$ is matched at most once.
* Therefore, the edges between $L$ and $R$ carrying a flow of $1$ represent a valid \textbf{maximum matching}, and $\text{Value of Max Flow} = \text{Size of Maximum Matching}$.

---

\subsection*{2. Explanation with a Simple Example}

Let's consider a bipartite graph where:
* Left set $L = \{A, B, C\}$
* Right set $R = \{X, Y, Z\}$
* Original Edges $E = \{(A, X), (A, Y), (B, X), (C, Y), (C, Z)\}$

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Network Construction}
We transform this into a flow network by adding $s$ and $t$, connecting them with unit capacities ($1$), and directing all intermediate edges from $L$ to $R$ with capacity $1$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=white, inner sep=1pt},
    every path/.style={->, line width=0.8pt},
]
\tikzset{>={Stealth[length=10pt, width=6pt]}}

  % Nodes
  \node (s) at (0, 0) {s};
  \node (A) at (2.5, 1.5) {A};
  \node (B) at (2.5, 0) {B};
  \node (C) at (2.5, -1.5) {C};
  \node (X) at (5.5, 1.5) {X};
  \node (Y) at (5.5, 0) {Y};
  \node (Z) at (5.5, -1.5) {Z};
  \node (t) at (8, 0) {t};

  % Edges from s
  \draw (s) -> node[edge label, above left] {1} (A);
  \draw (s) -> node[edge label, above] {1} (B);
  \draw (s) -> node[edge label, below left] {1} (C);

  % Bipartite Edges
  \draw (A) -> node[edge label, above, pos=0.3] {1} (X);
  \draw (A) -> node[edge label, pos=0.3] {1} (Y);
  \draw (B) -> node[edge label, above, pos=0.3] {1} (X);
  \draw (C) -> node[edge label, above, pos=0.3] {1} (Y);
  \draw (C) -> node[edge label, below, pos=0.3] {1} (Z);

  % Edges to t
  \draw (X) -> node[edge label, above right] {1} (t);
  \draw (Y) -> node[edge label, above] {1} (t);
  \draw (Z) -> node[edge label, below right] {1} (t);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Executing Ford-Fulkerson}
We find augmenting paths in the residual network to push flow:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path 1:} $s \to A \to X \to t$ (Bottleneck = 1). Push 1 unit.
    \item \textbf{Path 2:} $s \to C \to Y \to t$ (Bottleneck = 1). Push 1 unit.
    \item \textbf{Path 3:} Now, $s \to B \to X \dots$ is blocked because $X \to t$ is full. However, we can use the \textbf{backward edge} from $X \to A$ (since $A \to X$ has 1 unit of flow):
    $$ s \to B \to X \xrightarrow{\text{backward}} A \to Y \to t $$
    Wait, $Y \to t$ is already saturated by Path 2! Let's find an alternate open path:
    $$ s \to B \to X \xrightarrow{\text{backward}} A \to Y \xrightarrow{\text{backward}} C \to Z \to t $$
    The bottleneck along this path is $\mathbf{1}$. Pushing $1$ unit of flow through this path automatically shifts the assignments optimally.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Final Flow \& Matching Output}
After completing the Ford-Fulkerson execution, the maximum flow value achieved is \textbf{3}. 

The edges between $L$ and $R$ carrying a flow of $1/1$ are:
$$ \text{Maximum Matching} = \{ (B, X), (A, Y), (C, Z) \} $$

All vertices on both sides are perfectly matched, yielding a maximum matching size of **3**.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2019-20
\end{enumerate}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cA}{Maximum Bipartite Matching}
\label{sub:s1-bipartite}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2022-23
\item Formulate the problem of maximum bipartite matching. Give two examples of real-life problems that can be solved using maximum bipartite matching.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Formulation of Maximum Bipartite Matching}

\textbf{Definitions:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Bipartite Graph:} A graph $G = (V, E)$ is bipartite if its vertex set $V$ can be partitioned into two disjoint, independent sets $L$ and $R$ ($L \cup R = V$ and $L \cap R = \emptyset$) such that every edge $e \in E$ connects a vertex in $L$ to a vertex in $R$.
    \item \textbf{Matching:} A matching $M \subseteq E$ is a collection of edges such that no two edges in $M$ share a common vertex. In other words, every vertex in $V$ is incident to at most one edge in $M$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{The Problem:}
The Maximum Bipartite Matching problem asks us to find a matching $M$ of maximum possible size (i.e., containing the largest possible number of edges) for a given bipartite graph $G$.

\textbf{Mathematical Formulation (Integer Linear Program):}
Let $x_{ij}$ be a binary decision variable where $x_{ij} = 1$ if the edge $(i, j)$ is included in the matching $M$, and $x_{ij} = 0$ otherwise.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Objective:} Maximize the total number of matched edges.
    $$\text{Maximize} \sum_{(i,j) \in E} x_{ij}$$
    \item \textbf{Subject to (Constraints):}
    \begin{align*}
        \sum_{j: (i,j) \in E} x_{ij} &\le 1 \quad \forall i \in L \quad \text{(Each node in $L$ is matched at most once)} \\
        \sum_{i: (i,j) \in E} x_{ij} &\le 1 \quad \forall j \in R \quad \text{(Each node in $R$ is matched at most once)} \\
        x_{ij} &\in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall (i,j) \in E
    \end{align*}
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Real-Life Examples}

\textbf{Example 1: Applicant-to-Job Assignment}
\begin{itemize}
