    \item \textbf{Scenario:} A staffing agency has a pool of applicants and a list of open job positions. Each applicant possesses a specific set of skills, qualifying them for only certain jobs.
    \item \textbf{Graph Construction:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Set $L$: Job Applicants.
        \item Set $R$: Open Job Positions.
        \item Edges $E$: An edge connects applicant $A$ to job $J$ if $A$ is qualified for $J$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Objective:} By finding the maximum bipartite matching, the agency can assign applicants to jobs such that the maximum total number of jobs are filled, ensuring no applicant takes more than one job and no job is assigned to more than one applicant.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Example 2: Taxi Dispatch Systems (Ride-Hailing)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Scenario:} A ride-hailing app (like Uber or Lyft) receives a surge of ride requests. There are several available drivers in the area, but they can only be matched with passengers who are within a feasible driving distance (e.g., a 5-minute radius).
    \item \textbf{Graph Construction:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Set $L$: Available Drivers.
        \item Set $R$: Waiting Passengers.
        \item Edges $E$: An edge exists between a driver and a passenger if the driver is within the acceptable ETA threshold.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Objective:} Solving the maximum bipartite matching problem allows the dispatch algorithm to maximize the number of passengers picked up immediately during a specific time window, optimizing system throughput.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2022-23
\item Develop a solution for the maximum bipartite matching problem. What is the Edmonds-Karp optimization for the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm?
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Solution for Maximum Bipartite Matching}

The standard and most efficient way to solve the Maximum Bipartite Matching problem is by reducing it to a Maximum Network Flow problem. 

Given a bipartite graph $G = (L \cup R, E)$, we can develop a solution using the following algorithm:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Construct a Flow Network ($G'$):}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Create a supersource node $s$ and a supersink node $t$.
        \item Add directed edges from $s$ to every node $u \in L$, each with a capacity of $\mathbf{1}$.
        \item Direct all original bipartite edges from $L$ to $R$. For each edge $(u, v) \in E$ where $u \in L$ and $v \in R$, assign a capacity of $\mathbf{1}$ (or $\infty$).
        \item Add directed edges from every node $v \in R$ to $t$, each with a capacity of $\mathbf{1}$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Compute Maximum Flow:} Run a network flow algorithm (like Ford-Fulkerson or Edmonds-Karp) from $s$ to $t$ on the newly constructed graph $G'$.
    \item \textbf{Extract the Matching:} Because the capacities are integers, the Integrality Theorem guarantees an integer maximum flow. Any edge between $L$ and $R$ that carries a flow of $1$ is part of the maximum matching.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Complexity:} Since the maximum possible flow is bounded by the number of vertices $|V|$, running standard Ford-Fulkerson takes $O(|V||E|)$ time, making it highly efficient for this specific problem.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. The Edmonds-Karp Optimization for Ford-Fulkerson}

The original Ford-Fulkerson method dictates that we repeatedly find \textit{any} augmenting path in the residual network. If implemented using Depth-First Search (DFS), it can make poor path choices, leading to a worst-case time complexity of $O(E|f^*|)$, where $|f^*|$ is the maximum flow value. If capacities are irrational, it might not even terminate.

The \textbf{Edmonds-Karp optimization} resolves this by specifying exactly \textit{how} to find the augmenting path:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{The Core Idea:} Instead of finding just any path, Edmonds-Karp always chooses the \textbf{shortest augmenting path} from $s$ to $t$ in the residual graph, where "shortest" means the minimum number of edges (unweighted path length).
    \item \textbf{Implementation:} It achieves this by using \textbf{Breadth-First Search (BFS)} instead of DFS to find paths in the residual network. All edges in the residual graph are treated as having a weight of 1 for the BFS traversal.
    \item \textbf{Why it is an Optimization:} By strictly picking the shortest path, the algorithm guarantees that the length of the shortest augmenting path monotonically increases over time. It prevents the algorithm from endlessly ping-ponging back and forth across large capacity edges.
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity Bound:} This simple change bounds the maximum number of augmentations to $O(VE)$. Since each BFS takes $O(E)$ time, the overall worst-case time complexity becomes $\mathbf{O(VE^2)}$. 
\end{itemize}

Crucially, the Edmonds-Karp time complexity is \textbf{independent of the maximum flow value} ($|f^*|$) or the edge capacities, guaranteeing polynomial time termination for any network, even with very large or irrational capacities.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Explain with an example how to convert a bipartite graph $G$ to a flow network $G'$ such that a maximum bipartite matching $M$ in $G$ can be computed by finding a maximum flow $f$ in $G'$. Prove that there is a maximum matching $M$ in $G$ if and only if there is a maximum flow $f$ in $G'$. \mk{17}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2009--10}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Conversion of Bipartite Graph to Flow Network}

To compute a maximum bipartite matching for an undirected bipartite graph $G = (L \cup R, E)$, we construct a directed flow network $G' = (V', E')$ using the following steps:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Vertices ($V'$):} Add a supersource node $s$ and a supersink node $t$ to the existing vertices. Thus, $V' = L \cup R \cup \{s, t\}$.
    \item \textbf{Edges ($E'$) and Capacities ($c$):}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Source to Left:} For every vertex $u \in L$, add a directed edge $(s, u)$ with capacity $c(s, u) = 1$.
        \item \textbf{Left to Right (Original Edges):} For every undirected edge $\{u, v\} \in E$ (where $u \in L$ and $v \in R$), add a directed edge $(u, v)$ with capacity $c(u, v) = 1$ (or $\infty$).
        \item \textbf{Right to Sink:} For every vertex $v \in R$, add a directed edge $(v, t)$ with capacity $c(v, t) = 1$.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Example of Conversion}

Consider a bipartite graph $G$ with $L = \{A, B\}$, $R = \{X, Y\}$, and edges $E = \{(A, X), (A, Y), (B, Y)\}$. 
We construct $G'$ by adding $s$ and $t$, and directing the edges with unit capacities.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=white, inner sep=1pt},
    every path/.style={->, line width=0.8pt},
]
\tikzset{>={Stealth[length=10pt, width=6pt]}}

  % Nodes
  \node[draw=none] at (1, 2.5) {\textbf{Graph $G$}};
  \node (A1) at (0, 1.5) {A};
  \node (B1) at (0, 0) {B};
  \node (X1) at (2, 1.5) {X};
  \node (Y1) at (2, 0) {Y};

  \draw[-] (A1) -- (X1);
  \draw[-] (A1) -- (Y1);
  \draw[-] (B1) -- (Y1);

  % Flow Network Nodes
  \node[draw=none] at (6.5, 2.5) {\textbf{Flow Network $G'$}};
  \node (s) at (4, 0.75) {s};
  \node (A) at (5.5, 1.5) {A};
  \node (B) at (5.5, 0) {B};
  \node (X) at (7.5, 1.5) {X};
  \node (Y) at (7.5, 0) {Y};
  \node (t) at (9, 0.75) {t};

  % s to L
  \draw (s) -> node[edge label, above left] {1} (A);
  \draw (s) -> node[edge label, below left] {1} (B);

  % L to R
  \draw (A) -> node[edge label, above] {1} (X);
  \draw (A) -> node[edge label, near start] {1} (Y);
  \draw (B) -> node[edge label, below] {1} (Y);

  % R to t
  \draw (X) -> node[edge label, above right] {1} (t);
  \draw (Y) -> node[edge label, below right] {1} (t);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Proof: Maximum Matching $M$ in $G \iff$ Maximum Flow $f$ in $G'$}

To prove this, we must show that for any matching of size $k$, there exists a valid flow of value $k$, and conversely, for any integer flow of value $k$, there exists a matching of size $k$. By establishing this 1-to-1 correspondence, it strictly implies that the \textbf{maximum} sizes must also be perfectly equal ($|M_{max}| = |f_{max}|$).

\subsubsection*{Part A: Matching $\implies$ Flow}
\textbf{Claim:} If $G$ has a matching $M$ of size $k$, then $G'$ has a flow $f$ of value $k$. \\
\textbf{Proof:} 
Let $M$ be a valid matching in $G$. For every edge $(u, v) \in M$ (where $u \in L$ and $v \in R$), we route $1$ unit of flow along the path $s \to u \to v \to t$. 
Because $M$ is a matching, no two edges in $M$ share a vertex. Therefore:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Each node $u \in L$ is incident to at most one edge in $M$, meaning the flow entering $u$ from $s$ is at most $1$. This respects the capacity $c(s, u) = 1$.
    \item Each node $v \in R$ is incident to at most one edge in $M$, meaning the flow leaving $v$ to $t$ is at most $1$. This respects the capacity $c(v, t) = 1$.
\end{itemize}
Flow conservation is maintained at all intermediate nodes, and no capacities are violated. Thus, we have constructed a valid flow $f$ of value exactly $k = |M|$.

\subsubsection*{Part B: Flow $\implies$ Matching}
\textbf{Claim:} If $G'$ has a maximum flow $f$ of value $k$, then $G$ has a matching $M$ of size $k$. \\
\textbf{Proof:} 
Since all capacities in $G'$ are integers, the \textbf{Integrality Theorem} guarantees that the maximum flow $f$ will assign an integer flow (either $0$ or $1$) to every edge. 
We construct a matching $M$ by taking all edges $(u, v)$ between $L$ and $R$ that carry exactly $1$ unit of flow:
$$ M = \{ (u, v) \in E \mid f(u, v) = 1 \} $$
We must verify that $M$ is a valid matching. By flow conservation at node $u \in L$:
$$ \sum_{v \in R} f(u, v) = f(s, u) \le c(s, u) = 1 $$
This means at most $1$ unit of flow leaves $u$, so $u$ is paired with at most one vertex in $R$.
Similarly, by flow conservation at node $v \in R$:
$$ \sum_{u \in L} f(u, v) = f(v, t) \le c(v, t) = 1 $$
This means at most $1$ unit of flow enters $v$, so $v$ is paired with at most one vertex in $L$.
Since no vertex in $L$ or $R$ shares more than one edge in $M$, $M$ is a valid bipartite matching. Because 1 unit of flow reaches $t$ for every edge in $M$, the size of the matching $|M|$ is exactly the flow value $k$.

\subsubsection*{Conclusion}
Since any matching of size $k$ yields a flow of value $k$, and any integer flow of value $k$ yields a matching of size $k$, the largest possible matching directly corresponds to the largest possible flow. Therefore, finding the maximum flow in $G'$ computes the maximum matching in $G$.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Explain with an example how to convert a bipartite graph $G$ to a flow network
$G'$ such that a maximum bipartite matching $M$ in $G$ can be computed by finding
a maximum flow $f$ in $G'$. Prove that there is a maximum matching $M$ in $G$
if and only if there is a maximum flow $f$ in $G'$. \mk{17.5}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Conversion of Bipartite Graph to Flow Network}

To compute a maximum bipartite matching for an undirected bipartite graph $G = (L \cup R, E)$, we construct a directed flow network $G' = (V', E')$ using the following steps:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Vertices ($V'$):} Add a supersource node $s$ and a supersink node $t$ to the existing vertices. Thus, $V' = L \cup R \cup \{s, t\}$.
    \item \textbf{Edges ($E'$) and Capacities ($c$):}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Source to Left:} For every vertex $u \in L$, add a directed edge $(s, u)$ with capacity $c(s, u) = 1$.
        \item \textbf{Left to Right (Original Edges):} For every undirected edge $\{u, v\} \in E$ (where $u \in L$ and $v \in R$), add a directed edge $(u, v)$ with capacity $c(u, v) = 1$ (or $\infty$).
        \item \textbf{Right to Sink:} For every vertex $v \in R$, add a directed edge $(v, t)$ with capacity $c(v, t) = 1$.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Example of Conversion}

Consider a bipartite graph $G$ with $L = \{A, B\}$, $R = \{X, Y\}$, and edges $E = \{(A, X), (A, Y), (B, Y)\}$. 
We construct $G'$ by adding $s$ and $t$, and directing the edges with unit capacities.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge label/.style={rectangle, draw=none, font=\normalfont\small, fill=white, inner sep=1pt},
    every path/.style={->, line width=0.8pt},
]
\tikzset{>={Stealth[length=10pt, width=6pt]}}

  % Nodes
  \node[draw=none] at (1, 2.5) {\textbf{Graph $G$}};
  \node (A1) at (0, 1.5) {A};
  \node (B1) at (0, 0) {B};
  \node (X1) at (2, 1.5) {X};
  \node (Y1) at (2, 0) {Y};

  \draw[-] (A1) -- (X1);
  \draw[-] (A1) -- (Y1);
  \draw[-] (B1) -- (Y1);

  % Flow Network Nodes
  \node[draw=none] at (6.5, 2.5) {\textbf{Flow Network $G'$}};
  \node (s) at (4, 0.75) {s};
  \node (A) at (5.5, 1.5) {A};
  \node (B) at (5.5, 0) {B};
  \node (X) at (7.5, 1.5) {X};
  \node (Y) at (7.5, 0) {Y};
  \node (t) at (9, 0.75) {t};

  % s to L
  \draw (s) -> node[edge label, above left] {1} (A);
  \draw (s) -> node[edge label, below left] {1} (B);

  % L to R
  \draw (A) -> node[edge label, above] {1} (X);
  \draw (A) -> node[edge label, near start] {1} (Y);
  \draw (B) -> node[edge label, below] {1} (Y);

  % R to t
  \draw (X) -> node[edge label, above right] {1} (t);
  \draw (Y) -> node[edge label, below right] {1} (t);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Proof: Maximum Matching $M$ in $G \iff$ Maximum Flow $f$ in $G'$}

To prove this, we must show that for any matching of size $k$, there exists a valid flow of value $k$, and conversely, for any integer flow of value $k$, there exists a matching of size $k$. By establishing this 1-to-1 correspondence, it strictly implies that the \textbf{maximum} sizes must also be perfectly equal ($|M_{max}| = |f_{max}|$).

\subsubsection*{Part A: Matching $\implies$ Flow}
\textbf{Claim:} If $G$ has a matching $M$ of size $k$, then $G'$ has a flow $f$ of value $k$. \\
\textbf{Proof:} 
Let $M$ be a valid matching in $G$. For every edge $(u, v) \in M$ (where $u \in L$ and $v \in R$), we route $1$ unit of flow along the path $s \to u \to v \to t$. 
Because $M$ is a matching, no two edges in $M$ share a vertex. Therefore:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Each node $u \in L$ is incident to at most one edge in $M$, meaning the flow entering $u$ from $s$ is at most $1$. This respects the capacity $c(s, u) = 1$.
    \item Each node $v \in R$ is incident to at most one edge in $M$, meaning the flow leaving $v$ to $t$ is at most $1$. This respects the capacity $c(v, t) = 1$.
\end{itemize}
Flow conservation is maintained at all intermediate nodes, and no capacities are violated. Thus, we have constructed a valid flow $f$ of value exactly $k = |M|$.

\subsubsection*{Part B: Flow $\implies$ Matching}
\textbf{Claim:} If $G'$ has a maximum flow $f$ of value $k$, then $G$ has a matching $M$ of size $k$. \\
\textbf{Proof:} 
Since all capacities in $G'$ are integers, the \textbf{Integrality Theorem} guarantees that the maximum flow $f$ will assign an integer flow (either $0$ or $1$) to every edge. 
We construct a matching $M$ by taking all edges $(u, v)$ between $L$ and $R$ that carry exactly $1$ unit of flow:
$$ M = \{ (u, v) \in E \mid f(u, v) = 1 \} $$
We must verify that $M$ is a valid matching. By flow conservation at node $u \in L$:
$$ \sum_{v \in R} f(u, v) = f(s, u) \le c(s, u) = 1 $$
This means at most $1$ unit of flow leaves $u$, so $u$ is paired with at most one vertex in $R$.
Similarly, by flow conservation at node $v \in R$:
$$ \sum_{u \in L} f(u, v) = f(v, t) \le c(v, t) = 1 $$
This means at most $1$ unit of flow enters $v$, so $v$ is paired with at most one vertex in $L$.
Since no vertex in $L$ or $R$ shares more than one edge in $M$, $M$ is a valid bipartite matching. Because 1 unit of flow reaches $t$ for every edge in $M$, the size of the matching $|M|$ is exactly the flow value $k$.

\subsubsection*{Conclusion}
Since any matching of size $k$ yields a flow of value $k$, and any integer flow of value $k$ yields a matching of size $k$, the largest possible matching directly corresponds to the largest possible flow. Therefore, finding the maximum flow in $G'$ computes the maximum matching in $G$.

\end{tcolorbox}

\end{enumerate}


\subtopic{cA}{Stable Matching (Gale-Shapley)}
\label{sub:s1-stablematching}

\begin{enumerate}
  \item In a stable matching problem (\textbf{Gale-Shapley algorithm}) each participant has a true preference order. Now consider a woman $w$. Suppose $w$ prefers man $m$ to $m'$, but both $m$ and $m'$ are low on her list of preferences. Can it be the case that by switching the order of $m$ and $m'$ on her list of preferences (i.e., by falsely claiming that she prefers $m'$ to $m$) and running the algorithm with this false preference list, $w$ will end up with a man $m''$ that she truly prefers to both $m$ and $m'$? Give proof to support your answer.
  \mk{10}  \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2020--21}

  \begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Yes}, it can absolutely be the case. 

In the standard Gale-Shapley algorithm (where men propose to women), the mechanism is strategy-proof for the proposing side (men), but it is \textbf{not strategy-proof for the receiving side} (women). A woman can sometimes improve her final match by falsifying her preference list, even by swapping the order of two men who are lower on her true preference list.

We can prove this constructively by providing a scenario where this exact manipulation strictly benefits the woman $w$.

\subsection*{Proof by Counterexample}

Consider a matching market with three men $M = \{m, m', m''\}$ and three women $W = \{w, w_2, w_3\}$.

\subsubsection*{1. The True Preferences}
Assume the true preference lists for all participants are as follows (ordered from most preferred to least preferred):

\textbf{Men's Preferences:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $m$: \quad $w \succ w_2 \succ w_3$
    \item $m'$: \quad $w \succ w_3 \succ w_2$
    \item $m''$: \quad $w_2 \succ w \succ w_3$
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Women's True Preferences:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $\mathbf{w}$: \quad $\mathbf{m'' \succ m \succ m'}$ \quad \textit{(Note: $m''$ is her top choice, $m$ and $m'$ are lower)}
    \item $w_2$: \quad $m \succ m'' \succ m'$
    \item $w_3$: \quad $m' \succ m \succ m''$
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Execution 1: Truthful Bidding}
Let's trace the Gale-Shapley algorithm when woman $w$ submits her \textbf{true} preference list ($m'' \succ m \succ m'$).

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Round 1:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $m$ proposes to his first choice, $w$.
        \item $m'$ proposes to his first choice, $w$.
        \item $m''$ proposes to his first choice, $w_2$.
    \end{itemize}
    Woman $w$ receives proposals from both $m$ and $m'$. According to her true list, $m \succ m'$. She tentatively \textbf{accepts $m$} and \textbf{rejects $m'$}. Woman $w_2$ tentatively accepts $m''$.
    
    \item \textbf{Round 2:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item The rejected man $m'$ proposes to his next choice, $w_3$.
    \end{itemize}
    Woman $w_3$ accepts $m'$. 
    
    \item \textbf{Termination:} Everyone is matched. The algorithm ends.
    \item \textbf{Truthful Outcome for $w$:} She is matched with $\mathbf{m}$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Execution 2: Strategic Manipulation}
Now, suppose $w$ falsely submits a preference list where she swaps the lower two men, claiming $\mathbf{m' \succ m}$. Her submitted list is now: \textbf{$m'' \succ m' \succ m$}.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Round 1:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $m \to w$, $m' \to w$, $m'' \to w_2$.
    \end{itemize}
    Woman $w$ has proposals from $m$ and $m'$. According to her \textit{false} list, $m' \succ m$. She tentatively \textbf{accepts $m'$} and \textbf{rejects $m$}. Woman $w_2$ tentatively accepts $m''$.
    
    \item \textbf{Round 2:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item The rejected man $m$ proposes to his next choice, $w_2$.
    \end{itemize}
    Woman $w_2$ now has $m$ and $m''$. According to her true list ($m \succ m''$), she \textbf{accepts $m$} and \textbf{rejects $m''$}.
    
    \item \textbf{Round 3:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item The newly rejected man $m''$ proposes to his next choice, $w$.
    \end{itemize}
    Woman $w$ currently holds $m'$, but just received a proposal from $m''$. According to her (false and true) list, $m'' \succ m'$. She \textbf{accepts $m''$} and \textbf{rejects $m'$}.
    
    \item \textbf{Round 4:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item The rejected man $m'$ proposes to $w_3$, who accepts him.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Termination:} Everyone is matched.
    \item \textbf{Manipulated Outcome for $w$:} She is matched with $\mathbf{m''}$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Conclusion}
By falsely rejecting $m$ in Round 1 (due to the swapped preferences), $w$ forced $m$ to propose to $w_2$. This caused $w_2$ to dump $m''$. Consequently, $m''$ was freed up to propose to $w$. Therefore, by swapping two lower-preference men on her list, $w$ successfully manipulated the chain of proposals to capture a man ($m''$) whom she truly preferred to both.

\end{tcolorbox}

\end{enumerate}


%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cA}{Graph Traversal \& Properties}
\label{sub:s1-traversal}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2021-22
\item Classify the edges of the given graph after performing a Depth-First Search (DFS). If multiple nodes are available to explore at any point, always choose the node that comes first in alphabetical ordering. Start the DFS traversal from node $a$. (Possible classes: tree-edge, forward edge, back edge, and cross edge.)
\begin{center}
      \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.5,
        every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
        >=stealth, % তীরের মাথার স্টাইল সুন্দর করার জন্য
        thick
    ]
        % Nodes positioning
        \node (b) at (0,2) {b};
        \node (a) at (1.5,2) {a};
        \node (e) at (3,2) {e};
        
        \node (c) at (0,0) {c};
        \node (d) at (1.5,0) {d};
        \node (f) at (3,0) {f};

        % Directed Edges
        \path[->] (b) edge (a);
        \path[->] (b) edge (c);
        \path[->] (c) edge (a);
        \path[->] (a) edge (d);
        \path[->] (d) edge (c);
        \path[->] (e) edge (d);
        \path[->] (e) edge (f);

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{DFS Traversal and Edge Classification}

We perform the Depth-First Search (DFS) starting from node $a$. When multiple unvisited neighbors exist, we explore them in alphabetical order. We will track the \textbf{Discovery Time (d)} and \textbf{Finish Time (f)} to formally classify the edges.

\subsubsection*{Step-by-Step Execution}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Start at node $a$:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Node $a$ is discovered. Time $d[a] = 1$.
        \item Neighbors of $a$: only $d$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Explore $(a, d)$:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Node $d$ is unvisited. \textbf{$(a, d)$ is a Tree Edge}.
        \item Node $d$ is discovered. Time $d[d] = 2$.
        \item Neighbors of $d$: only $c$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Explore $(d, c)$:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Node $c$ is unvisited. \textbf{$(d, c)$ is a Tree Edge}.
        \item Node $c$ is discovered. Time $d[c] = 3$.
        \item Neighbors of $c$: only $a$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Explore $(c, a)$:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Node $a$ is already discovered but not yet finished (it is an ancestor in the current DFS path). \textbf{$(c, a)$ is a Back Edge}.
        \item No more neighbors for $c$. Finish $c$. Time $f[c] = 4$.
        \item No more neighbors for $d$. Finish $d$. Time $f[d] = 5$.
        \item No more neighbors for $a$. Finish $a$. Time $f[a] = 6$.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Find next unvisited node (Alphabetical order):} $b$ is unvisited.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Node $b$ is discovered. Time $d[b] = 7$.
        \item Neighbors of $b$: $a$ and $c$. Process $a$ first (alphabetically).
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Explore $(b, a)$:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Node $a$ is completely finished ($f[a] = 6$). Since $d[b] > d[a]$, \textbf{$(b, a)$ is a Cross Edge}.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Explore $(b, c)$:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Node $c$ is completely finished ($f[c] = 4$). Since $d[b] > d[c]$, \textbf{$(b, c)$ is a Cross Edge}.
        \item No more neighbors for $b$. Finish $b$. Time $f[b] = 8$.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Find next unvisited node (Alphabetical order):} $e$ is unvisited.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Node $e$ is discovered. Time $d[e] = 9$.
        \item Neighbors of $e$: $d$ and $f$. Process $d$ first.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Explore $(e, d)$:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Node $d$ is completely finished ($f[d] = 5$). Since $d[e] > d[d]$, \textbf{$(e, d)$ is a Cross Edge}.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Explore $(e, f)$:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Node $f$ is unvisited. \textbf{$(e, f)$ is a Tree Edge}.
        \item Node $f$ is discovered. Time $d[f] = 10$.
        \item Neighbors of $f$: None. Finish $f$. Time $f[f] = 11$.
        \item No more neighbors for $e$. Finish $e$. Time $f[e] = 12$.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{Final Edge Classifications}

Based on the DFS traversal above, the complete classification of all edges in the graph is:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Tree Edges:} $(a, d)$, $(d, c)$, $(e, f)$
    \item \textbf{Back Edges:} $(c, a)$
    \item \textbf{Forward Edges:} \textit{None}
    \item \textbf{Cross Edges:} $(b, a)$, $(b, c)$, $(e, d)$
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item Gotham city is in grave danger. Batman must suit up with the following constraints:
\begin{enumerate}
\item  He must wear the bodysuit first. 
\item  Both utility belt and armor plate can be worn in any order on top of the bodysuit. 
\item  The grappling gun and smoke bombs can be attached only after the utility belt is worn.
\item  The cape has to be mounted on top of the armor plate. 
\item  The glider wing must be attached to both the armor plate and the utility belt. 
\end{enumerate}
First, draw a dependency graph of all items. Then, recommend a valid ordering of the items to help Batman get ready quickly but properly.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Dependency Graph}

To model Batman's dressing constraints, we can create a \textbf{Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)} where each node represents an item of equipment, and a directed edge from node $X$ to node $Y$ ($X \to Y$) means that item $X$ must be put on before item $Y$.

Based on the constraints:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Bodysuit $\to$ Utility Belt (Belt is on top of bodysuit)
    \item Bodysuit $\to$ Armor Plate (Armor is on top of bodysuit)
    \item Utility Belt $\to$ Grappling Gun (Gun attaches to belt)
    \item Utility Belt $\to$ Smoke Bombs (Bombs attach to belt)
    \item Armor Plate $\to$ Cape (Cape mounts on armor)
    \item Armor Plate $\to$ Glider Wing (Glider attaches to armor)
    \item Utility Belt $\to$ Glider Wing (Glider attaches to belt)
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    scale=1.2,
    every node/.style={rectangle, rounded corners, draw, thick, align=center, fill=white, inner sep=6pt, font=\bfseries},
    >=stealth,
    thick
]
    % Nodes
    \node (B) at (0, 4) {Bodysuit};
    \node (U) at (-2.5, 2) {Utility Belt};
    \node (A) at (2.5, 2) {Armor Plate};
    
    \node (G) at (-4, 0) {Grappling Gun};
    \node (S) at (-1.5, 0) {Smoke Bombs};
    \node (W) at (0.5, 0) {Glider Wing};
    \node (C) at (3.5, 0) {Cape};

    % Edges
    \path[->] (B) edge (U);
    \path[->] (B) edge (A);
    \path[->] (U) edge (G);
    \path[->] (U) edge (S);
    \path[->] (U) edge (W);
    \path[->] (A) edge (C);
    \path[->] (A) edge (W);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Recommended Valid Ordering}

To find a valid order for Batman to suit up, we perform a \textbf{Topological Sort} on the dependency graph. A topological sort ensures that for every directed edge $X \to Y$, item $X$ always appears before item $Y$ in the ordering. 

Because there are independent parallel branches in the graph, multiple valid orderings exist. We construct one by repeatedly selecting a node with an in-degree of 0 (all its prerequisites have been fulfilled).

\textbf{Step-by-step Topological Sort:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Start with \textbf{Bodysuit} (in-degree 0).
    \item Now, \textit{Utility Belt} and \textit{Armor Plate} have in-degree 0. We can pick \textbf{Armor Plate}.
    \item Next, we can pick \textbf{Cape} (since Armor is on) or \textit{Utility Belt}. Let's pick \textbf{Utility Belt}.
    \item Now \textit{Grappling Gun}, \textit{Smoke Bombs}, and \textit{Glider Wing} (since both Armor and Belt are on) have in-degree 0. We can pick \textbf{Glider Wing}.
    \item Next, pick \textbf{Grappling Gun}.
    \item Next, pick \textbf{Smoke Bombs}.
    \item Finally, pick \textbf{Cape}.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Final Recommended Ordering:}
$$ \text{Bodysuit} \to \text{Armor Plate} \to \text{Utility Belt} \to \text{Glider Wing} \to \text{Grappling Gun} \to \text{Smoke Bombs} \to \text{Cape} $$

\textit{(Note: "Bodysuit $\to$ Utility Belt $\to$ Armor Plate $\to$ Grappling Gun $\to$ Smoke Bombs $\to$ Cape $\to$ Glider Wing" is another perfectly valid sequence.)}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item Mention the runtimes of the following algorithms:
\begin{enumerate}
\item  Kruskal's algorithm without Disjoint Set Union (DSU), 
\item  Kruskal's algorithm with DSU, 
\item  Ford-Fulkerson algorithm, 
\item  Edmonds-Karp algorithm.
\end{enumerate}
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

Here are the worst-case time complexities for the requested algorithms, where $V$ is the number of vertices, $E$ is the number of edges, and $|f^*|$ is the maximum flow value:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Kruskal's algorithm without Disjoint Set Union (DSU):} $\mathbf{O(E \cdot V)}$ or $\mathbf{O(E^2)}$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textit{Reasoning:} Sorting the edges takes $O(E \log E)$. Without an efficient DSU data structure, checking if adding an edge creates a cycle typically requires a standard graph traversal (like BFS or DFS) taking $O(V)$ time for each of the $E$ edges. This $O(E \cdot V)$ cycle-checking phase dominates the overall runtime.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \vspace{0.5em}
    
    \item \textbf{Kruskal's algorithm with DSU:} $\mathbf{O(E \log E)}$ or $\mathbf{O(E \log V)}$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textit{Reasoning:} Sorting the edges takes $O(E \log E)$. With a DSU utilizing path compression and union by rank, cycle checking and merging sets takes $O(E \cdot \alpha(V))$ time across all edges, where $\alpha$ is the nearly constant inverse Ackermann function. Thus, the sorting step dominates the runtime. Since $E \le V^2$, it follows that $\log E \le 2 \log V$, making $O(E \log E)$ asymptotically equivalent to $O(E \log V)$.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \vspace{0.5em}
    
    \item \textbf{Ford-Fulkerson algorithm:} $\mathbf{O(E \cdot |f^*|)}$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textit{Reasoning:} In the worst case (with integer capacities), the algorithm finds an augmenting path using DFS or BFS, taking $O(E)$ time, and pushes a minimum of 1 unit of flow per iteration. Therefore, it might require up to $|f^*|$ iterations before max flow is reached.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \vspace{0.5em}
    
    \item \textbf{Edmonds-Karp algorithm:} $\mathbf{O(V \cdot E^2)}$
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textit{Reasoning:} Edmonds-Karp is an optimization of the Ford-Fulkerson method that uses Breadth-First Search (BFS) to continuously find the shortest augmenting path (in terms of the number of edges). This guarantees that the number of augmenting paths found cannot exceed $O(V \cdot E)$. Since each BFS takes $O(E)$ time, the total complexity is firmly bounded independent of the maximum flow value.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Consider a peculiar city with N towns and M bidirectional roads (connecting towns) 
    classified into three types. This city is peculiar due to unconventional regulations 
    regarding road usage: men are permitted to travel only on roads of types 1 and 3, while 
    women are permitted to travel only on roads of types 2 and 3 exclusively. The Mayor 
    of the city intends to renovate the city by reconstructing some roads, with the condition 
    that the normal routine of the citizens should not be hampered.
    
    Now, you are hired as a computer scientist to answer an important question: What is 
    the maximum number of roads that can be renovated (remain closed for traffic during 
    renovation) while ensuring that the city remains connected for both men and women? 
    The city is said to be connected if it is still possible to travel form any town X to any 
    town Y using the existing roads.
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Formulate the problem as a graph problem.
  \item Develop the solution using a well-known graph algorithm.
  \item Write the pseudo-code of the solution.
\end{enumerate}
\mk{23} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{1. Graph Problem Formulation}

We can model the city as an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$, where:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $V$ is the set of $N$ towns (vertices).
    \item $E$ is the set of $M$ roads (edges).
\end{itemize}

Each edge $e \in E$ has a type $t(e) \in \{1, 2, 3\}$. We can divide $E$ into three disjoint subsets: $E_1$ (type 1, men only), $E_2$ (type 2, women only), and $E_3$ (type 3, both).

The problem requires us to find a subset of edges $E' \subseteq E$ such that:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item The graph for men, $G_{men} = (V, E' \cap (E_1 \cup E_3))$, is connected.
    \item The graph for women, $G_{women} = (V, E' \cap (E_2 \cup E_3))$, is connected.
\end{enumerate}

To \textbf{maximize} the number of roads that can be renovated (removed), we must \textbf{minimize} the size of our kept edge set, $|E'|$.
Thus, the objective is to find $\max(|E| - |E'|)$, provided both $G_{men}$ and $G_{women}$ contain a spanning tree. If it is impossible to connect all towns for either men or women even using all available edges, the answer is that it's impossible.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Solution Development}

This problem can be elegantly solved using a greedy approach based on \textbf{Kruskal's Algorithm} for finding Minimum Spanning Trees, utilizing the \textbf{Disjoint Set Union (DSU)} data structure.

\textbf{Core Logic (Greedy Choice):} 
A Type 3 edge is highly valuable because it contributes to the connectivity of \textit{both} the men's and women's graphs simultaneously at the cost of keeping just one road. Type 1 and Type 2 edges only contribute to one graph each. 
Therefore, to minimize the total number of roads kept, we must \textbf{prioritize keeping as many useful Type 3 roads as possible} before considering Type 1 or Type 2 roads.

\textbf{Algorithm Steps:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Initialize two separate DSU structures: $DSU_{men}$ and $DSU_{women}$, each with $N$ independent components representing the towns.
    \item \textbf{Phase 1 (Process Type 3 edges):} Iterate through all roads of Type 3. If a road connects two towns that are currently in different components in either $DSU_{men}$ or $DSU_{women}$, keep this road and union the components in the respective DSUs. 
    \textit{(Note: Since we process these first, a Type 3 edge will always either connect in both DSUs or be redundant in both).}
    \item \textbf{Phase 2 (Process Type 1 edges):} Iterate through all roads of Type 1. If a road connects two towns in $DSU_{men}$ that are not yet connected, keep it and union them in $DSU_{men}$.
    \item \textbf{Phase 3 (Process Type 2 edges):} Iterate through all roads of Type 2. If a road connects two towns in $DSU_{women}$ that are not yet connected, keep it and union them in $DSU_{women}$.
    \item \textbf{Verification:} Check if both $DSU_{men}$ and $DSU_{women}$ have successfully been reduced to exactly $1$ connected component. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item If yes, the maximum roads we can renovate is: $\text{Total Roads} - \text{Roads Kept}$.
        \item If no, return $-1$ (it is impossible to ensure full connectivity).
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. Pseudo-code}

\noindent
\hrule height.8pt depth0pt \kern2pt
\captionof{algorithm}{\textbf{\textit{Maximum Renovated Roads}}}
\label{alg:max_roads}
\kern2pt\hrule\kern2pt

\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Function{MaxRenovatedRoads}{$N, edges$}
    \State $DSU_{men} \gets \Call{InitDSU}{N}$
    \State $DSU_{women} \gets \Call{InitDSU}{N}$
    \State $kept\_edges \gets 0$
    
    \Statex \Comment{Phase 1: Prioritize Type 3 roads}
    \ForAll{$edge \in edges$}
        \If{$edge.type = 3$}
            \State $useful\_for\_men \gets DSU_{men}.\Call{Union}{edge.u, edge.v}$
            \State $useful\_for\_women \gets DSU_{women}.\Call{Union}{edge.u, edge.v}$
            \If{$useful\_for\_men \lor useful\_for\_women$}
                \State $kept\_edges \gets kept\_edges + 1$
            \EndIf
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
    
    \Statex \Comment{Phase 2: Process Type 1 roads (Men only)}
    \ForAll{$edge \in edges$}
        \If{$edge.type = 1$}
            \If{$DSU_{men}.\Call{Union}{edge.u, edge.v}$}
                \State $kept\_edges \gets kept\_edges + 1$
            \EndIf
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
    
    \Statex \Comment{Phase 3: Process Type 2 roads (Women only)}
    \ForAll{$edge \in edges$}
        \If{$edge.type = 2$}
            \If{$DSU_{women}.\Call{Union}{edge.u, edge.v}$}
                \State $kept\_edges \gets kept\_edges + 1$
            \EndIf
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
    
    \Statex \Comment{Verification: Are both graphs fully connected?}
    \If{$DSU_{men}.\Call{Components}{} = 1 \land DSU_{women}.\Call{Components}{} = 1$}
        \State \Return $|edges| - kept\_edges$
    \Else
        \State \Return $-1$ \Comment{Impossible to connect the city}
    \EndIf
\EndFunction

\Statex
\Statex \Comment{--- Helper DSU Procedures ---}
\Procedure{InitDSU}{$N$}
    \State $parent \gets \text{array of size } N$
    \For{$i \gets 1 \textbf{ to } N$}
        \State $parent[i] \gets i$
    \EndFor
    \State $num\_components \gets N$
\EndProcedure

\Function{Find}{$i$}
    \If{$parent[i] = i$}
        \State \Return $i$
    \EndIf
    \State $parent[i] \gets \Call{Find}{parent[i]}$ \Comment{Path compression}
    \State \Return $parent[i]$
\EndFunction

\Function{Union}{$i, j$}
    \State $root\_i \gets \Call{Find}{i}$
    \State $root\_j \gets \Call{Find}{j}$
    \If{$root\_i \neq root\_j$}
        \State $parent[root\_i] \gets root\_j$
        \State $num\_components \gets num\_components - 1$
        \State \Return \textbf{true}
    \EndIf
    \State \Return \textbf{false}
\EndFunction

\Function{Components}{}
    \State \Return $num\_components$
\EndFunction
\end{algorithmic}
\kern2pt\hrule

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item Define and distinguish the two basic properties of the greedy algorithm.
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

To prove that a greedy algorithm yields a globally optimal solution for a given problem, the problem must exhibit two fundamental properties: the \textbf{Greedy Choice Property} and \textbf{Optimal Substructure}.

\subsection*{1. Defining the Properties}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Greedy Choice Property:} This property states that a globally optimal solution can be arrived at by making a locally optimal (greedy) choice. In other words, at each step, the algorithm makes whatever choice seems best at that exact moment, without worrying about the future or solving smaller subproblems first. Once a choice is made, it is never reconsidered or reversed.
    \item \textbf{Optimal Substructure:} A problem exhibits optimal substructure if an optimal solution to the entire problem contains within it optimal solutions to its subproblems. This means that after making the greedy choice, the remaining problem is simply a smaller instance of the exact same problem, and solving it optimally will complete the global optimal solution.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Distinguishing the Properties}

While both are required for a greedy algorithm to yield a mathematically optimal result, they serve distinctly different roles in the algorithm's design and theory:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Decision-Making vs. Problem Structure:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item The \textbf{Greedy Choice Property} is about \textit{how} we make decisions. It dictates the action taken at each step (e.g., "always pick the lightest available edge" in Kruskal's algorithm). It tells us that we don't need to exhaustively search all possibilities to find the right path.
        \item \textbf{Optimal Substructure} is about the \textit{inherent nature of the problem} itself. It dictates how the problem breaks down structurally (e.g., "the shortest path from A to C via B is composed of the shortest path from A to B and the shortest path from B to C"). 
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Relation to Dynamic Programming (DP):}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Optimal Substructure} is a shared requirement for both Greedy Algorithms and Dynamic Programming. If a problem has optimal substructure but \textit{lacks} the greedy choice property (like the 0/1 Knapsack problem), you must use DP (which evaluates multiple choices) instead of a greedy approach.
        \item The \textbf{Greedy Choice Property} is the defining characteristic that separates Greedy Algorithms from DP. It allows the algorithm to blindly commit to a single path forward, making it much faster and more memory-efficient than DP, which must evaluate, compare, and store overlapping subproblems to find the true optimal answer.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item \textbf{Jack Straws:} In the game of Jack Straws, a number of plastic or wooden ``straws'' are dumped on the table and players try to remove them one by one without disturbing the other straws. Here, we are only concerned with whether various pairs of straws are connected by a path of touching straws. Given a list of the endpoints for $n > 1$ straws (as if they were dumped on a large piece of graph paper), determine all the pairs of straws that are connected. Note that touching is connecting, but also that two straws can be connected indirectly via other connected straws.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{Algorithm to Determine Connected Jack Straws}

This problem can be modeled as finding the \textbf{connected components} of an undirected graph. 

\textbf{Graph Formulation:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Vertices ($V$):} Each of the $n$ straws represents a vertex.
    \item \textbf{Edges ($E$):} An undirected edge exists between vertex $i$ and vertex $j$ if and only if straw $i$ physically intersects (touches or crosses) straw $j$.
\end{itemize}
Two straws are considered connected (directly or indirectly) if they belong to the same connected component in this graph.

To solve this efficiently, we break the solution into two main parts: geometrically determining if two line segments intersect, and algorithmically grouping the connected straws.

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Line Segment Intersection (The Geometric Step)}

Each straw is a line segment defined by two endpoints: $p_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $q_1 = (x_2, y_2)$. To check if two segments $p_1q_1$ and $p_2q_2$ intersect, we use the \textbf{Orientation test}.

The orientation of an ordered triplet of points $(p, q, r)$ can be:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Collinear (0)
    \item Clockwise (1)
    \item Counterclockwise (2)
\end{enumerate}

Two segments $p_1q_1$ and $p_2q_2$ intersect if:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{General Case:} The triplets $(p_1, q_1, p_2)$ and $(p_1, q_1, q_2)$ have different orientations, \textbf{AND} the triplets $(p_2, q_2, p_1)$ and $(p_2, q_2, q_1)$ have different orientations.
    \item \textbf{Collinear Case (Special):} The segments are collinear and overlap (their x-projections and y-projections intersect).
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Grouping with Disjoint Set Union (The Algorithmic Step)}

Instead of building an adjacency matrix and running Breadth-First Search (BFS), we can use a \textbf{Disjoint Set Union (DSU)} data structure to efficiently group the straws as we find intersections.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Initialize a DSU where each straw $1, 2, \dots, n$ is in its own set.
    \item Iterate through all unique pairs of straws $(i, j)$ where $1 \le i < j \le n$.
    \item For each pair, check if segment $i$ intersects segment $j$ using the geometric step above.
    \item If they intersect, perform a $\text{Union}(i, j)$ operation to merge their sets.
    \item After checking all pairs, two straws $u$ and $v$ are connected if and only if $\text{Find}(u) == \text{Find}(v)$.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{Complexity and Output}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} There are $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ pairs of straws. Checking intersection takes $O(1)$ time. DSU operations with path compression and union by rank take almost $O(1)$ time, specifically $O(\alpha(n))$, where $\alpha$ is the inverse Ackermann function. Thus, the total time complexity is strictly bounded by $\mathbf{O(n^2)}$. 
    \item \textbf{Generating the Output:} To determine \textit{all} pairs that are connected, you iterate through all pairs $(i, j)$ one final time and output the pair if they share the same DSU root. 
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item \textbf{Rumor spreading:} There are $n$ people, each in possession of a different rumor. They want to share all the rumors with each other by sending electronic messages. Assume that a sender includes all the rumors he or she knows at the time the message is sent and that a message may only have one addressee. Design a greedy algorithm that always yields the minimum number of messages they need to send to guarantee that every one of them gets all the rumors.
\mk{13} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsection*{The "Coordinator" (Hub-and-Spoke) Algorithm}

To solve the rumor spreading problem with the absolute minimum number of one-way messages, we can use a greedy approach where we maximize the consolidation of information before distributing it. This is often referred to as the \textbf{Hub-and-Spoke} or \textbf{Coordinator} algorithm.

\subsubsection*{Algorithm Steps}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Elect a Coordinator:} Select exactly one person out of the $n$ people to act as the central information hub (let's call them Person $P_1$).
    \item \textbf{Phase 1: Information Gathering (Greedy Accumulation)}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Every other person ($P_2, P_3, \dots, P_n$) sends exactly one message to the coordinator, $P_1$.
        \item \textit{Messages sent in this phase: $n - 1$}
        \item \textit{State at the end of Phase 1:} $P_1$ now knows all $n$ rumors. Everyone else still only knows their own original rumor.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Phase 2: Information Dissemination (Greedy Distribution)}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item The coordinator ($P_1$), who now possesses the complete set of rumors, sends exactly one message back to every other person ($P_2, P_3, \dots, P_n$).
        \item \textit{Messages sent in this phase: $n - 1$}
        \item \textit{State at the end of Phase 2:} Everyone has received a message containing all $n$ rumors. The goal is achieved.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\hrulefill
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{Why this is Optimal}

The total number of messages sent using this algorithm is:
$$ (n - 1) + (n - 1) = \mathbf{2n - 2} $$

This is the mathematically proven absolute minimum for one-way electronic messages. Here is the logical proof for the lower bound:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{The $n-1$ Gathering Bound:} There are $n$ distinct rumors initially isolated. For any single person to learn all of them, at least $n-1$ people must send a message to move their unique rumor out of isolation. Thus, it takes at least $n-1$ messages just to compile all rumors into one place.
    \item \textbf{The $n-1$ Distribution Bound:} Once a complete compilation of rumors exists, there are still $n-1$ people who do not have the full set. It requires at least $n-1$ distinct messages to deliver the missing information to those $n-1$ individuals.
\end{itemize}

By strictly separating the process into a pure gathering phase followed by a pure dissemination phase, the algorithm guarantees the minimum possible message count of $2n - 2$.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item The reverse of a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ is another directed graph $G_R = (V, E_R)$ on the same vertex set, but with all edges reversed; that is, $E_R = \{(v, u) : (u, v) \in E\}$. Give a linear-time algorithm for computing the reverse of a graph in adjacency list format.
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Problem Analysis}

We are given a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ represented as an adjacency list. In this representation, an array \texttt{Adj} of size $|V|$ stores a list of neighbors for each vertex. Specifically, a vertex $v$ is in the list \texttt{Adj[u]} if and only if there is a directed edge $(u, v) \in E$.

We need to compute the reversed graph $G_R = (V, E_R)$, where $E_R = \{(v, u) : (u, v) \in E\}$. In terms of adjacency lists, this means that if $v \in \texttt{Adj}[u]$ in the original graph $G$, we must have $u \in \texttt{Adj\_R}[v]$ in the reversed graph $G_R$. 

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Intuition and Approach}

To construct $G_R$ efficiently, we can systematically iterate through all edges of the original graph $G$. For every vertex $u \in V$, we traverse its adjacency list \texttt{Adj[u]}. For every vertex $v$ we encounter in this list, we have found an edge $(u, v) \in E$. We then immediately add $u$ to the new adjacency list \texttt{Adj\_R[v]}, which corresponds to the reversed edge $(v, u) \in E_R$. 

Because appending an element to the end of a linked list (or dynamic array) can be done in $O(1)$ time, processing each edge takes constant time.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Algorithm}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Compute Reverse Graph}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} \texttt{Adj}, an array of size $|V|$ representing the adjacency lists of $G$
\State \textbf{Output:} \texttt{Adj\_R}, an array of size $|V|$ representing the adjacency lists of $G_R$
\Statex
\State \textbf{Initialization:} Let \texttt{Adj\_R} be a new array of $|V|$ empty lists.
\For{each vertex $u \in \{1, 2, \dots, |V|\}$}
    \For{each vertex $v \in \texttt{Adj}[u]$}
        \State \text{Append } $u$ \text{ to } \texttt{Adj\_R}[v]
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\State \Return \texttt{Adj\_R}
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Initializing the array of $|V|$ empty lists takes $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ time.
        \item The outer loop runs exactly $|V|$ times, once for each vertex.
        \item The inner loop iterates over the elements of \texttt{Adj[u]}. Across the entire execution of the algorithm, the inner loop executes exactly once for every directed edge in the graph, totaling $|E|$ iterations.
        \item Inside the inner loop, appending an element to a list takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time.
        \item Summing these together, the total time complexity is strictly linear with respect to the size of the graph: $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item We allocate a new array \texttt{Adj\_R} of size $|V|$ to hold the list headers.
        \item We insert exactly $|E|$ elements into these lists across the entire execution.
        \item Thus, the total auxiliary space required to represent the output graph is $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Visualization}

Consider a simple directed graph $G$ and its reverse $G_R$. As seen below, traversing the adjacency list of $G$ allows us to map each edge directly to its reversed counterpart.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, node distance=2.5cm,
  thick,main node/.style={circle,draw,minimum size=8mm,font=\sffamily\bfseries}]

  % Graph G
  \node at (0, 1) {\textbf{Original Graph $G$}};
  
  \node[main node] (1) at (0, 0) {1};
  \node[main node] (2) at (-1.5, -2) {2};
  \node[main node] (3) at (1.5, -2) {3};

  \draw[->, cA] (1) edge (2);
  \draw[->, cA] (1) edge (3);
  \draw[->, cA] (2) edge (3);
  
  % Separator
  \draw[dashed, gray] (3.5, 1) -- (3.5, -3);

  % Graph G_R
  \begin{scope}[xshift=7cm]
  \node at (0, 1) {\textbf{Reversed Graph $G_R$}};
  
  \node[main node] (1r) at (0, 0) {1};
  \node[main node] (2r) at (-1.5, -2) {2};
  \node[main node] (3r) at (1.5, -2) {3};

  \draw[->, cC] (2r) edge (1r);
  \draw[->, cC] (3r) edge (1r);
  \draw[->, cC] (3r) edge (2r);
  \end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{|c|l|c|l|}
\hline
\multicolumn{2}{|c|}{\textbf{Adjacency List of $G$}} & \multicolumn{2}{c|}{\textbf{Adjacency List of $G_R$}} \\ \hline
\textbf{Vertex} & \textbf{Edges} & \textbf{Vertex} & \textbf{Edges} \\ \hline
1 & $\rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3$ & 1 & (empty) \\ \hline
2 & $\rightarrow 3$ & 2 & $\rightarrow 1$ \\ \hline
3 & (empty) & 3 & $\rightarrow 1 \rightarrow 2$ \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item The strength of a path in an edge-weighted graph $G$ is the minimum weight among its edges. The all-pairs strongest path problem asks to find the strongest path between every pair of vertices. Design an algorithm to solve this problem. \mk{5}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Problem Formulation}

Let $G = (V, E)$ be a directed, edge-weighted graph with weight function $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$. The strength of a path $p = \langle v_0, v_1, \dots, v_m \rangle$ is defined as the bottleneck or minimum edge weight along that path:
$$ \text{strength}(p) = \min_{1 \le i \le m} w(v_{i-1}, v_i) $$
We define the strongest path from vertex $u$ to vertex $v$ as the path that \textit{maximizes} this strength. If no path exists, the strength is $-\infty$. For a trivial path from a vertex to itself, the strength is defined as $\infty$ (as it imposes no bottleneck).

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Intuition and Dynamic Programming Approach}

We can adapt the \textbf{Floyd-Warshall algorithm}, which traditionally solves the All-Pairs Shortest Path problem, to solve the All-Pairs Strongest Path problem. 

In the standard Floyd-Warshall algorithm, we find the shortest path by minimizing the sum of edge weights. In our modified version, we want to \textit{maximize} the \textit{minimum} edge weight. 
Let $S^{(k)}_{i,j}$ be the maximum strength of all paths from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$ whose intermediate vertices all belong to the set $\{1, 2, \dots, k\}$. 

When considering whether to include vertex $k$ as an intermediate vertex on the strongest path from $i$ to $j$, we have two choices:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Do not use vertex $k$:} The strongest path only uses intermediate vertices from $\{1, \dots, k-1\}$. The strength is $S^{(k-1)}_{i,j}$.
    \item \textbf{Use vertex $k$:} The path goes from $i$ to $k$, and then from $k$ to $j$. The strength of this combined path is the bottleneck of the two subpaths: $\min\left(S^{(k-1)}_{i,k}, S^{(k-1)}_{k,j}\right)$.
\end{enumerate}
To find the optimal strength, we take the maximum of these two options. This yields the following recurrence relation:
$$ S^{(k)}_{i,j} = \max \left( S^{(k-1)}_{i,j}, \min \left( S^{(k-1)}_{i,k}, S^{(k-1)}_{k,j} \right) \right) $$

\textbf{Base Cases ($k=0$):}
$$
S^{(0)}_{i,j} = 
\begin{cases} 
\infty & \text{if } i = j \\
w(i, j) & \text{if } (i, j) \in E \\
-\infty & \text{if } i \neq j \text{ and } (i, j) \notin E 
\end{cases}
$$

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Algorithm}

Just like the standard Floyd-Warshall algorithm, we can optimize the space complexity from $\mathcal{O}(|V|^3)$ to $\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$ by dropping the $k$-dimension in our DP table, updating the matrix in place.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{All-Pairs Strongest Path}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} A graph $G=(V,E)$ represented by an adjacency matrix $W$ of size $n \times n$ where $W[i][j] = w(i, j)$.
\State \textbf{Output:} A matrix $S$ of size $n \times n$ where $S[i][j]$ is the strength of the strongest path from $i$ to $j$.
\Statex
\State \textbf{Initialization:}
\State Let $S$ be a new $n \times n$ matrix.
\For{$i = 1$ to $n$}
    \For{$j = 1$ to $n$}
        \If{$i == j$}
            \State $S[i][j] \gets \infty$
        \ElsIf{edge $(i, j)$ exists}
            \State $S[i][j] \gets W[i][j]$
        \Else
            \State $S[i][j] \gets -\infty$
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\Statex
\State \textbf{Dynamic Programming Computation:}
\For{$k = 1$ to $n$}
    \For{$i = 1$ to $n$}
        \For{$j = 1$ to $n$}
            \State $S[i][j] \gets \max\left(S[i][j], \min(S[i][k], S[k][j])\right)$
        \EndFor
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\State \Return $S$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V|^3)$. The algorithm consists of three tightly nested loops, each iterating $|V|$ times. The operations inside the innermost loop ($\min$ and $\max$) execute in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time. 
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$. We maintain a 2-dimensional DP matrix $S$ of size $|V| \times |V|$ to store the path strengths. By dropping the $k$-th dimension from the recurrence, we avoid $\mathcal{O}(|V|^3)$ space.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Visualization of the Recurrence Transition}

Consider the small graph below to illustrate why taking the maximum of minimums successfully identifies the strongest path. 

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, node distance=3cm,
  thick,main node/.style={circle,draw,minimum size=8mm,font=\sffamily\bfseries}]

  \node[main node] (1) at (0,0) {1};
  \node[main node] (2) at (2, 2) {2};
  \node[main node] (3) at (4,0) {3};

  \draw[->, cA] (1) edge node[above left] {10} (2);
  \draw[->, cA] (2) edge node[above right] {20} (3);
  \draw[->, cA, dashed] (1) edge node[below] {5} (3);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

Suppose we are evaluating the strongest path from vertex $1$ to vertex $3$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item Initially ($k=0$), the only known path is the direct edge: $S^{(0)}_{1,3} = 5$.
    \item When vertex $2$ is introduced as a possible intermediate vertex ($k=2$), the algorithm evaluates the path $1 \to 2 \to 3$.
    \item The strength of the path through vertex 2 is the bottleneck: $\min(w(1,2), w(2,3)) = \min(10, 20) = 10$.
    \item The algorithm updates the matrix: $S_{1,3} \gets \max(5, 10) = 10$.
\end{itemize}
Thus, the algorithm correctly identifies that taking the indirect route yields a strictly stronger bottleneck capacity.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$, give a linear-time algorithm to check whether there is a cycle of odd length in the graph. Briefly justify its correctness. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2015--16}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Theoretical Foundation and Graph Property}

To design a linear-time algorithm for detecting an odd-length cycle, we leverage a fundamental theorem from graph theory:
\begin{quote}
\textit{An undirected graph $G$ contains an odd cycle if and only if it is \textbf{not bipartite}.}
\end{quote}
A graph is bipartite if its vertices can be partitioned into two disjoint sets, say $U$ and $W$, such that every edge connects a vertex in $U$ to a vertex in $W$. If we attempt to color a graph using two colors (e.g., Red and Blue) such that no two adjacent vertices share the same color, a successful 2-coloring proves the graph is bipartite (hence, no odd cycles). If a conflict arises where an edge connects two vertices of the same color, an odd cycle is guaranteed to exist.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Algorithmic Strategy}

We can check for bipartiteness in linear time $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ using a Breadth-First Search (BFS) or Depth-First Search (DFS) traversal. The algorithm works as follows:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Initialize all vertices as uncolored.
    \item Loop through all vertices to handle disconnected components. For each uncolored vertex, initiate a BFS.
    \item Assign the starting vertex a default color (e.g., $0$).
    \item During the BFS traversal, for each vertex $u$ being processed, examine all its neighbors $v$:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item If $v$ is uncolored, assign it the opposite color of $u$ (i.e., $\text{color}[v] = 1 - \text{color}[u]$) and enqueue it.
        \item If $v$ is already colored and has the same color as $u$ ($\text{color}[v] == \text{color}[u]$), a cross-edge within the same layer or between identical color states has been found. This indicates the presence of an odd-length cycle. Immediately terminate and return \texttt{True}.
    \end{itemize}
    \item If the entire graph is traversed without encountering a color conflict, return \texttt{False}.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Algorithm}
\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Odd Cycle Detection via 2-Coloring}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} An undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ in adjacency list representation.
\State \textbf{Output:} \texttt{True} if $G$ contains an odd cycle, \texttt{False} otherwise.
\Statex
\State \textbf{Initialization:}
\State Let $\mathit{color}$ be an array of size $|V|$, initialized to $-1$ (representing uncolored) for all vertices.
\Statex
\ForAll{vertex $s \in V$}
    \If{$\mathit{color}[s] == -1$}
        \State $\mathit{color}[s] \gets 0$ \Comment{Color the root of a new component with 0}
        \State Create an empty queue $Q$
        \State Enqueue $s$ onto $Q$
        \Statex
        \While{$Q$ is not empty}
            \State Dequeue vertex $u$ from $Q$
            \ForAll{neighbor $v \in \text{Adj}[u]$}
                \If{$\mathit{color}[v] == -1$}
                    \State $\mathit{color}[v] \gets 1 - \mathit{color}[u]$ \Comment{Assign alternate color}
                    \State Enqueue $v$ onto $Q$
                \ElsIf{$\mathit{color}[v] == \mathit{color}[u]$}
                    \State \Return \texttt{True} \Comment{Odd cycle detected}
                \EndIf
            \EndFor
        \EndWhile
    \EndIf
\EndFor
\State \Return \texttt{False} \Comment{Graph is bipartite; no odd cycle exists}
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Justification of Correctness}

We must prove both directions of the implication:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{If the algorithm returns \texttt{True}, an odd cycle exists:} 
    Suppose the algorithm finds an edge $(u, v)$ where $\text{color}[u] == \text{color}[v]$. Let $s$ be the lowest common ancestor (LCA) of $u$ and $v$ in the BFS tree root. The tree path from $s$ to $u$ has length $L_1$, and the tree path from $s$ to $v$ has length $L_2$. 
    Because the coloring alternates at every level of the BFS tree, the color of any node $x$ is determined by the parity of its distance from $s$. Specifically:
    $$ \text{color}[u] \equiv L_1 \pmod 2 \quad \text{and} \quad \text{color}[v] \equiv L_2 \pmod 2 $$
    Since $\text{color}[u] == \text{color}[v]$, it must be that $L_1$ and $L_2$ have the same parity ($L_1 \equiv L_2 \pmod 2$). 
    The cycle formed by the tree path $s \leadsto u$, the cross-edge $(u, v)$, and the reverse tree path $v \leadsto s$ has a total length of:
    $$ \text{Length} = L_1 + L_2 + 1 $$
    Since $L_1$ and $L_2$ share the same parity, their sum $L_1 + L_2$ is guaranteed to be even. Adding the $1$ from the cross-edge makes the total length $L_1 + L_2 + 1$ strictly \textbf{odd}.

    \item \textbf{If an odd cycle exists, the algorithm will return \texttt{True}:}
    Assume for contradiction that the graph contains an odd cycle $C = \langle v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k, v_1 \rangle$ (where $k$ is odd), but the algorithm returns \texttt{False} (meaning it successfully 2-colors the graph).
    As we traverse the cycle, the colors must alternate:
    $$ \text{color}[v_2] = 1 - \text{color}[v_1], \quad \text{color}[v_3] = \text{color}[v_1], \quad \dots $$
    By induction, for any vertex $v_i$ in the cycle, $\text{color}[v_i] = \text{color}[v_1]$ if $i$ is odd, and $\text{color}[v_i] \neq \text{color}[v_1]$ if $i$ is even. 
    Since $k$ is odd, $\text{color}[v_k] = \text{color}[v_1]$. However, there is an edge connecting $v_k$ and $v_1$ to close the cycle. This means adjacent vertices $v_k$ and $v_1$ share the same color, which causes a direct conflict. The algorithm would inevitably catch this conflict and return \texttt{True}, contradicting our assumption.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$. Each vertex is enqueued and dequeued at most once due to the color check array. For every vertex examined, we iterate through its adjacency list. Across the entire execution, every edge is examined exactly twice (once from each endpoint). Thus, the total runtime is strictly linear.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ auxiliary space. This is required for the $\text{color}$ array and the BFS queue $Q$, which both scale linearly with the number of vertices.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Visualizing a Color Conflict (Odd Cycle Found)}

The diagram below showcases a 3-cycle (triangle) where a color conflict is detected upon evaluating the cross-edge between vertex 2 and vertex 3.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=2.5cm, thick,
  node0/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=blue!30, minimum size=8mm, font=\bfseries},
  node1/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=red!40, minimum size=8mm, font=\bfseries}]

  % Vertices
  \node[node0] (1) at (0, 1.5) {1 (C:0)};
  \node[node1] (2) at (-1.5, -0.5) {2 (C:1)};
  \node[node1] (3) at (1.5, -0.5) {3 (C:1)};

  % Tree edges
  \draw[-] (1) -- node[above left, font=\small] {BFS Tree} (2);
  \draw[-] (1) -- node[above right, font=\small] {BFS Tree} (3);
  
  % Conflict edge
  \draw[-, line width=1.5pt, red!80!black, dashed] (2) -- node[below, text=red!80!black, font=\bfseries] {Conflict Edge (Same Color)} (3);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Suppose a CS curriculum consists of $n$ mandatory courses. The prerequisite directed graph $G$ has a node for each course and an edge from $v$ to $w$ if $v$ is a prerequisite for $w$. Give a linear-time algorithm that computes the minimum number of semesters necessary to complete the curriculum (a student may take any number of courses per semester). \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2015--16}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Key Insight.} The minimum number of semesters equals the length of the \emph{longest path} in the prerequisite DAG $G$. A course can be taken in semester $k$ only after all its prerequisites are completed, so the earliest semester for a course is determined by the longest chain of prerequisites leading to it.

\medskip
\textbf{Observation.} Since prerequisites cannot be circular (a student must actually be able to finish), $G$ must be a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph). The minimum number of semesters is:
\[
  \text{Answer} = \max_{v \in V} \, d(v)
\]
where $d(v)$ denotes the length of the longest path ending at $v$ (i.e., the earliest semester in which $v$ can be taken), counting from semester $1$.

\medskip
\textbf{Recurrence.} Define $d(v)$ as the semester in which course $v$ is first available:
\[
  d(v) = \begin{cases}
    1 & \text{if } v \text{ has no prerequisites (in-degree } 0\text{)} \\
    1 + \displaystyle\max_{(u,v) \in E} \, d(u) & \text{otherwise}
  \end{cases}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{Algorithm.} We compute $d(v)$ for all nodes using \textbf{Kahn's topological sort} (BFS-based), processing nodes in topological order so that when we process $v$, all predecessors $u$ with edge $(u,v)$ have already been assigned $d(u)$.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Minimum Semesters via Longest Path in DAG}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Directed acyclic graph $G = (V, E)$, $|V| = n$
\Ensure Minimum number of semesters $S$

\State Compute $\mathit{indegree}[v]$ for all $v \in V$
\State Initialize $d[v] \leftarrow 0$ for all $v \in V$
\State Initialize queue $Q \leftarrow \emptyset$
\For{each $v \in V$}
  \If{$\mathit{indegree}[v] = 0$}
    \State $d[v] \leftarrow 1$
    \State $Q.\text{enqueue}(v)$
  \EndIf
\EndFor
\State $S \leftarrow 1$
\While{$Q \neq \emptyset$}
  \State $u \leftarrow Q.\text{dequeue}()$
  \State $S \leftarrow \max(S,\; d[u])$
  \For{each neighbor $w$ such that $(u, w) \in E$}
    \State $d[w] \leftarrow \max\bigl(d[w],\; d[u] + 1\bigr)$
    \State $\mathit{indegree}[w] \leftarrow \mathit{indegree}[w] - 1$
    \If{$\mathit{indegree}[w] = 0$}
      \State $Q.\text{enqueue}(w)$
    \EndIf
  \EndFor
\EndWhile
\State \Return $S$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\medskip
\textbf{Illustrative Example.} Consider 6 courses with the following prerequisites:

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node distance=1.6cm and 2.2cm,
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\small},
  >=Stealth
]
  \node (A) at (0,0)   {$A$};
  \node (B) at (0,-1.6){$B$};
  \node (C) at (2.2,-0.8){$C$};
  \node (D) at (2.2,-2.4){$D$};
  \node (E) at (4.4,-0.8){$E$};
  \node (F) at (4.4,-2.4){$F$};

  \draw[->] (A) -- (C);
  \draw[->] (B) -- (C);
  \draw[->] (B) -- (D);
  \draw[->] (C) -- (E);
  \draw[->] (D) -- (F);
  \draw[->] (E) -- (F);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

Computing $d$ values:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\hline
\textbf{Course} & \textbf{Prerequisites} & \textbf{Semester $d$} \\
\hline
$A$ & none & 1 \\
$B$ & none & 1 \\
$C$ & $A, B$ & $\max(d[A], d[B]) + 1 = 2$ \\
$D$ & $B$ & $d[B] + 1 = 2$ \\
$E$ & $C$ & $d[C] + 1 = 3$ \\
$F$ & $D, E$ & $\max(d[D], d[E]) + 1 = 4$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

The minimum number of semesters is $\max\{1,1,2,2,3,4\} = \boxed{4}$.

\medskip
\textbf{Correctness.}
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textit{Lower bound:} Any chain of $k$ prerequisite-dependent courses requires at least $k$ semesters, so the answer is at least the longest path length.
  \item \textit{Achievability:} By taking all courses with $d[v] = k$ in semester $k$, the schedule is valid since every prerequisite $u$ of $v$ satisfies $d[u] < d[v]$, meaning $u$ is taken in an earlier semester. Hence the answer equals the longest path.
\end{itemize}

\medskip
\textbf{Complexity Analysis.}
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Time:} $O(V + E)$. Computing in-degrees takes $O(V+E)$. Each node is enqueued and dequeued once: $O(V)$. Each edge is relaxed exactly once: $O(E)$. Total: $\boldsymbol{O(V+E)}$ --- linear in the size of the graph.
  \item \textbf{Space:} $O(V + E)$ for the adjacency list, in-degree array, $d$ array, and queue.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Find the transitive closure. Show the matrices computed at each step. What is the cost to find the transitive closure? \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
  every path/.style={->, line width=1pt},
  ]
\tikzset{>={Stealth[length=12pt, width=8pt, inset=2pt]}}

  \node (a) at (0, 2)  {a};
  \node (b) at (0, 0)  {b};
  \node (c) at (3, 0)  {c};
  \node (d) at (3, 2)  {d};

  \draw (b) -> (a);
  \draw (b) -> (c);
  \draw (d) -> (b);
  \draw (d) to[bend left=15] (c);
  \draw (c) to[bend left=15] (d);

  \end{tikzpicture}

\end{center}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Method: Warshall's Algorithm.} We compute the transitive closure using the recurrence:
\[
  t_{ij}^{(k)} = t_{ij}^{(k-1)} \;\vee\; \bigl(t_{ik}^{(k-1)} \wedge t_{kj}^{(k-1)}\bigr)
\]
Node ordering: $1=a,\; 2=b,\; 3=c,\; 4=d$.

\medskip
\textbf{Initial Matrix $T^{(0)}$} (direct edges only, diagonal = 1):

From the graph: $b{\to}a$, $b{\to}c$, $d{\to}b$, $d{\to}c$, $c{\to}d$.

\[
T^{(0)} = \bordermatrix{
  & a & b & c & d \cr
a & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \cr
b & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 \cr
c & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \cr
d & 0 & 1 & 1 & 1
}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{Step $k=1$ (intermediate vertex: $a$).} Check: can any pair $(i,j)$ be connected via $a$? Since column $a$ in $T^{(0)}$ has $t_{ia}=1$ only for $i=a$ and $i=b$, and row $a$ has $t_{aj}=1$ only for $j=a$, no new entries are added.
\[
T^{(1)} = \bordermatrix{
  & a & b & c & d \cr
a & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \cr
b & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 \cr
c & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \cr
d & 0 & 1 & 1 & 1
}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{Step $k=2$ (intermediate vertex: $b$).} For each $(i,j)$: if $t_{ib}^{(1)}=1$ and $t_{bj}^{(1)}=1$, set $t_{ij}=1$.

Row $b$ of $T^{(1)}$: $(a,b,c,d) = (1,1,1,0)$. Who reaches $b$? Column $b$: $d$ has $t_{db}=1$.

New connections via $b$:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $d \to b \to a$: set $t_{da} = 1$
  \item $d \to b \to c$: already $1$
\end{itemize}

\[
T^{(2)} = \bordermatrix{
  & a & b & c & d \cr
a & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \cr
b & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 \cr
c & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \cr
d & \mathbf{1} & 1 & 1 & 1
}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{Step $k=3$ (intermediate vertex: $c$).} Row $c$ of $T^{(2)}$: $(a,b,c,d)=(0,0,1,1)$. Who reaches $c$? Column $c$: $b,c,d$ all have $t_{ic}=1$.

New connections via $c$:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $b \to c \to d$: set $t_{bd} = 1$
  \item $d \to c \to d$: already $1$
\end{itemize}

\[
T^{(3)} = \bordermatrix{
  & a & b & c & d \cr
a & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \cr
b & 1 & 1 & 1 & \mathbf{1} \cr
c & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \cr
d & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1
}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{Step $k=4$ (intermediate vertex: $d$).} Row $d$ of $T^{(3)}$: $(a,b,c,d)=(1,1,1,1)$. Who reaches $d$? Column $d$: $b,c,d$ all have $t_{id}=1$.

New connections via $d$:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $b \to d \to a$: already $1$
  \item $b \to d \to b$: already $1$
  \item $b \to d \to c$: already $1$
  \item $c \to d \to a$: set $t_{ca} = 1$
  \item $c \to d \to b$: set $t_{cb} = 1$
  \item $c \to d \to c$: already $1$
\end{itemize}

\[
T^{(4)} = \bordermatrix{
  & a & b & c & d \cr
a & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \cr
b & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \cr
c & \mathbf{1} & \mathbf{1} & 1 & 1 \cr
d & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1
}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{Final Transitive Closure $T^* = T^{(4)}$:}

\[
T^{*} = \bordermatrix{
  & a & b & c & d \cr
a & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \cr
b & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \cr
c & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \cr
d & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1
}
\]

\textbf{Interpretation:} $a$ can only reach itself. $b$, $c$, and $d$ can all reach every node (including $a$), forming a strongly connected component among $\{b,c,d\}$ with $a$ reachable from all three.

\medskip
\textbf{Verification via Graph:}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.85cm, font=\bfseries},
  every path/.style={->, line width=0.9pt},
  >=Stealth
]
  \node (a) at (0, 2)  {a};
  \node (b) at (0, 0)  {b};
  \node (c) at (3, 0)  {c};
  \node (d) at (3, 2)  {d};

  \draw (b) -- (a);
  \draw (b) to[bend left=10] (c);
  \draw (d) -- (b);
  \draw (d) to[bend left=15] (c);
  \draw (c) to[bend left=15] (d);
  \draw[dashed, color=cA] (b) to[bend right=25] (d);
  \draw[dashed, color=cA] (c) to[bend right=25] (a);
  \draw[dashed, color=cA] (c) to[bend left=30] (b);
  \draw[dashed, color=cA] (d) to[bend right=40] (a);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
{\small Solid edges: original. Dashed \textcolor{cA}{blue} edges: newly inferred reachability.}

\medskip
\textbf{Cost Analysis.}

Warshall's algorithm runs three nested loops over $n$ vertices:
\[
  \text{Time Complexity} = O(n^3)
\]
For this graph $n = 4$, so the total number of operations is at most $4^3 = 64$ boolean updates.

\medskip
\textbf{Space Complexity} $= O(n^2)$ for storing the $n \times n$ boolean matrix (updated in-place across $n$ steps).

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Let $C$ and $C'$ be distinct strongly connected components in a directed graph $G = (V, E)$. Suppose there is an edge $(u, v) \in E$ where $u \in C$ and $v \in C'$. Prove that $f(C) > f(C')$, where $f(U)$ denotes the latest finishing time of any vertex in $U$. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\textbf{Theorem.} Let $C$ and $C'$ be distinct SCCs of $G$, and let $(u,v) \in E$ with $u \in C$, $v \in C'$. Then $f(C) > f(C')$, where $f(U) = \max_{x \in U} f(x)$.
