    \\ The memory footprint requires $\mathcal{O}(N)$ space to store the node links and attributes within the system. The runtime recursion stack scales linearly with the maximum height of the tree, requiring $\mathcal{O}(h) = \mathcal{O}(\log N)$ auxiliary space.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Prove that the height of an AVL tree with $n$ elements is $O(\log n)$.
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{1. Objective and Definitions}

To prove that the height $h$ of an AVL tree with $n$ elements is $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$, we must establish an upper bound on $h$ in terms of $n$. We can achieve this inversely by establishing a \textit{lower bound} on the minimum number of nodes $N(h)$ required to construct an AVL tree of height $h$. If we can prove that $N(h)$ grows exponentially with respect to $h$, it strictly implies that $h$ grows logarithmically with respect to $n$.

\textit{Note:} We define the height $h$ as the maximum number of edges from the root to a leaf. Therefore, a tree with a single node has $h = 0$.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\textbf{2. Establishing the Recurrence Relation}

To minimize the number of nodes in an AVL tree of height $h$, we must maximize the imbalance at every node without violating the AVL property (which allows a maximum height difference of $1$ between sibling subtrees).

Let $T_h$ be the minimal AVL tree of height $h$. The root of $T_h$ must have one subtree of height $h-1$. To minimize the total nodes, the other subtree must have the minimum allowed height, which is $h-2$. Both subtrees must also be minimal AVL trees for their respective heights.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    >=stealth,
    level distance=1.5cm,
    sibling distance=3.5cm,
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=cB, fill=cB!10, thick, minimum size=8mm, font=\bfseries},
    subtree/.style={regular polygon, regular polygon sides=3, draw=gray, fill=gray!10, thick, minimum size=18mm, yshift=-0.4cm, font=\small}
]
    \node[treenode] (Root) {$T_h$}
        child {node[subtree] (Left) {$T_{h-1}$} edge from parent node[above left] {$h-1$}}
        child {node[subtree] (Right) {$T_{h-2}$} edge from parent node[above right] {$h-2$}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

This structural decomposition yields the following recurrence relation for $N(h)$, the number of nodes in $T_h$:
$$ N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1 $$

With the base cases:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $N(0) = 1$ (A single node)
    \item $N(1) = 2$ (A root with one leaf child)
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\textbf{3. Solving the Recurrence (The Fibonacci Connection)}

We can transform this non-homogeneous recurrence into a homogeneous one. Let us define a new function $M(h) = N(h) + 1$. 

Adding $1$ to both sides of our recurrence relation gives:
$$ N(h) + 1 = (N(h-1) + 1) + (N(h-2) + 1) $$
$$ M(h) = M(h-1) + M(h-2) $$

This is precisely the recurrence relation for the \textbf{Fibonacci sequence} ($F_k$). We map our base cases to the standard Fibonacci sequence ($F_1 = 1, F_2 = 1, F_3 = 2, F_4 = 3, \dots$):
\begin{itemize}
    \item $M(0) = N(0) + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 = F_3$
    \item $M(1) = N(1) + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 = F_4$
\end{itemize}

By mathematical induction, it holds that for any $h \ge 0$:
$$ M(h) = F_{h+3} $$
$$ N(h) = F_{h+3} - 1 $$

\vspace{0.3cm}
\textbf{4. Asymptotic Bound via the Golden Ratio}

To establish the asymptotic bound, we use Binet's formula for the Fibonacci sequence:
$$ F_k = \frac{\phi^k - \psi^k}{\sqrt{5}} $$
Where $\phi = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ (the Golden Ratio) and $\psi = \frac{1 - \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx -0.618$. 

Since $|\psi| < 1$, the term $\psi^k \to 0$ as $k$ grows, so $F_k \approx \frac{\phi^k}{\sqrt{5}}$. Even for small values of $k$, we can establish a strict inequality: $F_k \ge \frac{\phi^{k-2}}{\sqrt{5}}$ or simpler yet, it is a known property that $F_{h+3} > \phi^h$ for $h \ge 1$.

Applying this to our node count $n$:
$$ n = N(h) = F_{h+3} - 1 $$
$$ n + 1 = F_{h+3} \ge \phi^h $$

Taking the base-$\phi$ logarithm of both sides:
$$ \log_\phi(n + 1) \ge h $$

By the change-of-base formula for logarithms, $\log_\phi(x) = \frac{\log_2(x)}{\log_2(\phi)}$. Since $\log_2(\phi) \approx 0.694$:
$$ h \le \frac{\log_2(n + 1)}{0.694} \approx 1.44 \log_2(n + 1) $$

\vspace{0.3cm}
\textbf{5. Conclusion}

We have shown that in the absolute worst-case scenario (a maximally unbalanced AVL tree), the height $h$ is strictly bounded by approximately $1.44 \log_2(n)$. Since $1.44$ is a constant, we drop it for asymptotic notation, yielding:
$$ h = \mathcal{O}(\log n) $$

This formally guarantees that all primary operations (Search, Insert, Delete) in an AVL tree, which traverse root-to-leaf paths, will run in strict $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ worst-case time complexity.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item Perform the following operations sequentially on an initially empty AVL tree. Show the intermediate trees after each step: (i) insert 15, (ii) insert 22, (iii) insert 24, (iv) insert 7, (v) insert 9, (vi) delete 24.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Execution of AVL Tree Operations}

An AVL tree maintains the property that for every node, the height difference between its left and right subtrees (Balance Factor, BF = Height(Left) - Height(Right)) is at most 1. We will trace the requested operations step-by-step, highlighting any imbalances and the rotations used to resolve them.

\tikzset{
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=cB, fill=cB!10, thick, minimum size=7mm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries},
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    every child node/.style={treenode},
    edge from parent/.style={thick, draw=black!70}
}

\subsection*{(i) Insert 15}
The tree is initially empty. We simply insert 15 as the root. The tree is perfectly balanced.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
    \node[treenode] {15};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsection*{(ii) Insert 22}
We insert 22. Since $22 > 15$, it becomes the right child of 15. The balance factor of 15 is $0 - 1 = -1$, which is within the allowed range $[-1, 1]$. No rotations are needed.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
    \node[treenode] {15}
        child[missing] {}
        child {node {22}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsection*{(iii) Insert 24}
We insert 24 as the right child of 22. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Imbalance:} The balance factor of 15 becomes $0 - 2 = -2$. 
    \item \textbf{Case:} The insertion happened in the right subtree of the right child of 15. This is a \textbf{Right-Right (RR)} case.
    \item \textbf{Rotation:} We perform a \textbf{Single Left Rotation} on node 15. Node 22 becomes the new root, 15 becomes its left child, and 24 remains its right child.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
    % Before
    \begin{scope}[xshift=0cm]
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.5cm] {\textit{Before Rotation}\\(Imbalance at 15)};
        \node[treenode, label=left:{\color{red}\small BF=-2}] {15}
            child[missing] {}
            child {node[label=right:{\small BF=-1}] {22}
                child[missing] {}
                child {node {24}}
            };
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (2.5, -1) -- node[above] {Left Rotate(15)} (4.5, -1);
    
    % After
    \begin{scope}[xshift=7cm]
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.5cm] {\textit{After Rotation}};
        \node[treenode, label=right:{\color{green!60!black}\small BF=0}] {22}
            child {node {15}}
            child {node {24}};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsection*{(iv) Insert 7}
We insert 7. Since $7 < 22$ and $7 < 15$, it is placed as the left child of 15. 
The balance factors are updated: BF(15) = +1, BF(22) = $2 - 1 = +1$. The tree remains balanced.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
    \node[treenode, label=right:{\small BF=+1}] {22}
        child {node[label=left:{\small BF=+1}] {15}
            child {node {7}}
            child[missing] {}
        }
        child {node[label=right:{\small BF=0}] {24}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsection*{(v) Insert 9}
We insert 9. Since $9 < 22$, $9 < 15$, and $9 > 7$, it becomes the right child of 7.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Imbalance:} The balance factor of 15 becomes $2 - 0 = +2$. 
    \item \textbf{Case:} The insertion occurred in the right subtree of the left child (7) of node 15. This is a \textbf{Left-Right (LR)} case.
    \item \textbf{Rotation:} A double rotation is required to restore balance:
        \begin{enumerate}
            \item \textbf{Left Rotation on 7:} Node 9 moves up, 7 becomes its left child.
            \item \textbf{Right Rotation on 15:} Node 9 moves up to replace 15, and 15 becomes the right child of 9.
        \end{enumerate}
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm, level distance=1cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1cm, level distance=1cm}
]
    % Before
    \begin{scope}[xshift=0cm]
        \node[text width=3.5cm, align=center, above=0.3cm] {\textit{Before Rotation}\\(Imbalance at 15)};
        \node[treenode] {22}
            child {node[label=right:{\color{red}\small BF=+2}] {15}
                child {node[label=left:{\small BF=-1}] {7}
                    child[missing] {}
                    child {node {9}}
                }
                child[missing] {}
            }
            child {node {24}};
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (2.5, -1) -- node[above] {LR Rotate(15)} (4.5, -1);
    
    % After
    \begin{scope}[xshift=7cm]
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.3cm] {\textit{After Rotation}};
        \node[treenode, label=right:{\small BF=+1}] {22}
            child {node[label=left:{\color{green!60!black}\small BF=0}] {9}
                child {node {7}}
                child {node {15}}
            }
            child {node {24}};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsection*{(vi) Delete 24}
We delete 24, which is a leaf node. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Imbalance:} The balance factor of the root (22) becomes $\text{Height(Left)} - \text{Height(Right)} = 2 - 0 = +2$. 
    \item \textbf{Case:} We look at the left child of 22, which is node 9. The balance factor of 9 is $1 - 1 = 0$. When an imbalance occurs due to deletion and the heavier child's BF is $\ge 0$, it is treated symmetrically to a \textbf{Left-Left (LL)} case.
    \item \textbf{Rotation:} We perform a \textbf{Single Right Rotation} on the unbalanced node 22. Node 9 becomes the new root. Node 22 becomes the right child of 9. The right child of 9 (node 15) is adopted as the left child of 22.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm}
]
    % Before
    \begin{scope}[xshift=0cm]
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.5cm] {\textit{After Deleting 24}\\(Imbalance at 22)};
        \node[treenode, label=right:{\color{red}\small BF=+2}] {22}
            child {node[label=left:{\small BF=0}] {9}
                child {node {7}}
                child {node {15}}
            }
            child[missing] {};
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (2.5, -1) -- node[above] {Right Rotate(22)} (4.5, -1);
    
    % After
    \begin{scope}[xshift=7cm]
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.5cm] {\textit{Final Tree}};
        \node[treenode, label=right:{\small BF=-1}] {9}
            child {node[label=left:{\small BF=0}] {7}}
            child {node[label=right:{\small BF=+1}] {22}
                child {node[label=left:{\small BF=0}] {15}}
                child[missing] {}
            };
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item Write down the properties of an AVL tree, and prove that it is a balanced binary search tree.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsection*{1. Properties of an AVL Tree}

An \textbf{AVL Tree} (named after inventors Adelson-Velsky and Landis) is a self-balancing data structure that strictly enforces both search and structural properties. It guarantees $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ time complexity for fundamental operations (Search, Insert, Delete).

The defining properties of an AVL tree are:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Binary Search Tree (BST) Property:} For every node $u$ in the tree, all keys in the left subtree $T_{left}$ of $u$ are strictly less than the key of $u$, and all keys in the right subtree $T_{right}$ are strictly greater than the key of $u$.
    \item \textbf{Height-Balance Property:} For \textit{every} node $u$ in the tree, the heights of its left and right subtrees differ by at most $1$. Formally, the Balance Factor (BF) must satisfy:
    \begin{equation*}
        \text{BF}(u) = \text{Height}(u.left) - \text{Height}(u.right) \in \{-1, 0, 1\}
    \end{equation*}
    \textit{(Note: The height of an empty subtree is defined as $-1$, and a single leaf node has a height of $0$.)}
    \item \textbf{Self-Balancing Guarantee:} Any structural modification (insertion or deletion) that violates the height-balance property is strictly resolved via localized, constant-time $\mathcal{O}(1)$ operations known as \textit{rotations} (LL, RR, LR, RL).
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.2cm,
    sibling distance=2cm,
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=cB, fill=cB!10, thick, minimum size=7mm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries},
    invalidnode/.style={circle, draw=red, fill=red!10, thick, minimum size=7mm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries},
    edge from parent/.style={thick, draw=black!70}
]

    % Valid AVL Tree
    \begin{scope}[xshift=0cm]
        \node[text width=4cm, align=center, above=0.4cm] {\textbf{Valid AVL Tree}\\ \small All nodes have $|\text{BF}| \le 1$};
        \node[treenode, label=left:{\color{green!60!black}\small BF=0}] {20}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\small BF=-1}] {10}
                child[missing] {}
                child {node[treenode, label=right:{\small BF=0}] {15}}
            }
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\small BF=0}] {30}};
    \end{scope}

    % Invalid AVL Tree
    \begin{scope}[xshift=7cm]
        \node[text width=4cm, align=center, above=0.4cm] {\textbf{Invalid AVL Tree}\\ \small Root violates property};
        \node[invalidnode, label=right:{\color{red}\small BF=+2}] {20}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\small BF=+1}] {10}
                child {node[treenode, label=left:{\small BF=0}] {5}}
                child[missing] {}
            }
            child[missing] {};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5cm}
\subsection*{2. Proof that an AVL Tree is a Balanced BST}

To mathematically prove that an AVL tree is balanced, we must demonstrate that its maximum height $h$ is strictly bounded by a logarithmic function of its total number of nodes $n$, i.e., $h = \mathcal{O}(\log n)$. We prove this by establishing a lower bound on the minimum number of nodes $N(h)$ required to build an AVL tree of height $h$.

\textbf{Step 1: Construct the Minimal AVL Tree} \\
To minimize the total nodes for a given height $h$, we must maximize the allowable imbalance at every node. For the root, one subtree must have height $h-1$. To keep the node count minimal while satisfying the AVL property ($|\text{BF}| \le 1$), the other subtree must have the minimum allowed height, which is $h-2$. Both subtrees must recursively be minimal AVL trees.

This structural requirement yields the linear recurrence relation:
\begin{equation*}
    N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1
\end{equation*}

Assuming height is defined by the number of edges (a single node has $h=0$):
\begin{itemize}
    \item $N(0) = 1$ (A single root node)
    \item $N(1) = 2$ (A root with exactly one child)
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Step 2: Connecting to the Fibonacci Sequence} \\
To solve the non-homogeneous recurrence, we add $1$ to both sides:
\begin{equation*}
    N(h) + 1 = (N(h-1) + 1) + (N(h-2) + 1)
\end{equation*}
Let $M(h) = N(h) + 1$. The recurrence simplifies to:
\begin{equation*}
    M(h) = M(h-1) + M(h-2)
\end{equation*}
This matches the homogeneous recurrence of the Fibonacci sequence $F_k$ ($1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8 \dots$).
Mapping our base cases $M(0) = 2 = F_3$ and $M(1) = 3 = F_4$, we deduce:
\begin{equation*}
    M(h) = F_{h+3} \implies N(h) = F_{h+3} - 1
\end{equation*}

\textbf{Step 3: Establishing the Asymptotic Bound} \\
A known property of the Fibonacci sequence is its exponential growth bounded by the Golden Ratio, $\phi = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$. It is a standard result that for all $k \ge 1$:
\begin{equation*}
    F_k \ge \frac{\phi^{k-2}}{\sqrt{5}}
\end{equation*}
For simplicity, we can rely on the strict lower bound $F_{h+3} > \phi^h$. Substituting $n$ for $N(h)$:
\begin{align*}
    n &= F_{h+3} - 1 \\
    n + 1 &= F_{h+3} > \phi^h
\end{align*}

Taking the base-$\phi$ logarithm of both sides:
\begin{equation*}
    \log_\phi(n + 1) > h
\end{equation*}

By applying the change-of-base formula for logarithms ($\log_b(x) = \frac{\log_c(x)}{\log_c(b)}$):
\begin{equation*}
    h < \frac{\log_2(n + 1)}{\log_2(\phi)}
\end{equation*}

Since $\log_2(\phi) = \log_2(1.618) \approx 0.694$:
\begin{equation*}
    h < \frac{1}{0.694} \log_2(n + 1) \approx 1.44 \log_2(n + 1)
\end{equation*}

\textbf{Conclusion} \\
We have proven that even in the absolute worst-case scenario (a tree sparsed out to the structural limits of the AVL property), the height $h$ scales logarithmically with the number of nodes $n$. Dropping the constant $1.44$, we formally conclude that:
\begin{equation*}
    h = \mathcal{O}(\log n)
\end{equation*}
Thus, an AVL tree is mathematically proven to be a balanced Binary Search Tree.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item Suppose you are inserting a node in an AVL tree, and $x$ is the lowest node violating AVL balance and $x$ is left-heavy. Demonstrate the balancing procedure using figures.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

   \textbf{Case 1: Left-Left (LL) Imbalance}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    >=stealth,
    node distance=1.5cm,
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=cB, fill=cB!10, thick, minimum size=7mm},
    subtree/.style={regular polygon, regular polygon sides=3, draw=gray, fill=gray!10, thick, minimum size=10mm, yshift=-0.2cm},
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm}
]

    % LL Before State
    \begin{scope}
        \node at (0, 1) {\textbf{Before (LL Imbalance)}};
        \node[treenode, label=right:{\color{red}\small BF=+2}] (x) at (0, 0) {$x$}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{orange}\small BF=+1}] (y) {$y$}
                child {node[treenode] (z) {$z$}
                    child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
                    child {node[subtree] {$T_2$}}
                }
                child {node[subtree] {$T_3$}}
            }
            child {node[subtree] {$T_4$}};
    \end{scope}

    % Rotation Arrow
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (2.5, -2) -- node[above] {Right-Rotate(x)} (4.5, -2);

    % LL After State
    \begin{scope}[xshift=7cm]
        \node at (0, 1) {\textbf{After Right Rotation}};
        \node[treenode, label=right:{\color{green!60!black}\small BF=0}] (y2) at (0, 0) {$y$}
            child {node[treenode] (z2) {$z$}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_2$}}
            }
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{green!60!black}\small BF=0}] (x2) {$x$}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_3$}}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_4$}}
            };
    \end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.8cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.8cm}

\textbf{Case 2: Left-Right (LR) Imbalance}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    >=stealth,
    node distance=1.5cm,
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=cB, fill=cB!10, thick, minimum size=7mm},
    subtree/.style={regular polygon, regular polygon sides=3, draw=gray, fill=gray!10, thick, minimum size=10mm, yshift=-0.2cm},
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm}
]

    % LR Initial State
    \begin{scope}
        \node at (0, 1) {\textbf{Initial (LR Imbalance)}};
        \node[treenode, label=right:{\color{red}\small BF=+2}] (x) at (0, 0) {$x$}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{orange}\small BF=-1}] (y) {$y$}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
                child {node[treenode] (z) {$z$}
                    child {node[subtree] {$T_2$}}
                    child {node[subtree] {$T_3$}}
                }
            }
            child {node[subtree] {$T_4$}};
    \end{scope}

    % Rotation Arrow 1
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (2.5, -2) -- node[above] {Left-Rotate(y)} (4.5, -2);

    % LR Intermediate State
    \begin{scope}[xshift=7cm]
        \node at (0, 1) {\textbf{Intermediate (LL State)}};
        \node[treenode, label=right:{\color{red}\small BF=+2}] (x2) at (0, 0) {$x$}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\small BF=+1}] (z2) {$z$}
                child {node[treenode, label=left:{\small BF=0}] (y2) {$y$}
                    child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
                    child {node[subtree] {$T_2$}}
                }
                child {node[subtree] {$T_3$}}
            }
            child {node[subtree] {$T_4$}};
    \end{scope}

    % Rotation Arrow 2
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (9.5, -2) -- node[above] {Right-Rotate(x)} (11.5, -2);

    % LR Final State
    \begin{scope}[xshift=14cm]
        \node at (0, 1) {\textbf{Final (Balanced)}};
        \node[treenode, label=right:{\color{green!60!black}\small BF=0}] (z3) at (0, 0) {$z$}
            child {node[treenode] (y3) {$y$}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_2$}}
            }
            child {node[treenode] (x3) {$x$}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_3$}}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_4$}}
            };
    \end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center} 

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2022-23
\item Insert a node with key $= 20$ to the given AVL tree. Then perform another insertion of a node with key $= 68$ on the resulting AVL tree. Show all the steps and the resulting AVL trees.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
        every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large, inner sep=0pt},
        edge/.style={thick, black}
    ]

        % ==========================================
        % TREE STRUCTURE (Coordinates Layout)
        % ==========================================
        % Level 0: Root
        \node (n60) at (0,0) {60};

        % Level 1
        \node (n40) at (-1.5,-1.3) {40};
        \node (n70) at (1.5,-1.3) {70};

        % Level 2
        \node (n30) at (-2.5,-2.6) {30};
        \node (n65) at (0.5,-2.6) {65};

        % ==========================================
        % EDGES / CONNECTIONS
        % ==========================================
        \draw[edge] (n60) -- (n40);
        \draw[edge] (n60) -- (n70);

        \draw[edge] (n40) -- (n30);
        \draw[edge] (n70) -- (n65);

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Objective:} Perform two sequential insertions into the given AVL tree and rebalance the tree whenever the AVL property is violated. 

\textbf{Balance Factor Convention:} We define the Balance Factor (BF) of a node $v$ as:
$$ \text{BF}(v) = \text{Height}(v.\text{left}) - \text{Height}(v.\text{right}) $$
An AVL tree requires that $\text{BF}(v) \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$ for all nodes. If an insertion causes a node to have a $\text{BF}$ of $+2$ or $-2$, rotations are required to restore balance.

\vspace{0.5cm}
\subsubsection*{Step 1: Insertion of Node 20}

\textbf{1. Standard BST Insertion:} 
We traverse the tree from the root (60) to find the correct insertion point for 20:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $20 < 60 \implies$ go left to 40.
    \item $20 < 40 \implies$ go left to 30.
    \item $20 < 30 \implies$ insert 20 as the left child of 30.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{2. Checking Balance Factors:}
We recalculate the Balance Factors bottom-up along the insertion path:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $\text{BF}(30) = 1 - 0 = +1$
    \item $\text{BF}(40) = 2 - 0 = +2$ \quad (\textbf{Imbalance Detected!})
\end{itemize}
The lowest unbalanced node is $40$. Since the new node (20) was inserted into the \textit{left} subtree of the \textit{left} child of 40, this is a \textbf{Left-Left (LL) case}.

\textbf{3. Rebalancing (Single Right Rotation):}
To fix the LL imbalance at 40, we perform a single \textbf{Right Rotation} around node 40. Node 30 becomes the new parent, its left child remains 20, and node 40 becomes its right child.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.5cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large, inner sep=0pt},
    imbalanced/.style={circle, draw=red, thick, fill=red!10, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large, inner sep=0pt},
    newnode/.style={circle, draw=green!60!black, thick, fill=green!10, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large, inner sep=0pt},
    edge/.style={thick, black}
]

    % Tree BEFORE Rotation
    \begin{scope}[xshift=-3.5cm]
        \node[text width=4cm, align=center, above=0.5cm] {\textbf{Tree After Inserting 20}\\ \textit{(Imbalance at 40)}};
        \node[treenode] {60}
            child {node[imbalanced, label=right:{\color{red}\small BF=+2}] {40}
                child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\small BF=+1}] {30}
                    child {node[newnode] {20}}
                    child[missing] {}
                }
                child[missing] {}
            }
            child {node[treenode] {70}
                child {node[treenode] {65}}
                child[missing] {}
            };
    \end{scope}

    \draw[->, thick, dashed, cB] (0, -2) -- node[above] {\small Right Rotate(40)} (2, -2);

    % Tree AFTER Rotation
    \begin{scope}[xshift=5.5cm]
        \node[text width=4cm, align=center, above=0.5cm] {\textbf{Resulting Tree}\\ \textit{(Balanced)}};
        \node[treenode] {60}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\small BF=0}] {30}
                child {node[treenode] {20}}
                child {node[treenode] {40}}
            }
            child {node[treenode] {70}
                child {node[treenode] {65}}
                child[missing] {}
            };
    \end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5cm}

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Insertion of Node 68}

\textbf{1. Standard BST Insertion:} 
Using the resulting balanced tree from Step 1, we traverse to insert 68:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $68 > 60 \implies$ go right to 70.
    \item $68 < 70 \implies$ go left to 65.
    \item $68 > 65 \implies$ insert 68 as the right child of 65.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{2. Checking Balance Factors:}
We recalculate the BFs bottom-up:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $\text{BF}(65) = 0 - 1 = -1$
    \item $\text{BF}(70) = 2 - 0 = +2$ \quad (\textbf{Imbalance Detected!})
\end{itemize}
The lowest unbalanced node is $70$. The insertion occurred in the \textit{right} subtree of the \textit{left} child of 70. This represents a \textbf{Left-Right (LR) case}.

\textbf{3. Rebalancing (Double Left-Right Rotation):}
To resolve the LR imbalance, a double rotation is required:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Left Rotation on 65:} Node 68 moves up, and 65 becomes its left child. This transforms the problem into a Left-Left structure.
    \item \textbf{Right Rotation on 70:} Node 68 moves up to become the parent, taking 70 as its right child. Node 65 remains the left child of 68.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.5cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large, inner sep=0pt},
    imbalanced/.style={circle, draw=red, thick, fill=red!10, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large, inner sep=0pt},
    newnode/.style={circle, draw=green!60!black, thick, fill=green!10, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large, inner sep=0pt},
    edge/.style={thick, black}
]

    % Tree BEFORE Rotation
    \begin{scope}[xshift=-3.5cm]
        \node[text width=4cm, align=center, above=0.5cm] {\textbf{Tree After Inserting 68}\\ \textit{(Imbalance at 70)}};
        \node[treenode] {60}
            child {node[treenode] {30}
                child {node[treenode] {20}}
                child {node[treenode] {40}}
            }
            child {node[imbalanced, label=right:{\color{red}\small BF=+2}] {70}
                child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\small BF=-1}] {65}
                    child[missing] {}
                    child {node[newnode] {68}}
                }
                child[missing] {}
            };
    \end{scope}

    \draw[->, thick, dashed, cB] (0, -2) -- node[above] {\small LR Double Rotate(70)} (2, -2);

    % Tree AFTER Rotation
    \begin{scope}[xshift=5.5cm]
        \node[text width=4cm, align=center, above=0.5cm] {\textbf{Final Resulting Tree}\\ \textit{(Balanced)}};
        \node[treenode] {60}
            child {node[treenode] {30}
                child {node[treenode] {20}}
                child {node[treenode] {40}}
            }
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\small BF=0}] {68}
                child {node[treenode] {65}}
                child {node[treenode] {70}}
            };
    \end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\subsubsection*{Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ for each insertion. Finding the insertion point requires traversing the height of the tree ($\mathcal{O}(\log n)$). Updating balance factors and performing rotations takes strictly constant $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time. 
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(1)$ auxiliary space if done iteratively, or $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ space on the call stack if implemented recursively.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2021-22
\item Prove or disprove: 
\begin{enumerate}
\item In an AVL tree, the difference in length between the shortest and the longest paths from the root to the leaves is at most one.
\item The minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree with height 5 is 20.
\end{enumerate}
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Statement:} \textit{In an AVL tree, the difference in length between the shortest and the longest paths from the root to the leaves is at most one.}\\
    \textbf{Verdict: \textcolor{red}{False} (Disproved)}
    
    \textbf{Proof by Counterexample:}
    The AVL property dictates a strictly \textit{local} condition: for any given node, the height difference between its left and right subtrees must be at most 1 ($\text{BF} \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$). However, this local property does not restrict the \textit{global} difference between the shortest and longest paths from the root to a leaf. The imbalances can accumulate along a path.
    
    Consider a minimal AVL tree of height $h = 4$. To construct such a tree, the root must have one subtree of height 3 and another of height 2. Let us trace the paths to the leaves in the concrete example drawn below:
    
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        level distance=1.3cm,
        level 1/.style={sibling distance=5cm},
        level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm},
        level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
        level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm},
        treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\normalsize, inner sep=0pt},
        leafnode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, fill=green!15, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\normalsize, inner sep=0pt},
        edge/.style={thick, black}
    ]

        \node[treenode] {50}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\small BF=+1}] {30}
                child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\small BF=-1}] {20}
                    child {node[leafnode, label=below:{\small depth 3}] {10}}
                    child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\small BF=+1}] {25}
                        child {node[leafnode, label=below:{\small depth 4}] {22}}
                        child[missing] {}
                    }
                }
                child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\small BF=+1}] {40}
                    child {node[leafnode, label=below:{\small depth 3}] {35}}
                    child[missing] {}
                }
            }
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\small BF=-1}] {80}
                child {node[leafnode, label=below:{\small depth 2}] {70}}
                child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\small BF=+1}] {90}
                    child {node[leafnode, label=below:{\small depth 3}] {85}}
                    child[missing] {}
                }
            };
    \end{tikzpicture}
    \end{center}
    
    In the valid AVL tree above (all nodes have $|\text{BF}| \le 1$):
    \begin{itemize}
        \item The \textbf{shortest path} to a leaf is $50 \to 80 \to 70$. Its length (depth) is \textbf{2}.
        \item The \textbf{longest path} to a leaf is $50 \to 30 \to 20 \to 25 \to 22$. Its length (depth) is \textbf{4}.
    \end{itemize}
    The difference in length is $4 - 2 = \mathbf{2}$, which is strictly greater than 1. This formally disproves the statement.

    \vspace{0.6cm}
    \hrule
    \vspace{0.6cm}

    \item \textbf{Statement:} \textit{The minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree with height 5 is 20.}\\
    \textbf{Verdict: \textcolor{green!60!black}{True} (Proved)}
    
    \textbf{Proof:}
    Let $N(h)$ be a function representing the minimum number of nodes required to construct an AVL tree of height $h$. (Note: We define height as the maximum number of edges from the root to a leaf, meaning a single node has height $0$).
    
    To minimize the total number of nodes, we must ensure the tree is as unbalanced as mathematically permissible under the AVL property. For the root node to have height $h$, one of its subtrees must have height $h-1$. To minimize nodes, the other subtree must take the minimal allowable height, which is $h-2$. Both of these subtrees must themselves be minimal AVL trees. 
    
    This structural requirement yields the linear recurrence relation:
    \begin{equation}
        N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1
    \end{equation}
    
    We establish the base cases:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $N(0) = 1$ (A single root node)
        \item $N(1) = 2$ (A root with exactly one child)
    \end{itemize}
    
    Using the recurrence relation, we calculate $N(h)$ iteratively up to $h = 5$:
    \begin{align*}
        N(2) &= N(1) + N(0) + 1 = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 \\
        N(3) &= N(2) + N(1) + 1 = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7 \\
        N(4) &= N(3) + N(2) + 1 = 7 + 4 + 1 = 12 \\
        N(5) &= N(4) + N(3) + 1 = 12 + 7 + 1 = \mathbf{20}
    \end{align*}
    
    The step-by-step derivation confirms that the minimal number of nodes for an AVL tree of height 5 is exactly 20. Hence, the statement is proved true.
\end{enumerate}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2020-21
\item What are the rotations that are required to balance an AVL tree?
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

To maintain the height-balance property of an AVL tree ($\text{Balance Factor} \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$), we perform structural adjustments called \textbf{rotations} whenever an insertion or deletion causes a node's balance factor to become $+2$ or $-2$. 

Rotations are strictly local, constant-time $\mathcal{O}(1)$ pointer manipulations that restore balance while preserving the strict Binary Search Tree (BST) ordering property. There are four possible imbalance scenarios, addressed by four specific types of rotations: two \textit{single rotations} and two \textit{double rotations}.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\subsection*{1. Single Right Rotation (LL Rotation)}
\textbf{Trigger:} The \textbf{Left-Left (LL)} case. An imbalance occurs because a node was inserted into the \textit{left subtree} of the \textit{left child} of the critical (unbalanced) node. \\
\textbf{Procedure:} The left child ($B$) becomes the new root of the subtree. The original unbalanced node ($A$) becomes the right child of $B$. The right child of $B$ ($T_2$) is adopted as the left child of $A$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.2cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\large, inner sep=0pt},
    subtree/.style={rectangle, draw=gray, thick, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\small, inner sep=2pt, fill=gray!10}
]
    \begin{scope}
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.3cm] {\textit{Before (LL Case)}\\ \small BF($A$) = +2};
        \node[treenode] (A) {$A$}
            child {node[treenode] (B) {$B$}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_2$}}
            }
            child {node[subtree] {$T_3$}};
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (2, -1) -- node[above] {\small Right Rotate($A$)} (4.5, -1);
    
    \begin{scope}[xshift=6.5cm]
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.3cm] {\textit{After Rotation}\\ \small Balanced};
        \node[treenode] (B2) {$B$}
            child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
            child {node[treenode] (A2) {$A$}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_2$}}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_3$}}
            };
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\subsection*{2. Single Left Rotation (RR Rotation)}
\textbf{Trigger:} The \textbf{Right-Right (RR)} case. An imbalance occurs because a node was inserted into the \textit{right subtree} of the \textit{right child} of the critical node. \\
\textbf{Procedure:} The right child ($B$) becomes the new root of the subtree. The original unbalanced node ($A$) becomes the left child of $B$. The left child of $B$ ($T_2$) is adopted as the right child of $A$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.2cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\large, inner sep=0pt},
    subtree/.style={rectangle, draw=gray, thick, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\small, inner sep=2pt, fill=gray!10}
]
    \begin{scope}
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.3cm] {\textit{Before (RR Case)}\\ \small BF($A$) = -2};
        \node[treenode] (A) {$A$}
            child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
            child {node[treenode] (B) {$B$}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_2$}}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_3$}}
            };
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (2, -1) -- node[above] {\small Left Rotate($A$)} (4.5, -1);
    
    \begin{scope}[xshift=6.5cm]
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.3cm] {\textit{After Rotation}\\ \small Balanced};
        \node[treenode] (B2) {$B$}
            child {node[treenode] (A2) {$A$}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_2$}}
            }
            child {node[subtree] {$T_3$}};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\subsection*{3. Left-Right Double Rotation (LR Rotation)}
\textbf{Trigger:} The \textbf{Left-Right (LR)} case. An imbalance occurs because a node was inserted into the \textit{right subtree} of the \textit{left child} of the critical node.\\
\textbf{Procedure:} A single rotation is insufficient. We apply two sequential rotations:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Left Rotation} on the left child ($B$), turning the subtree into a Left-Left (LL) structure.
    \item \textbf{Right Rotation} on the original unbalanced node ($A$). Node $C$ becomes the final root.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\normalsize, inner sep=0pt},
    subtree/.style={rectangle, draw=gray, thick, minimum size=0.5cm, font=\small, inner sep=2pt, fill=gray!10}
]
    \begin{scope}[xshift=0cm]
        \node[text width=2.5cm, align=center, above=0.1cm] {\small \textit{LR Case}};
        \node[treenode] {$A$}
            child {node[treenode] {$B$}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
                child {node[treenode] {$C$}
                    child {node[subtree] {\tiny $T_2$}}
                    child {node[subtree] {\tiny $T_3$}}
                }
            }
            child {node[subtree] {$T_4$}};
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (1.6, -1) -- node[above] {\small L-Rot($B$)} (3.0, -1);
    
    \begin{scope}[xshift=4.5cm]
        \node[text width=2.5cm, align=center, above=0.1cm] {\small \textit{Intermediate (LL)}};
        \node[treenode] {$A$}
            child {node[treenode] {$C$}
                child {node[treenode] {$B$}
                    child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
                    child {node[subtree] {\tiny $T_2$}}
                }
                child {node[subtree] {\tiny $T_3$}}
            }
            child {node[subtree] {$T_4$}};
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (6.2, -1) -- node[above] {\small R-Rot($A$)} (7.6, -1);
    
    \begin{scope}[xshift=9.5cm]
        \node[text width=2.5cm, align=center, above=0.1cm] {\small \textit{Balanced}};
        \node[treenode] {$C$}
            child {node[treenode] {$B$}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
                child {node[subtree] {\tiny $T_2$}}
            }
            child {node[treenode] {$A$}
                child {node[subtree] {\tiny $T_3$}}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_4$}}
            };
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\subsection*{4. Right-Left Double Rotation (RL Rotation)}
\textbf{Trigger:} The \textbf{Right-Left (RL)} case. An imbalance occurs because a node was inserted into the \textit{left subtree} of the \textit{right child} of the critical node.\\
\textbf{Procedure:} 
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Right Rotation} on the right child ($B$), turning the subtree into a Right-Right (RR) structure.
    \item \textbf{Left Rotation} on the original unbalanced node ($A$). Node $C$ becomes the final root.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\normalsize, inner sep=0pt},
    subtree/.style={rectangle, draw=gray, thick, minimum size=0.5cm, font=\small, inner sep=2pt, fill=gray!10}
]
    \begin{scope}[xshift=0cm]
        \node[text width=2.5cm, align=center, above=0.1cm] {\small \textit{RL Case}};
        \node[treenode] {$A$}
            child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
            child {node[treenode] {$B$}
                child {node[treenode] {$C$}
                    child {node[subtree] {\tiny $T_2$}}
                    child {node[subtree] {\tiny $T_3$}}
                }
                child {node[subtree] {$T_4$}}
            };
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (1.6, -1) -- node[above] {\small R-Rot($B$)} (3.0, -1);
    
    \begin{scope}[xshift=4.5cm]
        \node[text width=2.5cm, align=center, above=0.1cm] {\small \textit{Intermediate (RR)}};
        \node[treenode] {$A$}
            child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
            child {node[treenode] {$C$}
                child {node[subtree] {\tiny $T_2$}}
                child {node[treenode] {$B$}
                    child {node[subtree] {\tiny $T_3$}}
                    child {node[subtree] {$T_4$}}
                }
            };
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (6.2, -1) -- node[above] {\small L-Rot($A$)} (7.6, -1);
    
    \begin{scope}[xshift=9.5cm]
        \node[text width=2.5cm, align=center, above=0.1cm] {\small \textit{Balanced}};
        \node[treenode] {$C$}
            child {node[treenode] {$A$}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_1$}}
                child {node[subtree] {\tiny $T_2$}}
            }
            child {node[treenode] {$B$}
                child {node[subtree] {\tiny $T_3$}}
                child {node[subtree] {$T_4$}}
            };
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item How does a rotation balance an AVL tree?
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

A rotation balances an AVL tree by transferring depth (height) from one sub-tree to another while strictly preserving the Binary Search Tree (BST) property. It achieves this through localized pointer rearrangements that "lift" the heavier side of the tree and "push down" the lighter side.

\subsubsection*{1. The Mechanism of Height Transfer}
When an insertion or deletion causes a node's balance factor to exceed the permitted range $\{-1, 0, 1\}$, a rotation shifts the root of the unbalanced subtree. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Lifting:} The child node on the "heavy" side is promoted to become the new parent. This decreases the depth of all nodes in its subtrees by $1$, effectively reducing the overall height of the heavy side.
    \item \textbf{Pushing Down:} The original parent (which was unbalanced) is demoted to become a child. This increases the depth of its remaining subtrees by $1$, effectively adding height to the previously lighter side.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{2. Preservation of the BST Property}
For a tree to remain a valid BST, the in-order traversal (Left-Root-Right) must continuously yield a monotonically increasing sequence. 
Consider a generic right rotation on an unbalanced node $y$ with left child $x$. The original in-order sequence is $T_1 < x < T_2 < y < T_3$. 
When $x$ becomes the new root and $y$ becomes its right child, the sub-tree $T_2$ (which contains keys strictly between $x$ and $y$) is seamlessly detached from $x$'s right and reattached to $y$'s left. The in-order traversal remains exactly $T_1 < x < T_2 < y < T_3$.

\subsubsection*{3. Visual and Mathematical Demonstration}

Consider a Right Rotation applied to an unbalanced node $y$ (Left-Left case). Let the subtrees $T_1, T_2, T_3$ have heights $h, h, h-1$ respectively.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.5cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.8cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large, inner sep=0pt},
    subtree/.style={regular polygon, regular polygon sides=3, draw=gray, thick, minimum size=1.2cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt, fill=gray!10}
]
    % Before Rotation
    \begin{scope}[xshift=-4cm]
        \node[text width=3.5cm, align=center, above=0.3cm] {\textbf{Before Rotation}\\ \small $y$ is left-heavy};
        \node[treenode, label=right:{\color{red}\small BF=+2}] (y) {$y$}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\small BF=+1}] (x) {$x$}
                child {node[subtree, label=below:{\small height: $h$}] {$T_1$}}
                child {node[subtree, label=below:{\small height: $h$}] {$T_2$}}
            }
            child {node[subtree, label=below:{\small height: $h-1$}] {$T_3$}};
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (-1, -1.5) -- node[above] {\textbf{RightRotate($y$)}} (1, -1.5);
    
    % After Rotation
    \begin{scope}[xshift=4cm]
        \node[text width=3.5cm, align=center, above=0.3cm] {\textbf{After Rotation}\\ \small Balanced};
        \node[treenode, label=left:{\color{green!60!black}\small BF=0}] (x2) {$x$}
            child {node[subtree, label=below:{\small height: $h$}] {$T_1$}}
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{green!60!black}\small BF=0}] (y2) {$y$}
                child {node[subtree, label=below:{\small height: $h$}] {$T_2$}}
                child {node[subtree, label=below:{\small height: $h-1$}] {$T_3$}}
            };
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{Height Analysis Before Rotation:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $\text{Height}(x) = 1 + \max(h, h) = h + 1$
    \item $\text{Height}(y) = 1 + \max(\text{Height}(x), h-1) = 1 + \max(h+1, h-1) = h + 2$
    \item $\text{BF}(y) = \text{Height}(x) - \text{Height}(T_3) = (h + 1) - (h - 1) = +2$ (Unbalanced)
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Height Analysis After Rotation:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $\text{Height}(y) = 1 + \max(h, h-1) = h + 1$
    \item $\text{Height}(x) = 1 + \max(h, \text{Height}(y)) = 1 + \max(h, h+1) = h + 2$
    \item $\text{BF}(y) = h - (h-1) = +1$
    \item $\text{BF}(x) = h - (h+1) = -1$ (Strictly Balanced!)
\end{itemize}
\textit{Note: The exact balance factors depend on the specific initial heights, but they will uniformly drop into the $\{-1, 0, 1\}$ range.}

\subsubsection*{4. Algorithmic Procedure}

The algorithm relies on simple pointer assignments. Below is the standard approach for a single right rotation.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{RightRotate($y$)}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
    \State $x \gets y.\text{left}$ \Comment{Store the left child to become the new root}
    \State $T_2 \gets x.\text{right}$ \Comment{Store the right child of $x$}
    \State
    \State $x.\text{right} \gets y$ \Comment{Perform rotation: $y$ becomes right child of $x$}
    \State $y.\text{left} \gets T_2$ \Comment{Reattach the orphaned subtree $T_2$ to $y$'s left}
    \State
    \State $y.\text{height} \gets 1 + \max(\text{height}(y.\text{left}), \text{height}(y.\text{right}))$ \Comment{Update heights}
    \State $x.\text{height} \gets 1 + \max(\text{height}(x.\text{left}), \text{height}(x.\text{right}))$
    \State
    \State \Return $x$ \Comment{Return the new root of the subtree}
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsubsection*{5. Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(1)$. The rotation involves exactly a constant number of operations: $3$ pointer assignments (assuming standard parent pointers are handled if present) and $2$ integer operations to update height attributes. It is independent of the size $n$ of the subtrees.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(1)$. The algorithm only allocates a few auxiliary reference variables ($x$ and $T_2$) to hold pointers temporarily during the swap. No dynamic memory is allocated.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item What is the maximum height of an AVL tree having 10 nodes?
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Objective:} Determine the maximum possible height of an AVL tree containing exactly $10$ nodes.

\textbf{Convention:} In accordance with standard computer science literature (and earlier derivations), we define the \textit{height} $h$ of a tree as the number of \textbf{edges} on the longest downward path from the root to a leaf. A tree with a single node has a height of $h=0$.

\subsubsection*{1. Mathematical Reasoning}
To maximize the height of an AVL tree for a given number of nodes $N$, we must distribute the nodes as sparsely as possible while strictly maintaining the AVL balance property ($|\text{BF}| \le 1$). This corresponds to constructing a \textbf{worst-case AVL tree} (often called a Fibonacci tree).

Let $N(h)$ be the \textit{minimum} number of nodes required to construct a valid AVL tree of height $h$. For the tree to reach height $h$ with the fewest nodes:
\begin{itemize}
    \item One subtree of the root must have height $h-1$.
    \item To satisfy the AVL property with minimal nodes, the other subtree must have height $h-2$.
\end{itemize}

This gives the linear recurrence relation:
\begin{equation}
    N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1
\end{equation}

We compute the minimum nodes required for the first few heights:
\begin{align*}
    N(0) &= 1 \quad \text{(A single root node)} \\
    N(1) &= 2 \quad \text{(A root and one child)} \\
    N(2) &= N(1) + N(0) + 1 = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 \\
    N(3) &= N(2) + N(1) + 1 = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7 \\
    N(4) &= N(3) + N(2) + 1 = 7 + 4 + 1 = \mathbf{12}
\end{align*}

\subsubsection*{2. Conclusion}
We are given exactly $N = 10$ nodes.
\begin{itemize}
    \item An AVL tree of height 3 requires at least $7$ nodes. Since $10 \ge 7$, a height of 3 is entirely possible.
    \item An AVL tree of height 4 requires at least $12$ nodes. Since $10 < 12$, we do not have enough nodes to reach a height of 4.
\end{itemize}
Therefore, the \textbf{maximum height of an AVL tree with 10 nodes is 3}.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\subsubsection*{3. Visual Demonstration}
Below is an example of a valid AVL tree with exactly 10 nodes achieving the maximum height of 3. Notice that adding just 2 more nodes to specific empty positions would be required to stretch the tree to height 4.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.5cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!5},
    edge/.style={thick, black}
]

    % Tree Structure
    \node[treenode, label=right:{\small \color{blue} BF=0}] {50}
        child {node[treenode, label=left:{\small \color{blue} BF=+1}] {30}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\small \color{blue} BF=+1}] {20}
                child {node[treenode, label=below:{\small \color{blue} BF=0}] {10}}
                child[missing] {}
            }
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\small \color{blue} BF=0}] {40}}
        }
        child {node[treenode, label=right:{\small \color{blue} BF=0}] {80}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\small \color{blue} BF=-1}] {60}
                child[missing] {}
                child {node[treenode, label=below:{\small \color{blue} BF=0}] {70}}
            }
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\small \color{blue} BF=-1}] {90}
                child[missing] {}
                child {node[treenode, label=below:{\small \color{blue} BF=0}] {100}}
            }
        };

    % Height annotations
    \node[font=\small, text=gray] at (3.5, 0) {Level 0 (Depth 0)};
    \node[font=\small, text=gray] at (3.5, -1.5) {Level 1 (Depth 1)};
    \node[font=\small, text=gray] at (3.5, -3.0) {Level 2 (Depth 2)};
    \node[font=\small, text=gray] at (3.5, -4.5) {Level 3 (Depth 3)};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\subsubsection*{Complexity Note}
In general, determining the maximum height bounds for an AVL tree with $N$ nodes can be computed iteratively in $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ time, or in strict $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time using the closed-form Binet approximation: 
$$ h_{\max} \approx 1.44 \log_2(N+2) - 0.328 $$
For $N=10$, $1.44 \log_2(12) - 0.328 \approx 1.44(3.58) - 0.328 \approx 5.16 - 0.328 = 4.83$. The exact integer bounds are found via the exact recurrence sequence, proving $h_{\max} = 3$.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Prove that a tree with $n$ nodes where the heights of the two children of any node differ by at most 1 has $O(\log n)$ height. \mk{12}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Objective:} Prove that any tree satisfying the AVL property (the heights of the two children of any node differ by at most 1) with $n$ nodes has a height bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\subsubsection*{1. Defining the Minimal Tree (Worst-Case Scenario)}
Let $h$ be the height of the tree (defined as the maximum number of edges from the root to a leaf). To prove an upper bound on the height $h$ for a given number of nodes $n$, we equivalently want to find the \textbf{lower bound on the number of nodes} for a given height $h$. 

Let $N(h)$ denote the minimum number of nodes required to construct a valid tree of height $h$ under the given property.
\begin{itemize}
    \item To build a tree of height $h$ with the fewest possible nodes, the root must have one subtree of height $h-1$.
    \item To satisfy the property that child heights differ by at most 1 while minimizing nodes, the other subtree must have the minimum allowable height, which is $h-2$.
    \item Both of these subtrees must themselves be minimal trees for their respective heights.
\end{itemize}

This logical structure yields the following linear recurrence relation for the minimum number of nodes:
\begin{equation}
    N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1
\end{equation}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.5cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!5},
    subtree/.style={regular polygon, regular polygon sides=3, draw=black, thick, minimum size=2cm, font=\normalsize, inner sep=0pt, fill=gray!10}
]
    \node[text width=6cm, align=center, above=0.3cm] {\textbf{Minimal Tree Structure for Height $h$}\\ \small Total Nodes: $N(h)$};
    \node[treenode] {Root}
        child {node[subtree, label=below:{\small Min Nodes: $N(h-1)$}] {$h-1$}}
        child {node[subtree, label=below:{\small Min Nodes: $N(h-2)$}] {$h-2$}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{2. Establishing Base Cases}
By definition of our height $h$ (number of edges):
\begin{itemize}
    \item $N(0) = 1$ \quad (A tree with height 0 is just the root node).
    \item $N(1) = 2$ \quad (A tree with height 1 requires a root and exactly one child).
\end{itemize}
We can also calculate $N(2) = N(1) + N(0) + 1 = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4$. Notice that $N(h)$ is a strictly increasing function, meaning $N(h) > N(h-1)$ for all $h > 0$.

\subsubsection*{3. Bounding the Recurrence Relation}
We want to express $N(h)$ purely in terms of an exponential function of $h$. 
Starting with our recurrence:
\begin{equation*}
    N(h) = 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2)
\end{equation*}
Since $N(h)$ is strictly increasing, we know that $N(h-1) > N(h-2)$. Substituting $N(h-2)$ for $N(h-1)$ gives us a strict inequality:
\begin{align}
    N(h) &> 1 + N(h-2) + N(h-2) \nonumber \\
    N(h) &> 2 \cdot N(h-2)
\end{align}

Now, we can unroll this inequality down to the base cases:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Case 1: $h$ is even.} 
    \begin{align*}
        N(h) &> 2 \cdot N(h-2) \\
             &> 2^2 \cdot N(h-4) \\
             &\; \vdots \\
             &> 2^{\frac{h}{2}} \cdot N(0) \implies N(h) > 2^{\frac{h}{2}} \cdot 1 = 2^{\frac{h}{2}}
    \end{align*}
    
    \item \textbf{Case 2: $h$ is odd.} 
    \begin{align*}
        N(h) &> 2 \cdot N(h-2) \\
             &> 2^2 \cdot N(h-4) \\
             &\; \vdots \\
             &> 2^{\frac{h-1}{2}} \cdot N(1) \implies N(h) > 2^{\frac{h-1}{2}} \cdot 2 = 2^{\frac{h+1}{2}} > 2^{\frac{h}{2}}
    \end{align*}
\end{itemize}

In both cases, we have established a strict exponential lower bound:
\begin{equation}
    N(h) > 2^{\frac{h}{2}}
\end{equation}

\subsubsection*{4. Relating Height to $n$ (Total Nodes)}
Let our actual tree have $n$ nodes and height $h$. By definition, the actual number of nodes $n$ must be greater than or equal to the minimum number of nodes required for that height:
\begin{equation*}
    n \ge N(h)
\end{equation*}
Substitute the bound we derived:
\begin{equation*}
    n > 2^{\frac{h}{2}}
\end{equation*}

Taking the base-2 logarithm of both sides:
\begin{align*}
    \log_2 n &> \log_2\left(2^{\frac{h}{2}}\right) \\
    \log_2 n &> \frac{h}{2} \\
    h &< 2 \log_2 n
\end{align*}

\subsubsection*{Conclusion}
We have proven that $h \le 2 \log_2 n$. Dropping the constant multiplier, we get:
\begin{equation*}
    h = \mathcal{O}(\log n)
\end{equation*}
This formally demonstrates that any tree enforcing a child height difference of at most 1 (the AVL property) guarantees a logarithmic worst-case height. \hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Mention one advantage of AVL trees over red-black trees, and one advantage of red-black trees over AVL trees. \mk{4}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

Both AVL trees and Red-Black trees are self-balancing Binary Search Trees (BSTs) that guarantee $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ time complexity for fundamental operations. However, their distinct internal balancing mechanisms make them optimized for different types of workloads.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\subsubsection*{1. Advantage of AVL Trees over Red-Black Trees}
\textbf{Faster Retrievals (Lookups) due to Stricter Balancing:} \\
AVL trees enforce a highly rigid height-balancing property (the height difference between sibling subtrees is at most 1). As a result, an AVL tree of $n$ nodes has a tightly bounded maximum height of approximately $1.44 \log_2 n$. In contrast, a Red-Black tree's path length can be up to twice as long as the shortest path, yielding a maximum height of $2 \log_2 n$. Because the AVL tree is shallower, \textbf{search operations are consistently faster}. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textit{Best Use Case:} \textbf{Read-heavy} applications where lookups vastly outnumber structural modifications (e.g., read-optimized databases or dictionaries).
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Advantage of Red-Black Trees over AVL Trees}
\textbf{Faster Insertions and Deletions due to Fewer Rotations:} \\
Red-Black trees use a more relaxed balancing criterion based on color properties. Because they are less strictly balanced, they tolerate more localized imbalances without triggering structural changes. When restructuring is required, an AVL tree might perform $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ rotations cascading all the way to the root (particularly during deletions). A Red-Black tree, however, completes its balancing using constant-time recoloring and guarantees a maximum of \textbf{3 rotations} per insertion or deletion.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textit{Best Use Case:} \textbf{Write-heavy} applications where elements are constantly added or removed (e.g., OS process schedulers, C++ \texttt{std::map}, or Java's \texttt{TreeMap}).
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item If the $i$-th and $(i+2)$-th Fibonacci numbers are $x$ and $y$ respectively (where $i > 0$), what is the minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height $(i-1)$? \mk{4}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Objective:} Find the minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height $(i-1)$, given that the $i$-th Fibonacci number is $x$ and the $(i+2)$-th Fibonacci number is $y$.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\subsubsection*{1. Recurrence Relation for Minimal AVL Trees}
Let $N(h)$ be the minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height $h$. As established, a minimal AVL tree of height $h$ consists of a root, one minimal subtree of height $h-1$, and one minimal subtree of height $h-2$. This gives the recurrence:
\begin{equation}
    N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1
\end{equation}
with the base cases defined as:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $N(0) = 1$ (A single node has height $0$)
    \item $N(1) = 2$ (A root with exactly one child)
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{2. Relationship with the Fibonacci Sequence}
To solve this recurrence, we can add $1$ to both sides to homogenize it:
\begin{equation*}
    N(h) + 1 = (N(h-1) + 1) + (N(h-2) + 1)
\end{equation*}

Let $L(h) = N(h) + 1$. The sequence $L(h)$ perfectly follows the standard Fibonacci recurrence $L(h) = L(h-1) + L(h-2)$.
Let us align this with the standard Fibonacci sequence $F_k$, where $F_1 = 1, F_2 = 1, F_3 = 2, F_4 = 3, F_5 = 5$, and so on.

Let's compute the first few terms to find the exact index mapping:
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Height} $h$ & \textbf{Min Nodes} $N(h)$ & $L(h) = N(h) + 1$ & \textbf{Fibonacci Value} & \textbf{Fibonacci Index} \\ \hline
$0$ & $1$ & $2$ & $2$ & $F_3$ \\ \hline
$1$ & $2$ & $3$ & $3$ & $F_4$ \\ \hline
$2$ & $4$ & $5$ & $5$ & $F_5$ \\ \hline
$3$ & $7$ & $8$ & $8$ & $F_6$ \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

By observation (and formalifiable by induction), we can establish the exact mapping between $L(h)$ and the Fibonacci sequence $F$:
\begin{equation}
    L(h) = F_{h+3}
\end{equation}
Substituting back $L(h) = N(h) + 1$:
\begin{align}
    N(h) + 1 &= F_{h+3} \nonumber \\
    N(h) &= F_{h+3} - 1
\end{align}

\subsubsection*{3. Solving for Height $(i-1)$}
We are asked to find the minimum number of nodes for a tree of height $h = i-1$. 
Substitute $h = i-1$ into our derived formula:
\begin{align*}
    N(i-1) &= F_{(i-1)+3} - 1 \\
    N(i-1) &= F_{i+2} - 1
\end{align*}

The problem explicitly states that the $(i+2)$-th Fibonacci number is $y$ (i.e., $F_{i+2} = y$). The value of the $i$-th Fibonacci number ($x$) is contextual but not strictly required for the final substitution.

Substituting $y$ into the equation:
\begin{equation}
    N(i-1) = y - 1
\end{equation}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\textbf{Final Conclusion:} The minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height $(i-1)$ is mathematically equal to \textbf{$\mathbf{y - 1}$}.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What is an AVL tree? Draw the AVL tree that results after successively inserting the keys $11, 4, 15, 25, 36, 47, 61, 53, 75$ into an initially empty AVL tree. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2015--16}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsection*{1. Definition of an AVL Tree}
An \textbf{AVL Tree} (named after its inventors \textbf{A}delson-\textbf{V}elsky and \textbf{L}andis) is a self-balancing Binary Search Tree (BST). It maintains a strictly bounded logarithmic height by enforcing the \textbf{AVL Balance Property}:

$$\text{For every node } v \text{ in the tree}, |\text{Balance Factor}(v)| \le 1$$

where the balance factor is defined as:
$$\text{Balance Factor}(v) = \text{Height}(v.\text{left}) - \text{Height}(v.\text{right})$$

If at any time after an insertion or deletion the balance factor of a node falls outside the range $\{-1, 0, 1\}$, a rebalancing operation via local structural manipulations called \textbf{rotations} (Single or Double) is executed. This ensures that the worst-case time complexity for search, insertion, and deletion operations is strictly maintained at $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$, where $n$ is the number of nodes.

---

\subsection*{2. Step-by-Step Successive Insertion Sequence}

We insert the keys in the specified sequence: $[11, 4, 15, 25, 36, 47, 61, 53, 75]$.

\subsubsection*{Steps 1 to 3: Inserting 11, 4, and 15}
The first three keys are inserted using standard BST rules without causing any balance violations.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!5}
]
    \node[treenode] {11}
        child {node[treenode] {4}}
        child {node[treenode] {15}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Steps 4 \& 5: Inserting 25, then 36 (Imbalance \& Left Rotation)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Inserting 25 goes to the right of 15.
    \item Inserting 36 goes to the right of 25.
    \item Calculating balance factors reveals an imbalance at node \textbf{15} ($\text{BF} = 0 - 2 = -2$). 
    \item This forms a \textbf{Right-Right (RR) case} at 15, triggering a \textbf{Single Left Rotation} around 15.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.1cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!5},
    imbalanced/.style={circle, draw=red, thick, fill=red!10, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt}
]
    % Before
    \begin{scope}
        \node[text width=4cm, align=center, above=0.2cm] {\textbf{Before Rotation}\\ \small (Inserting 36)};
        \node[treenode] {11}
            child {node[treenode] {4}}
            child {node[imbalanced, label=right:{\color{red}\small -2}] {15}
                child[missing] {}
                child {node[treenode] {25}
                    child[missing] {}
                    child {node[treenode] {36}}
                }
            };
    \end{scope}

    \draw[->, thick, dashed, cB] (2, -1.5) -- node[above] {\small Left Rotate(15)} (4.5, -1.5);

    % After
    \begin{scope}[xshift=6.5cm]
        \node[text width=4cm, align=center, above=0.2cm] {\textbf{After Rotation}\\ \small Balanced Tree};
        \node[treenode] {11}
            child {node[treenode] {4}}
            child {node[treenode] {25}
                child {node[treenode] {15}}
                child {node[treenode] {36}}
            };
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 6: Inserting 47 (Imbalance \& Left Rotation at Root)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Key 47 is placed as the right child of 36.
    \item Recomputing balance factors reveals an imbalance at the root node \textbf{11} ($\text{BF} = 1 - 3 = -2$).
    \item Path to the newly inserted node from 11 is Right $\to$ Right, making it an \textbf{RR case} at 11.
    \item We perform a \textbf{Single Left Rotation} around the root node 11. Node 25 becomes the new root.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.1cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!5},
    imbalanced/.style={circle, draw=red, thick, fill=red!10, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt}
]
    % Before
    \begin{scope}
        \node[text width=4cm, align=center, above=0.2cm] {\textbf{Before Rotation}\\ \small (Inserting 47)};
        \node[imbalanced, label=left:{\color{red}\small -2}] {11}
            child {node[treenode] {4}}
            child {node[treenode] {25}
                child {node[treenode] {15}}
                child {node[treenode] {36}
                    child[missing] {}
                    child {node[treenode] {47}}
                }
            };
    \end{scope}

    \draw[->, thick, dashed, cB] (2, -1.5) -- node[above] {\small Left Rotate(11)} (4.5, -1.5);

    % After
    \begin{scope}[xshift=6.5cm]
        \node[text width=4cm, align=center, above=0.2cm] {\textbf{After Rotation}\\ \small Balanced Tree};
        \node[treenode] {25}
            child {node[treenode] {11}
                child {node[treenode] {4}}
                child {node[treenode] {15}}
            }
            child {node[treenode] {36}
                child[missing] {}
                child {node[treenode] {47}}
            };
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 7: Inserting 61 (Imbalance \& Left Rotation at 36)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Key 61 is inserted as the right child of 47.
    \item Checking balance factors shows node \textbf{36} has violated balance limits ($\text{BF} = 0 - 2 = -2$).
    \item This is an \textbf{RR case} at node 36, which is resolved by executing a \textbf{Single Left Rotation} around 36.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.1cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!5},
    imbalanced/.style={circle, draw=red, thick, fill=red!10, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt}
]
    % Before
    \begin{scope}
        \node[text width=4cm, align=center, above=0.2cm] {\textbf{Before Rotation}\\ \small (Inserting 61)};
        \node[treenode] {25}
            child {node[treenode] {11}
                child {node[treenode] {4}}
                child {node[treenode] {15}}
            }
            child {node[imbalanced, label=right:{\color{red}\small -2}] {36}
                child[missing] {}
                child {node[treenode] {47}
                    child[missing] {}
                    child {node[treenode] {61}}
                }
            };
    \end{scope}

    \draw[->, thick, dashed, cB] (2.2, -1.5) -- node[above] {\small Left Rotate(36)} (4.3, -1.5);

    % After
    \begin{scope}[xshift=6.5cm]
        \node[text width=4cm, align=center, above=0.2cm] {\textbf{After Rotation}\\ \small Balanced Tree};
        \node[treenode] {25}
            child {node[treenode] {11}
                child {node[treenode] {4}}
                child {node[treenode] {15}}
            }
            child {node[treenode] {47}
                child {node[treenode] {36}}
                child {node[treenode] {61}}
            };
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Steps 8 \& 9: Inserting 53, then 75}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Inserting 53:} Compares with 25 (go right), 47 (go right), 61 (go left) $\implies$ placed as left child of 61. All node balance factors remain well within parameters.
    \item \textbf{Inserting 75:} Compares with 25 (go right), 47 (go right), 61 (go right) $\implies$ placed as right child of 61. Balance factors along the path remain perfectly within range ($\text{BF}(61) = 0$, $\text{BF}(47) = -1$, $\text{BF}(25) = -1$). No further rotations are required.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\subsection*{3. Final Resulting AVL Tree}
Below is the final compiled AVL tree containing all 9 keys perfectly balanced. All localized balance factors are indicated in blue next to the respective node targets.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.5cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.4cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\large, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!8},
    edge/.style={thick, black}
]

    \node[treenode, label=above:{\color{blue}\small BF=-1}] {25}
        child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\small BF=0}] {11}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\small BF=0}] {4}}
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\small BF=0}] {15}}
        }
        child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\small BF=-1}] {47}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\small BF=0}] {36}}
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\small BF=0}] {61}
                child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\small BF=0}] {53}}
                child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\small BF=0}] {75}}
            }
        };

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\subsection*{Complexity Analysis of Final Tree}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Total Nodes ($n$):} 9
    \item \textbf{Height attained ($h$):} 3 (via paths $25 \to 47 \to 61 \to 53$ or $75$)
    \item \textbf{Optimal Bound Verification:} Since $h = 3$ and $\lfloor \log_2(9) \rfloor = 3$, the tree achieves perfect performance profile limits, keeping search lookups bounded by a maximum of 4 comparisons.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Explain, with examples, the single rotation and double rotation operations on AVL trees. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

In an AVL tree, the \textbf{Balance Factor (BF)} of a node is the difference between the height of its left subtree and the height of its right subtree ($\text{BF} = H_{\text{left}} - H_{\text{right}}$). For an AVL tree to remain valid, the balance factor of every node must be \textbf{-1, 0, or 1}. 

When an insertion or deletion causes a node's balance factor to become strictly greater than 1 or less than -1, the tree becomes unbalanced. To restore the AVL property, we perform localized restructuring operations known as \textbf{rotations}. 

There are two primary categories of rotations: \textbf{Single Rotations} and \textbf{Double Rotations}.

\vspace{0.4cm}
\subsection*{1. Single Rotations}
Single rotations are used when the imbalance occurs in a "straight line" (either entirely on the left or entirely on the right). It involves a single, continuous shift of nodes.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\subsubsection*{A. Right Rotation (LL Case)}
\textbf{Trigger:} An imbalance occurs because a node was inserted into the \textbf{Left subtree of the Left child} of the unbalanced node. The unbalanced node has a BF of +2, and its left child has a BF of +1.\\
\textbf{Example:} Insert \textbf{3}, then \textbf{2}, then \textbf{1}. Node 3 becomes unbalanced. We right-rotate around 3.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\normalsize, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!5}
]
    \begin{scope}
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.2cm] {\small \textit{Before (LL Case)}\\ BF(3)=+2};
        \node[treenode, label=right:{\color{red}\tiny +2}] {3}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\tiny +1}] {2}
                child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\tiny 0}] {1}}
                child[missing] {}
            }
            child[missing] {};
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (1.5, -1) -- node[above] {\small Right Rotate(3)} (4, -1);
