    \begin{scope}[xshift=5.5cm]
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.2cm] {\small \textit{After Rotation}\\ Balanced};
        \node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\tiny 0}] {2}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\tiny 0}] {1}}
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\tiny 0}] {3}};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\subsubsection*{B. Left Rotation (RR Case)}
\textbf{Trigger:} An imbalance occurs because a node was inserted into the \textbf{Right subtree of the Right child} of the unbalanced node. The unbalanced node has a BF of -2, and its right child has a BF of -1.\\
\textbf{Example:} Insert \textbf{1}, then \textbf{2}, then \textbf{3}. Node 1 becomes unbalanced. We left-rotate around 1.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\normalsize, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!5}
]
    \begin{scope}
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.2cm] {\small \textit{Before (RR Case)}\\ BF(1)=-2};
        \node[treenode, label=left:{\color{red}\tiny -2}] {1}
            child[missing] {}
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\tiny -1}] {2}
                child[missing] {}
                child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\tiny 0}] {3}}
            };
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (1.5, -1) -- node[above] {\small Left Rotate(1)} (4, -1);
    
    \begin{scope}[xshift=5.5cm]
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.2cm] {\small \textit{After Rotation}\\ Balanced};
        \node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\tiny 0}] {2}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\tiny 0}] {1}}
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\tiny 0}] {3}};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\subsection*{2. Double Rotations}
Double rotations are required when the imbalance occurs in a "zigzag" pattern. A single rotation would merely shift the imbalance to the other side. Instead, we perform two sequential single rotations.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\subsubsection*{A. Left-Right Rotation (LR Case)}
\textbf{Trigger:} The \textbf{Right subtree of the Left child} causes the imbalance. The unbalanced node has a BF of +2, and its left child has a BF of -1.\\
\textbf{Operation:} 1) Left-rotate the left child. 2) Right-rotate the original node.\\
\textbf{Example:} Insert \textbf{3}, then \textbf{1}, then \textbf{2}. 

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=0.9cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.5cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!5}
]
    \begin{scope}[xshift=0cm]
        \node[text width=2cm, align=center, above=0.1cm] {\small \textit{LR Case}\\ BF(3)=+2};
        \node[treenode] {3}
            child {node[treenode] {1}
                child[missing] {}
                child {node[treenode] {2}}
            }
            child[missing] {};
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (1.0, -1) -- node[above] {\scriptsize L-Rot(1)} (2.3, -1);
    
    \begin{scope}[xshift=3.5cm]
        \node[text width=2.5cm, align=center, above=0.1cm] {\small \textit{Intermediate}\\ (LL Case)};
        \node[treenode] {3}
            child {node[treenode] {2}
                child {node[treenode] {1}}
                child[missing] {}
            }
            child[missing] {};
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (4.5, -1) -- node[above] {\scriptsize R-Rot(3)} (5.8, -1);
    
    \begin{scope}[xshift=7cm]
        \node[text width=2cm, align=center, above=0.1cm] {\small \textit{Balanced}};
        \node[treenode] {2}
            child {node[treenode] {1}}
            child {node[treenode] {3}};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\subsubsection*{B. Right-Left Rotation (RL Case)}
\textbf{Trigger:} The \textbf{Left subtree of the Right child} causes the imbalance. The unbalanced node has a BF of -2, and its right child has a BF of +1.\\
\textbf{Operation:} 1) Right-rotate the right child. 2) Left-rotate the original node.\\
\textbf{Example:} Insert \textbf{1}, then \textbf{3}, then \textbf{2}. 

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=0.9cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.5cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!5}
]
    \begin{scope}[xshift=0cm]
        \node[text width=2cm, align=center, above=0.1cm] {\small \textit{RL Case}\\ BF(1)=-2};
        \node[treenode] {1}
            child[missing] {}
            child {node[treenode] {3}
                child {node[treenode] {2}}
                child[missing] {}
            };
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (1.3, -1) -- node[above] {\scriptsize R-Rot(3)} (2.6, -1);
    
    \begin{scope}[xshift=3.5cm]
        \node[text width=2.5cm, align=center, above=0.1cm] {\small \textit{Intermediate}\\ (RR Case)};
        \node[treenode] {1}
            child[missing] {}
            child {node[treenode] {2}
                child[missing] {}
                child {node[treenode] {3}}
            };
    \end{scope}
    
    \draw[->, thick, cB, dashed] (5.1, -1) -- node[above] {\scriptsize L-Rot(1)} (6.4, -1);
    
    \begin{scope}[xshift=7.5cm]
        \node[text width=2cm, align=center, above=0.1cm] {\small \textit{Balanced}};
        \node[treenode] {2}
            child {node[treenode] {1}}
            child {node[treenode] {3}};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What is a Multiway Search tree? How do we search a key in a Multiway Search tree? \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsection*{1. Definition of a Multiway Search Tree}
A \textbf{Multiway Search Tree} (or $m$-way search tree) is a sequential generalization of a Binary Search Tree (BST) where a single node can contain multiple keys and have more than two children. Formally, an $m$-way search tree is a tree where each node can have a maximum of $m$ children (where $m$ is the order of the tree).

A valid $m$-way search tree satisfies the following structural and ordering properties:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Node Capacity:} Each node contains $k$ keys and $k+1$ pointers to child subtrees, where $0 \le k \le m-1$.
    \item \textbf{Sorted Keys:} The keys within a single node are stored in a strictly ascending order: $K_1 < K_2 < K_3 < \dots < K_k$.
    \item \textbf{Subtree Partitioning:} The keys in the node partition the keys of its subtrees into distinct ranges managed by child pointers $P_0, P_1, \dots, P_k$:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item The subtree pointed to by $P_0$ contains only keys strictly \textbf{less than} $K_1$.
        \item For any $1 \le i < k$, the subtree pointed to by $P_i$ contains only keys strictly \textbf{between} $K_i$ and $K_{i+1}$ ($K_i < \text{keys} < K_{i+1}$).
        \item The subtree pointed to by $P_k$ contains only keys strictly \textbf{greater than} $K_k$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Recursive Property:} All subtrees are themselves valid multiway search trees.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.5cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3.5cm},
    treenode/.style={rectangle, draw=black, thick, rounded corners=2pt, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\normalsize, inner sep=4pt, fill=cB!5}
]
    % Sample 3-Way Search Tree (m=3)
    \node[treenode] {20 \,|\, 50}
        child {node[treenode] {10 \,|\, 15}}
        child {node[treenode] {35}}
        child {node[treenode] {60 \,|\, 80}};

    \node[font=\small, text=gray, anchor=west] at (3, 0) {Root Node: Keys partition values into $<20$, $20-50$, and $>50$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

---

\subsection*{2. How to Search for a Key in a Multiway Search Tree}
Searching for a target key $X$ in an $m$-way search tree is a recursive traversal that mirrors the structure of a binary tree search but involves a multi-directional branch choice at each node. 

\subsubsection*{Algorithmic Steps:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Start at the Root:} Set the current node to the root of the tree.
    \item \textbf{Base Case (Unsuccessful):} If the current node pointer is \texttt{NULL} (empty), the search terminates, indicating that the target key $X$ does not exist in the tree.
    \item \textbf{Internal Node Search:} Scan the ordered keys $K_1, K_2, \dots, K_k$ inside the current node (using either a linear scan or binary search since keys are sorted):
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Match Found:} If $X = K_i$ for any key index $i$, the search is successful. Return the matching node/data reference.
        \item \textbf{Left-Bounded Branch:} If $X < K_1$, follow the leftmost child pointer $P_0$ (set current node to $P_0$) and repeat from Step 2.
        \item \textbf{Intermediate Branch:} If $K_i < X < K_{i+1}$ for some index $i$, follow the matching intermediate child pointer $P_i$ and repeat from Step 2.
        \item \textbf{Right-Bounded Branch:} If $X > K_k$, follow the rightmost child pointer $P_k$ and repeat from Step 2.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Pseudo-code Representation:}
\begin{algorithm}[H]
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Function{SearchMWayTree}{$\text{node}, X$}
    \If{$\text{node} = \text{NULL}$}
        \State \Return $\text{NOT\_FOUND}$
    \EndIf
    \State $k \gets \text{node}.\text{key\_count}$
    \State $i \gets 1$
    \While{$i \le k$ \textbf{and} $X > \text{node}.K_i$}
        \State $i \gets i + 1$
    \EndWhile
    \If{$i \le k$ \textbf{and} $X = \text{node}.K_i$}
        \State \Return $\text{node}$ \Comment{Key found successfully}
    \Else
        \State \Return \Call{SearchMWayTree}{$\text{node}.P_{i-1}, X$} \Comment{Recurse into the appropriate subtree}
    \EndIf
\EndFunction
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsubsection*{Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} In a balanced $m$-way search tree (such as a B-Tree), the height is bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\log_m n)$, yielding an overall lookup efficiency of $\mathcal{O}(m \log_m n)$ if linear search is used internally within nodes.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(h)$ worst-case auxiliary space for the call stack during deep recursion, where $h$ is the height of the tree.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write the properties of a B-tree. \mk{5}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2013--14}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

A \textbf{B-tree} of order $m$ (where $m$ represents the maximum number of children a node can have) is a self-balancing, multiway search tree specialized for systems with large blocks of disk storage, such as databases and filesystems. It generalizes the Binary Search Tree and maintains uniform performance by adhering to strict structural and operational invariants.

The fundamental properties of a B-tree of order $m$ are categorized below:



\subsubsection*{1. Structural Properties}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Maximum Children:} Every node in the tree can have at most $m$ children.
    \item \textbf{Minimum Children (Internal Nodes):} Every internal (non-leaf, non-root) node must have at least $\lceil m/2 \rceil$ children. This prevents the tree from becoming sparse or leaning heavily to one side.
    \item \textbf{Root Node Constraint:} The root node must have at least $2$ children if it is not a leaf node. If the root is a leaf node (i.e., the tree contains no other nodes), it can have zero children and contain as few as $1$ key.
    \item \textbf{Perfect Balance:} All leaf nodes must exist at exactly the same level (depth). B-trees grow and shrink from the root upwards, ensuring that the distance from the root to any leaf is identical.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{2. Key Distribution and Ordering Properties}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Key-to-Child Relationship:} An internal node that has $k$ children always contains exactly $k-1$ keys.
    \item \textbf{Key Capacity Bounds:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item The maximum number of keys any node can hold is $m-1$.
        \item The minimum number of keys any non-root node must hold is $\lceil m/2 \rceil - 1$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Internal Node Sorting:} The keys within a single node are always stored in a strictly sorted, ascending order: $K_1 < K_2 < \dots < K_{k-1}$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{3. Subtree Partitioning (Search Property)}
The keys within an internal node act as separation boundaries for its child subtrees. For a node with keys $K_1, K_2, \dots, K_{k-1}$ and child pointers $P_0, P_1, \dots, P_{k-1}$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item The subtree pointed to by $P_0$ contains only keys strictly less than $K_1$ ($\text{keys} < K_1$).
    \item For any index $i$ (where $1 \le i < k-1$), the subtree pointed to by $P_i$ contains only keys strictly between $K_i$ and $K_{i+1}$ ($K_i < \text{keys} < K_{i+1}$).
    \item The subtree pointed to by $P_{k-1}$ contains only keys strictly greater than $K_{k-1}$ ($\text{keys} > K_{k-1}$).
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Let $T$ be an AVL tree where $T_1$ has height $h-1$. Determine the heights of $T_2$, $T_3$, and $T_4$. Discuss what happens when you delete an element from $T_1$; cover all possible cases for deletion in an AVL tree. \mk{4+4+7=15}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2012--13}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
        % Define style for the circular tree nodes
        tree node/.style={
            circle, 
            draw, 
            minimum size=7mm, % Size increased slightly to fit both symbols smoothly
            inner sep=0pt
        },
        % Define style for the triangular subtrees
        subtree/.style={
            isosceles triangle, 
            draw, 
            shape border rotate=90, 
            minimum width=8mm, 
            minimum height=10mm, 
            inner sep=0pt, 
            anchor=apex
        },
        % General settings for tree branching
        level distance=1.5cm,
        sibling distance=3.5cm
    ]

    % =========================================================================
    % MAIN TREE (Left Side)
    % ======================================================================

    % Root node
    \node(root) [tree node] at (0,0) {$\backslash$};
    
    % Level 1
    \node(t1) [subtree] at ([xshift=-2cm, yshift=-1.5cm]root.center) {$T_1$};
    \node(n1) [tree node] at ([xshift=2cm, yshift=-1.5cm]root.center) {$/$};
    
    % Level 2 (under n1)
    \node(n2) [tree node] at ([xshift=-1cm, yshift=-1.5cm]n1.center) {$\backslash$};
    \node(t4) [subtree] at ([xshift=1cm, yshift=-1.5cm]n1.center) {$T_4$};
    
    % Level 3 (under n2)
    \node(t2) [subtree] at ([xshift=-0.7cm, yshift=-1.5cm]n2.center) {$T_2$};
    \node(t3) [subtree] at ([xshift=0.7cm, yshift=-1.5cm]n2.center) {$T_3$};

    % Connect nodes to subtrees / other nodes
    \draw (root) -- (t1.apex);
    \draw (root) -- (n1);
    \draw (n1) -- (n2);
    \draw (n1) -- (t4.apex);
    \draw (n2) -- (t2.apex);
    \draw (n2) -- (t3.apex);

    % Height indicator label (h-1) next to T1
    \node[left=0.2cm of t1, yshift=0.3cm] {$(h-1)$};
    \draw[->] ([xshift=-0.6cm, yshift=0.3cm]t1.apex) -- ([xshift=-0.1cm, yshift=-0.2cm]t1.apex);

    % =========================================================================
    % SYMBOL MEANING KEY (Right Side)
    % =========================================================================
    
    \begin{scope}[xshift=5.5cm, yshift=0.5cm]
        % Title
        \node[anchor=west] at (-1, 0) {\large\textit{symbol meaning:}};
        
        % First Rule: Contains both / and \ safely inside math mode
        \node(k1) [tree node] at (0, -1) {$/$};
        \node[anchor=west] at (-1.5, -1.8) {left subtree $>$ right subtree};
        \draw (k1) -- (-1.2, -1.5);
        \draw (k1) -- (1.5, -1.5);
        
        % Second Rule
        \node(k2) [tree node] at (0, -3.2) {$\backslash$};
        \node[anchor=west] at (-1.5, -4) {left subtree $<$ right subtree};
        \draw (k2) -- (-1, -3.7);
        \draw (k2) -- (1, -3.7);
    \end{scope}

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsection*{1. Determining the Heights of $T_2$, $T_3$, and $T_4$}

Let $H(X)$ denote the height of a subtree or node $X$. Based on the AVL structural balance rules and the meaning of the symbols given in the problem statement, we can trace the heights from the left side of the tree to the right side step-by-step.

\textbf{Given:} 
\begin{equation*}
    H(T_1) = h - 1
\end{equation*}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Analyze the Root Node:} 
    The root node contains the symbol $\backslash$, meaning its left subtree ($T_1$) is strictly shorter than its right subtree (the node $n_1$). Because it is a valid AVL tree, the height difference can be at most $1$. Thus:
    \begin{equation*}
        H(n_1) = H(T_1) + 1 = (h - 1) + 1 = h
    \end{equation*}

    \item \textbf{Analyze Node $n_1$:}
    Node $n_1$ contains the symbol $/$, which means its left child ($n_2$) is strictly taller than its right child ($T_4$). The height of a node is defined as $1 + \max(H(\text{left}), H(\text{right}))$. Therefore:
    \begin{align*}
        H(n_1) &= 1 + H(n_2) \\
        h &= 1 + H(n_2) \implies H(n_2) = h - 1
    \end{align*}
    Since node $n_1$ is left-heavy ($/$), its right subtree $T_4$ must be exactly $1$ unit shorter than $n_2$:
    \begin{equation*}
        H(T_4) = H(n_2) - 1 = (h - 1) - 1 = h - 2
    \end{equation*}

    \item \textbf{Analyze Node $n_2$:}
    Node $n_2$ contains the symbol $\backslash$, meaning its left child ($T_2$) is strictly shorter than its right child ($T_3$). Following the same logic:
    \begin{align*}
        H(n_2) &= 1 + H(T_3) \\
        h - 1 &= 1 + H(T_3) \implies H(T_3) = h - 2
    \end{align*}
    Since node $n_2$ is right-heavy ($\backslash$), its left subtree $T_2$ must be exactly $1$ unit shorter than $T_3$:
    \begin{equation*}
        H(T_2) = H(T_3) - 1 = (h - 2) - 1 = h - 3
    \end{equation*}
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Summary of Subtree Heights:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $\mathbf{H(T_2) = h - 3}$
    \item $\mathbf{H(T_3) = h - 2}$
    \item $\mathbf{H(T_4) = h - 2}$
\end{itemize}

---

\subsection*{2. Analysis of Deleting an Element from $T_1$}

When a node is deleted from the subtree $T_1$, two scenarios can happen depending on whether the deletion changes the structural height of $T_1$:

\subsubsection*{Scenario A: Height of $T_1$ remains $h - 1$}
If the node deleted does not lie on the critical height-defining path of $T_1$, the height of $T_1$ remains unchanged. The balance factors across the rest of the tree are unaffected, and \textbf{no rotation is required}.

\subsubsection*{Scenario B: Height of $T_1$ decreases to $h - 2$}
If the deletion shortens the height of $T_1$ from $h-1$ to $h-2$, the imbalance propagates upward to the root:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Imbalance at Root:} $\text{BF}(\text{root}) = H(T_1) - H(n_1) = (h - 2) - h = -2$. The root is now critically right-heavy.
    \item \textbf{Identify the Rotation Type:} We examine the right child ($n_1$). Node $n_1$ has a balance symbol of $/$, meaning it is left-heavy ($\text{BF} = +1$).
    \item \textbf{The RL Case:} Since the root balance factor is $-2$ and its right child's balance factor is $+1$, this forms a \textbf{Right-Left (RL) zigzag case}.
    \item \textbf{Resolution:} A \textbf{Double Rotation (Right-Left Rotation)} must be executed:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Perform a \textbf{Right Rotation} around node $n_1$. This brings node $n_2$ up to replace $n_1$.
        \item Perform a \textbf{Left Rotation} around the \textbf{root node}. This brings node $n_2$ up to become the new root of the entire tree.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{itemize}

---

\subsection*{3. General Cases for Deletion in an AVL Tree}

When deleting a node in an AVL tree, we first apply standard Binary Search Tree deletion rules. Then, we retrace the path from the deep deletion point up toward the root, updating balance factors. If any node $X$ is found with a balance factor of $+2$ or $-2$, let $Y$ be its taller child, and $Z$ be the taller child of $Y$. This yields **four distinct structural cases**:

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\small
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|p{6.5cm}|}
\hline
\textbf{Case Type} & \textbf{BF of $X$} & \textbf{BF of $Y$} & \textbf{Required Rotation Operation to Restore Balance} \\ \hline
\textbf{Left-Left (LL)} & $+2$ & $+1$ or $0$ & \textbf{Single Right Rotation} around node $X$. \\ \hline
\textbf{Left-Right (LR)} & $+2$ & $-1$ & \textbf{Double Rotation:} Left Rotate around $Y$, then Right Rotate around $X$. \\ \hline
\textbf{Right-Right (RR)} & $-2$ & $-1$ or $0$ & \textbf{Single Left Rotation} around node $X$. \\ \hline
\textbf{Right-Left (RL)} & $-2$ & $+1$ & \textbf{Double Rotation:} Right Rotate around $Y$, then Left Rotate around $X$. \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\begin{tcolorbox}[colback=yellow!5,colframe=yellow!60!black,title=\textbf{Crucial Distinction: Deletion vs. Insertion}]
Unlike insertion—where a single structural fix always restores the pre-insertion height of the entire tree—a rotation after a deletion can **shorten the overall height of the processed subtree**. This height reduction can introduce new imbalances further up the tree. Consequently, rebalancing may cascade upward, requiring up to $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ rotations in the worst-case scenario.
\end{tcolorbox}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Design an AVL tree by inserting the keys $5, 12, 3, -2, -5, -3, 1$ in order. Show all steps. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[ansbox, enhanced,breakable,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,title={\textbf{Answer: AVL Tree Design Step-by-Step}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white}]

We will insert the keys $\mathbf{[5, 12, 3, -2, -5, -3, 1]}$ successively into an initially empty AVL tree. For each step, the Balance Factor ($\text{BF}$) is calculated as $\text{BF} = \text{Height}(\text{Left Subtree}) - \text{Height}(\text{Right Subtree})$.

---

\subsection*{Steps 1 to 4: Inserting 5, 12, 3, and -2}
The first four keys are inserted according to standard Binary Search Tree (BST) properties. After each insertion, the balance factors of all nodes remain within the acceptable range of $\{-1, 0, 1\}$. No rotations are required.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.2cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=cB, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!5}
]
    \node[treenode, label=above:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=+1}] {5}
        child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=+1}] {3}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=0}] {-2}}
            child[missing] {}
        }
        child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=0}] {12}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

---

\subsection*{Step 5: Inserting -5 (Imbalance \& Single Right Rotation)}
Key $-5$ is inserted as the left child of $-2$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{BF Calculation:} $\text{BF}(-2) = +1$, $\text{BF}(3) = 2 - 0 = +2$. 
    \item \textbf{Violation:} Node \textbf{3} becomes critically unbalanced ($\text{BF} = +2$).
    \item \textbf{Classification:} The path from node 3 to the newly inserted node $-5$ is Left $\to$ Left, making this an \textbf{LL Case}.
    \item \textbf{Resolution:} We perform a \textbf{Single Right Rotation} around node \textbf{3}.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.2cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.8cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=cB, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!5},
    imbalanced/.style={circle, draw=red, thick, fill=red!10, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt}
]
    % Before
    \begin{scope}
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.2cm] at (0,0.5) {\textbf{Before Rotation}};
        \node[treenode] {5}
            child {node[imbalanced, label=left:{\color{red}\scriptsize +2}] {3}
                child {node[treenode] {-2}
                    child {node[treenode] {-5}}
                    child[missing] {}
                }
                child[missing] {}
            }
            child {node[treenode] {12}};
    \end{scope}

    \draw[->, thick, dashed, cB] (2, -1) -- node[above] {\small Right Rotate(3)} (4.5, -1);

    % After
    \begin{scope}[xshift=6.5cm]
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.2cm] at (0,0.5) {\textbf{After Rotation}};
        \node[treenode, label=above:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=+1}] {5}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=0}] {-2}
                child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=0}] {-5}}
                child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=0}] {3}}
            }
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=0}] {12}};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

---

\subsection*{Step 6: Inserting -3 (Imbalance \& Single Right Rotation at Root)}
Key $-3$ is greater than $-5$, so it is inserted as the right child of $-5$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{BF Calculation:} $\text{BF}(-5) = -1$, $\text{BF}(-2) = 2 - 1 = +1$, $\text{BF}(5) = 3 - 1 = +2$.
    \item \textbf{Violation:} The root node \textbf{5} becomes critically unbalanced ($\text{BF} = +2$).
    \item \textbf{Classification:} The path from root 5 to the newly inserted node $-3$ goes Left (to $-2$) $\to$ Left (to $-5$). Since node $-2$ is left-heavy ($\text{BF} = +1$), this forms an \textbf{LL Case} at the root.
    \item \textbf{Resolution:} We perform a \textbf{Single Right Rotation} around the root node \textbf{5}. Node $-2$ is promoted to the new root.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.2cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.8cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=cB, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!5},
    imbalanced/.style={circle, draw=red, thick, fill=red!10, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\small, inner sep=0pt}
]
    % Before
    \begin{scope}
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.2cm] at (0,0.5) {\textbf{Before Rotation}};
        \node[imbalanced, label=above:{\color{red}\scriptsize +2}] {5}
            child {node[treenode] {-2}
                child {node[treenode] {-5}
                    child[missing] {}
                    child {node[treenode] {-3}}
                }
                child {node[treenode] {3}}
            }
            child {node[treenode] {12}};
    \end{scope}

    \draw[->, thick, dashed, cB] (2.2, -1.2) -- node[above] {\small Right Rotate(5)} (4.8, -1.2);

    % After
    \begin{scope}[xshift=7cm]
        \node[text width=3cm, align=center, above=0.2cm] at (0,0.5) {\textbf{After Rotation}};
        \node[treenode, label=above:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=0}] {-2}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=-1}] {-5}
                child[missing] {}
                child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=0}] {-3}}
            }
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=0}] {5}
                child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=0}] {3}}
                child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\scriptsize BF=0}] {12}}
            };
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

---

\subsection*{Step 7: Inserting 1 (Final Balanced Tree)}
Key $1$ is compared with $-2$ (go right), $5$ (go left), and $3$ (go left), placing it as the left child of $3$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item We recalculate all structural balance factors:
    $\text{BF}(1) = 0$, $\text{BF}(3) = +1$, $\text{BF}(5) = 2 - 1 = +1$, $\text{BF}(-5) = -1$, and $\text{BF}(-2) = 2 - 3 = -1$.
    \item Every node strictly satisfies the balance condition $|\text{BF}| \le 1$. No further rotations are needed.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\textbf{Final Resulting AVL Tree} \\[0.2cm]
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.4cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.4cm},
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=cB, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\normalsize, inner sep=0pt, fill=cB!8},
    edge from parent/.style={draw=black, thick}
]
    \node[treenode, label=above:{\color{blue}\small BF=-1}] {-2}
        child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\small BF=-1}] {-5}
            child[missing] {}
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\small BF=0}] {-3}}
        }
        child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\small BF=+1}] {5}
            child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\small BF=+1}] {3}
                child {node[treenode, label=left:{\color{blue}\small BF=0}] {1}}
                child[missing] {}
            }
            child {node[treenode, label=right:{\color{blue}\small BF=0}] {12}}
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What is the height of an AVL tree with $n$ internal nodes? \mk{5}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

The height $h$ of an AVL tree with $n$ internal nodes is strictly bounded by \textbf{logarithmic time complexity}, specifically $\mathcal{O}(\log_2 n)$. 

Because AVL trees enforce a strict balance condition (the height difference between left and right subtrees cannot exceed 1), we can define both the best-case (minimum) and worst-case (maximum) height boundaries for a given number of nodes $n$.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\subsubsection*{1. Best-Case Height (Perfectly Balanced Tree)}
In the best-case scenario, the AVL tree is a perfectly balanced full binary tree. Every level is completely filled. 
\begin{equation*}
    h_{\text{min}} = \lfloor \log_2(n) \rfloor
\end{equation*}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\subsubsection*{2. Worst-Case Height (Most Unbalanced AVL Tree)}
In the worst-case scenario, the AVL tree is as sparse as possible while still maintaining the AVL property. This occurs when every internal node has a balance factor of exactly $+1$ or $-1$. Such a tree is called a \textbf{Fibonacci Tree}.

Let $N(h)$ be the minimum number of nodes required to form an AVL tree of height $h$. To build this minimal tree, the root must have one subtree of height $h-1$ and the other of height $h-2$. This gives the recurrence relation:
\begin{equation*}
    N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1
\end{equation*}
with base cases:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $N(0) = 0$ (An empty tree)
    \item $N(1) = 1$ (A tree with just a root)
\end{itemize}

This recurrence relation is closely related to the Fibonacci sequence. By adding $1$ to both sides, we get $N(h) + 1 = (N(h-1) + 1) + (N(h-2) + 1)$, which perfectly matches the standard Fibonacci recurrence $F_{h+2}$.

Using Binet's formula for the Fibonacci sequence, it can be mathematically proven that the number of nodes grows exponentially with respect to the golden ratio ($\phi \approx 1.618$):
\begin{equation*}
    N(h) \approx \frac{\phi^{h+2}}{\sqrt{5}} - 1
\end{equation*}

By substituting $n$ for $N(h)$ and solving for $h$, we get the strict upper bound for the height:
\begin{align*}
    n &\approx \frac{\phi^{h+2}}{\sqrt{5}} - 1 \\
    n + 1 &\approx \frac{\phi^{h+2}}{\sqrt{5}} \\
    \log_\phi(\sqrt{5}(n + 1)) &\approx h + 2
\end{align*}

Converting the logarithm base from $\phi$ to $2$ (since $\log_2(\phi) \approx 0.694$ and $1 / 0.694 \approx 1.44$), we obtain the precise mathematical upper bound:
\begin{equation*}
    h_{\text{max}} \approx 1.44 \log_2(n + 2) - 0.328
\end{equation*}

\subsubsection*{Conclusion}
Because the maximum possible height is $1.44 \log_2(n + 2)$, an AVL tree's height is mathematically guaranteed to never exceed \textbf{$\approx 1.44$ times the optimal height}. Thus, the height of an AVL tree is rigorously constrained to $\mathbf{\mathcal{O}(\log n)}$.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Build a 2-3-4 tree with the keys $12, 5, 6, 10, 15, 8, 13, 17, 11, 14, 4$. Then delete elements in the following sequence: $4, 12, 13$. Show all steps. \mk{25}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: 2-3-4 Tree Construction and Deletion}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

We will use the standard \textbf{proactive top-down} approach for the 2-3-4 tree (B-tree of order 4):
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Insertion:} Any 4-node encountered on the path down to a leaf is split immediately before descending further.
    \item \textbf{Deletion:} Any 2-node encountered on the path down is refilled via borrowing or merging to prevent underflow at the leaf level.
\end{itemize}

---

\subsection*{Part 1: Construction of the 2-3-4 Tree}

\subsubsection*{Steps 1--3: Insert 12, 5, 6}
Keys are added sequentially into the single root node until it forms a 4-node:
\begin{equation*}
    [\text{Empty}] \longrightarrow [12] \longrightarrow [5 \ \vert\ 12] \longrightarrow [5 \ \vert\ 6 \ \vert\ 12] \quad \text{(Node is full)}
\end{equation*}

\subsubsection*{Step 4: Insert 10}
The root $[5 \ \vert\ 6 \ \vert\ 12]$ is a full 4-node. Before inserting 10, it must be split. The middle key ($6$) moves up to form a new root, creating two child nodes. Since $10 > 6$, it enters the right child node.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.2cm, sibling distance=2.5cm,
    treenode/.style={rectangle, draw=cB, thick, fill=cB!5, rounded corners=2pt, inner sep=4pt, font=\small}
]
    \node[treenode] {6}
        child {node[treenode] {5}}
        child {node[treenode] {$10 \ \vert\ 12$}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 5: Insert 15}
Key $15$ is greater than $6$, so it moves to the right child node $[10 \ \vert\ 12]$, filling it to maximum capacity: $[10 \ \vert\ 12 \ \vert\ 15]$.

\subsubsection*{Step 6: Insert 8}
As we descend to insert $8$ ($8 > 6$), we encounter the full 4-node $[10 \ \vert\ 12 \ \vert\ 15]$. It is proactively split: its middle key ($12$) moves up into the root node. Then, $8$ is added to the node containing $10$ ($6 < 8 < 12$).
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.2cm, sibling distance=2cm,
    treenode/.style={rectangle, draw=cB, thick, fill=cB!5, rounded corners=2pt, inner sep=4pt, font=\small}
]
    \node[treenode] {$6 \ \vert\ 12$}
        child {node[treenode] {5}}
        child {node[treenode] {$8 \ \vert\ 10$}}
        child {node[treenode] {15}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Steps 7--9: Insert 13, 17, 11}
These values are sequentially placed into their matching leaves without causing structural violations:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $13$ and $17$ are guided to the rightmost leaf node ($> 12$) $\rightarrow [13 \ \vert\ 15 \ \vert\ 17]$.
    \item $11$ is guided to the middle leaf node ($6 < 11 < 12$) $\rightarrow [8 \ \vert\ 10 \ \vert\ 11]$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 10: Insert 14}
Descending to place $14$ ($14 > 12$), we encounter the full child node $[13 \ \vert\ 15 \ \vert\ 17]$. It splits, moving $15$ up into the root. Since $12 < 14 < 15$, $14$ joins the left split child containing $13$.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.2cm, sibling distance=2cm,
    treenode/.style={rectangle, draw=cB, thick, fill=cB!5, rounded corners=2pt, inner sep=4pt, font=\small}
]
    \node[treenode] {$6 \ \vert\ 12 \ \vert\ 15$}
        child {node[treenode] {5}}
        child {node[treenode] {$8 \ \vert\ 10 \ \vert\ 11$}}
        child {node[treenode] {$13 \ \vert\ 14$}}
        child {node[treenode] {17}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 11: Insert 4 (Final Constructed Tree)}
Before descending, we observe that the root node $[6 \ \vert\ 12 \ \vert\ 15]$ is completely full. It must be split proactively. The middle key $12$ moves up to establish a new level. Following this split, $4$ safely descends to the leftmost leaf node.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.3cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
    treenode/.style={rectangle, draw=cB, thick, fill=cB!5, rounded corners=2pt, inner sep=4pt, font=\small}
]
    \node[treenode] {12}
        child {node[treenode] {6}
            child {node[treenode] {$4 \ \vert\ 5$}} % ম্যাথ মোড ($) যোগ করা হয়েছে
            child {node[treenode] {$8 \ \vert\ 10 \ \vert\ 11$}} 
        }
        child {node[treenode] {15}
            child {node[treenode] {$13 \ \vert\ 14$}} 
            child {node[treenode] {17}}
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
---

\subsection*{Part 2: Deletion Operations}

\subsubsection*{1. Delete 4}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path Adjustments:} We begin at the root node $[12]$. Its children ($[6]$ and $[15]$) are both 2-nodes. To prevent potential down-path structural underflows, the top-down rule dictates that we must merge the root with both children, compressing the tree back down by one level.
    \item \textbf{Tree Configuration after Merge:} The tree becomes a single root node with 4 children:
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        level distance=1.2cm, sibling distance=2.2cm,
        treenode/.style={rectangle, draw=cB, thick, fill=cB!5, rounded corners=2pt, inner sep=4pt, font=\small}
    ]
        \node[treenode] {$6 \ \vert\ 12 \ \vert\ 15$}
            child {node[treenode] {$4 \ \vert\ 5$}}
            child {node[treenode] {$8 \ \vert\ 10 \ \vert\ 11$}}
            child {node[treenode] {$13 \ \vert\ 14$}}
            child {node[treenode] {17}};
    \end{tikzpicture}
    \end{center}
    \item \textbf{Execution:} Now we target the leaf node $[4 \ \vert\ 5]$. Since it contains more than one key, $4$ can be safely deleted directly without causing an underflow.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{2. Delete 12}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path Adjustments:} The key $12$ is located inside an internal node ($[6 \ \vert\ 12 \ \vert\ 15]$). To delete an internal key, we must locate its \textbf{in-order predecessor} (the largest key in its left subtree). 
    \item \textbf{Execution:} The left subtree pointer leads to node $[8 \ \vert\ 10 \ \vert\ 11]$. This node is a 4-node (containing 3 keys), meaning it is completely safe to borrow from. The in-order predecessor is $11$. We copy $11$ up to replace $12$ in the root node, and then safely erase $11$ from its original leaf node.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        level distance=1.2cm, sibling distance=2.2cm,
        treenode/.style={rectangle, draw=cB, thick, fill=cB!5, rounded corners=2pt, inner sep=4pt, font=\small}
    ]
        \node[treenode] {$6 \ \vert\ 11 \ \vert\ 15$}
            child {node[treenode] {5}}
            child {node[treenode] {$8 \ \vert\ 10$}}
            child {node[treenode] {$13 \ \vert\ 14$}}
            child {node[treenode] {17}};
    \end{tikzpicture}
    \end{center}
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{3. Delete 13}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path Adjustments:} We trace the path down from the root $[6 \ \vert\ 11 \ \vert\ 15]$ to locate key $13$. The path guides us to the child node $[13 \ \vert\ 14]$.
    \item \textbf{Execution:} Since this leaf node is a 3-node (holding 2 keys), it can withstand a deletion without risking an underflow. We remove $13$ directly.
\end{itemize}

---

\subsection*{Final State of the 2-3-4 Tree}
The final structural state of the tree after completing all insertions and deletions is:
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.4cm, sibling distance=2.5cm,
    treenode/.style={rectangle, draw=cB, thick, fill=cB!8, rounded corners=2pt, inner sep=5pt, font=\normalsize},
    edge from parent/.style={draw=black, thick}
]
    \node[treenode] {$6 \ \vert\ 11 \ \vert\ 15$}
        child {node[treenode] {5}}
        child {node[treenode] {$8 \ \vert\ 10$}}
        child {node[treenode] {14}}
        child {node[treenode] {17}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2019-20
\end{enumerate}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cB}{Red-Black Trees}
\label{sub:s2-rbt}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item Starting from an empty red-black tree, perform the insertion operation of the keys 41, 38, 31, 12, 19, 8, 25, and 32 in the given order while maintaining all red-black properties. After completing all insertions, use the resulting tree and perform deletions of the keys 8, 12, and 19 in that order. During the insertion or deletion, you must show the tree after each operation, including the color of every node.
\mk{25} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Red-Black Tree Properties Maintained:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Every node is either Red or Black.
    \item The root is Black.
    \item Every leaf (NIL) is Black.
    \item If a node is Red, both of its children are Black (no two adjacent Red nodes).
    \item Every path from a given node to any of its descendant NIL leaves contains the same number of Black nodes.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\tikzset{
  treenode/.style = {circle, draw, align=center, inner sep=0pt, text centered, font=\sffamily\bfseries, minimum size=24pt},
  R/.style = {treenode, text=white, fill=red!80!black, draw=red!90!black, thick},
  B/.style = {treenode, text=white, fill=black!80, draw=black, thick},
  edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, -stealth}
}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
  \node[R, label=right:{Red Node}] at (0,0) {R};
  \node[B, label=right:{Black Node}] at (4,0) {B};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\subsection*{Part 1: Insertions}

\textbf{Step 1: Insert 41} \\
Insert 41 as a Red node. Since it is the root of the tree, its color is changed to Black to satisfy the root property.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm, level distance=1.2cm}]
  \node [B] {41};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 2: Insert 38} \\
Insert 38 as a Red node. It becomes the left child of 41. No properties are violated.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm, level distance=1.2cm}]
  \node [B] {41}
    child {node [R] {38}}
    child[missing] {};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 3: Insert 31} \\
Insert 31 as a Red left child of 38. \\
\textbf{Violation:} Node 38 is Red and 31 is Red (Adjacent Red nodes). \\
\textbf{Resolution:} The uncle of 31 is NIL (Black). Since 31 and 38 are both left children, this is the \textbf{Left-Left} case. We perform a Right Rotation around the grandparent (41) and recolor: the original parent (38) becomes Black, and the grandparent (41) becomes Red.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm, level distance=1.2cm}]
  \node [B] {38}
    child {node [R] {31}}
    child {node [R] {41}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 4: Insert 12} \\
Insert 12 as a Red left child of 31. \\
\textbf{Violation:} Node 31 is Red and 12 is Red. \\
\textbf{Resolution:} The uncle of 12 is 41, which is Red. We recolor the parent (31) and uncle (41) to Black, and the grandparent (38) to Red. Since 38 is the root, it is immediately recolored back to Black.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm, level distance=1.2cm},
  level 2/.style={sibling distance=2cm, level distance=1.2cm}
]
  \node [B] {38}
    child {node [B] {31}
      child {node [R] {12}}
      child[missing] {}
    }
    child {node [B] {41}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 5: Insert 19} \\
Insert 19 as a Red right child of 12. \\
\textbf{Violation:} Node 12 is Red and 19 is Red. \\
\textbf{Resolution:} The uncle of 19 is NIL (Black). This is the \textbf{Left-Right} case. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item First, perform a Left Rotation around 12 to convert it to a Left-Left case. Node 12 becomes the left child of 19.
    \item Next, perform a Right Rotation around the grandparent 31. Recolor the new parent 19 to Black and the original grandparent 31 to Red.
\end{itemize}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm, level distance=1.2cm},
  level 2/.style={sibling distance=2cm, level distance=1.2cm}
]
  \node [B] {38}
    child {node [B] {19}
      child {node [R] {12}}
      child {node [R] {31}}
    }
    child {node [B] {41}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 6: Insert 8} \\
Insert 8 as a Red left child of 12. \\
\textbf{Violation:} Node 12 is Red and 8 is Red. \\
\textbf{Resolution:} The uncle of 8 is 31 (Red). We recolor the parent (12) and uncle (31) to Black, and the grandparent (19) to Red. The parent of 19 is 38 (Black), so no further violations occur.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm, level distance=1.2cm},
  level 2/.style={sibling distance=2cm, level distance=1.2cm},
  level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm}
]
  \node [B] {38}
    child {node [R] {19}
      child {node [B] {12}
        child {node [R] {8}}
        child[missing] {}
      }
      child {node [B] {31}}
    }
    child {node [B] {41}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 7: Insert 25} \\
Insert 25 as a Red left child of 31. Its parent 31 is Black, so no properties are violated.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  level 1/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
  level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
  level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm}
]
  \node [B] {38}
    child {node [R] {19}
      child {node [B] {12}
        child {node [R] {8}}
        child[missing] {}
      }
      child {node [B] {31}
        child {node [R] {25}}
        child[missing] {}
      }
    }
    child {node [B] {41}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 8: Insert 32} \\
Insert 32 as a Red right child of 31. Its parent 31 is Black, so no properties are violated.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  level 1/.style={sibling distance=5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
  level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
  level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm}
]
  \node [B] {38}
    child {node [R] {19}
      child {node [B] {12}
        child {node [R] {8}}
        child[missing] {}
      }
      child {node [B] {31}
        child {node [R] {25}}
        child {node [R] {32}}
      }
    }
    child {node [B] {41}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\subsection*{Part 2: Deletions}

\textbf{Step 9: Delete 8} \\
Node 8 is a Red leaf. We can simply remove it without violating any Red-Black Tree properties (the black-height remains unchanged).
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  level 1/.style={sibling distance=5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
  level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
  level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm}
]
  \node [B] {38}
    child {node [R] {19}
      child {node [B] {12}}
      child {node [B] {31}
        child {node [R] {25}}
        child {node [R] {32}}
      }
    }
    child {node [B] {41}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 10: Delete 12} \\
Node 12 is a Black leaf. Removing it leaves a Double-Black NIL node in its place (let's call it $N$). 
\begin{itemize}
    \item $N$ is the left child of its parent 19 (Red).
    \item The sibling of $N$ is 31 (Black).
    \item The sibling W (31) has two Red children, 25 and 32.
\end{itemize}
\textbf{Resolution:} Because the sibling W is Black and its "far" child (32, the right child) is Red, this is the \textbf{Right-Right Case}. We apply the following steps:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Recolor the sibling (31) to the parent's color (Red).
    \item Recolor the parent (19) to Black.
    \item Recolor the far child (32) to Black.
    \item Perform a Left Rotation around the parent (19).
\end{enumerate}
After the rotation, the Double-Black node $N$ loses its extra black weight and is safely removed.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  level 1/.style={sibling distance=5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
  level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
  level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm}
]
  \node [B] {38}
    child {node [R] {31}
      child {node [B] {19}
        child[missing] {}
        child {node [R] {25}}
      }
      child {node [B] {32}}
    }
    child {node [B] {41}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 11: Delete 19} \\
Node 19 is a Black node with a single Red child (25). \\
\textbf{Resolution:} We remove 19 and replace it with its child 25. To maintain the black-height along the path, we simply recolor the new replacing node 25 to Black.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  level 1/.style={sibling distance=5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
  level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm}
]
  \node [B] {38}
    child {node [R] {31}
      child {node [B] {25}}
      child {node [B] {32}}
    }
    child {node [B] {41}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Delete the node 50 from the given Red-Black Tree. Note that doubly circled nodes represent Red and single circles represent Black.
\begin{center}
      \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.4,
        blacknode/.style={circle, fill=black, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries\color{white}},
        rednode/.style={circle, fill=red, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries\color{white}},
        thick
    ]

        \node[blacknode] (30) at (0,4) {30}
            child {node[blacknode] {20}}
            child {node[rednode] {45}
                child {node[blacknode] {40}
                    child {node[rednode] {35}}
                    child {node[rednode] {42}}
                }
                child {node[blacknode] {50}}
            };

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Step 1: Initial Deletion and Double Black Node} \\
Node 50 is a \textbf{Black} leaf node. When it is deleted, its position is replaced by a \texttt{NIL} leaf. To maintain the black-height property of the tree, this \texttt{NIL} node absorbs the black weight of Node 50, becoming a \textbf{Double Black} (DB) node.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    blacknode/.style={circle, fill=black, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries\color{white}},
    rednode/.style={circle, fill=red, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries\color{white}},
    dbnode/.style={circle, draw=black, very thick, fill=black!60, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries\color{white}},
    thick
]
\node[blacknode] (30) {30}
    child {node[blacknode] {20}}
    child {node[rednode] {45}
        child {node[blacknode] {40}
            child {node[rednode] {35}}
            child {node[rednode] {42}}
        }
        child {node[dbnode] {DB}}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{Step 2: Analysis of the Double Black Node} \\
We evaluate the structural relationships around the Double Black node (\texttt{DB}):
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Parent ($P$):} Node 45, which is \textcolor{red}{\textbf{Red}}.
    \item \textbf{Sibling ($W$):} Node 40, which is \textbf{Black}. (Since \texttt{DB} is a right child, the sibling is the left child).
    \item \textbf{Sibling's Far Child:} Node 35 (the left child of 40), which is \textcolor{red}{\textbf{Red}}.
    \item \textbf{Sibling's Near Child:} Node 42 (the right child of 40), which is \textcolor{red}{\textbf{Red}}.
\end{itemize}
Since the sibling is Black and its \textit{far child} is Red, this corresponds to \textbf{Case 4} of Red-Black Tree deletion.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Step 3: Resolving Case 4} \\
To resolve this violation, we apply the following operations:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Recolor Sibling:} Change the color of the sibling (40) to the parent's color (\textcolor{red}{\textbf{Red}}).
    \item \textbf{Recolor Parent:} Change the color of the parent (45) to \textbf{Black}.
    \item \textbf{Recolor Far Child:} Change the color of the far child (35) to \textbf{Black}.
    \item \textbf{Rotation:} Perform a \textbf{Right Rotation} around the parent (45).
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Step 4: Right Rotation around 45}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Node 40 moves up to take the place of 45.
    \item Node 45 becomes the right child of 40.
    \item Node 42 (which was the right child of 40) becomes the left child of 45.
    \item The extra black weight is stripped away, and the tree is perfectly balanced again.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Final Red-Black Tree:}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    blacknode/.style={circle, fill=black, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries\color{white}},
    rednode/.style={circle, fill=red, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries\color{white}},
    thick
]
\node[blacknode] (30) {30}
    child {node[blacknode] {20}}
    child {node[rednode] {40}
        child {node[blacknode] {35}}
        child {node[blacknode] {45}
            child {node[rednode] {42}}
            child[missing] {}
        }
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Complexity Analysis:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(1)$ for the deletion and restructuring (constant number of recolorings and $1$ rotation) once the node is found. Overall deletion time is $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ where $n$ is the number of nodes.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(1)$ auxiliary space used for pointers and recoloring.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item Write the pseudo-code of an insertion operation in a Red-Black tree and explain (graphically) different forms of transformation necessary to maintain the RB property while inserting an item. Briefly compare RB-tree and AVL-tree.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsection*{1. Pseudo-code for Insertion in a Red-Black Tree}

The insertion in a Red-Black Tree follows the standard Binary Search Tree (BST) insertion, where the new node is always colored \textbf{RED}. After the insertion, a fix-up procedure is called to restore any violated Red-Black Tree properties (primarily, the rule that no two adjacent nodes can be red).

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{RB-INSERT($T, z$)}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State $y \gets T.nil$
\State $x \gets T.root$
\While{$x \neq T.nil$}
    \State $y \gets x$
    \If{$z.key < x.key$}
        \State $x \gets x.left$
    \Else
        \State $x \gets x.right$
    \EndIf
\EndWhile
\State $z.p \gets y$
\If{$y == T.nil$}
    \State $T.root \gets z$
\ElsIf{$z.key < y.key$}
    \State $y.left \gets z$
\Else
    \State $y.right \gets z$
\EndIf
\State $z.left \gets T.nil$
\State $z.right \gets T.nil$
\State $z.color \gets \text{RED}$
\State \Call{RB-INSERT-FIXUP}{$T, z$}
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{RB-INSERT-FIXUP($T, z$)}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\While{$z.p.color == \text{RED}$}
    \If{$z.p == z.p.p.left$}  \Comment{Parent is a left child}
        \State $y \gets z.p.p.right$ \Comment{$y$ is the uncle of $z$}
        \If{$y.color == \text{RED}$} \Comment{\textbf{Case 1: Uncle is RED}}
            \State $z.p.color \gets \text{BLACK}$
            \State $y.color \gets \text{BLACK}$
            \State $z.p.p.color \gets \text{RED}$
            \State $z \gets z.p.p$
        \Else
            \If{$z == z.p.right$} \Comment{\textbf{Case 2: Uncle is BLACK, $z$ is a right child}}
                \State $z \gets z.p$
                \State \Call{LEFT-ROTATE}{$T, z$}
            \EndIf
            \State $z.p.color \gets \text{BLACK}$ \Comment{\textbf{Case 3: Uncle is BLACK, $z$ is a left child}}
            \State $z.p.p.color \gets \text{RED}$
            \State \Call{RIGHT-ROTATE}{$T, z.p.p$}
        \EndIf
    \Else
        \State \text{(Symmetric to the \texttt{then} clause with "left" and "right" exchanged)}
    \EndIf
\EndWhile
\State $T.root.color \gets \text{BLACK}$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{1em}
\subsection*{2. Graphical Explanation of Transformations}

We assume the new node $z$'s parent ($P$) is the left child of its grandparent ($G$). The right child scenario is entirely symmetric. 

\tikzset{
  node base/.style={circle, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries\color{white}, draw=none},
  R/.style={node base, fill=red},
  B/.style={node base, fill=black},
  G/.style={circle, minimum size=0.6cm, fill=gray!30, font=\small, draw=gray},
  thick
}

\subsubsection*{Case 1: Uncle is RED (Recoloring)}
If the uncle ($U$) is red, we simply recolor the parent ($P$) and uncle ($U$) to \textbf{BLACK}, and the grandparent ($G$) to \textbf{RED}. The pointer $z$ is moved to $G$ to check for further violations up the tree.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level distance=1.2cm, sibling distance=2.5cm]
    \node[B, label=above:{$G$}] at (0,0) {}
        child {node[R, label=left:{$P$}] {}
            child {node[R, label=left:{$z$}] {}}
            child {node[G] {$\alpha$}}
        }
        child {node[R, label=right:{$U$}] {}
        };
    
    \draw[->, very thick] (2, -1) -- (4, -1) node[midway, above] {\small Recolor};

    \begin{scope}[xshift=6cm]
    \node[R, label=above:{$z \to G$}] at (0,0) {}
        child {node[B, label=left:{$P$}] {}
            child {node[R] {}}
            child {node[G] {$\alpha$}}
        }
        child {node[B, label=right:{$U$}] {}
        };
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Case 2: Uncle is BLACK, $z$ is a Right Child (Left Rotation)}
If the uncle is black and $z$ forms a "zig-zag" shape (Left-Right), we perform a \textbf{Left Rotation} on the parent $P$. This transforms the problem into Case 3 without fixing the red-red violation yet.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level distance=1.2cm, sibling distance=2.5cm]
    \node[B, label=above:{$G$}] at (0,0) {}
        child {node[R, label=left:{$P$}] {}
            child {node[G] {$\alpha$}}
            child {node[R, label=right:{$z$}] {}}
        }
        child {node[B, label=right:{$U$}] {}
        };
    
    \draw[->, very thick] (2, -1) -- (4, -1) node[midway, above] {\small Left Rotate(P)};

    \begin{scope}[xshift=6cm]
    \node[B, label=above:{$G$}] at (0,0) {}
        child {node[R, label=left:{$P \leftarrow z$}] {}
            child {node[R, label=left:{$P$}] {}
                child {node[G] {$\alpha$}}
                child[missing]
            }
            child[missing]
        }
        child {node[B, label=right:{$U$}] {}
        };
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Case 3: Uncle is BLACK, $z$ is a Left Child (Right Rotation \& Recolor)}
If the uncle is black and $z$ forms a "straight" shape (Left-Left), we perform a \textbf{Right Rotation} on the grandparent $G$. Then, we recolor the original parent $P$ to \textbf{BLACK} and the old grandparent $G$ to \textbf{RED}. This fixes the local violation and ensures the black height remains unchanged.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level distance=1.2cm, sibling distance=2.5cm]
    \node[B, label=above:{$G$}] at (0,0) {}
        child {node[R, label=left:{$P$}] {}
            child {node[R, label=left:{$z$}] {}}
            child {node[G] {$\alpha$}}
        }
        child {node[B, label=right:{$U$}] {}
        };
    
    \draw[->, very thick] (2, -1) -- (4, -1) node[midway, above] {\small Right Rotate(G)};
    \draw[->, very thick] (2, -1.5) -- (4, -1.5) node[midway, below] {\small \& Recolor};

    \begin{scope}[xshift=6.5cm, yshift=-0.5cm]
    \node[B, label=above:{$P$}] at (0,0) {}
        child {node[R, label=left:{$z$}] {}}
        child {node[R, label=right:{$G$}] {}
            child {node[G] {$\alpha$}}
            child {node[B, label=right:{$U$}] {}}
        };
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\subsection*{3. Comparison between Red-Black Tree and AVL Tree}

Both Red-Black Trees and AVL Trees are self-balancing Binary Search Trees that guarantee $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ time for insertion, deletion, and search. However, they employ different balancing criteria:


\begin{center}
\vspace{0.5em}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\begin{tabular}{|c|p{6cm}|p{6cm}|}
\hline
\textbf{Feature} & \textbf{Red-Black Tree} & \textbf{AVL Tree} \\
\hline
\textbf{Balancing Rule} & Loosely balanced. The longest path from root to leaf is at most twice the shortest path. & Strictly balanced. The difference between heights of left and right subtrees is at most 1. \\
\hline
\textbf{Lookup Speed} & Fast $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$, but slightly slower than AVL due to lesser strictness in height. & Faster $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ due to strictly shorter maximum height. \\
\hline
\textbf{Insertion \& Deletion} & Faster. Requires at most 2 rotations for insertion and 3 for deletion. Changes mostly involve $\mathcal{O}(1)$ recoloring. & Slower. Deletion may require $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ rotations propagating all the way up to the root. \\
\hline
\textbf{Memory Space} & Minimal overhead. Requires only 1 extra bit per node (to store Red/Black color). & Higher overhead. Requires an integer per node to store the height or balance factor. \\
\hline
\textbf{Typical Use Cases} & Preferred for write-intensive applications (e.g., \texttt{std::map} in C++, Completely Fair Scheduler in Linux). & Preferred for read-intensive applications where searches are much more frequent than insertions/deletions. \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item What are the properties of a red-black tree? Derive that if a tree with $n$ keys follows the red-black properties, its height $h$ can be bounded as $h \leq 2\lg(n + 1)$.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Red-Black Tree Properties \& Height Bound Derivation}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]



\subsection*{1. Properties of a Red-Black Tree}
A \textbf{Red-Black Tree} is a self-balancing binary search tree where each node contains an extra attribute denoting its color (either \textbf{red} or \textbf{black}). To guarantee approximately balanced structure, a valid Red-Black tree must satisfy the following five invariants:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Color Property:} Every node in the tree is colored either red or black.
    \item \textbf{Root Property:} The root node is always black.
    \item \textbf{Leaf Property:} Every leaf node (\texttt{NIL} or \texttt{NULL} pointer) is considered black.
    \item \textbf{Red-Node Property:} If a node is red, then both of its children must be black. (This strictly ensures there are never two adjacent red nodes on any simple path).
    \item \textbf{Black-Height Property:} For any given node, all simple paths from that node down to its descendant leaves contain exactly the same number of black nodes.
\end{enumerate}

---

\subsection*{2. Derivation of the Height Bound: $h \le 2\lg(n + 1)$}

Let $h$ be the height of a red-black tree, and let $n$ be the total number of internal nodes (keys). We want to mathematically prove that the maximum height is bounded by $2\lg(n + 1)$.

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Define Black-Height}
Let $bh(x)$ denote the \textbf{black-height} of a node $x$. This is defined as the number of black nodes on any path from $x$ down to a leaf, \textit{excluding} the node $x$ itself. By Property 5, this value is uniform for all paths originating from $x$.

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Lemma on Subtree Size}
\textbf{Lemma:} \textit{The subtree rooted at any node $x$ contains at least $2^{bh(x)} - 1$ internal nodes.}

\textit{Proof by Induction on the height of $x$:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Base Case:} If $x$ has a height of $0$, it is a \texttt{NIL} leaf. Its black-height is $bh(x) = 0$, and the number of internal nodes is $0$. The formula yields $2^0 - 1 = 1 - 1 = 0$. The base case holds.
    \item \textbf{Inductive Step:} Consider an internal node $x$ with positive height and two children. Each child's black-height is either $bh(x)$ (if the child is red) or $bh(x) - 1$ (if the child is black). Therefore, the black-height of each child is \textit{at least} $bh(x) - 1$.
    \item By our inductive hypothesis, each child's subtree contains at least $2^{bh(x)-1} - 1$ internal nodes.
    \item The total number of internal nodes in the subtree rooted at $x$ is the sum of the nodes in its two subtrees plus $1$ (for $x$ itself):
    \begin{align*}
        \text{Total Nodes} &\ge \left(2^{bh(x)-1} - 1\right) + \left(2^{bh(x)-1} - 1\right) + 1 \\
        &= 2 \cdot \left(2^{bh(x)-1}\right) - 2 + 1 \\
        &= 2^{bh(x)} - 1
    \end{align*}
    This completes the proof of the lemma.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 3: Relate Black-Height to Tree Height}
Now, consider the root node of the tree. Let the overall height of the tree be $h$. 

Because of Property 4 (no consecutive red nodes), at most half of the nodes on any simple path from the root to a leaf can be red. Consequently, \textit{at least half} of the nodes on any such path must be black. Therefore, the black-height of the root must be at least half the total height:
\begin{equation*}
    bh(\text{root}) \ge \frac{h}{2}
\end{equation*}

\subsubsection*{Step 4: Final Algebraic Derivation}
Applying our lemma (from Step 2) directly to the root node, the total number of internal nodes $n$ in the entire tree must satisfy:
\begin{equation*}
    n \ge 2^{bh(\text{root})} - 1
\end{equation*}

Substitute the inequality from Step 3 into this equation:
\begin{equation*}
    n \ge 2^{h/2} - 1
\end{equation*}

Now, solve for $h$. First, add $1$ to both sides:
\begin{equation*}
    n + 1 \ge 2^{h/2}
\end{equation*}

Take the base-2 logarithm ($\lg$) of both sides:
\begin{equation*}
    \lg(n + 1) \ge \frac{h}{2}
\end{equation*}

Multiply by $2$:
\begin{equation*}
    2\lg(n + 1) \ge h
\end{equation*}

\begin{tcolorbox}[colback=green!5,colframe=green!60!black,title=\textbf{Conclusion}]
Rewriting the inequality, we get $\mathbf{h \le 2\lg(n + 1)}$. This proves that the maximum height of a Red-Black tree is logarithmic with respect to the number of nodes, guaranteeing that operations like search, insertion, and deletion strictly run in $\mathbf{\mathcal{O}(\log n)}$ time.
\end{tcolorbox}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Usually we have to fix a Red-Black tree after deleting a node when the properties of the Red-Black tree get violated because of node deletion. Explain whether it is possible to do so without performing any rotation. Delete the node with key $= 150$ from the given Red-Black tree. Show all the steps and the resulting Red-Black tree.
\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=0.85, transform shape,
        every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, font=\bfseries\small, inner sep=0pt, minimum size=0.75cm},
        red node/.style={fill=red, text=white, draw=red!80!black},
        black node/.style={fill=black, text=white, draw=black},
        nil leaf/.style={rectangle, fill=black, draw=black, minimum size=0.35cm, inner sep=0pt},
        edge/.style={thick, black}
    ]

        % ==========================================
        % TREE STRUCTURE (Coordinates Layout)
        % ==========================================
        
        % Level 0: Root
        \node[black node] (n240) at (0,0) {240};

        \node[nil leaf] (L50_2) at (-7.8,-6.0) {};

        \node[nil leaf] (L100_2) at (-6.4,-6.0) {};

        \node[nil leaf] (L140_2) at (-5.1,-4.8) {};

        \node[nil leaf] (L170_1) at (-3.9,-4.8) {};
        \node[nil leaf] (L170_2) at (-3.1,-4.8) {};
        % Under 195
        \node[nil leaf] (L195_1) at (-3.1,-7.3) {};
        \node[nil leaf] (L195_2) at (-2.3,-7.3) {};

        \draw[edge] (n150) -- (n120); \draw[edge] (n150) -- (n190);

        \draw[edge] (n50) -- (L50_1);   \draw[edge] (n50) -- (L50_2);
        \draw[edge] (n100) -- (L100_1); \draw[edge] (n100) -- (L100_2);
        \draw[edge] (n140) -- (L140_1); \draw[edge] (n140) -- (L140_2);
        \draw[edge] (n170) -- (L170_1); \draw[edge] (n170) -- (L170_2);
        \draw[edge] (n195) -- (L195_1); \draw[edge] (n195) -- (L195_2);
        \draw[edge] (n220) -- (L220_1); \draw[edge] (n220) -- (L220_2);
        \draw[edge] (n260) -- (L260_1); \draw[edge] (n260) -- (L260_2);
        \draw[edge] (n360) -- (L360_1); \draw[edge] (n360) -- (L360_2);

\mk{20} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Red-Black Tree Deletion Analysis and Execution}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsection*{1. Restoring Balance Without Rotations: Is It Possible?}

\textbf{Yes, it is entirely possible} to fix a Red-Black tree after node deletion without performing any tree rotations under specific conditions. 

When a node is deleted, a property violation (specifically an underflow of the black-height property) only occurs if the deleted physical node was \textbf{black}. If a structural violation does happen, the algorithm attempts to fix it by traversing up toward the root. Rotations can be completely avoided if the structural imbalance is resolved exclusively via one of the following scenarios:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Scenario A: The Deleted Node was Red.} If the physically removed node was Red, no properties are violated. The black-height remains perfectly consistent, and no red-red violations are created.
    \item \textbf{Scenario B: Color Recoloring (Case 2 of RB Deletion).} If the extra black (double-black) node has a black sibling, and \textbf{both children of that sibling are black}, the problem can be pushed up the tree simply by recoloring the sibling to \textbf{red} and shifting the double-black status to the parent node. If the parent node was red, it simply absorbs the double-black status to become a regular black node, terminating the fixup process without a single rotation.
    \item \textbf{Scenario C: Double-Black reaches the Root.} If the pushed double-black status climbs all the way up to the root of the entire tree, it is simply converted into a regular black node. This shrinks the overall black-height of the tree by 1 symmetrically, requiring no rotations.
\end{itemize}

---

\subsection*{2. Step-by-Step Deletion of Key $= 150$}

\subsubsection*{Step 2.1: Standard BST Deletion Phase}
Key $\mathbf{150}$ is located at an internal node. Following standard Binary Search Tree (BST) rules, we cannot delete it directly. Instead, we must replace its value with either its in-order predecessor or its in-order successor.
\begin{itemize}
    \item Let us select its \textbf{in-order predecessor} (the maximum key in its left subtree). 
    \item Tracking down the rightmost path of node $120$'s subtree leads directly to node $\mathbf{140}$.
    \item Node $140$ is a black leaf node whose children are both standard \texttt{NIL} leaves.
    \item We overwrite the key value of our target node ($150$) with the replacement value $\mathbf{140}$.
\end{itemize}

At this stage, the problem reduces down to physically deleting the old node $\mathbf{140}$ from its original position at the bottom of the tree.

\subsubsection*{Step 2.2: Red-Black Fixup Phase}
Because the physical node being deleted ($140$) is \textbf{black}, its removal creates a black-height deficit along that path. The child replacing it (a black \texttt{NIL} leaf) becomes a \textbf{double-black node}, which we will denote as $\mathbf{X}$.

Let us analyze the structural relationship surrounding the double-black node $X$:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Identify the Parent ($P$):} The parent of $X$ is node $\mathbf{120}$ (\textbf{Black}).
    \item \textbf{Identify the Sibling ($S$):} The sibling of $X$ is node $\mathbf{80}$ (\textbf{Red}).
\end{enumerate}

Since the sibling $S$ is \textbf{Red}, this matches \textbf{Case 1 of the standard Red-Black Deletion algorithm}. We must transform this case into Case 2, 3, or 4 by executing the following steps:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Change the color of sibling $80$ from Red to \textbf{Black}.
    \item Change the color of parent $120$ from Black to \textbf{Red}.
    \item Perform a \textbf{Single Right Rotation} around parent node $120$.
\end{itemize}

This structural transformation alters the local neighborhood layout: node $80$ becomes the new parent of node $120$. The double-black node $X$ is now a child of node $120$. Let us re-examine the properties from this new position:
\begin{itemize}
    \item New Parent of $X$ is node $\mathbf{120}$ (\textbf{Red}).
    \item New Sibling of $X$ is node $\mathbf{100}$ (\textbf{Black}).
    \item Both children of the new sibling $100$ are black \texttt{NIL} leaves.
\end{itemize}

This matches \textbf{Case 2 of Red-Black Deletion} (Sibling is black and both of its children are black):
\begin{itemize}
    \item We change the color of sibling $100$ from Black to \textbf{Red}.
    \item The extra black property shifts up to the parent node $120$.
    \item Since parent $120$ was \textbf{Red}, it absorbs the double-black completely and turns into a standard \textbf{Black} node.
    \item The tree is now perfectly balanced, satisfying all invariants. The execution terminates.
\end{itemize}

---

\subsection*{3. Final Resulting Red-Black Tree Diagram}

Below is the complete, valid configuration of the Red-Black tree after completing the structural adjustment steps:

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    scale=0.85, transform shape,
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, font=\bfseries\small, inner sep=0pt, minimum size=0.75cm},
    red node/.style={fill=red, text=white, draw=red!80!black},
    black node/.style={fill=black, text=white, draw=black},
    nil leaf/.style={rectangle, fill=black, draw=black, minimum size=0.35cm, inner sep=0pt},
    edge/.style={thick, black}
]

    % Level 0: Root
    \node[black node] (n240) at (0,0) {240};

    % Level 1
    \node[red node] (n140) at (-4.5,-1.2) {140}; % Replaced 150 with 140, remains RED
    \node[black node] (n390) at (4.5,-1.2) {390};

    % Level 2
    \node[black node] (n80) at (-6.5,-2.5) {80}; % Recolored to Black in Case 1
    \node[black node] (n190) at (-2.5,-2.5) {190}; % Intact untouched subtree
    \node[red node] (n280) at (3.0,-2.5) {280};
    \node[black node] (n450) at (6.0,-2.5) {450};

    % Level 3
    \node[black node] (n50) at (-7.5,-3.8) {50};
    \node[black node] (n120) at (-5.5,-3.8) {120}; % Parent absorbed double-black, now Black
    \node[black node] (n170) at (-3.5,-3.8) {170};
    \node[red node] (n210) at (-1.5,-3.8) {210};
    
    \node[black node] (n260) at (2.0,-3.8) {260};
    \node[black node] (n360) at (4.0,-3.8) {360};

    % Level 4
    \node[red node] (n100) at (-6.0,-5.1) {100}; % Recolored to Red in Case 2
    \node[nil leaf] (L120_R) at (-5.0,-5.1) {};  % Replaced 140 position, now NIL leaf
    \node[black node] (n200) at (-2.2,-5.1) {200};
    \node[black node] (n220) at (-0.8,-5.1) {220};

    % Level 5
    \node[red node] (n195) at (-2.7,-6.4) {195};

    % ==========================================
    % NIL LEAF NODES
    % ==========================================
    \node[nil leaf] (L50_1) at (-7.9,-4.8) {};
    \node[nil leaf] (L50_2) at (-7.1,-4.8) {};
    \node[nil leaf] (L100_1) at (-6.4,-6.0) {};
    \node[nil leaf] (L100_2) at (-5.6,-6.0) {};
    
    \node[nil leaf] (L170_1) at (-3.9,-4.8) {};
    \node[nil leaf] (L170_2) at (-3.1,-4.8) {};
    \node[nil leaf] (L195_1) at (-3.1,-7.3) {};
    \node[nil leaf] (L195_2) at (-2.3,-7.3) {};
    \node[nil leaf] (L220_1) at (-1.2,-6.0) {};
    \node[nil leaf] (L220_2) at (-0.4,-6.0) {};
    \node[nil leaf] (L260_1) at (1.6,-4.8) {};
    \node[nil leaf] (L260_2) at (2.4,-4.8) {};
    \node[nil leaf] (L360_1) at (3.6,-4.8) {};
    \node[nil leaf] (L360_2) at (4.4,-4.8) {};
    \node[nil leaf] (L450_1) at (5.6,-3.5) {};
    \node[nil leaf] (L450_2) at (6.4,-3.5) {};

    % ==========================================
    % EDGES / CONNECTIONS
    % ==========================================
    \draw[edge] (n240) -- (n140); \draw[edge] (n240) -- (n390);
    
    \draw[edge] (n140) -- (n80); \draw[edge] (n140) -- (n190);
    \draw[edge] (n390) -- (n280); \draw[edge] (n390) -- (n450);
    
    \draw[edge] (n80) -- (n50);  \draw[edge] (n80) -- (n120);
    \draw[edge] (n190) -- (n170); \draw[edge] (n190) -- (n210);
    \draw[edge] (n280) -- (n260); \draw[edge] (n280) -- (n360);
    
    \draw[edge] (n120) -- (n100); \draw[edge] (n120) -- (L120_R);
    \draw[edge] (n210) -- (n200); \draw[edge] (n210) -- (n220);
    \draw[edge] (n200) -- (n195);

    % Edges to NIL Leaves
    \draw[edge] (n50) -- (L50_1);   \draw[edge] (n50) -- (L50_2);
    \draw[edge] (n100) -- (L100_1); \draw[edge] (n100) -- (L100_2);
    \draw[edge] (n170) -- (L170_1); \draw[edge] (n170) -- (L170_2);
    \draw[edge] (n195) -- (L195_1); \draw[edge] (n195) -- (L195_2);
    \draw[edge] (n220) -- (L220_1); \draw[edge] (n220) -- (L220_2);
    \draw[edge] (n260) -- (L260_1); \draw[edge] (n260) -- (L260_2);
    \draw[edge] (n360) -- (L360_1); \draw[edge] (n360) -- (L360_2);
    \draw[edge] (n450) -- (L450_1); \draw[edge] (n450) -- (L450_2);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2020-21
\item Mr.\ X has proposed a new algorithm for insertion to a Red-Black tree, where the color of the newly inserted node is set to black by default. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of this algorithm over the traditional algorithm followed for insertion to a Red-Black tree.
\mk{5} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Comparative Analysis of Default-Black Insertion Algorithm}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

In the traditional Red-Black Tree insertion algorithm, a newly inserted node is always colored \textbf{Red} by default. Mr. X's alternative proposal suggests coloring the newly inserted node \textbf{Black} by default.
