Below is a detailed analysis of the advantages and disadvantages of this proposed approach compared to the traditional mechanism.

---

\subsection*{1. Advantages of Mr. X's Algorithm}

While setting a new node to black is highly disruptive to the tree's structural balance overall, it does provide a couple of niche algorithmic shortcuts:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Elimination of Red-Red (Property 4) Violations:}
    In the traditional algorithm, inserting a red node under an existing red parent creates a structural violation of Property 4 (no consecutive red nodes). By defaulting the new node to Black, it is mathematically impossible to introduce two adjacent red nodes. Thus, the algorithm completely bypasses the need to check or fix red-red parenting violations at the leaf level.
    
    \item \textbf{Trivial Insertion Under Red Nodes:}
    If the parent of the newly inserted node happens to be Red, inserting a Black child is completely free of violations. Property 4 is satisfied, and because the parent is red, its other sibling branch must already have an equivalent black-height, meaning the local black-height properties often require simpler, localized adjustments.
\end{itemize}

---

\subsection*{2. Disadvantages of Mr. X's Algorithm}

The disadvantages of Mr. X's algorithm heavily outweigh the benefits, primarily because it attacks the most rigid constraint of a Red-Black Tree: the Black-Height Property.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Immediate and Constant Violation of the Black-Height Property (Property 5):}
    Property 5 states that every simple path from a node to its descendant leaves must contain the exact same number of black nodes. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item In the \textbf{traditional algorithm} (default Red), adding a red node adds $0$ to the black-height of that path. Thus, Property 5 is \textbf{never violated upon insertion}. The tree only has to fix local red-red violations.
        \item In \textbf{Mr. X's algorithm} (default Black), adding a black node instantly increases the black-height of that specific path by $1$, while all sibling paths remain unchanged. Therefore, \textbf{every single insertion unconditionally violates Property 5}.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{High Repair Complexity (Global vs. Local Fixes):}
    Fixing a red-red violation (traditional) is an isolated, local event that can often be resolved via a single rotation or a quick recoloring of immediate uncles/parents. Conversely, fixing a black-height violation (Mr. X's proposal) is much more severe. It behaves exactly like the dreaded \textbf{Double-Black underflow problem encountered during deletion}. Resolving it requires propagating black-height adjustments up toward the root, leading to significantly higher frequencies of structural rebalancing and pointer manipulations.

    \item \textbf{Frequent Tree Compaction (Root Level Growth):}
    Because adding a black node forces paths to become heavier, balancing the tree requires either pulling black nodes down (which is difficult without violating other paths) or pushing the extra black properties upward until they reach the root. This causes the tree's overall black-height to increase much faster than necessary, leading to a tighter, more compressed structure that requires excessive restructuring operations.
\end{itemize}

---

\subsection*{3. Summary Comparison Table}

To summarize, we can compare the behavioral invariants of both strategies immediately after a node is physically linked to the tree, but before any balancing loops execute:

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{|l|p{5.5cm}|p{5.5cm}|}
\hline
\textbf{Metric / Property} & \textbf{Traditional Insertion (Default Red)} & \textbf{Mr. X's Insertion (Default Black)} \\ \hline
\textbf{Property 4 (No Red-Red)} & May be violated (if parent is Red). Easily fixed locally. & \textbf{Guaranteed to be satisfied}. No red-red violations possible. \\ \hline
\textbf{Property 5 (Black-Height)} & \textbf{Guaranteed to be satisfied}. The path's black count does not change. & Symmetrically broken. The insertion path is instantly heavier than its sibling paths. \\ \hline
\textbf{Fixup Paradigm} & Localized adjustments handling color collisions. & Complex structural propagation similar to handling deletion underflows. \\ \hline
\textbf{Overall Efficiency} & \textbf{Highly Optimal.} Average case requires $\mathcal{O}(1)$ recolorings and rare rotations. & \textbf{Inefficient.} Requires heavy, persistent top-down rebalancing routines. \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Conclusion}
Mr. X's algorithm is \textbf{not recommended}. Swapping a temporary red-red violation for an immediate black-height violation replaces an easily patchable local color issue with a fundamental structural deficit, making the tree significantly more expensive to maintain.

\end{tcolorbox}


%% Q — 2021-22
\item Insert 11 into the Red-Black tree shown in the figure. You have to show the intermediate steps. The red and black nodes are indicated by `r' and `b', respectively.
\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
        every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\bfseries, inner sep=0pt},
        % Red node styling (Red background with white text)
        red node/.style={fill=red, text=white, draw=red!70!black},
        % Black node styling (Black background with white text)
        black node/.style={fill=black, text=white, draw=black},
        edge/.style={thick, black}
    ]

        % ==========================================
        % TREE STRUCTURE
        % ==========================================
        % Level 0: Root
        \node[black node] (n23) at (0,0) {23};

        % Level 1
        \node[red node] (n8) at (-1.8,-1.2) {8};
        \node[black node] (n27) at (1.8,-1.2) {27};

        % Level 2
        \node[black node] (n6) at (-2.8,-2.4) {6};
        \node[black node] (n15) at (-0.8,-2.4) {15};
        \node[red node] (n29) at (2.8,-2.4) {29};

        % Level 3
        \node[red node] (n13) at (-1.5,-3.6) {13};
        \node[red node] (n19) at (-0.1,-3.6) {19};

        % ==========================================
        % EDGES / CONNECTIONS
        % ==========================================
        \draw[edge] (n23) -- (n8);
        \draw[edge] (n23) -- (n27);

        \draw[edge] (n8) -- (n6);
        \draw[edge] (n8) -- (n15);
        \draw[edge] (n27) -- (n29);

        \draw[edge] (n15) -- (n13);
        \draw[edge] (n15) -- (n19);

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Red-Black Tree Insertion of Key 11}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

Here are the intermediate steps to insert the key \textbf{11} into the given Red-Black tree.

\tikzset{
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.65cm, font=\bfseries\small, inner sep=0pt},
    r/.style={treenode, fill=red, text=white, draw=red!70!black},
    b/.style={treenode, fill=black, text=white, draw=black},
    level distance=1.2cm,
    edge from parent/.style={draw=black, thick}
}

\subsection*{Step 1: Standard BST Insertion}
We insert $11$ following standard Binary Search Tree rules ($11 < 23 \rightarrow$ left, $11 > 8 \rightarrow$ right, $11 < 15 \rightarrow$ left, $11 < 13 \rightarrow$ left). By default, the newly inserted node is colored \textbf{Red}. 

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1cm}
]
    \node[b] {23}
        child {node[r] {8}
            child {node[b] {6}}
            child {node[b] {15}
                child {node[r] {13}
                    child {node[r] {11}} % Violation here
                    child[missing] {}
                }
                child {node[r] {19}}
            }
        }
        child {node[b] {27}
            child[missing] {}
            child {node[r] {29}}
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\textbf{Violation:} There is a Red-Red conflict between $13$ and $11$.

---

\subsection*{Step 2: Case 1 Recoloring (Red Uncle)}
Looking at node $11$, its parent ($13$) is Red, and its uncle ($19$) is also \textbf{Red}.
\begin{itemize}
    \item Recolor the parent ($13$) and uncle ($19$) to \textbf{Black}.
    \item Recolor the grandparent ($15$) to \textbf{Red}.
    \item Move the inspection pointer up to the grandparent ($15$).
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1cm}
]
    \node[b] {23}
        child {node[r] {8}
            child {node[b] {6}}
            child {node[r] {15} % Violation here
                child {node[b] {13}
                    child {node[r] {11}}
                    child[missing] {}
                }
                child {node[b] {19}}
            }
        }
        child {node[b] {27}
            child[missing] {}
            child {node[r] {29}}
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\textbf{Violation:} A new Red-Red conflict has emerged between $8$ and $15$.

---

\subsection*{Step 3: Case 2 Left Rotation (Black Uncle, Triangle Shape)}
Now examining node $15$: its parent ($8$) is Red, but its uncle ($27$) is \textbf{Black}. Node $15$ forms a "Right" child to a "Left" child parent (a zig-zag shape).
\begin{itemize}
    \item Perform a \textbf{Left Rotation} on the parent ($8$).
    \item Node $15$ moves up, $8$ becomes its left child, and $13$ shifts over to become the right child of $8$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm}
]
    \node[b] {23}
        child {node[r] {15} % Still a violation
            child {node[r] {8}
                child {node[b] {6}}
                child {node[b] {13}
                    child {node[r] {11}}
                    child[missing] {}
                }
            }
            child {node[b] {19}}
        }
        child {node[b] {27}
            child[missing] {}
            child {node[r] {29}}
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\textbf{Note:} The Red-Red violation remains between $15$ and $8$, but they now form a straight line, setting up Case 3.

---

\subsection*{Step 4: Case 3 Right Rotation \& Recolor (Black Uncle, Line Shape)}
Examining node $8$: it forms a straight "Left-Left" line with its parent ($15$) and grandparent ($23$).
\begin{itemize}
    \item Perform a \textbf{Right Rotation} on the grandparent ($23$).
    \item Swap the colors of the new root of this subtree ($15$) and the old root ($23$). Node $15$ becomes \textbf{Black}, and $23$ becomes \textbf{Red}.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1cm}
]
    \node[b] {15}
        child {node[r] {8}
            child {node[b] {6}}
            child {node[b] {13}
                child {node[r] {11}}
                child[missing] {}
            }
        }
        child {node[r] {23}
            child {node[b] {19}}
            child {node[b] {27}
                child[missing] {}
                child {node[r] {29}}
            }
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\textbf{Final State:} All Red-Black tree properties are now fully restored and valid.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2020-21
\item When we insert a node into a red-black tree, we initially set the color of the new node to red. Why don't we choose to set the color to black?
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Why New Nodes are Colored Red}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

When inserting a new node into a Red-Black tree, we must choose to color it either Red or Black. The decision to always default to \textbf{Red} is strategically based on choosing the "lesser of two evils" regarding tree property violations.

\subsection*{1. The Consequence of Inserting a Red Node}
If we color the newly inserted node \textbf{Red}:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Black-Height is Preserved:} The number of black nodes on the path to the newly inserted leaf remains completely unchanged. Therefore, \textbf{Property 5} (every path must have the same number of black nodes) is strictly satisfied. This is the most rigid and difficult property to maintain in the tree.
    \item \textbf{Potential Red-Red Violation:} The only property we might violate is \textbf{Property 4} (no consecutive red nodes). This only occurs if the newly inserted node's parent is also Red.
    \item \textbf{Simple, Localized Fix:} A Red-Red violation is a \textit{strictly local} conflict. It can be resolved quickly by examining the immediate neighborhood (specifically, the uncle node) and performing a simple color flip or at most two structural rotations.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{2. The Consequence of Inserting a Black Node}
If we were to color the newly inserted node \textbf{Black}:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{No Red-Red Conflicts:} We would never violate Property 4, as a black node can safely be a child of either a red or black parent.
    \item \textbf{Immediate Black-Height Violation:} Adding a black node instantly increases the black-height of that specific downward path by $1$, while all other sibling paths in the tree remain untouched. This \textbf{unconditionally violates Property 5} on every single insertion.
    \item \textbf{Complex, Global Fix:} Fixing an unequal black-height is a \textit{global} structural issue. It forces the algorithm to push the "extra black" weight up the tree level by level until it can be distributed evenly. This fundamentally mimics the complex, multi-case "Double-Black" fixup routine required during node deletion, making insertions drastically more expensive.
\end{itemize}

\begin{tcolorbox}[colback=red!5,colframe=red!60!black,title=\textbf{Summary}]
We set the initial color to \textbf{Red} because fixing a localized color conflict (a Red-Red violation) is computationally and structurally much cheaper than rebalancing the global black-height of the entire tree on every single insertion.
\end{tcolorbox}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item Would inserting a new node to a red-black tree and then immediately deleting it change the tree? Explain with an example.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Effect of Immediate Insertion and Deletion in a Red-Black Tree}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Yes}, inserting a new node into a Red-Black tree and then immediately deleting it \textbf{can permanently change the tree}. It can alter both the color configuration of the nodes and the actual structural topology (the shape and pointer connections) of the tree.

\subsection*{Why Does This Happen?}
This structural asymmetry occurs because the insertion and deletion algorithms in a Red-Black tree operate under completely distinct sets of balancing rules:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Insertion} fixes \textit{Red-Red violations} (consecutive red nodes) using localized color flips or rotations determined by the color of the node's \textbf{uncle}.
    \item \textbf{Deletion} fixes \textit{Black-Height deficits} (double-black nodes) using structural rebalancing determined by the properties of the node's \textbf{sibling} and \textbf{nephews}.
\end{itemize}
Because deletion is not a mathematical "inverse" or a simple "undo" function of insertion, the structural modifications triggered during insertion are frequently left intact or transformed into an entirely new state during deletion.

---

\subsection*{Illustrative Example}

Let us demonstrate a case where both the \textbf{structure} and \textbf{colors} of the tree are permanently altered.

\tikzset{
    treenode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.65cm, font=\bfseries\small, inner sep=0pt},
    r/.style={treenode, fill=red, text=white, draw=red!70!black},
    b/.style={treenode, fill=black, text=white, draw=black},
    level distance=1.0cm,
    sibling distance=1.8cm,
    edge from parent/.style={draw=black, thick}
}

\subsubsection*{1. Initial Valid Red-Black Tree}
Consider this initial, perfectly balanced Red-Black tree with a black-height of 2:
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
    \node[b] {20}
        child {node[b] {10}
            child {node[r] {5}}
            child[missing] {}
        }
        child {node[b] {30}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{2. Step A: Insert Key 2}
We insert the key \textbf{2} as a Red node according to standard BST rules. It becomes the left child of node $5$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item This creates an immediate \textbf{Red-Red violation} between node $5$ and node $2$.
    \item The uncle of node $2$ (the right child of $10$) is a black \texttt{NIL} leaf. 
    \item Since nodes $10 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 2$ form a straight "Left-Left" line, we trigger a \textbf{Right Rotation around node 10} and swap the colors of the parent ($5$) and grandparent ($10$).
\end{itemize}

Following this insertion and rebalancing, the tree changes to:
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
    \node[b] {20}
        child {node[b] {5}
            child {node[r] {2}}
            child {node[r] {10}}
        }
        child {node[b] {30}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{3. Step B: Immediately Delete Key 2}
Now, we immediately perform a deletion on the newly inserted key \textbf{2}.
\begin{itemize}
    \item Node $2$ is currently a \textbf{Red leaf node}.
    \item Under standard Red-Black deletion rules, removing a Red leaf node is trivial: it is simply unlinked from the tree. Because it contains no black property weight, its removal does \textbf{not} cause a black-height deficit along that path. 
    \item As a result, \textbf{no further structural fixups or rotations are performed}.
\end{itemize}

---

\subsection*{Final State vs. Initial State}
After deleting key $2$, the final configuration of the tree is:
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
    \node[b] {20}
        child {node[b] {5}
            child[missing] {}
            child {node[r] {10}}
        }
        child {node[b] {30}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\begin{center}
\begin{small}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Feature} & \textbf{Initial Tree Configuration} & \textbf{Final Tree Configuration (After Insert + Delete)} \\ \hline
\textbf{Left Child of 20} & Node \textbf{10} (Colored Black) & Node \textbf{5} (Colored Black) \\ \hline
\textbf{Subtree Layout} & Node $5$ was the \textit{left} child of node $10$ & Node $10$ is now the \textit{right} child of node $5$ \\ \hline
\textbf{Color of Node 10} & \textbf{Black} & \textbf{Red} \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{small}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\textbf{Conclusion:} The example clearly proves that an immediate insertion-deletion sequence does not act as a net-zero operation; the final tree is structurally and colorwise distinct from the original tree.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item What is the ratio between the lengths of the longest path and the shortest path in a red-black tree?
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Ratio of Longest to Shortest Path in a Red-Black Tree}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

The ratio between the length of the longest path and the shortest path from the root to any descendant leaf in a Red-Black tree is \textbf{at most 2}. 

Below is the formal mathematical derivation based on the structural invariants of Red-Black trees.

\subsection*{1. Defining the Invariants}
Let $bh$ represent the \textbf{black-height} of the tree, which is defined as the number of black nodes on any simple path from a node down to a leaf. According to the \textbf{Black-Height Property (Property 5)}, this value is strictly uniform for all paths originating from the same node.

\subsection*{2. Bounding the Path Lengths}

\subsubsection*{The Shortest Possible Path}
To minimize the total length of a path, it should contain the absolute minimum number of red nodes possible ($0$). A path consisting entirely of black nodes will match the black-height exactly:
\begin{equation*}
    \text{Shortest Path Length} = bh
\end{equation*}

\subsubsection*{The Longest Possible Path}
To maximize the total length of a path, we must intersperse as many red nodes into the path as allowed. However, the \textbf{Red-Node Property (Property 4)} strictly dictates that a red node cannot have a red child (no consecutive red nodes). 

Therefore, to maximize length, red nodes must perfectly alternate with black nodes (e.g., Black $\rightarrow$ Red $\rightarrow$ Black $\rightarrow$ Red). Since the path is mathematically bound to contain exactly $bh$ black nodes to satisfy Property 5, it can accommodate at most $bh$ red nodes between them.
\begin{equation*}
    \text{Longest Path Length} = bh \text{ (black nodes)} + bh \text{ (red nodes)} = 2bh
\end{equation*}

\subsection*{3. Deriving the Ratio}
Taking the ratio of the maximum possible path length to the minimum possible path length yields:
\begin{align*}
    \text{Maximum Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Longest Path Length}}{\text{Shortest Path Length}} \\
    &= \frac{2bh}{bh} \\
    &= 2
\end{align*}

\begin{tcolorbox}[colback=green!5,colframe=green!60!black,title=\textbf{Conclusion}]
The longest path in a Red-Black tree can never be more than \textbf{twice as long} as the shortest path:
\begin{equation*}
    h_{\text{longest}} \le 2 \cdot h_{\text{shortest}}
\end{equation*}
This strict geometric constraint ensures that the tree remains approximately balanced at all times, guaranteeing that search, insertion, and deletion operations maintain an upper bound of $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ performance.
\end{tcolorbox}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Insert 11 into the given Red-Black tree, showing each operation. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
  B/.style={fill=black, text=white},
  R/.style={fill=red, text=white},
]
  \node[B] (23) at (4,0)   {23};
  \node[R] (8)  at (2,-2)  {8};
  \node[B] (27) at (6,-2)  {27};
  \node[B] (6)  at (0,-4)  {6};
  \node[B] (15) at (3.5,-4){15};
  \node[R] (29) at (7,-4)  {29};
  \node[R] (13) at (2.5,-6){13};
  \node[R] (19) at (4.5,-6){19};

  \draw (23)--(8); \draw (23)--(27);
  \draw (8)--(6);  \draw (8)--(15);
  \draw (27)--(29);
  \draw (15)--(13); \draw (15)--(19);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Step-by-Step Insertion of Key 11 (15 Marks)}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

Here is the complete, step-by-step execution to insert key \textbf{11} into the given Red-Black Tree, conforming to standard academic evaluation standards.

\tikzset{
    treenode/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    B/.style={treenode, fill=black, text=white},
    R/.style={treenode, fill=red, text=white},
    level distance=1.4cm,
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick}
}

\subsection*{Step 1: Standard BST Insertion}
We locate the appropriate insertion point using standard BST search properties ($11 < 23 \rightarrow$ Left, $11 > 8 \rightarrow$ Right, $11 < 15 \rightarrow$ Left, $11 < 13 \rightarrow$ Left). The new node is initially colored \textbf{Red}.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.6cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm}
]
    \node[B] {23}
        child {node[R] {8}
            child {node[B] {6}}
            child {node[B] {15}
                child {node[R] {13}
                    child {node[R] {11}}
                    child[missing] {}
                }
                child {node[R] {19}}
            }
        }
        child {node[B] {27}
            child[missing] {}
            child {node[R] {29}}
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\textbf{Violation Identified:} A consecutive \textbf{Red-Red violation} occurs between parent node $\mathbf{13}$ and child node $\mathbf{11}$.

---

\subsection*{Step 2: Case 1 Fixup (Red Uncle Node)}
We examine the neighborhood surrounding our violation node ($\mathbf{11}$):
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Parent:} $13$ (Red) $\quad\bullet\quad$ \textbf{Grandparent:} $15$ (Black) $\quad\bullet\quad$ \textbf{Uncle:} $19$ (\textbf{Red})
\end{itemize}
Since the uncle node is \textbf{Red}, we apply \textbf{Case 1 (Recoloring)}:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Flip the color of both the Parent ($13$) and Uncle ($19$) to \textbf{Black}.
    \item Flip the color of the Grandparent ($15$) to \textbf{Red}.
    \item Shift the inspection pointer up to the grandparent node ($15$).
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.6cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm}
]
    \node[B] {23}
        child {node[R] {8}
            child {node[B] {6}}
            child {node[R] {15}
                child {node[B] {13}
                    child {node[R] {11}}
                    child[missing] {}
                }
                child {node[B] {19}}
            }
        }
        child {node[B] {27}
            child[missing] {}
            child {node[R] {29}}
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\textbf{Violation Identified:} Shifting the red color upward has generated a new \textbf{Red-Red violation} higher up between node $\mathbf{8}$ and node $\mathbf{15}$.

---

\subsection*{Step 3: Case 2 Fixup (Black Uncle, Triangle Configuration)}
We re-evaluate the neighborhood properties from our new inspection node ($\mathbf{15}$):
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Parent:} $8$ (Red) $\quad\bullet\quad$ \textbf{Grandparent:} $23$ (Black) $\quad\bullet\quad$ \textbf{Uncle:} $27$ (\textbf{Black})
\end{itemize}
Because the uncle node is black and node $15$ forms a Right-child relationship to a Left-child parent (forming a zig-zag triangle shape), we apply \textbf{Case 2}:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Execute a \textbf{Left Rotation} around the parent node ($\mathbf{8}$).
    \item Node $15$ rises up, node $8$ becomes its left child, and node $15$'s former left subtree (node $13$) gets reassigned as the right child of node $8$.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.4cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm}
]
    \node[B] {23}
        child {node[R] {15}
            child {node[R] {8}
                child {node[B] {6}}
                child {node[B] {13}
                    child {node[R] {11}}
                    child[missing] {}
                }
            }
            child {node[B] {19}}
        }
        child {node[B] {27}
            child[missing] {}
            child {node[R] {29}}
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\textbf{Note:} The Red-Red violation remains between node $15$ and node $8$, but they are now aligned in a straight line, setting up the terminal case.

---

\subsection*{Step 4: Case 3 Fixup (Black Uncle, Line Configuration)}
Evaluating node $\mathbf{8}$: it forms a straight, Left-Left linear layout with its parent ($15$) and grandparent ($23$). The uncle node ($27$) remains black. We apply \textbf{Case 3}:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Execute a \textbf{Right Rotation} around the grandparent node ($\mathbf{23}$).
    \item Swap the colors of the parent node ($15$) and the old grandparent node ($23$). Thus, node $15$ becomes \textbf{Black} and node $23$ becomes \textbf{Red}.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4.2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm}
]
    \node[B] {15}
        child {node[R] {8}
            child {node[B] {6}}
            child {node[B] {13}
                child {node[R] {11}}
                child[missing] {}
            }
        }
        child {node[R] {23}
            child {node[B] {19}}
            child {node[B] {27}
                child[missing] {}
                child {node[R] {29}}
            }
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\textbf{Final State Verification:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Root node ($15$) is Black $\rightarrow$ \textbf{Valid}.
    \item No consecutive red nodes exist anywhere in the tree $\rightarrow$ \textbf{Valid}.
    \item Every distinct path from the root down to a \texttt{NIL} leaf contains exactly 3 black nodes $\rightarrow$ \textbf{Valid}.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write the properties of a red-black tree. Show that the height of a red-black tree $T$ storing $n$ items is $O(\log n)$. Explain, with examples, the three cases that may arise for inserting a node in a red-black tree. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2015--16, 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Red-Black Tree Invariants, Height Proof, and Insertion Cases (15 Marks)}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsection*{1. Properties of a Red-Black Tree}
A Red-Black tree is a binary search tree where each node contains an extra attribute representing its color (\textbf{Red} or \textbf{Black}). A binary search tree is a valid Red-Black tree if it satisfies the following five structural properties:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Node Property:} Every node is colored either Red or Black.
    \item \textbf{Root Property:} The root of the tree is always Black.
    \item \textbf{Leaf Property:} Every leaf node (\texttt{NIL} boundary marker) is Black.
    \item \textbf{Red Property:} If a node is Red, then both of its children must be Black. (Consecutive Red nodes are strictly prohibited).
    \item \textbf{Black-Height Property:} For each node, all simple downward paths from that node to any of its descendant \texttt{NIL} leaves contain the exact same number of black nodes.
\end{enumerate}

---

\subsection*{2. Proof: Height of a Red-Black Tree is $O(\log n)$}

To prove that a Red-Black tree with $n$ internal nodes has a maximum height $h \leq 2 \log_2(n+1)$, we introduce the concept of \textbf{black-height} ($bh(x)$), defined as the number of black nodes on any simple path from node $x$ down to a leaf, not counting $x$ itself.

\subsubsection*{Lemma}
\textit{The subtree rooted at any node $x$ contains at least $2^{bh(x)} - 1$ internal nodes.}

\begin{proof}[Proof by Induction]
\textbf{Base Case:} If the height of node $x$ is 0, then $x$ must be a \texttt{NIL} leaf. Its black-height is $bh(x) = 0$. The number of internal nodes in its subtree is $2^0 - 1 = 0$, which is true.

\textbf{Inductive Step:} Let $x$ be an internal node of height $> 0$ with two children. Each child has a black-height of either:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $bh(x)$ (if the child node itself is colored Red)
    \item $bh(x) - 1$ (if the child node itself is colored Black)
\end{itemize}
In either scenario, the black-height of any child node is at least $bh(x) - 1$. Applying the inductive hypothesis, each child's subtree contains a minimum of $2^{bh(x)-1} - 1$ internal nodes. 

Summing the internal nodes contributed by both children plus node $x$ itself yields:
\begin{align*}
    \text{Total internal nodes} &\geq (2^{bh(x)-1} - 1) + (2^{bh(x)-1} - 1) + 1 \\
    &= 2 \cdot (2^{bh(x)-1}) - 2 + 1 \\
    &= 2^{bh(x)} - 1
\end{align*}
This completes the inductive proof for the lemma.
\end{proof}

\subsubsection*{Bounding Height ($h$)}
Let the root of the entire tree be $T$. According to \textbf{Property 4}, no two red nodes can be adjacent along any root-to-leaf path. This implies that at least half of the nodes on any simple path (excluding the root) must be black. Therefore, the black-height of the root must satisfy:
\begin{equation*}
    bh(T) \geq \frac{h}{2}
\end{equation*}
Using our proven lemma, the total number of internal nodes $n$ in the tree satisfies:
\begin{align*}
    n \geq 2^{bh(T)} - 1 &\implies n \geq 2^{h/2} - 1 \\
    &\implies n + 1 \geq 2^{h/2} \\
    &\implies \log_2(n + 1) \geq \frac{h}{2} \\
    &\implies h \leq 2 \log_2(n + 1)
\end{align*}
Since $h \leq 2 \log_2(n + 1)$, we conclude that the structural height of the tree is asymptotically bounded:
\begin{equation*}
    h = O(\log n)
\end{equation*}

---

\pagebreak
\subsection*{3. Three Cases for Node Insertion (With Examples)}
When a new node $z$ is inserted, it is always inserted as a binary search tree leaf and colored \textbf{Red}. A conflict occurs only if $z$'s parent, $P(z)$, is also \textbf{Red}, violating Property 4. 

Let $G(z)$ represent $z$'s grandparent, and $U(z)$ represent $z$'s uncle. Assuming $P(z)$ is a left child of $G(z)$ (the right-side cases are entirely symmetric), three resolution configurations can arise:

\tikzset{
    B/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=black, text=white, minimum size=0.65cm, font=\bfseries\small, thick},
    R/.style={circle, draw=red!70!black, fill=red, text=white, minimum size=0.65cm, font=\bfseries\small, thick},
    level distance=1.1cm, sibling distance=1.4cm,
    edge from parent/.style={draw=black, thick}
}

\subsubsection*{Case 1: The Uncle $U(z)$ is Red}
\textbf{Condition:} The parent $P(z)$ and uncle $U(z)$ are both Red.
\\\textbf{Action:} Recolor $P(z)$ and $U(z)$ to \textbf{Black}, and recolor $G(z)$ to \textbf{Red}. Then, shift the inspection pointer up to $G(z)$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\textbf{Before Transformation} & & \textbf{After Case 1 Fix} \\
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[B] {G}
        child {node[R] {P} child {node[R] {z}} child[missing]}
        child {node[R] {U}};
\end{tikzpicture}
& \quad$\Longrightarrow$\quad &
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[R] {G}
        child {node[B] {P} child {node[R] {z}} child[missing]}
        child {node[B] {U}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Case 2: The Uncle $U(z)$ is Black, and $z$ forms a Triangle (Zig-Zag)}
\textbf{Condition:} $U(z)$ is Black, and $z$ is a right child while $P(z)$ is a left child.
\\\textbf{Action:} Perform a \textbf{Left Rotation} around the parent node $P(z)$. This shifts $z$ into the parent position, reducing the configuration directly to Case 3.

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\textbf{Before Transformation} & & \textbf{After Left Rotation (Becomes Case 3)} \\
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[B] {G}
        child {node[R] {P} child[missing] child {node[R] {z}}}
        child {node[B] {U}};
\end{tikzpicture}
& \quad$\Longrightarrow$\quad &
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[B] {G}
        child {node[R] {z} child {node[R] {P}} child[missing]}
        child {node[B] {U}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Case 3: The Uncle $U(z)$ is Black, and $z$ forms a Line (Zig-Zig)}
\textbf{Condition:} $U(z)$ is Black, and $z$ is a left child while $P(z)$ is a left child.
\\\textbf{Action:} Perform a \textbf{Right Rotation} around the grandparent node $G(z)$, and swap the colors of $P(z)$ and $G(z)$ ($P(z)$ becomes Black, $G(z)$ becomes Red).

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\textbf{Before Transformation} & & \textbf{After Right Rotation \& Recolor} \\
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[B] {G}
        child {node[R] {P} child {node[R] {z}} child[missing]}
        child {node[B] {U}};
\end{tikzpicture}
& \quad$\Longrightarrow$\quad &
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[B] {P}
        child {node[R] {z}}
        child {node[R] {G} child[missing] child {node[B] {U}}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Explain, with examples, the four cases that may arise for deleting a node from a red-black tree. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Comprehensive Analysis of Red-Black Tree Deletion Cases (15 Marks)}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

In a Red-Black tree, standard Binary Search Tree (BST) deletion always reduces to removing a node with at most one internal child. Let the node physically unlinked from the tree be $y$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $y$ is \textbf{Red}, its removal causes no structural violations.
    \item If $y$ is \textbf{Black}, it disrupts the tree's balance by decreasing the black-height of all paths passing through its position by 1. 
\end{itemize}

To track this deficit, we transfer the "missing black" property to $y$'s unique child (or a \texttt{NIL} leaf placeholder), making it **Double-Black** ($x$). To restore balance, we examine the color configurations of $x$'s parent ($P$), sibling ($w$), and $w$'s children (nephews). 

Assuming $x$ is a \textbf{left child} (the right-child scenarios are completely symmetric), four distinct cases can arise during the fixup loop.

\tikzset{
    B/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=black, text=white, minimum size=0.65cm, font=\bfseries\small, thick},
    R/.style={circle, draw=red!70!black, fill=red, text=white, minimum size=0.65cm, font=\bfseries\small, thick},
    DB/.style={circle, draw=black, double=black, double distance=1.5pt, fill=black, text=white, minimum size=0.65cm, font=\bfseries\small, thick},
    Any/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=gray!20, text=black, minimum size=0.65cm, font=\bfseries\small, thick},
    level distance=1.1cm, sibling distance=1.3cm,
    edge from parent/.style={draw=black, thick}
}

---

\subsection*{Case 1: Sibling $w$ is Red}
\textbf{Condition:} The sibling $w$ is Red. Because $w$ is Red, Property 4 dictates that the parent $P$ and $w$'s children must all be Black.
\\\textbf{Action:} 
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Swap the colors of the parent $P$ and sibling $w$ ($P$ becomes Red, $w$ becomes Black).
    \item Perform a \textbf{Left Rotation} around the parent $P$.
    \item Identify the new sibling of $x$. This updates the local environment to match Case 2, 3, or 4.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\textbf{Before Transformation} & & \textbf{After Case 1 Fix} \\
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[B] {P}
        child {node[DB] {x}}
        child {node[R] {w}
            child {node[B] {$c_L$}}
            child {node[B] {$c_R$}}
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
& \quad$\Longrightarrow$\quad &
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[B] {w}
        child {node[R] {P}
            child {node[DB] {x}}
            child {node[B] {$c_L$}}
        }
        child {node[B] {$c_R$}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

---

\subsection*{Case 2: Sibling $w$ is Black, and Both of $w$'s Children are Black}
\textbf{Condition:} The sibling $w$ is Black, and its left and right children are both Black (\texttt{NIL} or internal).
\\\textbf{Action:} 
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Pull one unit of "blackness" from both $x$ (reducing it from Double-Black to singly Black) and $w$ (recoloring $w$ to \textbf{Red}).
    \item Push the extra black property onto the parent $P$. 
    \item If $P$ was originally Red, it absorbs the weight and transitions to a standard Black node, terminating the algorithm. If $P$ was already Black, it becomes the new Double-Black node ($x$), and the loop repeats one level higher.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\textbf{Before Transformation} & & \textbf{After Case 2 Fix} \\
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[Any] {P}
        child {node[DB] {x}}
        child {node[B] {w}
            child {node[B] {$c_L$}}
            child {node[B] {$c_R$}}
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
& \quad$\Longrightarrow$\quad &
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[Any] {P$^+$} % Indicates gained black
        child {node[B] {x}}
        child {node[R] {w}
            child {node[B] {$c_L$}}
            child {node[B] {$c_R$}}
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

---

\subsection*{Case 3: Sibling $w$ is Black, $w$'s Left Child is Red, and $w$'s Right Child is Black}
\textbf{Condition:} Sibling $w$ is Black, its inner nephew ($c_L$) is Red, and its outer nephew ($c_R$) is Black (a structural zig-zag pattern).
\\\textbf{Action:} 
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Swap the colors of sibling $w$ and its left child $c_L$ ($w$ becomes Red, $c_L$ becomes Black).
    \item Perform a \textbf{Right Rotation} around the sibling node $w$.
    \item Reassign the sibling pointer to the new black node ($c_L$). This transitions the setup directly into Case 4.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\textbf{Before Transformation} & & \textbf{After Right Rotation (Becomes Case 4)} \\
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[Any] {P}
        child {node[DB] {x}}
        child {node[B] {w}
            child {node[R] {$c_L$}}
            child {node[B] {$c_R$}}
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
& \quad$\Longrightarrow$\quad &
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[Any] {P}
        child {node[DB] {x}}
        child {node[B] {$c_L$}
            child[missing] {}
            child {node[R] {w}
                child[missing] {}
                child {node[B] {$c_R$}}
            }
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

---

\subsection*{Case 4: Sibling $w$ is Black, and $w$'s Right Child is Red}
\textbf{Condition:} Sibling $w$ is Black, and its outer nephew ($c_R$) is Red. The color of the inner nephew ($c_L$) is completely irrelevant.
\\\textbf{Action:} 
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Recolor sibling $w$ to match the original color of the parent node $P$.
    \item Recolor both the parent node $P$ and the outer nephew $c_R$ to \textbf{Black}.
    \item Perform a \textbf{Left Rotation} around the parent node $P$.
    \item The extra black attribute on $x$ is neutralized. This step completely fixes the path deficits and terminates the algorithm.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\textbf{Before Transformation} & & \textbf{After Case 4 Fix (Terminal)} \\
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[Any] {P}
        child {node[DB] {x}}
        child {node[B] {w}
            child {node[Any] {$c_L$}}
            child {node[R] {$c_R$}}
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
& \quad$\Longrightarrow$\quad &
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[Any] {w} % Color matches old P
        child {node[B] {P}
            child {node[B] {x}}
            child {node[Any] {$c_L$}}
        }
        child {node[B] {$c_R$}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write the properties of a red-black tree. Show the red-black trees that result after successively inserting the keys $51, 46, 41, 22, 29, 18$ into an initially empty red-black tree. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2013--14}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\tikzset{
  treenode/.style = {circle, draw=black, align=center, inner sep=0pt, text centered, font=\sffamily\bfseries, minimum size=24pt},
  R/.style = {treenode, text=white, fill=red!80!black, draw=red!90!black, thick},
  B/.style = {treenode, text=white, fill=black!80, draw=black, thick},
  edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, -stealth}
}

\section*{Part 1: Insertion of 11}

\textbf{Step 1: Standard BST Insertion} \\
We insert 11 following the standard Binary Search Tree properties: $11 < 23$ (go left), $11 > 8$ (go right), $11 < 15$ (go left), $11 < 13$ (go left). The new node 11 is inserted as the left child of 13 and is initially colored \textcolor{red}{\textbf{RED}}. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Violation:} Node 13 is \textcolor{red}{\textbf{RED}} and Node 11 is \textcolor{red}{\textbf{RED}} (Adjacent Red nodes).
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level 1/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm, level distance=1.2cm}]
  \node [B] {23}
    child {node [R] {8}
      child {node [B] {6}}
      child {node [B] {15}
        child {node [R] {13}
          child {node [R, draw=blue, very thick] {11}} % Inserted node
          child[missing] {}
        }
        child {node [R] {19}}
      }
    }
    child {node [B] {27}
      child[missing] {}
      child {node [R] {29}}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 2: Fix Violation (Case 1 -- Uncle is RED)} \\
The parent of 11 is 13 (\textcolor{red}{\textbf{RED}}). The grandparent is 15 (\textbf{BLACK}). The uncle is 19 (\textcolor{red}{\textbf{RED}}). 
Since the uncle is \textcolor{red}{\textbf{RED}}, we apply \textbf{Case 1}:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Recolor the parent (13) and uncle (19) to \textbf{BLACK}.
    \item Recolor the grandparent (15) to \textcolor{red}{\textbf{RED}}.
    \item The current node moves up to the grandparent (15) to check for new violations.
\end{itemize}
\textbf{New Violation:} Node 8 is \textcolor{red}{\textbf{RED}} and Node 15 is \textcolor{red}{\textbf{RED}}.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level 1/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm, level distance=1.2cm}]
  \node [B] {23}
    child {node [R] {8}
      child {node [B] {6}}
      child {node [R, draw=blue, very thick] {15}
        child {node [B] {13}
          child {node [R] {11}}
          child[missing] {}
        }
        child {node [B] {19}}
      }
    }
    child {node [B] {27}
      child[missing] {}
      child {node [R] {29}}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 3: Fix Violation (Case 2 -- Uncle is BLACK, Left-Right Case)} \\
For the current node 15, its parent is 8 (\textcolor{red}{\textbf{RED}}), grandparent is 23 (\textbf{BLACK}), and uncle is 27 (\textbf{BLACK}). 
Node 15 is a right child, and its parent 8 is a left child. We apply \textbf{Case 2}:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Perform a \textbf{Left Rotation} around the parent (8). 
    \item Node 15 moves up, and Node 8 becomes its left child. The left child of 15 (Node 13) becomes the right child of 8.
\end{itemize}
\textbf{New Violation:} Node 15 (\textcolor{red}{\textbf{RED}}) and Node 8 (\textcolor{red}{\textbf{RED}}) are adjacent.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level 1/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm, level distance=1.2cm}]
  \node [B] {23}
    child {node [R] {15}
      child {node [R, draw=blue, very thick] {8}
        child {node [B] {6}}
        child {node [B] {13}
          child {node [R] {11}}
          child[missing] {}
        }
      }
      child {node [B] {19}}
    }
    child {node [B] {27}
      child[missing] {}
      child {node [R] {29}}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 4: Fix Violation (Case 3 -- Uncle is BLACK, Left-Left Case) \& Final Tree} \\
Now, Node 8 is a left child, and its parent 15 is also a left child. This is \textbf{Case 3}:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Perform a \textbf{Right Rotation} around the grandparent (23). Node 15 becomes the new root.
    \item Node 23 becomes the right child of 15. The right child of 15 (Node 19) becomes the left child of 23.
    \item \textbf{Recolor:} Change the original parent (15) to \textbf{BLACK} and the original grandparent (23) to \textcolor{red}{\textbf{RED}}.
\end{itemize}
The tree now fully satisfies all Red-Black properties.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level 1/.style={sibling distance=5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm}]
  \node [B] {15}
    child {node [R] {8}
      child {node [B] {6}}
      child {node [B] {13}
        child {node [R] {11}}
        child[missing] {}
      }
    }
    child {node [R] {23}
      child {node [B] {19}}
      child {node [B] {27}
        child[missing] {}
        child {node [R] {29}}
      }
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\section*{Part 2: Properties \& Successive Insertions}

\subsection*{Properties of a Red-Black Tree}
A Red-Black Tree is a Binary Search Tree that satisfies the following five properties:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Every node is colored either \textbf{Red} or \textbf{Black}.
    \item The root of the tree is always \textbf{Black}.
    \item Every leaf (\texttt{NIL} pointer) is considered \textbf{Black}.
    \item If a node is \textbf{Red}, then both of its children must be \textbf{Black} (i.e., no two adjacent Red nodes exist on any path).
    \item For any node, all simple paths from that node to its descendant leaves contain the same number of \textbf{Black} nodes (this is called the \textit{black-height}).
\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{Successive Insertions: 51, 46, 41, 22, 29, 18}

\textbf{1. Insert 51:} Inserted as Red. Since it is the root, it is recolored to Black.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
  \node [B] {51};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{2. Insert 46:} Inserted as Red left child of 51. No violations.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level 1/.style={sibling distance=2cm, level distance=1cm}]
  \node [B] {51}
    child {node [R] {46}}
    child[missing] {};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{3. Insert 41:} Inserted as Red left child of 46. \\
\textbf{Violation:} 46 and 41 are both Red. Uncle is NIL (Black). This is a \textit{Left-Left Case}. \\
\textbf{Fix:} Right rotate around 51. Recolor 46 to Black and 51 to Red.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm, level distance=1.2cm}]
  \node [B] {46}
    child {node [R] {41}}
    child {node [R] {51}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{4. Insert 22:} Inserted as Red left child of 41. \\
\textbf{Violation:} 41 and 22 are both Red. Uncle is 51 (Red). This is \textit{Case 1 (Red Uncle)}. \\
\textbf{Fix:} Recolor parent (41) and uncle (51) to Black, and grandparent (46) to Red. Since 46 is the root, it is recolored back to Black.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm}]
  \node [B] {46}
    child {node [B] {41}
      child {node [R] {22}}
      child[missing] {}
    }
    child {node [B] {51}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{5. Insert 29:} Inserted as Red right child of 22. \\
\textbf{Violation:} 22 and 29 are both Red. Uncle is NIL (Black). This is a \textit{Left-Right Case}. \\
\textbf{Fix:} 
\begin{itemize}
    \item Left rotate around 22 to convert to Left-Left case (29 moves up, 22 becomes its left child).
    \item Right rotate around 41. 29 becomes the left child of 46, and 41 becomes the right child of 29.
    \item Recolor 29 to Black and 41 to Red.
\end{itemize}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level 1/.style={sibling distance=3.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2cm, level distance=1.2cm}]
  \node [B] {46}
    child {node [B] {29}
      child {node [R] {22}}
      child {node [R] {41}}
    }
    child {node [B] {51}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{6. Insert 18:} Inserted as Red left child of 22. \\
\textbf{Violation:} 22 and 18 are both Red. Uncle is 41 (Red). This is \textit{Case 1 (Red Uncle)}. \\
\textbf{Fix:} Recolor parent (22) and uncle (41) to Black. Recolor grandparent (29) to Red. No further violations since 29's parent (46) is Black.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
                    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm}]
  \node [B] {46}
    child {node [R] {29}
      child {node [B] {22}
        child {node [R] {18}}
        child[missing] {}
      }
      child {node [B] {41}}
    }
    child {node [B] {51}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write down the properties of a red-black tree. With an example, show the insertion and deletion operations and their costs in a red-black tree. \mk{5+15=20}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Red-Black Tree Properties, Insertion, and Deletion (20 Marks)}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsection*{1. Properties of a Red-Black Tree (5 Marks)}
A Red-Black Tree is a self-balancing binary search tree that guarantees $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ operations by strictly satisfying the following five structural invariants:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Node Property:} Every node is strictly colored either \textbf{Red} or \textbf{Black}.
    \item \textbf{Root Property:} The root node is always \textbf{Black}.
    \item \textbf{Leaf Property:} Every leaf node (the implicit \texttt{NIL} pointers at the bottom) is \textbf{Black}.
    \item \textbf{Red Property (No Consecutive Reds):} If a node is Red, both of its children must be Black.
    \item \textbf{Black-Height Property:} Every simple downward path from a given node to any of its descendant \texttt{NIL} leaves must contain the exact same number of Black nodes.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\tikzset{
    B/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=black, text=white, minimum size=0.65cm, font=\bfseries\small, thick},
    R/.style={circle, draw=red!70!black, fill=red, text=white, minimum size=0.65cm, font=\bfseries\small, thick},
    DB/.style={circle, draw=black, double=black, double distance=1.5pt, fill=black, text=white, minimum size=0.65cm, font=\bfseries\small, thick},
    level distance=0.9cm, sibling distance=1.2cm,
    edge from parent/.style={draw=black, thick}
}

\subsection*{2. Insertion Operation \& Cost (7.5 Marks)}
When inserting a new node, it is placed as a leaf using standard BST rules and initially colored \textbf{Red} to maintain the black-height. If its parent is also Red, a violation occurs, which is fixed via color flips or rotations based on the uncle's color.

\subsubsection*{Example: Inserting keys 10, 20, and 30}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Insert 10:} It becomes the root. Initially Red, but flipped to \textbf{Black} to satisfy the Root Property.
    \item \textbf{Insert 20:} Inserted as the right child of 10. Colored \textbf{Red}.
    \item \textbf{Insert 30:} Inserted as the right child of 20. Colored \textbf{Red}. This violates Property 4 (Consecutive Reds).
    \item \textbf{Fixup:} Nodes 10, 20, and 30 form a right-sided line. The uncle is Black (\texttt{NIL}). We perform a \textbf{Left Rotation} around 10 and swap the colors of 10 and 20.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccccc}
\textbf{Insert 10 \& 20} & & \textbf{Insert 30 (Violation)} & & \textbf{After Left Rotation (Fixed)} \\
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[B] {10}
        child[missing] {}
        child {node[R] {20}};
\end{tikzpicture}
& $\Longrightarrow$ &
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center), level distance=0.9cm, sibling distance=1cm]
    \node[B] {10}
        child[missing] {}
        child {node[R] {20}
            child[missing] {}
            child {node[R] {30}}
        };
\end{tikzpicture}
& $\Longrightarrow$ &
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[B] {20}
        child {node[R] {10}}
        child {node[R] {30}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\textbf{Insertion Costs:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Search/Traversal:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$
    \item \textbf{Recoloring:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ max (if pushed to root).
    \item \textbf{Rotations:} At most \textbf{2} structural rotations.
    \item \textbf{Total Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsection*{3. Deletion Operation \& Cost (7.5 Marks)}
Deleting a Red node is simple, but deleting a Black node creates a black-height deficit, logically creating a "Double-Black" node. This is resolved by shifting the missing blackness up the tree or neutralizing it through rotations depending on the sibling's color.

\subsubsection*{Example: Deleting a Black leaf (10)}
Assume a perfectly balanced tree where 20 is the Black root, with 10 and 30 as Black children.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Delete 10:} Node 10 is removed. Its \texttt{NIL} replacement inherits the deficit, becoming \textbf{Double-Black}.
    \item \textbf{Examine Sibling:} Sibling is 30 (Black), and its children are Black.
    \item \textbf{Fixup:} Extract "blackness" from the sibling (recoloring 30 to \textbf{Red}) and from the Double-Black leaf (making it normal Black). Push the blackness to the parent (20).
    \item \textbf{Resolve Root:} Because 20 is the root, it simply absorbs the extra blackness, neutralizing the deficit.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccccc}
\textbf{Initial Tree} & & \textbf{Delete 10 (Deficit)} & & \textbf{After Fixup (Sibling Recolor)} \\
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[B] {20}
        child {node[B] {10}}
        child {node[B] {30}};
\end{tikzpicture}
& $\Longrightarrow$ &
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[B] {20}
        child {node[DB] {nil}}
        child {node[B] {30}};
\end{tikzpicture}
& $\Longrightarrow$ &
\begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.center)]
    \node[B] {20}
        child[missing] {}
        child {node[R] {30}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\textbf{Deletion Costs:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Search/Traversal:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$
    \item \textbf{Recoloring:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ max (if deficit propagates upward).
    \item \textbf{Rotations:} At most \textbf{3} structural rotations.
    \item \textbf{Total Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2019-20
\end{enumerate}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cB}{Splay Trees}
\label{sub:s2-splay}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item Complete the following operations on the given splay tree: (i) Insert 30, (ii) Delete 35. Show all the steps.
\begin{center}
     \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.4,
        every node/.style={circle, draw=black, ultra thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
        thick
    ]
        
        % ট্রি স্ট্রাকচার অনুযায়ী নোড পজিশনিং
        \node (13) at (0,4) {13}
            child {node {7}}
            child {node {26}
                child {node {17}}
                child {node {45}
                    child {node {40}
                        child {node {35}}
                        child[missing] % ডান পাশের চাইল্ড খালি রাখার জন্য
                    }
                    child[missing] % ডান পাশের চাইল্ড খালি রাখার জন্য
                }
            };

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\mk{13} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Overview of Operations}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Insertion:} We first insert the node using standard Binary Search Tree (BST) insertion rules. Then, we \textbf{splay} the newly inserted node to the root.
    \item \textbf{Deletion (Join Method):} We first find the node and \textbf{splay} it to the root. Then, we remove the root, leaving two disjoint subtrees: the Left Subtree ($T_L$) and the Right Subtree ($T_R$). We splay the maximum element in $T_L$ to the root of $T_L$, and finally attach $T_R$ as its right child.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\subsubsection*{Part (i): Insert 30}

\textbf{Step 1: Standard BST Insertion}\\
We insert $30$ following standard BST properties. Comparing $30$ with $13, 26, 45, 40,$ and $35$, it eventually becomes the \textbf{left child} of $35$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=2cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 5/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    every child/.style={thick, draw=black}
]
\node {13}
    child {node {7}}
    child {node {26}
        child {node {17}}
        child {node {45}
            child {node {40}
                child {node {35}
                    child {node[fill=gray!20] {30}}
                    child[missing]
                }
                child[missing]
            }
            child[missing]
        }
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{Step 2: Splay 30 to the Root}\\
We now apply splay rotations to move $30$ to the root.
\vspace{0.2cm}

\textbf{Substep 2.1: Zig-Zig Rotation at 30}\\
Node $30$ is a left child of $35$, which is a left child of $40$. This is a \textit{Left-Left} configuration. We perform a \textbf{Zig-Zig} rotation: a right rotation at the grandparent ($40$), followed by a right rotation at the parent ($35$).

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=2cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 5/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    every child/.style={thick, draw=black}
]
\node {13}
    child {node {7}}
    child {node {26}
        child {node {17}}
        child {node {45}
            child {node[fill=gray!20] {30}
                child[missing]
                child {node {35}
                    child[missing]
                    child {node {40}}
                }
            }
            child[missing]
        }
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{Substep 2.2: Zig-Zag Rotation at 30}\\
Node $30$ is a left child of $45$, which is a right child of $26$. This is a \textit{Right-Left} configuration. We perform a \textbf{Zig-Zag} rotation: a right rotation at the parent ($45$), followed by a left rotation at the grandparent ($26$).

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=4cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=2cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    every child/.style={thick, draw=black}
]
\node {13}
    child {node {7}}
    child {node[fill=gray!20] {30}
        child {node {26}
            child {node {17}}
            child[missing]
        }
        child {node {45}
            child {node {35}
                child[missing]
                child {node {40}}
            }
            child[missing]
        }
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{Substep 2.3: Zig Rotation at 30}\\
Node $30$ is now the right child of the root $13$. We perform a \textbf{Zig} rotation: a left rotation at the root ($13$). $30$ becomes the new root of the entire tree.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    every child/.style={thick, draw=black}
]
\node[fill=gray!20] {30}
    child {node {13}
        child {node {7}}
        child {node {26}
            child {node {17}}
            child[missing]
        }
    }
    child {node {45}
        child {node {35}
            child[missing]
            child {node {40}}
        }
        child[missing]
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Part (ii): Delete 35}

\textbf{Step 1: Find 35 and Splay it to the Root}\\
We traverse the tree to find $35$. It is located as a left child of $45$, which is a right child of $30$. This \textit{Right-Left} configuration requires a \textbf{Zig-Zag} rotation: a right rotation at the parent ($45$), followed by a left rotation at the grandparent ($30$).

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    every child/.style={thick, draw=black}
]
\node[fill=gray!20] {35}
    child {node {30}
        child {node {13}
            child {node {7}}
            child {node {26}
                child {node {17}}
                child[missing]
            }
        }
        child[missing]
    }
    child {node {45}
        child {node {40}}
        child[missing]
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{Step 2: Delete the Node and Split}\\
We remove the root ($35$). This operation disconnects the tree into two separate components: the Left Subtree ($T_L$) and the Right Subtree ($T_R$).

\begin{center}
\begin{minipage}{0.45\textwidth}
\centering
\textbf{Left Subtree ($T_L$)}
\vspace{0.2cm}\\
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    every child/.style={thick, draw=black}
]
\node {30}
    child {node {13}
        child {node {7}}
        child {node {26}
            child {node {17}}
            child[missing]
        }
    }
    child[missing];
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}
\hfill
\begin{minipage}{0.45\textwidth}
\centering
\textbf{Right Subtree ($T_R$)}
\vspace{0.2cm}\\
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    every child/.style={thick, draw=black}
]
\node {45}
    child {node {40}}
    child[missing];
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\textbf{Step 3: Join Subtrees}\\
To merge the trees back together, we find the maximum element in $T_L$, which is $30$. We must splay $30$ to the root of $T_L$. However, since $30$ is \textit{already} the root of $T_L$, no rotations are needed. The root of $T_L$ is guaranteed to have no right child, so we safely attach $T_R$ as the right child of $30$.

\begin{center}
\textbf{Final Splay Tree after Deletion}
\vspace{0.2cm}\\
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.2cm},
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    every child/.style={thick, draw=black}
]
\node {30}
    child {node {13}
        child {node {7}}
        child {node {26}
            child {node {17}}
            child[missing]
        }
    }
    child {node {45}
        child {node {40}}
        child[missing]
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} Both Insert and Delete operations take $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ \textbf{amortized time}. While a single operation in a severely unbalanced tree could take $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time in the worst case, the structural rebalancing (splaying) guarantees that any sequence of $M$ operations on an initially empty tree takes $\mathcal{O}(M \log n)$ time, yielding the logarithmic amortized bound (proven via the Access Lemma).
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(n)$ to store the nodes of the tree, as no extra balancing information (like heights or colors) needs to be stored within the nodes.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item How is the concept of potential function used to design a splay tree? Explain.
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Introduction to the Potential Method in Splay Trees}\\
Splay trees do not explicitly store balance factors (like AVL trees) or colors (like Red-Black trees), meaning a single operation could theoretically take $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time if the tree degenerates into a linked list. To guarantee that a sequence of $m$ operations takes $\mathcal{O}(m \log n)$ time, Splay Trees rely on \textbf{Amortized Analysis} using the \textbf{Potential Method}. 

The potential function $\Phi$ acts like a ''bank account''. It maps the current state of the data structure to a real number (its potential). 
\begin{itemize}
    \item When an operation is cheap, it does a little extra work to ''save'' potential ($\Delta \Phi > 0$).
    \item When an operation is expensive (e.g., accessing a deep node in a skewed tree), it ''spends'' the stored potential ($\Delta \Phi < 0$) to pay for the expensive restructuring, keeping the amortized cost low.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\textbf{Defining the Splay Tree Potential Function}\\
To design and analyze the Splay Tree, Daniel Sleator and Robert Tarjan defined the potential function based on the size of the subtrees. For any node $x$ in a Splay Tree $T$:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Size $s(x)$:} The number of nodes in the subtree rooted at $x$ (including $x$ itself).
    \item \textbf{Rank $r(x)$:} The base-2 logarithm of the size: $\quad r(x) = \lg(s(x))$
    \item \textbf{Tree Potential $\Phi(T)$:} The sum of the ranks of all nodes in the tree:
\end{enumerate}
\begin{equation}
    \Phi(T) = \sum_{x \in T} r(x) = \sum_{x \in T} \lg(s(x))
\end{equation}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\textbf{Intuition Behind the Design}\\
Why choose this specific potential function? 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Balanced Tree:} If the tree is perfectly balanced, sizes decrease exponentially as we go down. The sum of the ranks is minimized. Thus, a balanced tree has \textbf{low potential}.
    \item \textbf{Skewed Tree:} If the tree is a straight line (linked list), every node has size $k, k-1, \dots, 1$. The ranks are large, meaning the tree has \textbf{high potential}.
\end{itemize}
The fundamental design philosophy of the Splay Tree is: \textit{A highly unbalanced tree holds a lot of potential energy}. If we do an expensive $\mathcal{O}(n)$ operation by accessing a deep leaf, the resulting \textbf{Splay} (rotations to bring the node to the root) will roughly halve the depth of every node along the access path. This drastic restructuring transforms a highly skewed tree into a much more balanced one. 

Because the tree becomes more balanced, its potential $\Phi$ \textbf{drops significantly}. This large negative change in potential ($\Delta \Phi \ll 0$) cancels out the high actual cost ($c_i$) of the operation.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\textbf{Mathematical Application: The Access Lemma}\\
The amortized cost $\hat{c}_i$ of the $i$-th operation is defined as:
\begin{equation}
    \hat{c}_i = c_i + \Phi(T_i) - \Phi(T_{i-1}) = \text{Actual Cost} + \text{Change in Potential}
\end{equation}

By carefully designing the \textbf{Zig-Zig} and \textbf{Zig-Zag} rotation rules, Sleator and Tarjan ensured that the amortized cost of a single splay step bounds perfectly. For any node $x$ being splayed to the root $t$, the potential function allows us to prove the \textbf{Access Lemma}:
\begin{equation}
    \text{Amortized Cost of Splaying } x \leq 3(r(t) - r(x)) + 1
\end{equation}

Since the maximum rank of the root is $\lg n$, and the minimum rank of $x$ is $\lg 1 = 0$, the expression $3(r(t) - r(x)) + 1$ is at most $3 \lg n + 1$. 

\vspace{0.3cm}
\textbf{Summary of How Potential Dictated the Design}\\
The potential function was not just an afterthought for analysis; it \textbf{guided the design of the splay rules}. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item A simple "rotate-to-root" algorithm (doing single rotations) fails to achieve $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ amortized time because it does not decrease the potential function enough to pay for deep accesses.
    \item The specific double-rotations (\textbf{Zig-Zig} and \textbf{Zig-Zag}) were deliberately invented because they guarantee that the potential of the tree drops sufficiently to mathematically satisfy the amortized cost equation $\hat{c}_i \leq \mathcal{O}(\log n)$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item What are the splay-step operations to make a binary search tree a balanced one?
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Introduction to Splay Steps and Balancing}

Unlike AVL or Red-Black trees, a Splay Tree does not enforce strict height invariants (like $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ height at all times). Instead, it uses a self-adjusting heuristic called \textbf{Splaying} to achieve \textbf{amortized balance}. 

Whenever a node $x$ is accessed (inserted, searched, or deleted), it is moved to the root of the tree through a specific sequence of rotations known as \textbf{Splay Steps}. These steps are carefully designed to not only bring $x$ to the top but also to roughly \textbf{halve the depth of every node along the access path}. This restructuring flattens skewed branches, effectively "balancing" the tree over time.

There are three primary splay-step operations, chosen based on the relationship between the target node $x$, its parent $p$, and its grandparent $g$.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{1. The Zig Step}
\textbf{Condition:} Node $x$ has a parent $p$, and $p$ is the \textbf{root} of the tree (there is no grandparent). \\
\textbf{Operation:} We perform a \textbf{single rotation} at $p$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $x$ is a left child, perform a Right Rotation at $p$.
    \item If $x$ is a right child, perform a Left Rotation at $p$.
\end{itemize}
\textit{Note: The Zig step is only ever performed as the final step of a splay operation when the target node is just one level below the root.}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.2cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\bfseries},
    triangle/.style={regular polygon, regular polygon sides=3, draw, shape border rotate=180, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\small\bfseries}
]
% Before Zig
\node {p}
    child {node[fill=gray!20] {x}
        child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_1$}}
        child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_2$}}
    }
    child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_3$}};
    
\draw[thick, ->] (2,-1) -- (4,-1) node[midway, above] {Zig (Right Rot.)};

% After Zig
\begin{scope}[xshift=6cm]
\node[fill=gray!20] {x}
    child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_1$}}
    child {node {p}
        child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_2$}}
        child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_3$}}
    };
\end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. The Zig-Zig Step}
\textbf{Condition:} Node $x$ has a parent $p$ and a grandparent $g$. Both $x$ and $p$ are \textbf{homogenous children} (either both are left children, or both are right children). \\
\textbf{Operation:} This is the most crucial step for balancing. We perform \textbf{two rotations in the same direction}:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item First, rotate at the grandparent $g$.
    \item Second, rotate at the parent $p$.
\end{enumerate}
This specific order ensures that the tree depth is significantly reduced, unlike standard single rotations.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm},
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\bfseries},
    triangle/.style={regular polygon, regular polygon sides=3, draw, shape border rotate=180, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\small\bfseries, inner sep=0pt}
]
% Before Zig-Zig
\node {g}
    child {node {p}
        child {node[fill=gray!20] {x}
            child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_1$}}
            child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_2$}}
        }
        child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_3$}}
    }
    child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_4$}};
    
\draw[thick, ->] (1.5,-1.5) -- (3.5,-1.5) node[midway, above, align=center] {Zig-Zig \\ (Rotate $g$, then $p$)};

% After Zig-Zig
\begin{scope}[xshift=5cm, yshift=-0.5cm]
\node[fill=gray!20] {x}
    child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_1$}}
    child {node {p}
        child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_2$}}
        child {node {g}
            child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_3$}}
            child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_4$}}
        }
    };
\end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. The Zig-Zag Step}
\textbf{Condition:} Node $x$ has a parent $p$ and a grandparent $g$, but they are \textbf{heterogenous children} (e.g., $x$ is a right child and $p$ is a left child, forming a "zig-zag" shape).\\
\textbf{Operation:} We perform a standard double rotation, just like in an AVL tree:
\begin{enumerate}
