    \item First, rotate at the parent $p$ (bringing $x$ up to $p$'s level).
    \item Second, rotate at the grandparent $g$ (bringing $x$ up to $g$'s level).
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.2cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm},
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.7cm, font=\bfseries},
    triangle/.style={regular polygon, regular polygon sides=3, draw, shape border rotate=180, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\small\bfseries, inner sep=0pt}
]
% Before Zig-Zag
\node {g}
    child {node {p}
        child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_1$}}
        child {node[fill=gray!20] {x}
            child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_2$}}
            child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_3$}}
        }
    }
    child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_4$}};
    
\draw[thick, ->] (2,-1.5) -- (4,-1.5) node[midway, above, align=center] {Zig-Zag \\ (Rotate $p$, then $g$)};

% After Zig-Zag
\begin{scope}[xshift=6cm, yshift=-1cm]
\node[fill=gray!20] {x}
    child {node {p}
        child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_1$}}
        child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_2$}}
    }
    child {node {g}
        child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_3$}}
        child {node[triangle, draw=black] {$T_4$}}
    };
\end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\textbf{How these steps make the tree balanced:}
A naive "rotate-to-root" strategy (doing only single Zig rotations) pushes the target node to the root, but pushes all other nodes further down, leaving the tree highly unbalanced. The \textbf{Zig-Zig} step specifically combats this: by rotating at the grandparent first, it pulls the subtrees $T_2$ and $T_3$ up alongside $x$, drastically reducing the overall height of the path and resolving degenerated line-like structures.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2022-23
\item Define the rank of a node of a splay tree. Prove that the change in potential of a splay tree not resulting from any splay after an insertion operation is $O(\log n)$, where the splay tree had $n$ nodes before the insertion operation.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Splay Tree Rank and Potential Change Proof}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsection*{1. Definition of the Rank of a Node}

In a splay tree $T$, let $s(x)$ denote the \textbf{size} of the subtree rooted at node $x$, which is equal to the total number of internal nodes in that subtree. 

The \textbf{rank} $r(x)$ of a node $x$ is defined as the base-2 logarithm of its size:
\begin{equation*}
    r(x) = \log_2(s(x))
\end{equation*}

The \textbf{total potential} $\Phi$ of the splay tree is the sum of the ranks of all nodes in the tree:
\begin{equation*}
    \Phi = \sum_{v \in T} r(v)
\end{equation*}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsection*{2. Proof of Potential Change Due Only to Insertion}

We want to find the change in potential $\Delta \Phi_{\text{insert}}$ caused strictly by adding a new node $z$ as a leaf, \textbf{before} any subsequent splay operations are performed.

\subsubsection*{Setup and Notation}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Let $T$ be a splay tree containing $n$ nodes before insertion.
    \item A new node $z$ is inserted into $T$ using standard binary search tree insertion.
    \item Let $s(v)$ and $r(v)$ represent the size and rank of any node $v$ \textit{before} insertion.
    \item Let $s'(v)$ and $r'(v)$ represent the size and rank of any node $v$ \textit{after} insertion (but before splaying).
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Identifying Affected Nodes}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{For the new node $z$:} It is inserted as a leaf, so its new subtree size is $s'(z) = 1$. Its new rank is:
    \begin{equation*}
        r'(z) = \log_2(1) = 0
    \end{equation*}
    \item \textbf{For nodes not on the path from the root to $z$:} Their subtrees are unaffected, meaning $s'(v) = s(v)$ and $r'(v) = r(v)$.
    \item \textbf{For ancestors of $z$:} Let the path from the parent of $z$ up to the root be a sequence of $m$ nodes: $v_1, v_2, \dots, v_m$, where $v_1$ is the parent of $z$ and $v_m$ is the root of the tree. For each ancestor $v_i$, its size increases by exactly 1:
    \begin{equation*}
        s'(v_i) = s(v_i) + 1
    \end{equation*}
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Algebraic Formulation of $\Delta \Phi_{\text{insert}}$}
The change in potential is the difference between the new total potential and the old total potential:
\begin{equation*}
    \Delta \Phi_{\text{insert}} = \Phi' - \Phi = r'(z) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left(r'(v_i) - r(v_i)\right)
\end{equation*}

Since $r'(z) = 0$, substituting the rank definitions gives:
\begin{equation*}
    \Delta \Phi_{\text{insert}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left(\log_2(s(v_i) + 1) - \log_2 s(v_i)\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \log_2\left(\frac{s(v_i) + 1}{s(v_i)}\right)
\end{equation*}

\subsubsection*{Bounding the Summation}
Because $v_i$ is a child of $v_{i+1}$ in the tree layout, the size of $v_{i+1}$ before insertion must include the size of its child $v_i$ plus at least $1$ (for $v_{i+1}$ itself). This yields the structural inequality:
\begin{equation*}
    s(v_{i+1}) \geq s(v_i) + 1 \quad \text{for } 1 \leq i < m
\end{equation*}

We can use this inequality to set up an upper bound for the terms where $i < m$:
\begin{equation*}
    \frac{s(v_i) + 1}{s(v_i)} \leq \frac{s(v_{i+1})}{s(v_i)}
\end{equation*}

Taking the logarithm of both sides:
\begin{equation*}
    \log_2\left(\frac{s(v_i) + 1}{s(v_i)}\right) \leq \log_2\left(\frac{s(v_{i+1})}{s(v_i)}\right) = \log_2 s(v_{i+1}) - \log_2 s(v_i)
\end{equation*}

Now, we split our original total summation into the ancestors below the root ($1 \leq i < m$) and the root itself ($i = m$):
\begin{equation*}
    \Delta \Phi_{\text{insert}} \leq \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} \left(\log_2 s(v_{i+1}) - \log_2 s(v_i)\right) \right] + \log_2\left(\frac{s(v_m) + 1}{s(v_m)}\right)
\end{equation*}

\subsubsection*{Telescoping the Sum}
The summation from $1$ to $m-1$ is a standard telescoping series where internal terms cancel out completely:
\begin{equation*}
    \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} \left(\log_2 s(v_{i+1}) - \log_2 s(v_i)\right) = \log_2 s(v_m) - \log_2 s(v_1)
\end{equation*}

Substituting this back into our inequality gives:
\begin{equation*}
    \Delta \Phi_{\text{insert}} \leq \left(\log_2 s(v_m) - \log_2 s(v_1)\right) + \log_2(s(v_m) + 1) - \log_2 s(v_m)
\end{equation*}

Canceling $\log_2 s(v_m)$ results in:
\begin{equation*}
    \Delta \Phi_{\text{insert}} \leq \log_2(s(v_m) + 1) - \log_2 s(v_1)
\end{equation*}

\subsubsection*{Final Simplification}
\begin{itemize}
    \item The root node $v_m$ originally held all $n$ nodes of the tree before insertion, so $s(v_m) = n$.
    \item The parent of the leaf node must have a size of at least 1, so $s(v_1) \geq 1 \implies \log_2 s(v_1) \geq 0$.
\end{itemize}

Dropping the non-negative term $-\log_2 s(v_1)$ provides the final upper bound:
\begin{align*}
    \Delta \Phi_{\text{insert}} &\leq \log_2(n + 1) \\
    \Delta \Phi_{\text{insert}} &= \mathcal{O}(\log n)
\end{align*}

\begin{tcolorbox}[colback=green!5,colframe=green!60!black,title=\textbf{Conclusion}]
Thus, the change in the potential of a splay tree resulting strictly from an insertion operation (excluding any subsequent splays) is asymptotically bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$.
\end{tcolorbox}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2021-22
\item (i)For the splay tree of the following figure, show the resulting tree after successively accessing the keys 3 and 9 in the splay tree.
\\(ii)For the splay tree of the following figure, show the resulting tree after deleting key 6 after doing operations in (i) (i.e., accessing the keys 3 and 9).

\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsubsection*{Part (i): Successively Accessing Keys 3 and 9}

\textbf{1. Accessing Key 3}\\
We locate node $3$ in the initial tree. The path from the root is $10 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3$. To bring $3$ to the root, we perform the following splay steps:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Step 1.1: Zig-Zag on 3.} The parent of $3$ is $2$, and the grandparent is $4$. Since $2$ is a left child and $3$ is a right child, we perform a Left-Rotation at $2$, followed by a Right-Rotation at $4$.
    \item \textbf{Step 1.2: Zig on 3.} Now $3$ is the left child of the root $10$. We perform a single Right-Rotation at $10$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{2. Accessing Key 9}\\

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Step 2.1: Zig-Zig on 9.} Parent $8$ and grandparent $6$ are both right children. We perform a Left-Rotation at $6$, then a Left-Rotation at $8$.
    \item \textbf{Step 2.2: Zig-Zag on 9.} Parent $4$ is a left child, grandparent $10$ is a right child. We perform a Left-Rotation at $4$, then a Right-Rotation at $10$.
    \item \textbf{Step 2.3: Zig on 9.} Node $9$ is the right child of $3$. We perform a Left-Rotation at $3$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Part (ii): Deleting Key 6}

To delete $6$, we must first access it and \textbf{splay it to the root}. The path to $6$ is $9 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 8 \rightarrow 6$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Step 3.1: Zig-Zag on 6.} Parent $8$ is a right child, and $6$ is a left child. We Right-Rotate at $8$, then Left-Rotate at $4$.
    \item \textbf{Step 3.2: Zig-Zag on 6.} Parent $3$ is a left child, and $6$ is a right child. We Left-Rotate at $3$, then Right-Rotate at $9$. Node $6$ becomes the root.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\textbf{Deletion and Join Operation}\\
We remove the root $6$, splitting the tree into a Left Subtree ($T_L$ rooted at $3$) and a Right Subtree ($T_R$ rooted at $9$). To merge them:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item We find the maximum element in $T_L$, which is $5$.
    \item We splay $5$ to the root of $T_L$. This requires a \textbf{Zig-Zig} step (Left-Rotate at $3$, Left-Rotate at $4$).
    \item Since $5$ is the maximum, it has no right child. We attach $T_R$ as the right child of $5$.
\end{enumerate}

\item Perform \textsc{Splay}(1) on the given binary search tree, showing all intermediate steps and operations performed at each node. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.9cm, font=\bfseries},
  level distance=1.5cm,
]
  \node {5} child { node {4}
    child { node {3}
      child { node {2}
        child { node {1} }}}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Concept of Splay Operation}

In a Splay Tree, when a node $x$ is accessed, it is moved to the root of the tree using a sequence of rotations known as splaying. The specific rotation applied at each step depends on the relationship between $x$, its parent $p$, and its grandparent $g$ (if it exists):
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Zig:} If $x$ has a parent $p$ but no grandparent ($p$ is the root), we perform a single rotation at $p$.
    \item \textbf{Zig-Zig:} If $x$ and $p$ are either both left children or both right children, we perform a rotation at $g$ followed by a rotation at $p$.
    \item \textbf{Zig-Zag:} If $x$ is a left child and $p$ is a right child (or vice versa), we perform a rotation at $p$ followed by a rotation at $g$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\textbf{Step-by-Step Execution of \textsc{Splay}(1)}

The target node is $x = 1$. In the initial tree, the path from the root to $1$ is $5 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 1$. We will splay node $1$ bottom-up. 
\\
\textit{Note: In the diagrams below, the nodes are structurally aligned to their x-coordinates strictly based on their values ($x$-axis matches the Binary Search Tree ordering) to perfectly visualize the rotations.}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\textbf{\large Step 1: Zig-Zig on Node 1}

Currently, $x = 1$, parent $p = 2$, and grandparent $g = 3$. Since both $1$ and $2$ are left children, this is a \textbf{Left-Left} case. We perform a \textbf{Zig-Zig} operation, which consists of two right rotations:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Right Rotate at Grandparent (3)}
    \item \textbf{Right Rotate at Parent (2)}
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\noindent
\begin{minipage}{0.32\textwidth}
\centering
\textbf{Initial Tree}\\
\vspace{0.3cm}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries, fill=white},
  x=0.8cm, y=1.0cm, thick
]
\node (n5) at (5, 0) {5};
\node (n4) at (4, -1) {4};
\node (n3) at (3, -2) {3};
\node (n2) at (2, -3) {2};
\node (n1) at (1, -4) {1};

\draw (n5) -- (n4);
\draw (n4) -- (n3);
\draw (n3) -- (n2);
\draw (n2) -- (n1);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}
\hfill
\begin{minipage}{0.32\textwidth}
\centering
\textbf{1. Right Rotate at 3}\\
\vspace{0.3cm}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries, fill=white},
  x=0.8cm, y=1.0cm, thick
]
\node (n5) at (5, 0) {5};
\node (n4) at (4, -1) {4};
\node (n2) at (2, -2) {2};
\node (n1) at (1, -3) {1};
\node (n3) at (3, -3) {3};

\draw (n5) -- (n4);
\draw (n4) -- (n2);
\draw (n2) -- (n1);
\draw (n2) -- (n3);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}
\hfill
\begin{minipage}{0.32\textwidth}
\centering
\textbf{2. Right Rotate at 2}\\
\vspace{0.3cm}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries, fill=white},
  x=0.8cm, y=1.0cm, thick
]
\node (n5) at (5, 0) {5};
\node (n4) at (4, -1) {4};
\node (n1) at (1, -2) {1};
\node (n2) at (2, -3) {2};
\node (n3) at (3, -4) {3};

\draw (n5) -- (n4);
\draw (n4) -- (n1);
\draw (n1) -- (n2);
\draw (n2) -- (n3);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}

\vspace{0.6cm}
\textbf{\large Step 2: Zig-Zig on Node 1}

After Step 1, $x = 1$ has moved up. Now, its parent is $p = 4$, and its grandparent is $g = 5$. Since both $1$ and $4$ are left children, this is again a \textbf{Left-Left} case. We perform another \textbf{Zig-Zig} operation:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Right Rotate at Grandparent (5)}
    \item \textbf{Right Rotate at Parent (4)}
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\noindent
\begin{minipage}{0.32\textwidth}
\centering
\textbf{Current Tree}\\
\vspace{0.3cm}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries, fill=white},
  x=0.8cm, y=1.0cm, thick
]
\node (n5) at (5, 0) {5};
\node (n4) at (4, -1) {4};
\node (n1) at (1, -2) {1};
\node (n2) at (2, -3) {2};
\node (n3) at (3, -4) {3};

\draw (n5) -- (n4);
\draw (n4) -- (n1);
\draw (n1) -- (n2);
\draw (n2) -- (n3);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}
\hfill
\begin{minipage}{0.32\textwidth}
\centering
\textbf{1. Right Rotate at 5}\\
\vspace{0.3cm}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries, fill=white},
  x=0.8cm, y=1.0cm, thick
]
\node (n4) at (4, 0) {4};
\node (n1) at (1, -1) {1};
\node (n5) at (5, -1) {5};
\node (n2) at (2, -2) {2};
\node (n3) at (3, -3) {3};

\draw (n4) -- (n1);
\draw (n4) -- (n5);
\draw (n1) -- (n2);
\draw (n2) -- (n3);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}
\hfill
\begin{minipage}{0.32\textwidth}
\centering
\textbf{2. Right Rotate at 4}\\
\vspace{0.3cm}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries, fill=white},
  x=0.8cm, y=1.0cm, thick
]
\node (n1) at (1, 0) {1};
\node (n4) at (4, -1) {4};
\node (n2) at (2, -2) {2};
\node (n5) at (5, -2) {5};
\node (n3) at (3, -3) {3};

\draw (n1) -- (n4);
\draw (n4) -- (n2);
\draw (n4) -- (n5);
\draw (n2) -- (n3);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{minipage}

\vspace{0.5cm}
Node $1$ has now reached the root. The \textsc{Splay}(1) operation is strictly complete. The initial heavily skewed tree of height $4$ has been reorganized, and its height has been reduced, which is the self-optimizing characteristic of a Splay Tree. An inorder traversal of the final tree yields \texttt{1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, preserving the Binary Search Tree properties correctly.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\textbf{Complexity Analysis:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(h)$ where $h$ is the current height of the tree. For this specific operation on the skewed tree, it takes $\mathcal{O}(N)$ time. However, a sequence of $M$ splay operations takes $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$ time, yielding an amortized time complexity of $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ per operation.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(1)$ auxiliary space if the rotations are executed iteratively using pointers. 
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What is the main objective of splay trees? Do splay trees explicitly try to balance the height? \mk{5}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Main Objective of Splay Trees}

The primary objective of a Splay Tree is to optimize access times for frequently or recently accessed elements by leveraging the \textbf{temporal locality of reference}. In many practical applications, an element accessed once is highly likely to be accessed again in the near future. 

Splay trees achieve this objective by applying a heuristic: every time an element is accessed, searched, inserted, or modified, a sequence of specific rotations (splaying) is performed to move that element directly to the \textbf{root of the tree}. Consequently:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Subsequent accesses to the same node are extremely fast ($\mathcal{O}(1)$ time).
    \item Elements accessed frequently cluster near the root, ensuring rapid retrieval.
    \item The data structure guarantees an \textbf{amortized time complexity} of $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ for basic operations (insertion, look-up, deletion) over a sequence of $m$ operations, even if a single operation occasionally takes $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\textbf{Do Splay Trees Explicitly Try to Balance the Height?}

\textbf{No, Splay Trees do not explicitly attempt to balance their height.} 

Unlike strict self-balancing binary search trees such as \textbf{AVL Trees} or \textbf{Red-Black Trees}, Splay Trees operate without any structural invariants or metadata:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{No Explicit Invariants:} They do not enforce a maximum height difference between subtrees (like AVL trees) or adhere to color-path rules (like Red-Black trees). 
    \item \textbf{No Metadata Required:} Nodes in a Splay Tree do not store balance factors, heights, or colors, which makes the nodes structurally simpler and saves memory.
    \item \textbf{Worst-Case Height:} Because they do not enforce explicit balance, a Splay Tree can technically degrade into a completely skewed shape (a linked list) with a height of $\mathcal{O}(n)$ under certain access patterns (e.g., sequential insertions).
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Implicit (Heuristic) Balancing Effect:}\\
While Splay Trees do not \textit{explicitly} track or correct height imbalance, the splaying mechanism (specifically the Zig-Zig and Zig-Zag rotations) naturally reorganizes the tree. As a deep node is splayed to the root, the path to that node is roughly \textbf{halved in length}. This provides an \textit{implicit} self-adjusting effect; a highly imbalanced tree will rapidly "flatten" itself as soon as a deep, expensive node is accessed. Thus, balancing in Splay Trees is a beneficial side-effect of the access heuristic, not an explicitly enforced structural property.

\end{tcolorbox}


%% Q — 2020-21
\end{enumerate}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cB}{Skip Lists}
\label{sub:s2-skip}

\begin{enumerate}

\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{1. Proof: Expected Space Usage of a Skip List}

A skip list is a probabilistic data structure where each of the $n$ inserted items is stored in a "tower" of nodes. The total space usage is proportional to the total number of nodes across all levels.

\begin{itemize}
    \item Let $X_j$ be a random variable representing the height of the tower for the $j$-th item.
    \item In a standard skip list, an item always exists at level $L_0$. It promoted to level $L_{i+1}$ from level $L_i$ with a probability $p$ (typically $p = 1/2$).
    \item The probability that the height $X_j$ is exactly $k$ is given by the geometric distribution:
    $P(X_j = k) = p^{k-1}(1-p)$ for $k \ge 1$.
    \item The expected height of a single tower is:
    $E[X_j] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot P(X_j = k) = \frac{1}{1-p}$.
    \item Let $S$ be the total number of nodes in the skip list for $n$ items. By the linearity of expectation:
    \[ E[S] = E\left[ \sum_{j=1}^{n} X_j \right] = \sum_{j=1}^{n} E[X_j] = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{1}{1-p} = \frac{n}{1-p} \]
    \item For the common case where $p = 1/2$, $E[S] = 2n$.
\end{itemize}
Since the number of sentinel nodes ($-inf, +inf$) is proportional to the height of the tree ($\log n$ with high probability), they do not dominate the $O(n)$ term. Thus, the expected space usage is \textbf{$O(n)$}.

\vspace{0.4cm}
\textbf{2. Sequential Operations}

We use the convention that $i$ denotes the height of the node (number of levels it occupies, starting from $S_0$).

\textbf{(i) Insert key = 17 where $i = 2$}
The key 17 is inserted between 15 and 23. Since $i=2$, it will be present in levels $S_0$ and $S_1$.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item In a skip list with $n$ entries,
\begin{enumerate}
\item Prove that the expected space used is $O(n)$.
\item Prove that the expected search and insertion time is $O(\log n)$.
\end{enumerate}
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Skip List Complexity Proofs}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

In a standard skip list, each element is inserted at the base level (level $0$) and is repeatedly promoted to the next level with a fixed probability $p$ (typically $p = 1/2$). The promotion stops as soon as a "tails" (failure) is flipped. 

\subsection*{1. Proof: Expected Space Used is $\mathcal{O}(n)$}

To evaluate the space complexity, we must count the total number of pointers (or nodes) across all levels of the skip list.

\begin{itemize}
    \item Let $n$ be the total number of elements inserted.
    \item An element appears at level $i$ if and only if it has been promoted $i$ times successfully. The probability of an element reaching level $i$ is $p^i$.
    \item Let $X_i$ be a random variable representing the number of elements at level $i$. By the linearity of expectation, the expected number of elements at level $i$ is:
    \begin{equation*}
        E[X_i] = n \cdot p^i
    \end{equation*}
    \item The total expected space (total number of nodes/pointers $S$) is the sum of the expected number of elements at all possible levels $i$ from $0$ to $\infty$:
    \begin{equation*}
        E[S] = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} E[X_i] = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} n \cdot p^i = n \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} p^i
    \end{equation*}
    \item The summation is a standard infinite geometric series. Because $0 < p < 1$, it converges to $\frac{1}{1-p}$:
    \begin{equation*}
        E[S] = n \left( \frac{1}{1-p} \right)
    \end{equation*}
\end{itemize}

Since $p$ is a constant fraction (e.g., $1/2$), the term $\frac{1}{1-p}$ is a constant (e.g., $2$). Therefore, $E[S] = 2n$. 
\\[0.2cm]
\textbf{Conclusion:} The expected space complexity is asymptotically bounded by $\mathcal{O}(n)$.

\vspace{0.4cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.4cm}

\subsection*{2. Proof: Expected Search and Insertion Time is $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$}

We can analyze the time complexity by evaluating the expected height of the skip list and using a backwards search path analysis.

\subsubsection*{Part A: Expected Maximum Height}
The probability that a specific element reaches level $k$ is $p^k$. The probability that \textit{at least one} of the $n$ elements reaches level $k$ is bounded by the union bound: $n \cdot p^k$. 
If we define $k = c \log_{1/p} n$ (for some constant $c > 1$), then:
\begin{equation*}
    P(\text{Height} \geq c \log_{1/p} n) \leq n \cdot p^{c \log_{1/p} n} = n \cdot n^{-c} = n^{1-c}
\end{equation*}
As $n$ grows, this probability approaches $0$. Thus, with high probability, the maximum height of the skip list is bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$.

\subsubsection*{Part B: Backwards Path Analysis (Search Time)}
Consider a search path, but trace it \textit{backwards} from the target node at the bottom level back up to the top-left root node. At any given node in the path, the previous step was either:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{From above (Moved Down):} This occurs if the current node was promoted to the next level, which happens with probability $p$.
    \item \textbf{From the left (Moved Right):} This occurs if the current node was \textit{not} promoted, which happens with probability $1-p$.
\end{enumerate}

Let $C(k)$ be the expected number of steps required to climb $k$ levels. At each step, we flip a biased coin:
\begin{itemize}
    \item With probability $p$, we go up one level (height increases).
    \item With probability $1-p$, we go left (height stays the same).
\end{itemize}
The number of leftward steps taken before finally making an upward step follows a geometric distribution with success probability $p$. The expected number of steps to achieve one upward movement is:
\begin{equation*}
    \frac{1}{p}
\end{equation*}

Since we know from Part A that the maximum expected height $k$ is $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$, the total expected length of the search path is:
\begin{align*}
    E[\text{Total Steps}] &= (\text{Expected steps per level}) \times (\text{Expected max levels}) \\
    &= \left(\frac{1}{p}\right) \times \mathcal{O}(\log n) \\
    &= \mathcal{O}(\log n) \quad \text{(since } p \text{ is constant)}
\end{align*}

\subsubsection*{Part C: Insertion Time}
An insertion operation consists of two phases:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Search:} Finding the correct insertion points at each level, which takes $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ expected time as proven above.
    \item \textbf{Splicing/Promotion:} Generating a random level for the new node (takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ expected coin flips) and updating the pointers. Since the expected height of the new node is $\mathcal{O}(1)$, and at worst bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$, the pointer updates take $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ worst-case time.
\end{enumerate}
\textbf{Conclusion:} Adding the two phases together, the total expected insertion time is structurally bound to the search time: $\mathcal{O}(\log n) + \mathcal{O}(\log n) = \mathcal{O}(\log n)$.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Compare linked lists and skip lists. Show that the expected space used by a skip list with $n$ entries is $O(n)$. Draw the skip-list for the following table that shows the keys and consecutive numbers of heads found in a coin toss while inserting the keys:
\begin{center}
    \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5} % Adjusts row height to match the image
\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
Key                     & 15 & 40 & 35 & 28 & 21 & 65 & 84 & 79 & 50 \\ \hline
No. of consecutive heads & 1  & 4  & 1  & 0  & 3  & 2  & 2  & 3  & 1  \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\mk{15}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2014--15, 2013--14}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{1. Comparison: Linked Lists vs. Skip Lists}

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{tabular}{p{3.5cm} p{5.5cm} p{5.5cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Feature} & \textbf{Linked List} & \textbf{Skip List} \\
\midrule
\textbf{Structure} & Single-level, linear sequence of nodes. & Multi-level, hierarchical structure of linked lists. \\
\textbf{Search Time} & $\mathcal{O}(n)$ in both average and worst cases (must traverse sequentially). & Expected $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$, as higher levels allow "skipping" over elements. \\
\textbf{Insert / Delete} & $\mathcal{O}(n)$ to find the position, $\mathcal{O}(1)$ to relink. & Expected $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ to find and update pointers across levels. \\
\textbf{Space Complexity} & Exactly $\mathcal{O}(n)$, minimal pointer overhead (1 per node). & Expected $\mathcal{O}(n)$, higher constant factor due to multiple pointers per node. \\
\textbf{Determinism} & Completely deterministic operations. & Probabilistic/Randomized (uses coin tosses for node heights). \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\textbf{2. Proof: Expected Space of a Skip List is $\mathcal{O}(n)$}

A skip list stores $n$ entries. Every entry is guaranteed to appear at the base level ($S_0$). When an entry is inserted, a fair coin is flipped; as long as it comes up "heads" (probability $p = \frac{1}{2}$), the element is promoted to the next level.
\begin{itemize}
    \item Let $X_i$ be the random variable representing the total number of levels node $i$ occupies.
    \item A node occupies exactly $k$ levels if we flip $k-1$ consecutive heads followed by a tail. The probability of this event follows a geometric distribution: 
    \[ P(X_i = k) = p^{k-1}(1-p) \]
    \item The expected number of levels for a single node $i$ is the expected value of this geometric distribution:
    \[ E[X_i] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot P(X_i = k) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot p^{k-1}(1-p) = \frac{1}{1-p} \]
    \item For a fair coin ($p = \frac{1}{2}$), $E[X_i] = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5} = 2$. Thus, an average node occupies 2 levels.
    \item Let $S$ be the total space (number of node references) across all levels. By Linearity of Expectation:
    \[ E[S] = E\left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[X_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} 2 = 2n \]
\end{itemize}
Since $2n \in \mathcal{O}(n)$, the expected space complexity of a skip list with $n$ elements is $\mathcal{O}(n)$.

\vspace{0.4cm}
\textbf{3. Drawing the Skip List}

\textbf{Interpretation:} If an element yields $k$ consecutive heads, it is promoted $k$ times. Therefore, it will exist in exactly $k+1$ levels: $S_0, S_1, \dots, S_k$. \\
\textit{First, we sort the keys to maintain the ordered property of the skip list, and assign their heights based on the provided table:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{15:} 1 head $\rightarrow$ Levels $S_0, S_1$
    \item \textbf{21:} 3 heads $\rightarrow$ Levels $S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3$
    \item \textbf{28:} 0 heads $\rightarrow$ Level $S_0$
    \item \textbf{35:} 1 head $\rightarrow$ Levels $S_0, S_1$
    \item \textbf{40:} 4 heads $\rightarrow$ Levels $S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$
    \item \textbf{50:} 1 head $\rightarrow$ Levels $S_0, S_1$
    \item \textbf{65:} 2 heads $\rightarrow$ Levels $S_0, S_1, S_2$
    \item \textbf{79:} 3 heads $\rightarrow$ Levels $S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3$
    \item \textbf{84:} 2 heads $\rightarrow$ Levels $S_0, S_1, S_2$
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    xscale=1.1, yscale=0.9,
    node style/.style={rectangle, draw=black!80, thick, fill=white, minimum width=0.8cm, minimum height=0.55cm, inner sep=2pt, font=\small},
    label style/.style={text=black!70, anchor=east, font=\small\bfseries},
    edge style/.style={thick, black!80}
]

    % Coordinates X alignment:
    % -inf:0, 15:1, 21:2, 28:3, 35:4, 40:5, 50:6, 65:7, 79:8, 84:9, +inf:10

    % Level S4
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 4) {S4};
    \node[node style] (s4_inf1) at (0,4) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s4_40)   at (5,4) {40};
    \node[node style] (s4_inf2) at (10,4) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s4_inf1) -- (s4_40) -- (s4_inf2);

    % Level S3
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 3) {S3};
    \node[node style] (s3_inf1) at (0,3) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s3_21)   at (2,3) {21};
    \node[node style] (s3_40)   at (5,3) {40};
    \node[node style] (s3_79)   at (8,3) {79};
    \node[node style] (s3_inf2) at (10,3) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s3_inf1) -- (s3_21) -- (s3_40) -- (s3_79) -- (s3_inf2);

    % Level S2
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 2) {S2};
    \node[node style] (s2_inf1) at (0,2) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s2_21)   at (2,2) {21};
    \node[node style] (s2_40)   at (5,2) {40};
    \node[node style] (s2_65)   at (7,2) {65};
    \node[node style] (s2_79)   at (8,2) {79};
    \node[node style] (s2_84)   at (9,2) {84};
    \node[node style] (s2_inf2) at (10,2) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s2_inf1) -- (s2_21) -- (s2_40) -- (s2_65) -- (s2_79) -- (s2_84) -- (s2_inf2);

    % Level S1

    \node[node style] (s1_inf1) at (0,1) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s1_15)   at (1,1) {15};
    \node[node style] (s1_21)   at (2,1) {21};
    \node[node style] (s1_35)   at (4,1) {35};
    \node[node style] (s1_40)   at (5,1) {40};
    \node[node style] (s1_50)   at (6,1) {50};
    \node[node style] (s1_65)   at (7,1) {65};
    \node[node style] (s1_79)   at (8,1) {79};
    \node[node style] (s1_84)   at (9,1) {84};
    \node[node style] (s1_inf2) at (10,1) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s1_inf1) -- (s1_15) -- (s1_21) -- (s1_35) -- (s1_40) -- (s1_50) -- (s1_65) -- (s1_79) -- (s1_84) -- (s1_inf2);

    % Level S0
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 0) {S0};
    \node[node style] (s0_inf1) at (0,0) {$-\infty$};
