    \node[node style] (s0_21)   at (2,0) {21};
    \node[node style] (s0_28)   at (3,0) {28};
    \node[node style] (s0_35)   at (4,0) {35};
    \node[node style] (s0_40)   at (5,0) {40};
    \node[node style] (s0_50)   at (6,0) {50};
    \node[node style] (s0_65)   at (7,0) {65};
    \node[node style] (s0_79)   at (8,0) {79};
    \node[node style] (s0_84)   at (9,0) {84};
    \node[node style] (s0_inf2) at (10,0) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s0_inf1) -- (s0_15) -- (s0_21) -- (s0_28) -- (s0_35) -- (s0_40) -- (s0_50) -- (s0_65) -- (s0_79) -- (s0_84) -- (s0_inf2);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write algorithms for the search, insertion and deletion operations of skip lists. Show that the expected search, insertion and deletion time is $O(\log n)$ in a skip list with $n$ entries. Also show that the expected space used is $O(n)$. \mk{18}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2013--14}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Skip List Algorithms and Complexity Proofs (18 Marks)}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsection*{1. Algorithms for Skip List Operations}

Let \texttt{MAX\_LEVEL} be the maximum allowed height of the skip list, and $p$ be the fraction of nodes promoted to the next level (typically $p = 0.5$). Each node contains an array of forward pointers, \texttt{forward[0...level]}.

\subsubsection*{A. Search Algorithm}
To search for a key, we start at the highest level of the header (dummy) node and traverse forward. When the next node's key is greater than or equal to the target, we drop down to the next lower level.

\begin{tabbing}
\hspace{0.5cm}\=\hspace{1cm}\=\hspace{1cm}\=\hspace{1cm}\=\kill
\textbf{Search(list, searchKey)} \\
\> $x \gets \text{list.header}$ \\
\> \textbf{for} $i \gets \text{list.level} \textbf{ down to } 0 \textbf{ do} $ \\
\> \> \textbf{while} $x.\text{forward}[i].\text{key} < \text{searchKey} \textbf{ do}$ \\
\> \> \> $x \gets x.\text{forward}[i]$ \\
\> $x \gets x.\text{forward}[0]$ \quad \textit{// $x$ is now the largest element $<$ searchKey} \\
\> \textbf{if} $x.\text{key} == \text{searchKey} \textbf{ then}$ \\
\> \> \textbf{return} $x.\text{value}$ \quad \textit{// Success} \\
\> \textbf{else} \\
\> \> \textbf{return} \text{failure}
\end{tabbing}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.2cm}

\subsubsection*{B. Insertion Algorithm}
Insertion requires finding the correct position while keeping track of the "drop-down" nodes at each level. We store these nodes in an \texttt{update} array so we can rewire their pointers to include the new node.

\begin{tabbing}
\hspace{0.5cm}\=\hspace{1cm}\=\hspace{1cm}\=\hspace{1cm}\=\kill
\textbf{Insert(list, searchKey, newValue)} \\
\> \text{allocate array } $\text{update}[\text{MAX\_LEVEL}]$ \\
\> $x \gets \text{list.header}$ \\
\> \textit{// Phase 1: Search and record update path} \\
\> \textbf{for} $i \gets \text{list.level} \textbf{ down to } 0 \textbf{ do}$ \\
\> \> \textbf{while} $x.\text{forward}[i].\text{key} < \text{searchKey} \textbf{ do}$ \\
\> \> \> $x \gets x.\text{forward}[i]$ \\
\> \> $\text{update}[i] \gets x$ \\
\> $x \gets x.\text{forward}[0]$ \\
\> \textbf{if} $x.\text{key} == \text{searchKey} \textbf{ then}$ \\
\> \> $x.\text{value} \gets \text{newValue}$ \quad \textit{// Key exists, overwrite} \\
\> \textbf{else} \\
\> \> \textit{// Phase 2: Splicing in the new node} \\
\> \> $\text{lvl} \gets \text{RandomLevel}()$ \quad \textit{// Coin flips with probability } $p$ \\
\> \> \textbf{if} $\text{lvl} > \text{list.level} \textbf{ then}$ \\
\> \> \> \textbf{for} $i \gets \text{list.level} + 1 \textbf{ to } \text{lvl} \textbf{ do}$ \\
\> \> \> \> $\text{update}[i] \gets \text{list.header}$ \\
\> \> \> $\text{list.level} \gets \text{lvl}$ \\
\> \> $\text{newNode} \gets \text{MakeNode(lvl, searchKey, newValue)}$ \\
\> \> \textbf{for} $i \gets 0 \textbf{ to } \text{lvl} \textbf{ do}$ \\
\> \> \> $\text{newNode}.\text{forward}[i] \gets \text{update}[i].\text{forward}[i]$ \\
\> \> \> $\text{update}[i].\text{forward}[i] \gets \text{newNode}$
\end{tabbing}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.2cm}

\subsubsection*{C. Deletion Algorithm}
Deletion uses the same \texttt{update} array logic to find the node and bypass its pointers.

\begin{tabbing}
\hspace{0.5cm}\=\hspace{1cm}\=\hspace{1cm}\=\hspace{1cm}\=\kill
\textbf{Delete(list, searchKey)} \\
\> \text{allocate array } $\text{update}[\text{MAX\_LEVEL}]$ \\
\> $x \gets \text{list.header}$ \\
\> \textit{// Phase 1: Search and record update path} \\
\> \textbf{for} $i \gets \text{list.level} \textbf{ down to } 0 \textbf{ do}$ \\
\> \> \textbf{while} $x.\text{forward}[i].\text{key} < \text{searchKey} \textbf{ do}$ \\
\> \> \> $x \gets x.\text{forward}[i]$ \\
\> \> $\text{update}[i] \gets x$ \\
\> $x \gets x.\text{forward}[0]$ \\
\> \textbf{if} $x.\text{key} == \text{searchKey} \textbf{ then}$ \\
\> \> \textit{// Phase 2: Bypass and remove} \\
\> \> \textbf{for} $i \gets 0 \textbf{ to } \text{list.level} \textbf{ do}$ \\
\> \> \> \textbf{if} $\text{update}[i].\text{forward}[i] \neq x$ \textbf{ then break} \\
\> \> \> $\text{update}[i].\text{forward}[i] \gets x.\text{forward}[i]$ \\
\> \> $\text{FreeNode}(x)$ \\
\> \> \textit{// Shrink the max level if the top levels are now empty} \\
\> \> \textbf{while} $\text{list.level} > 0 \textbf{ and } \text{list.header.forward}[\text{list.level}] == \text{NIL} \textbf{ do}$ \\
\> \> \> $\text{list.level} \gets \text{list.level} - 1$
\end{tabbing}

\vspace{0.4cm}

\subsection*{2. Proof: Expected Search, Insertion, and Deletion Time is $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$}

All three operations depend fundamentally on the time it takes to traverse the list (the search path).

\textbf{Step 1: Expected Height of the Skip List} \\
The probability of a single node reaching level $k$ is $p^k$. Using the union bound, the probability that \textit{any} of the $n$ nodes reaches level $c \log_{1/p} n$ is:
\begin{equation*}
    P(\text{max level} \geq c \log_{1/p} n) \leq n \cdot p^{c \log_{1/p} n} = n \cdot n^{-c} = n^{1-c}
\end{equation*}
For a constant $c > 1$, this approaches $0$ as $n$ grows. Thus, the expected maximum height of the list is bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$.

\textbf{Step 2: Backwards Path Analysis} \\
To find the expected length of the search path, trace the search path \textit{backwards} from the target node at level 0 back up to the top-left header node. At any node, the preceding step was either:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{A step down:} This means the node was promoted to the next level (probability $p$).
    \item \textbf{A step right:} This means the node was not promoted (probability $1-p$).
\end{enumerate}

The expected number of leftward steps before making an upward step follows a geometric distribution. The expected number of steps to climb one level is $\frac{1}{p}$.

Since the maximum expected height is $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$, the total expected search path length is:
\begin{equation*}
    E[\text{Total Steps}] = \left( \frac{1}{p} \right) \times \mathcal{O}(\log n)
\end{equation*}
Since $p$ is a constant, this simplifies to \textbf{$\mathcal{O}(\log n)$}.

\textbf{Step 3: Total Complexities}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Search:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ traversal time.
    \item \textbf{Insertion:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ traversal time + at most $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ constant-time pointer updates = $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$.
    \item \textbf{Deletion:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ traversal time + at most $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ constant-time pointer updates = $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.4cm}

\subsection*{3. Proof: Expected Space Used is $\mathcal{O}(n)$}

The total space used is proportional to the total number of forward pointers across all nodes at all levels.

\begin{itemize}
    \item Every node is inserted at the base level (level 0). So, there are exactly $n$ nodes at level 0.
    \item A node is promoted to level $i$ with probability $p^i$.
    \item Let $X_i$ be a random variable representing the number of nodes at level $i$. By the linearity of expectation, the expected number of nodes at level $i$ is:
    \begin{equation*}
        E[X_i] = n \cdot p^i
    \end{equation*}
\end{itemize}

The total expected space $E[S]$ is the sum of expectations across all possible levels from $0$ to $\infty$:
\begin{equation*}
    E[S] = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} E[X_i] = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} n p^i = n \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} p^i
\end{equation*}

This summation is a standard infinite geometric series. For $0 < p < 1$, it converges to $\frac{1}{1-p}$:
\begin{equation*}
    E[S] = n \left( \frac{1}{1-p} \right)
\end{equation*}

Because $p$ is a constant fraction (e.g., $p = 1/2$), the term $\frac{1}{1-p}$ is also a constant ($2$). 
Therefore, $E[S] = 2n$, which proves that the expected space complexity is strictly bounded by \textbf{$\mathcal{O}(n)$}.

\end{tcolorbox}


\item Draw the skip-list for the following table that shows the keys and consecutive numbers of heads found in a coin toss while inserting the keys in a probabilistic skip-list:
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{lcccccccccc}
\toprule
Key & 3 & 6 & 7 & 9 & 12 & 17 & 19 & 21 & 25 & 26 \\
No.\ of consecutive heads & 1 & 4 & 1 & 2 & 1 & 2 & 1 & 1 & 3 & 1 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
Perform the following operations on the skip-list and show the procedures:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Search 21
  \item Insert 22 with 3 consecutive heads
  \item Delete 19
  \item Search 18
\end{enumerate}
\mk{7+8=15}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2011--12}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Assumption / Interpretation of "Consecutive Heads"} \\
In a skip list, the number of consecutive heads flipped during insertion dictates the height of the node's tower. To prevent all nodes from redundantly occupying a base level and an identical second level, we use the standard convention that a node with $k$ consecutive heads occupies exactly $k$ levels. Thus, it will be present in levels $S_0, S_1, \dots, S_{k-1}$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{1 head} $\implies$ occupies $1$ level ($S_0$)
    \item \textbf{2 heads} $\implies$ occupies $2$ levels ($S_0, S_1$)
    \item \textbf{3 heads} $\implies$ occupies $3$ levels ($S_0, S_1, S_2$)
    \item \textbf{4 heads} $\implies$ occupies $4$ levels ($S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3$)
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\textbf{Initial Skip List Construction} \\
Based on the table, the level distributions are:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{S3:} 6
    \item \textbf{S2:} 6, 25
    \item \textbf{S1:} 6, 9, 17, 25
    \item \textbf{S0:} 3, 6, 7, 9, 12, 17, 19, 21, 25, 26
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    xscale=1.05, yscale=0.85,
    node style/.style={rectangle, draw=black!80, thick, fill=white, minimum width=0.85cm, minimum height=0.5cm, inner sep=2pt, font=\footnotesize},
    label style/.style={text=black!70, anchor=east, font=\footnotesize\bfseries},
    edge style/.style={thick, black!80}
]
    % S3
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 3) {S3};
    \node[node style] (s3_inf1) at (0,3) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s3_6) at (2,3) {6};
    \node[node style] (s3_inf2) at (12,3) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s3_inf1) -- (s3_6) -- (s3_inf2);

    % S2
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 2) {S2};
    \node[node style] (s2_inf1) at (0,2) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s2_6) at (2,2) {6};
    \node[node style] (s2_25) at (10,2) {25};
    \node[node style] (s2_inf2) at (12,2) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s2_inf1) -- (s2_6) -- (s2_25) -- (s2_inf2);

    % S1
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 1) {S1};
    \node[node style] (s1_inf1) at (0,1) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s1_6) at (2,1) {6};
    \node[node style] (s1_9) at (4,1) {9};
    \node[node style] (s1_17) at (6,1) {17};
    \node[node style] (s1_25) at (10,1) {25};
    \node[node style] (s1_inf2) at (12,1) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s1_inf1) -- (s1_6) -- (s1_9) -- (s1_17) -- (s1_25) -- (s1_inf2);

    % S0
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 0) {S0};
    \node[node style] (s0_inf1) at (0,0) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s0_3) at (1,0) {3};
    \node[node style] (s0_6) at (2,0) {6};
    \node[node style] (s0_7) at (3,0) {7};
    \node[node style] (s0_9) at (4,0) {9};
    \node[node style] (s0_12) at (5,0) {12};
    \node[node style] (s0_17) at (6,0) {17};
    \node[node style] (s0_19) at (7,0) {19};
    \node[node style] (s0_21) at (8,0) {21};
    \node[node style] (s0_25) at (10,0) {25};
    \node[node style] (s0_26) at (11,0) {26};
    \node[node style] (s0_inf2) at (12,0) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s0_inf1) -- (s0_3) -- (s0_6) -- (s0_7) -- (s0_9) -- (s0_12) -- (s0_17) -- (s0_19) -- (s0_21) -- (s0_25) -- (s0_26) -- (s0_inf2);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\textbf{1. Search 21} \\
We start at the top-left node ($-\infty$ at level $S_3$) and move right if the next key is $\le 21$, or drop down if the next key is $> 21$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Level S3:} Next is $6 \le 21 \implies$ move to 6. Next is $+\infty > 21 \implies$ drop down to $S_2$.
    \item \textbf{Level S2:} Next is $25 > 21 \implies$ drop down to $S_1$ at 6.
    \item \textbf{Level S1:} Next is $9 \le 21 \implies$ move to 9. Next is $17 \le 21 \implies$ move to 17. Next is $25 > 21 \implies$ drop down to $S_0$ at 17.
    \item \textbf{Level S0:} Next is $19 \le 21 \implies$ move to 19. Next is $21 \implies$ move to 21. $21 == 21 \implies$ \textbf{Target Found}.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\textbf{2. Insert 22 with 3 consecutive heads} \\
\textbf{Procedure:}
With 3 heads, node 22 will occupy levels $S_0, S_1,$ and $S_2$. We first search for 22 to find the predecessor node at each level:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $S_3$: Next from 6 is $+\infty > 22 \implies$ drop down. (No insertion at $S_3$).
    \item $S_2$: Next from 6 is $25 > 22 \implies$ Predecessor is \textbf{6}. Insert 22 after 6.
    \item $S_1$: Path is $6 \rightarrow 9 \rightarrow 17$. Next is $25 > 22 \implies$ Predecessor is \textbf{17}. Insert 22 after 17.
    \item $S_0$: Path is $17 \rightarrow 19 \rightarrow 21$. Next is $25 > 22 \implies$ Predecessor is \textbf{21}. Insert 22 after 21.
\end{itemize}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    xscale=1.05, yscale=0.85,
    node style/.style={rectangle, draw=black!80, thick, fill=white, minimum width=0.85cm, minimum height=0.5cm, inner sep=2pt, font=\footnotesize},
    label style/.style={text=black!70, anchor=east, font=\footnotesize\bfseries},
    edge style/.style={thick, black!80}
]
    % S3
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 3) {S3};
    \node[node style] (s3_inf1) at (0,3) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s3_6) at (2,3) {6};
    \node[node style] (s3_inf2) at (12,3) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s3_inf1) -- (s3_6) -- (s3_inf2);

    % S2
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 2) {S2};
    \node[node style] (s2_inf1) at (0,2) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s2_6) at (2,2) {6};
    \node[node style, fill=gray!15] (s2_22) at (9,2) {22};
    \node[node style] (s2_25) at (10,2) {25};
    \node[node style] (s2_inf2) at (12,2) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s2_inf1) -- (s2_6) -- (s2_22) -- (s2_25) -- (s2_inf2);

    % S1
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 1) {S1};
    \node[node style] (s1_inf1) at (0,1) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s1_6) at (2,1) {6};
    \node[node style] (s1_9) at (4,1) {9};
    \node[node style] (s1_17) at (6,1) {17};
    \node[node style, fill=gray!15] (s1_22) at (9,1) {22};
    \node[node style] (s1_25) at (10,1) {25};
    \node[node style] (s1_inf2) at (12,1) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s1_inf1) -- (s1_6) -- (s1_9) -- (s1_17) -- (s1_22) -- (s1_25) -- (s1_inf2);

    % S0
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 0) {S0};
    \node[node style] (s0_inf1) at (0,0) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s0_3) at (1,0) {3};
    \node[node style] (s0_6) at (2,0) {6};
    \node[node style] (s0_7) at (3,0) {7};
    \node[node style] (s0_9) at (4,0) {9};
    \node[node style] (s0_12) at (5,0) {12};
    \node[node style] (s0_17) at (6,0) {17};
    \node[node style] (s0_19) at (7,0) {19};
    \node[node style] (s0_21) at (8,0) {21};
    \node[node style, fill=gray!15] (s0_22) at (9,0) {22};
    \node[node style] (s0_25) at (10,0) {25};
    \node[node style] (s0_26) at (11,0) {26};
    \node[node style] (s0_inf2) at (12,0) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s0_inf1) -- (s0_3) -- (s0_6) -- (s0_7) -- (s0_9) -- (s0_12) -- (s0_17) -- (s0_19) -- (s0_21) -- (s0_22) -- (s0_25) -- (s0_26) -- (s0_inf2);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\textbf{3. Delete 19} \\
\textbf{Procedure:}
Search for 19 to locate it and its predecessors:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $S_3, S_2$: Traverse and drop at 6.
    \item $S_1$: Traverse $6 \rightarrow 9 \rightarrow 17$. Next is $22 > 19 \implies$ drop down at 17.
    \item $S_0$: Next is 19. \textbf{Target Found}.
\end{itemize}
Since 19 exists only in $S_0$, we update its predecessor's pointer: node 17 at $S_0$ is updated to bypass 19 and point directly to node 21. Node 19 is then removed.
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    xscale=1.05, yscale=0.85,
    node style/.style={rectangle, draw=black!80, thick, fill=white, minimum width=0.85cm, minimum height=0.5cm, inner sep=2pt, font=\footnotesize},
    label style/.style={text=black!70, anchor=east, font=\footnotesize\bfseries},
    edge style/.style={thick, black!80}
]
    % S3
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 3) {S3};
    \node[node style] (s3_inf1) at (0,3) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s3_6) at (2,3) {6};
    \node[node style] (s3_inf2) at (12,3) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s3_inf1) -- (s3_6) -- (s3_inf2);

    % S2
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 2) {S2};
    \node[node style] (s2_inf1) at (0,2) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s2_6) at (2,2) {6};
    \node[node style] (s2_22) at (9,2) {22};
    \node[node style] (s2_25) at (10,2) {25};
    \node[node style] (s2_inf2) at (12,2) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s2_inf1) -- (s2_6) -- (s2_22) -- (s2_25) -- (s2_inf2);

    % S1
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 1) {S1};
    \node[node style] (s1_inf1) at (0,1) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s1_6) at (2,1) {6};
    \node[node style] (s1_9) at (4,1) {9};
    \node[node style] (s1_17) at (6,1) {17};
    \node[node style] (s1_22) at (9,1) {22};
    \node[node style] (s1_25) at (10,1) {25};
    \node[node style] (s1_inf2) at (12,1) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s1_inf1) -- (s1_6) -- (s1_9) -- (s1_17) -- (s1_22) -- (s1_25) -- (s1_inf2);

    % S0
    \node[label style] at (-0.6, 0) {S0};
    \node[node style] (s0_inf1) at (0,0) {$-\infty$};
    \node[node style] (s0_3) at (1,0) {3};
    \node[node style] (s0_6) at (2,0) {6};
    \node[node style] (s0_7) at (3,0) {7};
    \node[node style] (s0_9) at (4,0) {9};
    \node[node style] (s0_12) at (5,0) {12};
    \node[node style] (s0_17) at (6,0) {17};
    % 19 is deleted
    \node[node style] (s0_21) at (8,0) {21};
    \node[node style] (s0_22) at (9,0) {22};
    \node[node style] (s0_25) at (10,0) {25};
    \node[node style] (s0_26) at (11,0) {26};
    \node[node style] (s0_inf2) at (12,0) {$+\infty$};
    \draw[edge style] (s0_inf1) -- (s0_3) -- (s0_6) -- (s0_7) -- (s0_9) -- (s0_12) -- (s0_17) -- (s0_21) -- (s0_22) -- (s0_25) -- (s0_26) -- (s0_inf2);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4cm}
\textbf{4. Search 18} \\
\textbf{Procedure:}
We search the updated skip list for 18 starting at $S_3$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Level S3:} Move to 6. Next is $+\infty > 18 \implies$ drop down to $S_2$.
    \item \textbf{Level S2:} At 6, next is $22 > 18 \implies$ drop down to $S_1$.
    \item \textbf{Level S1:} Move to 9. Move to 17. Next is $22 > 18 \implies$ drop down to $S_0$.
    \item \textbf{Level S0:} At 17, next is 21 (because 19 was deleted). $21 > 18 \implies$ Since we are at the base level and the next element is strictly greater than the target, we conclude \textbf{Target 18 is Not Found}.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Show that the expected running time of searching in a probabilistic skip-list is $O(\log n)$. \mk{6}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2011--12}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer: Proof of $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ Expected Search Time (6 Marks)}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

To prove that the expected search time in a probabilistic skip list with $n$ entries is $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$, we use the standard technique of \textbf{Backward Analysis}. 

\subsection*{1. Tracing the Search Path Backwards}
Instead of tracing the forward search path down and to the right, we trace the path \textbf{backwards} starting from the target node at the base level (level 0) up to the header node at the top level.

At any given node $v$ at level $i$ during this reverse traversal:
\begin{itemize}
    \item The path moves \textbf{Up} to level $i+1$ if $v$ was promoted to level $i+1$. In the random choice configuration of a skip list, this occurs with a fixed constant probability $p$ (typically $p = 0.5$).
    \item The path moves \textbf{Left} to a preceding node at the same level $i$ if $v$ was not promoted to level $i+1$. This occurs with probability $1-p$.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{2. Cost Per Level (Geometric Distribution)}
Let $C$ be a random variable representing the total number of steps (upward and leftward) taken in the backward traversal. 

The number of leftward steps taken at any level before successfully moving up one level corresponds to a sequence of independent trials ending on the first success (moving up). This fits a \textbf{Geometric Distribution} with a success probability of $p$. 

The expected number of steps required to ascend exactly \textbf{one level} is:
\begin{equation*}
    E[\text{steps per level}] = \frac{1}{p}
\end{equation*}

\subsection*{3. Bounding the Maximum Height}
The probability that a given node reaches a level $k$ is $p^k$. Using the union bound, the probability that any of the $n$ nodes reaches a level $k = \log_{1/p} n$ is at most:
\begin{equation*}
    n \cdot p^{\log_{1/p} n} = n \cdot \frac{1}{n} = 1
\end{equation*}
With high probability, the maximum height $L$ of the skip list is strictly bounded by:
\begin{equation*}
    E[L] \leq \log_{1/p} n + \frac{1}{1-p} = \mathcal{O}(\log n)
\end{equation*}

\subsection*{4. Total Expected Search Time}
By the linearity of expectation, the total expected number of steps $E[C]$ to climb from level 0 to the top level is the expected number of steps per level multiplied by the expected maximum height:
\begin{align*}
    E[C] &\leq (\text{Expected steps per level}) \times E[L] \\
    E[C] &\leq \frac{1}{p} \cdot \left( \log_{1/p} n + \frac{1}{1-p} \right)
\end{align*}

Since $p$ is a fixed constant fraction independent of $n$, the terms $\frac{1}{p}$, $\log_{1/p}$, and $\frac{1}{1-p}$ are all constants. Thus, the expression simplifies asymptotically to:
\begin{equation*}
    E[C] = \mathcal{O}(\log n)
\end{equation*}

\textbf{Conclusion:} Because each step in a skip list search involves a constant number of pointer evaluations ($\mathcal{O}(1)$ time), the total expected running time for a search operation is bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2020-21
\end{enumerate}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cB}{Advanced Heaps (Binomial Heaps)}
\label{sub:s2-binheap}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item Prove that there are $\binom{k}{i}$ nodes at depth $i$ in a binomial heap $B_k$ of order $k$.
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25, 2019--20, 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Terminology Note:} In standard computer science literature, $B_k$ denotes a \textit{Binomial Tree} of order $k$. A \textit{Binomial Heap} is defined as a collection of binomial trees. The property regarding $\binom{k}{i}$ nodes at depth $i$ strictly applies to a single binomial tree $B_k$. We will construct the formal proof for the binomial tree $B_k$.

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{Theorem}
A binomial tree $B_k$ of order $k \ge 0$ has exactly $\binom{k}{i}$ nodes at depth $i$, for $i = 0, 1, 2, \dots, k$.

\subsubsection*{Proof by Mathematical Induction}

We will prove this theorem using induction on the order of the binomial tree, $k$. Let $D(k, i)$ denote the number of nodes at depth $i$ in a binomial tree $B_k$.

\textbf{1. Base Case ($k = 0$):}
A binomial tree of order 0, $B_0$, consists of a single node (the root). 
The root is at depth $i = 0$. 
The number of nodes at depth $0$ is $1$. 
Evaluating the binomial coefficient, we have:
$$ \binom{0}{0} = 1 $$
Since $D(0,0) = \binom{0}{0}$, the base case holds.

\textbf{2. Inductive Hypothesis:}
Assume that the theorem holds for a binomial tree $B_{k-1}$ of order $k-1$. That is, for all depths $i$, the number of nodes in $B_{k-1}$ is given by:
$$ D(k-1, i) = \binom{k-1}{i} $$
\textit{(Note: If $i > k-1$ or $i < 0$, $\binom{k-1}{i} = 0$, which correctly reflects that there are no nodes at those depths).}

\textbf{3. Inductive Step:}
We must show that the property holds for $B_k$, meaning $D(k, i) = \binom{k}{i}$.

By the structural definition of binomial trees, a tree $B_k$ is constructed by linking two binomial trees of order $k-1$. Let these two trees be $T_1$ and $T_2$. The linkage is performed by making the root of $T_2$ the leftmost child of the root of $T_1$.

Because $T_2$ is attached as a subtree to the root of $T_1$, the depth of every node in $T_2$ increases by exactly $1$ relative to its original depth in $T_1$. Thus, the nodes at depth $i$ in the newly formed $B_k$ originate from two disjoint sets:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Nodes from $T_1$ that were already at depth $i$.
    \item Nodes from $T_2$ that were previously at depth $i-1$ (since their depth has increased by $1$).
\end{itemize}

Therefore, the total number of nodes at depth $i$ in $B_k$ is the sum of these two quantities:
$$ D(k, i) = D(k-1, i) + D(k-1, i-1) $$

By substituting our inductive hypothesis into this equation, we get:
$$ D(k, i) = \binom{k-1}{i} + \binom{k-1}{i-1} $$

According to \textbf{Pascal's Identity} in combinatorics, the sum of these two adjacent binomial coefficients is identically $\binom{k}{i}$:
$$ \binom{k-1}{i} + \binom{k-1}{i-1} = \binom{k}{i} $$

Thus, we conclude:
$$ D(k, i) = \binom{k}{i} $$

\textbf{Boundary conditions check:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item For $i = 0$ (the root level): $D(k, 0) = D(k-1, 0) + 0 = \binom{k-1}{0} = 1 = \binom{k}{0}$.
    \item For $i = k$ (the maximum depth): $D(k, k) = 0 + D(k-1, k-1) = \binom{k-1}{k-1} = 1 = \binom{k}{k}$.
\end{itemize}

Both boundary conditions are satisfied. By the Principle of Mathematical Induction, the theorem is true for all $k \ge 0$. \hfill $\blacksquare$

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{Visualizing the Depths (Example for $B_3$)}

The property directly mirrors Pascal's Triangle. The diagram below illustrates $B_3$, formed by linking two $B_2$ trees. The depths correspond to the $3^{\text{rd}}$ row of Pascal's Triangle: $1, 3, 3, 1$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    thick,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3.5cm, level distance=1.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2cm, level distance=1.5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.5cm},
    every node/.style={circle, draw, fill=cB!15, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries\color{black}}
]

  % Root
  \node (root) {$0$}
    child { node {$1$} 
        child { node {$2$} 
            child { node {$3$} }
        }
        child { node {$2$} }
    }
    child { node {$1$} 
        child { node {$2$} }
    }
    child { node {$1$} };

  % Depth Labels
  \begin{scope}[every node/.style={right, draw=none, fill=none, font=\small, color=black!80}]
    \draw[dashed, thin, gray] (-4.5, 0) -- (4, 0) node {Depth 0: $\binom{3}{0} = 1$};
    \draw[dashed, thin, gray] (-4.5, -1.5) -- (4, -1.5) node {Depth 1: $\binom{3}{1} = 3$};
    \draw[dashed, thin, gray] (-4.5, -3.0) -- (4, -3.0) node {Depth 2: $\binom{3}{2} = 3$};
    \draw[dashed, thin, gray] (-4.5, -4.5) -- (4, -4.5) node {Depth 3: $\binom{3}{3} = 1$};
  \end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Structural Complexity}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Total Nodes:} Summing the nodes at all depths yields $\sum_{i=0}^{k} \binom{k}{i} = 2^k$. A binomial tree of order $k$ always contains exactly $2^k$ nodes.
    \item \textbf{Maximum Degree:} The root node has the highest degree, which is $k$ (i.e., $\log_2(N)$ where $N$ is the number of nodes). This guarantees that heap operations achieve logarithmic time bounds.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item Draw the maximally damaged tree resulting from $B_6$.
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25, 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsubsection*{Concept: The Maximally Damaged Tree}
In the context of advanced data structures like \textbf{Fibonacci Heaps}, a binomial tree $B_k$ becomes "damaged" when nodes are cut from it during `Decrease-Key` operations. 

To maintain a bounded maximum degree, Fibonacci heaps enforce a \textit{cascading cut} rule: a non-root node can lose at most \textbf{one} child before it is forcibly cut from its parent. Therefore, a \textbf{maximally damaged tree} of order $k$ (often denoted as $F_k$ or a tree of rank $k$) is formed from $B_k$ under the following worst-case conditions:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Every non-root node loses exactly one child (any more would trigger a cascading cut).
    \item To minimize the remaining size of the tree, the lost child is the \textit{heaviest} (highest degree) subtree.
    \item The root maintains its original $k$ children (thus remaining a tree of order/rank $k$).
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Deriving the Structure for $B_6$}
A standard binomial tree $B_6$ has exactly $2^6 = 64$ nodes. The root has 6 children with degrees: $5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0$.
Applying the maximum damage rule recursively:
\begin{itemize}
    \item The child of degree $5$ loses its heaviest child (degree 4), becoming degree $4$.
    \item The child of degree $4$ loses its heaviest child (degree 3), becoming degree $3$.
    \item \dots and so on. The child of degree $0$ cannot lose a child, so it remains degree $0$.
\end{itemize}
The new degrees of the root's children become precisely: \textbf{4, 3, 2, 1, 0, 0}. 

This recursive "thinning" mimics the Fibonacci sequence. Let $S_k$ be the minimum number of nodes in a maximally damaged tree of rank $k$. The recurrence relation is $S_k = S_{k-2} + S_{k-1}$ (with base cases $S_0 = 1, S_1 = 2$). The total size strictly follows the Fibonacci sequence $S_k = F_{k+2}$ (where $F_1=1, F_2=1, F_3=2, \dots$). 

For $B_6$, the maximally damaged tree has exactly $\mathbf{F_8 = 21}$ \textbf{nodes} (a significant reduction from 64).

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{Diagram: Maximally Damaged Tree from $B_6$}
\textit{Note: The integer inside each node represents its current \textbf{degree} (number of children). The tree is spread horizontally to prevent overlap.}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    scale=0.9, transform shape,
    thick,
    main/.style={circle, draw=cB!80!black, fill=cB!10, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge/.style={draw=black!70, thick}
]

    % Root Level 0
    \node[main, fill=cB!30] (root) at (1, 0) {6};

    % Level 1 (Children of Root)
    \node[main] (c1) at (-4, -1.5) {0};
    \node[main] (c2) at (-3, -1.5) {0};
    \node[main] (c3) at (-2, -1.5) {1};
    \node[main] (c4) at (-0.5, -1.5) {2};
    \node[main] (c5) at (2, -1.5) {3};
    \node[main] (c6) at (6, -1.5) {4};

    % Level 2
    \node[main] (c31) at (-2, -3) {0};
    \node[main] (c41) at (-1, -3) {0};
    \node[main] (c42) at (0, -3) {0};
    \node[main] (c51) at (1, -3) {0};
    \node[main] (c52) at (2, -3) {0};
    \node[main] (c53) at (3, -3) {1};
    \node[main] (c61) at (4.5, -3) {0};
    \node[main] (c62) at (5.5, -3) {0};
    \node[main] (c63) at (6.5, -3) {1};
    \node[main] (c64) at (8, -3) {2};

    % Level 3
    \node[main] (c531) at (3, -4.5) {0};
    \node[main] (c631) at (6.5, -4.5) {0};
    \node[main] (c641) at (7.5, -4.5) {0};
    \node[main] (c642) at (8.5, -4.5) {0};

    % Edges from Root
    \draw[edge] (root) -- (c1);
    \draw[edge] (root) -- (c2);
    \draw[edge] (root) -- (c3);
    \draw[edge] (root) -- (c4);
    \draw[edge] (root) -- (c5);
    \draw[edge] (root) -- (c6);

    % Edges from Level 1
    \draw[edge] (c3) -- (c31);
    
    \draw[edge] (c4) -- (c41); 
    \draw[edge] (c4) -- (c42);
    
    \draw[edge] (c5) -- (c51); 
    \draw[edge] (c5) -- (c52); 
    \draw[edge] (c5) -- (c53);
    
    \draw[edge] (c6) -- (c61); 
    \draw[edge] (c6) -- (c62); 
    \draw[edge] (c6) -- (c63); 
    \draw[edge] (c6) -- (c64);

    % Edges from Level 2
    \draw[edge] (c53) -- (c531);
    
    \draw[edge] (c63) -- (c631);
    
    \draw[edge] (c64) -- (c641); 
    \draw[edge] (c64) -- (c642);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{Complexity \& Analytical Properties}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Total Nodes:} Summing the nodes yields $1 \text{ (root)} + 1 + 1 + 2 + 3 + 5 + 8 = \textbf{21}$. 
    \item \textbf{Implication on Time Complexity:} Because a tree of order $k$ cannot have fewer than $F_{k+2}$ nodes, and $F_{k+2} \ge \phi^k$ (where $\phi$ is the golden ratio $\approx 1.618$), the maximum degree $D(n)$ of any node in an $n$-node Fibonacci Heap is bounded by $\log_\phi n = \mathcal{O}(\log n)$. This tight mathematical bound is the core reason \texttt{Extract-Min} operates in amortized $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ time despite extreme potential tree damage.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item Let $H_1$ and $H_2$ be binomial heaps. Suppose the highest order tree in heap $H_1$ is of order $m$, and the highest order tree in $H_2$ has degree $m+1$. Prove or disprove: ``$H_2$ must have more nodes than $H_1$, regardless of how many trees are present in $H_1$ and $H_2$.''
\mk{7} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsubsection*{Verdict: True}
We will \textbf{prove} the statement. The claim is rigorously true due to the foundational structural properties of binomial heaps, which possess a direct isomorphism with the binary numeral system.

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{Formal Proof}
By the strict definition of a binomial heap, for any integer $k \ge 0$, a binomial heap can contain \textbf{at most one} binomial tree of order $k$. Furthermore, the number of nodes in a single binomial tree of order $k$, denoted $B_k$, is exactly $2^k$.

\textbf{Step 1: Establishing the Upper Bound for $H_1$}
Let $N_1$ be the total number of nodes in $H_1$. We are given that the highest order tree in $H_1$ is $m$. To maximize the number of nodes in $H_1$, we must assume the "densest" possible configuration, where $H_1$ contains a binomial tree of \textit{every} possible order from $0$ up to $m$. 

Thus, the maximum possible number of nodes in $H_1$ is the sum of a geometric series:
$$ \max(N_1) = \sum_{k=0}^{m} |B_k| = \sum_{k=0}^{m} 2^k $$
Using the standard geometric series formula $\sum_{i=0}^{n} r^i = \frac{r^{n+1}-1}{r-1}$, this evaluates to:
$$ \max(N_1) = 2^{m+1} - 1 $$

\textbf{Step 2: Establishing the Lower Bound for $H_2$}
Let $N_2$ be the total number of nodes in $H_2$. We are given that the highest order tree in $H_2$ has order $m+1$. This guarantees that $H_2$ contains \textit{at least} the tree $B_{m+1}$. To minimize the number of nodes in $H_2$, we assume the "sparsest" possible configuration, where it contains \textit{only} this single tree and no others.

Thus, the minimum possible number of nodes in $H_2$ is exactly the size of $B_{m+1}$:
$$ \min(N_2) = |B_{m+1}| = 2^{m+1} $$

\textbf{Step 3: Direct Comparison}
Comparing the strict bounds established above, we observe:
$$ \max(N_1) = 2^{m+1} - 1 $$
$$ \min(N_2) = 2^{m+1} $$
Since $2^{m+1} - 1 < 2^{m+1}$ holds for all integers $m$, we can chain the inequalities:
$$ N_1 \le \max(N_1) < \min(N_2) \le N_2 $$
$$ N_1 < N_2 $$
Therefore, $H_2$ must strictly contain more nodes than $H_1$, proving the statement. \hfill $\blacksquare$

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{Intuition via Binary Representation}
A fundamental property of a binomial heap with $N$ nodes is its one-to-one mapping with the binary representation of the integer $N$. Specifically, a binomial tree $B_k$ is present in the heap if and only if the $k$-th bit of $N$ (using 0-based indexing from right to left) is $1$.

\begin{center}
    \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5}
    \begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
        \hline
        \textbf{Property} & \textbf{Heap $H_1$ (Highest order $m$)} & \textbf{Heap $H_2$ (Highest order $m+1$)} \\
        \hline
        Highest set bit position & $m$ & $m+1$ \\
        Binary layout & $01 \_ \_ \dots \_ _2$ (length $m+1$) & $1 \_ \_ \dots \_ _2$ (length $m+2$) \\
        Maximum possible value & $\underbrace{11\dots1_2}_{m+1 \text{ bits}} = 2^{m+1} - 1$ & Arbitrarily large, but at least $2^{m+1}$ \\
        Minimum possible value & $10\dots0_2 = 2^m$ & $10\dots0_2 = 2^{m+1}$ \\
        \hline
    \end{tabular}
\end{center}

Because adding a new, more significant bit (position $m+1$) to a binary number intrinsically results in a value larger than \textit{any} possible combination of lower-order bits (positions $0$ through $m$), the node count $N_2$ inherently dominates $N_1$.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Prove the following properties for a binomial tree $B_k$ of order $k$:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item The number of nodes is $2^k$.
  \item The height is $k$.
  \item The degree of root is $k$.
\end{enumerate}
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsubsection*{Structural Definition of a Binomial Tree}
Before proving the properties, we formally state the recursive definition of a binomial tree $B_k$ of order $k \ge 0$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Base Case:} A binomial tree $B_0$ consists of a single node.
    \item \textbf{Recursive Step:} A binomial tree $B_k$ (for $k > 0$) is constructed by linking two binomial trees of order $k-1$. Specifically, the root of one $B_{k-1}$ tree is made the leftmost child of the root of the other $B_{k-1}$ tree.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    thick,
    triangle/.style={regular polygon, regular polygon sides=3, draw, fill=cB!10, minimum size=2.5cm, shape border rotate=180, outer sep=0},
    node/.style={circle, draw, fill=cB!30, minimum size=0.6cm, inner sep=0, font=\bfseries}
]
    % Left B_{k-1}
    \node[node] (root1) at (0, 0) {};
    \node[triangle] (t1) at (0, -1.3) {$B_{k-1}$};
    
    % Right B_{k-1}
    \node[node] (root2) at (2.5, -1) {};
    \node[triangle] (t2) at (2.5, -2.3) {$B_{k-1}$};

    % Link
    \draw[->, thick, shorten >= 2pt, shorten <= 2pt] (root1) -- (root2) node[midway, above right, font=\small] {linked as child};

    % B_k Label
    \node[font=\large\bfseries] at (1.25, -3.8) {Formation of $B_k$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

We will prove all three properties using \textbf{Mathematical Induction} on the order $k$.

\subsubsection*{1. Proof: The number of nodes is $2^k$}
Let $N(k)$ be the number of nodes in a binomial tree $B_k$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Base Case ($k=0$):} $B_0$ is defined as a single node. Thus, $N(0) = 1$. Evaluating the formula, $2^0 = 1$. The base case holds.
    \item \textbf{Inductive Hypothesis:} Assume that a binomial tree $B_{k-1}$ has $N(k-1) = 2^{k-1}$ nodes.
    \item \textbf{Inductive Step:} By definition, $B_k$ is formed by linking two $B_{k-1}$ trees. Because the sets of nodes in both trees are disjoint, the total number of nodes in $B_k$ is the sum of the nodes in the two $B_{k-1}$ trees:
    $$ N(k) = N(k-1) + N(k-1) $$
    Substitute the inductive hypothesis:
    $$ N(k) = 2^{k-1} + 2^{k-1} = 2 \cdot 2^{k-1} = 2^k $$
    This proves the property for all $k \ge 0$. \hfill $\blacksquare$
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{2. Proof: The height is $k$}
Let $H(k)$ denote the height (maximum depth of any node, where the root is at depth 0) of $B_k$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Base Case ($k=0$):} $B_0$ consists of a single node (the root). Its height is $0$. The base case $H(0) = 0$ holds.
    \item \textbf{Inductive Hypothesis:} Assume that $H(k-1) = k-1$.
    \item \textbf{Inductive Step:} $B_k$ consists of a primary $B_{k-1}$ (whose root becomes the root of $B_k$) and an attached $B_{k-1}$ (whose root becomes a child of the primary root).
    The depth of every node in the attached $B_{k-1}$ increases by $1$ relative to the new root. Therefore, the maximum depth in the attached portion is $H(k-1) + 1$.
    The maximum depth in the primary portion remains $H(k-1)$.
    The height of $B_k$ is the maximum of these two:
    $$ H(k) = \max \big( H(k-1), \, H(k-1) + 1 \big) $$
    Clearly, $H(k-1) + 1$ is strictly greater. Substituting the inductive hypothesis:
    $$ H(k) = (k-1) + 1 = k $$
    This proves the property for all $k \ge 0$. \hfill $\blacksquare$
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{3. Proof: The degree of the root is $k$}
Let $D(k)$ denote the degree (number of direct children) of the root of $B_k$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Base Case ($k=0$):} $B_0$ is a single node with no children. Thus, $D(0) = 0$. The base case holds.
    \item \textbf{Inductive Hypothesis:} Assume that the root of $B_{k-1}$ has degree $D(k-1) = k-1$.
    \item \textbf{Inductive Step:} When forming $B_k$, the root of one $B_{k-1}$ tree is made a new child of the root of the other $B_{k-1}$ tree. The root of the primary $B_{k-1}$ retains all its original $k-1$ children and gains exactly one new child (the root of the attached tree).
    Thus, the degree of the new root is:
    $$ D(k) = D(k-1) + 1 $$
    Substituting the inductive hypothesis:
    $$ D(k) = (k-1) + 1 = k $$
    This proves the property for all $k \ge 0$. \hfill $\blacksquare$
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item Perform union of two binomial min heaps with six and seven nodes respectively. You can use any valid values in the nodes.
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsubsection*{1. Foundation and Structural Analysis}

The operation of uniting two binomial heaps fundamentally mirrors binary addition. 
Let $H_1$ be the heap with 6 nodes and $H_2$ be the heap with 7 nodes. 
The sizes dictate the exact structural composition of the heaps via their binary representations:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Size of $H_1 = 6_{10} = 110_2$. Therefore, $H_1$ contains binomial trees of orders $1$ and $2$ ($B_1$ and $B_2$).
    \item Size of $H_2 = 7_{10} = 111_2$. Therefore, $H_2$ contains binomial trees of orders $0$, $1$, and $2$ ($B_0$, $B_1$, and $B_2$).
\end{itemize}

When we perform $H_1 \cup H_2$, the final number of nodes is $6 + 7 = 13_{10} = 1101_2$. 
This mathematical certainty guarantees that our resulting heap, $H_{final}$, will contain exactly three binomial trees: $B_0$, $B_2$, and $B_3$.

\subsubsection*{2. Initial Heaps Setup}

We initialize $H_1$ and $H_2$ with valid values that rigorously maintain the \textbf{Min-Heap property} (every parent is strictly $\le$ its children). In standard binomial tree representation, children are typically ordered left-to-right by decreasing degree ($B_{k-1}, B_{k-2}, \dots, B_0$).

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    thick,
    node/.style={circle, draw, fill=cB!15, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0, font=\bfseries}
]
    % --- HEAP 1 ---
    \node[font=\bfseries, text=cB] at (1, 1) {Heap $H_1$ (6 nodes)};
    
    % B1 of H1
    \node[node] (h1_10) at (0, 0) {10};
    \node[node] (h1_20) at (0, -1) {20};
    \draw (h1_10) -- (h1_20);
    \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (0, 0.6) {$B_1$};
    
    % B2 of H1
    \node[node] (h1_5) at (3, 0) {5};
    \node[node] (h1_8) at (2, -1) {8};
    \node[node] (h1_15) at (4, -1) {15};
    \node[node] (h1_12) at (2, -2) {12};
    \draw (h1_5) -- (h1_8); \draw (h1_5) -- (h1_15); \draw (h1_8) -- (h1_12);
    \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (3, 0.6) {$B_2$};
    
    \draw[->, thick, dashed] (h1_10) -- (h1_5);

    % --- HEAP 2 ---
    \begin{scope}[xshift=8cm]
    \node[font=\bfseries, text=cB] at (2.5, 1) {Heap $H_2$ (7 nodes)};
    
    % B0 of H2
    \node[node] (h2_7) at (0, 0) {7};
    \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (0, 0.6) {$B_0$};
    
    % B1 of H2
    \node[node] (h2_11) at (2, 0) {11};
    \node[node] (h2_22) at (2, -1) {22};
    \draw (h2_11) -- (h2_22);
    \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (2, 0.6) {$B_1$};
    
    % B2 of H2
    \node[node] (h2_3) at (5, 0) {3};
    \node[node] (h2_6) at (4, -1) {6};
    \node[node] (h2_14) at (6, -1) {14};
    \node[node] (h2_18) at (4, -2) {18};
    \draw (h2_3) -- (h2_6); \draw (h2_3) -- (h2_14); \draw (h2_6) -- (h2_18);
    \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (5, 0.6) {$B_2$};
    
    \draw[->, thick, dashed] (h2_7) -- (h2_11);
    \draw[->, thick, dashed] (h2_11) -- (h2_3);
    \end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. The Union Operation Step-by-Step}

\textbf{Step 3.1: Merge Root Lists} \\
We merge the root lists of $H_1$ and $H_2$ into a single linked list, sorted strictly in monotonically increasing order of degree.
$$ \text{Merged List:} \quad \mathbf{B_0(7) \rightarrow B_1(10) \rightarrow B_1(11) \rightarrow B_2(5) \rightarrow B_2(3)} $$

\textbf{Step 3.2: Link Trees of Equal Degree} \\
We traverse the merged list using three pointers: \texttt{prev-x}, \texttt{x}, and \texttt{next-x}. We combine trees of the same degree using the standard Binomial Heap linkage rule (the root with the smaller value becomes the parent).

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Initial State:} \texttt{x} points to $B_0(7)$. \texttt{next-x} is $B_1(10)$. Degrees differ. We advance the pointers.
    
    \item \textbf{First Linkage ($B_1 \cup B_1$):} \texttt{x} points to $B_1(10)$, \texttt{next-x} points to $B_1(11)$. The following tree is $B_2(5)$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Degrees match ($1=1$). We compare roots: $10 < 11$.
        \item $B_1(11)$ is made the leftmost child of $B_1(10)$, forming a new $B_2(10)$.
    \end{itemize}
    \textit{Intermediate List:} $\quad B_0(7) \rightarrow B_2(10) \rightarrow B_2(5) \rightarrow B_2(3)$
    
    \item \textbf{Three trees of same degree ($B_2, B_2, B_2$):} \texttt{x} points to $B_2(10)$, \texttt{next-x} is $B_2(5)$, and \texttt{next-x.sibling} is $B_2(3)$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Because \textbf{three} consecutive trees have the same degree, standard algorithms prescribe advancing \texttt{x} by one position to avoid destroying the monotonically increasing property. We skip linking the first pair.
        \item \texttt{x} now points to $B_2(5)$.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Second Linkage ($B_2 \cup B_2$):} \texttt{x} points to $B_2(5)$, \texttt{next-x} points to $B_2(3)$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Degrees match ($2=2$). We compare roots: $3 < 5$.
        \item $B_2(5)$ is made the leftmost child of $B_2(3)$, forming a new $B_3(3)$.
    \end{itemize}
    \textit{Final List:} $\quad B_0(7) \rightarrow B_2(10) \rightarrow B_3(3)$
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{4. Final Resulting Heap ($H_{final}$)}

The resulting heap contains exactly 13 nodes, properly distributed among $B_0, B_2$, and $B_3$.

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    thick,
    node/.style={circle, draw, fill=cB!15, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0, font=\bfseries}
]
    % --- FINAL HEAP ---
    
    % B0
    \node[node] (f_7) at (-3, 0) {7};
    \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (-3, 0.6) {$B_0$};
    
    % B2 (Root 10)
    \node[node] (f_10) at (0, 0) {10};
    \node[node] (f_11) at (-1, -1) {11};
    \node[node] (f_20) at (1, -1) {20};
    \node[node] (f_22) at (-1, -2) {22};
    \draw (f_10) -- (f_11); \draw (f_10) -- (f_20); \draw (f_11) -- (f_22);
    \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (0, 0.6) {$B_2$};
    
    % B3 (Root 3)
    \node[node] (f_3) at (5, 0) {3};
    \node[node] (f_5) at (2, -1) {5};
    \node[node] (f_6) at (5, -1) {6};
    \node[node] (f_14) at (8, -1) {14};
    
    % Children of 5
    \node[node] (f_8) at (1, -2) {8};
    \node[node] (f_15) at (3, -2) {15};
    \node[node] (f_12) at (1, -3) {12};
    
    % Children of 6
    \node[node] (f_18) at (5, -2) {18};
    
    \draw (f_3) -- (f_5); \draw (f_3) -- (f_6); \draw (f_3) -- (f_14);
    \draw (f_5) -- (f_8); \draw (f_5) -- (f_15); \draw (f_8) -- (f_12);
    \draw (f_6) -- (f_18);
    \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (5, 0.6) {$B_3$};

    % Root list pointers
    \draw[->, thick, dashed] (f_7) -- (f_10);
    \draw[->, thick, dashed] (f_10) -- (f_3);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$. Let $N = N_1 + N_2$. The maximum degree of any tree is $\lfloor \log_2 N \rfloor$. Thus, merging the root lists takes $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$, and iterating through the merged list to link trees takes $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ pointer operations. 
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(1)$ auxiliary space. The union is performed strictly in-place by updating existing pointers.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item Answer the following questions for two max priority queues \texttt{pq1} and \texttt{pq2} implemented using binomial heap. Both are initially empty.
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Insert $-5, 20, 4, 19, 1, -3, 6, 12, 3, 2, 18$ in \texttt{pq1} and $25, 21, 10, 13, 17, 9, 8, 11, 7$ in \texttt{pq2}. Draw the two binomial heaps after the insertion.
  \item Meld \texttt{pq1} and \texttt{pq2} and then decrease the key of node 20 to $-20$ from the melded priority queue. Show the necessary steps.
  \item What will be the total actual cost in credits if we call the Extract-Max operation two times consecutively on the resultant priority queue? Assume that unlinking a node from its children takes one credit, two node addition operations take one credit, an insertion of a node takes one credit, and a two-key comparison takes one credit. All other operations take zero credit.
\end{enumerate}
\mk{23} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsubsection*{1. Binomial Heaps After Insertion}

Both priority queues are constructed by inserting elements one by one, maintaining the max-heap property (every parent is strictly $\ge$ its children), and merging trees of equal degrees. 

\textbf{Priority Queue 1 (\texttt{pq1})} \\
Elements inserted: $-5, 20, 4, 19, 1, -3, 6, 12, 3, 2, 18$ ($11$ nodes). \\
Binary representation of 11 is $1011_2$. Thus, \texttt{pq1} contains three binomial trees: $B_0, B_1,$ and $B_3$.

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    thick,
    level/.style={sibling distance=25mm/#1, level distance=12mm},
    node/.style={circle, draw, fill=cB!15, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0, font=\bfseries}
]
    % B0
    \node[node] (b0) at (0, 0) {18};
    \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (0, 0.6) {$B_0$};

    % B1
    \node[node] (b1) at (2, 0) {3}
        child { node[node] {2} };
    \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (2, 0.6) {$B_1$};

    % B3
    \begin{scope}[xshift=6cm]
        \node[node] (b3) {20}
            child { node[node] {12}
                child { node[node] {1}
                    child { node[node] {-3} }
                }
                child { node[node] {6} }
            }
            child { node[node] {19}
                child { node[node] {4} }
            }
            child { node[node] {-5} };
        \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (0, 0.6) {$B_3$};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{1em}

\textbf{Priority Queue 2 (\texttt{pq2})} \\
Elements inserted: $25, 21, 10, 13, 17, 9, 8, 11, 7$ ($9$ nodes). \\
Binary representation of 9 is $1001_2$. Thus, \texttt{pq2} contains two binomial trees: $B_0$ and $B_3$.

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    thick,
    level/.style={sibling distance=25mm/#1, level distance=12mm},
    node/.style={circle, draw, fill=cB!15, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0, font=\bfseries}
]
    % B0
    \node[node] (b0) at (0, 0) {7};
    \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (0, 0.6) {$B_0$};

    % B3
    \begin{scope}[xshift=4cm]
        \node[node] (b3) {25}
            child { node[node] {17}
                child { node[node] {11}
                    child { node[node] {8} }
                }
                child { node[node] {9} }
            }
            child { node[node] {13}
                child { node[node] {10} }
            }
            child { node[node] {21} };
        \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (0, 0.6) {$B_3$};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{1em}

\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Melding and Decrease-Key}

\textbf{Step A: Melding \texttt{pq1} and \texttt{pq2}} \\
To meld the two queues, we add them together like binary numbers ($11 + 9 = 20 \implies 10100_2$), merging trees of the same degree:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{$B_0$ merge:} $18$ (\texttt{pq1}) and $7$ (\texttt{pq2}). Max is $18$, so $7$ becomes a child of $18$. Forms a carry $B_1$.
    \item \textbf{$B_1$ merge:} $3$ (\texttt{pq1}) and carry $18$. Max is $18$, so $3$ becomes a child of $18$. Forms a carry $B_2$.
    \item \textbf{$B_2$ merge:} Only the carry $B_2(18)$ exists. It drops down into the melded root list.
    \item \textbf{$B_3$ merge:} $20$ (\texttt{pq1}) and $25$ (\texttt{pq2}). Max is $25$, so $20$ becomes a child of $25$. Forms a $B_4$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step B: Decrease-Key of 20 to -20} \\
When node $20$ is changed to $-20$, it violates the max-heap property. We sift it down:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Compare $-20$ with its children ($12, 19, -5$). The largest is $19$. Swap $-20$ and $19$.
    \item Node $-20$ inherits $19$'s old child, which is $4$.
    \item Compare $-20$ with its new child ($4$). $4 > -20$. Swap $-20$ and $4$.
    \item Node $-20$ is now a leaf. Max-heap property is restored.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    thick,
    level/.style={sibling distance=35mm/#1, level distance=12mm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=12mm},
    node/.style={circle, draw, fill=cB!15, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0, font=\bfseries}
]
    % Final B2
    \node[node] (b2) at (0, 0) {18}
        child { node[node] {3} 
            child { node[node] {2} }
        }
        child { node[node] {7} };
    \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (0, 0.6) {$B_2$};

    % Final B4
    \begin{scope}[xshift=6cm]
        \node[node] (b4) {25}
            child { node[node] {19}
                child { node[node] {12}
                    child { node[node] {1} }
                    child { node[node] {6} }
                }
                child { node[node] {4}
                    child { node[node] {-20} }
                }
                child { node[node] {-5} }
            }
            child { node[node] {17}
                child { node[node] {11}
                    child { node[node] {8} }
                }
                child { node[node] {9} }
            }
            child { node[node] {13}
                child { node[node] {10} }
            }
            child { node[node] {21} };
        \node[font=\small, color=gray] at (0, 0.6) {$B_4$};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{1em}

\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Cost Analysis of Consecutive \texttt{Extract-Max} Operations}

We evaluate the actual cost based on the explicit credit rules:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Unlink node from children: \textbf{1 credit}
    \item Two node additions: \textbf{1 credit} (1 addition = 0.5 credits)
    \item Node insertion: \textbf{1 credit}
    \item Two-key comparison: \textbf{1 credit}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{First \texttt{Extract-Max} Operation:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Find max root:} Compare the two roots ($18$ and $25$). $\rightarrow$ \textbf{1 credit}
    \item \textbf{Remove \& Unlink:} Remove $25$ and unlink it from its $4$ children. $\rightarrow$ \textbf{1 credit}
    \item \textbf{Add Children:} Add the $4$ severed children to the root list. ($4$ additions $= 2 \times$ ``two node additions''). $\rightarrow$ \textbf{2 credits}
    \item \textbf{Consolidate:} We now have trees of degree $0(21), 1(13), 2(18), 2(17),$ and $3(19)$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Compare $B_2(18)$ and $B_2(17)$ to merge. $\rightarrow$ \textbf{1 credit}
        \item Compare $B_3(18)$ and $B_3(19)$ to merge. $\rightarrow$ \textbf{1 credit}
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Update Max Pointer:} Scan the remaining $3$ roots ($21, 13, 19$) to cache the new max for the next operation. Requires $2$ comparisons. $\rightarrow$ \textbf{2 credits}
\end{enumerate}
\textit{Subtotal:} \textbf{8 credits}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Second \texttt{Extract-Max} Operation:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Find max root:} We cached it in the last step (node $21$). $\rightarrow$ \textbf{0 credits}
    \item \textbf{Remove \& Unlink:} Remove $21$ and unlink it from its children (it has $0$ children, but the operation triggers). $\rightarrow$ \textbf{1 credit}
    \item \textbf{Add Children:} Add $0$ children to the root list. $\rightarrow$ \textbf{0 credits}
    \item \textbf{Consolidate:} Left with roots of degree $1(13)$ and $4(19)$. No matching degrees. $\rightarrow$ \textbf{0 credits}
    \item \textbf{Update Max Pointer:} Scan the remaining $2$ roots ($13, 19$). Requires $1$ comparison. $\rightarrow$ \textbf{1 credit}
\end{enumerate}
\textit{Subtotal:} \textbf{2 credits}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Total Cost:} Calling \texttt{Extract-Max} twice sequentially will incur exactly \textbf{10 credits} of actual cost.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item Prove that in a binomial heap, the amortized cost of INSERT is $O(1)$ and the worst-case cost of EXTRACT-MIN and DECREASE-KEY is $O(\log n)$.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsubsection*{1. Prove that the amortized cost of INSERT is $O(1)$}

To prove this, we will use the \textbf{Potential Method} for amortized analysis. The insertion of a node into a binomial heap behaves much like adding 1 to a binary counter.

\textbf{Proof:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Define the Potential Function:} Let $\Phi(H)$ be the number of trees in the root list of the binomial heap $H$. Initially, for an empty heap, $\Phi(H) = 0$.
    \item \textbf{Actual Cost ($c$):} Suppose the newly inserted node triggers $k$ linking operations (because there are $k$ consecutive trees in the root list of degree $0, 1, \dots, k-1$). The actual cost of the insertion is the cost of placing the new node plus the cost of the $k$ links. Thus, $c = k + 1$.
    \item \textbf{Change in Potential ($\Delta\Phi$):} Before the insertion, the heap had some number of roots, say $R$. After the operation, $k$ trees have been combined into 1 tree, and the new node initially added 1 to the root count before linking. The new number of roots is $R - k + 1$. Therefore, the change in potential is:
    $$\Delta\Phi = (R - k + 1) - R = 1 - k$$
    \item \textbf{Amortized Cost ($\hat{c}$):} The amortized cost is the actual cost plus the change in potential:
    $$\hat{c} = c + \Delta\Phi$$
    $$\hat{c} = (k + 1) + (1 - k)$$
    $$\hat{c} = 2$$
\end{itemize}

Because the amortized cost $\hat{c}$ evaluates to a constant $2$ regardless of the number of links $k$, the amortized cost of the \texttt{INSERT} operation is strictly $O(1)$.

\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Prove that the worst-case cost of EXTRACT-MIN is $O(\log n)$}

The worst-case time complexity of extracting the minimum element is bounded by the maximum number of trees in a binomial heap and the maximum degree of any binomial tree.

\textbf{Proof:} \\
By definition, a binomial heap with $n$ nodes contains at most $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$ binomial trees, and the maximum degree of any root is $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$. The \texttt{EXTRACT-MIN} operation consists of four distinct phases:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Find the minimum root:} We must scan the root list to find the minimum value. Since there are at most $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$ roots, this scan takes $O(\log n)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Remove the minimum root:} Removing the node from the linked list of roots takes $O(1)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Process the children:} The extracted root has at most $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ children. Reversing this list of children and preparing them to be merged into the heap takes time proportional to the number of children, which is $O(\log n)$.
    \item \textbf{Consolidate the heap:} We merge the children back into the main root list. The new root list has at most $(\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1) + \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ trees. We iterate through this combined list, linking trees of the same degree. Since there are $O(\log n)$ trees, the consolidation phase requires at most $O(\log n)$ linking operations.
\end{enumerate}

Summing the costs of these phases: 
$$O(\log n) + O(1) + O(\log n) + O(\log n) = O(\log n)$$
Thus, the worst-case time complexity for \texttt{EXTRACT-MIN} is $O(\log n)$.

\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Prove that the worst-case cost of DECREASE-KEY is $O(\log n)$}

The \texttt{DECREASE-KEY} operation behaves almost identically to the decrease-key (or ``sift-up'' / ``bubble-up'') operation in a standard binary min-heap. 

\textbf{Proof:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Operation Mechanics:} When a node's key is decreased, it may become smaller than its parent's key, violating the min-heap property. To fix this, we compare the node with its parent and swap them if necessary, continuing this process up the tree until the min-heap property is restored or the node becomes a root.
    \item \textbf{Maximum Path Length:} The number of comparisons and swaps is strictly limited by the depth of the node. 
    \item \textbf{Height Bound:} In a binomial heap of $n$ nodes, the largest binomial tree can have an order of at most $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$. By the structural properties of binomial trees, a tree of order $k$ has a maximum height (or maximum depth from leaf to root) of $k$.
    \item \textbf{Conclusion:} Therefore, the maximum depth of any node in the entire binomial heap is $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$. The bubble-up process will perform at most $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ swaps.
\end{enumerate}

Because the maximum number of operations scales directly with the height of the tree, the worst-case time complexity for \texttt{DECREASE-KEY} is $O(\log n)$.

\end{tcolorbox}

The internal heap representation of the priority queue pq2 is show in figure 1. Now answer the following questions:

\item A friend of yours claims that it is possible to implement Find-Min operation in a Binomial Min Heap in $O(1)$ time using the idea of Fibonacci Min Heap. Justify his claim with proper explanation.
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\item A binomial heap has 19 nodes. Find out the binomial trees this binomial heap has.

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsubsection*{1. Algorithm to Convert Min Priority Queue to Max Priority Queue}

To convert a min priority queue to a max priority queue in $O(n)$ amortized time, we can extract all elements from the existing heap and systematically insert them into a newly initialized max binomial heap. 

\textbf{Algorithm (\texttt{Convert-To-Max-Heap}):}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Initialize an empty list $L$.
    \item Perform a standard tree traversal (e.g., Breadth-First Search) across all trees in the min priority queue to extract all $n$ elements into $L$. 
    \item Initialize an empty Binomial Max Priority Queue \texttt{max\_pq}.
    \item For each element $x$ in $L$:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Call \texttt{max\_pq.Insert(x)}
    \end{itemize}
    \item Return \texttt{max\_pq}.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Amortized Running Time Analysis (Potential Method):}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Actual Cost:} The BFS extraction takes $O(n)$ actual time. Suppose an insertion triggers $k$ linking operations. The actual cost of one insertion is $c = k + 1$.
    \item \textbf{Potential Function ($\Phi$):} Let $\Phi(H)$ be the number of trees in the root list of the binomial heap. Initially, $\Phi = 0$.
    \item \textbf{Change in Potential ($\Delta\Phi$):} Adding a node initially creates a new $B_0$ tree (increasing potential by 1). Then $k$ links merge $k+1$ trees into $1$ tree (decreasing potential by $k$). Thus, $\Delta\Phi = 1 - k$.
    \item \textbf{Amortized Cost of Insert ($\hat{c}$):} 
    $$\hat{c} = c + \Delta\Phi = (k + 1) + (1 - k) = 2$$
\end{itemize}
Because the amortized cost of a single insert evaluates to a constant $O(1)$, executing $n$ inserts takes $n \times O(1) = O(n)$ time. Therefore, the total amortized running time of the algorithm is $O(n) + O(n) = O(n)$.

\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Application of Algorithm on \texttt{pq2}}

\texttt{pq2} contains $26$ elements. The binary representation of $26$ is $11010_2$, meaning the resulting max binomial heap will contain three trees: $B_1, B_3,$ and $B_4$.

By applying the insertion algorithm, we logically restructure the elements so that the max-heap property is strictly maintained (parents are strictly greater than or equal to their children). While the exact shape depends on the traversal extraction order, here is the guaranteed structural representation mapping the exact $26$ extracted elements into a valid Max Binomial Heap:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Tree $B_1$ (2 nodes):} Root \textbf{183} $\rightarrow$ Child: $7$
    \item \textbf{Tree $B_3$ (8 nodes):} Root \textbf{188}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Children: \textbf{176} ($B_2$), \textbf{118} ($B_1$), \textbf{21} ($B_0$)
        \item Children of 176: \textbf{128} ($B_1$), \textbf{14} ($B_0$)
        \item Children of 128: \textbf{11} ($B_0$)
        \item Children of 118: \textbf{17} ($B_0$)
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Tree $B_4$ (16 nodes):} Root \textbf{189}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Children: \textbf{115} ($B_3$), \textbf{80} ($B_2$), \textbf{72} ($B_1$), \textbf{55} ($B_0$)
        \item Children of 115: \textbf{108} ($B_2$), \textbf{82} ($B_1$), \textbf{35} ($B_0$)
        \item Children of 108: \textbf{82} ($B_1$), \textbf{24} ($B_0$) $\rightarrow$ Child of 82: \textbf{22} ($B_0$)
        \item Children of 82 (from 115): \textbf{25} ($B_0$)
        \item Children of 80: \textbf{77} ($B_1$), \textbf{36} ($B_0$) $\rightarrow$ Child of 77: \textbf{35} ($B_0$)
        \item Children of 72: \textbf{38} ($B_0$)
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\textit{(Note: Every parent node here is explicitly larger than its attached children, using exactly the 26 integers provided in the Figure 1 source).}

\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Melding \texttt{pq1} and \texttt{pq2} into \texttt{pq3}}

First, let's establish the structure of \texttt{pq1} after its $7$ inserts ($210, 201, 220, 202, 221, 205, 209$):
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{$B_0$:} Root \textbf{209}
    \item \textbf{$B_1$:} Root \textbf{221} $\rightarrow$ Child: $205$
    \item \textbf{$B_2$:} Root \textbf{220} $\rightarrow$ Children: \textbf{210} ($B_1$), \textbf{202} ($B_0$) $\rightarrow$ Child of 210: $201$
\end{itemize}

Now, we meld \texttt{pq1} ($7$ nodes) and the updated max-heap \texttt{pq2} ($26$ nodes). Total nodes = $33$ ($100001_2$). \texttt{pq3} will have trees $B_0$ and $B_5$.

\textbf{Meld Steps (Binary Addition):}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{$B_0$:} \texttt{pq1} has $209$. \texttt{pq2} has none. Result keeps \textbf{$B_0$(209)}.
    \item \textbf{$B_1$:} Merge $221$ (\texttt{pq1}) and $183$ (\texttt{pq2}). $221 > 183$, so $183$ becomes a child. Carry $B_2(221)$.
    \item \textbf{$B_2$:} Merge carry $221$ with $220$ (\texttt{pq1}). $221 > 220$, so $220$ becomes a child. Carry $B_3(221)$.
    \item \textbf{$B_3$:} Merge carry $221$ with $188$ (\texttt{pq2}). $221 > 188$, so $188$ becomes a child. Carry $B_4(221)$.
    \item \textbf{$B_4$:} Merge carry $221$ with $189$ (\texttt{pq2}). $221 > 189$, so $189$ becomes a child. Results in $B_5(221)$.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Internal Heap Representation of \texttt{pq3}:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{$B_0$:} Root \textbf{209}
    \item \textbf{$B_5$:} Root \textbf{221}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Children (in decreasing degree order): \textbf{$B_4$(189), $B_3$(188), $B_2$(220), $B_1$(183), $B_0$(205)}.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{4. \texttt{pq3} After Three Extract-Max Operations}

\textbf{1st Extract-Max (Removes 221):}
\begin{itemize}
    \item The $5$ children of $221$ become roots.
    \item We consolidate these with the existing $B_0(209)$.
    \item Merge sequence: $209$ merges with $205 \rightarrow B_1(209)$. Then merges with $183 \rightarrow B_2(209)$. Merges with $220 \rightarrow B_3(220)$. Merges with $188 \rightarrow B_4(220)$. Merges with $189 \rightarrow B_5(220)$.
    \item \textbf{Result:} A single $B_5$ tree with root \textbf{220}.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{2nd Extract-Max (Removes 220):}
\begin{itemize}
    \item The $5$ children of $220$ become roots: $B_4(189), B_3(188), B_2(209), B_1(210), B_0(202)$.
    \item No matching degrees exist to consolidate. 
    \item \textbf{Result:} Five separate trees with roots \textbf{189, 188, 209, 210, 202}.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{3rd Extract-Max (Removes 210):}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $210$ is the max. Removing it exposes its child $B_0(201)$.
    \item Consolidate $B_0(201)$ with $B_0(202) \rightarrow B_1(202)$.
    \item \textbf{Final Internal Representation:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{$B_1$:} Root \textbf{202} $\rightarrow$ Child: $201$
        \item \textbf{$B_2$:} Root \textbf{209} $\rightarrow$ Children: $183, 205$
        \item \textbf{$B_3$:} Root \textbf{188} (with its 7 nested children from step 2)
        \item \textbf{$B_4$:} Root \textbf{189} (with its 15 nested children from step 2)
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{5. Credit Cost Analysis of Extract-Max Operations}

\textit{Assumptions based on provided rules:} 1 Unlink = 1 credit; 1 Node Addition (to root list or as a child) = 1 credit; 1 Comparison = 1 credit.

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{tabular}{|p{2.5cm}|p{9cm}|p{2cm}|}
\hline
\textbf{Operation} & \textbf{Steps} & \textbf{Cost (Credits)} \\
\hline
\textbf{1st Extract} & \textbf{Find max:} Compare roots $209$ \& $221$. & $1$ \\
\cline{2-3}
& \textbf{Unlink \& Add:} Unlink $221$ ($1$ cr) + add $5$ children to roots ($5$ cr). & $6$ \\
\cline{2-3}
& \textbf{Consolidate:} $5$ consecutive merges. Each merge requires $1$ comparison and $1$ addition (linking as child). ($5 \times 2$) & $10$ \\
\cline{2-3}
& \textbf{Update max:} $1$ root left, so $0$ comparisons. & $0$ \\
\cline{2-3}
& \textit{Subtotal 1st Extract} & \textbf{17 credits} \\
\hline
\textbf{2nd Extract} & \textbf{Find max:} Cached from previous. & $0$ \\
\cline{2-3}
& \textbf{Unlink \& Add:} Unlink $220$ ($1$ cr) + add $5$ children to roots ($5$ cr). & $6$ \\
\cline{2-3}
& \textbf{Consolidate:} No merges needed. & $0$ \\
\cline{2-3}
& \textbf{Update max:} Scan $5$ new roots ($4$ comparisons). & $4$ \\
\cline{2-3}
& \textit{Subtotal 2nd Extract} & \textbf{10 credits} \\
\hline
\textbf{3rd Extract} & \textbf{Find max:} Cached from previous. & $0$ \\
\cline{2-3}
& \textbf{Unlink \& Add:} Unlink $210$ ($1$ cr) + add $1$ child to roots ($1$ cr). & $2$ \\
\cline{2-3}
& \textbf{Consolidate:} $1$ merge ($201$ and $202$). ($1$ comp + $1$ add) & $2$ \\
\cline{2-3}
& \textbf{Update max:} Scan $4$ roots ($3$ comparisons). & $3$ \\
\cline{2-3}
& \textit{Subtotal 3rd Extract} & \textbf{7 credits} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Total Actual Cost:} $17 + 10 + 7 =$ \textbf{34 credits}.

\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{6. Justification of $O(1)$ Find-Min Claim}

Your friend's claim is completely accurate. 

In a standard Binomial Heap, finding the minimum requires scanning the root list, which takes $O(\log n)$ time because there are up to $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$ trees. However, borrowing the concept from Fibonacci Heaps, we can easily optimize this to $O(1)$ by adding a single global pointer, \texttt{min\_ptr}, to the data structure that always points to the tree root containing the minimum value.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{During \texttt{Insert} / \texttt{Meld}:} Whenever a new element is added or heaps are merged, we compare the newly added root(s) against the current \texttt{min\_ptr}. This requires exactly one comparison ($O(1)$) to update the pointer.
    \item \textbf{During \texttt{Extract-Min}:} After the minimum node is extracted and the trees are consolidated (which inherently takes $O(\log n)$ time), we scan the new root list to find the new minimum and update \texttt{min\_ptr}. Because the structural consolidation already limits the roots to $O(\log n)$, this adds no extra asymptotic complexity.
\end{itemize}

Because \texttt{min\_ptr} is aggressively maintained during mutating operations, \texttt{Find-Min} simply returns the value at \texttt{min\_ptr} in strict \textbf{$O(1)$ time}.

\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{7. Binomial Trees in a 19-Node Heap}

A binomial heap containing $n$ nodes is determined entirely by the binary representation of $n$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item $19$ in binary is \textbf{$10011_2$} (since $19 = 16 + 2 + 1$).
    \item The set bits determine the degrees (and therefore the sizes) of the binomial trees present in the heap.
    \item Bit 0 is $1$ $\rightarrow$ \textbf{$B_0$} (1 node)
    \item Bit 1 is $1$ $\rightarrow$ \textbf{$B_1$} (2 nodes)
    \item Bit 2 is $0$
    \item Bit 3 is $0$
    \item Bit 4 is $1$ $\rightarrow$ \textbf{$B_4$} (16 nodes)
\end{itemize}

A 19-node binomial heap explicitly contains exactly three binomial trees: \textbf{$B_0, B_1,$ and $B_4$}.

\end{tcolorbox}
%% Q — 2022-23

%% Q — 2019-20
