        % ==========================================
        % SUBTREE UNDER NODE '17'
        % ==========================================
        \node[below left=1.0cm and 0.6cm of n17] (n21) {21};
        \node[below=1.1cm of n17] (n18) {18};
        \draw[edge] (n17) -- (n21);
        \draw[edge] (n17) -- (n18);

        % Bottom-most left node
        \node[below=0.9cm of n21] (n25) {25};
        \draw[edge] (n21) -- (n25);

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\mk{17} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}


\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

The deletion of a node $X$ in a Binomial Heap is standardly performed in two distinct phases: 
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \texttt{Decrease-Key($X$, $-\infty$)}: Decrease the target node's value to $-\infty$ and bubble it up to the root to maintain the min-heap property.
    \item \texttt{Extract-Min()}: Remove the root ($-\infty$) and meld its children back into the remaining heap.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\textbf{Step 1: After \texttt{Decrease-Key($X$, $-\infty$)}} \\
The node $X$ (17) is decreased to $-\infty$. It swaps keys with its parent (15), and then swaps keys with its new parent (3). The node containing $-\infty$ is now the root of the $B_4$ tree.

\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=0.8, transform shape,
        >=stealth,
        vertex/.style={
            circle, draw=black, ultra thick, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\small
        },
        edge/.style={ thick, black }
    ]
        % Root
        \node[vertex] (n3) at (5,2) {$-\infty$};
        \node[vertex, right=3.5cm of n3] (n23_r) {23};
        \draw[edge] (n3) -- (n23_r);
        
        \node[vertex, below=1.0cm of n23_r] (n89) {89};
        \draw[edge] (n23_r) -- (n89);

        % Children of -\infty
        \node[vertex, below left=1.0cm and 3.5cm of n3] (n15) {3};
        \node[vertex, below left=1.2cm and 0.8cm of n3] (n11) {11};
        \node[vertex, below=1.2cm of n3] (n28) {28};
        \node[vertex, below right=1.2cm and 0.4cm of n3] (n33) {33};
        \draw[edge] (n3) -- (n15); \draw[edge] (n3) -- (n11);
        \draw[edge] (n3) -- (n28); \draw[edge] (n3) -- (n33);

        % Subtrees
        \node[vertex, below left=0.9cm and 0.5cm of n11] (n19) {19};
        \node[vertex, below right=0.9cm and 0.2cm of n11] (n16) {16};
        \draw[edge] (n11) -- (n19); \draw[edge] (n11) -- (n16);
        \node[vertex, below=0.9cm of n19] (n20) {20};
        \draw[edge] (n19) -- (n20);

        \node[vertex, below=0.9cm of n28] (n30) {30};
        \draw[edge] (n28) -- (n30);

        % Swapped Keys path
        \node[vertex, below left=1.0cm and 1.2cm of n15] (n17) {15};
        \node[vertex, below=1.2cm of n15] (n42) {42};
        \node[vertex, below right=1.0cm and 0.4cm of n15] (n23_l) {23};
        \draw[edge] (n15) -- (n17); \draw[edge] (n15) -- (n42); \draw[edge] (n15) -- (n23_l);

        \node[vertex, below=0.9cm of n42] (n45) {45};
        \draw[edge] (n42) -- (n45);

        \node[vertex, below left=1.0cm and 0.6cm of n17] (n21) {21};
        \node[vertex, below=1.1cm of n17] (n18) {18};
        \draw[edge] (n17) -- (n21); \draw[edge] (n17) -- (n18);

        \node[vertex, below=0.9cm of n21] (n25) {25};
        \draw[edge] (n21) -- (n25);
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\textbf{Step 2: \texttt{Extract-Min} and Consolidation (Meld Phase)} \\
Removing the $-\infty$ root exposes its children as independent binomial trees: a $B_3$ (root 3), a $B_2$ (root 11), a $B_1$ (root 28), and a $B_0$ (root 33). We meld these with the existing tree $B_1$ (root 23):
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Merge Degree 1:} Merge $B_1(28)$ and $B_1(23)$. Since $23 < 28$, 28 becomes a child of 23. This creates a new $B_2$ rooted at 23.
    \item \textbf{Merge Degree 2:} Merge $B_2(11)$ and the new $B_2(23)$. Since $11 < 23$, 23 becomes a child of 11. This creates a new $B_3$ rooted at 11.
    \item \textbf{Merge Degree 3:} Merge $B_3(3)$ and the new $B_3(11)$. Since $3 < 11$, 11 becomes a child of 3. This creates a new $B_4$ rooted at 3.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Final Binomial Heap:} \\
The only remaining roots in the heap are 33 ($B_0$) and 3 ($B_4$).
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    scale=0.85, transform shape,
    >=stealth,
    vertex/.style={
        circle, draw=black, ultra thick, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\small
    },
    edge/.style={ thick, black }
]
    % Roots
    \node[vertex] (n33) at (1, 0) {33};
    \node[vertex] (n3) at (4, 0) {3};
    \draw[edge] (n33) -- (n3);
    
    % Children of 3
    \node[vertex] (n11) at (0, -1.5) {11};
    \node[vertex] (n15) at (3.5, -1.5) {15};
    \node[vertex] (n42) at (6, -1.5) {42};
    \node[vertex] (n23a) at (7.5, -1.5) {23};
    
    \draw[edge] (n3) -- (n11);
    \draw[edge] (n3) -- (n15);
    \draw[edge] (n3) -- (n42);
    \draw[edge] (n3) -- (n23a);
    
    % Children of 11
    \node[vertex] (n23b) at (-2, -3) {23};
    \node[vertex] (n19) at (0.5, -3) {19};
    \node[vertex] (n16) at (2, -3) {16};
    
    \draw[edge] (n11) -- (n23b);
    \draw[edge] (n11) -- (n19);
    \draw[edge] (n11) -- (n16);
    
    % Children of 15
    \node[vertex] (n21) at (3.5, -3) {21};
    \node[vertex] (n18) at (5, -3) {18};
    
    \draw[edge] (n15) -- (n21);
    \draw[edge] (n15) -- (n18);
    
    % Child of 42
    \node[vertex] (n45) at (6, -3) {45};
    \draw[edge] (n42) -- (n45);
    
    % Children of 23b
    \node[vertex] (n28) at (-3, -4.5) {28};
    \node[vertex] (n89) at (-1, -4.5) {89};
    
    \draw[edge] (n23b) -- (n28);
    \draw[edge] (n23b) -- (n89);
    
    % Child of 19
    \node[vertex] (n20) at (0.5, -4.5) {20};
    \draw[edge] (n19) -- (n20);
    
    % Child of 21
    \node[vertex] (n25) at (3.5, -4.5) {25};
    \draw[edge] (n21) -- (n25);
    
    % Child of 28
    \node[vertex] (n30) at (-3, -6) {30};
    \draw[edge] (n28) -- (n30);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2022-23
\item Mention the amortized running times of the following operations for binomial and Fibonacci heaps:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Union
  \item Find min
  \item Extract min
  \item Decrease key
\end{enumerate}
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20, 2018--19}


\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

Below is the comparison of the amortized running times for the specified operations in standard Binomial Heaps and Fibonacci Heaps. 

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5}
\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Operation} & \textbf{Binomial Heap} & \textbf{Fibonacci Heap} \\
\hline
\textbf{1. Union (Meld)} & $O(\log n)$ & $O(1)$ \\
\hline
\textbf{2. Find Min} & $O(\log n)$\textsuperscript{*} & $O(1)$ \\
\hline
\textbf{3. Extract Min} & $O(\log n)$ & $O(\log n)$ \\
\hline
\textbf{4. Decrease Key} & $O(\log n)$ & $O(1)$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textit{\textsuperscript{*}Note: \textbf{Find Min} in a standard Binomial Heap is $O(\log n)$ because it requires scanning the root list. However, it can be optimized to $O(1)$ if an explicit pointer to the minimum root is maintained (similar to how a Fibonacci heap operates).}

\end{tcolorbox}




\item Write down the running times of the following operations for both Binomial heap and Fibonacci heap: (i) Insert key, (ii) Find min, (iii) Extract min, (iv) Union of two heaps, (v) Decrease key. A friend claims to have designed a mergeable heap with $O(1)$ time for all operations above. Would you believe it? Justify. \mk{6+6}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsubsection*{1. Running Times of Heap Operations}

Below are the standard running times for the requested operations. Note that standard Binomial Heap times are typically evaluated in the worst-case, while Fibonacci Heap operations are evaluated using amortized analysis.

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5}
\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Operation} & \textbf{Binomial Heap (Worst-Case)} & \textbf{Fibonacci Heap (Amortized)} \\
\hline
\textbf{(i) Insert Key} & $O(\log n)$ & $O(1)$ \\
\hline
\textbf{(ii) Find Min} & $O(\log n)$\textsuperscript{*} & $O(1)$ \\
\hline
\textbf{(iii) Extract Min} & $O(\log n)$ & $O(\log n)$ \\
\hline
\textbf{(iv) Union (Meld)} & $O(\log n)$ & $O(1)$ \\
\hline
\textbf{(v) Decrease Key} & $O(\log n)$ & $O(1)$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textit{\textsuperscript{*}Note: As previously mentioned, Find-Min in a standard Binomial Heap is $O(\log n)$, but it can be optimized to $O(1)$ worst-case if an explicit pointer to the minimum root is maintained.}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Evaluation of the Friend's $O(1)$ Claim}

\textbf{Conclusion:} No, I would \textbf{not} believe this claim.

\textbf{Justification:} 
If a comparison-based mergeable heap supported both \texttt{Insert} and \texttt{Extract-Min} in $O(1)$ worst-case (or even amortized) time, it would violate the fundamental lower bound of comparison-based sorting. 

Consider how we could use this hypothetical data structure to sort an array of $n$ elements:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Build the Heap:} We could iterate through the array and \texttt{Insert} each of the $n$ elements into the heap. If each insertion takes $O(1)$ time, building the heap takes $n \times O(1) = O(n)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Extract Elements:} We could then call \texttt{Extract-Min} exactly $n$ times to retrieve the elements in ascending order. If each extraction takes $O(1)$ time, this phase also takes $n \times O(1) = O(n)$ time.
\end{enumerate}

The total time to sort the array would be exactly $O(n) + O(n) = O(n)$. However, information theory dictates that any comparison-based sorting algorithm must perform at least $\Omega(n \log n)$ comparisons in the worst case. Because it is impossible to sort a general array in $O(n)$ time using comparisons, it is mathematically impossible for a comparison-based heap to achieve $O(1)$ time for \texttt{Extract-Min}. At least one operation (typically \texttt{Extract-Min}) must take $\Omega(\log n)$ time.

\end{tcolorbox}


\item Prove that there are $\binom{k}{i}$ nodes at depth $i$ in a binomial tree $B_k$. \mk{5}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

%% Q — 2019-20
\end{enumerate}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cB}{Advanced Heaps (Fibonacci Heaps)}
\label{sub:s2-fibheap}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item Show the Fibonacci heap that results when the minimum node (5) is deleted from the given Fibonacci heap. Show the intermediate steps.
\begin{center}
      \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.1,
        every node/.style={circle, draw=black, ultra thick, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries},
        thick
    ]

        % --- Nodes Positioning ---
        % Root list (Horizontal line elements)
        \node (7)  at (0, 3) {7};
        \node (24) at (2, 3) {24};
        \node (23) at (4, 3) {23};
        \node (17) at (5.8, 3) {17};
        \node (5)  at (7.6, 3) {5};

        % Tree under node 7
        \node (30) at (0, 1.5) {30};

        % Tree under node 24
        \node (26) at (1.3, 1.5) {26};
        \node (46) at (2.7, 1.5) {46};
        \node (35) at (1.3, 0) {35};

        % Tree under node 23
        \node (51) at (4, 1.5) {51};

        % Tree under node 5
        \node (18) at (6.6, 1.5) {18};
        \node (52) at (7.6, 1.5) {52};
        \node (41) at (8.6, 1.5) {41};
        \node (44) at (8.6, 0) {44};


        % --- Edges (Connections) ---
        % Horizontal dashed lines between root nodes
        \draw[dashed, thick] (7)  -- (24);
        \draw[dashed, thick] (24) -- (23);
        \draw[dashed, thick] (23) -- (17);
        \draw[dashed, thick] (17) -- (5);

        % Vertical and angled child connections
        \draw (7)  -- (30);
        
        \draw (24) -- (26);
        \draw (24) -- (46);
        \draw (26) -- (35);
        
        \draw (23) -- (51);
        
        \draw (5)  -- (18);
        \draw (5)  -- (52);
        \draw (5)  -- (41);
        \draw (41) -- (44);


        % --- "min" Pointer Annotation ---
        % Text label for min
        \node[draw=none, font=\bfseries\large] (min_text) at (7.6, 4.2) {min};
        % Arrow pointing down to node 5
        \draw[-{Stealth[scale=1.2]}, ultra thick] (min_text) -- (5.north);

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

The deletion of the minimum node from a Fibonacci heap occurs in three main phases: extracting the minimum node, appending its children to the root list, and consolidating the root list to ensure no two roots have the same degree.

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 1: Extracting the Minimum Node} \\
The minimum node \textbf{5} is removed from the heap. Its children, nodes \textbf{18}, \textbf{52}, and \textbf{41}, are elevated and added to the root list. The \texttt{min} pointer is temporarily ignored until consolidation is complete.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=0.95,
        every node/.style={circle, draw=black, ultra thick, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries},
        thick
    ]
        % Root list
        \node (7)  at (0, 3) {7};
        \node (24) at (2.2, 3) {24};
        \node (23) at (4.2, 3) {23};
        \node (17) at (5.7, 3) {17};
        \node (18) at (7.2, 3) {18};
        \node (52) at (8.7, 3) {52};
        \node (41) at (10.2, 3) {41};

        % Children
        \node (30) at (0, 1.5) {30};
        \node (26) at (1.5, 1.5) {26};
        \node (46) at (2.9, 1.5) {46};
        \node (35) at (1.5, 0) {35};
        \node (51) at (4.2, 1.5) {51};
        \node (44) at (10.2, 1.5) {44};

        % Edges
        \draw[dashed, thick] (7)  -- (24);
        \draw[dashed, thick] (24) -- (23);
        \draw[dashed, thick] (23) -- (17);
        \draw[dashed, thick] (17) -- (18);
        \draw[dashed, thick] (18) -- (52);
        \draw[dashed, thick] (52) -- (41);

        \draw (7)  -- (30);
        \draw (24) -- (26);
        \draw (24) -- (46);
        \draw (26) -- (35);
        \draw (23) -- (51);
        \draw (41) -- (44);
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 2: Consolidation} \\
We iterate through the root list and consolidate trees of the same degree using an auxiliary array $A[0..D]$. When two roots have the same degree, the root with the larger key becomes a child of the root with the smaller key, and the degree of the smaller root increases by 1.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Process 7} (degree 1): Insert into $A[1]$.
    \item \textbf{Process 24} (degree 2): Insert into $A[2]$.
    \item \textbf{Process 23} (degree 1): Conflicts with 7 in $A[1]$. Since $7 < 23$, node 23 becomes a child of 7. Node 7's degree becomes 2. Array slot $A[1]$ is cleared.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Node 7 now conflicts with 24 in $A[2]$. Since $7 < 24$, node 24 becomes a child of 7. Node 7's degree becomes 3. Array slot $A[2]$ is cleared. We set $A[3] = 7$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Process 17} (degree 0): Insert into $A[0]$.
    \item \textbf{Process 18} (degree 0): Conflicts with 17 in $A[0]$. Since $17 < 18$, node 18 becomes a child of 17. Node 17's degree becomes 1. Array slot $A[0]$ is cleared. We set $A[1] = 17$.
    \item \textbf{Process 52} (degree 0): Insert into $A[0]$.
    \item \textbf{Process 41} (degree 1): Conflicts with 17 in $A[1]$. Since $17 < 41$, node 41 becomes a child of 17. Node 17's degree becomes 2. Array slot $A[1]$ is cleared. We set $A[2] = 17$.
\end{itemize}
After consolidation, the non-empty entries in our array are $A[0] = 52$, $A[2] = 17$, and $A[3] = 7$. These become the roots of our new Fibonacci heap.

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 3: Final Fibonacci Heap} \\
We rebuild the root list with nodes 7, 17, and 52. We find the minimum root among them, which is 7, and update the \texttt{min} pointer accordingly.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.0,
        every node/.style={circle, draw=black, ultra thick, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries},
        thick
    ]
        % Roots
        \node (7)  at (0, 4) {7};
        \node (17) at (6.5, 4) {17};
        \node (52) at (9.5, 4) {52};

        % Tree 7
        \node (30) at (-1.5, 2.5) {30};
        \node (23) at (0, 2.5) {23};
        \node (24) at (2.5, 2.5) {24};
        
        \node (51) at (0, 1) {51};
        
        \node (26) at (1.5, 1) {26};
        \node (46) at (3.5, 1) {46};
        
        \node (35) at (1.5, -0.5) {35};

        % Tree 17
        \node (18) at (5.5, 2.5) {18};
        \node (41) at (7.5, 2.5) {41};
        \node (44) at (7.5, 1) {44};

        % Edges
        \draw[dashed, thick] (7)  -- (17);
        \draw[dashed, thick] (17) -- (52);

        \draw (7) -- (30);
        \draw (7) -- (23);
        \draw (7) -- (24);
        
        \draw (23) -- (51);
        
        \draw (24) -- (26);
        \draw (24) -- (46);
        
        \draw (26) -- (35);

        \draw (17) -- (18);
        \draw (17) -- (41);
        \draw (41) -- (44);

        % Min pointer
        \node[draw=none, font=\bfseries\large] (min_text) at (0, 5.2) {min};
        \draw[-{Stealth[scale=1.2]}, ultra thick] (min_text) -- (7.north);
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ amortized time. Removing the minimum node takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time. Consolidation merges trees and significantly reduces the size of the root list. The maximum degree $D(n)$ is bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$, yielding an amortized operation cost that is logarithmic.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ auxiliary space is required for the degree tracking array $A[0..D]$ during the consolidation process.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Illustrate step by step what happens if the Extract-Min operation is executed on the given Fibonacci heap (nodes with keys 6, 2, 1, 5, 3, 4, 7, 8, 9 where 1 is the minimum).

\begin{center}
     \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.4,
        normalnode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.6cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries},
        shadednode/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=gray!50, thick, minimum size=0.6cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries},
        thick
    ]
        \node[normalnode] (6) at (0,2) {6};
        \node[normalnode] (2) at (1.5,2) {2};
        \node[normalnode] (5) at (1.5,1) {5};
        
        \node[normalnode] (1) at (4,2) {1};
        \node[normalnode] (3) at (2.5,1) {3};
        \node[shadednode] (4) at (3.2,1) {4};
        \node[shadednode] (7) at (4,1) {7};
        \node[normalnode] (8) at (4,0) {8};
        \node[normalnode] (9) at (4,-1) {9};

 
        \draw[dashed] (6) -- (2);
        \draw[dashed] (2) -- (1);
        
    
        \draw (2) -- (5);
        \draw (1) -- (3);
        \draw (1) -- (4);
        \draw (1) -- (7);
        \draw (7) -- (8);
        \draw (8) -- (9);

        \node[anchor=south] (min_text) at (4.5,2.4) {minimum};

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

The \texttt{Extract-Min} operation in a Fibonacci Heap consists of three main phases: extracting the minimum node, elevating its children to the root list, and consolidating the root list to ensure that no two roots have the same degree.

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 1: Extract the Minimum Node} \\
We remove the minimum node \textbf{1}. Its children, nodes \textbf{3}, \textbf{4}, and \textbf{7}, are immediately added to the root list. 
\newline
\textit{Note:} In a Fibonacci heap, a marked node signifies that it has lost a child since it was last made a child of another node. Whenever a node becomes a root, its mark is cleared. Therefore, the previously marked (shaded) nodes \textbf{4} and \textbf{7} become unmarked.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.0,
        every node/.style={circle, draw=black, ultra thick, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries},
        thick
    ]
        % Root list
        \node (6) at (0, 3) {6};
        \node (2) at (1.5, 3) {2};
        \node (3) at (3, 3) {3};
        \node (4) at (4.5, 3) {4};
        \node (7) at (6, 3) {7};

        % Children
        \node (5) at (1.5, 1.5) {5};
        \node (8) at (6, 1.5) {8};
        \node (9) at (6, 0) {9};

        % Edges
        \draw[dashed, thick] (6) -- (2);
        \draw[dashed, thick] (2) -- (3);
        \draw[dashed, thick] (3) -- (4);
        \draw[dashed, thick] (4) -- (7);

        \draw (2) -- (5);
        \draw (7) -- (8);
        \draw (8) -- (9);
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 2: Root List Consolidation} \\
We iterate through the current root list (\textbf{6, 2, 3, 4, 7}) and merge trees of the same degree using an auxiliary array $A[0..D]$. Initially, $A$ is empty.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Process 6} (degree 0): Insert into $A[0]$. \\
    Array state: $A = [6, \text{NIL}, \text{NIL}]$
    
    \item \textbf{Process 2} (degree 1): Insert into $A[1]$. \\
    Array state: $A = [6, 2, \text{NIL}]$
    
    \item \textbf{Process 3} (degree 0): Conflicts with 6 in $A[0]$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Since $3 < 6$, node \textbf{6} becomes a child of node \textbf{3}. The degree of 3 increases to 1. $A[0]$ is cleared.
        \item Now, node \textbf{3} (degree 1) conflicts with 2 in $A[1]$.
        \item Since $2 < 3$, node \textbf{3} becomes a child of node \textbf{2}. The degree of 2 increases to 2. $A[1]$ is cleared.
        \item Insert node \textbf{2} into $A[2]$. \\
        Array state: $A = [\text{NIL}, \text{NIL}, 2]$
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Process 4} (degree 0): Insert into $A[0]$. \\
    Array state: $A = [4, \text{NIL}, 2]$
    
    \item \textbf{Process 7} (degree 1): Insert into $A[1]$. \\
    Array state: $A = [4, 7, 2]$
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Step 3: Final Fibonacci Heap} \\
We reconstruct the root list from the non-empty entries of array $A$: nodes \textbf{4}, \textbf{7}, and \textbf{2}. Finally, we scan this new root list to find the minimum root, which is \textbf{2}, and update the \texttt{min} pointer.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.1,
        every node/.style={circle, draw=black, ultra thick, minimum size=0.7cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\bfseries},
        thick
    ]
        % Roots
        \node (4) at (0, 3) {4};
        \node (7) at (2.5, 3) {7};
        \node (2) at (6, 3) {2};

        % Tree 7
        \node (8) at (2.5, 1.5) {8};
        \node (9) at (2.5, 0) {9};

        % Tree 2
        \node (5) at (5, 1.5) {5};
        \node (3) at (7, 1.5) {3};
        \node (6) at (7, 0) {6};

        % Edges
        \draw[dashed, thick] (4) -- (7);
        \draw[dashed, thick] (7) -- (2);

        \draw (7) -- (8);
        \draw (8) -- (9);

        \draw (2) -- (5);
        \draw (2) -- (3);
        \draw (3) -- (6);

        % Min pointer
        \node[draw=none, font=\bfseries\large] (min_text) at (6, 4.4) {min};
        \draw[-{Stealth[scale=1.2]}, ultra thick] (min_text) -- (2.north);
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ amortized time. Although consolidation may take up to $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time in the worst case (if the root list is very long), the amortized cost evaluates to $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ because merging trees drastically reduces the potential (number of roots) of the heap.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ auxiliary space. The temporary array $A$ used during consolidation requires space proportional to the maximum degree of any node in the heap, which is bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item Recall the deletion operation of the Fibonacci Heap data structure. Professor Rahsut has proposed a variant of the deletion operation (RAHSUT-DELETE). For this variant:

\begin{lstlisting}
RAHSUT-DELETE(H, x)
    if x == H.min
        FIB-HEAP-EXTRACT-MIN(H)
    else
        y = x.p
        if y != NIL
            CUT(H, x, y) // cuts the link between x and its parent y, making x a root.
            CASCADING-CUT(H, y) // recursively marks and cuts the parents if has to
        add x's child to the root list of H
        remove x from the root list of H
\end{lstlisting}

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Construct a good upper bound on the actual time of RAHSUT-DELETE when $x$ is not \texttt{H.min}. Your bound should be in terms of \texttt{x.degree} and the number $c$ of calls to the CASCADING-CUT procedure.
  \item Suppose we call RAHSUT-DELETE($H$, $x$) and let $H'$ be the Fibonacci heap that results. Assuming that node $x$ is not a root, find the potential of $H'$ in terms of \texttt{x.degree}, the number $c$ of calls to the CASCADING-CUT procedure, $\Phi(H)$, and $m(H)$.
\end{enumerate}
 
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsection*{Part 1: Upper Bound on the Actual Time}

To determine the actual time complexity of \texttt{RAHSUT-DELETE(H, x)} when $x \neq \texttt{H.min}$, we must analyze the cost of each operation performed within the \texttt{else} branch of the procedure. We assume standard Fibonacci heap structures, where node children are stored in a circular doubly linked list.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{\texttt{CUT(H, x, y)}:} This operation removes $x$ from the child list of its parent $y$, decrements $y.degree$, and adds $x$ to the root list of $H$. Updating the doubly linked list pointers and adding to the root list takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ actual time.
    \item \textbf{\texttt{CASCADING-CUT(H, y)}:} We are given that this procedure makes exactly $c$ calls. Each individual call to \texttt{CASCADING-CUT} performs $\mathcal{O}(1)$ operations (checking marks, clearing marks, and executing a \texttt{CUT}). Thus, the total actual time for $c$ calls is $\mathcal{O}(c)$.
    \item \textbf{Adding $x$'s children to the root list:} Splicing the circular doubly linked list of $x$'s children into the root list of $H$ takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time. However, to maintain the correct structural properties of the heap, the parent pointer (\texttt{p}) of every child of $x$ must be updated to \texttt{NIL}. Since $x$ has exactly $x.degree$ children, iterating through them to update their pointers takes $\Theta(x.degree)$ actual time.
    \item \textbf{Removing $x$ from the root list:} Node $x$ is extracted from the circular doubly linked root list of $H$. This requires updating adjacent node pointers, which takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ actual time.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Total Actual Time Bound:} Summing the individual costs, the actual time is $\mathcal{O}(1) + \mathcal{O}(c) + \mathcal{O}(x.degree) + \mathcal{O}(1)$. Therefore, a tight upper bound on the actual time is:
\begin{equation}
    T_{\text{actual}} = \mathcal{O}(x.degree + c)
\end{equation}

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=30mm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=15mm},
    vertex/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=1pt, fill=cB!10, font=\small},
    rootvertex/.style={circle, draw, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=1pt, fill=white, font=\small},
    edge/.style={->, thick},
    cutedge/.style={->, thick, dashed, red}
]

% Before Deletion
\node[rootvertex] (r) at (0, 0) {$y$};
\node[vertex] (x) at (0, -1.5) {$x$};
\node[vertex] (c1) at (-1, -3) {$c_1$};
\node (c2) at (0, -3) {$\dots$};
\node[vertex] (ck) at (1, -3) {$c_k$};

\draw[edge] (r) -- (x) node[midway, right] {\small \texttt{CUT}};
\draw[edge] (x) -- (c1);
\draw[edge] (x) -- (ck);

\node[font=\bfseries] at (0, -4) {(a) Before \texttt{RAHSUT-DELETE}};

% After Deletion
\begin{scope}[xshift=7cm]
    \node[rootvertex] (r2) at (0, 0) {$y$};
    \node[rootvertex] (c12) at (-1.5, 0) {$c_1$};
    \node (c22) at (-0.5, 0) {$\dots$};
    \node[rootvertex] (ck2) at (0.5, 0) {$c_k$};
    \node[rootvertex, dashed, opacity=0.5] (x2) at (2, 0) {$x$ (deleted)};

    \draw[thick, dotted] (-2.5,0.5) -- (3,0.5) node[right, font=\small] {Root List};
    
    \node[font=\bfseries] at (0, -4) {(b) After \texttt{RAHSUT-DELETE}};
\end{scope}

\draw[->, ultra thick, cB] (2.5, -1.5) -- (4.5, -1.5) node[midway, above] {Transform};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{1em}

\rule{\textwidth}{0.4pt}

\subsection*{Part 2: Potential of the Resulting Heap $H'$}

The standard potential function for a Fibonacci heap $H$ is given by:
\begin{equation}
    \Phi(H) = t(H) + 2m(H)
\end{equation}
where $t(H)$ is the number of trees in the root list, and $m(H)$ is the number of marked nodes in the heap. Let $H'$ be the heap after executing \texttt{RAHSUT-DELETE(H, x)}. We evaluate the changes to $t(H)$ and $m(H)$.

\paragraph{Step 1: Change in Number of Trees ($t(H)$)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item The initial \texttt{CUT(H, x, y)} moves $x$ to the root list, creating $\mathbf{+1}$ new tree.
    \item The \texttt{CASCADING-CUT(H, y)} procedure makes $c$ calls. The first $c-1$ calls recursively cut marked ancestors and move them to the root list, creating $\mathbf{+(c-1)}$ new trees.
    \item Adding $x$'s children to the root list brings all $x.degree$ children to the root level, creating $\mathbf{+x.degree}$ new trees.
    \item Removing $x$ from the root list permanently removes $1$ tree, resulting in $\mathbf{-1}$ tree.
\end{itemize}
Summing these changes gives the total number of trees in $H'$:
\begin{align}
    t(H') &= t(H) + 1 + (c - 1) + x.degree - 1 \nonumber \\
    &= t(H) + c + x.degree - 1
\end{align}

\paragraph{Step 2: Change in Number of Marked Nodes ($m(H)$)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item The initial \texttt{CUT} clears the mark on $x$ (if it was marked). This removes at most $1$ marked node.
    \item The $c-1$ recursive cuts in \texttt{CASCADING-CUT} clear the marks of the nodes being cut. Because these nodes triggered the recursion, they were definitely marked. This reduces the number of marked nodes by exactly $\mathbf{-(c-1)}$.
    \item The final $c$-th call to \texttt{CASCADING-CUT} stops at a node that is unmarked. It then marks this node (unless it is a root). This adds at most $\mathbf{+1}$ marked node.
\end{itemize}
In the worst case (where $x$ was unmarked initially and the final cascading call marks a node), the upper bound for the new number of marked nodes is:
\begin{align}
    m(H') &\le m(H) - (c - 1) + 1 \nonumber \\
    &= m(H) - c + 2
\end{align}

\paragraph{Step 3: Calculating Final Potential $\Phi(H')$}
Substituting the expressions for $t(H')$ and $m(H')$ back into the potential function:
\begin{align}
    \Phi(H') &= t(H') + 2m(H') \nonumber \\
    &\le \left( t(H) + c + x.degree - 1 \right) + 2 \left( m(H) - c + 2 \right) \nonumber \\
    &= t(H) + 2m(H) + c + x.degree - 1 - 2c + 4 \nonumber \\
    &= \Phi(H) + x.degree - c + 3
\end{align}

\textbf{Final Result:} 
The potential of $H'$ in terms of the given variables is bounded by:
\begin{equation}
    \Phi(H') \le \Phi(H) + x.degree - c + 3
\end{equation}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2020-21
\item Describe how Fibonacci numbers are related to Fibonacci Heaps.
\mk{5} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

Fibonacci Heaps derive their name directly from the \textbf{Fibonacci numbers} because the mathematical properties of the sequence govern the structural bounds of the heap's internal trees. Specifically, the relation establishes that the size (number of nodes) of a tree in a Fibonacci Heap grows exponentially with respect to its root's degree, heavily restricting the maximum degree of any node.

\subsubsection*{1. The Core Structural Property}
In a Fibonacci Heap, trees are formed by linking two roots of the same degree during the \texttt{Consolidate} phase of an \texttt{Extract-Min} operation. Furthermore, to maintain a shallow structure, the \textit{cascading cut} rule dictates that a node is completely removed from its parent if it loses a second child. 

This leads to a guaranteed minimum density for any subtree:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Let $x$ be any node in a Fibonacci heap, and let $k = \text{degree}(x)$.
    \item Let $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_k$ be the children of $x$ in the order they were linked to $x$.
    \item When $y_i$ was linked to $x$, $x$ already had at least $i-1$ children (namely, $y_1, \dots, y_{i-1}$).
    \item Because nodes are only linked when their degrees are equal, $y_i$ must have had a degree of at least $i-1$ at the moment it was linked.
    \item Since a node can lose at most one child before being cut itself (cascading cut rule), the \textbf{current degree} of $y_i$ is at least $i-2$ (for $i \ge 2$). The degree of $y_1$ is trivially $\ge 0$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{2. Mathematical Connection to Fibonacci Numbers}
We want to find the minimum number of nodes $S_k$ in a tree whose root has degree $k$. Based on the child-degree bounds established above:
\begin{align*}
    S_0 &= 1 \quad \text{(a single node has degree 0)} \\
    S_1 &= 2 \quad \text{(a root with 1 child, both of degree 0)} \\
    S_k &\ge 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{size}(y_i) \\
    S_k &\ge 1 + S_0 + \sum_{i=2}^{k} S_{i-2} \quad \text{for } k \ge 2
\end{align*}

Let us define the Fibonacci sequence as $F_0 = 0, F_1 = 1, F_2 = 1, F_3 = 2, F_4 = 3, \dots$
We can prove by strong induction that $S_k \ge F_{k+2}$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Base Cases:} \\
    $S_0 = 1 = F_2$ \\
    $S_1 = 2 = F_3$
    \item \textbf{Inductive Step:} Assume $S_i \ge F_{i+2}$ holds for all $i < k$.
    \begin{align*}
        S_k &\ge 1 + S_0 + S_0 + S_1 + S_2 + \dots + S_{k-2} \\
            &\ge 1 + F_2 + F_2 + F_3 + F_4 + \dots + F_k
    \end{align*}
    Using the well-known Fibonacci identity $1 + \sum_{j=1}^{k} F_j = F_{k+2}$, and substituting $1 = F_1$:
    \begin{align*}
        S_k &\ge F_1 + F_2 + \sum_{j=2}^{k} F_j \\
        S_k &\ge \sum_{j=1}^{k} F_j + 1 = F_{k+2}
    \end{align*}
\end{itemize}
Thus, a tree of degree $k$ contains at least $F_{k+2}$ nodes. 

\subsubsection*{3. Diagram: Minimal Fibonacci Tree Structure}
Below is a visualization of the minimal possible tree structure for a node of degree 3 in a Fibonacci Heap. Notice how the number of nodes perfectly aligns with $F_{3+2} = F_5 = 5$.

\vspace{0.5cm}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=1.5cm},
    every node/.style={circle, draw=cB, very thick, fill=cB!10, minimum size=8mm, font=\bfseries},
    edge from parent/.style={draw=gray, thick}
]

\node[label=above:{\color{cB}$k=3$ (Root)}] (root) {$x$}
    child { node[label=below:{\color{gray}$d=0$}] {$y_1$} }
    child { node[label=below:{\color{gray}$d=0$}] {$y_2$} }
    child { node[label=right:{\color{gray}$d=1$}] {$y_3$} 
        child { node[label=below:{\color{gray}$d=0$}] {$z_1$} }
    };

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{center}
    \textit{Figure 1: A minimal tree of degree 3. Nodes = 5 = $F_5$. The children have degrees 0, 0, and 1 respectively.}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{4. Algorithmic Impact (Complexity Analysis)}
Why is this relationship vital? It bounds the maximum degree $D(n)$ of any node in a Fibonacci heap of $n$ elements. 
By Binet's Formula, Fibonacci numbers grow exponentially:
\[ F_{k+2} \ge \frac{\phi^{k+2}}{\sqrt{5}} \]
where $\phi = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ is the Golden Ratio.

Since the size of the tree $n \ge S_k \ge F_{k+2}$, we have:
\[ n \ge \frac{\phi^{k+2}}{\sqrt{5}} \implies \log_\phi(\sqrt{5}n) \ge k+2 \]
Solving for $k$, we get:
\[ k \le \log_\phi n + \frac{\log_\phi \sqrt{5}}{2} - 2 = O(\log n) \]

\textbf{Conclusion:} 
Because $k = O(\log n)$, the maximum number of children any node can have is logarithmic with respect to the total number of nodes. Consequently:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Consolidation Time:} During \texttt{Extract-Min}, we iterate through the roots and bucket them by degree. Because the maximum degree is $O(\log n)$, the array size needed for consolidation and the time spent restructuring the heap is strictly bounded, giving an amortized \textbf{Time Complexity of $O(\log n)$}.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} The auxiliary degree array required during \texttt{Extract-Min} uses \textbf{$O(\log n)$ Space}.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Prove or disprove the claim that ``We do not mark the root node of any tree in the decrease key operation performed on a Fibonacci Min Heap, even if the root node loses a child''. Perform an extract-min operation on the Fibonacci Min Heap shown below. Show all the steps and the resulting Fibonacci Min Heap.
\begin{center}
      \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.1, transform shape,
        % Base style for standard single-circled nodes

            inner sep=0pt
        },

            thick, 
            black!90
        }
    ]

        % ==========================================
        % ROOT LIST (Horizontal Row)
        % ==========================================
        \node[node style] (n17)  at (0, 0) {17};
        \node[node style] (n129) at (1.8, 0) {129};
        \node[node style] (n90)  at (3.6, 0) {90};
        \node[node style] (n110) at (5.1, 0) {110};

        % Root list horizontal connections
        \draw[edge style] (n17) -- (n129);
        \draw[edge style] (n129) -- (n90);
        \draw[edge style] (n90) -- (n110);

        % Minimal pointer marker above node 17
        \draw[thick, <-, >=Stealth] (n17.north) -- ++(0, 0.6) 
            node[above] {min};

        % ==========================================

        % ==========================================
        \node[marked style] (n163) at (-2.0, -1.5) {163};
        \node[node style]   (n350) at (-0.8, -1.5) {350};
        \node[node style]   (n479) at (0.4, -1.5) {479};

        \draw[edge style] (n17) -- (n163);
        \draw[edge style] (n17) -- (n350);
        \draw[edge style] (n17) -- (n479);

        % Deepest child under 163 (Marked)
        \node[marked style] (n382) at (-2.0, -3.0) {382};
        \draw[edge style] (n163) -- (n382);

        % Deepest child under 350
        \node[node style] (n575) at (-0.8, -3.0) {575};
        \draw[edge style] (n350) -- (n575);

        % ==========================================
        % SUBTREE UNDER NODE 129
        % ==========================================

Here double-circled nodes are marked and single-circled nodes are unmarked.

\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsection*{Part 1: Proof of Claim}

\textbf{Claim:} \textit{``We do not mark the root node of any tree in the decrease key operation performed on a Fibonacci Min Heap, even if the root node loses a child.''}

\textbf{Verdict: True.}

\textbf{Proof:} 
In a Fibonacci Heap, the boolean attribute \texttt{mark} associated with a node $x$ indicates whether node $x$ has lost a child \textit{since the last time $x$ was made the child of another node}. By definition, nodes in the root list have no parent ($x.p = \text{NIL}$). 

During a \texttt{Decrease-Key} operation, if a node's key is decreased such that it violates the min-heap property with its parent, it is cut and added to the root list. Whenever a node is moved to the root list, its \texttt{mark} is strictly cleared (set to \texttt{FALSE}).

If a root node loses a child (because its child undergoes a \texttt{Decrease-Key} and is cut), the \texttt{Cascading-Cut} procedure is invoked. The logic of \texttt{Cascading-Cut} is as follows:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Let $y$ be the parent of the node that was just cut.
    \item If $y.p \neq \text{NIL}$ (i.e., $y$ is \textbf{not} a root), we check its mark. If unmarked, we mark it. If marked, we cut it and recurse.
    \item If $y.p = \text{NIL}$ (i.e., $y$ \textbf{is} a root), the procedure terminates immediately doing absolutely nothing to the root's mark.
\end{itemize}
Therefore, a root node is never marked. This is structurally necessary to bound the potential function used in the amortized analysis:
\begin{equation}
    \Phi(H) = t(H) + 2m(H)
\end{equation}
where $t(H)$ is the number of trees in the root list and $m(H)$ is the number of marked nodes. Marks are used to "pay" for future cascading cuts. Since root nodes can be freely linked during \texttt{Extract-Min} and their degree bounds are maintained independently of them losing children while in the root list, we do not need to penalize them or track their lost children via marks. 

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{Part 2: Extract-Min Operation}

The \texttt{Extract-Min} operation on a Fibonacci Heap involves extracting the current minimum node, promoting all its children to the root list, and then consolidating the root list to combine trees of equal degrees.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Fibonacci-Heap-Extract-Min and Consolidate Concepts}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State Extract the minimum node $z$ from the root list.
\For{each child $x$ of $z$}
    \State Add $x$ to the root list.
    \State $x.p \gets \text{NIL}$
    \State $x.mark \gets \text{FALSE}$ \Comment{Marks are inherently cleared for roots}
\EndFor
\State \Call{Consolidate}{$H$} \Comment{Merge roots of equal degrees using an array $A$}
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Extract Minimum and Promote Children}
The minimum node is \textbf{17}. We remove 17 from the root list.
Its children are \textbf{163, 350,} and \textbf{479}. We promote these nodes to the root list. 
\textit{Crucial Note:} Node 163 is currently marked. As it is promoted to the root list, it loses its parent. Thus, \textbf{node 163 becomes unmarked}. Its child (382) remains unchanged and keeps its mark.

The new root list (before consolidation) is: 
$$\{129, 90, 110, 163, 350, 479\}$$

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Consolidation Phase}
We use an auxiliary array $A[0 \dots D(n)]$ to track the roots by their degrees and link trees of the same degree. We process the roots sequentially.

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Root} & \textbf{Initial Deg} & \textbf{Action / Linking} & \textbf{Array State $A$} \\ \hline
129 & 1 & Empty at $A[1]$. & $A[1] = 129$ \\ \hline
90  & 0 & Empty at $A[0]$. & $A[0] = 90$, $A[1] = 129$ \\ \hline
110 & 0 & Collides with $A[0]=90$. $90 < 110 \implies$ link 110 below 90. & \\
    &   & Node 90 is now degree 1. Collides with $A[1]=129$. & \\
    &   & $90 < 129 \implies$ link 129 below 90. Node 90 is now degree 2. & $A[2] = 90$ ($A[0], A[1]$ empty)\\ \hline
163 & 1 & Empty at $A[1]$. & $A[1] = 163, A[2] = 90$ \\ \hline
350 & 1 & Collides with $A[1]=163$. $163 < 350 \implies$ link 350 below 163. & \\
    &   & Node 163 is now degree 2. Collides with $A[2]=90$. & \\
    &   & $90 < 163 \implies$ link 163 below 90. Node 90 is now degree 3. & $A[3] = 90$ ($A[1], A[2]$ empty) \\ \hline
479 & 0 & Empty at $A[0]$. & $A[0] = 479, A[3] = 90$ \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 3: Final Heap Reconstruction}
We rebuild the root list from the non-empty entries of array $A$. 
The new roots are \textbf{90} and \textbf{479}. 
Since $90 < 479$, the new minimum pointer points to \textbf{90}.

Node 90 has three children resulting from the links: \textbf{110, 129,} and \textbf{163}. 
Node 163 has a new child \textbf{350} (which itself retains 575), and its original child \textbf{382}. 
Node 129 retains its original child \textbf{849}.

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Resulting Fibonacci Min Heap Diagram:}

\begin{center}
      \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.1, transform shape,
        node style/.style={
            circle, 
            draw=black, 
            thick, 
            fill=white,
            minimum size=0.8cm, 
            inner sep=0pt
        },
        marked style/.style={
            circle, 
            draw=black, 
            thick, 
            double, 
            double distance=1.5pt,
            fill=white,
            minimum size=0.8cm, 
            inner sep=0pt
        },
        edge style/.style={
            thick, 
            black!90
        }
    ]

        % ==========================================
        % NEW ROOT LIST
        % ==========================================
        \node[node style] (n90)  at (0, 0) {90};
        \node[node style] (n479) at (4.0, 0) {479};

        % Root list horizontal connection
        \draw[edge style] (n90) -- (n479);

        % Minimal pointer marker above node 90
        \draw[thick, <-, >=Stealth] (n90.north) -- ++(0, 0.6) node[above] {min};

        % ==========================================
        % SUBTREE UNDER NODE 90
        % ==========================================
        % Children of 90: 163, 129, 110
        \node[node style] (n163) at (-2.5, -1.5) {163};
        \node[node style] (n129) at (0, -1.5) {129};
        \node[node style] (n110) at (2.0, -1.5) {110};

        \draw[edge style] (n90) -- (n163);
        \draw[edge style] (n90) -- (n129);
        \draw[edge style] (n90) -- (n110);

        % ==========================================
        % SUBTREE UNDER NODE 163
        % ==========================================
        % Children of 163: 350, 382
        \node[node style]   (n350) at (-3.5, -3.0) {350};
        \node[marked style] (n382) at (-1.5, -3.0) {382}; % Still marked

        \draw[edge style] (n163) -- (n350);
        \draw[edge style] (n163) -- (n382);

        % ==========================================
        % SUBTREE UNDER NODE 350
        % ==========================================
        \node[node style] (n575) at (-3.5, -4.5) {575};
        \draw[edge style] (n350) -- (n575);

        % ==========================================
        % SUBTREE UNDER NODE 129
        % ==========================================
        \node[node style] (n849) at (0, -3.0) {849};
        \draw[edge style] (n129) -- (n849);

    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ \textit{amortized}. In the worst-case scenario, the root list might initially contain up to $n$ elements, making the actual runtime $\mathcal{O}(n)$. However, consolidating the heap drastically reduces the root list size, and the high actual cost is mathematically offset by the release of potential ($\Phi$) stored in the roots, yielding a strictly $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ amortized time.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ auxiliary space. The `Consolidate` phase requires an array $A$ of size $D(n) + 1$, where $D(n) \le \log_\phi n$ is the maximum degree of any node in a Fibonacci heap of $n$ elements.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2022-23
\item Show the Fibonacci heap that results when the minimum node (3) is deleted from the Fibonacci heap shown below. Show the intermediate steps.
\begin{center}
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    >=stealth,

]

\node[root, right=of n24] (n23) {23};
\node[root, right=of n23] (n17) {17};
\node[root, right=of n17] (n3) {3};

% Child nodes and trees for each root
% Tree B1 (root 7)

\draw (n7) -- (n30);

% Tree B2 (root 24)

\node[below right=1.2cm and 0.3cm of n24] (n46) {46};
\draw (n24) -- (n26);
\draw (n24) -- (n46);
\node[below=of n26] (n35) {35};
\draw (n26) -- (n35);

% Tree B0 (root 23) - no children
% Tree B0 (root 17) - no children

\node[below left=1.3cm and 0.6cm of n3] (n18) {18};
\node[below=1.6cm of n3] (n52) {52};
\node[below right=1.3cm and 0.6cm of n3] (n41) {41};
\draw (n3) -- (n18);
\draw (n3) -- (n52);
\draw (n3) -- (n41);

\node[below=of n18] (n39) {39};
\draw (n18) -- (n39);

\node[below=of n41] (n44) {44};
\draw (n41) -- (n44);

% Sibling connections for root nodes
\draw[dashed] (n7) -- (n24);

\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Problem Objective:} We are tasked with executing the \texttt{EXTRACT-MIN} operation on the given Fibonacci heap. This involves removing the minimum node, appending its children to the root list, and then consolidating the roots to ensure no two roots have the same degree.

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Remove Minimum and Append Children}
The minimum node is identified by the \textit{min} pointer, which points to the node with key \textbf{3}.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item We remove node \textbf{3} from the root list.
    \item Node 3 has three children: \textbf{18}, \textbf{52}, and \textbf{41}.
    \item We elevate these children to the root list.
\end{enumerate}

Assuming a standard left-to-right processing order, the intermediate un-consolidated root list is now: \textbf{7, 24, 23, 17, 18, 52, 41}. 

\vspace{0.5em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    >=stealth,
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, minimum size=0.85cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\large},
    scale=0.9, transform shape
]

% First four roots
\node (n7) at (0, 0) {7};
\node (n30) at (0, -1.5) {30};
\draw (n7) -- (n30);

\node (n24) at (2.5, 0) {24};
\node (n26) at (1.7, -1.5) {26};
\node (n46) at (3.3, -1.5) {46};
\node (n35) at (1.7, -3) {35};
\draw (n24) -- (n26);
\draw (n24) -- (n46);
\draw (n26) -- (n35);

\node (n23) at (5, 0) {23};
\node (n17) at (6.8, 0) {17};

% Elevated children
\node (n18) at (8.8, 0) {18};
\node (n39) at (8.8, -1.5) {39};
\draw (n18) -- (n39);

\node (n52) at (10.8, 0) {52};

\node (n41) at (12.8, 0) {41};
\node (n44) at (12.8, -1.5) {44};
\draw (n41) -- (n44);

% Sibling connections
\draw[dashed] (n7) -- (n24);
\draw[dashed] (n24) -- (n23);
\draw[dashed] (n23) -- (n17);
\draw[dashed] (n17) -- (n18);
\draw[dashed] (n18) -- (n52);
\draw[dashed] (n52) -- (n41);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.5em}

\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Root Consolidation}
We iteratively process the root list and use an auxiliary array $A[0 \dots D(n)]$ to track roots by their degree, merging trees of the same degree so that the final root list contains at most one tree of any given degree. When merging two roots, the one with the larger key becomes a child of the one with the smaller key to maintain the min-heap property.

Initially, $A = [\text{NIL}, \text{NIL}, \text{NIL}, \text{NIL}]$. Let $d(x)$ denote the degree of node $x$.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Process 7:} $d(7) = 1$. $A[1]$ is empty $\implies A[1] \gets 7$.
    \item \textbf{Process 24:} $d(24) = 2$. $A[2]$ is empty $\implies A[2] \gets 24$.
    \item \textbf{Process 23:} $d(23) = 0$. $A[0]$ is empty $\implies A[0] \gets 23$.
    \item \textbf{Process 17:} $d(17) = 0$. $A[0]$ is occupied by 23. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textit{Merge (17, 23):} $17 < 23$, so 23 becomes a child of 17. $d(17)$ becomes 1. $A[0] \gets \text{NIL}$.
        \item Now $d(17) = 1$. $A[1]$ is occupied by 7.
        \item \textit{Merge (7, 17):} $7 < 17$, so 17 becomes a child of 7. $d(7)$ becomes 2. $A[1] \gets \text{NIL}$.
        \item Now $d(7) = 2$. $A[2]$ is occupied by 24.
        \item \textit{Merge (7, 24):} $7 < 24$, so 24 becomes a child of 7. $d(7)$ becomes 3. $A[2] \gets \text{NIL}$.
        \item Now $d(7) = 3$. $A[3]$ is empty $\implies A[3] \gets 7$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Process 18:} $d(18) = 1$. $A[1]$ is empty $\implies A[1] \gets 18$.
    \item \textbf{Process 52:} $d(52) = 0$. $A[0]$ is empty $\implies A[0] \gets 52$.
    \item \textbf{Process 41:} $d(41) = 1$. $A[1]$ is occupied by 18.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textit{Merge (18, 41):} $18 < 41$, so 41 becomes a child of 18. $d(18)$ becomes 2. $A[1] \gets \text{NIL}$.
        \item Now $d(18) = 2$. $A[2]$ is empty $\implies A[2] \gets 18$.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Final Array State:} 
$A[0] = 52$, $A[1] = \text{NIL}$, $A[2] = 18$, $A[3] = 7$.

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{Step 3: Final Fibonacci Heap}
We reconstruct the root list from the non-empty slots in array $A$. The resulting roots are \textbf{52}, \textbf{18}, and \textbf{7}. The new minimum node is \textbf{7}.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    >=stealth,
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, minimum size=0.9cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\large},
    min_pointer/.style={draw=none, minimum size=0.1cm, inner sep=0pt}
]

% Tree 7
\node (n7) at (0, 0) {7};
\node (n30) at (-2, -1.8) {30};
\node (n17) at (0, -1.8) {17};
\node (n23) at (0, -3.6) {23};
\node (n24) at (2.5, -1.8) {24};
\node (n26) at (1.7, -3.6) {26};
\node (n35) at (1.7, -5.4) {35};
\node (n46) at (3.3, -3.6) {46};

\draw (n7) -- (n30);
\draw (n7) -- (n17);
\draw (n7) -- (n24);
\draw (n17) -- (n23);
\draw (n24) -- (n26);
\draw (n24) -- (n46);
\draw (n26) -- (n35);

% Tree 18
\node (n18) at (7, 0) {18};
\node (n39) at (6, -1.8) {39};
\node (n41) at (8, -1.8) {41};
\node (n44) at (8, -3.6) {44};

\draw (n18) -- (n39);
\draw (n18) -- (n41);
\draw (n41) -- (n44);

% Tree 52
\node (n52) at (11, 0) {52};

% Roots connections
\draw[dashed] (n7) -- (n18);
\draw[dashed] (n18) -- (n52);

% Min pointer
\node[min_pointer, above=0.8cm of n7, label={[label distance=0.1cm, font=\large]above:min}] (minptr) {};
\draw[->, ultra thick] (minptr.south) -- (n7.north);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} The true cost of \texttt{EXTRACT-MIN} is proportional to the number of children of the minimum node plus the number of roots processed during consolidation. The number of roots processed is $O(D(n))$, where $D(n) \le \lfloor \log_\phi n \rfloor$. However, through potential method analysis, the \textbf{amortized time complexity is $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$}.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} The structural consolidation requires an auxiliary array $A$ of size $D(n) + 1$, yielding an auxiliary \textbf{space complexity of $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$}.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write the algorithm for extracting the minimum key from a Fibonacci heap. \mk{8}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\textbf{Problem Objective:} Provide the complete algorithm for extracting the minimum key from a Fibonacci Heap. This operation is fundamental to the data structure and involves removing the minimum node, elevating its children, and consolidating the root list to merge trees of equal degrees.

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{1. Extract-Min Algorithm}
The main procedure removes the minimum node, adds its children to the root list, and triggers consolidation.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{\texttt{FIB-HEAP-EXTRACT-MIN(H)}}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State $z \gets H.min$
\If{$z \neq \text{NIL}$}
    \For{each child $x$ of $z$}
        \State add $x$ to the root list of $H$
        \State $x.p \gets \text{NIL}$
    \EndFor
    \State remove $z$ from the root list of $H$
    \If{$z == z.right$} \Comment{Only node in the root list}
        \State $H.min \gets \text{NIL}$
    \Else
        \State $H.min \gets z.right$ \Comment{Temporary assignment}
        \State \texttt{CONSOLIDATE}(H) \Comment{Merge trees of equal degree}
    \EndIf
    \State $H.n \gets H.n - 1$
\EndIf
\State \Return $z$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{2. Root Consolidation}
The consolidation step is crucial for maintaining the efficiency of the Fibonacci Heap. It ensures that no two roots in the root list have the same degree. Let $D(H.n)$ be the maximum degree of any node in an $n$-node Fibonacci heap (typically bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$).

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{\texttt{CONSOLIDATE(H)}}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State let $A[0 \dots D(H.n)]$ be a new array
\For{$i \gets 0$ \textbf{to} $D(H.n)$}
    \State $A[i] \gets \text{NIL}$
\EndFor
\For{each node $w$ in the root list of $H$}
    \State $x \gets w$
    \State $d \gets x.degree$
    \While{$A[d] \neq \text{NIL}$} \Comment{Another node with the same degree exists}
        \State $y \gets A[d]$
        \If{$x.key > y.key$}
            \State exchange $x$ \textbf{with} $y$
        \EndIf
        \State \texttt{FIB-HEAP-LINK(H, y, x)} \Comment{Make $y$ a child of $x$}
        \State $A[d] \gets \text{NIL}$
        \State $d \gets d + 1$
    \EndWhile
    \State $A[d] \gets x$
\EndFor
\State $H.min \gets \text{NIL}$
\For{$i \gets 0$ \textbf{to} $D(H.n)$} \Comment{Reconstruct the root list}
    \If{$A[i] \neq \text{NIL}$}
        \If{$H.min == \text{NIL}$}
            \State create a root list for $H$ containing just $A[i]$
            \State $H.min \gets A[i]$
        \Else
            \State insert $A[i]$ into $H$'s root list
            \If{$A[i].key < H.min.key$}
                \State $H.min \gets A[i]$
            \EndIf
        \EndIf
    \EndIf
\EndFor
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{3. Linking Trees}
The linking helper function makes one root node a child of another, maintaining the min-heap property.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{\texttt{FIB-HEAP-LINK(H, y, x)}}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State remove $y$ from the root list of $H$
\State make $y$ a child of $x$, incrementing $x.degree$
\State $y.mark \gets \text{FALSE}$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} The \textbf{amortized time complexity is $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$}. The actual execution time is proportional to the number of children of the minimum node plus the number of trees in the root list. While the root list can theoretically contain up to $\mathcal{O}(n)$ trees in the worst case (e.g., after many \texttt{INSERT} operations), the potential method analysis ensures that over a sequence of operations, the average cost per \texttt{EXTRACT-MIN} is bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ because the \texttt{CONSOLIDATE} routine drastically reduces the number of roots.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} The structural consolidation algorithm requires an auxiliary array $A$ of size $D(H.n) + 1$, where $D(n) \le \lfloor \log_\phi n \rfloor$. Thus, the auxiliary \textbf{space complexity is $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$}.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What is a maximally damaged tree in the context of Fibonacci heaps? Draw the maximally damaged tree of order 5 (degree of root is 5). \mk{4+6}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsubsection*{1. Definition of a Maximally Damaged Tree (4 Marks)}

In a Fibonacci heap, trees are initially formed by linking binomial trees. A node can lose children due to \texttt{Decrease-Key} operations. To maintain the structural bounds of the heap, a non-root node is allowed to lose \textbf{at most one child}. If it loses a second child, it is cascaded (cut) and moved to the root list. 

A \textbf{maximally damaged tree} of order (or degree) $k$ is a tree that contains the \textbf{absolute minimum possible number of nodes} for a root of degree $k$. Structurally, this occurs when every non-root node in the tree has lost exactly one child—the maximum damage permitted before the node is cut.

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Key Mathematical Properties:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Degree Sequence:} For a maximally damaged tree rooted at node $x$ with degree $k$, its children $c_1, c_2, \dots, c_k$ (ordered by the time they were linked to $x$) have the minimum possible degrees. Specifically, the degree of the $i$-th child is exactly $d(c_i) = \max(0, i-2)$.
    \item \textbf{Size Bound:} The number of nodes $N(k)$ in a maximally damaged tree of degree $k$ strictly follows the Fibonacci sequence. Specifically, $N(k) = F_{k+2}$, where $F_1 = 1, F_2 = 1, F_3 = 2, F_4 = 3, \dots$
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{2. Maximally Damaged Tree of Order 5 (6 Marks)}

For a tree of order $k = 5$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item The total number of nodes is $F_{5+2} = F_7 = 13$.
    \item The root has $5$ children.
    \item Based on the degree sequence rule $d(c_i) = \max(0, i-2)$, the degrees of the root's five children are \textbf{0, 0, 1, 2, and 3}.
    \item This recursive property applies to all subtrees (e.g., the child of degree 3 has children of degrees 0, 0, and 1).
\end{itemize}

Below is the exact structure of the maximally damaged tree of order 5. \textit{Note: The number inside each node represents its current degree.}

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    >=stealth,
    every node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=0.9cm, inner sep=0pt, font=\Large\bfseries},
    level distance=1.8cm
]

% Root Node
\node[fill=cB!15] (R) at (2, 0) {5};

% Level 1: Children of the Root (Degrees: 0, 0, 1, 2, 3)
\node (C1) at (-3.5, -1.8) {0};
\node (C2) at (-1.5, -1.8) {0};
\node (C3) at (0.5, -1.8) {1};
\node (C4) at (3.0, -1.8) {2};
\node (C5) at (7.0, -1.8) {3};

\draw[thick] (R) -- (C1);
\draw[thick] (R) -- (C2);
\draw[thick] (R) -- (C3);
\draw[thick] (R) -- (C4);
\draw[thick] (R) -- (C5);

% Level 2
% Child of C3 (Degree: 0)
\node (C31) at (0.5, -3.8) {0};
\draw[thick] (C3) -- (C31);

% Children of C4 (Degrees: 0, 0)
\node (C41) at (2.0, -3.8) {0};
\node (C42) at (4.0, -3.8) {0};
\draw[thick] (C4) -- (C41);
\draw[thick] (C4) -- (C42);

% Children of C5 (Degrees: 0, 0, 1)
\node (C51) at (5.0, -3.8) {0};
\node (C52) at (7.0, -3.8) {0};
\node (C53) at (9.0, -3.8) {1};
\draw[thick] (C5) -- (C51);
\draw[thick] (C5) -- (C52);
\draw[thick] (C5) -- (C53);

% Level 3
% Child of C53 (Degree: 0)
\node (C531) at (9.0, -5.8) {0};
\draw[thick] (C53) -- (C531);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{1em}

\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{Implications on Complexity Analysis}
While this is a structural property rather than an algorithm, understanding the maximally damaged tree is foundational to proving the complexity bounds of Fibonacci heaps:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Maximum Degree Bounding:} Because the worst-case (minimum size) tree of degree $k$ grows exponentially at the rate of the golden ratio $\phi$ ($size \ge \phi^k$), we can invert this to prove that the maximum degree $D(n)$ of any node in an $n$-node Fibonacci heap is bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$.
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity Impact:} This $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ maximum degree ensures that the \texttt{Extract-Min} and \texttt{Delete} operations—which must iterate over a node's children and process the root list—run in $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ amortized time.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\end{enumerate}

%% ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
%%  PART III — HASHING
%% ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
\secdivider{Part III}{Hashing}{cC}{cC}{Hash Functions and Collision Resolution}

%% ── S3: Hashing ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{S3 --- Hashing}
\label{sec:S3}
\markboth{S3 --- Hashing}{}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cC}{Hash Functions and Collision Resolution}
\label{sub:s3-hash}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item Consider a hash table of size 11 into which the keys 10, 22, 31, 4, 15, 28, 17, 88, and 59 are inserted in the given order using $h(k) = k \bmod 11$. For collision resolution, four different strategies are to be used independently: (i) separate chaining, (ii) linear probing with $h(k,i) = (h(k) + 2i) \bmod 11$, (iii) quadratic probing with $h(k,i) = (h(k) + 3 + i^2) \bmod 11$, and (iv) double hashing where $h_1(k) = k \bmod 11$ and $h_2(k) = 7 - (k \bmod 7)$. For each method, construct the final hash table and compute the total number of unsuccessful probes required during insertion.
\mk{25} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cC!4,colframe=cC,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cC]

We are tasked with inserting the sequence of keys $K = \langle 10, 22, 31, 4, 15, 28, 17, 88, 59 \rangle$ into a hash table of size $m = 11$, using the base hash function $h(k) = k \bmod 11$. We will evaluate four different collision resolution strategies, constructing the final table and calculating the number of unsuccessful probes. 

\textbf{Base Hash Values:}
\begin{align*}
h(10) &= 10 \bmod 11 = 10 & h(4) &= 4 \bmod 11 = 4 & h(17) &= 17 \bmod 11 = 6 \\
h(22) &= 22 \bmod 11 = 0 & h(15) &= 15 \bmod 11 = 4 & h(88) &= 88 \bmod 11 = 0 \\
h(31) &= 31 \bmod 11 = 9 & h(28) &= 28 \bmod 11 = 6 & h(59) &= 59 \bmod 11 = 4
\end{align*}

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{(i) Separate Chaining}
In separate chaining, collisions are resolved by appending elements to a linked list at each table slot. An unsuccessful probe during insertion is defined as probing an existing node in the chain (assuming we traverse the list to check for duplicates or append to the tail).

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{10, 22, 31, 4:} Inserted into slots 10, 0, 9, 4 respectively. \textit{(0 probes each)}
    \item \textbf{15:} $h(15) = 4$. Traverses $[4]$ and appends. \textit{(1 unsuccessful probe)}
    \item \textbf{28:} $h(28) = 6$. Inserted into slot 6. \textit{(0 probes)}
    \item \textbf{17:} $h(17) = 6$. Traverses $[28]$ and appends. \textit{(1 unsuccessful probe)}
    \item \textbf{88:} $h(88) = 0$. Traverses $[22]$ and appends. \textit{(1 unsuccessful probe)}
    \item \textbf{59:} $h(59) = 4$. Traverses $[4, 15]$ and appends. \textit{(2 unsuccessful probes)}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Total unsuccessful probes = $0 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 2 = 5$.}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={font=\small},
    slot/.style={draw, rectangle, minimum width=1.0cm, minimum height=0.6cm, fill=cC!20, font=\bfseries},
    box/.style={draw, rectangle, minimum width=1.0cm, minimum height=0.6cm, fill=white},
    ->, >=stealth, thick
]
% Draw array slots
\foreach \i in {0,...,10} {
    \node[slot] (S\i) at (0, -\i*0.8) {\i};
}

% Draw lists
\node[box] (N0_1) at (2, 0) {22};
\node[box] (N0_2) at (3.5, 0) {88};
\draw (S0.east) -- (N0_1.west); \draw (N0_1.east) -- (N0_2.west);

\node[box] (N4_1) at (2, -3.2) {4};
\node[box] (N4_2) at (3.5, -3.2) {15};
\node[box] (N4_3) at (5.0, -3.2) {59};
\draw (S4.east) -- (N4_1.west); \draw (N4_1.east) -- (N4_2.west); \draw (N4_2.east) -- (N4_3.west);

\node[box] (N6_1) at (2, -4.8) {28};
\node[box] (N6_2) at (3.5, -4.8) {17};
\draw (S6.east) -- (N6_1.west); \draw (N6_1.east) -- (N6_2.west);

\node[box] (N9_1) at (2, -7.2) {31};
\draw (S9.east) -- (N9_1.west);

\node[box] (N10_1) at (2, -8.0) {10};
\draw (S10.east) -- (N10_1.west);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{(ii) Linear Probing}
Formula: $h(k,i) = (h(k) + 2i) \bmod 11$.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{10, 22, 31, 4:} $i=0$ maps to empty slots 10, 0, 9, 4. \textit{(0 probes)}
    \item \textbf{15:} $h(15)=4$. $i=0$: slot $4$ (Occupied). $i=1$: $(4 + 2) \bmod 11 = 6$ (Empty). \textit{(1 probe)}
    \item \textbf{28:} $h(28)=6$. $i=0$: slot $6$ (Occupied). $i=1$: $(6 + 2) \bmod 11 = 8$ (Empty). \textit{(1 probe)}
    \item \textbf{17:} $h(17)=6$.
        \begin{itemize}
            \item $i=0, 1, 2$: Slots $6, 8, 10$ are occupied (by 15, 28, 10 respectively).
            \item $i=3$: $(6 + 6) \bmod 11 = 1$ (Empty). \textit{(3 probes)}
        \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{88:} $h(88)=0$. $i=0$: slot $0$ (Occupied). $i=1$: $(0 + 2) \bmod 11 = 2$ (Empty). \textit{(1 probe)}
    \item \textbf{59:} $h(59)=4$.
        \begin{itemize}
            \item $i=0 \dots 4$: Slots $4, 6, 8, 10, 1$ are occupied (by 4, 15, 28, 10, 17 respectively).
            \item $i=5$: $(4 + 10) \bmod 11 = 3$ (Empty). \textit{(5 probes)}
        \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Total unsuccessful probes = $0+0+0+0 + 1 + 1 + 3 + 1 + 5 = 11$.}

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\rowcolor{cC!15}
\textbf{Index} & \textbf{0} & \textbf{1} & \textbf{2} & \textbf{3} & \textbf{4} & \textbf{5} & \textbf{6} & \textbf{7} & \textbf{8} & \textbf{9} & \textbf{10} \\
\hline
\textbf{Key} & 22 & 17 & 88 & 59 & 4 & & 15 & & 28 & 31 & 10 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{(iii) Quadratic Probing}
Formula: $h(k,i) = (h(k) + 3 + i^2) \bmod 11$. \textit{Note the constant offset $+3$ even when $i=0$.}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{10:} $i=0 \implies (10 + 3 + 0) \bmod 11 = 2$ (Empty). \textit{(0 probes)}
    \item \textbf{22:} $i=0 \implies (0 + 3 + 0) \bmod 11 = 3$ (Empty). \textit{(0 probes)}
    \item \textbf{31:} $i=0 \implies (9 + 3 + 0) \bmod 11 = 1$ (Empty). \textit{(0 probes)}
    \item \textbf{4:} $i=0 \implies (4 + 3 + 0) \bmod 11 = 7$ (Empty). \textit{(0 probes)}
    \item \textbf{15:} $h(15)=4$. $i=0 \implies 7$ (Occupied). $i=1 \implies (4 + 3 + 1) \bmod 11 = 8$ (Empty). \textit{(1 probe)}
    \item \textbf{28:} $h(28)=6$. $i=0 \implies (6 + 3 + 0) \bmod 11 = 9$ (Empty). \textit{(0 probes)}
    \item \textbf{17:} $h(17)=6$. $i=0 \implies 9$ (Occupied). $i=1 \implies (6 + 3 + 1) \bmod 11 = 10$ (Empty). \textit{(1 probe)}
    \item \textbf{88:} $h(88)=0$. $i=0 \implies 3$ (Occupied). $i=1 \implies (0 + 3 + 1) \bmod 11 = 4$ (Empty). \textit{(1 probe)}
    \item \textbf{59:} $h(59)=4$.
        \begin{itemize}
            \item $i=0 \implies 7$ (Occupied).
            \item $i=1 \implies (4 + 3 + 1) \bmod 11 = 8$ (Occupied).
            \item $i=2 \implies (4 + 3 + 4) \bmod 11 = 11 \equiv 0$ (Empty). \textit{(2 probes)}
        \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Total unsuccessful probes = $0+0+0+0 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 2 = 5$.}

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\rowcolor{cC!15}
\textbf{Index} & \textbf{0} & \textbf{1} & \textbf{2} & \textbf{3} & \textbf{4} & \textbf{5} & \textbf{6} & \textbf{7} & \textbf{8} & \textbf{9} & \textbf{10} \\
\hline
\textbf{Key} & 59 & 31 & 10 & 22 & 88 & & & 4 & 15 & 28 & 17 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.5em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsubsection*{(iv) Double Hashing}
Formulas: $h_1(k) = k \bmod 11$, $h_2(k) = 7 - (k \bmod 7)$, and $h(k,i) = (h_1(k) + i \cdot h_2(k)) \bmod 11$.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{10, 22, 31, 4:} Inserted directly into their $h_1$ slots: 10, 0, 9, 4 respectively. \textit{(0 probes)}
    \item \textbf{15:} $h_1(15)=4$ (Occupied). $h_2(15) = 7 - 1 = 6$.
        \begin{itemize}
            \item $i=1: (4 + 6) \bmod 11 = 10$ (Occupied by 10).
            \item $i=2: (4 + 12) \bmod 11 = 5$ (Empty). \textit{(2 probes)}
        \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{28:} $h_1(28)=6$. Slot 6 is empty. \textit{(0 probes)}
    \item \textbf{17:} $h_1(17)=6$ (Occupied). $h_2(17) = 7 - 3 = 4$.
        \begin{itemize}
            \item $i=1: (6 + 4) \bmod 11 = 10$ (Occupied by 10).
            \item $i=2: (6 + 8) \bmod 11 = 14 \equiv 3$ (Empty). \textit{(2 probes)}
        \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{88:} $h_1(88)=0$ (Occupied). $h_2(88) = 7 - 4 = 3$.
        \begin{itemize}
            \item $i=1: 3$ (Occupied by 17).
            \item $i=2: 6$ (Occupied by 28).
            \item $i=3: 9$ (Occupied by 31).
            \item $i=4: 12 \equiv 1$ (Empty). \textit{(4 probes)}
        \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{59:} $h_1(59)=4$ (Occupied). $h_2(59) = 7 - 3 = 4$.
        \begin{itemize}
            \item $i=1: (4 + 4) \bmod 11 = 8$ (Empty). \textit{(1 probe)}
        \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Total unsuccessful probes = $0+0+0+0 + 2 + 0 + 2 + 4 + 1 = 9$.}

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\rowcolor{cC!15}
\textbf{Index} & \textbf{0} & \textbf{1} & \textbf{2} & \textbf{3} & \textbf{4} & \textbf{5} & \textbf{6} & \textbf{7} & \textbf{8} & \textbf{9} & \textbf{10} \\
\hline
\textbf{Key} & 22 & 88 & & 17 & 4 & 15 & 28 & & 59 & 31 & 10 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Show that the expected time for a successful search in a hash table implemented using chaining is $O(1 + \alpha)$ where $\alpha$ is the load factor.
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24, 2019--20, 2016--17, 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cC!4,colframe=cC,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cC]

\subsubsection*{1. Preliminaries and Assumptions}
To analyze the expected time for a successful search in a hash table with collision resolution by separate chaining, we must first establish our definitions and the underlying probabilistic assumptions.

\begin{itemize}
    \item Let $m$ be the number of slots in the hash table $T$.
    \item Let $n$ be the number of keys currently stored in the hash table.
    \item Let $\alpha = \frac{n}{m}$ be the \textbf{load factor}, which represents the average number of elements stored in a single chain.
    \item \textbf{Simple Uniform Hashing Assumption (SUHA):} We assume that any given key $k$ is equally likely to hash into any of the $m$ slots, independently of the slot chosen by any other key. Mathematically, $\Pr[h(k_i) = h(k_j)] = \frac{1}{m}$ for any $i \neq j$.
    \item We assume that new elements are inserted at the \textit{head} of the linked list. (The asymptotic bound remains the same even if inserted at the tail).
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    listnode/.style={draw, rectangle, minimum width=0.8cm, minimum height=0.6cm, fill=white, thick},
    slot/.style={draw, rectangle, minimum width=1.2cm, minimum height=0.6cm, fill=cC!20, font=\bfseries, thick},
    ->, >=stealth, thick
]

% Array slots
\node[slot] (A0) at (0, 0) {0};
\node[slot] (A1) at (0, -0.8) {1};
\node[slot] (A2) at (0, -1.6) {2};
\node[slot] (A3) at (0, -2.4) {\dots};
\node[slot] (Am) at (0, -3.2) {$m-1$};

\node[left=0.2cm of A0] {$T[0]$};
\node[left=0.2cm of A1] {$T[1]$};
\node[left=0.2cm of A2] {$T[2]$};
\node[left=0.2cm of Am] {$T[m-1]$};

% List for slot 1
\node[listnode] (N1_1) at (2, -0.8) {$k_j$};
\node[listnode] (N1_2) at (3.5, -0.8) {$k_i$};
\node[listnode] (N1_3) at (5.0, -0.8) {$k_l$};
\draw (A1.east) -- (N1_1.west);
\draw (N1_1.east) -- (N1_2.west);
\draw (N1_2.east) -- (N1_3.west);

% List for slot m-1
\node[listnode] (Nm_1) at (2, -3.2) {$k_x$};
\draw (Am.east) -- (Nm_1.west);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{1em}

\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{2. Mathematical Proof}

In a \textbf{successful search}, we are searching for an element $x$ that is guaranteed to already be in the hash table. 
The time required to find $x$ is proportional to:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time to compute the hash value $h(x)$.
    \item The time to traverse the linked list at $T[h(x)]$ until the element $x$ is found.
\end{enumerate}

Let the $n$ elements inserted into the hash table be denoted as $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$, indexed by their insertion order.
Under SUHA, the target element $x$ is equally likely to be any of the $n$ elements. Thus, the probability that we are searching for the $i$-th inserted element $x_i$ is $\frac{1}{n}$.

When we search for $x_i$, the number of elements we must examine in the linked list consists of:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $1$ (for examining $x_i$ itself).
    \item The number of elements inserted \textit{after} $x_i$ that hashed to the same slot (since new elements are inserted at the head, they appear before $x_i$ in the chain).
\end{itemize}

Define an indicator random variable $X_{ij}$:
\[
X_{ij} = 
\begin{cases} 
1 & \text{if } h(x_i) = h(x_j) \\
0 & \text{otherwise} 
\end{cases}
\]
By the Simple Uniform Hashing Assumption, the probability that two keys hash to the same slot is $\frac{1}{m}$, so:
\[
\mathbb{E}[X_{ij}] = 1 \cdot \Pr[h(x_i) = h(x_j)] + 0 \cdot \Pr[h(x_i) \neq h(x_j)] = \frac{1}{m}
\]

The expected number of elements examined during a successful search for $x_i$ is therefore:
\begin{equation}
\mathbb{E}[\text{Search for } x_i] = 1 + \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} X_{ij} \right] = 1 + \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[X_{ij}] = 1 + \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \frac{1}{m} = 1 + \frac{n-i}{m}
\end{equation}

To find the \textbf{average expected time over all possible searches}, we compute the expectation over all $n$ elements:
\begin{align}
\mathbb{E}[\text{Successful Search}] &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[\text{Search for } x_i] \nonumber \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( 1 + \frac{n-i}{m} \right) \nonumber \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} 1 + \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (n - i) \right) \nonumber \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \left( n + \frac{1}{m} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} n - \sum_{i=1}^{n} i \right] \right) \nonumber \\
&= 1 + \frac{1}{nm} \left( n^2 - \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \right) \nonumber \\
&= 1 + \frac{1}{nm} \left( \frac{n^2 - n}{2} \right) \nonumber \\
&= 1 + \frac{n-1}{2m}
\end{align}

\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{3. Conclusion and Complexity Analysis}

Using the definition of the load factor $\alpha = \frac{n}{m}$, we substitute into our result:
\begin{equation}
\mathbb{E}[\text{Successful Search}] = 1 + \frac{n-1}{2m} = 1 + \frac{n}{2m} - \frac{1}{2m} = 1 + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\alpha}{2n}
\end{equation}

Asymptotically, as $n \to \infty$, the strictly constant and lower-order terms $\left(-\frac{\alpha}{2n}\right)$ become negligible. The number of elements examined is bounded by $\Theta(1 + \frac{\alpha}{2})$.

\vspace{0.5em}
\textbf{Final Time Complexity:}
Taking into account the $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time required to initially compute the hash function, the total expected time for a successful search is:
\[
T(n) = \mathcal{O}(1) + \mathcal{O}\left(1 + \frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \mathcal{O}(1 + \alpha)
\]
Thus, we have successfully shown that the expected time for a successful search in a hash table using chaining is strictly $\mathcal{O}(1 + \alpha)$.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item Explain how you can handle collisions in a hash table using the double hashing method.
\mk{7} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cC!4,colframe=cC,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cC]

\subsubsection*{1. Introduction to Double Hashing}

Double hashing is a highly efficient collision resolution technique used in open-addressed hash tables.
