To maintain the integrity of the dictionary operations, we must resolve these collisions. The two primary paradigms for collision resolution are \textbf{Chaining} and \textbf{Open Addressing}.

\subsection*{1. Chaining (Separate Chaining)}

In chaining, the hash table $T[0 \dots m-1]$ acts as an array of pointers. Elements that hash to the same slot are stored in a secondary data structure, typically a linked list.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Insertion:} A new element $x$ is inserted at the head of the list $T[h(x.key)]$. This takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time if we assume elements are distinct or if we do not check for duplicates.
    \item \textbf{Search:} To find an element with key $k$, we compute $h(k)$ and perform a linear search through the list $T[h(k)]$. The expected search time is proportional to the load factor $\alpha = n/m$.
    \item \textbf{Deletion:} An element is removed from the linked list $T[h(x.key)]$. Using doubly linked lists, this takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    listnode/.style={rectangle split, rectangle split parts=2, draw, rectangle split horizontal, fill=cC!10, minimum height=0.6cm, font=\small},
    arraycell/.style={draw, rectangle, minimum width=1.2cm, minimum height=0.7cm, fill=black!5, font=\bfseries\small},
    pointer/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, cC!80!black}
]
    % Array
    \node[arraycell] (A0) at (0,0) {0};
    \node[arraycell, below=0mm of A0] (A1) {1};
    \node[arraycell, below=0mm of A1] (A2) {2};
    \node[arraycell, below=0mm of A2] (A3) {3};
    \node[arraycell, below=0mm of A3] (A4) {4};

    % Lists
    \node[listnode, right=0.8cm of A1] (N1) {$k_1$ \nodepart{second} $\bullet$};
    \node[listnode, right=0.6cm of N1] (N2) {$k_4$ \nodepart{second} $\slash$};
    
    \node[listnode, right=0.8cm of A3] (N3) {$k_2$ \nodepart{second} $\bullet$};
    \node[listnode, right=0.6cm of N3] (N4) {$k_5$ \nodepart{second} $\bullet$};
    \node[listnode, right=0.6cm of N4] (N5) {$k_7$ \nodepart{second} $\slash$};

    % Pointers
    \draw[pointer] (A1.east) -- (N1.west);
    \draw[pointer] (N1.two) -- (N2.west);      % Fixed: .two center -> .two
    
    \draw[pointer] (A3.east) -- (N3.west);
    \draw[pointer] (N3.two) -- (N4.west);      % Fixed: .two center -> .two
    \draw[pointer] (N4.two) -- (N5.west);      % Fixed: .two center -> .two

    % Null pointers
    \node[right=0.2cm of A0] {$\slash$};
    \node[right=0.2cm of A2] {$\slash$};
    \node[right=0.2cm of A4] {$\slash$};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.5em}

\subsection*{2. Open Addressing}

In open addressing, all elements are stored \textit{directly} within the hash table itself; no external linked lists or pointers are used. When a collision occurs, the algorithm systematically \textbf{probes} (examines) other slots in the hash table until an empty slot is found.

To define this sequence of probes, the hash function is extended to include the probe number as a second argument:
\begin{equation}
    h: U \times \{0, 1, \dots, m-1\} \to \{0, 1, \dots, m-1\}
\end{equation}
For a given key $k$, the probe sequence $\langle h(k,0), h(k,1), \dots, h(k,m-1) \rangle$ must be a permutation of $\langle 0, 1, \dots, m-1 \rangle$ to ensure every slot can eventually be checked. The load factor $\alpha$ can never exceed $1$.

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. Probe Sequence Techniques}

There are three commonly used techniques to compute the probe sequence in open addressing.

\subsubsection*{(i) Linear Probing}
Given an ordinary hash function $h'(k)$, linear probing resolves collisions by sequentially searching the next available slot, wrapping around the table if necessary.
\begin{equation}
    h(k, i) = (h'(k) + i) \bmod m
\end{equation}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Advantage:} Extremely simple to implement and provides excellent cache locality, making it fast in practice for low load factors.
    \item \textbf{Disadvantage:} Suffers from \textbf{primary clustering}. Long runs of occupied slots tend to build up, increasing the expected search time. If a slot is preceded by $i$ full slots, the probability of it being filled next is $(i+1)/m$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{(ii) Quadratic Probing}
Quadratic probing uses a quadratic function of the probe number $i$ to determine the interval between probes.
\begin{equation}
    h(k, i) = (h'(k) + c_1 i + c_2 i^2) \bmod m
\end{equation}
where $c_1$ and $c_2 \neq 0$ are auxiliary constants.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Advantage:} Eliminates primary clustering by ensuring the probe sequence skips ahead at quadratically increasing intervals.
    \item \textbf{Disadvantage:} Suffers from \textbf{secondary clustering}. If two keys map to the exact same initial slot ($h'(k_1) = h'(k_2)$), they will follow the exact same probe sequence. Additionally, $c_1, c_2$, and $m$ must be carefully constrained to ensure the sequence probes the entire table.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{(iii) Double Hashing}
Double hashing represents the most robust open addressing technique. It uses two independent hash functions, $h_1(k)$ and $h_2(k)$. The second hash function determines the \textit{step size} for the probe sequence.
\begin{equation}
    h(k, i) = (h_1(k) + i \cdot h_2(k)) \bmod m
\end{equation}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Advantage:} Eliminates both primary and secondary clustering. Even if two keys hash to the same initial slot ($h_1(k_1) = h_1(k_2)$), they will almost certainly have a different step size ($h_2(k_1) \neq h_2(k_2)$), causing their probe sequences to diverge immediately. It produces $\mathcal{O}(m^2)$ distinct probe sequences.
    \item \textbf{Requirement:} The step size $h_2(k)$ \textit{must} be relatively prime to the table size $m$ for all keys. This guarantees that the probe sequence will visit every single slot in the table before returning to the original slot. This is typically achieved by making $m$ a prime number and designing $h_2(k)$ to return a strictly positive integer smaller than $m$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What makes a good hash function? Explain the three commonly used techniques to compute the probe sequences required for open addressing in a hash table. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2014--15, 2011--12}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cC!4,colframe=cC,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cC]

\subsection*{1. Characteristics of a Good Hash Function}

A hash function $h(k)$ maps a universe of keys $U$ to a finite set of array indices $\{0, 1, \dots, m-1\}$. A high-quality hash function must satisfy several theoretical and practical properties to ensure efficient dictionary operations:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Simple Uniform Hashing (Uniform Distribution):} This is the most fundamental requirement. A good hash function should distribute keys uniformly across all $m$ slots of the hash table. Every key should have an equal probability of hashing to any given slot, independent of where other keys map. Mathematically, for any slot $j$:
    \begin{equation}
        P(h(k) = j) = \frac{1}{m}
    \end{equation}
    \item \textbf{Determinism:} The function must be completely deterministic. A specific key $k$ must consistently map to the exact same hash value $h(k)$ every time it is evaluated.
    \item \textbf{Computational Efficiency:} The hash function must be fast to compute, operating in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time. If the function is computationally expensive, it negates the primary advantage of using a hash table.
    \item \textbf{Full Key Utilization:} The resulting hash value should depend on \textit{all} parts of the key (e.g., all characters in a string), rather than just a prefix or suffix. This guarantees that closely related keys (e.g., \texttt{"pt1"} and \texttt{"pt2"}) hash to vastly different slots.
    \item \textbf{Collision Minimization:} While collisions are mathematically inevitable (due to the Pigeonhole Principle), a good function avoids patterns that map frequently occurring clusters of keys (in the input distribution) to the same slot.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Probe Sequence Techniques in Open Addressing}

In open addressing, all elements are stored directly inside the hash table. When a collision occurs (i.e., the target slot is already occupied), the algorithm systematically "probes" (examines) alternative slots until an empty one is found. 

To achieve this, the hash function is extended to include the \textbf{probe number} $i$ as a second parameter:
\begin{equation}
    h: U \times \{0, 1, \dots, m-1\} \to \{0, 1, \dots, m-1\}
\end{equation}
For a given key $k$, the probe sequence $\langle h(k,0), h(k,1), \dots, h(k,m-1) \rangle$ must form a complete permutation of $\langle 0, 1, \dots, m-1 \rangle$ so that every slot can eventually be checked.

The three commonly used techniques to compute these sequences are:

\subsubsection*{(i) Linear Probing}
Given an ordinary (primary) hash function $h'(k)$, linear probing resolves collisions by sequentially checking the immediate next slot, wrapping around the end of the table if necessary.
\begin{equation}
    h(k, i) = (h'(k) + i) \bmod m
\end{equation}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Mechanism:} If slot $h'(k)$ is full, it checks $h'(k)+1$, then $h'(k)+2$, and so on.
    \item \textbf{Advantage:} Very simple to implement and exhibits excellent cache locality, making it fast in practice when the table is relatively empty.
    \item \textbf{Disadvantage:} Suffers heavily from \textbf{primary clustering}. Contiguous blocks of occupied slots build up, and any key that hashes into a cluster will sequentially extend the cluster, degrading average search/insertion time.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{(ii) Quadratic Probing}
Quadratic probing attempts to eliminate primary clustering by using a quadratic polynomial of the probe number $i$ to define the step size.
\begin{equation}
    h(k, i) = (h'(k) + c_1 i + c_2 i^2) \bmod m
\end{equation}
where $c_1$ and $c_2 \neq 0$ are auxiliary constants.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Mechanism:} The gap between consecutive probes increases quadratically (e.g., checking indices at offsets $+1, +4, +9, +16 \dots$).
    \item \textbf{Advantage:} Successfully resolves primary clustering because the sequence skips over contiguous occupied blocks.
    \item \textbf{Disadvantage:} Suffers from \textbf{secondary clustering}. If two distinct keys map to the same initial slot ($h'(k_1) = h'(k_2)$), they will traverse the exact same probe sequence. Furthermore, specific parameters (like $m$ being prime or a power of 2, and precise values for $c_1, c_2$) are required to ensure the sequence probes the entire table.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{(iii) Double Hashing}
Double hashing is the most robust and theoretically sound open addressing technique. It uses \textit{two} independent hash functions, $h_1(k)$ and $h_2(k)$. The second function dictates the specific step size between probes for that key.
\begin{equation}
    h(k, i) = (h_1(k) + i \cdot h_2(k)) \bmod m
\end{equation}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Mechanism:} The first probe is at $h_1(k)$. Subsequent probes are spaced apart by an interval of exactly $h_2(k)$.
    \item \textbf{Advantage:} Eliminates \textbf{both primary and secondary clustering}. Even if two keys hash to the same initial slot ($h_1(k_1) = h_1(k_2)$), it is highly improbable that they will have the exact same step size ($h_2(k_1) \neq h_2(k_2)$). Their probe sequences diverge immediately. It produces $\Theta(m^2)$ distinct probe sequences, closely approximating ideal simple uniform hashing.
    \item \textbf{Requirement:} To ensure the entire table is searched, the step size $h_2(k)$ \textbf{must be relatively prime} to the table size $m$. A common approach is to set $m$ as a prime number and use $h_2(k) = 1 + (k \bmod m')$, where $m' < m$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Draw the 11-item hash table that results from using the hash function $h(i) = (2i + 5) \bmod 11$ to hash the keys 12, 44, 13, 88, 23, 94, 11, 39, 20, 16, 5, assuming collisions are handled by double hashing using a secondary hash function $h'(k) = 7 - (k \bmod 7)$. \mk{12}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2013--14}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cC!4,colframe=cC,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cC]

\subsection*{1. Hash Functions and Parameters}

We are given a hash table of size $m = 11$ and the following functions for double hashing:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Primary Hash Function:} $h(k) = (2k + 5) \bmod 11$
    \item \textbf{Secondary Hash Function:} $h'(k) = 7 - (k \bmod 7)$
    \item \textbf{Double Hashing Probe Sequence:} $H(k, i) = (h(k) + i \cdot h'(k)) \bmod 11$ for $i = 0, 1, 2, \dots$
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{2. Step-by-Step Insertion Trace}

We insert the sequence of keys $K = \langle 12, 44, 13, 88, 23, 94, 11, 39, 20, 16, 5 \rangle$ one by one. The table below traces the calculations for each key.

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\begin{tabularx}{\linewidth}{|c|c|c|X|c|}
\hline
\rowcolor{cC!15} \textbf{Key ($k$)} & \textbf{$h(k)$} & \textbf{$h'(k)$} & \textbf{Probe Sequence: $H(k, i) = (h(k) + i \cdot h'(k)) \bmod 11$} & \textbf{Final Slot} \\ \hline
12 & 7 & -- & $i=0 \implies 7$ (Empty) & \textbf{7} \\ \hline
44 & 5 & -- & $i=0 \implies 5$ (Empty) & \textbf{5} \\ \hline
13 & 9 & -- & $i=0 \implies 9$ (Empty) & \textbf{9} \\ \hline
88 & 5 & 3 & $i=0 \implies 5$ (Col), \ $i=1 \implies (5+3) \bmod 11 = 8$ (Empty) & \textbf{8} \\ \hline
23 & 7 & 5 & $i=0 \implies 7$ (Col), \ $i=1 \implies (7+5) \bmod 11 = 1$ (Empty) & \textbf{1} \\ \hline
94 & 6 & -- & $i=0 \implies 6$ (Empty) & \textbf{6} \\ \hline
11 & 5 & 3 & $i=0 \implies 5$ (Col), \ $i=1 \implies 8$ (Col), \ $i=2 \implies (5+6) \bmod 11 = 0$ & \textbf{0} \\ \hline
39 & 6 & 3 & $i=0 \implies 6$ (Col), \ $i=1 \implies 9$ (Col), \ $i=2 \implies (6+6) \bmod 11 = 1$ (Col) \newline $i=3 \implies (6+9) \bmod 11 = 4$ (Empty) & \textbf{4} \\ \hline
20 & 1 & 1 & $i=0 \implies 1$ (Col), \ $i=1 \implies (1+1) \bmod 11 = 2$ (Empty) & \textbf{2} \\ \hline
16 & 4 & 5 & $i=0 \implies 4$ (Col), \ $i=1 \implies (4+5) \bmod 11 = 9$ (Col) \newline $i=2 \implies (4+10) \bmod 11 = 3$ (Empty) & \textbf{3} \\ \hline
5  & 4 & 2 & $i=0 \implies 4$ (Col), \ $i=1 \implies 6$ (Col), \ $i=2 \implies 8$ (Col) \newline $i=3 \implies (4+6) \bmod 11 = 10$ (Empty) & \textbf{10} \\ \hline
\end{tabularx}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. Final Hash Table}

After inserting all 11 keys, the hash table is completely full. The resulting array is visualized below:

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    cell/.style={draw, rectangle, minimum width=1.1cm, minimum height=0.9cm, fill=cC!10, font=\Large\bfseries},
    idx/.style={font=\small, text=gray}
]
    % Loop to create the array cells based on the final computed values
    \foreach \i/\val in {
        0/11, 
        1/23, 
        2/20, 
        3/16, 
        4/39, 
        5/44, 
        6/94, 
        7/12, 
        8/88, 
        9/13, 
        10/5} {
        \node[cell] (C\i) at (\i*1.15, 0) {\val};
        \node[idx] at (\i*1.15, 0.75) {\i};
    }
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write pseudocode for finding a key, inserting a key, and deleting a key in a hash table using quadratic probing. Explain your method with an example. \mk{12}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2012--13, 2011--12}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cC!4,colframe=cC,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cC]

\subsection*{1. Introduction and The Necessity of Tombstones}

In open addressing with quadratic probing, collisions are resolved by probing the sequence:
\begin{equation}
    h(k, i) = (h'(k) + c_1 i + c_2 i^2) \bmod m
\end{equation}
For simplicity, we will use the standard form where $c_1 = 0$ and $c_2 = 1$, giving $h(k, i) = (h'(k) + i^2) \bmod m$.

\textbf{Handling Deletions:} We cannot simply mark a deleted slot as \texttt{NULL} (empty). If we did, a search for an element that previously collided and was placed after this slot would prematurely terminate when it encounters the \texttt{NULL}. To solve this, we introduce a special sentinel value called a \textbf{Tombstone} (e.g., \texttt{DELETED}). 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Search:} Skips over \texttt{DELETED} slots and continues probing.
    \item \textbf{Insert:} Treats \texttt{DELETED} slots as available space and can overwrite them.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Pseudocode}

Let $T$ be the hash table of size $m$. Each slot can be in one of three states: \texttt{NULL} (never occupied), \texttt{DELETED} (tombstone), or containing a valid \texttt{key}.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Hash-Search(T, k)}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Hash table $T$, key $k$ to find
\State \textbf{Output:} Index of $k$ if found, else \texttt{NIL}
\State $i \gets 0$
\While{$i < m$}
    \State $j \gets (h'(k) + i^2) \bmod m$
    \If{$T[j] == \texttt{NULL}$}
        \State \Return \texttt{NIL} \Comment{Hit an empty slot, key is not in table}
    \EndIf
    \If{$T[j] \neq \texttt{DELETED}$ \textbf{and} $T[j] == k$}
        \State \Return $j$ \Comment{Key found}
    \EndIf
    \State $i \gets i + 1$
\EndWhile
\State \Return \texttt{NIL} \Comment{Table exhausted}
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Hash-Insert(T, k)}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Hash table $T$, key $k$ to insert
\State \textbf{Output:} Index where $k$ is inserted, or error if table is full
\State $i \gets 0$
\While{$i < m$}
    \State $j \gets (h'(k) + i^2) \bmod m$
    \If{$T[j] == \texttt{NULL}$ \textbf{or} $T[j] == \texttt{DELETED}$}
        \State $T[j] \gets k$
        \State \Return $j$
    \EndIf
    \If{$T[j] == k$}
        \State \Return \texttt{ERROR: Key already exists}
    \EndIf
    \State $i \gets i + 1$
\EndWhile
\State \Return \texttt{ERROR: Hash table overflow}
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Hash-Delete(T, k)}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Hash table $T$, key $k$ to delete
\State \textbf{Output:} \texttt{TRUE} if successfully deleted, \texttt{FALSE} otherwise
\State $j \gets \text{Hash-Search}(T, k)$
\If{$j \neq \texttt{NIL}$}
    \State $T[j] \gets \texttt{DELETED}$ \Comment{Place tombstone}
    \State \Return \texttt{TRUE}
\EndIf
\State \Return \texttt{FALSE} \Comment{Key not found}
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. Explanatory Example}

Consider a hash table of size $m = 7$ and a primary hash function $h'(k) = k \bmod 7$. We trace the following operations:

\textbf{1. Insert(17):} $h'(17) = 3$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item $i=0$: Probe $(3 + 0^2) \bmod 7 = 3$. Slot 3 is \texttt{NULL}. Insert $17$ at index 3.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{2. Insert(24):} $h'(24) = 3$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item $i=0$: Probe $(3 + 0^2) \bmod 7 = 3$. Slot 3 is occupied (17). Collision!
    \item $i=1$: Probe $(3 + 1^2) \bmod 7 = 4$. Slot 4 is \texttt{NULL}. Insert $24$ at index 4.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{3. Insert(10):} $h'(10) = 3$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item $i=0$: Probe $(3 + 0^2) \bmod 7 = 3$. Slot 3 is occupied (17). Collision!
    \item $i=1$: Probe $(3 + 1^2) \bmod 7 = 4$. Slot 4 is occupied (24). Collision!
    \item $i=2$: Probe $(3 + 2^2) \bmod 7 = 7 \bmod 7 = 0$. Slot 0 is \texttt{NULL}. Insert $10$ at index 0.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{4. Delete(24):} 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \texttt{Hash-Search(24)} probes $i=0$ (Slot 3) and $i=1$ (Slot 4), finds $24$ at index 4.
    \item Slot 4 is overwritten with the \texttt{DELETED} tombstone.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{5. Find(10):} (Demonstrating why the tombstone is necessary)
\begin{itemize}
    \item $h'(10) = 3$. 
    \item $i=0$: Probe 3. Contains $17 \neq 10$.
    \item $i=1$: Probe 4. Contains \texttt{DELETED}. The algorithm \textit{does not stop} here.
    \item $i=2$: Probe 0. Contains $10$. Key found! (If slot 4 were \texttt{NULL}, the search would have falsely returned \texttt{NIL} at $i=1$).
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    cell/.style={draw, rectangle, minimum width=1.4cm, minimum height=0.9cm, fill=cC!10, font=\bfseries},
    emptycell/.style={draw, rectangle, minimum width=1.4cm, minimum height=0.9cm, fill=black!5, font=\itshape\color{gray}},
    delcell/.style={draw, rectangle, minimum width=1.4cm, minimum height=0.9cm, fill=red!10, font=\bfseries\small\color{red!70!black}},
    idx/.style={font=\small, text=gray}
]
    \node[cell] (C0) at (0, 0) {10};
    \node[emptycell] (C1) at (1.4, 0) {NULL};
    \node[emptycell] (C2) at (2.8, 0) {NULL};
    \node[cell] (C3) at (4.2, 0) {17};
    \node[delcell] (C4) at (5.6, 0) {DEL};
    \node[emptycell] (C5) at (7.0, 0) {NULL};
    \node[emptycell] (C6) at (8.4, 0) {NULL};

    \foreach \i in {0,...,6} {
        \node[idx] at (\i*1.4, 0.75) {\i};
    }
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\subsection*{4. Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} Under the assumption of uniform hashing, operations operate in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ average expected time. In the worst case (e.g., table approaches capacity, or high collision rates leading to long probe sequences), the time complexity degenerates to $\mathcal{O}(m)$.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(m)$, since all elements and tombstones are stored directly inside the array of size $m$, without external data structures.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Insert the keys $18, 41, 22, 44, 59, 32, 31, 73$ into a hash table of size 13 using double hashing with
\[ h_1(k) = k \bmod 13, \qquad h_2(k) = 8 - (k \bmod 8). \]
Draw the resulting hash table. \mk{13}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cC!4,colframe=cC,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cC]

\subsection*{1. Hash Functions and Setup}

We are inserting keys into a hash table of size $m = 13$ using double hashing.
The probe sequence is defined as:
\begin{equation}
    H(k, i) = (h_1(k) + i \cdot h_2(k)) \bmod m
\end{equation}
where:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $h_1(k) = k \bmod 13$ (Primary Hash Function)
    \item $h_2(k) = 8 - (k \bmod 8)$ (Secondary Hash Function/Step Size)
    \item $i = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ (Probe Number)
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{2. Step-by-Step Insertion Trace}

We process the keys in the order given: $\langle 18, 41, 22, 44, 59, 32, 31, 73 \rangle$.

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|l|c|}
\hline
\rowcolor{cC!15} \textbf{Key ($k$)} & \textbf{$h_1(k)$} & \textbf{$h_2(k)$} & \textbf{Probe Sequence: $H(k, i) = (h_1(k) + i \cdot h_2(k)) \bmod 13$} & \textbf{Final Slot} \\ \hline
\textbf{18} & $5$ & $6$ & $i=0 \implies 5 \bmod 13 = 5$ (Empty) & \textbf{5} \\ \hline
\textbf{41} & $2$ & $7$ & $i=0 \implies 2 \bmod 13 = 2$ (Empty) & \textbf{2} \\ \hline
\textbf{22} & $9$ & $2$ & $i=0 \implies 9 \bmod 13 = 9$ (Empty) & \textbf{9} \\ \hline
\textbf{44} & $5$ & $4$ & \makecell[l]{$i=0 \implies 5$ (Collision with 18) \\ $i=1 \implies (5 + 1 \cdot 4) \bmod 13 = 9$ (Collision with 22) \\ $i=2 \implies (5 + 2 \cdot 4) \bmod 13 = 13 \bmod 13 = 0$ (Empty)} & \textbf{0} \\ \hline
\textbf{59} & $7$ & $5$ & $i=0 \implies 7 \bmod 13 = 7$ (Empty) & \textbf{7} \\ \hline
\textbf{32} & $6$ & $8$ & $i=0 \implies 6 \bmod 13 = 6$ (Empty) & \textbf{6} \\ \hline
\textbf{31} & $5$ & $1$ & \makecell[l]{$i=0 \implies 5$ (Collision with 18) \\ $i=1 \implies (5 + 1 \cdot 1) \bmod 13 = 6$ (Collision with 32) \\ $i=2 \implies (5 + 2 \cdot 1) \bmod 13 = 7$ (Collision with 59) \\ $i=3 \implies (5 + 3 \cdot 1) \bmod 13 = 8$ (Empty)} & \textbf{8} \\ \hline
\textbf{73} & $8$ & $7$ & \makecell[l]{$i=0 \implies 8$ (Collision with 31) \\ $i=1 \implies (8 + 1 \cdot 7) \bmod 13 = 15 \bmod 13 = 2$ (Collision with 41) \\ $i=2 \implies (8 + 2 \cdot 7) \bmod 13 = 22 \bmod 13 = 9$ (Collision with 22) \\ $i=3 \implies (8 + 3 \cdot 7) \bmod 13 = 29 \bmod 13 = 3$ (Empty)} & \textbf{3} \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. Final Hash Table}

Below is the visualization of the resulting 13-slot hash table after all insertions are complete. Empty slots are denoted by a dash.

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    cell/.style={draw, rectangle, minimum width=1.05cm, minimum height=0.9cm, fill=cC!10, font=\Large\bfseries},
    emptycell/.style={draw, rectangle, minimum width=1.05cm, minimum height=0.9cm, fill=black!5, font=\Large\color{gray}},
    idx/.style={font=\small, text=gray}
]
    
    % Array cells
    \node[cell] (C0) at (0*1.1, 0) {44};
    \node[emptycell] (C1) at (1*1.1, 0) {--};
    \node[cell] (C2) at (2*1.1, 0) {41};
    \node[cell] (C3) at (3*1.1, 0) {73};
    \node[emptycell] (C4) at (4*1.1, 0) {--};
    \node[cell] (C5) at (5*1.1, 0) {18};
    \node[cell] (C6) at (6*1.1, 0) {32};
    \node[cell] (C7) at (7*1.1, 0) {59};
    \node[cell] (C8) at (8*1.1, 0) {31};
    \node[cell] (C9) at (9*1.1, 0) {22};
    \node[emptycell] (C10) at (10*1.1, 0) {--};
    \node[emptycell] (C11) at (11*1.1, 0) {--};
    \node[emptycell] (C12) at (12*1.1, 0) {--};
    
    % Indices
    \foreach \i in {0,...,12} {
        \node[idx] at (\i*1.1, 0.75) {\i};
    }
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\end{enumerate}

%% ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
%%  PART IV — NP-COMPLETENESS
%% ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
\secdivider{Part IV}{NP-Completeness}{cD}{cD}{P vs NP Classes, Reductions, NP-hard and NP-complete Problems}

%% ── S4: NP-Completeness ───────────────────────────────────────────────
\section{S4 --- NP-Completeness}
\label{sec:S4}
\markboth{S4 --- NP-Completeness}{}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cD}{P vs NP Classes}
\label{sub:s4-pvsnp}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item Prove that $P = NP \Rightarrow NP = \text{co-}NP$.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25, 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{1. Definitions and Key Properties}

To formally prove that $P = NP \implies NP = \text{co-}NP$, we must first establish the formal definitions of the complexity classes involved and a fundamental property of the class $P$.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Class $P$:} The set of decision problems (languages) that can be decided by a Deterministic Turing Machine (DTM) in polynomial time.
    \item \textbf{Class $NP$:} The set of decision problems that can be decided by a Non-deterministic Turing Machine (NTM) in polynomial time (or equivalently, whose positive instances have polynomial-time verifiable certificates).
    \item \textbf{Class $\text{co-}NP$:} The set of decision problems whose \textit{complement} is in $NP$. Formally, a language $L \in \text{co-}NP \iff \overline{L} \in NP$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Lemma: $P$ is closed under complementation ($P = \text{co-}P$)}
\begin{proof}[Proof of Lemma]
Let $L \in P$. By definition, there exists a DTM $M$ that decides $L$ in polynomial time $O(n^k)$. We can construct a new DTM $M'$ for the complement language $\overline{L}$. $M'$ simulates $M$ on the input $x$. If $M$ accepts, $M'$ rejects. If $M$ rejects, $M'$ accepts. Since $M$ halts in polynomial time, $M'$ also halts in polynomial time $O(n^k)$. Thus, $\overline{L} \in P$, which implies $P \subseteq \text{co-}P$. By symmetry, $\text{co-}P \subseteq P$, meaning $P = \text{co-}P$.
\end{proof}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Formal Proof}

\textbf{Assumption:} Let us assume that $P = NP$. 

We need to prove that $NP = \text{co-}NP$. To do this, we will prove subset inclusion in both directions: $NP \subseteq \text{co-}NP$ and $\text{co-}NP \subseteq NP$.

\subsubsection*{Part A: Prove $NP \subseteq \text{co-}NP$}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Let $L$ be an arbitrary language such that $L \in NP$.
    \item By our assumption $P = NP$, it follows that $L \in P$.
    \item By the Lemma ($P$ is closed under complement), if $L \in P$, then its complement $\overline{L} \in P$.
    \item Using our assumption $P = NP$ again, since $\overline{L} \in P$, it must be that $\overline{L} \in NP$.
    \item By the definition of $\text{co-}NP$, a language whose complement is in $NP$ belongs to $\text{co-}NP$. Therefore, since $\overline{L} \in NP$, we have $L \in \text{co-}NP$.
    \item Since this holds for any arbitrary $L \in NP$, we conclude that $NP \subseteq \text{co-}NP$.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Part B: Prove $\text{co-}NP \subseteq NP$}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Let $L$ be an arbitrary language such that $L \in \text{co-}NP$.
    \item By the definition of $\text{co-}NP$, its complement $\overline{L} \in NP$.
    \item By our assumption $P = NP$, it follows that $\overline{L} \in P$.
    \item By the Lemma ($P$ is closed under complement), if $\overline{L} \in P$, then the complement of $\overline{L}$ (which is $L$ itself) must also be in $P$. Thus, $L \in P$.
    \item Using our assumption $P = NP$ once more, since $L \in P$, we immediately get $L \in NP$.
    \item Since this holds for any arbitrary $L \in \text{co-}NP$, we conclude that $\text{co-}NP \subseteq NP$.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Conclusion:} 
Since we have shown both $NP \subseteq \text{co-}NP$ and $\text{co-}NP \subseteq NP$, it must be true that $NP = \text{co-}NP$. 
Therefore, $P = NP \implies NP = \text{co-}NP$. $\blacksquare$

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. Visualization}

The diagram below illustrates the structural collapse of complexity classes under the assumption that $P = NP$.

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
    % Left Diagram: Standard View (P != NP)
    \node[font=\bfseries] at (0, 3) {Standard View ($P \neq NP$)};
    
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!10] (-1.5, 0) ellipse (2cm and 2.5cm);
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!20] (1.5, 0) ellipse (2cm and 2.5cm);
    
    % Intersection P
    \begin{scope}
        \clip (-1.5, 0) ellipse (2cm and 2.5cm);
        \fill[cD!40] (1.5, 0) ellipse (2cm and 2.5cm);
    \end{scope}
    
    % Redraw outlines to stay on top
    \draw[thick] (-1.5, 0) ellipse (2cm and 2.5cm);
    \draw[thick] (1.5, 0) ellipse (2cm and 2.5cm);
    \draw[thick, dashed] (0, -0.5) ellipse (1.2cm and 1cm);
    
    \node[font=\Large\bfseries] at (-2.2, 1) {$NP$};
    \node[font=\Large\bfseries] at (2.2, 1) {$\text{co-}NP$};
    \node[font=\large\bfseries] at (0, -0.5) {$P$};
    \node[font=\footnotesize] at (0, 1) {$NP \cap \text{co-}NP$};
    
    % Arrow
    \draw[->, ultra thick, dashed, cD!80!black] (4, 0) -- (5.5, 0) node[midway, above] {$P = NP$};
    
    % Right Diagram: Collapsed View
    \node[font=\bfseries] at (8, 3) {Collapsed View ($P = NP$)};
    
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!40] (8, 0) circle (2.2cm);
    \node[font=\Large\bfseries, align=center] at (8, 0) {$P = NP = \text{co-}NP$};
    
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item Prove or disprove that $P = NP \cap \text{co-}NP$.
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{1. Status of the Proposition}

The proposition $P = NP \cap \text{co-}NP$ can currently \textbf{neither be proved nor disproved}. It stands as one of the major open problems in structural complexity theory. 

To evaluate the statement, we decompose the equality into two subset inclusions:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $P \subseteq NP \cap \text{co-}NP$: This is known to be \textbf{true} and can be formally proved.
    \item $NP \cap \text{co-}NP \subseteq P$: This is \textbf{unknown}, but widely conjectured by computer scientists to be \textbf{false}.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Proof of $P \subseteq NP \cap \text{co-}NP$}

To prove this subset inclusion, we must show that any problem in $P$ belongs to both $NP$ and $\text{co-}NP$.

\begin{proof}
Let $L$ be an arbitrary language such that $L \in P$. 

\textbf{Step 1: Show $P \subseteq NP$}
By definition, $L \in P$ implies there exists a deterministic Turing machine (DTM) $M$ that decides $L$ in polynomial time. A non-deterministic Turing machine (NTM) is a generalization of a DTM. Therefore, an NTM can simply ignore its non-deterministic capabilities and simulate $M$ exactly, deciding $L$ in polynomial time. Alternatively, using the verifier definition of $NP$, the verifier can simply solve the problem in polynomial time without needing to read any certificate. Thus, $L \in NP$.

\textbf{Step 2: Show $P \subseteq \text{co-}NP$}
Since $L \in P$, its complement $\overline{L}$ must also be in $P$. We construct a DTM $M'$ for $\overline{L}$ that simulates $M$ on input $x$ and simply reverses the output: if $M$ accepts, $M'$ rejects; if $M$ rejects, $M'$ accepts. Since $M$ runs in polynomial time, $M'$ also runs in polynomial time. Thus, $\overline{L} \in P$. 
From Step 1, we know $P \subseteq NP$, so $\overline{L} \in NP$. By the definition of $\text{co-}NP$, if the complement of a language is in $NP$, the language itself is in $\text{co-}NP$. Therefore, $L \in \text{co-}NP$.

Since $L \in NP$ and $L \in \text{co-}NP$, we have $L \in NP \cap \text{co-}NP$. Therefore, $P \subseteq NP \cap \text{co-}NP$.
\end{proof}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. The Unknown Direction: $NP \cap \text{co-}NP \subseteq P$}

For the equality $P = NP \cap \text{co-}NP$ to hold, every problem that has both a polynomial-time verifiable "yes" certificate (in $NP$) and a polynomial-time verifiable "no" certificate (in $\text{co-}NP$) must also be solvable from scratch in polynomial time (in $P$). 

Most complexity theorists conjecture that \textbf{$P \neq NP \cap \text{co-}NP$}. The evidence for this comes from several well-known problems that lie within $NP \cap \text{co-}NP$ but have resisted all attempts to find polynomial-time algorithms. 

\textbf{Candidate Problem in $(NP \cap \text{co-}NP) \setminus P$: Integer Factorization}
Consider the decision version of factoring: \textit{"Given an integer $N$ and an integer $k$, does $N$ have a non-trivial prime factor less than $k$?"}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Why it is in $NP$:} A "yes" certificate is simply the prime factorization of $N$. A verifier can multiply the factors to check they equal $N$, use a polynomial-time primality test (like AKS) to verify all factors are prime, and check if any factor is less than $k$.
    \item \textbf{Why it is in $\text{co-}NP$:} A "no" certificate is \textit{also} the prime factorization of $N$. The verifier performs the exact same polynomial-time checks (multiplication and primality), but accepts if \textit{none} of the factors are less than $k$.
\end{itemize}
Despite centuries of mathematical effort, no deterministic polynomial-time algorithm is known for factoring. If $P = NP \cap \text{co-}NP$ were true, Integer Factorization would necessarily be in $P$.

\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{4. Visualizing the Complexity Classes}

The Venn diagram below illustrates the standard conjecture regarding these complexity classes, showing $P$ strictly contained within the intersection, and candidate problems like Factoring occupying the annulus between $P$ and $NP \cap \text{co-}NP$.

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9]
    % Draw NP
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!15] (-2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
    \node[font=\Large\bfseries] at (-4, 1.5) {$NP$};

    % Draw co-NP
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!25, fill opacity=0.8] (2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
    \node[font=\Large\bfseries] at (4, 1.5) {$\text{co-}NP$};

    % Intersection region (NP intersect co-NP)
    \begin{scope}
        \clip (-2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
        \fill[cD!40] (2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
    \end{scope}
    
    % Redraw outlines to stay on top
    \draw[thick] (-2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
    \draw[thick] (2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
    
    \node[font=\bfseries, align=center] at (0, 1.3) {$NP \cap \text{co-}NP$};
    
    % Draw P
    \draw[thick, fill=white] (0, -0.8) ellipse (1.5cm and 1cm);
    \node[font=\Large\bfseries] at (0, -0.8) {$P$};
    
    % Example problems
    \node[font=\small\bfseries, align=center] at (-3, -0.5) {SAT \\ Clique \\ TSP};
    \node[font=\small\bfseries, align=center] at (3, -0.5) {UnSAT \\ Tautology};
    
    % Problems in intersection but outside P
    \node[font=\small\itshape, align=center] at (0, 0.4) {Factoring};
    
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item ``It is possible that $P \neq NP$, yet $NP = \text{co-}NP$.'' Do you agree with this statement? Justify your answer.
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{1. The Verdict}

\textbf{Yes, I agree with the statement.} It is logically and theoretically possible that $P \neq NP$ while simultaneously $NP = \text{co-}NP$. There is no known mathematical contradiction in this scenario, even though it contradicts the mainstream conjecture held by most complexity theorists.

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Theoretical Justification}

To understand why this is possible, we must examine the definitions and the logical dependencies (or lack thereof) between these classes:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{$P$:} The class of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
    \item \textbf{$NP$:} The class of decision problems for which a "YES" answer can be verified by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time, given a valid certificate.
    \item \textbf{$\text{co-}NP$:} The class of decision problems for which a "NO" answer can be verified by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{The Logical Independence}
We know definitively that $P \subseteq NP \cap \text{co-}NP$. We also know that if $P = NP$, it forces $NP = \text{co-}NP$ (because $P$ is closed under complementation). 

However, the inverse implication—that $P \neq NP \implies NP \neq \text{co-}NP$—\textbf{has never been proven}. The relationship between the ability to \textit{find} an answer (which separates $P$ from $NP$) and the symmetry of \textit{verifying} "YES" versus "NO" instances (which separates $NP$ from $\text{co-}NP$) are two distinct questions.

\subsubsection*{What would a world where $P \neq NP$ and $NP = \text{co-}NP$ look like?}
If this scenario were true, it would imply the following:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Verification is symmetrical:} For every problem in $NP$ (including $NP$-Complete problems like Boolean Satisfiability), if the answer is "NO", there exists a succinct, polynomial-time verifiable proof of that "NO" answer. For example, there would exist a short certificate proving that a given boolean formula is \textit{unsatisfiable}.
    \item \textbf{Solving is still hard:} Despite having succinct proofs for both "YES" and "NO" instances, no deterministic polynomial-time algorithm can \textit{construct} or find these answers from scratch. The computational effort to find the solution remains super-polynomial.
\end{enumerate}

This represents a universe where nondeterminism is strictly more powerful than determinism ($P \subsetneq NP$), but nondeterminism is closed under complementation ($NP = \text{co-}NP$).

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. Visualization of Complexity Worlds}

To clarify this, we can contrast the "Standard Conjecture" with the "Hypothetical Scenario" described in the prompt using Venn diagrams.

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.85]
    
    % --- LEFT DIAGRAM: Standard Belief ---
    \begin{scope}[shift={(0,0)}]
        \node[font=\bfseries, align=center] at (0, 3.5) {Standard Belief \\ ($P \neq NP$ and $NP \neq \text{co-}NP$)};
        
        % NP
        \draw[thick, fill=cD!15] (-1.5, 0) ellipse (2.5cm and 2cm);
        \node[font=\large\bfseries] at (-2.5, 1) {$NP$};
        
        % co-NP
        \draw[thick, fill=cD!25, fill opacity=0.8] (1.5, 0) ellipse (2.5cm and 2cm);
        \node[font=\large\bfseries] at (2.5, 1) {$\text{co-}NP$};
        
        % Intersection shading
        \begin{scope}
            \clip (-1.5, 0) ellipse (2.5cm and 2cm);
            \fill[cD!40] (1.5, 0) ellipse (2.5cm and 2cm);
        \end{scope}
        
        % Redraw borders
        \draw[thick] (-1.5, 0) ellipse (2.5cm and 2cm);
        \draw[thick] (1.5, 0) ellipse (2.5cm and 2cm);
        
        % P
        \draw[thick, fill=white] (0, -0.8) ellipse (1.2cm and 0.8cm);
        \node[font=\large\bfseries] at (0, -0.8) {$P$};
        
        \node[font=\small, align=center] at (0, 0.5) {$NP \cap \text{co-}NP$};
    \end{scope}

    % --- RIGHT DIAGRAM: Hypothetical Scenario ---
    \begin{scope}[shift={(8,0)}]
        \node[font=\bfseries, align=center] at (0, 3.5) {Hypothetical Scenario \\ ($P \neq NP$ and $NP = \text{co-}NP$)};
        
        % NP = co-NP
        \draw[thick, fill=cD!30] (0, 0) circle (2.2cm);
        \node[font=\large\bfseries, align=center] at (0, 1.2) {$NP = \text{co-}NP$};
        
        % P
        \draw[thick, fill=white] (0, -0.8) ellipse (1.2cm and 0.8cm);
        \node[font=\large\bfseries] at (0, -0.8) {$P$};
        
        % Labels indicating strict containment
        \node[font=\small\itshape, align=center] at (0, 0.2) {NPC / co-NPC \\ (Hard Problems)};
    \end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Conclusion:} The diagram on the right is mathematically consistent. Until a proof emerges showing that $NP = \text{co-}NP \implies P = NP$ (or vice versa), the statement remains a valid possibility in theoretical computer science.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Define the classes of problems P, NP, and NP-complete. Draw a Venn diagram showing the widely believed relationship among these classes.
\mk{9} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{1. Formal Definitions of Complexity Classes}

In computational complexity theory, problems are categorized based on the resources (usually time) required to solve them. We focus on \textbf{decision problems} (problems with a ``YES'' or ``NO'' answer).

\subsubsection*{The Class $P$}
The class $\boldsymbol{P}$ (Polynomial time) consists of all decision problems that can be \textbf{solved} efficiently. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Formal Definition:} A language $L$ is in $P$ if there exists a Deterministic Turing Machine (DTM) $M$ and a polynomial $p(n)$ such that for any input $x$ of length $n$, $M$ decides whether $x \in L$ in at most $p(n)$ steps.
    \item \textbf{Intuition:} These are the ``tractable'' problems, such as sorting, shortest path, and matrix multiplication.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{The Class $NP$}
The class $\boldsymbol{NP}$ (Nondeterministic Polynomial time) consists of all decision problems for which a ``YES'' answer can be \textbf{verified} efficiently, even if finding the answer from scratch might be difficult.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Formal Definition (Verifier-based):} A language $L$ is in $NP$ if there exists a polynomial-time deterministic verifier $V$ and a polynomial $p(n)$ such that for every $x \in L$, there exists a certificate (proof) $y$ with $|y| \le p(|x|)$ such that $V(x, y)$ accepts. If $x \notin L$, no such certificate exists.
    \item \textbf{Alternative Definition (Machine-based):} $L \in NP$ if it can be solved by a \textit{Non-deterministic} Turing Machine (NTM) in polynomial time.
    \item \textbf{Intuition:} $NP$ includes all problems in $P$ (since we can just ignore the certificate and solve the problem), plus many potentially harder problems like the Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) or Boolean Satisfiability (SAT).
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{The Class $NP$-complete}
The class of $\boldsymbol{NP}$\textbf{-complete} (NPC) problems represents the computationally ``hardest'' problems within $NP$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Formal Definition:} A language $L$ is $NP$-complete if it satisfies two conditions:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{Membership:} $L \in NP$.
        \item \textbf{Hardness:} Every problem $L' \in NP$ is polynomial-time reducible to $L$ (denoted $L' \leq_p L$). This means there exists a polynomial-time computable function $f$ such that $x \in L' \iff f(x) \in L$.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Intuition:} If anyone discovers a polynomial-time algorithm for \textit{any} single $NP$-complete problem, it would imply that every problem in $NP$ can be solved in polynomial time (thus proving $P = NP$).
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Visualizing the Believed Relationship (Assuming $P \neq NP$)}

The overwhelming consensus among computer scientists is that $P \neq NP$. Under this assumption, $P$ is a strict subset of $NP$, $NP$-complete problems exist strictly outside of $P$, and there exist intermediate problems (like potentially Integer Factorization) that are in $NP$, but are neither in $P$ nor $NP$-complete (Ladner's Theorem).

\vspace{2em}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1]

    % Define the overall NP set
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!15] (0, 0) ellipse (4.5cm and 3cm);
    \node[font=\Large\bfseries] at (-3.5, 2) {$NP$};

    % Define the P set
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!30] (-1.5, -1) ellipse (1.5cm and 1cm);
    \node[font=\large\bfseries] at (-1.5, -1) {$P$};
    \node[font=\small\itshape, align=center] at (-1.5, -1.5) {(e.g., Shortest Path)};

    % Define the NP-complete set
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!45] (1.5, 1) ellipse (1.8cm and 1.2cm);
    \node[font=\large\bfseries, align=center] at (1.5, 1.2) {$NP$-complete};
    \node[font=\small\itshape, align=center] at (1.5, 0.6) {(e.g., SAT, TSP)};

    % Define the NP-intermediate region
    \node[font=\large\bfseries, align=center] at (0, -0.2) {$NPI$};
    \node[font=\small\itshape, align=center] at (0, -0.6) {($NP$-Intermediate)};
    \node[font=\small\itshape, align=center] at (0, -1.0) {(e.g., Factoring?)};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\textbf{Note:} If the unlikely scenario that $P = NP$ were true, the Venn diagram would collapse entirely into a single circle where $P = NP = NP\text{-complete}$ (except for trivial languages $\emptyset$ and $\Sigma^*$).

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item Differentiate among NP, NP-hard, and NP-complete classes of problems.
\mk{6} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{1. Formal Definitions}

To differentiate among the classes $NP$, $NP$-hard, and $NP$-complete, we must examine their formal definitions based on polynomial-time verifiability and polynomial-time reducibility.

\subsubsection*{The Class $\boldsymbol{NP}$ (Nondeterministic Polynomial Time)}
The class $NP$ contains all \textbf{decision problems} (problems with a ``YES'' or ``NO'' answer) for which a ``YES'' answer can be efficiently verified.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Definition:} A language $L$ is in $NP$ if there exists a deterministic polynomial-time verifier $V$ such that for every string $x \in L$, there exists a polynomial-size certificate (proof) $y$ where $V(x, y)$ accepts. 
    \item \textbf{Intuition:} If someone hands you a proposed solution to a problem, you can check whether it is correct in polynomial time. 
    \item \textbf{Examples:} Shortest Path (is there a path shorter than $k$?), Boolean Satisfiability (SAT), Integer Factorization. Note that $NP$ includes the class $P$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{The Class $\boldsymbol{NP}$-hard}
The class $NP$-hard represents problems that are \textbf{at least as hard} as the hardest problems in $NP$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Definition:} A problem $H$ is $NP$-hard if every problem $L \in NP$ can be reduced to $H$ in polynomial time ($L \leq_p H$). 
    \item \textbf{Characteristics:} Crucially, $NP$-hard problems \textit{do not have to be in $NP$}. They do not even have to be decision problems (they can be optimization problems), and they do not have to be decidable.
    \item \textbf{Examples:} The Halting Problem (undecidable but $NP$-hard), the Traveling Salesperson \textit{Optimization} Problem (finding the exact shortest route, rather than just asking if a route shorter than $k$ exists).
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{The Class $\boldsymbol{NP}$-complete (NPC)}
The class $NP$-complete represents the computationally \textbf{hardest problems that are still within $\boldsymbol{NP}$}.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Definition:} A problem $C$ is $NP$-complete if it satisfies two strict conditions:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $C \in NP$ (It is verifiable in polynomial time).
        \item $C$ is $NP$-hard (Every problem in $NP$ reduces to it in polynomial time).
    \end{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Intuition:} $NP$-complete problems form the critical intersection of $NP$ and $NP$-hard. A polynomial-time algorithm for \textit{any} $NP$-complete problem would immediately imply $P = NP$.
    \item \textbf{Examples:} 3-SAT, Vertex Cover, Traveling Salesperson \textit{Decision} Problem.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Summary of Differences}

The following table highlights the critical distinctions between the three classes:

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5}
\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|l|}
\hline
\rowcolor{cD!15} \textbf{Complexity Class} & \textbf{Is it in $\boldsymbol{NP}$?} & \textbf{Is every $\boldsymbol{NP}$ problem reducible to it?} & \textbf{Problem Types Allowed} \\ \hline
$\boldsymbol{NP}$ & Yes & No (generally) & Decision only \\ \hline
$\boldsymbol{NP}$-hard & Not necessarily & Yes & Decision, Optimization, Undecidable \\ \hline
$\boldsymbol{NP}$-complete & Yes & Yes & Decision only \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. Visual Representation}

Assuming $P \neq NP$, the relationship among these classes is best visualized with the following Euler diagram. Notice how $NP$-complete is strictly the intersection of $NP$ and $NP$-hard, while $NP$-hard extends infinitely upward into harder complexity classes (like EXP or Undecidable).

\vspace{1.5em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9]

    % Define the universe (All Problems)
    \draw[thick, rounded corners=15pt, fill=black!2] (-6, -3) rectangle (6, 5);
    \node[font=\bfseries, text=gray] at (0, 4.5) {Space of All Computational Problems};

    % NP Set (Left oval)
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!15] (-2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
    \node[font=\Large\bfseries] at (-4, 1.5) {$NP$};

    % NP-Hard Set (Right oval)
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!25, fill opacity=0.8] (2, 0.5) ellipse (3.5cm and 3cm);
    \node[font=\Large\bfseries, align=center] at (3.5, 2.5) {$NP$-hard};

    % NP-Complete (Intersection)
    \begin{scope}
        \clip (-2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
        \fill[cD!45] (2, 0.5) ellipse (3.5cm and 3cm);
    \end{scope}
    
    % Redraw borders for clarity
    \draw[thick] (-2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
    \draw[thick] (2, 0.5) ellipse (3.5cm and 3cm);

    % Intersection Label
    \node[font=\large\bfseries, align=center] at (0, 1) {$NP$-complete};
    \node[font=\small\itshape, align=center] at (0, 0.3) {SAT, \\ TSP (Decision)};

    % P Set (Inside NP, outside intersection)
    \draw[thick, fill=white] (-3, -1) ellipse (1.2cm and 0.8cm);
    \node[font=\large\bfseries] at (-3, -1) {$P$};
    \node[font=\scriptsize\itshape, align=center] at (-3, -1.4) {Sorting};

    % NP (but not NPC or P)
    \node[font=\small\itshape, align=center] at (-1.5, -1) {Factoring};

    % NP-Hard (but not NP)
    \node[font=\small\itshape, align=center] at (3.5, 0) {Halting Problem, \\ TSP (Optimization)};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item Using an example, show that if a problem is in NP, the complement of that problem is in co-NP. Draw the general consensus regarding P, NP and co-NP in a Venn diagram.
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{1. Theoretical Foundation: NP and co-NP}

To show that the complement of a problem in $NP$ is in $\text{co-}NP$, we must first look at the formal definition of these complexity classes.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{The Class $\boldsymbol{NP}$:} A decision problem (language) $L$ is in $NP$ if there exists a polynomial-time verifier $V$ such that for every ``YES'' instance $x \in L$, there is a polynomial-sized certificate $c$ that $V$ can use to verify the ``YES'' answer in polynomial time.
    \item \textbf{The Complement of a Problem:} For a decision problem $L$ defined over an alphabet $\Sigma$, its complement is denoted as $\overline{L} = \Sigma^* \setminus L$. In practical terms, this reverses the ``YES'' and ``NO'' answers of the problem.
    \item \textbf{The Class $\boldsymbol{\text{co-}NP}$:} By formal definition, the class $\text{co-}NP$ is exactly the set of all languages whose complements are in $NP$. Mathematically, $\text{co-}NP = \{ L \mid \overline{L} \in NP \}$.
\end{itemize}

Therefore, the statement \textit{``if a problem is in $NP$, its complement is in $\text{co-}NP$''} is true by the very definition of $\text{co-}NP$. If $L \in NP$, then its complement $\overline{L}$ trivially satisfies the condition that its complement (which is $L$ itself) is in $NP$. Thus, $\overline{L} \in \text{co-}NP$.

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Illustrative Example}

To understand this intuitively, let us look at the \textbf{Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT)} and its complement, \textbf{UNSAT}.

\subsubsection*{Problem $\boldsymbol{L}$: SAT (Is the formula satisfiable?)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Question:} Given a boolean formula $\phi$, does there exist an assignment of truth values to its variables that makes $\phi$ evaluate to \texttt{TRUE}?
    \item \textbf{Why SAT $\in \boldsymbol{NP}$:} If the answer is ``YES'', a prover can provide the satisfying truth assignment as the certificate. A verifier can easily plug these values into $\phi$ and evaluate it in polynomial time to check if it equals \texttt{TRUE}.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Problem $\boldsymbol{\overline{L}}$: UNSAT (Is the formula unsatisfiable?)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Question:} Given a boolean formula $\phi$, is it true that \textit{no} assignment of truth values makes $\phi$ evaluate to \texttt{TRUE}? (i.e., does every assignment evaluate to \texttt{FALSE}?)
    \item \textbf{Why UNSAT $\in \boldsymbol{\text{co-}NP}$:} By definition, since SAT $\in NP$ and UNSAT is the complement of SAT, UNSAT $\in \text{co-}NP$. 
    \item \textbf{The Asymmetry of Verification:} Notice why UNSAT is not obviously in $NP$. If the answer is ``YES'' (the formula is unsatisfiable), there is no obvious short certificate to prove it. A verifier would seemingly have to check all $2^n$ possible truth assignments to ensure none of them work, taking exponential time. However, a ``NO'' answer to UNSAT (meaning the formula \textit{is} satisfiable) can be easily verified by providing the satisfying assignment. This is the hallmark of a $\text{co-}NP$ problem: \textit{NO instances have succinct, easily verifiable proofs}.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. The Consensus Venn Diagram}

The general consensus in theoretical computer science is that $P \neq NP$, and furthermore, that $NP \neq \text{co-}NP$. 

Under this belief, $P$ is a proper subset of both $NP$ and $\text{co-}NP$ (since deterministic polynomial time computation is perfectly symmetric with respect to YES/NO answers). There also exist problems in the intersection $NP \cap \text{co-}NP$ that are believed not to be in $P$ (such as Integer Factorization).

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.95]

    % Draw NP (Left Oval)
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!15] (-2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
    \node[font=\Large\bfseries] at (-4, 1.5) {$NP$};

    % Draw co-NP (Right Oval)
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!25, fill opacity=0.8] (2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
    \node[font=\Large\bfseries] at (4, 1.5) {$\text{co-}NP$};

    % Intersection region (NP intersect co-NP)
    \begin{scope}
        \clip (-2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
        \fill[cD!40] (2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
    \end{scope}
    
    % Redraw outlines to stay on top
    \draw[thick] (-2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
    \draw[thick] (2, 0) ellipse (3.5cm and 2.5cm);
    
    % Intersection Label
    \node[font=\bfseries, align=center] at (0, 1.4) {$NP \cap \text{co-}NP$};
    
    % Draw P (Inside the intersection)
    \draw[thick, fill=white] (0, -0.8) ellipse (1.5cm and 1cm);
    \node[font=\Large\bfseries] at (0, -0.6) {$P$};
    \node[font=\scriptsize\itshape, align=center] at (0, -1.2) {Shortest Path, \\ Sorting};
    
    % Example problems in NP \ P
    \node[font=\small\bfseries, align=center] at (-3.2, -0.5) {SAT \\ Clique \\ TSP (Decision)};
    
    % Example problems in co-NP \ P
    \node[font=\small\bfseries, align=center] at (3.2, -0.5) {UNSAT \\ No-Clique \\ Tautology};
    
    % Example problems in intersection but outside P
    \node[font=\small\itshape, align=center] at (0, 0.4) {Integer \\ Factoring};
    
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2022-23
\item Suppose $X \in \text{NP-complete}$. Prove that $X \in P$ if and only if $P = NP$.
\mk{8} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{1. Problem Statement and Definitions}
We are given a problem $X$ such that $X \in \text{NP-complete}$. We must prove a biconditional statement:
\[ X \in P \iff P = NP \]
By the definition of NP-completeness, a problem $X$ is NP-complete if and only if:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $X \in NP$ (Membership)
    \item For every language $L \in NP$, $L \leq_p X$ (NP-hardness), where $\leq_p$ denotes a polynomial-time Karp reduction.
\end{enumerate}

To mathematically establish the biconditional ($\iff$), we must prove both directions: the forward implication ($\Rightarrow$) and the reverse implication ($\Leftarrow$).

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Proof of Forward Implication ($\Rightarrow$)}
\textbf{Assume:} $X \in P$.\\
\textbf{To Show:} $P = NP$.

\begin{proof}
It is a well-known, fundamental property of complexity classes that $P \subseteq NP$. To show the equality $P = NP$, it strictly suffices to prove the reverse inclusion: $NP \subseteq P$. 

Let $L$ be an arbitrary language in $NP$. Since $X$ is NP-complete, we know by definition that $L$ is polynomial-time reducible to $X$, denoted as $L \leq_p X$. This means there exists a deterministic polynomial-time computable function $f$ such that for any input string $w$:
\[ w \in L \iff f(w) \in X \]
Let the time taken to compute the reduction function $f(w)$ be bounded by a polynomial $p(|w|)$.

By our assumption, $X \in P$. This means there exists a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm $A_X$ that decides $X$. Let the time taken by $A_X$ to process an input of size $m$ be bounded by a polynomial $q(m)$.

Using these pieces, we can construct a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm $A_L$ to decide $L$ as follows:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Given an input $w$, compute the reduction $f(w)$. This takes at most $O(p(|w|))$ time.
    \item The size of the reduced string, $|f(w)|$, must be at most $O(p(|w|))$ because a Deterministic Turing Machine can write at most one symbol per computational step.
    \item Run the decider algorithm $A_X$ on the transformed input $f(w)$. The time taken is $O(q(|f(w)|))$, which is bounded by $O(q(p(|w|)))$.
    \item Return the exact result yielded by $A_X$.
\end{enumerate}
The total running time of $A_L$ is the sum of the time for the reduction and the time for the solver: $O(p(|w|) + q(p(|w|)))$. Since the composition of two polynomials $q(p(n))$ is itself a polynomial, algorithm $A_L$ runs in deterministic polynomial time. Thus, $L \in P$. 

Because this holds true for \textit{any} arbitrary $L \in NP$, we conclude $NP \subseteq P$. Combined with $P \subseteq NP$, we rigorously establish $P = NP$.
\end{proof}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. Proof of Reverse Implication ($\Leftarrow$)}
\textbf{Assume:} $P = NP$.\\
\textbf{To Show:} $X \in P$.

\begin{proof}
By the premise of the problem, we are given that $X \in \text{NP-complete}$. By the first criterion of the definition of NP-completeness, this explicitly guarantees that $X \in NP$. 

Since we have assumed that $P = NP$, the two complexity classes are entirely identical. This means every single problem contained in $NP$ is fundamentally also contained in $P$. 

Therefore, since $X \in NP$ and $NP = P$, it immediately follows by direct substitution that $X \in P$.
\end{proof}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{4. Visualizing the Polynomial-Time Reduction (Forward Implication)}

The diagram below conceptually illustrates the mechanics of the forward proof. It demonstrates how a polynomial-time reduction allows us to build a polynomial-time decider for \textit{any} problem $L \in NP$ by leveraging the assumed polynomial-time algorithm for the NP-complete problem $X$.

\vspace{1.5em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    box/.style={draw, thick, rounded corners, fill=cD!10, minimum width=3cm, minimum height=1.5cm, align=center},
    arrow/.style={->, thick, >=stealth},
    % Added a standard text node style to fix the LR mode error globally
    textnode/.style={align=center}
]

    % Nodes
    % Applied textnode style to allow multiline text (\\)
    \node[textnode] (input) at (0,0) {\textbf{Input} $w$ \\ (Size $n$)};
    
    \node[box] (reduction) at (4,0) {\textbf{Reduction} $f(w)$ \\ Computable in \\ $O(p(n))$ time};
    
    \node[textnode] (intermediate) at (8.5,0) {\textbf{Instance} $f(w)$ \\ (Size $m \le p(n)$)};
    
    \node[box] (solver) at (13,0) {\textbf{Algorithm} for $X$ \\ Runs in \\ $O(q(m))$ time};
    
    \node[textnode] (output) at (16.5,0) {\textbf{YES/NO}};

    % Arrows
    \draw[arrow] (input) -- (reduction);
    \draw[arrow] (reduction) -- (intermediate);
    \draw[arrow] (intermediate) -- (solver);
    \draw[arrow] (solver) -- (output);
    
    % Braces/Annotations
    % Fixed: 'decorate' must come before 'decoration={...}'
    \draw[thick, decorate, decoration={brace, mirror, raise=0.5cm}] (2,-0.5) -- (15,-0.5) 
        node[pos=0.5, below=0.8cm, font=\bfseries] {Constructed Algorithm for $L$: Total Time = $O(p(n) + q(p(n)))$ = Polynomial};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\end{tcolorbox}

\item How do we cope with hard problems? Discuss. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsection*{1. The Core Challenge of Hard Problems}

When a problem is proven to be $\mathcal{NP}$-hard (or $\mathcal{NP}$-complete), it implies that it is highly unlikely to admit a polynomial-time algorithm that optimally solves all possible instances (assuming $\mathcal{P} \neq \mathcal{NP}$). However, in real-world applications (e.g., routing networks, scheduling, packing), we cannot simply refuse to solve the problem. 

To cope with computational hardness, we must relax at least one of the three properties of an ideal algorithmic solution:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Speed:} Solving the problem in polynomial time.
    \item \textbf{Optimality:} Finding the exact, mathematically best solution.
    \item \textbf{Generality:} Solving the problem for any arbitrary input instance.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Strategies for Coping with Hardness}

Depending on which property we are willing to sacrifice, there are four primary strategies to handle hard problems in practice.

\subsubsection*{Strategy A: Relax Optimality (Approximation Algorithms)}
If we require a fast solution for all inputs but can accept a slightly sub-optimal answer, we use approximation algorithms.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Mechanism:} These are polynomial-time algorithms that come with a mathematically proven \textit{worst-case guarantee} on the quality of the solution. 
    \item \textbf{Approximation Ratio ($\rho$):} An algorithm has an approximation ratio $\rho \ge 1$ if for any input size $n$, the cost of the approximate solution $C$ and the optimal cost $C^*$ satisfy $\max\left(\frac{C}{C^*}, \frac{C^*}{C}\right) \le \rho$.
    \item \textbf{Example:} The Vertex Cover problem is $\mathcal{NP}$-hard, but a simple greedy strategy (picking both endpoints of arbitrary edges) guarantees a solution that is at most twice the size of the optimal cover ($\rho = 2$).
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Strategy B: Relax Optimality and Guarantees (Heuristics \& Metaheuristics)}
When we need fast solutions but cannot find good theoretical approximation ratios, we rely on heuristics.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Mechanism:} Rule-of-thumb algorithms that tend to find "good enough" solutions quickly in practice, though they offer no formal guarantees on worst-case optimality or running time.
    \item \textbf{Metaheuristics:} Higher-level frameworks that guide the search process to avoid local optima, such as \textbf{Simulated Annealing}, \textbf{Genetic Algorithms}, or \textbf{Tabu Search}.
    \item \textbf{Example:} Using the 2-Opt heuristic for the Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) works exceptionally well in practice, even though it can theoretically perform poorly on adversarial inputs.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Strategy C: Relax Generality (Special Cases \& Restrictions)}
If we require both optimal solutions and polynomial time, we must restrict the inputs to special cases that exploit underlying structural properties.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Mechanism:} We design algorithms tailored to specific data structures (e.g., trees, planar graphs, or bipartite graphs) where the problem is no longer $\mathcal{NP}$-hard.
    \item \textbf{Example:} The Maximum Independent Set problem is $\mathcal{NP}$-hard on general graphs. However, if we restrict the input to \textit{trees}, the problem can be solved optimally in $O(V)$ time using Dynamic Programming.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Strategy D: Relax Speed (Exact Exponential \& Parameterized Algorithms)}
If we absolutely must have the exact optimal solution for general instances, we accept that the algorithm will take exponential time in the worst case, but we try to minimize the practical blow-up.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Intelligent Search (Branch \& Bound):} Systematically enumerating candidate solutions while using upper/lower bounds to dynamically prune large portions of the search space that cannot contain the optimal solution.
    \item \textbf{Fixed-Parameter Tractability (FPT):} Isolating the exponential complexity to a small parameter $k$ rather than the overall input size $n$. An algorithm is FPT if it runs in $O(f(k) \cdot \text{poly}(n))$ time. 
    \item \textbf{Example:} Finding a Vertex Cover of size $k$ can be solved exactly in $O(2^k \cdot n)$ time. If $k$ is small, this is highly efficient despite being $\mathcal{NP}$-hard in general.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. The "Pick Two" Trade-off Visualization}

The dilemma of coping with $\mathcal{NP}$-hard problems is traditionally visualized as an algorithmic triangle, illustrating that you can typically achieve at most two of the three ideal properties simultaneously.

\vspace{1.5em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.2, every node/.style={transform shape}]
    
    % Coordinates for the triangle
    \coordinate (Top) at (0, 3);
    \coordinate (Left) at (-3.5, -2);
    \coordinate (Right) at (3.5, -2);
    
    % Draw the triangle
    \draw[ultra thick, fill=cE!10, draw=cE!80!black] (Top) -- (Left) -- (Right) -- cycle;
    
    % Vertex Labels
    \node[font=\bfseries, align=center, fill=white, inner sep=4pt] at (Top) {Polynomial Time\\(Fast Computation)};
    \node[font=\bfseries, align=center, fill=white, inner sep=4pt] at (Left) {Optimal Solution\\(Exact Accuracy)};
    \node[font=\bfseries, align=center, fill=white, inner sep=4pt] at (Right) {General Instances\\(Any Valid Input)};
    
    % Edge Labels (The Trade-offs)
    \node[font=\small\itshape, align=center, rotate=55, text=cE!80!black] at (-1.9, 0.7) {Special Cases\\(e.g., Trees, Planar)};
    \node[font=\small\itshape, align=center, rotate=-55, text=cE!80!black] at (1.9, 0.7) {Approximations \&\\Heuristics};
    \node[font=\small\itshape, align=center, text=cE!80!black] at (0, -2.4) {Branch \& Bound, FPT algorithms\\(Exponential Time)};
    
    % Center Text
    \node[font=\large\bfseries, align=center, text=black] at (0, -0.2) {Handling\\ $\boldsymbol{\mathcal{NP}}$-Hardness:\\[0.5em] \textcolor{red!70!black}{Choose Any Two!}};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Suppose $X \in \text{NP-complete}$. Prove that $X \in P$ if and only if $P = NP$. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2016--17}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{1. Problem Statement and Formal Definitions}

We are given a problem $X$ such that $X \in \text{NP-complete}$. We need to prove the biconditional statement:
\[ X \in P \iff P = NP \]

To do this, we must recall the formal definition of NP-completeness. A decision problem $X$ is NP-complete if it satisfies two conditions:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Membership in NP:} $X \in NP$.
    \item \textbf{NP-Hardness:} For every language $L \in NP$, $L$ is polynomial-time reducible to $X$ (denoted as $L \leq_p X$).
\end{enumerate}

To prove a biconditional statement ($A \iff B$), we must prove both directions: the forward implication ($\Rightarrow$) and the reverse implication ($\Leftarrow$).

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Proof of the Forward Implication ($\Rightarrow$)}

\textbf{Assume:} $X \in P$. \\
\textbf{To Prove:} $P = NP$.

\begin{proof}
It is a well-established fact in complexity theory that $P \subseteq NP$, because any problem that can be solved in deterministic polynomial time can easily be verified in polynomial time. Therefore, to prove $P = NP$, it suffices to show that $NP \subseteq P$.

Let $L$ be an arbitrary language such that $L \in NP$. We want to show that $L \in P$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item Since $X$ is NP-complete, by definition, $L \leq_p X$.
    \item This means there exists a deterministic polynomial-time computable reduction function $f$ such that for any input string $w$: 
    \[ w \in L \iff f(w) \in X \]
    Let the time complexity to compute $f(w)$ be bounded by a polynomial $p(|w|)$.
    \item Since we assumed $X \in P$, there exists a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm $A_X$ that decides $X$. Let the time complexity of $A_X$ on an input of size $m$ be bounded by a polynomial $q(m)$.
\end{itemize}

We can construct a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm $A_L$ to decide $L$:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Given an input $w$, use the reduction function to compute $f(w)$. This takes $O(p(|w|))$ time.
    \item The size of the transformed input, $|f(w)|$, can be at most $O(p(|w|))$ because a deterministic Turing machine can write at most one symbol per step.
    \item Feed $f(w)$ into algorithm $A_X$. The time taken by $A_X$ is bounded by $O(q(|f(w)|))$, which is at most $O(q(p(|w|)))$.
    \item Return the answer produced by $A_X$.
\end{enumerate}

The total running time of $A_L$ is bounded by $O(p(|w|) + q(p(|w|)))$. Since the composition of two polynomials $q(p(n))$ is also a polynomial, algorithm $A_L$ runs in deterministic polynomial time. Thus, $L \in P$.

Since $L$ was an arbitrary problem in $NP$, we have shown that every problem in $NP$ is also in $P$, meaning $NP \subseteq P$. Combined with $P \subseteq NP$, we conclude $P = NP$.
\end{proof}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. Proof of the Reverse Implication ($\Leftarrow$)}

\textbf{Assume:} $P = NP$. \\
\textbf{To Prove:} $X \in P$.

\begin{proof}
By the premise of the problem, we are given that $X$ is NP-complete. 
According to the first condition of the definition of NP-completeness, we know that $X \in NP$.

Since our assumption states that $P = NP$, the two complexity classes are identical. Every problem that belongs to $NP$ inherently belongs to $P$. 

Therefore, by direct substitution since $X \in NP$ and $NP = P$, it trivially follows that $X \in P$.
\end{proof}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{4. Visualizing the Forward Proof (Reduction Mechanics)}

The following diagram illustrates how the polynomial-time reduction works to solve an arbitrary $NP$ problem using the polynomial-time algorithm for the NP-complete problem $X$.

\vspace{1.5em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    box/.style={draw, thick, rounded corners, fill=cD!10, minimum width=3cm, minimum height=1.5cm, align=center},
    arrow/.style={->, thick, >=stealth},
    % Added textnode style to handle multi-line labels safely
    textnode/.style={align=center}
]

    % Nodes
    \node[textnode] (input) at (0,0) {\textbf{Input} $w$ \\ (Size $n$)};
    
    \node[box] (reduction) at (4,0) {\textbf{Reduction} $f(w)$ \\ Computable in \\ $O(p(n))$ time};
    
    \node[textnode] (intermediate) at (8.5,0) {\textbf{Instance} $f(w)$ \\ (Size $m \le p(n)$)};
    
    \node[box] (solver) at (13,0) {\textbf{Algorithm} $A_X$ \\ Runs in \\ $O(q(m))$ time};
    
    \node[textnode] (output) at (16.5,0) {\textbf{YES/NO}};

    % Arrows
    \draw[arrow] (input) -- (reduction) node[midway, above] {$L \leq_p X$};
    \draw[arrow] (reduction) -- (intermediate);
    \draw[arrow] (intermediate) -- (solver);
    \draw[arrow] (solver) -- (output);
    
    % Braces/Annotations
    % Fixed: placed 'decorate' before its 'decoration={...}' options
    \draw[thick, decorate, decoration={brace, mirror, raise=0.5cm}] (1.5,-0.5) -- (14.5,-0.5) 
        node[pos=0.5, below=0.8cm, font=\bfseries] {New Algorithm $A_L$: Total Time = $O(p(n) + q(p(n)))$ $\in$ Polynomial Time};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\end{tcolorbox}

\item Prove that $\text{NP} \neq \text{co-NP} \Rightarrow P \neq NP$. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2016--17}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{1. Proof Strategy: Proof by Contraposition}

We are asked to prove the logical implication:
\[ \text{NP} \neq \text{co-NP} \implies P \neq NP \]

In formal logic, an implication $A \implies B$ is logically equivalent to its \textbf{contrapositive}, $\neg B \implies \neg A$. Therefore, it is strictly mathematically equivalent to prove the following statement:
\[ P = NP \implies \text{NP} = \text{co-NP} \]

By proving this contrapositive statement, we will inherently prove the original claim.

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Foundational Properties}

Before proceeding with the proof, we must establish a fundamental property of the complexity class $P$: \textbf{Closure under complementation}.

\begin{itemize}
    \item Let $L$ be a language in $P$. By definition, there exists a deterministic polynomial-time Turing machine $M$ that decides $L$.
    \item To decide the complement language, $\overline{L}$, we can construct a new Turing machine $M'$ that simulates $M$ exactly, but simply \textit{inverts} the final output (accepts if $M$ rejects, and rejects if $M$ accepts).
    \item Since inverting a boolean output takes $O(1)$ time, $M'$ also runs in deterministic polynomial time.
    \item Therefore, if $L \in P$, then $\overline{L} \in P$. This means the class $P$ is exactly equal to the class $\text{co-}P$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{3. Formal Proof of the Contrapositive}

\textbf{Assume:} $P = NP$. \\
\textbf{To Show:} $\text{NP} = \text{co-NP}$.

\begin{proof}
To prove set equality ($\text{NP} = \text{co-NP}$), we must show that any language in $\text{NP}$ is also in $\text{co-NP}$, and vice versa. 

\textbf{Part 1: $\text{NP} \subseteq \text{co-NP}$}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Let $L$ be an arbitrary language such that $L \in \text{NP}$.
    \item Because we assumed $P = NP$, it follows that $L \in P$.
    \item As established, $P$ is closed under complementation. Thus, its complement language $\overline{L} \in P$.
    \item Since $P = NP$, we can substitute $P$ with $NP$, meaning $\overline{L} \in \text{NP}$.
    \item By the formal definition of $\text{co-NP}$, a language $L$ is in $\text{co-NP}$ if and only if its complement $\overline{L}$ is in $\text{NP}$. Since we just showed $\overline{L} \in \text{NP}$, we conclude that $L \in \text{co-NP}$.
    \item Since $L$ was an arbitrary language in $\text{NP}$, we have shown that $\text{NP} \subseteq \text{co-NP}$.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Part 2: $\text{co-NP} \subseteq \text{NP}$}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Let $L$ be an arbitrary language such that $L \in \text{co-NP}$.
    \item By the definition of $\text{co-NP}$, its complement $\overline{L} \in \text{NP}$.
    \item Because we assumed $P = NP$, it follows that $\overline{L} \in P$.
    \item Since $P$ is closed under complementation, the complement of $\overline{L}$ (which is $L$ itself) must also be in $P$. Thus, $L \in P$.
    \item Since $P = NP$, it follows that $L \in \text{NP}$.
    \item Since $L$ was an arbitrary language in $\text{co-NP}$, we have shown that $\text{co-NP} \subseteq \text{NP}$.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Conclusion of Proof:}
Since we have shown both $\text{NP} \subseteq \text{co-NP}$ and $\text{co-NP} \subseteq \text{NP}$, it rigorously follows that $\text{NP} = \text{co-NP}$. 

Thus, we have proven that $P = NP \implies \text{NP} = \text{co-NP}$. By contraposition, the original statement $\text{NP} \neq \text{co-NP} \implies P \neq NP$ is proven true.
\end{proof}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{4. Visualizing the Logical Deduction}

The diagram below maps the logical flow of the contrapositive proof. The inherent symmetry of deterministic computation forces nondeterministic computation to be symmetric if $P=NP$.

\vspace{1.5em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    box/.style={draw, thick, rounded corners, fill=cD!10, minimum width=3.5cm, minimum height=1.2cm, align=center},
    arrow/.style={->, ultra thick, >=stealth, draw=cD!80!black}
]

    % Nodes
    \node[box] (P_NP) at (0, 0) {\textbf{Assumption} \\ $P = NP$};
