    \node[box] (closure) at (0, -2.5) {\textbf{Property of } $\boldsymbol{P}$ \\ $P$ is closed under \\ complement ($P = \text{co-}P$)};
    
    \node[box, fill=cD!25] (substitution) at (6, -1.25) {\textbf{Substitution} \\ Since $P=NP$, \\ replace $P$ with $NP$ in \\ the closure property};
    
    \node[box, fill=cD!40] (conclusion) at (12, -1.25) {\textbf{Conclusion} \\ $NP = \text{co-}NP$};

    % Arrows
    \draw[arrow] (P_NP.east) -- ++(1,0) |- (substitution.west);
    \draw[arrow] (closure.east) -- ++(1,0) |- (substitution.west);
    \draw[arrow] (substitution.east) -- (conclusion.west);

    % Contraposition label
    \node[font=\bfseries\itshape, align=center, text=cD!80!black] at (6, 1) {Contrapositive Logic Frame};
    \draw[dashed, thick, draw=cD!50!black] (-2.5, -3.8) rectangle (14.5, 1.5);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are two languages such that $L_1 \leq_p L_2$, show that $L_2 \in P$ implies $L_1 \in P$. If an NP-complete problem is polynomially solvable, prove that $P = NP$. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2013--14}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{Part 1: Proving that $L_1 \leq_p L_2$ and $L_2 \in P \implies L_1 \in P$}

\textbf{Given:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $L_1 \leq_p L_2$: Language $L_1$ is polynomial-time reducible to language $L_2$.
    \item $L_2 \in P$: There exists a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm $A_2$ that decides $L_2$.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{To Show:} $L_1 \in P$.

\begin{proof}
By the definition of polynomial-time reduction ($L_1 \leq_p L_2$), there exists a deterministic polynomial-time computable function $f: \Sigma^* \to \Sigma^*$ such that for every input string $x$:
\[ x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2 \]
Let the time required to compute $f(x)$ on an input $x$ of length $n$ be bounded by a polynomial $p(n)$. Because a deterministic Turing machine can write at most one symbol per step, the length of the output string $f(x)$ is also strictly bounded by $p(n)$; that is, $|f(x)| \le p(n)$.

Since $L_2 \in P$, the algorithm $A_2$ decides whether any string $y \in L_2$ in time bounded by some polynomial $q(|y|)$.

We can now construct a new algorithm $A_1$ to decide $L_1$:
\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Decider for $L_1$}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Input string $x$ of length $n$
\Ensure \textbf{True} if $x \in L_1$, \textbf{False} otherwise
\State Compute $y = f(x)$ \Comment{Takes $O(p(n))$ time}
\State Run $A_2(y)$ \Comment{Takes $O(q(|y|))$ time}
\If{$A_2(y)$ returns \textbf{True}}
    \State \Return \textbf{True}
\Else
    \State \Return \textbf{False}
\EndIf
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\textbf{Complexity Analysis of $A_1$:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} Computing $f(x)$ takes $O(p(n))$ time. Running $A_2$ on $f(x)$ takes $O(q(|f(x)|))$ time. Since $|f(x)| \le p(n)$, the time to run $A_2$ is bounded by $O(q(p(n)))$. The total time complexity is $O(p(n) + q(p(n)))$. The composition and sum of polynomials yield another polynomial. Thus, $A_1$ runs in deterministic polynomial time.
    \item \textbf{Correctness:} $A_1(x)$ accepts $\iff A_2(f(x))$ accepts $\iff f(x) \in L_2 \iff x \in L_1$.
\end{itemize}
Since $A_1$ decides $L_1$ in polynomial time, $L_1 \in P$.
\end{proof}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{Part 2: Proving that if an NP-Complete problem is in P, then $P = NP$}

\textbf{Given:} Let $X$ be an NP-complete problem, and suppose $X$ is polynomially solvable ($X \in P$).\\
\textbf{To Show:} $P = NP$.

\begin{proof}
To rigorously prove $P = NP$, we must demonstrate both set inclusions: $P \subseteq NP$ and $NP \subseteq P$.

\textbf{Step 1: Show $P \subseteq NP$}
\begin{itemize}
    \item By definition, the class $P$ contains problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time. 
    \item The class $NP$ contains problems solvable by a non-deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time. 
    \item Since every deterministic Turing machine is simply a restricted case of a non-deterministic Turing machine (where the degree of non-determinism is exactly 1 at every step), any problem in $P$ is trivially in $NP$. Thus, $P \subseteq NP$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Step 2: Show $NP \subseteq P$}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Let $L$ be an arbitrary language in $NP$.
    \item By the definition of NP-completeness, since $X$ is NP-complete, \textbf{every} problem in $NP$ is polynomial-time reducible to $X$. Therefore, $L \leq_p X$.
    \item We are given that $X \in P$. 
    \item By the theorem proven in Part 1, if $L \leq_p X$ and $X \in P$, it directly implies that $L \in P$.
    \item Since $L$ was an arbitrarily chosen problem from $NP$, this holds for all problems in $NP$. Thus, $NP \subseteq P$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Conclusion:}
Since $P \subseteq NP$ and $NP \subseteq P$, we conclude that $P = NP$.
\end{proof}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{Visualization of the Subroutine Construction}

The following TikZ diagram illustrates the algorithm $A_1$ constructed in Part 1, showing how the polynomial runtime is preserved through the reduction pipeline.

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    box/.style={draw, thick, rounded corners, fill=cD!10, minimum width=3.5cm, minimum height=1.5cm, align=center},
    arrow/.style={->, thick, >=stealth},
    % Added textnode style to automatically enable multiline text tracking
    textnode/.style={align=center}
]

    % Nodes
    \node[textnode] (input) at (0,0) {\textbf{Input} $x$ \\ (Size $n$)};
    
    \node[box] (reduction) at (4,0) {\textbf{Reduction} $f(x)$ \\ Computable in \\ $O(p(n))$ time};
    
    \node[textnode] (intermediate) at (8.5,0) {\textbf{Transformed} \\ \textbf{Instance} $f(x)$ \\ Size $\le p(n)$};
    
    \node[box] (solver) at (13,0) {\textbf{Decider} $A_2$ \\ for $L_2$ \\ $O(q(m))$ time};
    
    \node[textnode] (output) at (16.5,0) {\textbf{YES/NO}};

    % Arrows
    \draw[arrow] (input) -- (reduction);
    \draw[arrow] (reduction) -- (intermediate);
    \draw[arrow] (intermediate) -- (solver);
    \draw[arrow] (solver) -- (output);
    
    % Grouping Brace for A_1
    % Fixed: 'decorate' must precede its key-value 'decoration' configurations
    \draw[thick, decorate, decoration={brace, mirror, raise=0.5cm}] (1.5,-0.5) -- (14.5,-0.5) 
        node[pos=0.5, below=0.8cm, font=\bfseries] {Decider $A_1$ for $L_1$: Total Time = $O(p(n) + q(p(n)))$ $\in P$};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Define the following six classes of problems: P, Co-P, NP, Co-NP, NPC, and NP-hard. By a diagram show the relationship among P, Co-P, NP, Co-NP, and NPC that most researchers regard as the most likely. \mk{6+4=10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2011--12}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{1. Definitions of the Six Complexity Classes}

Computational complexity theory classifies decision problems (problems requiring a \texttt{YES} or \texttt{NO} answer) into distinct classes based on the resources required to solve or verify them.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Class $\boldsymbol{P}$ (Polynomial Time):} 
    The class of decision problems that can be solved by a deterministic Turing machine in a time bounded by a polynomial function of the input size $n$. Formally, $P = \bigcup_{k \ge 1} \text{TIME}(n^k)$. These problems are considered computationally tractable.
    
    \item \textbf{Class $\boldsymbol{\text{co-}P}$ (Complementary Polynomial Time):} 
    The class containing the complements of all decision problems in $P$. A problem belongs to $\text{co-}P$ if its \texttt{NO} instances can be recognized in deterministic polynomial time. Because deterministic polynomial-time algorithms can simply invert their acceptance states in $O(1)$ time, $P$ is closed under complementation, meaning $P = \text{co-}P$.
    
    \item \textbf{Class $\boldsymbol{NP}$ (Nondeterministic Polynomial Time):} 
    The class of decision problems that can be solved by a non-deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time. Equivalently, it is the class of problems for which any \texttt{YES} instance possesses a polynomial-sized certificate (witness) that can be verified by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
    
    \item \textbf{Class $\boldsymbol{\text{co-}NP}$ (Complementary Nondeterministic Polynomial Time):} 
    The class containing the complements of all decision problems in $NP$. Mathematically, $\text{co-}NP = \{ L \mid \overline{L} \in NP \}$. For any problem in $\text{co-}NP$, every \texttt{NO} instance has a short, polynomial-sized certificate that can be verified deterministically in polynomial time.
    
    \item \textbf{Class $\boldsymbol{NPC}$ (NP-Complete):} 
    The class of the computationally hardest decision problems within $NP$. A problem $L$ is $NP$-complete if it strictly fulfills two conditions:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $L \in NP$
        \item $\forall L' \in NP, L' \leq_p L$ (Every problem in $NP$ can be reduced to $L$ via a polynomial-time Karp reduction).
    \end{enumerate}
    
    \item \textbf{Class $\boldsymbol{NP\text{-hard}}$:} 
    The class of problems to which any problem in $NP$ can be reduced in polynomial time ($\forall L' \in NP, L' \leq_p L$). Unlike $NP$-complete problems, $NP$-hard problems do not need to be in $NP$, nor do they even have to be decision problems or decidable (e.g., the Halting Problem is $NP$-hard).
\end{itemize}

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{2. Structural Relationship Under the Consensus Hypothesis}

The general consensus among theoretical computer scientists is that $P \neq NP$. This hypothesis structurally implies that $P = \text{co-}P \subsetneq (NP \cap \text{co-}NP)$ and that $NP \neq \text{co-}NP$. 

Under this assumption, the intersection of $NP$ and $\text{co-}NP$ contains problems that are solvable in polynomial time ($P$), alongside certain problems not known to be in $P$ (such as Integer Factoring). The $NP$-complete problems ($NPC$) occupy a distinct boundary within $NP$, completely separated from $\text{co-}NP$.

\vspace{1.5em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.1]

    % Draw NP Oval (Left side)
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!8, draw=cD] (-2, 0) ellipse (3.8cm and 2.6cm);
    \node[font=\Large\bfseries, text=cD!60!black] at (-4.5, 1.6) {$NP$};

    % Draw co-NP Oval (Right side)
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!15, fill opacity=0.7, draw=cD!80!black] (2, 0) ellipse (3.8cm and 2.6cm);
    \node[font=\Large\bfseries, text=cD!80!black] at (4.5, 1.6) {$\text{co-}NP$};

    % Intersecting region fill correction for visual clarity
    \begin{scope}
        \clip (-2, 0) ellipse (3.8cm and 2.6cm);
        \fill[cD!25] (2, 0) ellipse (3.8cm and 2.6cm);
    \end{scope}

    % Re-establish lines of intersection
    \draw[thick, draw=cD] (-2, 0) ellipse (3.8cm and 2.6cm);
    \draw[thick, draw=cD!80!black] (2, 0) ellipse (3.8cm and 2.6cm);
    
    % Label the intersection area
    \node[font=\small\bfseries, text=black!70] at (0, 1.8) {$NP \cap \text{co-}NP$};

    % Draw P and co-P (at the bottom center of the intersection)
    \draw[thick, fill=white, draw=black] (0, -0.8) ellipse (1.6cm and 1.0cm);
    \node[font=\large\bfseries] at (0, -0.6) {$P = \text{co-}P$};
    \node[font=\scriptsize\itshape, align=center] at (0, -1.1) {e.g., Shortest Path, \\ Sorting};

    % Draw NPC (inside NP, but completely separate from co-NP under this consensus)
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!50, draw=cD!90!black] (-3.8, -0.2) ellipse (1.3cm and 0.9cm);
    \node[font=\bfseries, color=white] at (-3.8, 0.1) {$NPC$};
    \node[font=\tiny\itshape, color=white, align=center] at (-3.8, -0.3) {e.g., 3-SAT, \\ Vertex Cover};

    % Problems in co-NP but not P or NP (co-NPC region)
    \node[font=\bfseries, text=black!80] at (3.8, -0.2) {$\text{co-}NPC$};
    \node[font=\tiny\itshape, align=center] at (3.8, -0.6) {e.g., TAUTOLOGY, \\ UNSAT};

    % Problems in the intersection but believed not in P
    \node[font=\small\itshape, align=center] at (0, 0.4) {Integer \\ Factoring};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2019-20
\end{enumerate}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cD}{Reductions}
\label{sub:s4-reductions}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item For a graph $G = (V, E)$, a set of vertices $I$ in $G$ is an \textit{independent set} if for every two vertices in $I$, there is no edge between them, and a set $C$ of vertices in $V$ is a \textit{clique} if every two distinct vertices are adjacent. Given a graph $G$ and a number $K$, the independent set problem is known to be NP-complete. The clique problem asks: given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $K$, does $G$ have a clique of size at least $K$? Prove that the clique problem is as hard as the independent set problem.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

To formally prove that the \textsc{Clique} problem is as hard as the \textsc{Independent Set} problem, we must demonstrate a polynomial-time Karp reduction from \textsc{Independent Set} to \textsc{Clique}, denoted as $\textsc{Independent Set} \leq_p \textsc{Clique}$. 

\subsubsection*{1. NP Membership of CLIQUE}
Although the question only strictly asks to show it is "as hard as" (NP-Hardness), formally classifying a problem as NP-Complete requires proving it belongs to NP.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Certificate:} A subset of vertices $C \subseteq V$ such that $|C| = K$.
    \item \textbf{Verifier:} An algorithm checks that $|C| = K$ and iterates over all pairs $u, v \in C$ to verify that the edge $(u,v) \in E$. For a subset of size $K$, this requires checking $\mathcal{O}(K^2)$ pairs. Since this verification runs in polynomial time with respect to the input size, $\textsc{Clique} \in \text{NP}$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{2. Polynomial-Time Reduction Construction}
We must transform any generic instance of the \textsc{Independent Set} problem $\langle G, K \rangle$ into an instance of the \textsc{Clique} problem $\langle G', K' \rangle$. 

We construct $G' = (V', E')$ as the \textbf{complement graph} of $G$, denoted $\overline{G}$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item $V' = V$ (the vertex set remains identical).
    \item $E' = \overline{E} = \{(u,v) \mid u,v \in V, u \neq v, \text{ and } (u,v) \notin E\}$ (edges exist in $G'$ if and only if they do not exist in $G$).
    \item $K' = K$ (the target size remains exactly the same).
\end{itemize}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Reduction from INDEPENDENT-SET to CLIQUE}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Graph $G = (V,E)$, integer $K$
\State \textbf{Output:} Graph $G' = (V',E')$, integer $K'$
\State $V' \gets V$
\State $E' \gets \emptyset$
\For{each pair of distinct vertices $u, v \in V$}
    \If{$(u,v) \notin E$}
        \State $E' \gets E' \cup \{(u,v)\}$
    \EndIf
\EndFor
\State $K' \gets K$
\State \Return $\langle G' = (V', E'), K' \rangle$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsubsection*{3. Complexity of the Reduction}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$. The algorithm iterates through all possible pairs of vertices in $V$ to construct the complement edge set. Checking non-adjacency and adding an edge takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time, yielding a quadratic total time bound.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$ to store the newly constructed complement graph $G'$ as an adjacency matrix or list.
\end{itemize}
Both bounds are strictly polynomial, making this a valid polynomial-time transformation.

\subsubsection*{4. Visual Example of the Reduction}
Below is a clear illustration of how an Independent Set in $G$ directly maps to a Clique in the complement graph $\overline{G}$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    vertex/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, minimum size=7mm, inner sep=0pt},
    isnode/.style={fill=green!30},
    edge/.style={thick, black},
    cliqueedge/.style={thick, blue!80!black}
]

% Graph G
\begin{scope}[xshift=0cm]
    \node[vertex] (A) at (0,0) {$A$};
    \node[vertex, isnode] (B) at (0, 1.5) {$B$};
    \node[vertex, isnode] (C) at (-1.3, -0.75) {$C$};
    \node[vertex, isnode] (D) at (1.3, -0.75) {$D$};

    \draw[edge] (A) -- (B);
    \draw[edge] (A) -- (C);
    \draw[edge] (A) -- (D);

    \node at (0, -1.8) {\textbf{$G = (V, E)$}};
    \node at (0, -2.4) {Independent Set: $\{B, C, D\}$};
\end{scope}

% Arrow
\draw[->, ultra thick] (2.2, 0) -- (4.8, 0) node[midway, above, font=\small] {Complement};

% Graph \overline{G}
\begin{scope}[xshift=7cm]
    \node[vertex] (A) at (0,0) {$A$};
    \node[vertex, isnode] (B) at (0, 1.5) {$B$};
    \node[vertex, isnode] (C) at (-1.3, -0.75) {$C$};
    \node[vertex, isnode] (D) at (1.3, -0.75) {$D$};

    \draw[cliqueedge] (B) -- (C);
    \draw[cliqueedge] (C) -- (D);
    \draw[cliqueedge] (B) -- (D);

    \node at (0, -1.8) {\textbf{$\overline{G} = (V, \overline{E})$}};
    \node at (0, -2.4) {Clique: $\{B, C, D\}$};
\end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{5. Proof of Correctness}
We must prove that the mapping preserves the \textit{YES} answer: $G$ has an independent set of size $K$ $\iff$ $\overline{G}$ has a clique of size $K$.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Forward Direction ($\Rightarrow$):} 
    Suppose $G$ has an independent set $I \subseteq V$ of size $K$. By the definition of an independent set, no two vertices in $I$ share an edge in $E$. 
    \begin{align*}
        \forall u, v \in I \text{ where } u \neq v \implies & (u, v) \notin E \\
        \implies & (u, v) \in \overline{E} \quad \text{(by construction of } \overline{G})
    \end{align*}
    Because every pair of distinct vertices in $I$ is connected by an edge in $\overline{G}$, the set $I$ forms a clique of size $K$ in $\overline{G}$.

    \item \textbf{Backward Direction ($\Leftarrow$):} 
    Suppose $\overline{G}$ has a clique $C \subseteq V'$ of size $K$. By the definition of a clique, every pair of distinct vertices in $C$ shares an edge in $\overline{E}$.
    \begin{align*}
        \forall u, v \in C \text{ where } u \neq v \implies & (u, v) \in \overline{E} \\
        \implies & (u, v) \notin E \quad \text{(by construction of } \overline{G})
    \end{align*}
    Because no two vertices in $C$ are adjacent in the original graph $G$, the set $C$ forms an independent set of size $K$ in $G$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{6. Conclusion}
We have shown a valid polynomial-time reduction from \textsc{Independent Set} to \textsc{Clique}. Since the existence of a solution is flawlessly preserved bi-directionally, solving the \textsc{Clique} problem gives a direct solution to the \textsc{Independent Set} problem. Therefore, the \textsc{Clique} problem is at least as hard as the \textsc{Independent Set} problem (NP-Hard).

\end{tcolorbox}

\item In the \textit{Set Cover} problem (decision version), we are given a set of elements $U$ (the 
universe), a collection of subsets $S = \{S_1, S_2, \dots S_m\}$ where each $S_i \subseteq U$, and an integer 
$k$. The goal is to determine whether there exists a sub-collection $C \subseteq S$ of size $\le k$ such 
that $\bigcup_{S_i \in C} S_i = U$. Prove that the set cover problem is NP-complete.

\textbf{Hint:} Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k$, the vertex cover problem (decision 
version) is to determine whether there is a subset of vertices $V' \subseteq V$ with $|V'| \le k$ such 
that every edge in $E$ is incident to at least one vertex in $V'$. This problem is known to be 
NP-complete.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

To prove that the \textsc{Set Cover} problem is NP-complete, we must satisfy two formal conditions:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{NP Membership:} Prove that a proposed solution (certificate) can be verified in polynomial time.
    \item \textbf{NP-Hardness:} Prove that a known NP-complete problem can be reduced to \textsc{Set Cover} in polynomial time. We will use the \textsc{Vertex Cover} problem for this reduction, as suggested.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{1. Proof of NP Membership}
Given a candidate sub-collection of sets $C \subseteq S$ as a certificate for a \textsc{Set Cover} instance, a deterministic polynomial-time verification algorithm can operate as follows:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Check if the size of the sub-collection satisfies $|C| \le k$. If not, return \texttt{False}.
    \item Compute the union of all subsets contained in $C$: $U_{\text{verified}} = \bigcup_{S_i \in C} S_i$.
    \item Verify whether $U_{\text{verified}} = U$.
\end{enumerate}

Let $n = |U|$ and $m = |S|$. The sub-collection $C$ contains at most $m$ subsets, and each subset has at most $n$ elements. Computing the union and checking membership takes at most $\mathcal{O}(m \cdot n)$ operations, which is polynomial in the size of the input. Thus, \textsc{Set Cover} $\in$ NP.

\subsection*{2. Polynomial-Time Reduction from Vertex Cover}
We now construct a polynomial-time mapping from an instance of the \textsc{Vertex Cover} problem to an instance of the \textsc{Set Cover} problem.

\subsubsection*{2.1 Construction Mapping}
Let $\langle G = (V, E), k \rangle$ be an arbitrary instance of the \textsc{Vertex Cover} problem, where $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\}$ and $E = \{e_1, e_2, \dots, e_p\}$. We construct an equivalent \textsc{Set Cover} instance $\langle U, S, k' \rangle$ as follows:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Universe ($U$):} The elements to be covered are the edges of the graph $G$. Therefore, $U = E$.
    \item \textbf{Collection of Subsets ($S$):} For each vertex $v_i \in V$, we construct a corresponding subset $S_i \in S$. The subset $S_i$ contains all the edges incident to $v_i$ in $G$:
    \[
    S_i = \{e \in E \mid e \text{ is incident to } v_i\}
    \]
    The collection is $S = \{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n\}$, yielding $|S| = |V|$.
    \item \textbf{Target Bound ($k'$):} The allowed budget for the set cover is chosen to be identical to the vertex cover budget, so $k' = k$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{2.2 Formal Reduction Algorithm}
\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Reduction from Vertex Cover to Set Cover}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:}{Graph $G = (V, E)$, Integer $k$}
\State \textbf{Output:}{Universe $U$, Collection $S$, Integer $k'$}
\State $U \gets E$ \Comment{The elements to cover are the graph edges}
\State $S \gets \emptyset$
\For{each vertex $v_i \in V$}
    \State $S_i \gets \{ e \in E \mid e \text{ is incident to } v_i \}$
    \State $S \gets S \cup \{S_i\}$
\EndFor
\State $k' \gets k$
\State \Return $\langle U, S, k' \rangle$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsubsection*{2.3 Complexity Analysis of the Reduction}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} For each vertex, we examine all incident edges to construct the corresponding subset. In an adjacency list representation, this requires traversing the entire graph exactly once, which takes $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ time. This is strictly polynomial.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} The universe has size $|E|$ and the total number of elements stored across all sets in $S$ is $\sum |S_i| = 2|E|$, since every edge is incident to exactly two vertices. Thus, the space complexity is $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{3. Concrete Visual Example}
Consider a simple graph $G$ with vertices $V = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4\}$ and edges $E = \{e_1, e_2, e_3\}$ where $e_1 = (v_1, v_2)$, $e_2 = (v_2, v_3)$, and $e_3 = (v_3, v_4)$. Let $k = 2$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    vertex/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=blue!10, thick, minimum size=7mm},
    vc_vertex/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=red!40, thick, minimum size=7mm},
    edge/.style={thick, black}
]
    % Draw Graph G
    \node[vertex] (v1) at (0, 1) {$v_1$};
    \node[vc_vertex] (v2) at (2, 1) {$v_2$};
    \node[vc_vertex] (v3) at (4, 1) {$v_3$};
    \node[vertex] (v4) at (6, 1) {$v_4$};
    
    \draw[edge] (v1) -- node[above] {$e_1$} (v2);
    \draw[edge] (v2) -- node[above] {$e_2$} (v3);
    \draw[edge] (v3) -- node[above] {$e_3$} (v4);
    
    \node at (3, -0.2) {\textbf{Graph $G$ with Vertex Cover $V' = \{v_2, v_3\}$}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

Following our reduction, the generated instance of \textsc{Set Cover} is:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $U = \{e_1, e_2, e_3\}$
    \item $S_1 = \{e_1\}$, \quad $S_2 = \{e_1, e_2\}$, \quad $S_3 = \{e_2, e_3\}$, \quad $S_4 = \{e_3\}$
    \item $k' = 2$
\end{itemize}
Selecting the subset collection $C = \{S_2, S_3\}$ ensures that $S_2 \cup S_3 = \{e_1, e_2, e_3\} = U$, which uses $|C| = 2 \le k'$. This matches our vertex cover $V' = \{v_2, v_3\}$.

\subsection*{4. Proof of Correctness}
We must show that $G$ has a vertex cover of size at most $k$ if and only if the constructed universe $U$ has a set cover of size at most $k$.

\subsubsection*{4.1 Forward Direction ($\Rightarrow$)}
Suppose $G$ has a vertex cover $V' \subseteq V$ such that $|V'| \le k$. By definition, for every edge $e \in E$, at least one of its endpoints belongs to $V'$. 

Let us choose our sub-collection $C \subseteq S$ using the indices from the vertex cover:
\[
C = \{S_i \mid v_i \in V'\}
\]
Clearly, $|C| = |V'| \le k = k'$. We verify that $C$ forms a valid set cover for $U$:
\begin{align*}
    \text{Let } e \in U. & \implies e \in E \\
    & \implies \exists v_i \in V' \text{ such that } e \text{ is incident to } v_i \quad \text{(since } V' \text{ is a vertex cover)} \\
    & \implies e \in S_i \quad \text{(by construction of } S_i) \\
    & \implies e \in \bigcup_{S_j \in C} S_j
\end{align*}
Since this holds for every element $e \in U$, we conclude that $\bigcup_{S_j \in C} S_j = U$. Hence, $C$ is a valid set cover of size $\le k'$.

\subsubsection*{4.2 Backward Direction ($\Leftarrow$)}
Conversely, suppose there exists a sub-collection $C \subseteq S$ such that $|C| \le k'$ and $\bigcup_{S_i \in C} S_i = U$. We select our vertex set $V' \subseteq V$ as:
\[
V' = \{v_i \in V \mid S_i \in C\}
\]
Clearly, $|V'| = |C| \le k' = k$. We verify that $V'$ forms a valid vertex cover for $G$:
\begin{align*}
    \text{Let } e \in E. & \implies e \in U \\
    & \implies \exists S_i \in C \text{ such that } e \in S_i \quad \text{(since } C \text{ is a valid set cover)} \\
    & \implies e \text{ is incident to vertex } v_i \in V \quad \text{(by construction of } S_i) \\
    & \implies v_i \in V' \quad \text{(by definition of } V')
\end{align*}
Since every edge $e \in E$ is incident to at least one vertex in $V'$, the subset $V'$ is a valid vertex cover of size $\le k$.

\subsection*{5. Conclusion}
We have shown that \textsc{Set Cover} is in NP, and that \textsc{Vertex Cover} $\leq_p$ \textsc{Set Cover}. Because \textsc{Vertex Cover} is an established NP-complete problem, it follows that \textsc{Set Cover} is NP-hard. Combining both parts, the \textsc{Set Cover} problem is NP-complete.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item Imagine you are managing a team of employees, each with their own set of tasks to 
    complete. However, some tasks cannot be worked on simultaneously due to 
    dependencies or resource constraints. Your goal is to maximize productivity by 
    scheduling as many tasks as possible while ensuring that no two conflicting tasks are 
    assigned to the same team member simultaneously. You described the problem to your 
    friend. He commented, ``The problem can be mapped to a popular NP-complete 
    problem and can be proved to be NP-hard by reduction technique.''
    
    \textbf{Justify} your friend's comment by mapping the problem to an NP-complete problem 
    and reducing it to another known NP-complete problem.
\mk{17} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

The problem described by your friend is a classic constraint satisfaction and optimization problem that maps directly to the \textbf{Maximum Independent Set (MIS)} problem. To justify your friend's comment, we will formally define the mapping, state the decision version of the problem, and provide a rigorous mathematical proof of its NP-completeness by reducing the known NP-complete \textbf{Clique} problem to it.

\subsection*{1. Problem Mapping and Formulation}
We can model the task scheduling problem as an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Vertices ($V$):} Each vertex $v \in V$ represents a specific task.
    \item \textbf{Edges ($E$):} An edge $e = (u, v) \in E$ exists if and only if tasks $u$ and $v$ conflict (i.e., they have dependencies or resource constraints and cannot be scheduled simultaneously).
\end{itemize}
\textbf{Objective:} Maximize the number of scheduled tasks such that no two tasks conflict. In graph-theoretic terms, we want to find the largest subset of vertices $S \subseteq V$ such that no two vertices in $S$ share an edge. This is precisely the \textbf{Maximum Independent Set} problem. 

To discuss NP-completeness, we formulate its \textbf{Decision Version (Independent Set)}:
\begin{quote}
\textit{Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k$, does there exist a subset $S \subseteq V$ such that $|S| \geq k$ and for all $u, v \in S$, $(u, v) \notin E$?}
\end{quote}

\subsection*{2. Proof of NP-Completeness}
To prove that Independent Set (IS) is NP-complete, we must show two things:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $\text{IS} \in \text{NP}$
    \item A known NP-complete problem can be reduced to IS in polynomial time (we will use \textbf{Clique $\leq_p$ IS}).
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Step 2.1: NP Membership ($\text{IS} \in \text{NP}$)}
To show $\text{IS} \in \text{NP}$, we must demonstrate that a proposed solution (certificate) can be verified in polynomial time.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Certificate:} A proposed subset of vertices $S \subseteq V$.
    \item \textbf{Verification:} 
        1. Count the vertices in $S$ to ensure $|S| \geq k$. This takes $O(|V|)$ time.
        2. For every pair of vertices $u, v \in S$, check that $(u, v) \notin E$. Since there are at most $\frac{|V|(|V|-1)}{2}$ pairs, this check takes $O(|V|^2)$ time.
\end{itemize}
Since the verification algorithm runs in strictly polynomial time ($O(|V|^2)$), we conclude that $\text{IS} \in \text{NP}$.

\subsubsection*{Step 2.2: Polynomial Time Reduction (Clique $\leq_p$ IS)}
We reduce from the \textbf{Clique} problem, which is known to be NP-complete.
\begin{quote}
\textit{Clique Problem: Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k$, does $G$ contain a complete subgraph (clique) of size $\geq k$?}
\end{quote}

\textbf{Construction:} Given an instance $\langle G = (V, E), k \rangle$ of the Clique problem, we construct an instance $\langle \overline{G} = (V, \overline{E}), k \rangle$ for the Independent Set problem, where $\overline{G}$ is the \textit{complement graph} of $G$. 

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Polynomial Time Reduction: $\text{Clique}(G, k) \to \text{IS}(\overline{G}, k)$}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Graph $G = (V, E)$, integer $k$
\Ensure Complement Graph $\overline{G} = (V, \overline{E})$, integer $k$
\State $\overline{E} \gets \emptyset$
\For{each pair of distinct vertices $u, v \in V$}
    \If{$(u, v) \notin E$}
        \State $\overline{E} \gets \overline{E} \cup \{(u, v)\}$
    \EndIf
\EndFor
\State \Return $\langle \overline{G} = (V, \overline{E}), k \rangle$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\textbf{Complexity of Reduction:} The algorithm iterates through all possible vertex pairs. Thus, the time complexity is $O(|V|^2)$ and space complexity is $O(|V|^2)$, meaning the construction is achieved in polynomial time.

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9, transform shape]
    % Graph G
    \node[circle, draw, thick, minimum size=8mm, fill=blue!15] (1) at (0, 1.5) {$v_1$};
    \node[circle, draw, thick, minimum size=8mm, fill=blue!15] (2) at (-1.5, -0.5) {$v_2$};
    \node[circle, draw, thick, minimum size=8mm, fill=blue!15] (3) at (1.5, -0.5) {$v_3$};
    \node[circle, draw, thick, minimum size=8mm] (4) at (0, -2) {$v_4$};

    \draw[thick] (1) -- (2);
    \draw[thick] (2) -- (3);
    \draw[thick] (1) -- (3);
    \draw[thick] (3) -- (4);

    \node at (0, -3.2) {\textbf{Graph $G$: 3-Clique highlighted}};
    \node at (0, -3.7) {$E = \{(v_1,v_2), (v_2,v_3), (v_1,v_3), (v_3,v_4)\}$};

    % Arrow
    \draw[->, thick, double] (3.5, 0) -- (4.5, 0) node[midway, above] {\small Reduction};

    % Graph G-bar
    \begin{scope}[xshift=8cm]
    \node[circle, draw, thick, minimum size=8mm, fill=red!15] (1b) at (0, 1.5) {$v_1$};
    \node[circle, draw, thick, minimum size=8mm, fill=red!15] (2b) at (-1.5, -0.5) {$v_2$};
    \node[circle, draw, thick, minimum size=8mm, fill=red!15] (3b) at (1.5, -0.5) {$v_3$};
    \node[circle, draw, thick, minimum size=8mm] (4b) at (0, -2) {$v_4$};

    \draw[thick, dashed] (1b) -- (4b);
    \draw[thick, dashed] (2b) -- (4b);

    \node at (0, -3.2) {\textbf{Graph $\overline{G}$: Size 3 IS highlighted}};
    \node at (0, -3.7) {$\overline{E} = \{(v_1,v_4), (v_2,v_4)\}$};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{Step 2.3: Proof of Correctness}
We must prove that $G$ has a clique of size $k$ if and only if $\overline{G}$ has an independent set of size $k$.

\textbf{Forward Direction ($\implies$):} 
Assume $G$ has a clique $C \subseteq V$ of size $k$. 
By the definition of a clique, for every pair of distinct vertices $u, v \in C$, the edge $(u, v)$ is in $E$. 
By the construction of the complement graph $\overline{G}$, an edge exists in $\overline{E}$ \textit{if and only if} it does not exist in $E$. 
Therefore, for every pair $u, v \in C$, the edge $(u, v) \notin \overline{E}$. 
By definition, $C$ forms an independent set of size $k$ in $\overline{G}$.

\textbf{Backward Direction ($\impliedby$):}
Assume $\overline{G}$ has an independent set $S \subseteq V$ of size $k$.
By the definition of an independent set, for every pair of distinct vertices $u, v \in S$, the edge $(u, v) \notin \overline{E}$.
By the construction of $\overline{G}$, any edge missing in $\overline{E}$ must inherently be present in $E$.
Therefore, for every pair $u, v \in S$, the edge $(u, v) \in E$.
By definition, $S$ forms a clique of size $k$ in $G$.

\subsection*{3. Conclusion}
Since we have shown that the Independent Set problem is in NP, and that the NP-complete Clique problem can be reduced to it in polynomial time, we have successfully proven that the Independent Set problem is \textbf{NP-complete}. Because your optimization problem (maximizing non-conflicting tasks) asks for the \textit{maximum} Independent Set, it is inherently \textbf{NP-hard}. Your friend's comment is mathematically justified.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Suppose you are a developer of a social media platform. The platform wants to 
    add a feature in the admin section. The feature is to identify the minimal subset of 
    users who can reach out to all other users of the platform through their (selected 
    subset users') connections. Now you have to formulate an algorithm to develop the 
    feature. Your colleague commented that the problem can be mapped to a popular np-
    complete problem.
\begin{enumerate}

\item \textbf{[Minimal subset reachability problem]} Map the problem to the NP-complete problem that your colleague suggested.
\mk{5} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}

%% Q — 2021-22
\item \textbf{[Minimal subset reachability problem]} Prove that the problem is NP-complete. You have to use the 3-SAT problem in the proof.
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}
\end{enumerate}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{Part (a): Minimal Subset Reachability Problem Mapping}

The problem of identifying a minimal subset of users who can reach out to all other users through their connections maps directly to the \textbf{Minimum Dominating Set} problem in graph theory.

\textbf{Problem Formulation:}
Let the social media platform be represented as an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$, where:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Vertices ($V$):} Each vertex $v \in V$ represents a user on the platform.
    \item \textbf{Edges ($E$):} An edge $(u, v) \in E$ represents a connection (friendship/follow) between user $u$ and user $v$.
\end{itemize}

A subset $D \subseteq V$ is called a \textit{Dominating Set} if every vertex $v \in V \setminus D$ is adjacent to at least one vertex in $D$. The objective is to find a Dominating Set $D$ such that its size $|D|$ is minimized. For the purpose of NP-completeness, we consider its decision version:
\begin{quote}
\textbf{Dominating Set (DS) Decision Problem:} Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k \geq 0$, does there exist a dominating set $D \subseteq V$ such that $|D| \leq k$?
\end{quote}


\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{Part (b): Proof of NP-Completeness using 3-SAT}

To prove that the Dominating Set problem is NP-complete, we must show two properties:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item The problem belongs to the class NP.
    \item A known NP-complete problem (in this case, 3-SAT) can be reduced to it in polynomial time.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Step 1: NP Membership (DS $\in$ NP)}
To show that DS is in NP, we must provide a polynomial-time verification algorithm for a given certificate.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Certificate:} A subset of vertices $D \subseteq V$.
    \item \textbf{Verification:} 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Check if $|D| \leq k$. This takes $O(|V|)$ time.
        \item For each vertex $v \in V$, verify that either $v \in D$ or there exists a neighbor $u$ of $v$ such that $u \in D$. This requires traversing the adjacency list, taking $O(|V| + |E|)$ time.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{itemize}
Since the verification is completed in strictly polynomial time $O(|V| + |E|)$, Dominating Set $\in$ NP.

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Polynomial-Time Reduction (3-SAT $\leq_p$ DS)}
We map a given instance of 3-SAT to an instance of the Dominating Set problem. 
Let $\phi$ be a boolean formula in 3-Conjunctive Normal Form (3-CNF) with $n$ variables $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ and $m$ clauses $C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_m\}$. Each clause contains exactly 3 literals. We construct a graph $G=(V, E)$ and set a target size $k$ as follows:

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Reduction from 3-SAT to Dominating Set}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require 3-CNF formula $\phi$ with variables $X$ and clauses $C$
\Ensure Graph $G = (V, E)$ and integer $k$
\State $V \gets \emptyset$, $E \gets \emptyset$
\State $k \gets |X|$ \Comment{Budget is exactly the number of variables}
\For{each variable $x_i \in X$}
    \State Add vertices $v_i$ (for $x_i$), $\bar{v}_i$ (for $\neg x_i$), and a structural node $w_i$ to $V$
    \State Add edges $(v_i, \bar{v}_i), (v_i, w_i), (\bar{v}_i, w_i)$ to $E$ \Comment{Forms a triangle}
\EndFor
\For{each clause $c_j \in C$}
    \State Add vertex $u_j$ to $V$
    \For{each literal $l$ in $c_j$}
        \If{$l$ is the positive literal $x_i$}
            \State Add edge $(u_j, v_i)$ to $E$
        \ElsIf{$l$ is the negative literal $\neg x_i$}
            \State Add edge $(u_j, \bar{v}_i)$ to $E$
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\State \Return $\langle G = (V, E), k \rangle$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\textbf{Complexity of Reduction:} The construction iterates through $n$ variables and $m$ clauses. Creating the vertices and edges takes $O(n + m)$ time and space. Thus, the reduction is strictly polynomial.

\subsubsection*{Visualization of the Reduction}
Below is a TikZ diagram demonstrating the reduction for $\phi = (x_1 \lor \neg x_2 \lor x_3) \land (\neg x_1 \lor x_2 \lor \neg x_3)$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.85, transform shape, node distance=2cm]
    \tikzstyle{vnode} = [circle, draw, thick, fill=blue!10, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=0pt]
    \tikzstyle{wnode} = [circle, draw, thick, fill=gray!20, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=0pt]
    \tikzstyle{cnode} = [rectangle, draw, thick, fill=green!15, minimum width=1.2cm, minimum height=0.8cm, rounded corners=2pt]
    \tikzstyle{edge} = [draw, thick]
    \tikzstyle{cedge} = [draw, thick, dashed, ->, >=stealth, color=red!70!black]

    % Variable 1
    \node[wnode] (w1) at (1, 3) {$w_1$};
    \node[vnode] (v1) at (0, 1) {$x_1$};
    \node[vnode] (nv1) at (2, 1) {$\neg x_1$};
    \draw[edge] (w1) -- (v1); \draw[edge] (w1) -- (nv1); \draw[edge] (v1) -- (nv1);

    % Variable 2
    \node[wnode] (w2) at (5, 3) {$w_2$};
    \node[vnode] (v2) at (4, 1) {$x_2$};
    \node[vnode] (nv2) at (6, 1) {$\neg x_2$};
    \draw[edge] (w2) -- (v2); \draw[edge] (w2) -- (nv2); \draw[edge] (v2) -- (nv2);

    % Variable 3
    \node[wnode] (w3) at (9, 3) {$w_3$};
    \node[vnode] (v3) at (8, 1) {$x_3$};
    \node[vnode] (nv3) at (10, 1) {$\neg x_3$};
    \draw[edge] (w3) -- (v3); \draw[edge] (w3) -- (nv3); \draw[edge] (v3) -- (nv3);

    % Clauses
    \node[cnode] (c1) at (2.5, -2) {$c_1$};
    \node[cnode] (c2) at (7.5, -2) {$c_2$};

    % Clause 1 edges (x1 or ~x2 or x3)
    \draw[cedge] (c1) -- (v1);
    \draw[cedge] (c1) -- (nv2);
    \draw[cedge] (c1) -- (v3);

    % Clause 2 edges (~x1 or x2 or ~x3)
    \draw[cedge] (c2) -- (nv1);
    \draw[cedge] (c2) -- (v2);
    \draw[cedge] (c2) -- (nv3);

    % Legend
    \node[anchor=west] at (11, 2) {\textbf{Legend:}};
    \node[anchor=west] at (11, 1.4) {$\bigcirc$ : Literal Vertices};
    \node[anchor=west] at (11, 0.8) {$\bigcirc$ : Auxiliary Vertices ($w_i$)};
    \node[anchor=west] at (11, 0.2) {$\square$ : Clause Vertices};
    \node[anchor=west] at (11, -0.4) {\textbf{Target Size:} $k = 3$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 3: Proof of Correctness}

We must prove that the formula $\phi$ is satisfiable \textit{if and only if} the graph $G$ has a dominating set $D$ of size $k = n$.

\textbf{Forward Direction ($\implies$): Satisfiable $\implies$ Dominating Set of size $n$}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Suppose $\phi$ has a satisfying truth assignment. We construct a set $D$ by picking exactly one vertex from each variable gadget:
    \item If $x_i$ is True, add $v_i$ to $D$. If $x_i$ is False, add $\bar{v}_i$ to $D$.
    \item Clearly, $|D| = n$.
    \item \textit{Domination of variables:} For each $i$, the chosen literal dominates itself, the other literal, and $w_i$. Thus, all variable gadget vertices are covered.
    \item \textit{Domination of clauses:} Because the assignment satisfies $\phi$, every clause $c_j$ contains at least one True literal. By our construction, the corresponding True literal vertex is in $D$. Since $u_j$ is connected to its literals, $u_j$ is dominated by $D$.
    \item Therefore, $D$ is a valid dominating set of size $n$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Backward Direction ($\impliedby$): Dominating Set of size $n \implies$ Satisfiable}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Suppose $G$ has a dominating set $D$ of size $n$. 
    \item To dominate the isolated structural node $w_i$, $D$ must contain at least one vertex from the triangle $\{v_i, \bar{v}_i, w_i\}$. Since there are $n$ such disjoint triangles and $|D| = n$, $D$ must contain \textbf{exactly one} vertex from each triangle, and no clause vertices $u_j$.
    \item If $D$ contains $w_i$, we can swap it out for $v_i$ without losing domination over the triangle $\{v_i, \bar{v}_i, w_i\}$ (and potentially gaining domination over some clauses). Thus, without loss of generality, we can assume $D$ consists entirely of literal vertices ($v_i$ or $\bar{v}_i$).
    \item Since $D$ contains exactly one of $v_i$ or $\bar{v}_i$ for each $i$, it maps to a well-defined boolean truth assignment ($x_i = \text{True}$ if $v_i \in D$, else False).
    \item Since $D$ is a dominating set, every clause vertex $u_j$ must be adjacent to at least one vertex in $D$. This means for every clause $c_j$, at least one of its literals is assigned True.
    \item Therefore, the truth assignment satisfies all clauses, meaning $\phi$ is satisfiable.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Conclusion:} Because Dominating Set is in NP and 3-SAT (an NP-complete problem) can be polynomially reduced to it, the Dominating Set problem (and thus the Minimal Subset Reachability problem) is NP-complete.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2022-23
\item Explain how we can prove that an optimization problem is NP-complete.
\mk{5} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

From a strict theoretical perspective, the complexity classes $\text{P}$, $\text{NP}$, and $\text{NP-complete}$ are defined exclusively for \textbf{decision problems}---problems whose outputs are a simple binary \texttt{YES} or \texttt{NO}. An \textbf{optimization problem} seeks to find the \textit{best} solution among a set of feasible options (minimizing or maximizing an objective function) and therefore cannot be strictly classified as NP-complete.

To show that an optimization problem is computationally intractable, we use a standard four-step pedagogical paradigm: we convert the optimization problem into its corresponding decision variant, prove that this decision variant is NP-complete, and establish that the optimization problem is at least as hard (\textbf{NP-hard}).

---

\subsection*{The 4-Step Proof Framework}

To establish the hardness of an optimization problem $\mathcal{O}$, follow these precise mathematical steps:

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Formulate the Decision Variant ($\mathcal{D}$)}
Convert the optimization objective into a threshold constraint. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $\mathcal{O}$ is a \textbf{minimization problem} (e.g., finding the shortest path or minimum cost), the decision variant $\mathcal{D}$ asks: \textit{"Is there a feasible solution with a cost $\leq k$?"}
    \item If $\mathcal{O}$ is a \textbf{maximization problem} (e.g., finding the maximum independent set or profit), the decision variant $\mathcal{D}$ asks: \textit{"Is there a feasible solution with a value $\geq k$?"}
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Prove NP-Membership ($\mathcal{D} \in \text{NP}$)}
Show that the decision variant $\mathcal{D}$ belongs to the class $\text{NP}$. This requires demonstrating that a given candidate solution (certificate $y$) can be verified by a deterministic algorithm in polynomial time:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Certificate:} Define what the certificate $y$ contains (e.g., a list of vertices, a sequence of edges, or a partition of a set).
    \item \textbf{Verification Algorithm:} Describe a deterministic algorithm $V(x, y)$ that takes the problem instance $x$ and certificate $y$ as inputs and checks:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Whether $y$ is syntactically valid and represents a feasible solution.
        \item Whether the objective value of $y$ satisfies the threshold constraint ($\leq k$ or $\geq k$).
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Complexity Analysis:} Explicitly demonstrate that the verification steps run in $O(n^c)$ time, where $n = |x|$ and $c$ is a constant.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Step 3: Prove NP-Hardness via Polynomial-Time Reduction ($\mathcal{L} \leq_p \mathcal{D}$)}
Select a known NP-complete problem $\mathcal{L}$ (such as 3-SAT, Vertex Cover, or Clique) and construct a polynomial-time reduction to your decision variant $\mathcal{D}$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{The Mapping function ($f$):} Design an algorithm that transforms any arbitrary instance $I_{\mathcal{L}}$ of the known problem into a specific instance $I_{\mathcal{D}}$ of your problem. Prove that this transformation takes $O(n^d)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Forward Correctness ($\implies$):} Prove that if $I_{\mathcal{L}}$ is a \texttt{YES} instance, then $I_{\mathcal{D}}$ must also be a \texttt{YES} instance.
    \item \textbf{Backward Correctness ($\impliedby$):} Prove that if $I_{\mathcal{D}}$ is a \texttt{YES} instance, then $I_{\mathcal{L}}$ must also be a \texttt{YES} instance (often proved via contrapositive: $\neg I_{\mathcal{L}} \implies \neg I_{\mathcal{D}}$).
\end{itemize}
Completing Steps 2 and 3 formally establishes that \textbf{$\mathcal{D}$ is NP-complete}.

\subsubsection*{Step 4: Establish NP-Hardness of the Optimization Problem ($\mathcal{D} \leq_p \mathcal{O}$)}
Finally, demonstrate that if you had a black-box solver (oracle) capable of solving the optimization problem $\mathcal{O}$ in a single step, you could use it to solve the decision problem $\mathcal{D}$ in polynomial time. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item Simply query the optimization oracle for the instance. If the optimal value returned meets or beats the threshold $k$, output \texttt{YES}; otherwise, output \texttt{NO}.
\end{itemize}
Since $\mathcal{D}$ is NP-complete and $\mathcal{D} \leq_p \mathcal{O}$, the optimization problem $\mathcal{O}$ is formally classified as \textbf{NP-hard}.

---

\subsection*{Structural Relationship and Reduction Flow}

The following diagram illustrates how the components interface with one another. The polynomial-time reduction links the known hard problem to the decision variant, while a Turing reduction (often implemented via binary search over the parameter space $k$) establishes the equivalence between the decision and optimization variants.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.95, transform shape, every node/.style={rectangle, draw, thick, rounded corners, minimum height=1cm, text width=4cm, align=center}]

    % Nodes
    \node[fill=red!10, draw=red!70!black] (known) at (0, 2) {\textbf{Known NP-Complete Problem} \\ ($\mathcal{L}$)};
    \node[fill=blue!10, draw=blue!70!black] (decision) at (6, 2) {\textbf{Target Decision Variant} \\ ($\mathcal{D}$)};
    \node[fill=green!10, draw=green!70!black] (opt) at (6, -0.5) {\textbf{Target Optimization Problem} \\ ($\mathcal{O}$)};

    % Paths
    \draw[->,>=stealth,ultra thick,dashed] (known) -- (decision) 
        node[midway, above, text width=3cm, align=center, scale=0.8] {Polynomial Reduction \\ ($I_{\mathcal{L}} \xrightarrow{f} I_{\mathcal{D}}$)};
        
    \draw[<->,>=stealth,ultra thick] (decision) -- (opt) 
        node[midway, right, text width=3.5cm, align=center, scale=0.8] {Turing Equivalence \\ (e.g., Binary Search on $k$)};

    % Background Labels
    \node[draw=none, fill=none, text width=5cm, align=left, anchor=west] at (-0.5, -0.5) {
        $\bullet$ $\mathcal{D} \in \text{NP}$ (Easy to verify) \\
        $\bullet$ $\mathcal{L} \leq_p \mathcal{D}$ ($\mathcal{D}$ is NP-hard) \\
        $\implies \mathcal{D}$ is \textbf{NP-complete} \\
        $\implies \mathcal{O}$ is \textbf{NP-hard}
    };

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

---

\subsection*{Concrete Example: Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP)}

To illustrate this framework clearly, let us look at the classic Traveling Salesperson Problem:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{p{7cm} | p{7cm}}
\hline
\textbf{Optimization Variant ($\mathcal{O}_{TSP}$)} & \textbf{Decision Variant ($\mathcal{D}_{TSP}$)} \\ \hline
Given a complete graph $G=(V,E)$ with edge weights $w(e)$, find a Hamiltonian cycle that \textbf{minimizes} the total edge weight. & Given a complete graph $G=(V,E)$ with edge weights $w(e)$ and a threshold $k$, is there a Hamiltonian cycle with total weight \textbf{$\leq k$}? \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{NP-Membership:} A certificate $y$ is an ordered sequence of vertices. Verification checks if the sequence visits every vertex exactly once, returns to the start, and sums the weights to verify if $\text{Total Cost} \leq k$. This runs in linear time $O(|V|)$, proving $\mathcal{D}_{TSP} \in \text{NP}$.
    \item \textbf{NP-Hardness Reduction:} We reduce from the standard \textbf{Hamiltonian Cycle Problem} ($\mathcal{L}$). Given an arbitrary graph $G$, we construct a complete graph $G'$ where existing edges retain weight $1$ and missing edges are given a massive penalty weight $M > |V|$. We set the threshold $k = |V|$. 
    \item \textbf{Correctness:} $G$ has a Hamiltonian cycle if and only if $G'$ has a TSP tour of total weight $\leq |V|$. This proves $\mathcal{D}_{TSP}$ is NP-complete, implying $\mathcal{O}_{TSP}$ is NP-hard.
\end{enumerate}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2021-22
\item For a graph $G = (V, E)$, a set $S$ of vertices in $G$ is a \textit{vertex cover} if every edge $e$ in $G$ has at least one end in $S$. Given a graph $G$ and a number $K$, the vertex cover problem asks if $G$ contains a vertex cover of size at most $K$. Given a set $U$ of $n$ elements, a collection $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_m$ of subsets of $U$, and a number $k$, the set cover problem asks if there exists a collection of at most $k$ of these subsets whose union is equal to $U$. Prove that the set cover problem is at least as hard as the vertex cover problem.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

To prove that the \textbf{Set Cover (SC)} problem is at least as hard as the \textbf{Vertex Cover (VC)} problem, we must demonstrate a polynomial-time reduction from Vertex Cover to Set Cover, denoted as $\text{VC} \le_p \text{SC}$. 

Since Vertex Cover is a known NP-Complete problem, proving this reduction establishes that Set Cover is NP-Hard (and since Set Cover is in NP, it is also NP-Complete).

\subsection*{1. Polynomial-Time Reduction Construction}

Let us take an arbitrary instance of the Vertex Cover problem. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Given:} A graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $K$.
    \item \textbf{Question:} Does there exist a subset $V' \subseteq V$ such that $|V'| \le K$ and every edge $e \in E$ is incident to at least one vertex in $V'$?
\end{itemize}

We construct an instance of the Set Cover problem as follows:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Universe ($U$):} We must cover all edges in the graph. Thus, we set the universe of elements to be the set of edges: $U = E$.
    \item \textbf{Subsets ($\mathcal{S}$):} A vertex in a graph "covers" its incident edges. For each vertex $v \in V$, we create a subset $S_v$ containing all edges incident to $v$. Therefore, our collection of subsets is $\mathcal{S} = \{S_v \mid v \in V\}$.
    \item \textbf{Parameter ($k$):} We set the maximum number of allowed subsets in the set cover to be exactly the maximum allowed size of the vertex cover: $k = K$.
\end{itemize}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Reduction from Vertex Cover to Set Cover}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Graph $G=(V,E)$, Integer $K$
\Ensure Set Cover Instance $(U, \mathcal{S}, k)$
\State $U \gets E$ \Comment{Universe is the set of edges}
\State $\mathcal{S} \gets \emptyset$
\For{each $v \in V$}
    \State $S_v \gets \emptyset$
    \For{each edge $e \in E$ incident to $v$}
        \State $S_v \gets S_v \cup \{e\}$
    \EndFor
    \State $\mathcal{S} \gets \mathcal{S} \cup \{S_v\}$
\EndFor
\State $k \gets K$
\State \Return $(U, \mathcal{S}, k)$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\textbf{Time Complexity of Reduction:} Building the universe takes $O(|E|)$ time. Creating the subsets requires iterating over all vertices and their incident edges, taking $O(|V| + |E|)$ time. Setting $k$ takes $O(1)$ time. Thus, the total reduction takes $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ time, which is strictly polynomial.

\vspace{1em}
\subsection*{2. Visualizing the Transformation}

To build intuition, consider a simple graph and its corresponding Set Cover instance generated by our reduction.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, node distance=2.5cm, thick]
    % Graph Nodes
    \node[circle, draw=cD, fill=cD!10, minimum size=8mm] (v1) at (0, 2) {$v_1$};
    \node[circle, draw=cD, fill=cD!10, minimum size=8mm] (v2) at (-1.5, 0) {$v_2$};
    \node[circle, draw=cD, fill=cD!10, minimum size=8mm] (v3) at (1.5, 0) {$v_3$};

    % Graph Edges
    \draw (v1) -- (v2) node[midway, left=2pt, text=blue] {$e_1$};
    \draw (v2) -- (v3) node[midway, below=2pt, text=blue] {$e_2$};
    \draw (v1) -- (v3) node[midway, right=2pt, text=blue] {$e_3$};

    \node[above] at (0, 3) {\textbf{Vertex Cover Instance}};

    % Arrow
    \draw[->, very thick, cD] (2.5, 1) -- (4.5, 1) node[midway, above] {\small Reduction};

    % Set Cover Nodes
    \node[right, align=left] at (5, 2.5) {\textbf{Set Cover Instance}};
    \node[right, align=left] at (5, 1.5) {$U = \{ \textcolor{blue}{e_1}, \textcolor{blue}{e_2}, \textcolor{blue}{e_3} \}$};
    \node[right, align=left] at (5, 0.5) {$S_{v_1} = \{ \textcolor{blue}{e_1}, \textcolor{blue}{e_3} \}$};
    \node[right, align=left] at (5, -0.2) {$S_{v_2} = \{ \textcolor{blue}{e_1}, \textcolor{blue}{e_2} \}$};
    \node[right, align=left] at (5, -0.9) {$S_{v_3} = \{ \textcolor{blue}{e_2}, \textcolor{blue}{e_3} \}$};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsection*{3. Proof of Correctness}

To complete the proof, we must show that the original graph $G$ has a Vertex Cover of size $\le K$ \textbf{if and only if} the constructed instance $(U, \mathcal{S}, k)$ has a Set Cover of size $\le k$.

\subsubsection*{Forward Direction: $\text{VC} \implies \text{SC}$}
\textbf{Claim:} If $G$ has a vertex cover $V'$ of size $\le K$, then $\mathcal{S}$ contains a set cover $\mathcal{C}$ of size $\le k$.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Let $V' \subseteq V$ be a valid vertex cover such that $|V'| \le K$.
    \item We construct our set cover $\mathcal{C}$ by choosing the subsets corresponding to the vertices in $V'$. Thus, $\mathcal{C} = \{S_v \mid v \in V'\}$.
    \item By definition of a vertex cover, every edge $e = (u, w) \in E$ has at least one endpoint in $V'$ (either $u \in V'$ or $w \in V'$).
    \item In our reduction, $e \in S_u$ and $e \in S_w$. Since at least one of $u$ or $w$ is in $V'$, at least one of $S_u$ or $S_w$ is in $\mathcal{C}$.
    \item Therefore, the element $e$ is covered by the union of sets in $\mathcal{C}$. Since this holds true for all edges $e \in E = U$, $\mathcal{C}$ is a valid set cover for $U$.
    \item Because $|V'| \le K$ and we mapped each vertex one-to-one to a subset, $|\mathcal{C}| \le K = k$.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Backward Direction: $\text{SC} \implies \text{VC}$}
\textbf{Claim:} If $\mathcal{S}$ contains a set cover $\mathcal{C}$ of size $\le k$, then $G$ has a vertex cover $V'$ of size $\le K$.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Let $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{S}$ be a valid set cover such that $|\mathcal{C}| \le k$.
    \item We construct our vertex cover $V'$ by choosing the vertices that correspond to the subsets in $\mathcal{C}$. Thus, $V' = \{v \mid S_v \in \mathcal{C}\}$.
    \item Because $\mathcal{C}$ is a valid set cover, the union of its subsets contains every element in $U$. Since $U = E$, every edge $e \in E$ is present in at least one subset $S_v \in \mathcal{C}$.
    \item By the definition of our reduction, an element $e$ is in $S_v$ if and only if edge $e$ is incident to vertex $v$ in $G$.
    \item Therefore, for every edge $e \in E$, there exists some $v \in V'$ such that $e$ is incident to $v$. This exactly satisfies the definition of a vertex cover.
    \item Since $|\mathcal{C}| \le k$ and $k = K$, the size of our vertex cover $|V'| \le K$.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{4. Conclusion}
We have successfully provided a polynomial-time reduction from Vertex Cover to Set Cover, and mathematically proven that a solution exists for the Vertex Cover instance if and only if a solution exists for the Set Cover instance. Therefore, the Set Cover problem is at least as hard as the Vertex Cover problem.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2019-20
\item A vertex cover of a graph $G = (V, E)$ is a subset of vertices $S \subseteq V$ that includes at least one endpoint of every edge in $E$. Give a linear-time algorithm for the following task.\\
\textbf{Input:} An undirected tree $T = (V, E)$\\
\textbf{Output:} The size of the smallest vertex cover of $T$.
\mk{13} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

To find the size of the smallest vertex cover (Minimum Vertex Cover) in an undirected tree $T = (V, E)$ in linear time, we can use \textbf{Dynamic Programming (DP) on Trees}. 

While the Minimum Vertex Cover problem is NP-Hard for general graphs, the hierarchical structure of a tree allows us to solve it optimally in $O(|V|)$ time by converting it into a rooted tree and computing optimal substructures from the leaves up to the root.

\subsection*{1. Dynamic Programming Formulation}

We arbitrarily root the tree $T$ at any vertex (e.g., vertex $r$). For any subtree rooted at a node $u$, we define two states:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $DP[u][0]$: The size of the minimum vertex cover for the subtree rooted at $u$, given that $u$ is \textbf{not included} in the vertex cover.
    \item $DP[u][1]$: The size of the minimum vertex cover for the subtree rooted at $u$, given that $u$ \textbf{is included} in the vertex cover.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Transitions:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{If $u$ is not included ($DP[u][0]$):} To cover the edges between $u$ and its children, \textit{every} child $v$ of $u$ \textbf{must} be included in the vertex cover. Thus:
    \[
    DP[u][0] = \sum_{v \in \text{children}(u)} DP[v][1]
    \]
    
    \item \textbf{If $u$ is included ($DP[u][1]$):} The edges between $u$ and its children are already covered by $u$. Therefore, each child $v$ can independently either be included or not included in the vertex cover. We take the optimal (minimum) choice for each child. Since $u$ itself is included, we add 1:
    \[
    DP[u][1] = 1 + \sum_{v \in \text{children}(u)} \min(DP[v][0], DP[v][1])
    \]
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Base Case (Leaf Nodes):}
For any leaf node $l$:
\[
DP[l][0] = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad DP[l][1] = 1
\]
(Note: Our recurrence relation naturally handles leaf nodes since the sum over an empty set of children evaluates to $0$).

\vspace{1em}
\subsection*{2. Visualizing the DP State}

Consider the following simple tree rooted at node 1. The optimal vertex cover consists of the filled nodes $\{2, 3\}$, yielding a minimum vertex cover size of 2.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, level distance=1.5cm, sibling distance=2.5cm, thick]
    \node[circle, draw=cA, fill=white, minimum size=8mm, label=left:{Root}] (1) {1}
        child {node[circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!30, minimum size=8mm] (2) {2}
            child {node[circle, draw=cA, fill=white, minimum size=8mm] (4) {4}}
            child {node[circle, draw=cA, fill=white, minimum size=8mm] (5) {5}}
        }
        child {node[circle, draw=cA, fill=cA!30, minimum size=8mm] (3) {3}};

    % DP Table explanation
    \node[right=3cm of 1, anchor=north] (table) {
        \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
        \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|}
            \hline
            \textbf{Node $u$} & \textbf{$DP[u][0]$} & \textbf{$DP[u][1]$} & \textbf{Min VC Subtree} \\
            \hline
            4 (Leaf) & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
            5 (Leaf) & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
            3 (Leaf) & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
            2 & $1+1=2$ & $1+0+0=1$ & 1 \\
            1 (Root) & $1+1=2$ & $1+1+0=2$ & 2 \\
            \hline
        \end{tabular}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsection*{3. Algorithm}

The algorithm uses Depth-First Search (DFS) to process the tree in a post-order fashion, computing the values for the children before computing the values for the parent. We pass the `parent` parameter to avoid traversing backward up the undirected tree.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Minimum Vertex Cover on a Tree}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require An undirected tree $T=(V,E)$ represented as an adjacency list.
\Ensure The size of the minimum vertex cover.
\State Initialize $DP[u][0] \gets 0$ and $DP[u][1] \gets 0$ for all $u \in V$

\Procedure{DFS}{$u$, \textit{parent}}
    \State $DP[u][0] \gets 0$
    \State $DP[u][1] \gets 1$ \Comment{Cost of including node $u$ itself}
    
    \For{each neighbor $v$ of $u$ in $T$}
        \If{$v \neq parent$}
            \State \Call{DFS}{$v$, $u$} \Comment{Recursively compute for child}
            \State $DP[u][0] \gets DP[u][0] + DP[v][1]$
            \State $DP[u][1] \gets DP[u][1] + \min(DP[v][0], DP[v][1])$
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndProcedure

\State \Call{DFS}{$root$, $NULL$} \Comment{Arbitrarily pick any vertex as root, e.g., vertex 1}
\State \Return $\min(DP[root][0], DP[root][1])$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\subsection*{4. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$}. The algorithm traverses the tree using a standard Depth-First Search. Because $T$ is a tree, it has exactly $|V| - 1$ edges. Each edge is examined exactly twice (once from each endpoint), and the work done at each node (summing values from children) is proportional to its degree. The total sum of degrees in a tree is $2(|V| - 1)$. Therefore, the time complexity is strictly linear, bounded by $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$}. The space complexity is governed by two factors:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item The $DP$ table of dimensions $|V| \times 2$, which takes $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ space.
        \item The implicit call stack during the recursive DFS traversal. In the worst-case scenario (a skewed tree or linear chain), the recursion depth can reach $|V|$, taking $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ stack space.
    \end{enumerate}
    Thus, the overall space complexity is $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2020-21
\item The TAUTOLOGY problem asks if a given Boolean formula is true for all possible assignments to the Boolean variables. Prove or disprove that TAUTOLOGY is in co-NP.
\mk{6} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\textbf{Claim:} The statement is \textbf{True}. TAUTOLOGY is in co-NP.

We will strictly prove this claim by utilizing the formal definition of the complexity class co-NP and demonstrating the relationship between TAUTOLOGY and its complement.

\subsection*{1. Definition of the Class co-NP}
By definition in computational complexity theory, a decision problem (or language) $L$ belongs to the class \textbf{co-NP} if and only if its complement, denoted as $\overline{L}$, belongs to the class \textbf{NP}. 

The class NP contains all decision problems for which a "yes" answer can be verified in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine, given a valid, polynomial-sized certificate (or witness). Therefore, to prove that TAUTOLOGY $\in$ co-NP, we must demonstrate that the complement problem, $\overline{\text{TAUTOLOGY}}$, is in NP.

\subsection*{2. Analyzing the Complement: $\overline{\text{TAUTOLOGY}}$}
The TAUTOLOGY problem asks if a formula is true for \textit{all} possible assignments. Formally:
\[ \text{TAUTOLOGY} = \{ \phi \mid \phi \text{ is a Boolean formula such that } \forall \text{ assignments } x, \phi(x) = \text{True} \} \]

The complement of this problem, $\overline{\text{TAUTOLOGY}}$, asks whether a given Boolean formula is \textit{not} a tautology. A formula is not a tautology if there exists \textit{at least one} variable assignment that makes the formula evaluate to False. 
\[ \overline{\text{TAUTOLOGY}} = \{ \phi \mid \phi \text{ is a Boolean formula such that } \exists \text{ an assignment } x \text{ where } \phi(x) = \text{False} \} \]
This problem is universally known as the \textbf{FALSIFIABILITY} problem (or Non-Tautology).

\subsection*{3. Proving $\overline{\text{TAUTOLOGY}} \in \text{NP}$}
To prove that $\overline{\text{TAUTOLOGY}}$ is in NP, we must construct a polynomial-time verifier $V$ that takes an instance of the problem and a certificate $c$, and outputs "yes" if and only if the instance belongs to $\overline{\text{TAUTOLOGY}}$.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Instance:} A Boolean formula $\phi$ containing $n$ Boolean variables.
    \item \textbf{Certificate ($c$):} A specific truth assignment to the $n$ variables of $\phi$. Since there are $n$ variables, a truth assignment can be represented by an $n$-bit string. Thus, the size of the certificate is $O(n)$, which is polynomial in the size of the input formula $\phi$.
    \item \textbf{Verifier Algorithm ($V$):} 
    Given the formula $\phi$ and the assignment $c$, the verifier simply evaluates the boolean expression.
    
    \begin{algorithm}[H]
    \caption{Verifier for $\overline{\text{TAUTOLOGY}}$}
    \begin{algorithmic}[1]
    \Require Boolean formula $\phi$, truth assignment $c$
    \Ensure \textbf{Accept} if $\phi$ is falsified by $c$, \textbf{Reject} otherwise
    \State Evaluate the Boolean formula $\phi$ using the variable assignment $c$
    \If{the result of $\phi(c)$ is False}
        \State \Return \textbf{Accept}
    \Else
        \State \Return \textbf{Reject}
    \EndIf
    \end{algorithmic}
    \end{algorithm}
    
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity of Verifier:} Evaluating a Boolean formula $\phi$ given a complete truth assignment requires a single bottom-up pass of its abstract syntax tree (or parsing it left-to-right depending on the representation). The time taken is strictly proportional to the number of operators and variables in the formula. Hence, the evaluation takes deterministic $\mathcal{O}(|\phi|)$ time, which is linear (and thus polynomial) with respect to the input size.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{4. Conclusion}
We have shown that any "yes" instance of $\overline{\text{TAUTOLOGY}}$ can be verified in polynomial time using a polynomial-sized certificate (the falsifying assignment). Therefore, $\overline{\text{TAUTOLOGY}} \in \text{NP}$. 

By the foundational definition of co-NP ($L \in \text{co-NP} \iff \overline{L} \in \text{NP}$), since the complement of TAUTOLOGY is in NP, it rigorously follows that \textbf{TAUTOLOGY is in co-NP}.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item For a graph $G = (V, E)$, a set $S$ of vertices in $G$ is a \textit{vertex cover} if every edge $e$ in $G$ has at least one end in $S$. A set of vertices $I$ in $G$ is an \textit{independent set} if for every two vertices in $I$, there is no edge between them. Given a graph $G$ and a number $K$, the vertex cover problem asks if $G$ contains a vertex cover of size at most $K$. Given a graph $G$ and a number $K$, the independent set problem asks if $G$ contains an independent set of size at least $K$. Prove that the vertex cover problem is at least as hard as the independent set problem.
\mk{12} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

To prove that the \textbf{Vertex Cover (VC)} problem is at least as hard as the \textbf{Independent Set (IS)} problem, we must demonstrate a polynomial-time reduction from Independent Set to Vertex Cover, denoted as $\text{IS} \le_p \text{VC}$.

The fundamental insight behind this reduction is the complementary relationship between independent sets and vertex covers in any given graph. 

\subsection*{1. The Core Theorem}
\textbf{Theorem:} Let $G = (V, E)$ be an undirected graph, and let $S \subseteq V$. The set $S$ is an independent set if and only if its complement, $V \setminus S$, is a vertex cover.

\subsection*{2. Polynomial-Time Reduction Construction}
We want to transform an arbitrary instance of the Independent Set problem into an instance of the Vertex Cover problem.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Given (IS Instance):} A graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $K$. We are asking if $G$ has an independent set of size at least $K$.
    \item \textbf{Construct (VC Instance):} We pass the exact same graph $G' = G$, and we set the target vertex cover size to $K' = |V| - K$. We ask if $G'$ has a vertex cover of size at most $K'$.
\end{itemize}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Reduction from Independent Set to Vertex Cover}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Graph $G=(V,E)$, Integer $K$
\Ensure Vertex Cover Instance $(G', K')$
\State $G' \gets G$ \Comment{The graph structure remains identical}
\State $K' \gets |V| - K$ \Comment{Compute the complement size}
\State \Return $(G', K')$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\textbf{Time Complexity of Reduction:} Computing $|V| - K$ takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time. Passing the graph representation takes $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ time (or $\mathcal{O}(1)$ if passed by reference). Thus, the reduction takes strictly polynomial time.

\vspace{1em}
\subsection*{3. Visualizing the Relationship}

Consider the graph below. The vertices in blue form an Independent Set of size 2. Their complement, the vertices in red, forms a Vertex Cover of size $4 - 2 = 2$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, node distance=2cm, thick]
    % Left Graph: Independent Set
    \begin{scope}[xshift=-3.5cm]
        \node[above] at (1, 2) {\textbf{Independent Set} $S$ ($|S|=2$)};
        
        \node[circle, draw=blue, fill=blue!20, minimum size=8mm] (v1) at (0, 0) {$v_1$};
        \node[circle, draw=black, fill=white, minimum size=8mm] (v2) at (2, 0) {$v_2$};
        \node[circle, draw=blue, fill=blue!20, minimum size=8mm] (v3) at (2, -2) {$v_3$};
        \node[circle, draw=black, fill=white, minimum size=8mm] (v4) at (0, -2) {$v_4$};

        \draw (v1) -- (v2);
        \draw (v2) -- (v3);
        \draw (v3) -- (v4);
        \draw (v4) -- (v1);
        \draw (v2) -- (v4);
    \end{scope}

    % Arrow
    \draw[<->, very thick, cD] (0, -1) -- (1, -1) node[midway, above] {Complement};

    % Right Graph: Vertex Cover
    \begin{scope}[xshift=2cm]
        \node[above] at (1, 2) {\textbf{Vertex Cover} $V \setminus S$ ($|V \setminus S|=2$)};
        
        \node[circle, draw=black, fill=white, minimum size=8mm] (u1) at (0, 0) {$v_1$};
        \node[circle, draw=red, fill=red!20, minimum size=8mm] (u2) at (2, 0) {$v_2$};
        \node[circle, draw=black, fill=white, minimum size=8mm] (u3) at (2, -2) {$v_3$};
        \node[circle, draw=red, fill=red!20, minimum size=8mm] (u4) at (0, -2) {$v_4$};

        \draw (u1) -- (u2);
        \draw (u2) -- (u3);
        \draw (u3) -- (u4);
        \draw (u4) -- (u1);
        \draw (u2) -- (u4);
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsection*{4. Proof of Correctness}

To complete the proof, we must show that $G$ has an independent set of size at least $K$ \textbf{if and only if} $G$ has a vertex cover of size at most $|V| - K$.

\subsubsection*{Forward Direction: $\text{IS} \implies \text{VC}$}
\textbf{Claim:} If $S \subseteq V$ is an independent set of size $\ge K$, then $C = V \setminus S$ is a vertex cover of size $\le |V| - K$.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Let $S$ be an independent set such that $|S| \ge K$. Let $C = V \setminus S$.
    \item Consider any arbitrary edge $e = (u, v) \in E$.
    \item By the definition of an independent set, there are no edges between any two vertices in $S$. Therefore, it is impossible for both $u$ and $v$ to be in $S$.
    \item This implies that at least one of $u$ or $v$ must not be in $S$. 
    \item Consequently, at least one of $u$ or $v$ must be in the complement set $C = V \setminus S$.
    \item Since this holds for every edge in $E$, $C$ is a valid vertex cover.
    \item The size of $C$ is $|C| = |V| - |S|$. Since $|S| \ge K$, we have $|C| \le |V| - K$.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Backward Direction: $\text{VC} \implies \text{IS}$}
\textbf{Claim:} If $C \subseteq V$ is a vertex cover of size $\le |V| - K$, then $S = V \setminus C$ is an independent set of size $\ge K$.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Let $C$ be a vertex cover such that $|C| \le |V| - K$. Let $S = V \setminus C$.
    \item Assume, for the sake of contradiction, that $S$ is \textit{not} an independent set.
    \item This would mean there exists an edge $e = (u, v) \in E$ such that both $u \in S$ and $v \in S$.
    \item If both $u$ and $v$ are in $S$, then neither $u$ nor $v$ is in $C$ (since $S$ and $C$ are disjoint complements).
    \item If neither endpoint of $e$ is in $C$, then $C$ fails to cover edge $e$, which contradicts the premise that $C$ is a valid vertex cover.
    \item Thus, our assumption is false. There can be no edges between vertices in $S$, making $S$ a valid independent set.
    \item The size of $S$ is $|S| = |V| - |C|$. Since $|C| \le |V| - K$, we have $|S| \ge |V| - (|V| - K) = K$.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{5. Conclusion}
We have provided a valid polynomial-time reduction from the Independent Set problem to the Vertex Cover problem. We rigorously proved that a "yes" instance of IS directly maps to a "yes" instance of VC and vice versa. Therefore, the Vertex Cover problem is at least as hard as the Independent Set problem.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What do you mean by $X \leq_p Y$? Show that the vertex cover problem $\leq_p$ set cover problem. \mk{3+7}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{Part 1: Meaning of $X \leq_p Y$}

The notation $\textbf{X} \leq_p \textbf{Y}$ denotes a \textbf{Polynomial-Time Reduction} from decision problem $X$ to decision problem $Y$. It means that problem $X$ is "no harder than" problem $Y$, up to a polynomial factor. 

Formally, $X \leq_p Y$ if there exists a deterministic polynomial-time computable function $f: \{0, 1\}^* \to \{0, 1\}^*$ such that for every input instance $x$:
\[
x \in X \iff f(x) \in Y
\]
In practical terms, this means that if we have a black-box algorithm (an oracle) that solves problem $Y$, we can use it to solve problem $X$ in polynomial time by taking an instance $x$ of $X$, transforming it into an instance $f(x)$ of $Y$ in polynomial time, and returning the exact same "yes" or "no" answer.

\vspace{1em}
\subsection*{Part 2: Proving Vertex Cover $\leq_p$ Set Cover}

To prove that the Vertex Cover (VC) problem is polynomial-time reducible to the Set Cover (SC) problem, we must construct a polynomial-time mapping from any VC instance to an SC instance, and prove that the VC instance has a valid cover if and only if the mapped SC instance has a valid cover.

\subsubsection*{1. Reduction Construction}

Let us take an arbitrary instance of the Vertex Cover problem:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Input:} A graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k$.
    \item \textbf{Goal:} Does there exist a subset $V' \subseteq V$ of size at most $k$ such that every edge in $E$ has at least one endpoint in $V'$?
\end{itemize}

We construct an equivalent instance of the Set Cover problem $(U, \mathcal{S}, k')$ as follows:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Universe ($U$):} We need to cover all edges, so the universe of elements is the set of all edges in $G$. Thus, $U = E$.
    \item \textbf{Subsets ($\mathcal{S}$):} A vertex in $G$ "covers" its incident edges. For each vertex $v \in V$, we create a subset $S_v$ containing exactly the edges incident to $v$. Thus, the collection of subsets is $\mathcal{S} = \{S_v \mid v \in V\}$.
