    \item \textbf{Parameter ($k'$):} The maximum allowed number of sets is equal to the maximum allowed size of the vertex cover. Thus, $k' = k$.
\end{itemize}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Reduction Algorithm ($f$)}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Graph $G=(V,E)$, Integer $k$
\Ensure Set Cover Instance $(U, \mathcal{S}, k')$
\State $U \gets E$
\State $\mathcal{S} \gets \emptyset$
\For{each $v \in V$}
    \State $S_v \gets \{ e \in E \mid e \text{ is incident to } v \}$
    \State $\mathcal{S} \gets \mathcal{S} \cup \{S_v\}$
\EndFor
\State $k' \gets k$
\State \Return $(U, \mathcal{S}, k')$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\textbf{Time Complexity Analysis:} Constructing the universe takes $\mathcal{O}(|E|)$ time. Constructing the subsets requires iterating over all vertices and their adjacent edges, taking $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ time. The overall transformation takes strictly polynomial time $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$.

\subsubsection*{2. Visualization of the Mapping}

Consider the simple graph and its corresponding Set Cover instance generated by our reduction:

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, node distance=2.5cm, thick]
    % Graph Nodes
    \node[circle, draw=cD, fill=cD!10, minimum size=8mm] (v1) at (0, 2) {$v_1$};
    \node[circle, draw=cD, fill=cD!10, minimum size=8mm] (v2) at (-1.5, 0) {$v_2$};
    \node[circle, draw=cD, fill=cD!10, minimum size=8mm] (v3) at (1.5, 0) {$v_3$};

    % Graph Edges
    \draw (v1) -- (v2) node[midway, left=2pt, text=blue] {$e_1$};
    \draw (v2) -- (v3) node[midway, below=2pt, text=blue] {$e_2$};
    \draw (v1) -- (v3) node[midway, right=2pt, text=blue] {$e_3$};

    \node[above] at (0, 3) {\textbf{Graph Instance $G$}};

    % Arrow
    \draw[->, very thick, cD] (2.5, 1) -- (4.5, 1) node[midway, above] {$f(x)$};

    % Set Cover Nodes
    \node[right, align=left] at (5, 2.5) {\textbf{Set Cover Instance}};
    \node[right, align=left] at (5, 1.5) {Universe $U = \{ \textcolor{blue}{e_1}, \textcolor{blue}{e_2}, \textcolor{blue}{e_3} \}$};
    \node[right, align=left] at (5, 0.5) {$S_{v_1} = \{ \textcolor{blue}{e_1}, \textcolor{blue}{e_3} \}$};
    \node[right, align=left] at (5, -0.2) {$S_{v_2} = \{ \textcolor{blue}{e_1}, \textcolor{blue}{e_2} \}$};
    \node[right, align=left] at (5, -0.9) {$S_{v_3} = \{ \textcolor{blue}{e_2}, \textcolor{blue}{e_3} \}$};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{3. Proof of Correctness}

We must mathematically prove that $G$ has a vertex cover of size $\le k$ if and only if $(U, \mathcal{S}, k')$ has a set cover of size $\le k'$.

\textbf{Forward Direction ($\implies$):}
Suppose $G$ has a vertex cover $V' \subseteq V$ where $|V'| \le k$. 
\begin{enumerate}
    \item We choose the corresponding subsets for our set cover: $\mathcal{C} = \{S_v \mid v \in V'\}$.
    \item By the definition of a vertex cover, every edge $e = (u, w) \in E$ is incident to at least one vertex in $V'$ (either $u \in V'$ or $w \in V'$).
    \item Consequently, in our SC instance, the element $e$ is contained in at least one subset in $\mathcal{C}$ (either $S_u \in \mathcal{C}$ or $S_w \in \mathcal{C}$).
    \item Thus, $\mathcal{C}$ covers all elements in $U$. Since $|\mathcal{C}| = |V'| \le k$, we have a valid set cover of size $\le k'$.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Backward Direction ($\impliedby$):}
Suppose the constructed instance has a set cover $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{S}$ where $|\mathcal{C}| \le k'$.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item We select the corresponding vertices for our vertex cover: $V' = \{v \mid S_v \in \mathcal{C}\}$.
    \item Because $\mathcal{C}$ is a valid set cover, every element $e \in U$ is present in at least one subset $S_v \in \mathcal{C}$.
    \item Since $U = E$, this means every edge $e \in E$ is in some set $S_v \in \mathcal{C}$.
    \item By our construction, $e \in S_v$ if and only if edge $e$ is incident to vertex $v$ in $G$.
    \item Thus, every edge $e \in E$ is incident to at least one vertex in $V'$. Since $|V'| = |\mathcal{C}| \le k'$, we have a valid vertex cover of size $\le k$.
\end{enumerate}

Since the reduction operates in polynomial time and the instances are logically equivalent, we have definitively proven that $\text{Vertex Cover} \leq_p \text{Set Cover}$.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Given clauses $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k$ each of length 3 over Boolean variables (3-SAT), and a graph $G$ with number $K$ (Vertex Cover asks if $G$ has a vertex cover of size $\leq K$). Prove that Vertex Cover is at least as hard as 3-SAT. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2016--17}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

To prove that Vertex Cover is at least as hard as 3-SAT, we will show that 3-SAT is polynomial-time reducible to Vertex Cover ($3\text{-SAT} \le_p \text{VERTEX-COVER}$). This means we can transform any instance of 3-SAT into an instance of Vertex Cover in polynomial time, such that the 3-SAT instance has a solution if and only if the corresponding Vertex Cover instance has a solution. Since 3-SAT is NP-complete, this will prove that Vertex Cover is NP-hard.

\subsection*{The Reduction}

Let $\phi$ be an arbitrary instance of 3-SAT. The formula $\phi$ is a conjunction of $k$ clauses, $\phi = C_1 \land C_2 \land \ldots \land C_k$, over $n$ Boolean variables, $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$. Each clause $C_j$ is a disjunction of three literals, where a literal is a variable $x_i$ or its negation $\neg x_i$.

We construct an instance of Vertex Cover, which consists of a graph $G=(V,E)$ and an integer $K$, from the 3-SAT formula $\phi$ as follows:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Variable Gadgets:} For each variable $x_i$ in $\phi$, we create two vertices, $v_i$ and $\bar{v}_i$, representing the literals $x_i$ and $\neg x_i$ respectively. We add an edge $(v_i, \bar{v}_i)$ between them. This is done for all $i = 1, \ldots, n$. These are the \textbf{variable edges}.

    \item \textbf{Clause Gadgets:} For each clause $C_j = (l_{j1} \lor l_{j2} \lor l_{j3})$ in $\phi$, we create three new vertices, $c_{j1}, c_{j2}, c_{j3}$. These three vertices are connected to form a triangle, i.e., we add the edges $(c_{j1}, c_{j2}), (c_{j2}, c_{j3}),$ and $(c_{j3}, c_{j1})$. This is done for all $j = 1, \ldots, k$. These are the \textbf{clause edges}.

    \item \textbf{Communication Edges:} We connect the variable gadgets to the clause gadgets. For each clause $C_j = (l_{j1} \lor l_{j2} \lor l_{j3})$, we add an edge between the clause vertex $c_{jp}$ and the vertex in the variable gadgets corresponding to the literal $l_{jp}$. For example, if $l_{jp}$ is the literal $x_i$, we add an edge $(c_{jp}, v_i)$. If $l_{jp}$ is the literal $\neg x_i$, we add an edge $(c_{jp}, \bar{v}_i)$. This is done for all three literals in every clause.

    \item \textbf{Target Size K:} We set the target size for the vertex cover to be $K = n + 2k$.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Example Construction:}
Consider the 3-SAT formula $\phi = (x_1 \lor \neg x_2 \lor x_3) \land (\neg x_1 \lor x_2 \lor \neg x_3)$.
Here, $n=3$ variables ($x_1, x_2, x_3$) and $k=2$ clauses. The target vertex cover size is $K = n + 2k = 3 + 2(2) = 7$. The constructed graph is shown below.

\begin{figure}[H]
    \centering
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        var/.style={circle, draw=blue!60, fill=blue!20, thick, minimum size=6mm},
        clause/.style={circle, draw=red!60, fill=red!20, thick, minimum size=6mm},
        vlabel/.style={above=2mm},
        clabel/.style={below=2mm}
    ]
    % Variable Gadgets
    \node[var] (v1) at (-4, 2) {$v_1$};
    \node[var] (vb1) at (-2, 2) {$\bar{v}_1$};
    \node[var] (v2) at (0, 2) {$v_2$};
    \node[var] (vb2) at (2, 2) {$\bar{v}_2$};
    \node[var] (v3) at (4, 2) {$v_3$};
    \node[var] (vb3) at (6, 2) {$\bar{v}_3$};
    
    \path[-, thick, blue!80] (v1) edge node[vlabel] {$x_1$} (vb1);
    \path[-, thick, blue!80] (v2) edge node[vlabel] {$x_2$} (vb2);
    \path[-, thick, blue!80] (v3) edge node[vlabel] {$x_3$} (vb3);

    % Clause Gadgets
    % C1 = (x1 v ~x2 v x3)
    \node[clause] (c11) at (-3, -1) {$c_{11}$};
    \node[clause] (c12) at (-1, -1) {$c_{12}$};
    \node[clause] (c13) at (-2, -2.5) {$c_{13}$};
    
    \path[-, thick, red!80] (c11) edge (c12);
    \path[-, thick, red!80] (c12) edge (c13);
    \path[-, thick, red!80] (c13) edge (c11);
    \node at (-2, -3.2) {$C_1: x_1 \lor \neg x_2 \lor x_3$};

    % C2 = (~x1 v x2 v ~x3)
    \node[clause] (c21) at (3, -1) {$c_{21}$};
    \node[clause] (c22) at (5, -1) {$c_{22}$};
    \node[clause] (c23) at (4, -2.5) {$c_{23}$};

    \path[-, thick, red!80] (c21) edge (c22);
    \path[-, thick, red!80] (c22) edge (c23);
    \path[-, thick, red!80] (c23) edge (c21);
    \node at (4, -3.2) {$C_2: \neg x_1 \lor x_2 \lor \neg x_3$};

    % Communication Edges
    \path[-, thick, gray, dashed] (c11) edge (v1);   % C1, l1=x1
    \path[-, thick, gray, dashed] (c12) edge (vb2);  % C1, l2=~x2
    \path[-, thick, gray, dashed] (c13) edge (v3);   % C1, l3=x3

    \path[-, thick, gray, dashed] (c21) edge (vb1);  % C2, l1=~x1
    \path[-, thick, gray, dashed] (c22) edge (v2);   % C2, l2=x2
    \path[-, thick, gray, dashed] (c23) edge (vb3);  % C2, l3=~x3

    \end{tikzpicture}
    \caption{Graph constructed for $\phi = (x_1 \lor \neg x_2 \lor x_3) \land (\neg x_1 \lor x_2 \lor \neg x_3)$.}
\end{figure}

\subsection*{Polynomial-Time Transformation}
The construction of the graph $G$ and integer $K$ can be performed in polynomial time with respect to the size of the 3-SAT formula $\phi$. Let $n$ be the number of variables and $k$ be the number of clauses.
\begin{itemize}
    \item The number of vertices in $G$ is $|V| = 2n + 3k$.
    \item The number of edges in $G$ is $|E| = n$ (variable edges) + $3k$ (clause edges) + $3k$ (communication edges) = $n + 6k$.
\end{itemize}
Both $|V|$ and $|E|$ are linear in $n$ and $k$, so the construction is efficient and can be completed in polynomial time.

\subsection*{Proof of Correctness}
We now prove that $\phi$ is satisfiable if and only if $G$ has a vertex cover of size at most $K = n+2k$.

\subsubsection*{($\Rightarrow$) If $\phi$ is satisfiable, then $G$ has a vertex cover of size $K$.}
Assume $\phi$ is satisfiable. Let $\mathcal{A}$ be a satisfying truth assignment for the variables $x_1, \ldots, x_n$. We construct a set of vertices $VC \subseteq V$ and show that it is a vertex cover of size $K$.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{From variables:} For each variable $x_i$: if $\mathcal{A}$ sets $x_i$ to TRUE, we add vertex $v_i$ to $VC$. If $\mathcal{A}$ sets $x_i$ to FALSE, we add vertex $\bar{v}_i$ to $VC$. This adds exactly $n$ vertices to $VC$. This choice ensures that all $n$ variable edges $(v_i, \bar{v}_i)$ are covered, as one endpoint of each edge is in $VC$.

    \item \textbf{From clauses:} For each clause $C_j = (l_{j1} \lor l_{j2} \lor l_{j3})$: since $\mathcal{A}$ is a satisfying assignment, at least one of its literals must be true. Let's pick one such true literal, say $l_{jp}$. The vertex corresponding to this literal is already in $VC$ by step 1. To cover the internal edges of the clause gadget (the triangle), we add the other two vertices from the clause gadget to $VC$. That is, we add the two vertices from $\{c_{j1}, c_{j2}, c_{j3}\}$ that are not $c_{jp}$. This adds exactly $2$ vertices to $VC$ for each clause, for a total of $2k$ vertices.
\end{enumerate}

The total size of the constructed set $VC$ is $|VC| = n + 2k = K$. Now we must verify that $VC$ is indeed a vertex cover for $G$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Variable edges $(v_i, \bar{v}_i)$:} Covered by step 1, as for each $i$, either $v_i$ or $\bar{v}_i$ is in $VC$.
    \item \textbf{Clause edges $(c_{ja}, c_{jb})$:} For each clause $C_j$, we added two of its three vertices to $VC$. Any edge in the triangle has at least one of its endpoints in $VC$. So all clause edges are covered.
    \item \textbf{Communication edges $(c_{jp}, u_p)$:} Here $u_p$ is the vertex corresponding to literal $l_{jp}$. Consider such an edge for a clause $C_j$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item If $l_{jp}$ is the true literal we picked for clause $C_j$ in step 2, then its corresponding vertex $u_p$ was added to $VC$ in step 1. Thus, the edge is covered.
        \item If $l_{jp}$ is not the chosen true literal, then its corresponding clause vertex $c_{jp}$ was added to $VC$ in step 2. Thus, the edge is covered.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}
All edges in $E$ are covered, so $VC$ is a vertex cover of size $K$.

\subsubsection*{($\Leftarrow$) If $G$ has a vertex cover of size $\le K$, then $\phi$ is satisfiable.}
Assume there exists a vertex cover $VC'$ for $G$ with $|VC'| \le K = n+2k$.

First, observe the structure of $G$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item To cover the $n$ disjoint variable edges $(v_i, \bar{v}_i)$, any vertex cover must include at least one vertex from each pair $\{v_i, \bar{v}_i\}$. This requires at least $n$ vertices.
    \item To cover the $3k$ edges of the $k$ disjoint clause triangles, any vertex cover must include at least two vertices from each triple $\{c_{j1}, c_{j2}, c_{j3}\}$. This requires at least $2k$ vertices.
\end{itemize}
Since the variable gadgets and clause gadgets are disjoint, any vertex cover for $G$ must have a size of at least $n+2k$.
Given $|VC'| \le K = n+2k$, it must be that $|VC'| = K$.
Furthermore, to achieve this minimum size, $VC'$ must contain:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Exactly one} vertex from each pair $\{v_i, \bar{v}_i\}$ for $i=1, \ldots, n$.
    \item \textbf{Exactly two} vertices from each triple $\{c_{j1}, c_{j2}, c_{j3}\}$ for $j=1, \ldots, k$.
\end{itemize}

We can now define a truth assignment $\mathcal{A}$ for variables $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ based on $VC'$:
For each $i=1, \ldots, n$:
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $v_i \in VC'$, set $x_i = \text{TRUE}$.
    \item If $\bar{v}_i \in VC'$, set $x_i = \text{FALSE}$.
\end{itemize}
Since exactly one of $\{v_i, \bar{v}_i\}$ is in $VC'$, this assignment is well-defined.

We now show that this assignment $\mathcal{A}$ satisfies $\phi$. Consider an arbitrary clause $C_j = (l_{j1} \lor l_{j2} \lor l_{j3})$.
The vertex cover $VC'$ contains exactly two vertices from $\{c_{j1}, c_{j2}, c_{j3}\}$. Let $c_{jp}$ be the single vertex from this triple that is \textbf{not} in $VC'$.
Consider the communication edge $(c_{jp}, u_p)$, where $u_p$ is the vertex corresponding to the literal $l_{jp}$.
Since $VC'$ is a vertex cover, this edge must be covered. As $c_{jp} \notin VC'$, its other endpoint $u_p$ must be in $VC'$.
By our definition of the assignment $\mathcal{A}$, if the vertex $u_p$ is in $VC'$, the literal $l_{jp}$ is assigned a TRUE value.
For example:
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $l_{jp} = x_i$, then $u_p = v_i$. Since $v_i \in VC'$, our assignment sets $x_i=$ TRUE, so $l_{jp}$ is true.
    \item If $l_{jp} = \neg x_i$, then $u_p = \bar{v}_i$. Since $\bar{v}_i \in VC'$, our assignment sets $x_i=$ FALSE, so $l_{jp}=\neg x_i$ is true.
\end{itemize}
In either case, the literal $l_{jp}$ is true under $\mathcal{A}$. Therefore, the clause $C_j$ is satisfied. Since this holds for any clause $C_j$, the entire formula $\phi$ is satisfied by $\mathcal{A}$.

\subsection*{Conclusion}
We have shown a polynomial-time reduction from any instance of 3-SAT to an instance of Vertex Cover. The 3-SAT formula is satisfiable if and only if the constructed graph has a vertex cover of size $K = n+2k$. Since 3-SAT is a known NP-complete problem, this reduction proves that Vertex Cover is NP-hard, meaning it is at least as hard as 3-SAT.

\end{tcolorbox}


\item Explain the term ``reducibility''. If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are two languages such that $L_1 \leq_p L_2$, show that $L_2 \in P$ implies $L_1 \in P$. If any NP-complete problem is polynomial-time solvable, prove that $P = NP$. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2015--16}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{1. The Term ``Reducibility''}

In computational complexity theory, \textbf{reducibility} (specifically, polynomial-time many-one reducibility or Karp reducibility) is a method to formally relate the difficulty of two computational problems. 

We say that a language $L_1 \subseteq \Sigma^*$ is \textbf{polynomial-time reducible} to a language $L_2 \subseteq \Sigma^*$, denoted as $L_1 \leq_p L_2$, if there exists a polynomial-time computable function $f: \Sigma^* \to \Sigma^*$ such that for every input string $x \in \Sigma^*$:
\[ x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2 \]

Intuitively, $L_1 \leq_p L_2$ means that $L_1$ is ``no harder than'' $L_2$ (up to a polynomial factor), because any algorithm that solves $L_2$ can be used as a subroutine to solve $L_1$. The function $f$ acts as a translator, converting instances of $L_1$ into equivalent instances of $L_2$ efficiently.

\vspace{1em}
\subsection*{2. Proof: $L_1 \leq_p L_2$ and $L_2 \in P \implies L_1 \in P$}

\textbf{Theorem:} If a language $L_1$ is polynomial-time reducible to a language $L_2$, and $L_2$ is solvable in polynomial time, then $L_1$ is also solvable in polynomial time.

\begin{proof}
Let us break down the given premises:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Since $L_2 \in P$, there exists a deterministic algorithm $A_2$ that decides $L_2$ in polynomial time. Let its time complexity be bounded by $O(n^k)$, where $n$ is the length of the input and $k \ge 1$ is a constant.
    \item Since $L_1 \leq_p L_2$, there exists a polynomial-time computable reduction function $f$. Let $A_f$ be the algorithm computing $f$. The time taken by $A_f$ on an input $x$ of length $n = |x|$ is bounded by $O(n^c)$ for some constant $c \ge 1$.
\end{enumerate}

We must construct a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm $A_1$ for $L_1$. We define $A_1$ on input $x$ as follows:
\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Algorithm $A_1$ for $L_1$}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} A string $x \in \Sigma^*$
\State Compute $y = f(x)$ using algorithm $A_f$.
\State Run algorithm $A_2$ on input $y$.
\If{$A_2(y)$ accepts}
    \State \Return \text{ACCEPT}
\Else
    \State \Return \text{REJECT}
\EndIf
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\textbf{Correctness:} By the definition of reducibility, $x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2$. Since $A_2$ correctly decides $L_2$, $A_1$ correctly decides $L_1$.

\textbf{Complexity Analysis:} Let $n = |x|$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Step 2:} Computing $y = f(x)$ takes $O(n^c)$ time.
    \item A crucial observation is that a Turing machine can only write one symbol per step. Therefore, the length of the output string $y$ cannot exceed the number of steps taken to compute it. Thus, $|y| \leq O(n^c)$.
    \item \textbf{Step 3:} Running $A_2$ on $y$ takes time proportional to $O(|y|^k)$. Substituting the upper bound for $|y|$, the time taken is $O((n^c)^k) = O(n^{ck})$.
\end{itemize}
The total running time of $A_1$ is the sum of the time for the reduction and the time to solve the transformed instance:
\[ T(n) = O(n^c) + O(n^{ck}) = O(n^{ck}) \]
Since $c$ and $k$ are constants, $ck$ is a constant. Therefore, $T(n)$ is a polynomial in $n$. This proves that there exists a polynomial-time algorithm for $L_1$, meaning $L_1 \in P$.
\end{proof}

\vspace{1em}
\subsection*{3. Proof: If any NP-Complete problem is in P, then $P = NP$}

\textbf{Theorem:} Let $L_{NPC}$ be an NP-complete problem. If $L_{NPC} \in P$, then $P = NP$.

\begin{proof}
To prove that $P = NP$, we must show that $P \subseteq NP$ and $NP \subseteq P$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Part 1: $P \subseteq NP$.} This is trivially true by definition. Any problem solvable in deterministic polynomial time ($P$) can also be solved (or verified) in non-deterministic polynomial time ($NP$).
    
    \item \textbf{Part 2: $NP \subseteq P$.} Let $L$ be an arbitrary language in $NP$. We need to show that $L \in P$.
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item By the definition of NP-Completeness, since $L_{NPC}$ is NP-complete, every problem in $NP$ is polynomial-time reducible to $L_{NPC}$. Therefore, $L \leq_p L_{NPC}$.
        \item We are given the premise that $L_{NPC} \in P$.
        \item Using the theorem proven in Section 2, if $L \leq_p L_{NPC}$ and $L_{NPC} \in P$, it strictly implies that $L \in P$.
    \end{enumerate}
    Since $L$ was an arbitrary problem in $NP$, we conclude that every problem in $NP$ is also in $P$. Thus, $NP \subseteq P$.
\end{itemize}

Since $P \subseteq NP$ and $NP \subseteq P$, we conclude that $P = NP$. This formally demonstrates why finding a polynomial-time algorithm for even a single NP-complete problem (like 3-SAT or Vertex Cover) would automatically yield polynomial-time algorithms for thousands of notoriously hard problems.
\end{proof}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Define the classes P, NP, and NP-complete. What are the steps to prove a problem $X$ is NP-complete? Prove that the decision version of the independent set problem is NP-complete using a reduction from 3-CNF-SAT. Give an intuitive sketch of the proof of the Cook-Levin theorem (CIRCUIT-SAT is NP-complete). \mk{4+4+12+5}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{1. Definitions of Complexity Classes}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{The Class P:} P is the class of decision problems that can be solved by a \textit{deterministic} Turing machine in polynomial time. These are the problems considered computationally tractable. For any problem in P, there exists an algorithm with a worst-case time complexity of $O(n^k)$, where $n$ is the input size and $k$ is a constant.
    
    \item \textbf{The Class NP:} NP (Nondeterministic Polynomial time) is the class of decision problems for which a "yes" answer can be \textit{verified} by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time. Equivalently, it is the class of problems solvable by a \textit{non-deterministic} Turing machine in polynomial time. If the answer is "yes," there exists a polynomial-size certificate (or proof) that a verifier can check efficiently.
    
    \item \textbf{The Class NP-complete (NPC):} A decision problem $X$ is NP-complete if it satisfies two conditions:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $X \in \text{NP}$ (it is verifiable in polynomial time).
        \item $X$ is NP-hard, meaning every problem $L \in \text{NP}$ is polynomial-time reducible to $X$ ($L \leq_p X$).
    \end{enumerate}
    NP-complete problems are the "hardest" problems in NP. If any NP-complete problem can be solved in polynomial time, then $\text{P} = \text{NP}$.
\end{itemize}

\subsection*{2. Steps to Prove a Problem $X$ is NP-Complete}

To rigorously prove that a problem $X$ is NP-complete, the following sequence of steps must be executed:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Prove $X \in \text{NP}$:} Describe a polynomial-size certificate for a "yes" instance of the problem, and construct a deterministic algorithm that verifies this certificate in polynomial time.
    \item \textbf{Select a known NP-complete problem $Y$:} Choose a well-known NP-complete problem (such as 3-SAT, Vertex Cover, Clique) that has structural similarities to $X$.
    \item \textbf{Construct a reduction function $f$:} Design an algorithm that transforms any arbitrary instance of $Y$ into an instance of $X$.
    \item \textbf{Prove Polynomial Time:} Demonstrate that the transformation function $f$ executes in time bounded by a polynomial in the size of the input.
    \item \textbf{Prove Correctness:} Show that the reduction preserves the outcome, meaning the instance of $Y$ is a "yes" instance if and only if the transformed instance $f(Y)$ is a "yes" instance of $X$ ($y \in Y \iff f(y) \in X$).
\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{3. Proof: Independent Set is NP-Complete}

\textbf{Problem Statement:} Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $K$, does there exist a subset $V' \subseteq V$ such that $|V'| \geq K$ and no two vertices in $V'$ are connected by an edge?

\textbf{Step 1: Independent Set (IS) $\in$ NP}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textit{Certificate:} A subset of vertices $V' \subseteq V$.
    \item \textit{Verifier:} Count the number of vertices in $V'$ to check if $|V'| \geq K$. Then, for every pair of vertices $u, v \in V'$, verify that $(u, v) \notin E$. This takes $O(|V'|^2)$ time, which is strictly polynomial with respect to the input size. Thus, IS $\in$ NP.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Step 2 \& 3: Reduction from 3-CNF-SAT}
We reduce 3-CNF-SAT to Independent Set. Let $\phi = C_1 \land C_2 \land \dots \land C_k$ be a boolean formula in 3-CNF format with $k$ clauses. Each clause $C_r = (l_{r1} \lor l_{r2} \lor l_{r3})$ contains exactly 3 literals.
We construct an instance $(G, K)$ of IS as follows:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Vertices:} For each clause $C_r = (l_{r1} \lor l_{r2} \lor l_{r3})$, create three vertices $v_{r1}, v_{r2}, v_{r3}$. Total vertices $|V| = 3k$.
    \item \textbf{Clause Edges:} Connect the three vertices of each clause to form a triangle. This ensures at most one vertex per clause can be chosen in an independent set.
    \item \textbf{Conflict Edges:} Add an edge between any two vertices $v_{ri}$ and $v_{sj}$ if they represent contradictory literals (i.e., one is $x$ and the other is $\neg x$).
    \item \textbf{Target Size:} Set $K = k$ (the number of clauses).
\end{itemize}

\begin{figure}[H]
    \centering
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        node style/.style={circle, draw=cD, fill=cD!10, thick, minimum size=7mm},
        conflict/.style={thick, dashed, red},
        clause edge/.style={thick, black}
    ]
    % Clause 1
    \node[node style] (v11) at (-3, 0) {$x_1$};
    \node[node style] (v12) at (-5, -3) {$\neg x_2$};
    \node[node style] (v13) at (-1, -3) {$x_3$};
    
    \draw[clause edge] (v11) -- (v12) -- (v13) -- (v11);
    \node[above] at (-3, 0.5) {$C_1: (x_1 \lor \neg x_2 \lor x_3)$};

    % Clause 2
    \node[node style] (v21) at (3, 0) {$\neg x_1$};
    \node[node style] (v22) at (1, -3) {$x_2$};
    \node[node style] (v23) at (5, -3) {$\neg x_3$};

    \draw[clause edge] (v21) -- (v22) -- (v23) -- (v21);
    \node[above] at (3, 0.5) {$C_2: (\neg x_1 \lor x_2 \lor \neg x_3)$};

    % Conflict Edges
    \draw[conflict] (v11) edge[bend left=20] node[midway, above, text=red] {conflict} (v21);
    \draw[conflict] (v12) edge[bend right=20] node[midway, below, text=red] {conflict} (v22);
    \draw[conflict] (v13) edge[bend left=20] node[midway, below, text=red] {conflict} (v23);

    \end{tikzpicture}
    \caption{Reduction graph for $\phi = (x_1 \lor \neg x_2 \lor x_3) \land (\neg x_1 \lor x_2 \lor \neg x_3)$. Triangles represent clauses; dashed red lines are conflict edges.}
\end{figure}

\textbf{Step 4: Polynomial Time Bound}
The constructed graph has exactly $3k$ vertices. The number of edges is at most $3k$ (clause edges) plus $O(k^2)$ (conflict edges). Building the graph takes $O(k^2)$ time, which is polynomial in the size of the boolean formula $\phi$.

\textbf{Step 5: Correctness ($\phi \text{ is satisfiable} \iff G \text{ has an IS of size } \ge k$)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item ($\Rightarrow$) Suppose $\phi$ is satisfiable. There is a truth assignment making at least one literal true in every clause. Select exactly one true literal's vertex from each clause's triangle. Because we chose exactly one vertex per clause, we have selected $k$ vertices. Since they evaluate to true simultaneously under a valid assignment, no chosen pair can represent a variable and its negation. Thus, no conflict edges exist between chosen vertices. They form an Independent Set of size $k$.
    \item ($\Leftarrow$) Suppose $G$ has an independent set $V'$ of size $\ge k$. Since each clause is a triangle, at most one vertex per clause can be in $V'$. To achieve size $k$, $V'$ must contain exactly one vertex from each of the $k$ clauses. We assign TRUE to the literals corresponding to the vertices in $V'$. Because no two vertices in $V'$ have a conflict edge, we will never assign TRUE to both $x$ and $\neg x$. This assignment makes at least one literal true in every clause, satisfying $\phi$.
\end{itemize}
Since Independent Set is in NP and 3-CNF-SAT $\leq_p$ IS, Independent Set is NP-complete.

\subsection*{4. Intuitive Sketch of the Cook-Levin Theorem}

The Cook-Levin Theorem establishes that \textbf{CIRCUIT-SAT is NP-complete} (and consequently, Boolean Satisfiability is NP-complete). It forms the bedrock of NP-completeness.

\textbf{Concept:} The core idea is to physically simulate the computation of a Turing machine using boolean logic gates.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{CIRCUIT-SAT $\in$ NP:} Given a boolean circuit and a proposed assignment of inputs, a verifier can simply propagate the boolean values through the gates in topological order. This takes linear time relative to the number of gates. Thus, verification is in polynomial time.
    
    \item \textbf{NP-Hardness Reduction Sketch:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Let $L$ be \textit{any} problem in NP. By definition, there exists a deterministic polynomial-time verifier Turing Machine $M$ that takes an input string $x$ and a certificate $c$, and verifies if $x \in L$ within time $T(n) \leq n^k$.
        \item The execution of $M$ can be visualized as a 2D computational tableau (a grid). The rows represent time steps ($1 \text{ to } T(n)$), and the columns represent the tape cells ($1 \text{ to } T(n)$).
        \item The state of any cell $(i, j)$ at time $t$ depends \textit{strictly locally} on the state of cell $(i, j)$ and its immediate neighbors at time $t-1$, along with the internal state of the Turing machine's read/write head.
        \item Because the transition rules of the Turing machine are fixed and strictly local, this step-by-step state transition can be hardwired using a constant number of boolean logic gates (AND, OR, NOT).
        \item We construct a massive, polynomial-sized boolean circuit that strings together these small gate clusters to simulate the entire $T(n) \times T(n)$ tableau. 
        \item The "inputs" to this circuit are the unknown bits of the certificate $c$. The output of the circuit is wired to the "ACCEPT" state of the Turing Machine $M$.
        \item Therefore, the boolean circuit evaluates to 1 (True) \textit{if and only if} there exists a valid certificate $c$ that causes $M$ to accept $x$. 
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}
By constructing this polynomial-size circuit, we have shown that finding a satisfying assignment for the circuit is equivalent to finding a proof for \textit{any} problem in NP. Hence, CIRCUIT-SAT is NP-hard, making it NP-complete.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are two languages such that $L_1 \leq_p L_2$, then show that $L_2 \in P$ implies $L_1 \in P$. If any NP-complete problem is polynomial-time solvable, then prove that $P = NP$. \mk{7+3=10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2011--12}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsection*{Part 1: Proof that $L_1 \leq_p L_2$ and $L_2 \in P \implies L_1 \in P$}

\textbf{Theorem:} If a language $L_1$ is polynomial-time reducible to a language $L_2$, and $L_2$ is solvable in deterministic polynomial time, then $L_1$ is also solvable in deterministic polynomial time.

\begin{proof}
Let us formalize the definitions based on the given premises:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Premise 1 ($L_1 \leq_p L_2$):} By the definition of polynomial-time many-one reducibility, there exists a deterministic polynomial-time computable function $f: \Sigma^* \to \Sigma^*$ such that for any input string $x$:
    \[ x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2 \]
    Let $M_f$ be the Turing machine that computes $f$. Since $M_f$ operates in polynomial time, there exists a polynomial $p(n)$ such that $M_f$ halts in at most $p(|x|)$ steps.
    \item \textbf{Premise 2 ($L_2 \in P$):} There exists a deterministic algorithm (Turing machine) $A_2$ that decides $L_2$ in polynomial time. Let $q(m)$ be the polynomial bounding the running time of $A_2$ on any input of length $m$.
\end{enumerate}

We must construct a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm $A_1$ that decides $L_1$. We define $A_1$ as follows:

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Algorithm $A_1$ deciding $L_1$}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} A string $x \in \Sigma^*$ of length $n = |x|$
\State Run $M_f$ on $x$ to compute the transformed string $y = f(x)$.
\State Run $A_2$ on the string $y$.
\If{$A_2$ accepts $y$}
    \State \Return \text{ACCEPT}
\Else
    \State \Return \text{REJECT}
\EndIf
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\textbf{Correctness Analysis:} 
By the property of the reduction function $f$, $x \in L_1$ if and only if $y \in L_2$. Since $A_2$ correctly decides $L_2$, $A_2$ accepts $y$ if and only if $y \in L_2$. Therefore, algorithm $A_1$ accepts $x$ if and only if $x \in L_1$. Thus, $A_1$ correctly decides $L_1$.

\textbf{Complexity Analysis:} 
Let $n = |x|$ be the size of the input.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Step 2:} Computing $y = f(x)$ takes at most $p(n)$ time. Crucially, a Turing machine can output at most one symbol per computational step. Therefore, the length of the generated string $y$ cannot exceed the number of steps taken to compute it. Thus, we have a strict bound on the output size: $|y| \leq p(n)$.
    \item \textbf{Step 3:} We run $A_2$ on $y$. The running time of $A_2$ on an input of size $m$ is bounded by $q(m)$. Substituting our bound for $|y|$, the time taken by $A_2$ is at most $q(|y|) \leq q(p(n))$.
\end{itemize}

The total running time for algorithm $A_1$ is the time to compute the reduction plus the time to solve the reduced instance:
\[ T_{total}(n) = O(p(n) + q(p(n))) \]
Since polynomials are closed under addition and composition, $p(n) + q(p(n))$ is also a polynomial in $n$. Thus, algorithm $A_1$ is a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm for $L_1$. Therefore, $L_1 \in P$.
\end{proof}

\vspace{1em}

\subsection*{Part 2: Proof that if an NP-Complete problem is in P, then $P = NP$}

\textbf{Theorem:} Let $L_{NPC}$ be an NP-complete problem. If $L_{NPC} \in P$, then the complexity classes $P$ and $NP$ are strictly equal ($P = NP$).

\begin{proof}
To prove that two sets $P$ and $NP$ are equal, we must demonstrate that $P \subseteq NP$ and $NP \subseteq P$.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Step 1: Prove $P \subseteq NP$} \\
    This inclusion holds trivially by definition. Any problem in $P$ can be solved by a deterministic polynomial-time Turing machine. A non-deterministic polynomial-time Turing machine can simply ignore its non-deterministic capabilities and execute the deterministic algorithm. Alternatively, if a problem is in $P$, its solution can be "verified" efficiently by simply computing the answer from scratch. Thus, $P \subseteq NP$.

    \item \textbf{Step 2: Prove $NP \subseteq P$} \\
    Let $L$ be an arbitrary language belonging to the class $NP$.
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item By the definition of NP-Completeness, since $L_{NPC}$ is NP-complete, \textbf{every} problem in $NP$ is polynomial-time reducible to $L_{NPC}$. Because $L \in NP$, it strictly follows that $L \leq_p L_{NPC}$.
        \item We are given the premise that $L_{NPC}$ is solvable in deterministic polynomial time, i.e., $L_{NPC} \in P$.
        \item Applying the theorem proven in Part 1: if $L \leq_p L_{NPC}$ and $L_{NPC} \in P$, it logically guarantees that $L \in P$.
    \end{enumerate}
    Since $L$ was chosen as an arbitrary language in $NP$, the implication $L \in NP \implies L \in P$ holds universally. Therefore, every problem in $NP$ is also contained in $P$, establishing that $NP \subseteq P$.
\end{itemize}

Because we have established both $P \subseteq NP$ and $NP \subseteq P$, we conclude by mutual inclusion that $P = NP$. 
\end{proof}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item \textbf{Instance of Satisfiability Problem}\\
Boolean variables: $X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4, X_5$\\
Clauses:
\begin{align*}
C_1 &= X_1 \vee \overline{X_2}, \quad C_2 = \overline{X_1} \vee X_2 \vee X_4 \vee \overline{X_5}\\
C_3 &= \overline{X_2}, \quad C_4 = X_2 \vee X_3 \vee \overline{X_4}
\end{align*}
Mention the number of boolean variables and clauses in the 3-SAT problem.

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

To convert a general Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) instance into a 3-SAT instance, we use the standard Karp reduction. The strict definition of 3-SAT requires every clause to have \textbf{exactly 3 distinct literals}. We achieve this by introducing new "dummy" or "bridging" variables to expand or break down the original clauses.

Let the original set of variables be $V = \{X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4, X_5\}$, so there are initially \textbf{5 variables}.
We will process each clause $C_i$ individually based on its length $k$:

\subsubsection*{1. Clause $C_1 = X_1 \vee \overline{X_2}$ \quad (Length $k=2$)}
For a clause with exactly 2 literals, we introduce $1$ new variable, say $Y_1$. We expand the clause into 2 new clauses to ensure each has exactly 3 literals without changing the satisfiability:
\begin{align*}
C_{1a} &= X_1 \vee \overline{X_2} \vee Y_1 \\
C_{1b} &= X_1 \vee \overline{X_2} \vee \overline{Y_1}
\end{align*}
\textbf{Added:} 1 new variable ($Y_1$), 2 clauses.

\subsubsection*{2. Clause $C_2 = \overline{X_1} \vee X_2 \vee X_4 \vee \overline{X_5}$ \quad (Length $k=4$)}
For a clause with $k > 3$ literals, we must break it apart. We introduce $k - 3$ new bridging variables. Here, $k=4$, so we introduce $4 - 3 = 1$ new variable, say $Y_2$. The clause is split into $k - 2 = 2$ clauses:
\begin{align*}
C_{2a} &= \overline{X_1} \vee X_2 \vee Y_2 \\
C_{2b} &= \overline{Y_2} \vee X_4 \vee \overline{X_5}
\end{align*}
\textbf{Added:} 1 new variable ($Y_2$), 2 clauses.

\subsubsection*{3. Clause $C_3 = \overline{X_2}$ \quad (Length $k=1$)}
For a clause with exactly 1 literal, we must pad it to reach length 3. We introduce $2$ new variables, say $Y_3$ and $Y_4$. We expand it into $2^2 = 4$ combinations to neutralize the new variables:
\begin{align*}
C_{3a} &= \overline{X_2} \vee Y_3 \vee Y_4 \\
C_{3b} &= \overline{X_2} \vee Y_3 \vee \overline{Y_4} \\
C_{3c} &= \overline{X_2} \vee \overline{Y_3} \vee Y_4 \\
C_{3d} &= \overline{X_2} \vee \overline{Y_3} \vee \overline{Y_4}
\end{align*}
\textbf{Added:} 2 new variables ($Y_3, Y_4$), 4 clauses.

\subsubsection*{4. Clause $C_4 = X_2 \vee X_3 \vee \overline{X_4}$ \quad (Length $k=3$)}
This clause already has exactly 3 distinct literals. No transformation is needed.
\begin{align*}
C_{4a} &= X_2 \vee X_3 \vee \overline{X_4}
\end{align*}
\textbf{Added:} 0 new variables, 1 clause.

\vspace{1em}
\hrule
\vspace{1em}

\subsubsection*{Final Count}

\textbf{Total Number of Boolean Variables:}
We have the $5$ original variables $\{X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4, X_5\}$ plus the $4$ newly introduced variables $\{Y_1, Y_2, Y_3, Y_4\}$.
\[ \text{Total Variables} = 5 + 4 = \mathbf{9} \]

\textbf{Total Number of Clauses:}
Summing the clauses generated from each step:
\[ \text{Total Clauses} = 2 \ (\text{from } C_1) + 2 \ (\text{from } C_2) + 4 \ (\text{from } C_3) + 1 \ (\text{from } C_4) = \mathbf{9} \]

\vspace{1em}
\textit{Note: In some relaxed definitions of 3-SAT where literal duplication within a clause is allowed, $C_1$ could be written as $(X_1 \vee \overline{X_2} \vee \overline{X_2})$ and $C_3$ as $(\overline{X_2} \vee \overline{X_2} \vee \overline{X_2})$. Under that specific relaxed assumption, the reduction would only require 1 new variable for $C_2$ (total 6 variables) and produce exactly 5 clauses. However, the standard algorithmic reduction (strict 3-SAT) guarantees distinct literals, resulting in \textbf{9 variables} and \textbf{9 clauses}.}

\end{tcolorbox}


%% Q — 2019-20
\end{enumerate}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cD}{NP-hard and NP-complete Problems}
\label{sub:s4-nphard}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item What are some approaches to deal with an NP-complete problem?
\mk{6} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

When dealing with an NP-complete problem, it is widely believed that no algorithm can solve all instances optimally in polynomial time (assuming $\mathcal{P} \neq \mathcal{NP}$). Therefore, algorithm designers must compromise. Every algorithmic approach to an NP-complete problem relaxes at least one of the three desired guarantees:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Optimal Solution} (Finding the absolute best answer)
    \item \textbf{Polynomial Time} (Running fast, efficiently scaling with input size)
    \item \textbf{Arbitrary Instances} (Working on any general input graph/data)
\end{enumerate}

The trade-offs between these guarantees are visualized in the diagram below. 

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9, thick, >=stealth, every node/.style={font=\small}]
    % Nodes
    \node[draw, rounded corners, fill=cE!15, text width=2.8cm, align=center, minimum height=1.2cm] (A) at (0,4) {\textbf{Optimal} \\ \textbf{Solution}};
    \node[draw, rounded corners, fill=cE!15, text width=2.8cm, align=center, minimum height=1.2cm] (B) at (-4,0) {\textbf{Polynomial} \\ \textbf{Time}};
    \node[draw, rounded corners, fill=cE!15, text width=2.8cm, align=center, minimum height=1.2cm] (C) at (4,0) {\textbf{Arbitrary} \\ \textbf{Instances}};

    % Edges and labels
    \draw[<->, line width=1pt] (A) -- (B) node[midway, left, xshift=-0.2cm, text width=3.5cm, align=right] {Special Cases \\ (e.g., Trees, Bipartite)};
    \draw[<->, line width=1pt] (B) -- (C) node[midway, below, yshift=-0.2cm, text width=5cm, align=center] {Approximations \& Heuristics \\ (Sub-optimal solutions)};
    \draw[<->, line width=1pt] (A) -- (C) node[midway, right, xshift=0.2cm, text width=3.5cm, align=left] {Exponential / FPT \\ (e.g., Branch \& Bound)};

    % Center text
    \node[text width=4cm, align=center] at (0,1.5) {\textbf{The "Pick Two" Dilemma}\\(Assuming $\mathcal{P} \neq \mathcal{NP}$)};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

Here are the primary algorithmic approaches to deal with NP-complete problems:

\vspace{0.5cm}
\subsubsection*{1. Relaxing Optimality: Approximation Algorithms}
When a problem must be solved in polynomial time for arbitrary instances, we can accept a near-optimal solution with a mathematically proven worst-case guarantee.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Concept:} An algorithm produces a valid solution that is guaranteed to be within a factor $\rho$ (the approximation ratio) of the true optimal solution $OPT$.
    \item \textbf{Mathematical Bound:} For a minimization problem, the algorithm yields a cost $ALG \leq \rho \cdot OPT$, where $\rho \geq 1$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Example: 2-Approximation for Minimum Vertex Cover}
\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Vertex Cover Approximation}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Undirected graph $G = (V, E)$
\State \textbf{Output:} A vertex cover $C \subseteq V$
\State $C \gets \emptyset$
\State $E' \gets E$
\While{$E' \neq \emptyset$}
    \State Pick an arbitrary edge $(u, v) \in E'$
    \State $C \gets C \cup \{u, v\}$
    \State Remove all edges incident to $u$ or $v$ from $E'$
\EndWhile
\State \Return $C$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ because each edge and vertex is processed a constant number of times. Space Complexity is $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ to store the cover.
    \item \textbf{Approximation Guarantee:} Since the algorithm picks a maximal matching, and any valid vertex cover MUST pick at least one vertex from each matching edge, taking both vertices ensures we are at most $2 \times OPT$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5cm}
\subsubsection*{2. Relaxing Polynomial Time: Exact Algorithms}
If we absolutely need the optimal solution for general instances, we must sacrifice polynomial time bounds. However, we can use techniques to make the exponential runtime feasible for practically sized inputs.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Branch and Bound / Backtracking:} Intelligently prune the search space. If a partial solution is already worse than the current best-known solution, the algorithm aborts exploring that branch.
    \item \textbf{Dynamic Programming over Subsets:} Classic example is the Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) using the Held-Karp algorithm, reducing naive $\mathcal{O}(n!)$ brute force to $\mathcal{O}(n^2 2^n)$.
    \item \textbf{Integer Linear Programming (ILP):} Formulate the problem as an ILP and use highly optimized commercial solvers (like Gurobi or CPLEX) which employ advanced cutting planes and branch-and-cut heuristics.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5cm}
\subsubsection*{3. Fixed-Parameter Tractability (FPT)}
Instead of analyzing complexity solely based on the input size $n$, Parameterized Complexity analyzes it with respect to an additional parameter $k$ (e.g., the size of the vertex cover sought, or the treewidth of the graph).
\begin{itemize}
    \item A problem is FPT if it can be solved in time:
    $$ \mathcal{T}(n, k) = f(k) \cdot n^{\mathcal{O}(1)} $$
    where $f$ is a computable function exclusively dependent on $k$.
    \item \textbf{Intuition:} All the combinatorial explosion (the exponential part of the runtime) is confined to the parameter $k$. If $k$ is small, the algorithm remains highly efficient even for massive $n$. 
    \item \textbf{Example:} Vertex Cover parameterized by solution size $k$ can be solved in $\mathcal{O}(2^k \cdot n)$ using bounded search trees.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5cm}
\subsubsection*{4. Relaxing Generality: Special Cases}
Many NP-complete problems become easily solvable in polynomial time if the input instances belong to a restricted class of graphs or data.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Trees:} Maximum Independent Set is NP-complete on general graphs but can be solved in $\mathcal{O}(n)$ on trees using Dynamic Programming.
    \item \textbf{Bipartite Graphs:} Maximum Clique is trivially solvable (maximum size is 2, unless the graph is empty). Minimum Vertex Cover can be solved exactly in polynomial time using Network Flow / Maximum Matching (Kőnig's Theorem).
    \item \textbf{Planar Graphs:} Problems like Max Cut or perfect matching have specialized polynomial-time algorithms or admits very strong approximations (PTAS) on planar graphs.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.5cm}
\subsubsection*{5. Heuristics and Metaheuristics}
When mathematical guarantees are not required, but "good enough" solutions are needed quickly.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Local Search:} Start with a random solution and iteratively make small changes (e.g., swapping two cities in TSP) to improve the cost until reaching a local optimum.
    \item \textbf{Simulated Annealing / Genetic Algorithms:} Metaheuristics designed to escape local optima by occasionally accepting worse solutions probabilistically or simulating evolutionary crossover.
    \item \textbf{Note:} Unlike approximation algorithms, these offer \textbf{no formal worst-case bounds} on solution quality or runtime to converge, but often perform phenomenally well in industrial applications.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Define the satisfiability problem. Show that the independent set problem is NP-hard by reducing the 3-SAT problem to the independent set problem. \mk{3+7}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsubsection*{1. The Satisfiability Problem (SAT) \hfill \normalsize\mdseries\textit{[3 Marks]}}

To define the Satisfiability (SAT) problem formally, we first establish the foundational components of Boolean logic:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Variables \& Literals:} Let $U = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ be a set of Boolean variables. A \textit{literal} is either a variable $x_i$ or its negation $\bar{x}_i$.
    \item \textbf{Clause:} A clause $C_j$ is a disjunction (logical OR, $\vee$) of one or more literals. For example: $C_j = (x_1 \vee \bar{x}_2 \vee x_3)$.
    \item \textbf{Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF):} A Boolean formula $\phi$ is in CNF if it is expressed as a conjunction (logical AND, $\wedge$) of clauses. For example: $\phi = C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge \dots \wedge C_k$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Definition (SAT):} Given a Boolean formula $\phi$ in Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) over a set of variables $U$, the Satisfiability problem asks whether there exists a truth assignment (mapping each variable to \texttt{True} or \texttt{False}) such that $\phi$ evaluates to \texttt{True}. For $\phi$ to be \texttt{True}, \textit{every} clause $C_j$ must contain at least one literal that evaluates to \texttt{True}.

\vspace{0.5cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.5cm}

\subsubsection*{2. NP-Hardness of Independent Set \hfill \normalsize\mdseries\textit{[7 Marks]}}

To prove that the Independent Set (IS) problem is NP-hard, we provide a polynomial-time reduction from the known NP-hard problem \textbf{3-SAT}. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{3-SAT Problem:} A special case of SAT where every clause contains exactly 3 literals.
    \item \textbf{Independent Set Problem:} Given an undirected graph $G=(V, E)$ and an integer $K$, does $G$ contain an independent set of size at least $K$ (a subset of $K$ vertices where no two vertices are connected by an edge)?
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Step 2.1: Polynomial-Time Reduction Construction ($f: \text{3-SAT} \to \text{IS}$)}
Let $\phi = C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge \dots \wedge C_k$ be an instance of 3-SAT with $k$ clauses, where each clause $C_r = (l_{r,1} \vee l_{r,2} \vee l_{r,3})$. We construct a graph $G = (V, E)$ and define a target size $K$ as follows:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Vertices ($V$):} For each clause $C_r$, create 3 vertices corresponding to its literals. Thus, $|V| = 3k$. Let the vertex corresponding to literal $l_{r,i}$ be $v_{r,i}$.
    \item \textbf{Clause Edges ($E_{\text{clause}}$):} For every clause $C_r$, connect its three vertices to form a triangle ($K_3$). This ensures at most one vertex per clause can be chosen for an independent set.
    \item \textbf{Conflict Edges ($E_{\text{conflict}}$):} Add an edge between any two vertices $v_{r,i}$ and $v_{s,j}$ if they represent conflicting literals (i.e., one is $x$ and the other is $\bar{x}$). This enforces logical consistency.
    \item \textbf{Parameter ($K$):} Set the target independent set size to $K = k$ (the number of clauses).
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Example Visualization of the Reduction}
Consider $\phi = (x_1 \vee x_2 \vee x_3) \wedge (\bar{x}_1 \vee \bar{x}_2 \vee \bar{x}_3)$. Here $k=2$. We construct $G$ as shown below:

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=2.5cm, main node/.style={circle,draw=cD!80,thick,minimum size=8mm,inner sep=1pt,fill=cD!10}]
    
    % Clause 1
    \node[main node] (v11) at (0, 2) {$x_1$};
    \node[main node] (v12) at (-1.5, 0) {$x_2$};
    \node[main node] (v13) at (1.5, 0) {$x_3$};
    
    \draw[very thick, cD!80!black] (v11) -- (v12) node[midway, left] {};
    \draw[very thick, cD!80!black] (v12) -- (v13) node[midway, below] {};
    \draw[very thick, cD!80!black] (v13) -- (v11) node[midway, right] {};
    \node[font=\bfseries] at (0, -1) {Clause $C_1$};

    % Clause 2
    \node[main node] (v21) at (7, 2) {$\bar{x}_1$};
    \node[main node] (v22) at (5.5, 0) {$\bar{x}_2$};
    \node[main node] (v23) at (8.5, 0) {$\bar{x}_3$};
    
    \draw[very thick, cD!80!black] (v21) -- (v22) node[midway, left] {};
    \draw[very thick, cD!80!black] (v22) -- (v23) node[midway, below] {};
    \draw[very thick, cD!80!black] (v23) -- (v21) node[midway, right] {};
    \node[font=\bfseries] at (7, -1) {Clause $C_2$};

    % Conflict edges
    \draw[dashed, thick, red!80!black] (v11) to[out=20,in=160] node[midway, above, font=\small] {Conflict} (v21);
    \draw[dashed, thick, red!80!black] (v12) to[out=-20,in=200] node[midway, below, font=\small] {Conflict} (v22);
    \draw[dashed, thick, red!80!black] (v13) to[out=20,in=160] (v23);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\paragraph{Step 2.2: Correctness Proof ($\phi$ is satisfiable $\iff G$ has an Independent Set of size $k$)}

\textbf{Forward Direction ($\implies$):} 
Assume $\phi$ is satisfiable. There exists a truth assignment making every clause evaluate to \texttt{True}. Therefore, in each clause $C_r$, at least one literal is \texttt{True}. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item Select exactly one \texttt{True} literal from each clause and pick its corresponding vertex in $G$. 
    \item Because we select exactly one vertex per clause, no \textit{clause edges} connect our chosen vertices.
    \item Because the truth assignment is strictly consistent (a variable cannot be both \texttt{True} and \texttt{False} simultaneously), our selection does not contain conflicting literals. Thus, no \textit{conflict edges} connect our chosen vertices.
    \item We have selected $k$ vertices with no edges between them, forming an independent set of size $k$.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Backward Direction ($\impliedby$):} 
Assume $G$ has an independent set $I$ of size $k$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item The vertices in $G$ are partitioned into $k$ disjoint triangles (one for each clause). An independent set can contain at most 1 vertex from any triangle. 
    \item Since $|I| = k$, $I$ must contain exactly 1 vertex from \textit{every} triangle.
    \item The vertices in $I$ represent a set of literals. Because they form an independent set, there are no \textit{conflict edges} between them. This implies we never selected both $x_i$ and $\bar{x}_i$.
    \item We can safely set all literals represented in $I$ to \texttt{True} (and assign any unmapped variables arbitrarily). 
    \item Because we picked one literal from every triangle, every clause in $\phi$ contains at least one \texttt{True} literal. Thus, $\phi$ is satisfiable.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Step 2.3: Complexity of Reduction}
The reduction creates exactly $3k$ vertices and at most $3k + (3k)^2$ edges. This entire construction can be carried out in $\mathcal{O}(k^2)$ time, which is strictly polynomial with respect to the input size of $\phi$. Since 3-SAT $\leq_p$ Independent Set, and 3-SAT is NP-hard, the \textbf{Independent Set problem is NP-hard}.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Your friend claims to have developed a polynomial-time algorithm to solve the set-packing problem. What will happen if this claim is proved to be true? \mk{5}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

If your friend's claim is proven to be true, the most immediate and monumental consequence is that it would prove \textbf{$\mathcal{P} = \mathcal{NP}$}. This is one of the most famous open problems in computer science and mathematics.

Below is a detailed breakdown of the theoretical and practical implications of this discovery.

\subsubsection*{1. Immediate Theoretical Implication: $\mathcal{P} = \mathcal{NP}$}
The \textbf{Set-Packing} problem is one of Karp's original 21 \textbf{NP-complete} problems. By definition, a problem is NP-complete if:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item It is in $\mathcal{NP}$ (a proposed solution can be verified in polynomial time).
    \item It is NP-hard (every problem in $\mathcal{NP}$ can be reduced to it in polynomial time).
\end{enumerate}

Let $X$ be any arbitrary problem in $\mathcal{NP}$. Because Set-Packing is NP-hard, there exists a polynomial-time reduction function $f$ such that $X \leq_p \text{Set-Packing}$. 

If your friend has a polynomial-time algorithm $A$ for Set-Packing (running in $\mathcal{O}(n^k)$ time), we can solve \textit{any} problem $X \in \mathcal{NP}$ in polynomial time using the following composite algorithm:
\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Solving any $X \in \mathcal{NP}$ in Polynomial Time}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} An instance $x$ of problem $X$.
\State Transform $x$ into an instance $y$ of Set-Packing using the polynomial reduction $f$. \Comment{Takes poly time}
\State Feed $y$ into your friend's algorithm $A$. \Comment{Takes poly time}
\State \Return the boolean answer provided by $A$.
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}
Since the composition of two polynomial-time operations is itself bounded by a polynomial, every problem in $\mathcal{NP}$ is now in $\mathcal{P}$, collapsing the complexity classes such that $\mathcal{P} = \mathcal{NP}$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8, every node/.style={font=\small}]
    % World 1: P != NP
    \node[font=\bfseries] at (-3, 3.5) {Current Belief: $\mathcal{P} \neq \mathcal{NP}$};
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!10] (-3, 0) ellipse (2cm and 3cm);
    \node[font=\bfseries] at (-3, 2.2) {$\mathcal{NP}$};
    
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!30] (-3, -1.2) ellipse (1.2cm and 1cm);
    \node[font=\bfseries] at (-3, -1.2) {$\mathcal{P}$};
    
    \draw[thick, fill=cE!30] (-3, 1) ellipse (1.2cm and 0.8cm);
    \node[font=\bfseries, text width=2cm, align=center] at (-3, 1) {$\mathcal{NP}$-Complete\\(Set-Packing)};

    % Arrow
    \draw[->, ultra thick, dashed] (-0.5, 0) -- (1.5, 0) node[midway, above] {Friend's} node[midway, below] {Algorithm};

    % World 2: P = NP
    \node[font=\bfseries] at (4, 3.5) {New Reality: $\mathcal{P} = \mathcal{NP}$};
    \draw[thick, fill=cD!30] (4, 0) circle (2cm);
    \node[font=\bfseries, text width=3cm, align=center] at (4, 0) {$\mathcal{P} = \mathcal{NP} = \mathcal{NPC}$\\ \vspace{0.2cm} (All problems become tractable)};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{2. Mathematical and Financial Consequences}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{The Millennium Prize:} The $\mathcal{P}$ vs $\mathcal{NP}$ problem is one of the seven Millennium Prize Problems designated by the Clay Mathematics Institute. Proving this claim would instantly earn your friend \$1,000,000 and eternal fame in the scientific community.
    \item \textbf{Automated Theorem Proving:} Finding a formal mathematical proof of length $N$ is an $\mathcal{NP}$ problem. If $\mathcal{P} = \mathcal{NP}$, computers could efficiently generate proofs for any unproven mathematical theorem (e.g., the Riemann Hypothesis), fundamentally changing the nature of mathematics.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{3. Practical Consequences (The "Good")}
Thousands of computationally intractable optimization problems across all industries would become efficiently solvable:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Operations Research:} The Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP), Vehicle Routing, and optimal scheduling could be solved exactly, saving billions of dollars in global logistics and supply chain management.
    \item \textbf{Computational Biology:} Protein folding, DNA sequence alignment, and drug discovery processes would become computationally trivial, likely curing many diseases.
    \item \textbf{Machine Learning:} Training complex neural networks (currently optimized using heuristic gradient descent) could theoretically be solved to find the absolute global minimum efficiently.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{4. Security Consequences (The "Bad")}
Modern digital security would instantly collapse:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Cryptography Destruction:} Public-key cryptography (like RSA and ECC) relies on the premise that certain problems, like Integer Factorization and Discrete Logarithm, are computationally hard (these are in $\mathcal{NP}$).
    \item If $\mathcal{P} = \mathcal{NP}$, factoring massive primes would take seconds. Every bank transaction, encrypted message, blockchain network, and national security database would become publicly readable and forgeable.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Conclusion:} Unless your friend has made one of the greatest intellectual breakthroughs in human history, they likely made an error. They most likely either developed a heuristic/approximation, solved only a special sub-case of the Set-Packing problem, or made a mistake in their algorithmic complexity analysis.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write the names of four NP-complete problems. Define the following six classes of problems: P, Co-P, NP, Co-NP, NP-complete, and NP-hard. By a diagram show the relationship among these classes that most researchers regard as most likely.
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2013--14}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsubsection*{1. Four NP-Complete Problems}
Here are four classic examples of NP-complete problems:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT / 3-SAT):} Given a Boolean formula, is there an assignment of truth values to the variables that makes the entire formula true?
    \item \textbf{Traveling Salesperson Problem (Decision Version):} Given a set of cities and a distance matrix, is there a route that visits every city exactly once and returns to the origin with a total distance $\leq K$?
    \item \textbf{Minimum Vertex Cover (Decision Version):} Given a graph $G$ and an integer $K$, does there exist a subset of vertices of size $\leq K$ such that every edge in $G$ is incident to at least one vertex in the subset?
    \item \textbf{Maximum Clique (Decision Version):} Given a graph $G$ and an integer $K$, does $G$ contain a complete subgraph (clique) of size $\geq K$?
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Definitions of Complexity Classes}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{$\mathcal{P}$ (Polynomial Time):} The class of decision problems that can be \textit{solved} by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time (i.e., time $\mathcal{O}(n^k)$ for some constant $k$).
    
    \item \textbf{$\text{Co-}\mathcal{P}$:} The class of decision problems whose complement (inverting "yes" and "no" answers) is in $\mathcal{P}$. Because deterministic algorithms can easily invert their boolean output in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time without affecting the polynomial time bound, $\mathcal{P}$ is closed under complementation. Thus, $\mathcal{P} = \text{Co-}\mathcal{P}$.
    
    \item \textbf{$\mathcal{NP}$ (Nondeterministic Polynomial Time):} The class of decision problems for which a "yes" answer has a polynomial-size certificate (or proof) that can be \textit{verified} by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time. Equivalently, it is the class of problems solvable by a nondeterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
    
    \item \textbf{$\text{Co-}\mathcal{NP}$:} The class of decision problems whose complement is in $\mathcal{NP}$. Equivalently, it is the class of problems for which a "no" answer has a polynomial-size certificate that can be verified in polynomial time.
    
    \item \textbf{$\mathcal{NP}$-hard:} A class of problems that are at least as hard as the hardest problems in $\mathcal{NP}$. Formally, a problem $H$ is $\mathcal{NP}$-hard if \textit{every} problem $L \in \mathcal{NP}$ can be reduced to $H$ in polynomial time ($L \leq_p H$). $\mathcal{NP}$-hard problems do not themselves have to be in $\mathcal{NP}$ (they can be undecidable).
    
    \item \textbf{$\mathcal{NP}$-complete ($\mathcal{NPC}$):} The intersection of $\mathcal{NP}$ and $\mathcal{NP}$-hard. A decision problem is $\mathcal{NP}$-complete if it is \textit{both} in $\mathcal{NP}$ and is $\mathcal{NP}$-hard. 
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Diagram of the Most Likely Relationship ($\mathcal{P} \neq \mathcal{NP}$)}
Most researchers strongly believe that $\mathcal{P} \neq \mathcal{NP}$ and $\mathcal{NP} \neq \text{Co-}\mathcal{NP}$. Under this assumption, the relationship among these classes is depicted below:

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.0, thick, every node/.style={font=\small\bfseries}]
    
    % Fill the intersection of NP and NP-Hard to highlight NPC
    \begin{scope}
        \clip (-2.0, 4.5) circle (2.2cm); 
        \clip (-2.0, 0.5) ellipse (3cm and 3.5cm);
        \fill[cD!30] (-6, 0) rectangle (2, 8);
    \end{scope}

    % Fill P
    \fill[white] (0, -1.5) ellipse (1.8cm and 1.2cm);

    % Outlines
    \draw (-2.0, 0.5) ellipse (3cm and 3.5cm); % NP
    \draw (2.0, 0.5) ellipse (3cm and 3.5cm);  % Co-NP
    
    % NP-hard
    \draw[dashed, fill=cD!10, fill opacity=0.3] (-2.0, 4.5) circle (2.2cm); 
    
    % P
    \draw (0, -1.5) ellipse (1.8cm and 1.2cm);     

    % Labels
    \node at (-3.5, 0) {$\mathcal{NP}$};
    \node at (3.5, 0) {$\text{Co-}\mathcal{NP}$};
    \node[align=center] at (0, -1.5) {$\mathcal{P} = \text{Co-}\mathcal{P}$};
    
    % NP-complete label
    \node[text=cD!80!black] at (-2.0, 3.1) {$\mathcal{NP}$-complete};
    
    % NP-hard label
    \node at (-2.0, 5.5) {$\mathcal{NP}$-hard};
    
    % Explanation node
    \node[font=\footnotesize, align=center, text width=6cm] at (5.5, 4.5) {
        \textit{Note:} $\mathcal{NP}$-hard extends outside of $\mathcal{NP}$. Its intersection with $\mathcal{NP}$ is exactly the class of $\mathcal{NP}$-complete problems. $\mathcal{P}$ sits precisely at the intersection of $\mathcal{NP}$ and $\text{Co-}\mathcal{NP}$.
    };
    \draw[->, densely dotted] (3.5, 4.5) -- (0.2, 4.5);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Show that the decision version of the vertex cover optimization problem is NP-complete using a reduction from the Independent Set decision problem. \mk{12}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

To prove that the Vertex Cover (VC) decision problem is NP-complete, we must satisfy two conditions:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Show that \textbf{VC $\in \mathcal{NP}$}.
    \item Show that \textbf{VC is NP-hard} by providing a polynomial-time reduction from a known NP-complete problem (in this case, Independent Set) to VC.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{1. Formal Problem Definitions}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Vertex Cover (VC):} Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k$, does there exist a subset $V' \subseteq V$ such that $|V'| \leq k$ and for every edge $(u, v) \in E$, at least one of $u$ or $v$ belongs to $V'$?
    \item \textbf{Independent Set (IS):} Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $m$, does there exist a subset $S \subseteq V$ such that $|S| \geq m$ and for all $u, v \in S$, $(u, v) \notin E$?
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Proof of Membership in $\mathcal{NP}$}
A problem is in $\mathcal{NP}$ if a proposed solution (certificate) can be verified in polynomial time.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Certificate:} A subset $V' \subseteq V$ containing at most $k$ vertices.
    \item \textbf{Verification Algorithm:} 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Count the number of vertices in $V'$. If $|V'| > k$, reject.
        \item Iterate through every edge $(u, v) \in E$. If neither $u \in V'$ nor $v \in V'$, reject.
        \item If all edges are covered, accept.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} Checking the size of $V'$ takes $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ time. Iterating through edges takes $\mathcal{O}(|E|)$ time. The total verification time is $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$, which is polynomial. Therefore, \textbf{VC $\in \mathcal{NP}$}.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Polynomial-Time Reduction (IS $\leq_p$ VC)}
We must construct a function $f$ that transforms an arbitrary instance of the Independent Set problem $\langle G=(V,E), m \rangle$ into an instance of the Vertex Cover problem $\langle G'=(V',E'), k \rangle$ in polynomial time.

\paragraph{Construction:}
Given $\langle G=(V, E), m \rangle$:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Keep the exact same graph: $G' = G$ (so $V' = V$ and $E' = E$).
    \item Set the target vertex cover size to: $k = |V| - m$.
\end{enumerate}
This transformation simply requires counting the vertices and doing a single subtraction. It takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time if $|V|$ is known, or $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ to count, both of which are strictly polynomial.

\paragraph{Visualization of the Relationship:}
The fundamental intuition behind this reduction is that a set of vertices $S$ is an Independent Set if and only if its complement $V \setminus S$ is a Vertex Cover.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=2cm, 
    isnode/.style={circle,draw=green!60!black,thick,fill=green!10,minimum size=8mm},
    vcnode/.style={circle,draw=red!80!black,thick,fill=red!10,minimum size=8mm}]

    % Nodes
    \node[isnode, label=left:{$v_1$}] (1) at (90:1.5) {};
    \node[vcnode, label=right:{$v_2$}] (2) at (18:1.5) {};
    \node[isnode, label=right:{$v_3$}] (3) at (306:1.5) {};
    \node[vcnode, label=left:{$v_4$}] (4) at (234:1.5) {};
    \node[vcnode, label=left:{$v_5$}] (5) at (162:1.5) {};

    % Edges
    \draw[thick] (1) -- (2);
    \draw[thick] (2) -- (3);
    \draw[thick] (3) -- (4);
    \draw[thick] (4) -- (5);
    \draw[thick] (5) -- (1);

    % Legend
    \begin{scope}[shift={(3.5, 0)}]
        \node[isnode, label=right:{\small Independent Set $S$ ($m=2$)}] at (0, 0.6) {};
        \node[vcnode, label=right:{\small Vertex Cover $V \setminus S$ ($k=3$)}] at (0, -0.6) {};
    \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Proof of Correctness ($G$ has an IS of size $m \iff G'$ has a VC of size $k$)}

\paragraph{Forward Direction ($\implies$):} 
Assume $G$ has an independent set $S$ of size $\geq m$. We must show $V' = V \setminus S$ is a vertex cover of size $\leq k$.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Size constraint:} Since $|S| \geq m$, the size of the complement is $|V'| = |V| - |S| \leq |V| - m = k$.
    \item \textbf{Coverage constraint:} Consider any arbitrary edge $(u, v) \in E$. Because $S$ is an independent set, $u$ and $v$ cannot \textit{both} belong to $S$ (otherwise, there would be an edge between two vertices in the independent set, violating its definition). Therefore, at least one of $u$ or $v$ must not be in $S$. By definition of the complement, at least one of $u$ or $v$ must be in $V \setminus S = V'$.
    \item Thus, $V'$ covers all edges and is a valid Vertex Cover of size $k$.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Backward Direction ($\impliedby$):} 
Assume $G'$ (which is $G$) has a vertex cover $V'$ of size $\leq k$. We must show $S = V \setminus V'$ is an independent set of size $\geq m$.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Size constraint:} Since $|V'| \leq k = |V| - m$, the size of the complement is $|S| = |V| - |V'| \geq |V| - (|V| - m) = m$.
    \item \textbf{Independence constraint:} Assume, for the sake of contradiction, that $S$ is not an independent set. This means there exists an edge $(u, v) \in E$ where both $u \in S$ and $v \in S$. If both $u$ and $v$ are in $S$, then neither $u$ nor $v$ is in $V'$ (since $S$ and $V'$ are disjoint). However, if neither $u$ nor $v$ is in $V'$, then the edge $(u, v)$ is not covered by $V'$. This contradicts our assumption that $V'$ is a valid vertex cover.
    \item Therefore, no two vertices in $S$ can share an edge, meaning $S$ is an Independent Set of size $m$.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Conclusion}
We have shown that VC is in $\mathcal{NP}$, and we have provided a valid polynomial-time reduction from the NP-complete Independent Set problem to the Vertex Cover problem. Therefore, the \textbf{Vertex Cover decision problem is NP-complete}.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Prove that the optimization version of the Independent Set problem is NP-hard. \mk{8}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2012--13}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

To prove that the optimization version of the Independent Set problem is NP-hard, we use a \textbf{Turing reduction} from its corresponding decision version. Standard Karp reductions (many-one reductions) map decision problems to decision problems, but to relate a decision problem to an optimization problem, we show that if we had a polynomial-time solver (an "oracle") for the optimization problem, we could solve the NP-complete decision problem in polynomial time.

\subsubsection*{1. Formal Problem Definitions}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Decision Independent Set (IS-DEC):} Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k$, does $G$ contain an independent set of size at least $k$? (This is a known NP-complete problem).
    \item \textbf{Optimization Independent Set (IS-OPT):} Given a graph $G = (V, E)$, find an independent set $S_{max} \subseteq V$ of maximum possible size.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Polynomial-Time Turing Reduction (IS-DEC $\leq_T^p$ IS-OPT)}

Assume, for the sake of contradiction, that there exists a hypothetical algorithm $\mathcal{A}_{OPT}$ that solves the \textbf{IS-OPT} problem in polynomial time. We construct an algorithm $\mathcal{A}_{DEC}$ to solve the \textbf{IS-DEC} problem using $\mathcal{A}_{OPT}$ as a subroutine.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Solving IS-DEC using IS-OPT Oracle}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} A graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k$.
\State \textbf{Output:} \texttt{TRUE} if $G$ has an independent set of size $\ge k$, else \texttt{FALSE}.
\State $S_{max} \gets \mathcal{A}_{OPT}(G)$ \Comment{Call the optimization oracle}
\State $m \gets |S_{max}|$ \Comment{Find the size of the maximum independent set}
\If{$m \ge k$}
    \State \Return \texttt{TRUE}
\Else
    \State \Return \texttt{FALSE}
\EndIf
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\paragraph{Diagram of the Turing Reduction:}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick, node distance=2cm]
    % Draw blocks
    \node[draw=cD!80!black, fill=cD!10, rounded corners, minimum width=3.5cm, minimum height=4cm, align=center] (dec_algo) at (0,0) {};
    \node[align=center, font=\bfseries] at (0, 1.5) {$\mathcal{A}_{DEC}(G, k)$ \\ (Polynomial Time)};
    
    \node[draw=red!80!black, fill=red!10, rounded corners, minimum width=3cm, minimum height=1.5cm, align=center] (opt_oracle) at (0, -0.5) {$\mathcal{A}_{OPT}(G)$ \\ \small (Assumed Poly-Time Oracle)};

    % Draw inputs and outputs
    \node (input) at (-4, 0.5) {Input $\langle G, k \rangle$};
    \node (output_yes) at (4, 0.8) {\texttt{TRUE}};
    \node (output_no) at (4, -0.8) {\texttt{FALSE}};

    % Arrows
    \draw[->] (input) -- (-1.75, 0.5);
    \draw[->] (-1.2, 0) -- (opt_oracle.north west) node[midway, left, font=\small] {$G$};
    \draw[->] (opt_oracle.north east) -- (1.2, 0) node[midway, right, font=\small] {$S_{max}$};
    
    \draw[->] (1.75, 0.8) -- (output_yes) node[midway, above, font=\small] {if $|S_{max}| \ge k$};
    \draw[->] (1.75, -0.8) -- (output_no) node[midway, above, font=\small] {if $|S_{max}| < k$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Proof of Correctness}
We must prove that $\mathcal{A}_{DEC}$ returns \texttt{TRUE} if and only if $G$ contains an independent set of size at least $k$.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Forward Direction ($\implies$):} 
    Suppose $\mathcal{A}_{DEC}$ returns \texttt{TRUE}. According to our algorithm, this happens if and only if $m \ge k$. Since $m$ is the size of $S_{max}$ (a valid independent set returned by $\mathcal{A}_{OPT}$), the graph contains at least one independent set of size $\ge k$.
    
    \item \textbf{Backward Direction ($\impliedby$):} 
    Suppose $G$ has an independent set $S$ of size at least $k$ (so $|S| \ge k$). By definition, the optimization oracle $\mathcal{A}_{OPT}$ returns an independent set $S_{max}$ of the maximum possible size in $G$. Therefore, $|S_{max}| \ge |S| \ge k$. The algorithm $\mathcal{A}_{DEC}$ evaluates $m \ge k$ as \texttt{TRUE} and correctly accepts the input.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{4. Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} The algorithm $\mathcal{A}_{DEC}$ performs exactly one call to the oracle $\mathcal{A}_{OPT}$. Counting the size of the set $S_{max}$ takes $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ time, and the comparison $m \ge k$ takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time. If $\mathcal{A}_{OPT}$ runs in polynomial time $\mathcal{P}_{OPT}(|V|, |E|)$, the total running time of $\mathcal{A}_{DEC}$ is $\mathcal{O}(\mathcal{P}_{OPT}(|V|, |E|) + |V|)$, which is strictly polynomial.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Conclusion}
We have shown that if the optimization version of the Independent Set problem can be solved in polynomial time, then the NP-complete decision version can also be solved in polynomial time. This means the optimization version is at least as hard as the decision version. Therefore, \textbf{IS-OPT is NP-hard}.

\textit{(Note: Optimization problems do not simply answer YES/NO, so they technically do not belong to the class $\mathcal{NP}$, which is restricted to decision problems. Hence, the optimization version is specifically $\mathcal{NP}$-hard, but not $\mathcal{NP}$-complete.)}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What are the traveling-salesman problem, Euler tour problem and Hamiltonian cycle problem? If $L_1 \leq_p L_2$ and $L_2 \leq_p L_3$, then prove that $L_1 \leq_p L_3$. \mk{3+7=10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2011--12}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsubsection*{1. Problem Definitions \hfill \normalsize\mdseries\textit{[3 Marks]}}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Hamiltonian Cycle Problem:} 
    Given an undirected (or directed) graph $G = (V, E)$, a \textit{Hamiltonian cycle} is a simple cycle that visits every vertex $v \in V$ exactly once and returns to the starting vertex. The problem asks whether such a cycle exists in $G$. It is a well-known \textbf{NP-complete} problem.

    \item \textbf{Traveling-Salesman Problem (TSP):} 
    The TSP is the weighted generalization of the Hamiltonian cycle problem. Given a complete graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative edge weights $w(u,v)$ and a cost bound $K$, the decision version of TSP asks whether there exists a Hamiltonian cycle (a tour visiting every vertex exactly once) with a total weight of at most $K$. It is also \textbf{NP-complete}.

    \item \textbf{Euler Tour Problem:} 
    Given a connected graph $G = (V, E)$, an \textit{Euler tour} (or Eulerian circuit) is a cycle that traverses every edge $e \in E$ exactly once (vertices may be visited multiple times). The problem asks whether such a tour exists in $G$. Unlike the previous two, this problem is in \textbf{$\mathcal{P}$} and can be solved in $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ time (a connected undirected graph has an Euler tour if and only if every vertex has an even degree).
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Proof of Transitivity of Polynomial-Time Reductions \hfill \normalsize\mdseries\textit{[7 Marks]}}

\textbf{Theorem:} If $L_1 \leq_p L_2$ and $L_2 \leq_p L_3$, then $L_1 \leq_p L_3$.

\paragraph{Proof:}
By the definition of a polynomial-time reduction ($L_A \leq_p L_B$), there exists a polynomial-time computable function that maps instances of $L_A$ to instances of $L_B$ while preserving the ``yes/no'' answer.

\begin{enumerate}
