    \item \textbf{Given $L_1 \leq_p L_2$:} There exists a function $f: \Sigma^* \to \Sigma^*$ computable in polynomial time such that for any input string $x$:
    $$x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2$$
    Let the time complexity of computing $f(x)$ be bounded by $p(|x|)$, where $p$ is a polynomial (e.g., $p(n) = \mathcal{O}(n^k)$).

    \item \textbf{Given $L_2 \leq_p L_3$:} There exists a function $g: \Sigma^* \to \Sigma^*$ computable in polynomial time such that for any input string $y$:
    $$y \in L_2 \iff g(y) \in L_3$$
    Let the time complexity of computing $g(y)$ be bounded by $q(|y|)$, where $q$ is a polynomial (e.g., $q(m) = \mathcal{O}(m^j)$).

    \item \textbf{Constructing the Reduction $L_1 \leq_p L_3$:} 
    We define a new composite function $h: \Sigma^* \to \Sigma^*$ as:
    $$h(x) = g(f(x))$$
    To prove $L_1 \leq_p L_3$, we must show two things about $h(x)$:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{Correctness (Mapping Preserves Validity):}
        For any input $x$:
        $$x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2 \iff g(f(x)) \in L_3 \iff h(x) \in L_3$$
        Thus, $h$ correctly maps ``yes'' instances of $L_1$ to ``yes'' instances of $L_3$, and ``no'' instances to ``no'' instances.
        
        \item \textbf{Polynomial Time Bound:}
        We must show that $h(x)$ can be computed in polynomial time. 
        \begin{itemize}
            \item The Turing machine computing $f(x)$ halts in at most $p(|x|)$ steps. Since a machine can write at most one symbol per step, the length of the output $f(x)$ is strictly bounded by the time it takes to compute it. Therefore, $|f(x)| \leq p(|x|)$.
            \item The function $g$ takes the string $f(x)$ as input. The time to compute $g(f(x))$ is bounded by $q(|f(x)|)$.
            \item Substituting the maximum length of $f(x)$, the time to compute $g(f(x))$ is at most $q(p(|x|))$.
        \end{itemize}
        Because the composition of two polynomials $q(p(n))$ is itself a polynomial, the total time to compute $h(x)$ is polynomial in the size of the original input $x$.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Diagrammatic Representation:}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick, node distance=4cm]
    % Nodes
    \node[draw=cD!80!black, fill=cD!10, rounded corners, minimum height=1.2cm, minimum width=2.5cm, align=center] (L1) at (0,0) {Instance $x$ \\ $\in L_1$};
    
    \node[draw=cD!80!black, fill=cD!10, rounded corners, minimum height=1.2cm, minimum width=2.5cm, align=center] (L2) at (4.5,0) {Instance $y = f(x)$ \\ $\in L_2$};
    
    \node[draw=cD!80!black, fill=cD!10, rounded corners, minimum height=1.2cm, minimum width=2.5cm, align=center] (L3) at (9,0) {Instance $z = g(y)$ \\ $\in L_3$};

    % Edges
    \draw[->] (L1) -- (L2) node[midway, above, font=\small] {Function $f$} node[midway, below, font=\small] {Time: $\mathcal{O}(|x|^k)$};
    
    \draw[->] (L2) -- (L3) node[midway, above, font=\small] {Function $g$} node[midway, below, font=\small] {Time: $\mathcal{O}(|y|^j)$};

    % Composite Edge
    \draw[->, dashed, color=cD!80!black] (L1.south) to[out=-25, in=205] node[midway, below, font=\small\bfseries] {Composite Function $h(x) = g(f(x))$ in Time $\mathcal{O}(|x|^{k \cdot j})$} (L3.south);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{Conclusion:} Since $h(x)$ correctly maps instances of $L_1$ to $L_3$ and runs in polynomial time, we have successfully proven that \textbf{$L_1 \leq_p L_3$}. Polynomial-time reductions are transitive.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Prove that the decision version of the maximum clique problem is NP-complete by reducing from 3-CNF-SAT. \mk{17}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2009--10}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

To prove that the decision version of the Maximum Clique problem is NP-complete, we must satisfy two fundamental conditions:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Show that \textbf{CLIQUE $\in \mathcal{NP}$}.
    \item Show that \textbf{CLIQUE is NP-hard} by providing a polynomial-time reduction from a known NP-complete problem, specifically \textbf{3-CNF-SAT}, to CLIQUE ($\text{3-CNF-SAT} \leq_p \text{CLIQUE}$).
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{1. Formal Problem Definitions}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{3-CNF-SAT:} Given a Boolean formula $\phi$ in Conjunctive Normal Form where each clause contains exactly 3 literals (e.g., $\phi = C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge \dots \wedge C_k$), is there a truth assignment to the variables that makes $\phi$ evaluate to \texttt{TRUE}?
    \item \textbf{CLIQUE:} Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $K$, does $G$ contain a complete subgraph (clique) of size at least $K$?
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Proof of Membership in $\mathcal{NP}$}
A problem is in $\mathcal{NP}$ if any proposed solution (certificate) can be verified by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Certificate:} A subset of vertices $V' \subseteq V$ where $|V'| = K$.
    \item \textbf{Verification:} Iterate through all pairs of vertices $u, v \in V'$. Check if the edge $(u, v)$ exists in $E$.
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} There are $\binom{K}{2}$ pairs to check, which takes $\mathcal{O}(K^2)$ time. Since $K \le |V|$, the verification takes $\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$ time, which is strictly polynomial. Thus, \textbf{CLIQUE $\in \mathcal{NP}$}.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Polynomial-Time Reduction (\text{3-CNF-SAT} $\leq_p$ \text{CLIQUE})}

We must construct a polynomial-time algorithm that transforms any 3-CNF-SAT instance $\phi$ into a graph instance $\langle G, K \rangle$ such that $\phi$ is satisfiable \textit{if and only if} $G$ has a clique of size $K$.

\paragraph{Construction Algorithm:}
Let $\phi = C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge \dots \wedge C_k$ be a formula with $k$ clauses, where each clause $C_r = (l_1^r \vee l_2^r \vee l_3^r)$.
\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Reduction from 3-CNF-SAT to CLIQUE}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} A 3-CNF formula $\phi$ with $k$ clauses.
\State \textbf{Output:} An undirected graph $G=(V, E)$ and target clique size $K$.
\State $V \gets \emptyset$, $E \gets \emptyset$, $K \gets k$
\For{each clause $C_r = (l_1^r \vee l_2^r \vee l_3^r)$ for $r = 1 \dots k$}
    \State Add vertices $v_1^r, v_2^r, v_3^r$ to $V$. \Comment{Create a vertex for every literal}
\EndFor
\For{each pair of vertices $v_i^r, v_j^s \in V$}
    \If{$r \neq s$} \Comment{Vertices belong to \textbf{different} clauses}
        \If{$l_i^r \neq \neg l_j^s$} \Comment{Literals are \textbf{not} logical negations of each other}
            \State Add edge $(v_i^r, v_j^s)$ to $E$.
        \EndIf
    \EndIf
\EndFor
\State \Return $\langle G, K \rangle$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\paragraph{Complexity of Reduction:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time \& Space Complexity:} The algorithm creates exactly $3k$ vertices. It checks every pair of vertices to add edges, requiring $\binom{3k}{2}$ comparisons. The total time and space complexity is $\mathcal{O}(k^2)$, which is polynomial in the size of the input formula.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Visualization of the Construction:}
Consider a simple instance $\phi = C_1 \wedge C_2$ where $C_1 = (x_1 \vee \neg x_2 \vee x_3)$ and $C_2 = (\neg x_1 \vee \neg x_2 \vee \neg x_3)$. We map this to a graph seeking a clique of size $K = 2$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, node distance=2.5cm, 
    vnode/.style={circle, draw=cD!80!black, thick, fill=cD!10, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=0pt},
    cliquenode/.style={circle, draw=red!80!black, very thick, fill=red!20, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=0pt}]

    % Clause 1 Nodes
    \node[vnode] (L1) at (0, 2) {$x_1$};
    \node[cliquenode] (L2) at (0, 0) {$\neg x_2$};
    \node[vnode] (L3) at (0, -2) {$x_3$};

    % Clause 2 Nodes
    \node[vnode] (R1) at (5, 2) {$\neg x_1$};
    \node[cliquenode] (R2) at (5, 0) {$\neg x_2$};
    \node[vnode] (R3) at (5, -2) {$\neg x_3$};

    % Clause Boxes
    \draw[dashed, thick, rounded corners, draw=gray] (-1, 3) rectangle (1, -3);
    \node[above, font=\bfseries] at (0, 3) {Clause $C_1$};

    \draw[dashed, thick, rounded corners, draw=gray] (4, 3) rectangle (6, -3);
    \node[above, font=\bfseries] at (5, 3) {Clause $C_2$};

    % Edges
    \draw[gray, thick] (L1) -- (R2);
    \draw[gray, thick] (L1) -- (R3);
    % L1 to R1 is NO EDGE (x1 and \neg x1)
    
    \draw[gray, thick] (L2) -- (R1);
    \draw[red!80!black, line width=1.5pt] (L2) -- (R2); % CLIQUE EDGE
    \draw[gray, thick] (L2) -- (R3);

    \draw[gray, thick] (L3) -- (R1);
    \draw[gray, thick] (L3) -- (R2);
    % L3 to R3 is NO EDGE (x3 and \neg x3)

    \node[below, text width=10cm, align=center, font=\small] at (2.5, -3.5) {
        \textbf{Figure:} Edges connect non-contradictory literals from different clauses. The highlighted nodes ($\neg x_2, \neg x_2$) form a $K=2$ clique, corresponding to the satisfying assignment $x_2 = \text{False}$.
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Proof of Correctness ($\phi$ is satisfiable $\iff G$ has a $K$-clique)}

\paragraph{Forward Direction ($\implies$):} 
Assume $\phi$ is satisfiable. 
\begin{enumerate}
    \item There exists a truth assignment that makes at least one literal \texttt{TRUE} in every clause $C_r$ ($1 \le r \le k$).
    \item From each clause $C_r$, select exactly one vertex $v_i^r$ corresponding to a \texttt{TRUE} literal. This gives us a subset $V'$ of exactly $k = K$ vertices.
    \item We must show $V'$ is a clique. Take any two vertices $v_i^r, v_j^s \in V'$. 
    \item Because we picked one vertex per clause, $r \neq s$. 
    \item Because both literals evaluate to \texttt{TRUE} under the same assignment, they cannot be logical opposites (i.e., we cannot have $x$ and $\neg x$ both \texttt{TRUE}). Thus, $l_i^r \neq \neg l_j^s$.
    \item By our construction algorithm, an edge exists between $v_i^r$ and $v_j^s$. Therefore, $V'$ is a clique of size $K$.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Backward Direction ($\impliedby$):} 
Assume $G$ has a clique $V'$ of size $K = k$.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Since no edges exist between vertices in the same clause, a clique can contain at most one vertex from any given clause.
    \item Because the clique has size $k$ and there are exactly $k$ clauses, the clique must contain \textit{exactly} one vertex from every clause.
    \item For every vertex $v_i^r \in V'$, assign its corresponding literal $l_i^r$ to \texttt{TRUE}. 
    \item This assignment is mathematically valid because all vertices in the clique are connected by edges. By construction, an edge guarantees that $l_i^r \neq \neg l_j^s$. Thus, we will never assign \texttt{TRUE} to both a variable and its negation.
    \item Assign any unmapped variables arbitrarily.
    \item Since every clause contains at least one literal set to \texttt{TRUE}, the entire formula $\phi$ is satisfied.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Conclusion}
Because CLIQUE is in $\mathcal{NP}$ and 3-CNF-SAT can be reduced to CLIQUE in polynomial time, the \textbf{Maximum Clique Decision Problem is NP-complete}.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What do you mean by NP-hard and NP-complete problems? Prove that finding a clique in a graph is NP-complete. \mk{5+12}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2008--09}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsubsection*{1. Definitions of NP-Hard and NP-Complete \hfill \normalsize\mdseries\textit{[5 Marks]}}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{NP-Hard:} A problem $H$ is NP-hard if \textit{every} problem $L \in \mathcal{NP}$ can be reduced to $H$ in polynomial time (denoted as $L \leq_p H$). Intuitively, NP-hard problems are "at least as hard" as the hardest problems in $\mathcal{NP}$. An NP-hard problem does not necessarily have to be in $\mathcal{NP}$ (it could be undecidable, for example).
    
    \item \textbf{NP-Complete ($\mathcal{NPC}$):} A problem $C$ is NP-complete if it satisfies two strict conditions:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $C \in \mathcal{NP}$ (Its solutions can be verified in polynomial time).
        \item $C$ is NP-hard (Every problem in $\mathcal{NP}$ reduces to it in polynomial time).
    \end{enumerate}
    The $\mathcal{NPC}$ class represents the absolute hardest problems \textit{within} the $\mathcal{NP}$ complexity class.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Proof: Finding a Clique is NP-Complete \hfill \normalsize\mdseries\textit{[12 Marks]}}

To formally prove that the \textbf{CLIQUE} decision problem is NP-complete, we must show that it is in $\mathcal{NP}$ and that it is NP-hard by reducing a known NP-complete problem to it. We will use the \textbf{Independent Set (IS)} problem for our reduction.

\paragraph{Definitions:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{CLIQUE:} Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $K$, does $G$ contain a complete subgraph of size $\geq K$?
    \item \textbf{Independent Set (IS):} Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k$, does $G$ contain a subset of vertices of size $\geq k$ such that no two vertices in the subset share an edge? (This is a known NP-complete problem).
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Step 1: Prove CLIQUE $\in \mathcal{NP}$}
A problem is in $\mathcal{NP}$ if a proposed solution (a certificate) can be verified in polynomial time.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Certificate:} A subset of vertices $V' \subseteq V$ where $|V'| = K$.
    \item \textbf{Verification:} Iterate through all pairs of distinct vertices $u, v \in V'$ and check if the edge $(u, v)$ exists in $E$. 
    \item \textbf{Complexity:} There are $\binom{K}{2}$ pairs to check. Checking an edge takes $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time using an adjacency matrix. The total verification time is $\mathcal{O}(K^2)$. Since $K \le |V|$, this bounds to $\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$, which is strictly polynomial. Thus, \textbf{CLIQUE $\in \mathcal{NP}$}.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Step 2: Polynomial-Time Reduction (IS $\leq_p$ CLIQUE)}
We must construct an algorithm that transforms any Independent Set instance $\langle G, k \rangle$ into a CLIQUE instance $\langle G', K \rangle$ such that $G$ has an independent set of size $k$ \textit{if and only if} $G'$ has a clique of size $K$.

The core intuition is to use the \textbf{Complement Graph} $\overline{G}$.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Reduction from Independent Set to CLIQUE}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Undirected graph $G=(V, E)$ and integer $k$.
\State \textbf{Output:} Undirected graph $\overline{G}=(V, \overline{E})$ and integer $K$.
\State $\overline{E} \gets \emptyset$ \Comment{Initialize the complement edge set}
\For{every pair of distinct vertices $u, v \in V$}
    \If{$(u, v) \notin E$}
        \State $\overline{E} \gets \overline{E} \cup \{(u, v)\}$ \Comment{Add edge if it does NOT exist in original graph}
    \EndIf
\EndFor
\State $K \gets k$ \Comment{The target size remains identical}
\State \Return $\langle \overline{G}, K \rangle$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\textbf{Complexity of Reduction:} Generating the complement graph requires checking every possible pair of vertices in $V$. There are exactly $\frac{|V|(|V|-1)}{2}$ pairs, so the reduction takes \textbf{$\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$ time and space}, making it a valid polynomial-time reduction.

\paragraph{Visualization of the Reduction:}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=2cm, 
    vnode/.style={circle, draw=cD!80!black, thick, fill=white, minimum size=6mm},
    highlight/.style={circle, draw=green!80!black, very thick, fill=green!10, minimum size=6mm},
    clique/.style={circle, draw=red!80!black, very thick, fill=red!10, minimum size=6mm}]

    % Graph G
    \node[vnode] (4a) at (0,0) {4};
    \node[highlight] (1a) at (0,1.6) {1};
    \node[highlight] (2a) at (-1.4,-0.8) {2};
    \node[highlight] (3a) at (1.4,-0.8) {3};
    
    \draw[thick] (4a) -- (1a);
    \draw[thick] (4a) -- (2a);
    \draw[thick] (4a) -- (3a);
    
    \node[align=center] at (0, -2.2) {\textbf{Original Graph $G$} \\ Independent Set $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$};

    % Arrow
    \draw[->, very thick] (2.5, 0.2) -- (4.0, 0.2) node[midway, above, font=\small\bfseries] {Complement};

    % Graph G-bar
    \begin{scope}[shift={(6.5,0)}]
        \node[vnode] (4b) at (0,0) {4};
        \node[clique] (1b) at (0,1.6) {1};
        \node[clique] (2b) at (-1.4,-0.8) {2};
        \node[clique] (3b) at (1.4,-0.8) {3};
        
        \draw[very thick, red!80!black] (1b) -- (2b);
        \draw[very thick, red!80!black] (2b) -- (3b);
        \draw[very thick, red!80!black] (1b) -- (3b);
        
        \node[align=center] at (0, -2.2) {\textbf{Complement Graph $\overline{G}$} \\ Clique $C = \{1, 2, 3\}$};
    \end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\paragraph{Step 3: Proof of Correctness}
We must prove that $G$ has an independent set of size $k \iff \overline{G}$ has a clique of size $k$.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Forward Direction ($\implies$):} 
    Assume $G$ contains an independent set $S$ of size $k$. By the definition of an independent set, for every pair of distinct vertices $u, v \in S$, the edge $(u, v) \notin E$. By the construction of our complement graph $\overline{G}$, an edge $(u, v)$ is added to $\overline{E}$ if and only if $(u, v) \notin E$. Therefore, in $\overline{G}$, \textit{every} pair of vertices in $S$ is connected by an edge. This means $S$ forms a complete subgraph (a clique) of size $k$ in $\overline{G}$.
    
    \item \textbf{Backward Direction ($\impliedby$):} 
    Assume $\overline{G}$ contains a clique $C$ of size $K = k$. By the definition of a clique, for every pair of distinct vertices $u, v \in C$, the edge $(u, v) \in \overline{E}$. Based on our reduction algorithm, an edge exists in $\overline{E}$ if and only if it does \textit{not} exist in $E$. Therefore, no two vertices in $C$ share an edge in the original graph $G$. This means $C$ is an independent set of size $k$ in $G$.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Conclusion:}
Because CLIQUE $\in \mathcal{NP}$ and the known NP-complete problem Independent Set is reducible to CLIQUE in polynomial time, we conclude that finding a clique in a graph is \textbf{NP-complete}.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write down the procedure of proving that a problem is NP-complete. \mk{5}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2008--09}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

To formally prove that a given decision problem $L$ is NP-complete, one must demonstrate that $L$ is both in the class $\mathcal{NP}$ and is NP-hard. The standard procedure to establish this consists of the following four structured steps:

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Prove Membership in $\mathcal{NP}$ ($L \in \mathcal{NP}$)}
First, you must show that the problem belongs to the complexity class $\mathcal{NP}$. This requires proving that if the answer to an instance of the problem is ``YES'', there exists a proposed solution (often called a \textbf{certificate} or witness) that can be verified in polynomial time.
\begin{itemize}
    \item Define what constitutes a valid certificate for the problem.
    \item Provide a deterministic verification algorithm.
    \item Analyze the verification algorithm to ensure its time complexity is bounded by a polynomial in the size of the input.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Select a Known NP-Complete Problem}
Choose a well-known decision problem $L'$ that has already been proven to be NP-complete (e.g., 3-SAT, Vertex Cover, Clique, or Hamiltonian Cycle). The choice of $L'$ is strategic; you should select a problem whose underlying structure closely resembles the target problem $L$.

\subsubsection*{Step 3: Construct a Polynomial-Time Reduction ($L' \leq_p L$)}
Design an algorithm (or transformation function $f$) that maps any arbitrary instance $x$ of the known problem $L'$ to an instance $f(x)$ of the target problem $L$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Polynomial Time:} You must strictly prove that the process of translating instance $x$ into instance $f(x)$ takes time polynomial in the size of $x$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 4: Prove Correctness of the Reduction}
You must prove that the transformation preserves the validity of the instances. Specifically, you must show that $x$ is a ``YES'' instance of $L'$ if and only if $f(x)$ is a ``YES'' instance of $L$. This requires a two-part mathematical proof:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Forward Direction ($\implies$):} Prove that if $x \in L'$, then the constructed instance $f(x) \in L$.
    \item \textbf{Backward Direction ($\impliedby$):} Prove that if the constructed instance $f(x) \in L$, then the original instance $x \in L'$. (Alternatively, prove the contrapositive: if $x \notin L'$, then $f(x) \notin L$).
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\paragraph{Visualizing the Reduction Process:}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick, node distance=3cm]

    % Bounding Box for L'
    \node[draw=red!80!black, fill=red!5, rounded corners, minimum width=3.5cm, minimum height=3cm, align=center] (L_prime) at (0,0) {};
    \node[align=center, font=\bfseries, text=red!80!black] at (0, 1.1) {Known NP-Complete \\ Problem $L'$};
    \node[draw=black, fill=white, rounded corners, minimum width=2cm, minimum height=0.8cm, align=center] (x) at (0, -0.4) {Instance $x$};

    % Bounding Box for L
    \node[draw=cD!80!black, fill=cD!5, rounded corners, minimum width=3.5cm, minimum height=3cm, align=center] (L) at (7,0) {};
    \node[align=center, font=\bfseries, text=cD!80!black] at (7, 1.1) {Target Problem $L$};
    \node[draw=black, fill=white, rounded corners, minimum width=2cm, minimum height=0.8cm, align=center] (fx) at (7, -0.4) {Instance $f(x)$};

    % Reduction Arrow
    \draw[->, line width=1.2pt] (x.east) -- (fx.west) node[midway, above, font=\small\bfseries] {Reduction $f$} node[midway, below, font=\small] {Polynomial Time};

    % Yes/No equivalence
    \draw[<->, dashed, color=blue!70!black] (0, -1.8) -- (7, -1.8) node[midway, fill=white, font=\small\bfseries] {YES $\iff$ YES};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{Conclusion:} If all four steps are successfully demonstrated, the problem $L$ is formally proven to be NP-complete.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item There are clauses $C_1 = (X_1 \vee \overline{X_2})$, $C_2 = (\overline{X_1} \vee X_2 \vee X_4 \vee \overline{X_5})$, $C_3 = \overline{X_2}$, and $C_4 = (X_2 \vee X_3 \vee \overline{X_4})$. How the above instance of the satisfiability problem can be transformed into an instance of 3-SAT problem? ($X_1$, $X_2$, $X_3$, $X_4$ and $X_5$ are boolean variables) \mk{13}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2008--09}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

To transform a general Satisfiability (SAT) instance into a 3-SAT instance, we must convert every clause $C_i$ into a set of clauses where each clause contains exactly 3 literals. We achieve this by introducing auxiliary (dummy) boolean variables. 

The original SAT formula $\Phi = C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge C_3 \wedge C_4$. We will transform each clause individually based on its length $k$ (the number of literals).

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{1. General Transformation Rules}

For a clause $C = (l_1 \vee l_2 \vee \dots \vee l_k)$ with $k$ literals:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{If $k = 1$:} Introduce two new auxiliary variables, $Y_a$ and $Y_b$. Replace $C$ with $4$ clauses to cover all truth assignments of the new variables:
    $$ (l_1 \vee Y_a \vee Y_b) \wedge (l_1 \vee Y_a \vee \overline{Y_b}) \wedge (l_1 \vee \overline{Y_a} \vee Y_b) \wedge (l_1 \vee \overline{Y_a} \vee \overline{Y_b}) $$
    \item \textbf{If $k = 2$:} Introduce one new auxiliary variable, $Y_c$. Replace $C$ with $2$ clauses:
    $$ (l_1 \vee l_2 \vee Y_c) \wedge (l_1 \vee l_2 \vee \overline{Y_c}) $$
    \item \textbf{If $k = 3$:} The clause is already in 3-SAT form. Keep it unchanged.
    \item \textbf{If $k > 3$:} Introduce $k-3$ new auxiliary variables $Z_1, Z_2, \dots, Z_{k-3}$. Replace $C$ with a "chain" of $k-2$ clauses:
    $$ (l_1 \vee l_2 \vee Z_1) \wedge (\overline{Z_1} \vee l_3 \vee Z_2) \wedge \dots \wedge (\overline{Z_{k-3}} \vee l_{k-1} \vee l_k) $$
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Step-by-Step Transformation of the Instance}

\paragraph{Clause 1: $C_1 = (X_1 \vee \overline{X_2})$}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Here, $k = 2$.
    \item We introduce a new dummy variable $Y_1$.
    \item The transformed clauses are:
    $$ C_1' = (X_1 \vee \overline{X_2} \vee Y_1) \wedge (X_1 \vee \overline{X_2} \vee \overline{Y_1}) $$
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Clause 2: $C_2 = (\overline{X_1} \vee X_2 \vee X_4 \vee \overline{X_5})$}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Here, $k = 4$.
    \item We introduce $k-3 = 1$ new dummy variable, let's call it $Z_1$.
    \item We split the clause, linking the two parts with $Z_1$ and $\overline{Z_1}$.
    \item The transformed clauses are:
    $$ C_2' = (\overline{X_1} \vee X_2 \vee Z_1) \wedge (\overline{Z_1} \vee X_4 \vee \overline{X_5}) $$
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick, node distance=2cm, 
    clnode/.style={rectangle, draw=cD!80!black, rounded corners, fill=cD!10, minimum height=8mm, align=center},
    varnode/.style={circle, draw=black, fill=white, minimum size=6mm, inner sep=0pt}]

    % Original variables
    \node (l1) at (-4, 1.5) {$\overline{X_1}$};
    \node (l2) at (-2, 1.5) {$X_2$};
    \node (l3) at (2, 1.5) {$X_4$};
    \node (l4) at (4, 1.5) {$\overline{X_5}$};

    % Split Clauses
    \node[clnode] (C2a) at (-3, 0) {$\overline{X_1} \vee X_2 \vee \mathbf{Z_1}$};
    \node[clnode] (C2b) at (3, 0) {$\mathbf{\overline{Z_1}} \vee X_4 \vee \overline{X_5}$};

    % Linking Variable
    \node[varnode] (Z) at (0, 0) {$Z_1$};

    % Arrows
    \draw[->] (l1) -- (C2a);
    \draw[->] (l2) -- (C2a);
    \draw[->] (l3) -- (C2b);
    \draw[->] (l4) -- (C2b);

    \draw[->, blue!70!black, dashed] (Z) -- (C2a) node[midway, above, font=\scriptsize] {$Z_1$};
    \draw[->, red!70!black, dashed] (Z) -- (C2b) node[midway, above, font=\scriptsize] {$\overline{Z_1}$};

    \node[below, font=\small, text width=10cm, align=center] at (0, -1.2) {
        \textbf{Figure:} Splitting a 4-literal clause into two 3-literal clauses using a linking variable $Z_1$.
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\paragraph{Clause 3: $C_3 = (\overline{X_2})$}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Here, $k = 1$.
    \item We introduce two new dummy variables, $Y_2$ and $Y_3$.
    \item The transformed clauses are:
    $$ C_3' = (\overline{X_2} \vee Y_2 \vee Y_3) \wedge (\overline{X_2} \vee Y_2 \vee \overline{Y_3}) \wedge (\overline{X_2} \vee \overline{Y_2} \vee Y_3) \wedge (\overline{X_2} \vee \overline{Y_2} \vee \overline{Y_3}) $$
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Clause 4: $C_4 = (X_2 \vee X_3 \vee \overline{X_4})$}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Here, $k = 3$.
    \item The clause already has exactly 3 literals, so no dummy variables are needed.
    \item The transformed clause is:
    $$ C_4' = (X_2 \vee X_3 \vee \overline{X_4}) $$
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Final 3-SAT Instance}

The complete transformed 3-SAT instance, $\Phi'$, is the conjunction of all the transformed clause sets $C_1' \wedge C_2' \wedge C_3' \wedge C_4'$.

\begin{align*}
\Phi' = \quad & (X_1 \vee \overline{X_2} \vee Y_1) \wedge (X_1 \vee \overline{X_2} \vee \overline{Y_1}) \\
\wedge \quad & (\overline{X_1} \vee X_2 \vee Z_1) \wedge (\overline{Z_1} \vee X_4 \vee \overline{X_5}) \\
\wedge \quad & (\overline{X_2} \vee Y_2 \vee Y_3) \wedge (\overline{X_2} \vee Y_2 \vee \overline{Y_3}) \wedge (\overline{X_2} \vee \overline{Y_2} \vee Y_3) \wedge (\overline{X_2} \vee \overline{Y_2} \vee \overline{Y_3}) \\
\wedge \quad & (X_2 \vee X_3 \vee \overline{X_4})
\end{align*}

\subsubsection*{4. Correctness and Complexity}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Correctness:} The original formula $\Phi$ is satisfiable if and only if $\Phi'$ is satisfiable. Any satisfying assignment for $\Phi$ can be extended to satisfy $\Phi'$ by appropriately choosing the truth values of the dummy variables (e.g., $Z_1$ acts as a carry signal for the truth value).
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} Each clause of size $k$ is replaced by $\max(1, k-2)$ or at most 4 clauses. Generating these takes $\mathcal{O}(k)$ time per clause. Thus, the total reduction time is strictly polynomial: $\mathcal{O}(N)$ where $N$ is the total length of the original formula.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(N)$ auxiliary space to store the new variables and the new strictly 3-CNF string.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Define: (i) size of a problem, (ii) time complexity function, (iii) the class P, (iv) the class NP, (v) NP-Complete problems. Show how an instance of the satisfiability problem can be transformed into an instance of 3-SAT. Prove that 3-colouring of a graph is NP-complete. \mk{10+10+15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2007--08}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsubsection*{1. Definitions \hfill \normalsize\mdseries\textit{[10 Marks]}}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{(i) Size of a problem:} The number of bits (or symbols) required to represent a specific instance of the problem under a reasonable and concise encoding scheme. It is typically denoted as $n$ and dictates the input size for analyzing algorithmic complexity.
    \item \textbf{(ii) Time complexity function:} A mathematical function $T(n)$ that expresses the worst-case maximum number of basic operations (or steps on a deterministic Turing machine) required to solve a problem instance of size $n$.
    \item \textbf{(iii) The class $\mathcal{P}$:} The set of all decision problems that can be solved (decided) by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time. That is, $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n^k)$ for some constant $k$.
    \item \textbf{(iv) The class $\mathcal{NP}$:} The set of all decision problems for which a proposed solution (certificate) can be verified as correct by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time. Equivalently, it is the class of problems solvable in polynomial time by a \textit{non-deterministic} Turing machine.
    \item \textbf{(v) NP-Complete problems ($\mathcal{NPC}$):} The hardest problems in $\mathcal{NP}$. A problem $L$ is NP-Complete if:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $L \in \mathcal{NP}$.
        \item Every problem $L' \in \mathcal{NP}$ is polynomial-time reducible to $L$ ($L' \leq_p L$).
    \end{enumerate}
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Transformation: SAT to 3-SAT \hfill \normalsize\mdseries\textit{[10 Marks]}}

To transform a general Satisfiability (SAT) instance into a 3-SAT instance, we process every clause $C = (l_1 \vee l_2 \vee \dots \vee l_k)$ individually to ensure it contains exactly 3 literals, using auxiliary variables.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Case $k = 1$:} $C = (l_1)$. Introduce two new variables $y_1, y_2$. Expand into 4 clauses:
    $$ (l_1 \vee y_1 \vee y_2) \wedge (l_1 \vee y_1 \vee \overline{y_2}) \wedge (l_1 \vee \overline{y_1} \vee y_2) \wedge (l_1 \vee \overline{y_1} \vee \overline{y_2}) $$
    \item \textbf{Case $k = 2$:} $C = (l_1 \vee l_2)$. Introduce one new variable $y_1$. Expand into 2 clauses:
    $$ (l_1 \vee l_2 \vee y_1) \wedge (l_1 \vee l_2 \vee \overline{y_1}) $$
    \item \textbf{Case $k = 3$:} $C = (l_1 \vee l_2 \vee l_3)$. The clause is already in 3-CNF format. Leave as is.
    \item \textbf{Case $k > 3$:} $C = (l_1 \vee l_2 \vee \dots \vee l_k)$. Introduce $k-3$ new variables $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{k-3}$. Transform into a chain of $k-2$ clauses:
    $$ (l_1 \vee l_2 \vee y_1) \wedge (\overline{y_1} \vee l_3 \vee y_2) \wedge (\overline{y_2} \vee l_4 \vee y_3) \wedge \dots \wedge (\overline{y_{k-3}} \vee l_{k-1} \vee l_k) $$
\end{itemize}
\textbf{Complexity:} This transformation takes time and space linear to the size of the original formula, $\mathcal{O}(N)$. Satisfiability is perfectly preserved.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Proof: 3-Coloring is NP-Complete \hfill \normalsize\mdseries\textit{[15 Marks]}}

\paragraph{Step 1: Show 3-Coloring $\in \mathcal{NP}$}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Certificate:} An array $C[1 \dots |V|]$ containing a color assignment $c \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ for each vertex.
    \item \textbf{Verification:} Iterate through every edge $(u, v) \in E$ and check if $C[u] \neq C[v]$. This takes $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ time, which is polynomial.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Step 2: Reduction from 3-SAT (3-SAT $\leq_p$ 3-Coloring)}
Given a 3-CNF formula $\Phi$, we construct a graph $G$ that is 3-colorable if and only if $\Phi$ is satisfiable. Let the colors be \textbf{T} (True), \textbf{F} (False), and \textbf{B} (Base).

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Base Gadget:} Create a triangle with vertices $\{T, F, B\}$. This forces the three vertices to each take a distinct color.
    \item \textbf{Variable Gadget:} For each boolean variable $x_i$, create a pair of vertices $v_i$ and $\overline{v_i}$. Connect them to each other and to the Base node $B$. This triangle $\{v_i, \overline{v_i}, B\}$ forces $v_i$ and $\overline{v_i}$ to take the remaining colors $T$ and $F$.
    \item \textbf{Clause Gadget (OR-Gate):} For each clause $C_j = (l_1 \vee l_2 \vee l_3)$, we build a 6-node subgraph with vertices $a, b, c, d, e, f$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Form two triangles: $(a, b, c)$ and $(d, e, f)$.
        \item Add edges $(l_1, a), (l_2, b), (B, c)$ and $(c, d), (l_3, e), (B, f)$.
        \item Finally, connect $f$ to the global $F$ node. (This forces $f = T$).
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick, node distance=2cm, 
    vnode/.style={circle, draw=cD!80!black, thick, fill=white, minimum size=6mm, inner sep=0pt},
    bnode/.style={circle, draw=black, very thick, fill=gray!20, minimum size=7mm, inner sep=0pt},
    tnode/.style={circle, draw=blue!80!black, very thick, fill=blue!10, minimum size=7mm, inner sep=0pt},
    fnode/.style={circle, draw=red!80!black, very thick, fill=red!10, minimum size=7mm, inner sep=0pt}]

    % Base Gadget
    \node[tnode] (T) at (-5, 1) {T};
    \node[fnode] (F) at (-5, -1) {F};
    \node[bnode] (B) at (-3.5, 0) {B};
    \draw (T) -- (F) -- (B) -- (T);
    \node[below, font=\small\bfseries] at (-4.5, -1.8) {Base Gadget};

    % Variable Gadget
    \node[vnode] (xi) at (-1.5, 1) {$v_i$};
    \node[vnode] (nxi) at (-1.5, -1) {$\overline{v_i}$};
    \draw (xi) -- (nxi);
    \draw[bend right=20, dashed] (xi) to (B);
    \draw[bend left=20, dashed] (nxi) to (B);
    \node[below, font=\small\bfseries] at (-1.5, -1.8) {Variable $x_i$};

    % Clause Gadget
    \node[vnode] (l1) at (2, 1.5) {$l_1$};
    \node[vnode] (l2) at (2, -1.5) {$l_2$};
    \node[vnode] (l3) at (4.5, -1.5) {$l_3$};

    \node[vnode] (a) at (3, 0.5) {$a$};
    \node[vnode] (b) at (3, -0.5) {$b$};
    \node[vnode] (c) at (4, 0) {$c$};

    \node[vnode] (d) at (5.5, 0.5) {$d$};
    \node[vnode] (e) at (5.5, -0.5) {$e$};
    \node[vnode] (f) at (6.5, 0) {$f$};

    \draw (a) -- (b) -- (c) -- (a);
    \draw (d) -- (e) -- (f) -- (d);
    
    \draw (l1) -- (a);
    \draw (l2) -- (b);
    \draw (c) -- (d);
    \draw (l3) -- (e);

    % Connect to B and F using local labels for diagram clarity
    \node[bnode, minimum size=5mm] (Bc) at (4, 1.2) {B};
    \node[bnode, minimum size=5mm] (Bf) at (6.5, 1.2) {B};
    \node[fnode, minimum size=5mm] (Ff) at (7.5, 0) {F};

    \draw[dashed] (c) -- (Bc);
    \draw[dashed] (f) -- (Bf);
    \draw[line width=1.2pt, red!80!black] (f) -- (Ff);

    \node[below, font=\small\bfseries] at (4.5, -2.5) {Clause Gadget for $(l_1 \vee l_2 \vee l_3)$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\paragraph{Step 3: Correctness}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Forward ($\implies$):} If $\Phi$ is satisfiable, assign $T$ and $F$ colors to the variable gadgets based on the truth assignment. For every clause, at least one literal is $T$. By carefully checking the OR-gadget rules, if at least one input literal ($l_1, l_2, \text{ or } l_3$) is $T$, the graph allows vertex $f$ to be colored $T$, satisfying the restrictive edge $(f, F)$. The graph is 3-colorable.
    \item \textbf{Backward ($\impliedby$):} If $G$ is 3-colorable, the colors of the variable gadgets yield a valid truth assignment. If there were a clause with all $F$ literals ($l_1=F, l_2=F, l_3=F$), the properties of triangles $(a,b,c)$ and $(d,e,f)$ combined with the connections to $B$ would forcefully propagate and dictate that $f$ \textit{must} be colored $F$. However, $f$ is connected to the global $F$ node. An edge between two $F$-colored nodes violates 3-coloring. Thus, no clause can have all $F$ literals. $\Phi$ is satisfiable.
\end{itemize}

Since the reduction is polynomial and preserves validity, 3-Coloring is \textbf{NP-Complete}.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Prove that 3-colouring of a graph is NP-complete.
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2007--08}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

To prove that the \textbf{3-Coloring} problem is NP-complete, we must formally demonstrate two properties:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item 3-Coloring $\in \mathcal{NP}$.
    \item 3-Coloring is NP-hard (by providing a polynomial-time reduction from a known NP-complete problem, such as \textbf{3-SAT}, to 3-Coloring).
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Step 1: Prove 3-Coloring $\in \mathcal{NP}$}
A problem is in $\mathcal{NP}$ if a proposed solution (certificate) can be verified in polynomial time.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Certificate:} An array or mapping $C: V \to \{1, 2, 3\}$ assigning a color to each vertex in the graph $G=(V,E)$.
    \item \textbf{Verification:} Iterate through every edge $(u, v) \in E$ and check if $C(u) \neq C(v)$.
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} Checking all edges takes $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ time, which is strictly polynomial in the size of the input. Thus, 3-Coloring $\in \mathcal{NP}$.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection*{Step 2: Polynomial-Time Reduction (3-SAT $\leq_p$ 3-Coloring)}
Given an arbitrary instance of 3-SAT (a boolean formula $\Phi$ with $n$ variables and $m$ clauses), we construct an undirected graph $G=(V,E)$ such that $G$ is 3-colorable \textit{if and only if} $\Phi$ is satisfiable. Let the three available colors be \textbf{T} (True), \textbf{F} (False), and \textbf{B} (Base). 

We construct $G$ using three types of "gadgets":

\paragraph{1. The Palette (Base) Gadget}
Create three vertices named $T$, $F$, and $B$, and connect them to form a triangle ($K_3$). 
\textit{Effect:} This forces the three vertices to each take a distinct color. We identify these colors as the foundational $T$, $F$, and $B$ values.

\paragraph{2. The Variable Gadget}
For each boolean variable $x_i$, create two vertices $v_i$ and $\overline{v_i}$. Connect them to each other, and connect both of them to the $B$ vertex of the Palette.
\textit{Effect:} The triangle $\{v_i, \overline{v_i}, B\}$ forces $v_i$ and $\overline{v_i}$ to be colored with $T$ and $F$ in some order, perfectly modeling a boolean variable and its negation.

\paragraph{3. The Clause Gadget (OR-Gate)}
For each clause $C_j = (l_1 \vee l_2 \vee l_3)$, we construct a 6-vertex gadget (vertices $a, b, c, d, e, f$) to act as an OR-gate:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Connect $(l_1, a)$ and $(l_2, b)$.
    \item Form a triangle $(a, b, c)$. Connect $c$ to the global $B$ node.
    \item Connect $c$ to $d$, and $l_3$ to $e$.
    \item Form a triangle $(d, e, f)$.
    \item Connect $f$ to both the global $B$ and global $F$ nodes. (This severely restricts $f$; it \textit{must} be colored $T$).
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick, node distance=2cm, 
    vnode/.style={circle, draw=cD!80!black, thick, fill=white, minimum size=6mm, inner sep=0pt},
    bnode/.style={circle, draw=black, very thick, fill=gray!20, minimum size=6mm, inner sep=0pt},
    tnode/.style={circle, draw=blue!80!black, very thick, fill=blue!10, minimum size=6mm, inner sep=0pt},
    fnode/.style={circle, draw=red!80!black, very thick, fill=red!10, minimum size=6mm, inner sep=0pt}]

    % Palette Gadget
    \begin{scope}[shift={(0,0)}]
        \node[tnode] (T) at (0, 1.2) {T};
        \node[fnode] (F) at (0, -1.2) {F};
        \node[bnode] (B) at (1.5, 0) {B};
        \draw (T) -- (F) -- (B) -- (T);
        \node[below, font=\small\bfseries] at (0.5, -1.8) {1. Palette Gadget};
    \end{scope}

    % Variable Gadget
    \begin{scope}[shift={(4,0)}]
        \node[vnode] (xi) at (1.5, 1) {$v_i$};
        \node[vnode] (nxi) at (1.5, -1) {$\overline{v_i}$};
        \node[bnode] (B2) at (0, 0) {B};
        \draw (xi) -- (nxi);
        \draw (xi) -- (B2);
        \draw (nxi) -- (B2);
        \node[below, font=\small\bfseries] at (1, -1.8) {2. Variable Gadget};
    \end{scope}

    % Clause Gadget
    \begin{scope}[shift={(8,0)}]
        \node[vnode] (l1) at (0, 2) {$l_1$};
        \node[vnode] (l2) at (0, 0) {$l_2$};
        \node[vnode] (l3) at (3.5, -1.5) {$l_3$};

        \node[vnode] (a) at (1.2, 1.5) {$a$};
        \node[vnode] (b) at (1.2, 0.5) {$b$};
        \node[vnode] (c) at (2.2, 1) {$c$};

        \node[vnode] (d) at (3.5, 1) {$d$};
        \node[vnode] (e) at (3.5, 0) {$e$};
        \node[vnode] (f) at (4.5, 0.5) {$f$};

        \draw (a) -- (b) -- (c) -- (a);
        \draw (d) -- (e) -- (f) -- (d);
        
        \draw (l1) -- (a);
        \draw (l2) -- (b);
        \draw (c) -- (d);
        \draw (l3) -- (e);

        % Connections to B and F (using local nodes for diagram clarity)
        \node[bnode, minimum size=5mm] (Bc) at (2.2, 2.2) {B};
        \node[bnode, minimum size=5mm] (Bf) at (4.5, 1.8) {B};
        \node[fnode, minimum size=5mm] (Ff) at (5.8, 0.5) {F};

        \draw[dashed] (c) -- (Bc);
        \draw[dashed] (f) -- (Bf);
        \draw[line width=1.2pt, red!80!black] (f) -- (Ff);

        \node[below, font=\small\bfseries] at (2.5, -2) {3. Clause Gadget ($l_1 \vee l_2 \vee l_3$)};
    \end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Step 3: Proof of Correctness}
We must show that $\Phi$ is satisfiable $\iff G$ is 3-colorable.

\paragraph{Backward Direction ($\impliedby$):}
Assume $G$ is 3-colorable.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Because of the Palette and Variable gadgets, every variable literal $v_i$ is assigned either $T$ or $F$. We use this to extract a truth assignment for $\Phi$.
    \item Consider the Clause Gadget. Node $f$ is connected to $B$ and $F$, so $f$ \textbf{must} be colored $T$.
    \item Suppose for contradiction that a clause is not satisfied (i.e., $l_1 = F$, $l_2 = F$, and $l_3 = F$).
    \item Since $l_1 = F$ and $l_2 = F$, the nodes $a$ and $b$ must be colored from $\{T, B\}$. Because $(a,b)$ is an edge, one is $T$ and the other is $B$. 
    \item Therefore, $c$ (which forms a triangle with $a, b$) must be colored $F$.
    \item Since $c = F$ and $c$ connects to $d$, $d$ must be colored from $\{T, B\}$.
    \item Since $l_3 = F$, node $e$ must also be colored from $\{T, B\}$. 
    \item Because $(d,e)$ is an edge, one is $T$ and the other is $B$. Therefore, $f$ (which forms a triangle with $d, e$) \textbf{must} be colored $F$.
    \item However, we already established that $f$ must be colored $T$. This creates a conflict ($T \neq F$), meaning the graph cannot be 3-colored if all three literals are $F$.
    \item Thus, in a valid 3-coloring, at least one literal per clause must be $T$, meaning $\Phi$ is satisfiable.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Forward Direction ($\implies$):}
Assume $\Phi$ is satisfiable.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Color the Palette gadget with $T, F, B$. Color the variable gadgets according to the satisfying truth assignment (if $x_i$ is True, color $v_i = T$ and $\overline{v_i} = F$).
    \item For each clause, since it evaluates to True, at least one literal is $T$. 
    \item By symmetrically routing the colors through the OR-gate (Clause Gadget), we can always avoid the $(F,F)$ contradiction at node $f$. 
    \item For example, if $l_1 = T$, we can set $a = B$, $b = T$ (if $l_2 = F$), making $c = T$. Since $c = T$, $d$ can be $F$. Then even if $l_3 = F \implies e \in \{T, B\}$, we can set $e = B$, allowing $f$ to safely be $T$.
    \item Thus, the entire graph $G$ can be validly 3-colored.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Conclusion}
The construction of $G$ requires $3 + 2n + 6m$ vertices and is clearly computable in polynomial time $\mathcal{O}(n + m)$. Since 3-Coloring is in $\mathcal{NP}$ and we have provided a polynomial-time reduction from the NP-complete 3-SAT problem, \textbf{3-Coloring is NP-complete}.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What is the difference between NP-complete and NP-hard problems? \\
Prove that the satisfiability of Boolean formulas is NP-complete.\\
 Prove that $3\text{-SAT} \leq_p \text{Independent Set}$. \\
 Give an example of reduction by equivalence .
 \mk{5+15+10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2006--07}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cD!4,colframe=cD,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cD]

\subsubsection*{1. Difference Between NP-Complete and NP-Hard}

The complexity classes \textbf{NP-complete} and \textbf{NP-hard} classify the difficulty of computational problems relative to the class $\mathcal{NP}$ (Nondeterministic Polynomial time).

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{NP-Hard:} A problem $H$ is NP-hard if \textit{every} problem $L \in \mathcal{NP}$ can be reduced to $H$ in polynomial time ($L \leq_p H$). Informally, $H$ is "at least as hard" as the hardest problems in $\mathcal{NP}$. Crucially, an NP-hard problem \textit{does not} have to be in $\mathcal{NP}$ itself (e.g., it could be an optimization problem, or even undecidable, like the Halting Problem).
    \item \textbf{NP-Complete (NPC):} A decision problem $C$ is NP-complete if it satisfies \textbf{two} conditions:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $C \in \mathcal{NP}$ (solutions can be verified in polynomial time).
        \item $C$ is NP-hard.
    \end{enumerate}
    In summary, NP-complete problems are the hardest problems \textit{strictly within} the boundary of $\mathcal{NP}$, whereas NP-hard problems are at least that hard, but not necessarily restricted to $\mathcal{NP}$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Prove that the Satisfiability of Boolean Formulas (SAT) is NP-Complete}

This is the famous \textbf{Cook-Levin Theorem}. The proof requires showing that $\text{SAT} \in \mathcal{NP}$ and that $\text{SAT}$ is NP-hard.

\paragraph{Step 1: $\text{SAT} \in \mathcal{NP}$}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Certificate:} A boolean truth assignment to the variables $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$.
    \item \textbf{Verification:} Given the truth assignment, evaluate the formula $\phi$. Evaluating a boolean formula takes time linearly proportional to its length $\mathcal{O}(|\phi|)$. Thus, verification is in polynomial time.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Step 2: SAT is NP-Hard (Generic Reduction)}
We must show that for any language $L \in \mathcal{NP}$, $L \leq_p \text{SAT}$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item By definition, if $L \in \mathcal{NP}$, there exists a nondeterministic Turing machine (NDTM) $M$ that decides $L$ in polynomial time $p(n)$ on input $x$ of length $n$.
    \item We can construct a Boolean formula $\phi_{M, x}$ that algebraically "simulates" the computation of $M$ on $x$.
    \item We introduce boolean variables to represent the state of $M$ at every time step $t$ from $0$ to $p(n)$:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $Q_{i,t}$: True if $M$ is in state $q_i$ at time $t$.
        \item $H_{j,t}$: True if the tape head is at position $j$ at time $t$.
        \item $T_{j,k,t}$: True if tape cell $j$ contains symbol $s_k$ at time $t$.
    \end{itemize}
    \item The formula $\phi_{M, x}$ is the conjunction of several clause groups:
    $$ \phi_{M, x} = \phi_{\text{start}} \wedge \phi_{\text{accept}} \wedge \phi_{\text{valid\_state}} \wedge \phi_{\text{transition}} $$
    where $\phi_{\text{start}}$ encodes the initial state and input $x$, $\phi_{\text{valid\_state}}$ ensures $M$ is exactly in one state and reading one cell per time step, $\phi_{\text{transition}}$ enforces valid NDTM transition rules, and $\phi_{\text{accept}}$ asserts that at time $p(n)$, $M$ is in an accepting state.
    \item The size of this formula is $\mathcal{O}(p(n)^2)$, and it can be constructed in polynomial time. $\phi_{M, x}$ is satisfiable \textit{if and only if} there exists a valid sequence of non-deterministic choices leading $M$ to accept $x$. Thus, $L \leq_p \text{SAT}$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Prove that $3\text{-SAT} \leq_p \text{Independent Set}$}

We must construct a polynomial-time reduction from 3-SAT to the Independent Set (IS) problem.

\paragraph{Construction:}
Let $\phi$ be a 3-SAT formula with $k$ clauses: $C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge \dots \wedge C_k$, where each $C_i = (l_{i1} \vee l_{i2} \vee l_{i3})$.
We construct an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $K$ as follows:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item For each clause $C_i$, create a triangle (a clique of 3 vertices) in $G$. Label the vertices with the literals $l_{i1}, l_{i2}, l_{i3}$. (This gives $3k$ vertices).
    \item Add "conflict edges" between any two vertices that represent complementary literals (e.g., an edge between a vertex labeled $x_a$ and a vertex labeled $\overline{x_a}$).
    \item Set the target independent set size $K = k$.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=1.5cm, main node/.style={circle, draw=cD!80!black, fill=white, thick, minimum size=0.9cm, inner sep=0pt}]
    % Clause 1
    \node[main node] (1) at (0, 2) {$x_1$};
    \node[main node] (2) at (-1, 0) {$x_2$};
    \node[main node] (3) at (1, 0) {$x_3$};
    \draw[thick] (1) -- (2) -- (3) -- (1);
    \node at (0, -0.6) {\small $C_1 = (x_1 \vee x_2 \vee x_3)$};

    % Clause 2
    \node[main node] (4) at (5, 2) {$\overline{x_1}$};
    \node[main node] (5) at (4, 0) {$\overline{x_2}$};
    \node[main node] (6) at (6, 0) {$x_4$};
    \draw[thick] (4) -- (5) -- (6) -- (4);
    \node at (5, -0.6) {\small $C_2 = (\overline{x_1} \vee \overline{x_2} \vee x_4)$};

    % Conflict edges
    \draw[dashed, red, very thick] (1) to[out=20, in=160] node[midway, above, text=black]{\small conflict} (4);
    \draw[dashed, red, very thick] (2) to[out=340, in=200] node[midway, below, text=black]{\small conflict} (5);
\end{tikzpicture}\\
\textit{\small Figure: Graph construction for $\phi = (x_1 \vee x_2 \vee x_3) \wedge (\overline{x_1} \vee \overline{x_2} \vee x_4)$}
\end{center}

\paragraph{Correctness Proof:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Forward ($\implies$):} If $\phi$ is satisfiable, there is a truth assignment that makes at least one literal true in every clause. Pick one true literal vertex from each triangle. Because we pick exactly one vertex from each of the $k$ triangles, we have $k$ vertices. Since they map to a valid truth assignment, we cannot pick both $x_a$ and $\overline{x_a}$, meaning no conflict edges are chosen. Thus, $G$ has an independent set of size $k$.
    \item \textbf{Backward ($\impliedby$):} If $G$ has an independent set of size $K=k$, it must contain exactly one vertex from each triangle (since picking two from the same triangle violates independence). Assign the literal associated with each chosen vertex to True. Because there are no edges between chosen vertices, we never simultaneously assign True to both $x_a$ and $\overline{x_a}$. This gives a consistent truth assignment satisfying all $k$ clauses.
\end{itemize}
Since the graph is constructed in $\mathcal{O}(k)$ time, $3\text{-SAT} \leq_p \text{Independent Set}$.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Example of Reduction by Equivalence}

Two problems $A$ and $B$ are \textit{polynomially equivalent} ($A \equiv_p B$) if there is a polynomial-time reduction from $A$ to $B$ ($A \leq_p B$) AND from $B$ to $A$ ($B \leq_p A$). 

\textbf{Example: Independent Set $\equiv_p$ Clique}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Independent Set (IS):} Given $G=(V,E)$ and integer $k$, is there a set of $k$ mutually non-adjacent vertices?
    \item \textbf{Clique:} Given $G'=(V',E')$ and integer $k'$, is there a set of $k'$ mutually adjacent vertices (a complete subgraph)?
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Reduction:} 
For any graph $G$, let $\overline{G}$ be its complement graph (an edge exists in $\overline{G}$ if and only if it does \textit{not} exist in $G$).
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{IS $\leq_p$ Clique:} A subset of vertices $S \subseteq V$ is an independent set in $G$ of size $k$ $\iff$ $S$ is a clique in $\overline{G}$ of size $k$. The reduction maps $(G, k) \to (\overline{G}, k)$.
    \item \textbf{Clique $\leq_p$ IS:} A subset of vertices $S \subseteq V$ is a clique in $G$ of size $k$ $\iff$ $S$ is an independent set in $\overline{G}$ of size $k$. The reduction maps $(G, k) \to (\overline{G}, k)$.
\end{itemize}
Since the transformation of computing a complement graph requires $\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$ time, the reductions run in polynomial time in both directions, establishing computational equivalence.

\end{tcolorbox}


\end{enumerate}

%% ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
%%  PART V — COPING WITH HARDNESS
%% ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
\secdivider{Part V}{Coping with Hardness}{cE}{cE}{Approximation Algorithms, Backtracking, Branch and Bound}

%% ── S5: Coping with Hardness ──────────────────────────────────────────
\section{S5 --- Coping with Hardness}
\label{sec:S5}
\markboth{S5 --- Coping with Hardness}{}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cE}{Approximation Algorithms}
\label{sub:s5-approx}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item Give an approximation algorithm for the vertex cover problem. Analyze the approximation ratio of your algorithm.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25, 2018--19, 2016--17}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. The Approximation Algorithm}

The Vertex Cover problem asks for the minimum sized set of vertices such that every edge in the graph is incident to at least one vertex in the set. Finding the exact optimal vertex cover is NP-hard. However, a simple greedy approach based on finding a \textit{maximal matching} yields a guaranteed $2$-approximation.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{2-Approximation for Vertex Cover}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require An undirected graph $G = (V, E)$
\Ensure A vertex cover $C \subseteq V$
\State $C \gets \emptyset$
\State $E' \gets E$
\While{$E'$ is not empty}
    \State Let $(u, v)$ be an arbitrary edge in $E'$
    \State $C \gets C \cup \{u, v\}$
    \State Remove from $E'$ every edge incident on either $u$ or $v$
\EndWhile
\State \Return $C$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$. We can implement this algorithm efficiently using adjacency lists. In the worst case, we iterate through all edges and vertices once while building the maximal matching and deleting incident edges.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$ to store the graph and the output set $C$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Analysis of the Approximation Ratio}

We must prove that the size of the vertex cover $C$ returned by our algorithm is at most twice the size of the optimal vertex cover $C^*$. That is, we want to show an approximation ratio of $\rho = 2$.

\paragraph{Proof:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Validity of the Cover:} The algorithm terminates only when $E'$ is empty. An edge is removed from $E'$ if and only if one of its endpoints is added to $C$. Therefore, every edge in $E$ has at least one endpoint in $C$, meaning $C$ is a valid vertex cover.
    \item \textbf{Properties of the Chosen Edges:} Let $M$ be the set of edges explicitly picked in step 4 of the algorithm. By the algorithm's design, whenever an edge $(u, v)$ is added to $M$, all other edges incident to $u$ or $v$ are deleted. Thus, no two edges in $M$ share an endpoint. The set $M$ forms a \textit{maximal matching}.
    \item \textbf{Size of our Cover ($|C|$):} For every edge chosen in $M$, exactly $2$ vertices are added to $C$. Therefore, the total size of our vertex cover is exactly:
    $$|C| = 2|M|$$
    \item \textbf{Lower Bound on Optimal Cover ($|C^*|$):} Consider the optimal vertex cover $C^*$. Since $M$ is a matching, all edges in $M$ are strictly disjoint (they share no vertices). To cover the edges in $M$, $C^*$ \textbf{must} pick at least one distinct vertex for \textit{each} edge in $M$. Consequently, the size of the optimal vertex cover must be at least the size of the matching $M$:
    $$|C^*| \ge |M|$$
    \item \textbf{Conclusion:} Substituting $|M| \le |C^*|$ into our equation for $|C|$, we get:
    $$|C| = 2|M| \le 2|C^*|$$
    This proves that the algorithm produces a vertex cover that is at most twice as large as the optimal one, giving a deterministic \textbf{approximation ratio of 2}.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Visual Example}

The diagram below illustrates why the approximation yields a factor of 2. We are given a path graph.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick, node distance=2.5cm,
    vnode/.style={circle, draw=black, very thick, fill=white, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=0pt},
    cnode/.style={circle, draw=cE!80!black, very thick, fill=cE!20, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=0pt},
    onode/.style={circle, draw=red!80!black, very thick, fill=red!20, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=0pt}]

    % Subfigure 1: The Algorithm's result
    \begin{scope}[shift={(0,0)}]
        \node[cnode] (v1) at (0, 0) {$v_1$};
        \node[cnode] (v2) at (2, 0) {$v_2$};
        \node[cnode] (v3) at (4, 0) {$v_3$};
        \node[cnode] (v4) at (6, 0) {$v_4$};

        \draw[line width=2pt, cE] (v1) -- (v2) node[midway, above] {$M_1$};
        \draw (v2) -- (v3);
        \draw[line width=2pt, cE] (v3) -- (v4) node[midway, above] {$M_2$};

        \node[below, align=center, font=\small] at (3, -1) {\textbf{Algorithm's Cover} \\ Matching $M = \{(v_1,v_2), (v_3,v_4)\}$ \\ Added $C = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4\}$ \\ $|C| = 4$};
    \end{scope}

    % Subfigure 2: The Optimal Result
    \begin{scope}[shift={(0,-3.5)}]
        \node[vnode] (u1) at (0, 0) {$v_1$};
        \node[onode] (u2) at (2, 0) {$v_2$};
        \node[onode] (u3) at (4, 0) {$v_3$};
        \node[vnode] (u4) at (6, 0) {$v_4$};

        \draw (u1) -- (u2);
        \draw (u2) -- (u3);
        \draw (u3) -- (u4);

        \node[below, align=center, font=\small] at (3, -1) {\textbf{Optimal Cover} \\ $C^* = \{v_2, v_3\}$ \\ $|C^*| = 2$};
    \end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

Notice that in the worst-case scenario (like the path graph above), our algorithm picks edges $(v_1, v_2)$ and $(v_3, v_4)$ as the maximal matching, thus taking all 4 vertices. The optimal cover only requires $v_2$ and $v_3$. Here, $|C| = 4$ and $|C^*| = 2$, perfectly matching our $|C| \le 2|C^*|$ bound.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Let $\pi = \pi_1 \pi_2 \dots \pi_n$ be a permutation of $1\ 2 \dots n$ (the identity permutation). A reversal 
$(i, j)$ reverses the contiguous subsequence between $i$ and $j$ i.e. transforms $\pi_1 \dots \pi_{i-1}\pi_i$ 
$\pi_{i+1} \dots \pi_{j-1}\ \pi_j\ \pi_{j+1} \dots \pi_{n}$ into $\pi_1 \dots \pi_{i-1}\pi_j\ \pi_{j-1} \dots \pi_{i+1}\ \pi_i\ \pi_{j+1} \dots \pi_n$. The sorting by 
reversals problem is to find the minimum number of reversals that transforms a given 
permutation to the identity permutation. For example, the permutation $[4\ 1\ 2\ 3]$ can be 
sorted by reversing $[1\ 2\ 3]$ to get $[4\ 3\ 2\ 1]$ and then reversing this entire sequence.

Let's extend the permutation by adding $\pi_0 = 0$ and $\pi_{n+1} = n + 1$ to the ends ($\pi_0$ and $\pi_{n+1}$ 
are not moved during sorting). We say there is a breakpoint between $\pi_i$ and $\pi_{i+1}$ if they 
are not consecutive. For example, $[4\ 1\ 2\ 3]$ is converted to $[0\ 4\ 1\ 2\ 3\ 5]$ and there are 
three breakpoints: between $0$ and $4$, $4$ and $1$, and $3$ and $5$.
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Provide a lower bound on the number of reversals needed to sort a permutation $\pi$, in terms of the number of breakpoints in it.
  \item Design an approximation algorithm for the sorting by reversals problem with an approximation ratio at most 4. (Hint: consider maximal runs of contiguously increasing and contiguously decreasing elements.)
\end{enumerate}
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{Part 1: Lower Bound on the Number of Reversals}

Let $\pi = [0, \pi_1, \pi_2, \dots, \pi_n, n+1]$. We define a \textbf{breakpoint} to exist between adjacent elements $\pi_i$ and $\pi_{i+1}$ if they are not consecutive in value, i.e., $|\pi_i - \pi_{i+1}| \neq 1$. Let $b(\pi)$ denote the total number of breakpoints in the permutation $\pi$.

\paragraph{Theorem:} The minimum number of reversals required to sort $\pi$, denoted as $OPT$, satisfies:
$$ OPT \ge \left\lceil \frac{b(\pi)}{2} \right\rceil $$

\paragraph{Proof:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Target State:} The sorted identity permutation $I = [0, 1, 2, \dots, n, n+1]$ has exactly $b(I) = 0$ breakpoints.
    \item \textbf{Effect of a Reversal:} A reversal $\rho(i, j)$ acting on the segment from index $i$ to $j$ only modifies the adjacencies at the boundaries of the segment. Specifically, it breaks the pairs $(\pi_{i-1}, \pi_i)$ and $(\pi_j, \pi_{j+1})$, and replaces them with $(\pi_{i-1}, \pi_j)$ and $(\pi_i, \pi_{j+1})$. 
    \item \textbf{Internal Pairs:} The pairs strictly inside the segment are flipped in order, but their absolute differences remain invariant. If $|\pi_k - \pi_{k+1}| = 1$, then $|\pi_{k+1} - \pi_k| = 1$. Thus, no internal breakpoints are created or removed.
    \item \textbf{Conclusion:} Since a single reversal affects at most $2$ adjacencies, it can eliminate at most $2$ breakpoints. To reduce the number of breakpoints from $b(\pi)$ to $0$, we mathematically require at least $b(\pi) / 2$ reversals. Since the number of reversals must be an integer, $OPT \ge \lceil b(\pi) / 2 \rceil$.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick, node distance=1cm]
    \node (p1) at (0,0) {$\pi_{i-1}$};
    \node (p2) at (1.5,0) {$\pi_i$};
    \node (p3) at (3.5,0) {$\dots$};
    \node (p4) at (5.5,0) {$\pi_j$};
    \node (p5) at (7,0) {$\pi_{j+1}$};
    
    \draw[red, very thick, dashed] (p1) -- (p2) node[midway, above, text=red, font=\scriptsize] {break};
    \draw (p2) -- (p3) -- (p4);
    \draw[red, very thick, dashed] (p4) -- (p5) node[midway, above, text=red, font=\scriptsize] {break};
    
    \draw[<->, blue!80!black, line width=1pt] (1.5,-0.3) to[bend right=20] node[midway, below, font=\small] {Reversal $\rho(i, j)$} (5.5,-0.3);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Part 2: 4-Approximation Algorithm}

We define a \textbf{strip} as a maximal contiguous subsequence of elements without any internal breakpoints. A strip is \textit{increasing} if it is strictly ascending (e.g., $4, 5, 6$) and \textit{decreasing} if descending (e.g., $8, 7, 6$). A single element flanked by breakpoints is considered an increasing strip.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Greedy Breakpoint Reduction (4-Approximation)}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Permutation $\pi = [0, \pi_1, \dots, \pi_n, n+1]$
\Ensure A sequence of reversals to sort $\pi$
\While{$b(\pi) > 0$}
    \If{$\pi$ contains at least one decreasing strip}
        \State Let $k$ be the smallest element in \textit{any} decreasing strip.
        \State Find $k-1$ (which must be at the right end of some increasing strip).
        \If{$k-1$ appears \textbf{before} $k$ in $\pi$}
            \State Let the segment be $\dots, k-1 \ | \ x, \dots, k \ | \dots$
            \State Reverse the segment from $x$ to $k$. \Comment{Makes $k-1$ and $k$ adjacent}
        \Else
            \State Let the segment be $\dots, k \ | \ x, \dots, k-1 \ | \dots$
            \State Reverse the segment from $x$ to $k-1$. \Comment{Makes $k$ and $k-1$ adjacent}
        \EndIf
    \Else
        \State Let $S$ be any increasing strip that does not contain $0$ or $n+1$.
        \State Reverse the strip $S$. \Comment{Turns an increasing strip into a decreasing one}
    \EndIf
\EndWhile
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Part 3: Approximation Ratio Analysis}

We must prove that the algorithm sorts the array using at most $4 \times OPT$ reversals. Let's analyze the greedy choices:

\paragraph{Case 1: A decreasing strip exists (Lines 3-10)}
By choosing $k$ as the smallest element in a decreasing strip, we guarantee that $k-1$ is the largest element in an increasing strip. The algorithm performs a targeted reversal to bring $k$ and $k-1$ together.
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $k-1$ precedes $k$, the reversal of $x \dots k$ transforms $\dots, k-1 \ | \ x \dots k$ into $\dots, k-1, k \dots x$. The elements $k-1$ and $k$ are now consecutive, eliminating at least the breakpoint between $k-1$ and $x$.
    \item If $k$ precedes $k-1$, reversing $x \dots k-1$ transforms $k \ | \ x \dots k-1$ into $k, k-1 \dots x$. The sequence $k, k-1$ represents a valid continuation of a decreasing strip, removing at least one breakpoint.
\end{itemize}
\textbf{Result:} One reversal eliminates $\ge 1$ breakpoint.

\paragraph{Case 2: No decreasing strips exist (Lines 11-14)}
All elements are structured in increasing strips separated by breakpoints. By picking an internal increasing strip $S$ and reversing it, $S$ becomes a \textit{decreasing} strip. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item Since $S$ was bounded by breakpoints, reversing it alters the boundary adjacencies, but does not increase the total number of breakpoints (worst-case: $\Delta b = 0$).
    \item Critically, in the \textit{very next iteration}, the permutation is guaranteed to have a decreasing strip. This triggers Case 1, which will decrease the breakpoints by at least $1$.
\end{itemize}
\textbf{Result:} At most $2$ reversals eliminate $\ge 1$ breakpoint.

\paragraph{Conclusion:}
In the absolute worst-case scenario, the algorithm requires $2$ reversals to remove $1$ breakpoint. Therefore, the total number of reversals $R$ executed by the algorithm is bounded by:
$$ R \le 2 \cdot b(\pi) $$
From our lower bound in Part 1, we know that $b(\pi) \le 2 \cdot OPT$. Substituting this into our worst-case performance gives:
$$ R \le 2 \cdot (2 \cdot OPT) = 4 \cdot OPT $$
Thus, the algorithm correctly yields an \textbf{approximation ratio of at most 4}.

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item Give a 2-approximation algorithm for the vertex cover problem and prove that the algorithm computes the vertex cover at most twice the size of the actual vertex cover size.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Introduction and Algorithm}

The \textbf{Vertex Cover} problem asks for the smallest set of vertices $C \subseteq V$ in an undirected graph $G=(V, E)$ such that every edge in $E$ is incident to at least one vertex in $C$. Finding the optimal (minimum) vertex cover is known to be NP-complete. 

However, we can achieve a guaranteed \textbf{2-approximation} (a solution at most twice the optimal size) using a simple greedy approach that finds a \textit{maximal matching}.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{2-Approximation for Vertex Cover}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require An undirected graph $G = (V, E)$
\Ensure A vertex cover $C \subseteq V$
\State $C \gets \emptyset$ \Comment{Initialize the vertex cover as empty}
\State $E' \gets E$ \Comment{Let $E'$ be the set of remaining edges to cover}
\While{$E'$ is not empty}
    \State Let $(u, v)$ be an arbitrary edge in $E'$
    \State $C \gets C \cup \{u, v\}$ \Comment{Add both endpoints to the cover}
    \State Remove from $E'$ the edge $(u,v)$ and every edge incident to $u$ or $v$
\EndWhile
\State \Return $C$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$. The algorithm can be efficiently implemented using an adjacency list. We scan the edges and vertices, and once an edge or vertex is processed and removed, it is not visited again.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$. We need this space to store the graph representations and the resulting set $C$.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Proof of the Approximation Ratio}

We must prove two things: first, that the algorithm returns a valid vertex cover, and second, that its size is at most twice the size of the optimal vertex cover $C^*$.

\paragraph{Part A: Validity of the Cover}
The condition of the `while` loop guarantees that the algorithm only terminates when $E'$ is entirely empty. An edge is only removed from $E'$ if at least one of its endpoints is placed into $C$. Therefore, by the time the algorithm finishes, every single edge in the original set $E$ is incident to at least one vertex in $C$. Thus, $C$ is a valid vertex cover.

\paragraph{Part B: Approximation Ratio Bound}
Let $M \subseteq E$ be the exact set of edges arbitrarily picked in \textbf{Step 5} of the algorithm. 
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Properties of $M$:} When an edge $(u, v)$ is added to $M$, the algorithm subsequently removes all other edges touching either $u$ or $v$. This guarantees that no two edges in $M$ share an endpoint. By definition, $M$ forms a \textit{matching} (specifically, a maximal matching).
    \item \textbf{Size of our algorithm's cover ($|C|$):} For every edge $(u,v) \in M$, the algorithm adds exactly two distinct vertices ($u$ and $v$) to $C$. Therefore, the total size of our vertex cover is exactly:
    \begin{equation}
        |C| = 2|M|
    \end{equation}
    \item \textbf{Lower bound on the optimal cover ($|C^*|$):} Let $C^*$ be the optimal (minimum) vertex cover. Because $M$ consists of strictly disjoint edges (no shared vertices), covering the edges in $M$ inherently requires picking at least one distinct vertex for \textit{every single edge} in $M$. Even an optimal algorithm cannot "cheat" by using one vertex to cover two edges of $M$. Thus, the optimal vertex cover must be at least as large as the matching:
    \begin{equation}
        |C^*| \ge |M|
    \end{equation}
    \item \textbf{Conclusion:} Substituting equation (2) into equation (1), we establish the approximation ratio:
    \begin{equation}
        |C| = 2|M| \le 2|C^*|
    \end{equation}
\end{enumerate}
This proves that the output $C$ is strictly bounded by $2 \times |C^*|$, making it a valid 2-approximation.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Visual Example}

The following diagram illustrates a scenario where the algorithm exactly hits the upper bound of the 2-approximation ratio.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=stealth, thick, node distance=2.5cm,
    vnode/.style={circle, draw=black, very thick, fill=white, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=0pt},
    cnode/.style={circle, draw=cE!80!black, very thick, fill=cE!20, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=0pt},
    onode/.style={circle, draw=red!80!black, very thick, fill=red!20, minimum size=8mm, inner sep=0pt}]

    % Subfigure 1: The Algorithm's result
    \begin{scope}[shift={(0,0)}]
        \node[cnode] (v1) at (0, 0) {$v_1$};
        \node[cnode] (v2) at (2, 0) {$v_2$};
        \node[cnode] (v3) at (4, 0) {$v_3$};
        \node[cnode] (v4) at (6, 0) {$v_4$};

        \draw[line width=2pt, cE] (v1) -- (v2) node[midway, above] {$e_1 \in M$};
        \draw[dashed, gray] (v2) -- (v3) node[midway, above] {deleted};
        \draw[line width=2pt, cE] (v3) -- (v4) node[midway, above] {$e_2 \in M$};

        \node[below, align=center, font=\small] at (3, -1) {\textbf{Algorithm's Cover} \\ Matching $M = \{(v_1,v_2), (v_3,v_4)\}$ \\ Picked $C = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4\}$ \\ Size $|C| = 4$};
    \end{scope}

    % Subfigure 2: The Optimal Result
    \begin{scope}[shift={(0,-4)}]
        \node[vnode] (u1) at (0, 0) {$v_1$};
        \node[onode] (u2) at (2, 0) {$v_2$};
        \node[onode] (u3) at (4, 0) {$v_3$};
        \node[vnode] (u4) at (6, 0) {$v_4$};

        \draw (u1) -- (u2);
        \draw (u2) -- (u3);
        \draw (u3) -- (u4);

        \node[below, align=center, font=\small] at (3, -1) {\textbf{Optimal Cover} \\ $C^* = \{v_2, v_3\}$ \\ Size $|C^*| = 2$};
    \end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

In the path graph $v_1 - v_2 - v_3 - v_4$, if the algorithm greedily selects edge $(v_1, v_2)$ first, it adds both $v_1$ and $v_2$ to $C$ and removes $(v_2, v_3)$. It then is forced to pick $(v_3, v_4)$, adding $v_3$ and $v_4$ to $C$. Here, $|C| = 4$, while the optimal cover simply picks the internal nodes $v_2, v_3$ with $|C^*| = 2$. The relation $|C| \le 2|C^*|$ holds perfectly.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Suppose you want to fill up your luggage with the required items for your upcoming 
tour. But the luggage has a capacity of 600 kg. The items have different values and 
weights which are given below:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Item} & \textbf{Value} & \textbf{Weight} \\ \hline
1    & 1200  & 150    \\ \hline
2    & 1100  & 100    \\ \hline
3    & 950   & 250    \\ \hline
4    & 1350  & 350    \\ \hline
5    & 800   & 200    \\ \hline
6    & 1500  & 300    \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

You want to fill up your luggage so that the total values of the items are maximized. 
So you plan to use an approximation algorithm that you learned from your teacher to 
solve the problem. The approximation algorithm scales down the high values by 
$\epsilon V_{max}/2n$ ($\epsilon = \text{precision parameter}$, $V_{max} = \text{largest value and } n = \text{total items}$) and 
utilizes a dynamic programing technique. The dynamic programing technique uses 
the subproblem $OPT(i,v)$ (the minimum weight of the luggage for which we can 
obtain a solution of value at least $v$ using a subset of items 1 to i), instead of using 
$OPT(i, w)$ (the max value subset of items 1 to i with weight limit w).

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{[Luggage maximization using scaling and dynamic programming]} Why does the approximation algorithm use the $OPT(i, v)$ subproblem instead of $OPT(i, w)$? 
\mk{5} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}

\item Apply the approximation algorithm to solve the luggage problem with $\varepsilon = 0.1$.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}

\item Prove that for any $\varepsilon > 0$, the approximation algorithm computes a feasible solution whose value is within a $(1 + \varepsilon)$ factor of the optimum in $O(n^3/\varepsilon)$.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}
\end{enumerate}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Why the approximation algorithm uses $OPT(i, v)$ instead of $OPT(i, w)$}

The standard 0-1 Knapsack DP formulation uses $OPT(i, w)$, which computes the maximum value obtainable using a subset of the first $i$ items bounded strictly by weight limit $w$. This yields a time complexity of $\mathcal{O}(nW)$, which is \textbf{pseudo-polynomial} because it grows exponentially with the number of bits required to represent the capacity $W$. For huge values of $W$, this becomes intractable.

By swapping the roles of values and weights, the formulation $OPT(i, v)$ computes the \textbf{minimum weight} required to achieve exactly a value $v$ using the first $i$ items. This approach takes $\mathcal{O}(n V_{sum})$ time. The critical advantage here is that it acts as the foundation for a \textbf{Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme (FPTAS)}. We can systematically \textit{scale down} the values to artificially shrink $V_{sum}$ into a polynomial size, gaining computational speed at the cost of a small, controllable precision error. Conversely, scaling down \textit{weights} is impossible because rounding errors could easily cause the selected items to exceed the rigid physical capacity $W$.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Applying the Approximation Algorithm ($\varepsilon = 0.1$)}

\paragraph{Step 1: Compute Scaling Factor}
Given variables: Capacity $W = 600$, Items $n = 6$, precision $\varepsilon = 0.1$.
The maximum value among all items is $V_{max} = 1500$ (Item 6).
According to the provided formula, the scaling factor $b$ is:
$$b = \frac{\varepsilon V_{max}}{2n} = \frac{0.1 \times 1500}{2 \times 6} = \frac{150}{12} = 12.5$$

\paragraph{Step 2: Scale Down the Values}
We scale every item's value using $v'_i = \lfloor \frac{v_i}{b} \rfloor$:

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Item} ($i$) & \textbf{Original Value} ($v_i$) & \textbf{Weight} ($w_i$) & $v_i / 12.5$ & \textbf{Scaled Value} ($v'_i$) \\ \hline
1 & 1200 & 150 & 96 & \textbf{96} \\ \hline
2 & 1100 & 100 & 88 & \textbf{88} \\ \hline
3 & 950  & 250 & 76 & \textbf{76} \\ \hline
4 & 1350 & 350 & 108 & \textbf{108} \\ \hline
5 & 800  & 200 & 64 & \textbf{64} \\ \hline
6 & 1500 & 300 & 120 & \textbf{120} \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\paragraph{Step 3: DP Execution via Pareto-Optimal States}
Rather than drawing a massive $6 \times 552$ DP matrix, we trace the states iteratively. Let $S_i$ be the set of \textit{Pareto-optimal reachable states} after considering item $i$. A state is a tuple $(v', w)$. We enforce the capacity limit $W = 600$. 
\textit{Filtering rule:} State $A$ strictly dominates state $B$ if $v'_A \ge v'_B$ and $w_A \le w_B$. Dominated states are discarded.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Initialize:} $S_0 = \{(0, 0)\}$
    \item \textbf{Add Item 1} (96, 150): \\
          $S_1 = \{(0, 0), \mathbf{(96, 150)}\}$
    \item \textbf{Add Item 2} (88, 100): \\
          New subsets: $(88, 100)$ and $(96+88, 150+100) \implies (184, 250)$.\\
          $S_2 = \{(0, 0), \mathbf{(88, 100)}, (96, 150), \mathbf{(184, 250)}\}$
    \item \textbf{Add Item 3} (76, 250): \\
          New: $(76, 250)$, $(164, 350)$, $(172, 400)$, $(260, 500)$. \\
          \textit{Filter:} $(76, 250)$ is dominated by $(88, 100)$. $(164, 350)$ and $(172, 400)$ are dominated by $(184, 250)$. \\
          $S_3 = \{(0, 0), (88, 100), (96, 150), (184, 250), \mathbf{(260, 500)}\}$
    \item \textbf{Add Item 4} (108, 350): \\
