We iterate through the edges; if an edge $(u,v)$ has neither $u$ nor $v$ marked as \texttt{visited}, we mark both as \texttt{visited} (adding them to $C$). Marking vertices automatically "removes" their incident edges from future consideration. Every vertex and edge is processed a constant number of times.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$} \\
    The algorithm requires $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ auxiliary space to store the selected vertex cover $C$ and the \texttt{visited} array.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Proof of Approximation Ratio}

\textbf{Theorem:} Let $C$ be the vertex cover returned by the algorithm, and let $C^*$ be the optimal (minimum) vertex cover. The approximation ratio is 2, i.e., $|C| \le 2 \cdot |C^*|$.

\textbf{Proof:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Validity of Cover:} The \textbf{while} loop only terminates when $E' = \emptyset$. Since every edge in $E$ is either explicitly selected or removed because one of its endpoints was added to $C$, every edge is covered. Thus, $C$ is a valid vertex cover.
    
    \item \textbf{Maximal Matching Property:} Let $M \subseteq E$ be the set of edges selected in step 5 of the algorithm. By the algorithm's design, whenever an edge $(u, v)$ is selected, all other incident edges are removed. Therefore, no two edges in $M$ share an endpoint. This means $M$ forms a \textbf{matching}.
    
    \item \textbf{Size of the Approximate Cover:} For every edge in $M$, the algorithm adds exactly 2 distinct vertices to the cover $C$. Thus, the total number of vertices in our output is:
    \begin{equation}
        |C| = 2 |M|
    \end{equation}
    
    \item \textbf{Lower Bound on the Optimal Cover:} Since $M$ is a matching, it consists of mutually disjoint edges. To cover just the edges in $M$, \textit{any} valid vertex cover must select at least one unique vertex for each edge in $M$. Therefore, the size of the optimal vertex cover $C^*$ is bounded below by the size of the matching:
    \begin{equation}
        |C^*| \ge |M|
    \end{equation}
    
    \item \textbf{Establishing the Ratio:} Combining the equations from steps 3 and 4, we multiply the optimal bound by 2:
    \begin{equation}
        2 |C^*| \ge 2 |M| = |C|
    \end{equation}
    Yielding:
    \begin{equation}
        |C| \le 2 |C^*|
    \end{equation}
\end{enumerate}
Because the algorithm runs in polynomial time and guarantees a solution size no greater than twice the optimal, it is a strictly \textbf{2-approximation algorithm}. \hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write a polynomial-time 2-approximation algorithm for the vertex-cover problem, and prove the approximation ratio of the algorithm. If $P = NP$, then prove that for any constant $\rho \geq 1$, there is no polynomial-time approximation algorithm with ratio $\rho$ for the general traveling-salesman problem. \mk{8+7=15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2011--12}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\textit{Note: There is a common typographical error in the second part of the prompt. If $P = NP$, we could compute the exact optimal TSP tour in polynomial time, making the statement trivially false. The classical theorem relies on the assumption that $P \neq NP$. We will provide the standard proof for the statement: "If $P \neq NP$, then for any constant $\rho \ge 1$, there is no polynomial-time $\rho$-approximation algorithm for general TSP."}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Part 1: 2-Approximation Algorithm for Vertex Cover}

The Vertex Cover problem is NP-Complete, but a strict 2-approximation can be achieved in polynomial time by finding a \textbf{maximal matching} and adding all matched vertices to the cover.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{2-Approximation for Vertex Cover}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require An undirected graph $G = (V, E)$
\Ensure A vertex cover $C \subseteq V$
\State $C \gets \emptyset$
\State $E' \gets E$ \Comment{Working set of edges}
\While{$E' \neq \emptyset$}
    \State Let $(u, v)$ be an arbitrary edge in $E'$
    \State $C \gets C \cup \{u, v\}$ \Comment{Add both endpoints to the cover}
    \State Remove from $E'$ all edges incident to $u$ or $v$
\EndWhile
\State \Return $C$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\paragraph{Visualization of the Algorithm:}
\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.0,
        vertex/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, minimum size=0.5cm},
        picked/.style={circle, draw=cE, thick, fill=cE!20, inner sep=2pt, minimum size=0.5cm},
        edge/.style={thick, black},
        match/.style={thick, cE, line width=2pt}
    ]
        % Graph 1
        \node[vertex] (v1) at (0, 0) {a};
        \node[vertex] (v2) at (1, 1) {b};
        \node[vertex] (v3) at (2, 0) {c};
        \node[vertex] (v4) at (1, -1) {d};
        \node[vertex] (v5) at (3, 1) {e};
        
        \draw[edge] (v1) -- (v2);
        \draw[edge] (v2) -- (v3);
        \draw[edge] (v2) -- (v4);
        \draw[edge] (v3) -- (v5);
        \node at (1.5, -2) {\textbf{1. Input Graph $G$}};
        
        % Arrow
        \draw[->, thick, >=stealth] (3.8, 0) -- (4.8, 0) node[midway, above] {Select $(b,c)$};

        % Graph 2
        \node[vertex] (u1) at (5.5, 0) {a};
        \node[picked] (u2) at (6.5, 1) {b};
        \node[picked] (u3) at (7.5, 0) {c};
        \node[vertex] (u4) at (6.5, -1) {d};
        \node[vertex] (u5) at (8.5, 1) {e};
        
        \draw[edge, dashed, gray] (u1) -- (u2);
        \draw[match] (u2) -- (u3);
        \draw[edge, dashed, gray] (u2) -- (u4);
        \draw[edge, dashed, gray] (u3) -- (u5);
        \node at (7.0, -2) {\textbf{2. Approximate Cover $C$}};
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\paragraph{Complexity Analysis:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$. Using an adjacency list, we can iterate through the edges. If an edge connects two unvisited vertices, we mark both as visited (added to $C$). Marking vertices implicitly removes their incident edges from consideration. 
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ to maintain the set of vertices in the cover $C$.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Proof of Approximation Ratio:}
Let $C$ be the vertex cover returned by the algorithm, and let $C^*$ be the optimal (minimum) vertex cover. Let $M$ be the set of edges selected in step 4 of the algorithm.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Validity:} The algorithm only terminates when all edges are removed from $E'$. Since an edge is only removed if at least one of its endpoints is in $C$, every edge in $E$ is covered. Thus, $C$ is a valid vertex cover.
    \item \textbf{Matching Property:} Every time an edge is added to $M$, all incident edges are removed. Therefore, no two edges in $M$ share an endpoint. $M$ is a maximal matching.
    \item \textbf{Lower Bound on $OPT$:} Because $M$ consists of $|M|$ disjoint edges, any valid vertex cover (including the optimal cover $C^*$) must pick at least one unique vertex for each edge in $M$. Therefore, $|C^*| \ge |M|$.
    \item \textbf{Upper Bound on $C$:} For each edge in $M$, the algorithm adds exactly 2 vertices to $C$. Therefore, $|C| = 2|M|$.
    \item \textbf{Conclusion:} Substituting the lower bound into the upper bound gives $|C| = 2|M| \le 2|C^*|$. Hence, the approximation ratio is exactly 2. \hfill $\blacksquare$
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{Part 2: Inapproximability of the General TSP}

\textbf{Theorem:} If $P \neq NP$, then for any constant $\rho \ge 1$, there is no polynomial-time $\rho$-approximation algorithm for the general Traveling-Salesman Problem (TSP).

\textbf{Proof (by contradiction via reduction from Hamiltonian Cycle):}
Assume, for the sake of contradiction, that there exists a polynomial-time algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ that can guarantee a $\rho$-approximation for the general TSP. We will show that $\mathcal{A}$ can be used to solve the NP-Complete \textbf{Hamiltonian Cycle (HC)} problem in polynomial time.

Let $G = (V,E)$ be an arbitrary unweighted graph with $|V| = n$ vertices. We construct a complete weighted graph $G' = (V, E')$ on the same set of vertices. We define the edge weight function $w(u,v)$ for all pairs $u, v \in V$ as follows:
\begin{equation}
    w(u,v) = 
    \begin{cases} 
      1 & \text{if } (u,v) \in E \\
      \rho \cdot n + 1 & \text{if } (u,v) \notin E 
   \end{cases}
\end{equation}
This construction can be completed in polynomial time $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$. Now we evaluate the possible TSP solutions in $G'$ based on whether $G$ contains a Hamiltonian Cycle:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Case 1: $G$ has a Hamiltonian Cycle.} \\
    The sequence of vertices in the Hamiltonian Cycle forms a valid TSP tour in $G'$ using only edges from $E$. Each of these $n$ edges has a weight of $1$. The optimal TSP cost is thus $OPT = n$. Because $\mathcal{A}$ is a $\rho$-approximation algorithm, running $\mathcal{A}$ on $G'$ is guaranteed to return a tour of cost $C_{\mathcal{A}}$ such that:
    \begin{equation}
        C_{\mathcal{A}} \le \rho \cdot OPT = \rho \cdot n
    \end{equation}
    
    \item \textbf{Case 2: $G$ does not have a Hamiltonian Cycle.} \\
    Any TSP tour in $G'$ must traverse at least one "non-edge" (an edge not present in the original graph $G$). The weight of this non-edge is $\rho \cdot n + 1$. The remaining $n-1$ edges in the tour have a minimum weight of $1$. Therefore, the total cost of \textit{any} valid tour (including the one found by $\mathcal{A}$) must be strictly lower-bounded by:
    \begin{equation}
        C_{\mathcal{A}} \ge OPT \ge (\rho \cdot n + 1) + (n - 1) = \rho \cdot n + n > \rho \cdot n
    \end{equation}
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{The Contradiction:}
Our chosen edge weights create a massive, strict gap in the resulting costs. If we run our hypothetical algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ on $G'$, we can simply inspect the total cost $C_{\mathcal{A}}$ of the returned tour:
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $C_{\mathcal{A}} \le \rho \cdot n$, then $G$ \textbf{must} contain a Hamiltonian Cycle.
    \item If $C_{\mathcal{A}} > \rho \cdot n$, then $G$ \textbf{cannot} contain a Hamiltonian Cycle.
\end{itemize}
Because $\mathcal{A}$ runs in polynomial time, this procedure would allow us to decide the Hamiltonian Cycle problem in polynomial time. Since Hamiltonian Cycle is NP-Complete, this would imply that $P = NP$. This contradicts our assumption that $P \neq NP$. 

Therefore, no such polynomial-time $\rho$-approximation algorithm can exist for the general TSP. \hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Give a 2-approximation algorithm for the Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) for graphs obeying the triangle inequality. Prove the approximation ratio. \mk{17}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2009--10}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Algorithm Design}

The Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) is NP-hard. However, when the edge weights satisfy the \textbf{triangle inequality} (i.e., for any three vertices $u, v, w$, the cost satisfies $c(u,w) \le c(u,v) + c(v,w)$), we can use a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) approach to achieve a 2-approximation.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{2-Approximation for Metric TSP}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require A complete undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with a metric cost function $c$.
\Ensure A Hamiltonian cycle (TSP tour) $H$.
\State Compute a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) $T$ of $G$.
\State Construct a multigraph $M$ by doubling every edge in $T$. \Comment{Every vertex now has even degree.}
\State Find an Eulerian tour $\mathcal{E}$ in the multigraph $M$.
\State Form a Hamiltonian cycle $H$ by traversing $\mathcal{E}$ and skipping (shortcutting) any previously visited vertices.
\State Append the starting vertex to the end of $H$ to close the cycle.
\State \Return $H$.
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Visualization of the Algorithm Steps}

The following sequence visualizes the algorithm on a small metric graph instance.

\begin{center}
    \begin{minipage}{0.3\textwidth}
        \centering
        \textbf{1. MST ($T$)}\par\vspace{0.2cm}
        \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.2, 
            vertex/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, minimum size=0.5cm},
            edge/.style={thick, cE, line width=1.5pt}]
            \node[vertex] (v1) at (0, 0) {a};
            \node[vertex] (v2) at (0, 1.5) {b};
            \node[vertex] (v3) at (1.5, 1.5) {c};
            \node[vertex] (v4) at (1.5, 0) {d};
            
            \draw[edge] (v1) -- (v2);
            \draw[edge] (v2) -- (v3);
            \draw[edge] (v3) -- (v4);
        \end{tikzpicture}
    \end{minipage}
    \hfill
    \begin{minipage}{0.3\textwidth}
        \centering
        \textbf{2. Eulerian Tour ($\mathcal{E}$)}\par\vspace{0.2cm}
        \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.2, 
            vertex/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, minimum size=0.5cm},
            edge/.style={thick, gray, ->, >=stealth, bend right=15, line width=1pt}]
            \node[vertex] (v1) at (0, 0) {a};
            \node[vertex] (v2) at (0, 1.5) {b};
            \node[vertex] (v3) at (1.5, 1.5) {c};
            \node[vertex] (v4) at (1.5, 0) {d};
            
            \draw[edge] (v1) to (v2); \draw[edge] (v2) to (v1);
            \draw[edge] (v2) to (v3); \draw[edge] (v3) to (v2);
            \draw[edge] (v3) to (v4); \draw[edge] (v4) to (v3);
        \end{tikzpicture}
    \end{minipage}
    \hfill
    \begin{minipage}{0.3\textwidth}
        \centering
        \textbf{3. Shortcut Tour ($H$)}\par\vspace{0.2cm}
        \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=1.2, 
            vertex/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, minimum size=0.5cm},
            edge/.style={thick, cE, ->, >=stealth, line width=1.5pt},
            short/.style={thick, cE, ->, >=stealth, dashed, line width=1.5pt}]
            \node[vertex] (v1) at (0, 0) {a};
            \node[vertex] (v2) at (0, 1.5) {b};
            \node[vertex] (v3) at (1.5, 1.5) {c};
            \node[vertex] (v4) at (1.5, 0) {d};
            
            \draw[edge] (v1) -- (v2);
            \draw[edge] (v2) -- (v3);
            \draw[edge] (v3) -- (v4);
            \draw[short] (v4) -- (v1); 
        \end{tikzpicture}
    \end{minipage}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.1cm}
\textit{In Step 3, the Eulerian tour traverses $a \to b \to c \to d \to c \to b \to a$. The shortcutting process directly connects $d \to a$ to avoid revisiting $c$ and $b$.}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$} \\
    Since the input graph for TSP is typically a complete graph ($|E| = \mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$), finding the MST using Prim's algorithm takes $\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$ time. Doubling the edges and finding the Eulerian tour takes $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ time because the MST only has $|V|-1$ edges. The shortcutting traversal also takes $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ time. Therefore, the overall time complexity is dominated by the MST construction.
    
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$} \\
    Storing the complete graph requires $\mathcal{O}(|V|^2)$ space. The structures for the MST, multigraph, and Eulerian tour require only $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ auxiliary space.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Proof of the Approximation Ratio}

\textbf{Theorem:} Let $H$ be the Hamiltonian cycle returned by the algorithm, and let $H^*$ be the optimal (minimum cost) TSP tour. The cost of $H$ satisfies $c(H) \le 2 \cdot c(H^*)$.

\textbf{Proof:}
Let $c(T)$ be the total cost of the Minimum Spanning Tree, $c(\mathcal{E})$ be the cost of the Eulerian tour, and $c(H)$ be the cost of the final algorithm output. We establish three bounding relationships:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Bounding the MST ($c(T) \le c(H^*)$):} \\
    Consider the optimal TSP tour $H^*$. If we remove any single edge from this cycle, we obtain a simple path that visits every vertex exactly once (a spanning path). A spanning path is a valid spanning tree. Since $T$ is the \textit{minimum} cost spanning tree out of all possible spanning trees, its cost must be less than or equal to the cost of this spanning path. 
    \begin{equation}
        c(T) \le c(H^*)
    \end{equation}
    
    \item \textbf{Bounding the Eulerian Tour ($c(\mathcal{E}) \le 2 \cdot c(H^*)$):} \\
    The multigraph is constructed by making two copies of every edge in the MST $T$. An Eulerian tour traverses every edge in the multigraph exactly once. Therefore, the total cost of the Eulerian tour is exactly twice the cost of the MST.
    \begin{equation}
        c(\mathcal{E}) = 2 \cdot c(T)
    \end{equation}
    Substituting the inequality from step 1, we obtain:
    \begin{equation}
        c(\mathcal{E}) \le 2 \cdot c(H^*)
    \end{equation}
    
    \item \textbf{Bounding the Shortcuts ($c(H) \le c(\mathcal{E})$):} \\
    The final Hamiltonian cycle $H$ is formed by extracting a subsequence of vertices from the Eulerian tour $\mathcal{E}$, skipping any vertices that have already been visited. Because the graph satisfies the \textbf{triangle inequality}, going directly from a vertex $u$ to a vertex $w$ is never more expensive than going from $u$ to $w$ via an intermediate vertex $v$.
    \begin{equation}
        c(u, w) \le c(u, v) + c(v, w)
    \end{equation}
    Therefore, every time we "shortcut" a previously visited vertex, the total cost of the tour either decreases or remains the same. Thus, the cost of the shortened tour $H$ is bounded by the Eulerian tour:
    \begin{equation}
        c(H) \le c(\mathcal{E})
    \end{equation}
\end{enumerate}

Combining these three results yields the final approximation bound:
\begin{equation}
    c(H) \le c(\mathcal{E}) = 2 \cdot c(T) \le 2 \cdot c(H^*)
\end{equation}
Because the algorithm yields a valid tour with a cost at most twice the optimal cost in polynomial time, it is a 2-approximation algorithm. \hfill $\blacksquare$

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write a 2-approximation algorithm for the ``vertex cover'' problem. Show that your algorithm has the mentioned approximation ratio. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | 2008--2009}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Algorithm Design}

The Vertex Cover problem is a classical NP-Complete problem. The goal is to find the minimum number of vertices such that every edge in the graph is incident to at least one chosen vertex. While finding the optimal cover is computationally intractable, we can achieve a strict \textbf{2-approximation} using a greedy approach based on finding a maximal matching.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{2-Approximation Algorithm for Vertex Cover}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require An undirected graph $G = (V, E)$
\Ensure A vertex cover $C \subseteq V$
\State $C \gets \emptyset$ \Comment{Initialize the vertex cover}
\State $E' \gets E$ \Comment{Working set of uncovered edges}
\While{$E' \neq \emptyset$}
    \State Let $(u, v)$ be an arbitrary edge in $E'$
    \State $C \gets C \cup \{u, v\}$ \Comment{Add both endpoints to the cover}
    \State Remove from $E'$ every edge incident on either $u$ or $v$
\EndWhile
\State \Return $C$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Execution Visualization}

To illustrate, consider a star graph where the optimal vertex cover clearly consists of only the central node. The greedy algorithm will randomly select an edge, covering the entire graph but picking an extra (unnecessary) leaf node.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.2,
        vertex/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, minimum size=0.6cm},
        picked/.style={circle, draw=cE, thick, fill=cE!20, inner sep=2pt, minimum size=0.6cm},
        edge/.style={thick, black},
        match/.style={thick, cE, line width=2pt}
    ]
        % Input Graph
        \node[vertex] (v1) at (0, 0) {1};
        \node[vertex] (v2) at (1.5, 1) {2};
        \node[vertex] (v3) at (1.5, 0) {3};
        \node[vertex] (v4) at (1.5, -1) {4};
        
        \draw[edge] (v1) -- (v2);
        \draw[edge] (v1) -- (v3);
        \draw[edge] (v1) -- (v4);
        
        \node at (0.75, -1.8) {\textbf{Input Graph}};
        \node at (0.75, -2.2) {\small Optimal Cover: $C^* = \{1\}$};
        
        % Arrow
        \draw[->, thick, >=stealth] (2.2, 0) -- (3.2, 0) node[midway, above] {Select (1,2)};

        % Result
        \node[picked] (u1) at (4, 0) {1};
        \node[picked] (u2) at (5.5, 1) {2};
        \node[vertex] (u3) at (5.5, 0) {3};
        \node[vertex] (u4) at (5.5, -1) {4};
        
        \draw[match] (u1) -- (u2);
        \draw[edge, dashed, gray] (u1) -- (u3);
        \draw[edge, dashed, gray] (u1) -- (u4);
        
        \node at (4.75, -1.8) {\textbf{Approximation Result}};
        \node at (4.75, -2.2) {\small Approx Cover: $C = \{1, 2\}$};
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Proof of Approximation Ratio}

\textbf{Theorem:} Let $C$ be the vertex cover returned by the algorithm, and let $C^*$ be the optimal (minimum) vertex cover. The algorithm guarantees that $|C| \le 2 \cdot |C^*|$.

\textbf{Proof:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Validity of the Cover:} The loop condition ensures the algorithm terminates only when $E' = \emptyset$. Because an edge is removed from $E'$ only when at least one of its endpoints is placed in $C$, all edges in the original edge set $E$ are covered by $C$. Thus, $C$ is a valid vertex cover.
    
    \item \textbf{Maximal Matching Property:} Let $M \subseteq E$ denote the exact set of edges explicitly chosen in step 4 of the algorithm. By the algorithm's design, as soon as an edge $(u,v)$ is selected, all other edges sharing an endpoint with $u$ or $v$ are discarded. Thus, no two edges in $M$ share an endpoint. The set of edges $M$ forms a \textbf{matching}.
    
    \item \textbf{Lower Bound on Optimal Solution ($OPT$):} The optimal vertex cover $C^*$ must cover every edge in the graph, which includes the edges in our matching $M$. Since the edges in $M$ are completely disjoint (they share no vertices), the only way to cover them is to select at least one distinct vertex for every single edge in $M$. Therefore, the size of the optimal vertex cover must be at least the size of the matching:
    \begin{equation}
        |C^*| \ge |M|
    \end{equation}
    
    \item \textbf{Size of the Approximate Cover:} For every edge $(u,v) \in M$, our algorithm adds exactly two vertices ($u$ and $v$) to the set $C$. Therefore, the total number of vertices chosen by the algorithm is exactly twice the number of edges in the matching:
    \begin{equation}
        |C| = 2 |M|
    \end{equation}
    
    \item \textbf{Establishing the Ratio:} We substitute the inequality from step 3 into the equation from step 4. Since $|M| \le |C^*|$, we can multiply both sides by 2 to get $2|M| \le 2|C^*|$. Replacing $2|M|$ with $|C|$ yields:
    \begin{equation}
        |C| \le 2 |C^*|
    \end{equation}
\end{enumerate}
This confirms that the size of the cover produced by the greedy maximal matching algorithm is at most twice the optimal size. \hfill $\blacksquare$

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$} \\
    By representing the graph with an adjacency list, we can implement this algorithm in linear time. We maintain a boolean array \texttt{visited} of size $|V|$. We iterate through the edge list; if we encounter an edge $(u,v)$ where both $u$ and $v$ are unvisited, we mark both as \texttt{visited} and add them to $C$. Marking a node implicitly "removes" all its incident edges since any future edge touching a visited node is simply skipped. Each vertex and edge is processed a constant number of times.
    
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$} \\
    The algorithm requires $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ auxiliary space to store the selected vertex cover $C$ and the \texttt{visited} array used to track the endpoints of the matching. 
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Write a 2-approximation algorithm for the vertex cover problem. Justify your answer. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2006--07}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Algorithm Design}

The Vertex Cover optimization problem aims to find the minimum number of vertices such that every edge in the graph is incident to at least one selected vertex. Finding the exact minimum vertex cover is NP-Hard. However, a highly efficient greedy approach that computes a \textbf{maximal matching} yields a strict 2-approximation. 

The strategy is simple: iteratively select an arbitrary uncovered edge, add \textit{both} of its endpoints to the vertex cover, and remove all edges incident to either endpoint.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{2-Approximation Algorithm for Vertex Cover}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require An undirected graph $G = (V, E)$
\Ensure A vertex cover $C \subseteq V$
\State $C \gets \emptyset$ \Comment{Initialize the vertex cover}
\State $E' \gets E$ \Comment{Working set of uncovered edges}
\While{$E' \neq \emptyset$}
    \State Let $(u, v)$ be an arbitrary edge in $E'$
    \State $C \gets C \cup \{u, v\}$ \Comment{Add both endpoints to the cover}
    \State Remove from $E'$ every edge incident on either $u$ or $v$
\EndWhile
\State \Return $C$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Execution Visualization}

To understand why this is a 2-approximation, consider a graph where a single vertex covers many edges (a star graph). The greedy algorithm will select one edge, thereby adding an optimal central node, but also adding an unnecessary leaf node.

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[
        scale=1.2,
        vertex/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, minimum size=0.6cm},
        picked/.style={circle, draw=cE, thick, fill=cE!20, inner sep=2pt, minimum size=0.6cm},
        edge/.style={thick, black},
        match/.style={thick, cE, line width=2pt}
    ]
        % Input Graph
        \node[vertex] (v1) at (0, 0) {1};
        \node[vertex] (v2) at (1.5, 1) {2};
        \node[vertex] (v3) at (1.5, 0) {3};
        \node[vertex] (v4) at (1.5, -1) {4};
        
        \draw[edge] (v1) -- (v2);
        \draw[edge] (v1) -- (v3);
        \draw[edge] (v1) -- (v4);
        
        \node at (0.75, -1.8) {\textbf{Input Graph}};
        \node at (0.75, -2.2) {\small Optimal Cover: $C^* = \{1\}$};
        
        % Arrow
        \draw[->, thick, >=stealth] (2.2, 0) -- (3.2, 0) node[midway, above] {Select (1,2)};

        % Result
        \node[picked] (u1) at (4, 0) {1};
        \node[picked] (u2) at (5.5, 1) {2};
        \node[vertex] (u3) at (5.5, 0) {3};
        \node[vertex] (u4) at (5.5, -1) {4};
        
        \draw[match] (u1) -- (u2);
        \draw[edge, dashed, gray] (u1) -- (u3);
        \draw[edge, dashed, gray] (u1) -- (u4);
        
        \node at (4.75, -1.8) {\textbf{Approximation Result}};
        \node at (4.75, -2.2) {\small Approx Cover: $C = \{1, 2\}$};
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Justification (Proof of Approximation Ratio)}

\textbf{Theorem:} Let $C$ be the vertex cover returned by the algorithm, and let $C^*$ be the optimal (minimum) vertex cover. The algorithm guarantees that $|C| \le 2 \cdot |C^*|$.

\textbf{Proof:}
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Validity of the Cover:} The loop condition ensures the algorithm terminates only when $E' = \emptyset$. Because an edge is removed from $E'$ only when at least one of its endpoints is placed in $C$, all edges in the original edge set $E$ are covered by $C$. Thus, $C$ is a valid vertex cover.
    
    \item \textbf{Maximal Matching Property:} Let $M \subseteq E$ denote the exact set of edges explicitly chosen in step 5 of the algorithm. By the algorithm's design, as soon as an edge $(u,v)$ is selected, all other edges sharing an endpoint with $u$ or $v$ are discarded. Thus, no two edges in $M$ share an endpoint. The set of edges $M$ forms a \textbf{matching}.
    
    \item \textbf{Lower Bound on Optimal Solution ($OPT$):} The optimal vertex cover $C^*$ must cover every edge in the graph, which includes the edges in our matching $M$. Since the edges in $M$ are completely disjoint (they share no vertices), the only way to cover them is to select at least one distinct vertex for every single edge in $M$. Therefore, the size of the optimal vertex cover must be at least the size of the matching:
    \begin{equation}
        |C^*| \ge |M|
    \end{equation}
    
    \item \textbf{Size of the Approximate Cover:} For every edge $(u,v) \in M$, our algorithm adds exactly two vertices ($u$ and $v$) to the set $C$. Therefore, the total number of vertices chosen by the algorithm is exactly twice the number of edges in the matching:
    \begin{equation}
        |C| = 2 |M|
    \end{equation}
    
    \item \textbf{Establishing the Ratio:} We substitute the inequality from step 3 into the equation from step 4. Since $|M| \le |C^*|$, we can multiply both sides by 2 to get $2|M| \le 2|C^*|$. Replacing $2|M|$ with $|C|$ yields:
    \begin{equation}
        |C| \le 2 |C^*|
    \end{equation}
\end{enumerate}
This confirms that the size of the cover produced by the greedy maximal matching algorithm is at most twice the optimal size, proving it is a 2-approximation. \hfill $\blacksquare$

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(|V| + |E|)$} \\
    By representing the graph using an adjacency list, we can implement this algorithm in linear time. We maintain a boolean array \texttt{visited} of size $|V|$ initialized to false. We iterate through the list of edges $E$. If we encounter an edge $(u,v)$ where both $u$ and $v$ are \texttt{false} (unvisited), we set both to \texttt{true} and add them to $C$. Marking a node implicitly removes all its incident edges because any subsequent edge touching a visited node is skipped in $O(1)$ time. Thus, each vertex and edge is processed a constant number of times.
    
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$} \\
    The algorithm requires $\mathcal{O}(|V|)$ auxiliary space to store the selected vertex cover $C$ and the \texttt{visited} array used to track which vertices are already covered.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2019-20
\end{enumerate}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cE}{Backtracking}
\label{sub:s5-backtrack}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2018-19
\item Consider the following Boolean formula:
\[
  (x' \vee y') \wedge (x' \vee y) \wedge (x \vee y' \vee z) \wedge (x \vee y \vee z)
\]
Use a backtracking algorithm to decide whether this is satisfiable or not. Show the backtracking tree.
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Problem Setup and Algorithm}

We are given the Boolean formula $F$ in Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF):
\[
  F = C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge C_3 \wedge C_4
\]
where the clauses are:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $C_1 = (x' \vee y')$
    \item $C_2 = (x' \vee y)$
    \item $C_3 = (x \vee y' \vee z)$
    \item $C_4 = (x \vee y \vee z)$
\end{itemize}

To determine if $F$ is satisfiable, we use a \textbf{Backtracking Algorithm}. The algorithm incrementally assigns truth values to variables (in the order $x, y, z$). After each assignment, it evaluates the formula. If any clause becomes $0$ (False), the current partial assignment is a dead end (conflict), and the algorithm \textit{prunes} the branch and backtracks. If all clauses evaluate to $1$ (True), a satisfying assignment is found.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Backtracking SAT Solver}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require A CNF formula $F$ over variables $V$, Current Assignment $A$
\Ensure \texttt{True} if satisfiable, \texttt{False} otherwise
\Function{SolveSAT}{$F, A$}
    \State Evaluate $F$ under the partial assignment $A$
    \If{any clause in $F$ evaluates to $0$}
        \State \Return \texttt{False} \Comment{Conflict detected, backtrack}
    \EndIf
    \If{all clauses in $F$ evaluate to $1$}
        \State \Return \texttt{True} \Comment{Satisfying assignment found}
    \EndIf
    \State Let $v$ be the next unassigned variable in $V$
    \If{\Call{SolveSAT}{$F, A \cup \{v = 0\}$}} \Comment{Try $v = 0$ (False)}
        \State \Return \texttt{True}
    \EndIf
    \State \Return \Call{SolveSAT}{$F, A \cup \{v = 1\}$} \Comment{Try $v = 1$ (True)}
\EndFunction
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Step-by-Step Execution}

We systematically construct the search tree:

\textbf{Branch $x = 0$ (False):}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $C_1 = (1 \vee y') = 1$, \quad $C_2 = (1 \vee y) = 1$
    \item $C_3 = (0 \vee y' \vee z) = y' \vee z$, \quad $C_4 = (0 \vee y \vee z) = y \vee z$
    \item The formula simplifies to $F = (y' \vee z) \wedge (y \vee z)$.
    \item \textbf{Sub-branch $y = 0$:}
        \begin{itemize}
            \item $C_3 = (1 \vee z) = 1$, \quad $C_4 = (0 \vee z) = z$. $F$ simplifies to $z$.
            \item \textbf{Try $z = 0$:} $C_4 = 0$. Conflict! (Return False).
            \item \textbf{Try $z = 1$:} $C_4 = 1$. All clauses satisfied! (Return True).
        \end{itemize}
    \item \textit{Note: A standard solver stops here. For completeness, we also show the other branches.}
    \item \textbf{Sub-branch $y = 1$:}
        \begin{itemize}
            \item $C_3 = (0 \vee z) = z$, \quad $C_4 = (1 \vee z) = 1$. $F$ simplifies to $z$.
            \item \textbf{Try $z = 0$:} $C_3 = 0$. Conflict! (Return False).
            \item \textbf{Try $z = 1$:} $C_3 = 1$. All clauses satisfied! (Return True).
        \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Branch $x = 1$ (True):}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $C_1 = (0 \vee y') = y'$, \quad $C_2 = (0 \vee y) = y$
    \item $C_3 = (1 \vee y' \vee z) = 1$, \quad $C_4 = (1 \vee y \vee z) = 1$
    \item The formula simplifies to $F = y' \wedge y$.
    \item \textbf{Sub-branch $y = 0$:} $C_2 = 0$. Conflict! The branch is pruned; $z$ is not evaluated.
    \item \textbf{Sub-branch $y = 1$:} $C_1 = 0$. Conflict! The branch is pruned; $z$ is not evaluated.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Backtracking Search Tree}

The full backtracking tree is visualized below. Green leaf nodes mark a satisfying assignment, while red leaf nodes indicate conflicts that trigger backtracking.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=6.5cm, level distance=1.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=3.2cm, level distance=1.5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.5cm},
    node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, fill=white, inner sep=1pt, minimum size=0.7cm},
    leafF/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=cE!10, inner sep=4pt, font=\small\bfseries, minimum size=0.6cm},
    leafT/.style={rectangle, draw=green!60!black, thick, fill=green!10, inner sep=4pt, font=\small\bfseries, minimum size=0.6cm},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth}
]

\node[node] (root) {\textbf{root}}
    child {
        node[node] {$x=0$}
        child {
            node[node] {$y=0$}
            child {
                node[leafF] {F}
                edge from parent node[left, pos=0.4, font=\scriptsize] {$z=0$}
            }
            child {
                node[leafT] {T}
                edge from parent node[right, pos=0.4, font=\scriptsize] {$z=1$}
            }
            edge from parent node[left, pos=0.4, font=\scriptsize] {$y=0$}
        }
        child {
            node[node] {$y=1$}
            child {
                node[leafF] {F}
                edge from parent node[left, pos=0.4, font=\scriptsize] {$z=0$}
            }
            child {
                node[leafT] {T}
                edge from parent node[right, pos=0.4, font=\scriptsize] {$z=1$}
            }
            edge from parent node[right, pos=0.4, font=\scriptsize] {$y=1$}
        }
        edge from parent node[left, pos=0.4, font=\scriptsize] {$x=0$}
    }
    child {
        node[node] {$x=1$}
        child {
            node[leafF] {F}
            edge from parent node[left, pos=0.4, font=\scriptsize] {$y=0$}
        }
        child {
            node[leafF] {F}
            edge from parent node[right, pos=0.4, font=\scriptsize] {$y=1$}
        }
        edge from parent node[right, pos=0.4, font=\scriptsize] {$x=1$}
    };

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\textbf{Conclusion:} The formula is \textbf{Satisfiable}. The first satisfying assignment found by a standard left-to-right backtracking search is $x=0, y=0, z=1$. A second satisfying assignment is $x=0, y=1, z=1$.

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(2^n \cdot m)$} \\
    Where $n$ is the number of variables ($n=3$) and $m$ is the number of clauses ($m=4$). In the worst-case scenario (an unsatisfiable formula without early pruning), the algorithm explores a complete binary tree of depth $n$, which has $2^n$ leaves. At each node, evaluating the formula takes $\mathcal{O}(m)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(n)$} \\
    The space complexity is determined by the maximum depth of the recursion stack. Since the tree depth is strictly bounded by the number of variables $n$, the memory footprint is linear with respect to the number of variables.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Consider the following boolean formula:
\[
  (x \vee y') \wedge (x' \vee w) \wedge (x \vee y \vee z') \wedge (x' \vee w \vee z)
\]
Use the backtracking algorithm to decide whether the boolean formula is satisfiable or not. If satisfiable, find all the assignments of the boolean variables for which the boolean formula is satisfiable. You must show the backtracking tree.
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Problem Setup and Simplification}

We are given the Boolean formula $F$ in Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF):
\[
  F = C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge C_3 \wedge C_4
\]
where the clauses are:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $C_1 = (x \vee y')$
    \item $C_2 = (x' \vee w)$
    \item $C_3 = (x \vee y \vee z')$
    \item $C_4 = (x' \vee w \vee z)$
\end{itemize}

To determine satisfiability and find all satisfying assignments, we apply a \textbf{backtracking algorithm}. We will sequentially assign truth values to the variables in the order $x, y, z, w$. At each step, we simplify the formula. If any clause evaluates to $0$ (False), a conflict occurs, and the branch is pruned. If all clauses evaluate to $1$ (True), a valid assignment is recorded.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Step-by-Step Backtracking Execution}

\textbf{Branch 1: $x = 0$ (False)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $C_1 = (0 \vee y') = y'$, \quad $C_2 = (1 \vee w) = 1$
    \item $C_3 = (0 \vee y \vee z') = y \vee z'$, \quad $C_4 = (1 \vee w \vee z) = 1$
    \item \textbf{Sub-branch $y = 1$:} $C_1 = 0$. \textbf{Conflict!} (Prune).
    \item \textbf{Sub-branch $y = 0$:} $C_1 = 1$, $C_3 = (0 \vee z') = z'$.
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \textbf{Try $z = 1$:} $C_3 = 0$. \textbf{Conflict!} (Prune).
            \item \textbf{Try $z = 0$:} $C_3 = 1$. All clauses are satisfied. The remaining variable $w$ can be freely assigned either $0$ or $1$, producing two valid assignments.
        \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Branch 2: $x = 1$ (True)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $C_1 = (1 \vee y') = 1$, \quad $C_3 = (1 \vee y \vee z') = 1$
    \item $C_2 = (0 \vee w) = w$, \quad $C_4 = (0 \vee w \vee z) = w \vee z$
    \item Because $C_1$ and $C_3$ are trivially satisfied, $y$ and $z$ can take any values, but we still evaluate them sequentially to build the complete tree.
    \item No matter the choices for $y$ and $z$, we are left evaluating $w$ against $C_2$ and $C_4$.
    \item \textbf{Try $w = 0$:} $C_2 = 0$. \textbf{Conflict!} (Prune in all sub-branches).
    \item \textbf{Try $w = 1$:} $C_2 = 1, C_4 = (1 \vee z) = 1$. All clauses are satisfied.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Backtracking Search Tree}

The decision tree visualizes the variable assignments. Edges are labeled with the assigned boolean value ($0$ or $1$). Green \textbf{T} leaves represent satisfying assignments, while red \textbf{F} leaves represent pruned conflicts.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=7.0cm, level distance=1.3cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=3.5cm, level distance=1.3cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.75cm, level distance=1.3cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=0.85cm, level distance=1.3cm},
    varNode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, fill=white, inner sep=1pt, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\bfseries},
    leafF/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=cE!10, inner sep=3pt, font=\scriptsize\bfseries, minimum size=0.4cm},
    leafT/.style={rectangle, draw=green!60!black, thick, fill=green!10, inner sep=3pt, font=\scriptsize\bfseries, minimum size=0.4cm},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth},
    edgeLabel/.style={pos=0.45, font=\scriptsize\bfseries, fill=white, inner sep=1.5pt}
]

\node[varNode] {x}
    child { node[varNode] {y}
        child { node[varNode] {z}
            child { node[varNode] {w}
                child { node[leafT] {T} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0} }
                child { node[leafT] {T} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1} }
                edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0}
            }
            child { node[leafF] {F} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1} }
            edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0}
        }
        child { node[leafF] {F} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1} }
        edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0}
    }
    child { node[varNode] {y}
        child { node[varNode] {z}
            child { node[varNode] {w}
                child { node[leafF] {F} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0} }
                child { node[leafT] {T} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1} }
                edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0}
            }
            child { node[varNode] {w}
                child { node[leafF] {F} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0} }
                child { node[leafT] {T} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1} }
                edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1}
            }
            edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0}
        }
        child { node[varNode] {z}
            child { node[varNode] {w}
                child { node[leafF] {F} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0} }
                child { node[leafT] {T} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1} }
                edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0}
            }
            child { node[varNode] {w}
                child { node[leafF] {F} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0} }
                child { node[leafT] {T} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1} }
                edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1}
            }
            edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1}
        }
        edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1}
    };

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Satisfiable Assignments}

The boolean formula is \textbf{Satisfiable}. Based on the backtracking tree, there are exactly \textbf{6 valid assignments} for the variables $(x, y, z, w)$ that make the formula evaluate to True:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item $x = 0, \quad y = 0, \quad z = 0, \quad w = 0$
    \item $x = 0, \quad y = 0, \quad z = 0, \quad w = 1$
    \item $x = 1, \quad y = 0, \quad z = 0, \quad w = 1$
    \item $x = 1, \quad y = 0, \quad z = 1, \quad w = 1$
    \item $x = 1, \quad y = 1, \quad z = 0, \quad w = 1$
    \item $x = 1, \quad y = 1, \quad z = 1, \quad w = 1$
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{5. Algorithm \& Complexity}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Backtracking All-SAT Solver}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require A CNF formula $F$, Variables $V$, Current Assignment $A$
\Ensure Prints all satisfying assignments
\Function{SolveAllSAT}{$F, V, A$}
    \State Evaluate $F$ under the partial assignment $A$
    \If{any clause in $F$ evaluates to $0$}
        \State \Return \Comment{Conflict: Prune branch}
    \EndIf
    \If{all variables in $V$ are assigned in $A$}
        \State \textbf{Print} $A$ \Comment{Valid assignment found}
        \State \Return
    \EndIf
    \State Let $v$ be the next unassigned variable in $V$
    \State \Call{SolveAllSAT}{$F, V, A \cup \{v = 0\}$}
    \State \Call{SolveAllSAT}{$F, V, A \cup \{v = 1\}$}
\EndFunction
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(2^n \cdot m)$} \\
    Where $n$ is the number of variables ($n=4$) and $m$ is the number of clauses ($m=4$). In the worst case, the algorithm explores the entire binary tree of depth $n$, containing $2^n$ leaves. Evaluating the $m$ clauses at each node takes linear time with respect to the formula size.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(n)$} \\
    The space complexity is dominated by the recursion stack. Since variables are assigned one at a time, the maximum depth of the stack strictly equals the number of variables, $n$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item Consider the following Boolean formula:
\[
  (x' \vee y') \wedge (x \vee y') \wedge (x' \vee y) \wedge (x \vee y \vee z') \wedge (x \vee y \vee z)
\]
Use the backtracking algorithm to decide whether this is satisfiable or not. You must show the backtracking tree.
\mk{10} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2018--19}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Problem Setup and Simplification}

We are given the Boolean formula $F$ in Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF):
\[
  F = C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge C_3 \wedge C_4 \wedge C_5
\]
where the clauses are defined as follows:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $C_1 = (x' \vee y')$
    \item $C_2 = (x \vee y')$
    \item $C_3 = (x' \vee y)$
    \item $C_4 = (x \vee y \vee z')$
    \item $C_5 = (x \vee y \vee z)$
\end{itemize}

To decide the satisfiability of the formula, we use a \textbf{backtracking algorithm}. We incrementally assign truth values to the variables in the lexicographical order $x, y, z$. At each assignment, we evaluate the formula. If any clause evaluates to $0$ (False), a conflict is detected and the search branch is immediately pruned. If all clauses evaluate to $1$ (True), a satisfying assignment is found.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Step-by-Step Execution}

We systematically evaluate the assignments:

\textbf{Branch 1: $x = 0$ (False)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $C_1 = (1 \vee y') = 1$
    \item $C_2 = (0 \vee y') = y'$
    \item $C_3 = (1 \vee y) = 1$
    \item $C_4 = (0 \vee y \vee z') = y \vee z'$
    \item $C_5 = (0 \vee y \vee z) = y \vee z$
    \item The formula simplifies to: $F|_{x=0} = y' \wedge (y \vee z') \wedge (y \vee z)$.
    
    \item \textbf{Sub-branch $y = 1$:} 
        \begin{itemize}
            \item $C_2 = 0$. \textbf{Conflict!} The branch is pruned; $z$ is not evaluated.
        \end{itemize}
        
    \item \textbf{Sub-branch $y = 0$:} 
        \begin{itemize}
            \item $C_2 = 1$
            \item $C_4 = (0 \vee z') = z'$
            \item $C_5 = (0 \vee z) = z$
            \item The formula simplifies to: $F|_{x=0, y=0} = z' \wedge z$.
            \item \textbf{Try $z = 0$:} $C_5 = 0$. \textbf{Conflict!} (Prune).
            \item \textbf{Try $z = 1$:} $C_4 = 0$. \textbf{Conflict!} (Prune).
        \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Branch 2: $x = 1$ (True)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $C_1 = (0 \vee y') = y'$
    \item $C_2 = (1 \vee y') = 1$
    \item $C_3 = (0 \vee y) = y$
    \item $C_4 = (1 \vee y \vee z') = 1$
    \item $C_5 = (1 \vee y \vee z) = 1$
    \item The formula simplifies to: $F|_{x=1} = y' \wedge y$.
    
    \item \textbf{Sub-branch $y = 0$:} 
        \begin{itemize}
            \item $C_3 = 0$. \textbf{Conflict!} The branch is pruned; $z$ is not evaluated.
        \end{itemize}
        
    \item \textbf{Sub-branch $y = 1$:} 
        \begin{itemize}
            \item $C_1 = 0$. \textbf{Conflict!} The branch is pruned; $z$ is not evaluated.
        \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Backtracking Search Tree}

The decision tree illustrates the assignments and the ensuing conflicts. Red \textbf{F} leaves signify that a clause evaluated to False (a conflict), prompting the algorithm to backtrack.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=6cm, level distance=1.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=3cm, level distance=1.5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.5cm},
    varNode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\bfseries},
    leafF/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=cE!10, inner sep=4pt, font=\small\bfseries, minimum size=0.5cm},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth},
    edgeLabel/.style={pos=0.45, font=\scriptsize\bfseries, fill=white, inner sep=1.5pt}
]

\node[varNode] {x}
    child { node[varNode] {y}
        child { node[varNode] {z}
            child { node[leafF] {F} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0} }
            child { node[leafF] {F} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1} }
            edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0}
        }
        child { node[leafF] {F} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1} }
        edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0}
    }
    child { node[varNode] {y}
        child { node[leafF] {F} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0} }
        child { node[leafF] {F} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1} }
        edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1}
    };

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Conclusion}

Because every single branch in the backtracking search tree results in a conflict (terminates in \textbf{F}), there is no combination of boolean assignments that satisfies all clauses simultaneously. 

Therefore, the given boolean formula is \textbf{Unsatisfiable}.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{5. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(2^n \cdot m)$} \\
    Here, $n = 3$ is the number of variables and $m = 5$ is the number of clauses. In the worst case, the backtracking algorithm explores a full binary tree of height $n$, producing $2^n$ paths. At each node, evaluating the expression takes $\mathcal{O}(m)$ operations. Thus, the time complexity is bounded by $\mathcal{O}(2^n \cdot m)$.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(n)$} \\
    The space complexity corresponds to the maximum depth of the recursion stack, which maps directly to the number of variables $n$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Apply backtracking to the problem of finding a Hamiltonian circuit in a given graph. Show the corresponding state-space tree. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{center}
  
  \begin{tikzpicture}[
    every node/.style={circle, fill=black, 
                       minimum size=6pt, inner sep=0pt},
    every edge/.style={draw}
  ]

  % Outer rectangle (back)
  \node[label=above left:$a$]  (a) at (0, 3)   {};
  \node[label=above right:$b$] (b) at (6, 3)   {};
  \node[label=below right:$c$] (c) at (6, 0)   {};
  \node[label=below left:$d$]  (d) at (0, 0)   {};

  % Inner rectangle (front)
  \node[label=above left:$f$]  (f) at (1.5, 2) {};
  \node[label=above right:$g$] (g) at (4.5, 2) {};
  \node[label=below right:$h$] (h) at (4.5, 1) {};
  \node[label=below left:$e$]  (e) at (1.5, 1) {};

  % Outer rectangle
  \draw (a) -- (b) -- (c) -- (d) -- (a);

  % Inner rectangle
  \draw (f) -- (g) -- (h) -- (e) -- (f);

  % Connecting edges
  \draw (a) -- (f);
  \draw (b) -- (g);
  \draw (c) -- (h);
  \draw (d) -- (e);

  \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Problem Setup and Graph Representation}

The problem requires us to find a Hamiltonian circuit (a simple cycle that visits every vertex exactly once and returns to the starting vertex) in the given graph using backtracking. Let the total number of vertices be $N = 8$.

First, we extract the adjacency list for the graph based on the given visual representation:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{$a$}: $b, d, f$
    \item \textbf{$b$}: $a, c, g$
    \item \textbf{$c$}: $b, d, h$
    \item \textbf{$d$}: $a, c, e$
    \item \textbf{$e$}: $d, f, h$
    \item \textbf{$f$}: $a, e, g$
    \item \textbf{$g$}: $b, f, h$
    \item \textbf{$h$}: $c, e, g$
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Backtracking Algorithm}

The backtracking algorithm builds a path vertex by vertex. At each step, it attempts to add an unvisited neighbor of the current vertex to the path. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Base Case:} If the path length equals $N$, we check if there is an edge between the last vertex and the starting vertex. If so, a Hamiltonian circuit is found.
    \item \textbf{Recursive Step:} Iterate through all unvisited neighbors. If adding a neighbor leads to a dead end, we \textit{backtrack} by removing it from the path and trying the next available neighbor.
\end{itemize}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Backtracking for Hamiltonian Circuit}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Adjacency list of Graph $G = (V, E)$, starting vertex $s$
\Ensure A sequence of vertices forming a Hamiltonian Circuit, or failure.
\State \textbf{Initialize:} $path = [s]$, $visited[v] = \text{False}$ for all $v \in V$, $visited[s] = \text{True}$
\Function{FindCircuit}{$u, count$}
    \If{$count == |V|$}
        \If{edge $(u, s)$ exists in $E$}
            \State \textbf{Print} $path \cup \{s\}$ \Comment{Circuit found}
            \State \Return \text{True}
        \Else
            \State \Return \text{False} \Comment{Dead end, backtrack}
        \EndIf
    \EndIf
    \For{each neighbor $v$ of $u$}
        \If{\textbf{not} $visited[v]$}
            \State $visited[v] \gets \text{True}$
            \State $path \gets path \cup \{v\}$
            \If{\Call{FindCircuit}{$v, count + 1$}}
                \State \Return \text{True}
            \EndIf
            \State $visited[v] \gets \text{False}$ \Comment{Backtrack}
            \State Remove $v$ from $path$
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
    \State \Return \text{False}
\EndFunction
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Step-by-Step Execution}

We systematically trace the algorithm starting at vertex \textbf{$a$}, exploring neighbors in alphabetical order whenever multiple choices are available.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Start at \textbf{$a$}. Neighbors are $b, d, f$. Try $b$. Path: $[a, b]$
    \item From \textbf{$b$}, unvisited neighbors are $c, g$. Try $c$. Path: $[a, b, c]$
    \item From \textbf{$c$}, unvisited neighbors are $d, h$. Try $d$. Path: $[a, b, c, d]$
    \item From \textbf{$d$}, unvisited neighbor is $e$. Path: $[a, b, c, d, e]$
    \item From \textbf{$e$}, unvisited neighbors are $f, h$. Try $f$. Path: $[a, b, c, d, e, f]$
    \item From \textbf{$f$}, unvisited neighbor is $g$. Path: $[a, b, c, d, e, f, g]$
    \item From \textbf{$g$}, unvisited neighbor is $h$. Path: $[a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h]$
    \item \textbf{Check Circuit:} Path length is 8. Is there an edge between $h$ and start node $a$? No. 
    \item \textbf{Backtrack:} Backtrack to $g \to f \to e$. At vertex $e$, the next unvisited neighbor is $h$.
    \item From \textbf{$e$}, try $h$. Path: $[a, b, c, d, e, h]$
    \item From \textbf{$h$}, unvisited neighbor is $g$. Path: $[a, b, c, d, e, h, g]$
    \item From \textbf{$g$}, unvisited neighbor is $f$. Path: $[a, b, c, d, e, h, g, f]$
    \item \textbf{Check Circuit:} Path length is 8. Is there an edge between $f$ and $a$? Yes, edge $(f,a)$ exists!
    \item \textbf{Success!} Valid Hamiltonian Circuit found: \textbf{$a \to b \to c \to d \to e \to h \to g \to f \to a$}.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. State-Space Tree}

The diagram below represents the partial state-space tree demonstrating the successful search path and the first backtracking instance. Dashed lines indicate branches that are either pruned later or left unexplored once the first solution is found.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3.5cm, level distance=1.1cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.2cm, level distance=1.1cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.8cm, level distance=1.1cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.1cm},
    level 5/.style={sibling distance=2.8cm, level distance=1.1cm},
    level 6/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.1cm},
    level 7/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.1cm},
    level 8/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.1cm},
    node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\bfseries},
    sol/.style={rectangle, draw=green!60!black, thick, fill=green!10, inner sep=4pt, font=\small\bfseries, rounded corners},
    fail/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=cE!10, inner sep=4pt, font=\small\bfseries, rounded corners},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth}
]

\node[node] {a}
    child { node[node] {b}
        child { node[node] {c}
            child { node[node] {d}
                child { node[node] {e}
                    child { node[node] {f}
                        child { node[node] {g}
                            child { node[node] {h}
                                child { node[fail] {Fail} edge from parent node[left, font=\scriptsize, align=center, xshift=-0.1cm] {No edge\\$(h,a)$} }
                            }
                        }
                    }
                    child { node[node] {h}
                        child { node[node] {g}
                            child { node[node] {f}
                                child { node[sol] {Solution!} edge from parent node[right, font=\scriptsize, align=center, xshift=0.1cm] {Edge\\$(f,a)$} }
                            }
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
            child { node[node] {h} edge from parent[dashed] }
        }
        child { node[node] {g} edge from parent[dashed] }
    }
    child { node[node] {d} edge from parent[dashed] }
    child { node[node] {f} edge from parent[dashed] };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.3cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{5. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(N!)$} \\
    In the worst-case scenario (e.g., a highly connected graph with no Hamiltonian circuits), the backtracking algorithm behaves essentially like generating permutations of vertices, exploring all possible paths. At each step, the number of choices decreases by 1, leading to a factorial upper bound $\mathcal{O}(N!)$ where $N$ is the number of vertices.
    
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(N)$} \\
    The space complexity is defined by the maximum depth of the recursion stack and the auxiliary structures (the \texttt{path} array and the \texttt{visited} boolean array). Since the maximum path length cannot exceed the total number of vertices $N$, the space complexity is strictly bounded by $\mathcal{O}(N)$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Discuss the Presumed Optimal Solution for the Multipeg Tower of Hanoi Problem with its properties. Solve the problem for $(n, p) = (395, 8)$. \mk{5+10}
\yr{CSE 203 | L-2/T-1 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cB!4,colframe=cB,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cB]

\subsubsection*{1. Discussion of the Presumed Optimal Solution (Frame-Stewart Algorithm)}

The \textbf{Multipeg Tower of Hanoi} problem generalizes the classical 3-peg puzzle to $p$ pegs ($p \ge 3$) with $n$ distinct disks. The standard, widely accepted framework for generating the optimal sequence of moves is the \textbf{Frame-Stewart Algorithm}, proposed independently by J.S. Frame and B.M. Stewart in 1941. 

\paragraph{The Algorithmic Strategy:}
To transfer $n$ disks from a source peg to a destination peg using $p$ available pegs:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item Choose an optimal partitioning integer $k$ such that $1 \le k < n$.
    \item Recursively transfer the top $k$ smallest disks from the source peg to an intermediate peg using all $p$ pegs.
    \item Transfer the remaining $n - k$ larger disks from the source peg to the destination peg using only the remaining $p - 1$ pegs (since the peg holding the $k$ smaller disks cannot accommodate larger ones).
    \item Recursively transfer the $k$ smallest disks from the intermediate peg to the destination peg using all $p$ pegs.
\end{enumerate}

\[
T(n, p) = \min_{1 \le k < n} \left\{ 2T(k, p) + T(n - k, p - 1) \right\}
\]

\paragraph{Base Cases:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item $T(0, p) = 0$ and $T(1, p) = 1$ for all $p \ge 3$.
    \item For $p = 3$, it reduces to the classical Tower of Hanoi: $T(n, 3) = 2^n - 1$.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.85, every node/.style={transform shape}]
    % Draw Pegs
    \foreach \x in {0, 4, 8, 12} {
        \draw[line width=3pt, gray!80] (\x, 0) -- (\x, 3.5);
    }
    \draw[line width=2pt, black] (-1,0) -- (13,0);
    
    % Labels
    \node at (0, -0.4) {\textbf{Source}};
    \node at (4, -0.4) {\textbf{Peg 2}};
    \node at (8, -0.4) {\textbf{Peg 3 (Temp)}};
    \node at (12, -0.4) {\textbf{Destination}};
    
    % Representation of Disk Partitioning
    \draw[fill=cB!40, draw=black, thick, rounded corners=2pt] (-1.2, 1.4) rectangle (1.2, 2.6);
    \node at (0, 2.0) {\textbf{Top $k$ Disks}};
    
    \draw[fill=orange!40, draw=black, thick, rounded corners=2pt] (-1.6, 0.1) rectangle (1.6, 1.3);
    \node at (0, 0.7) {\textbf{Bottom $n-k$ Disks}};
    
    % Arrows showing transitions
    \draw[->,>=stealth,line width=1.2pt,dashed,cB] (0,2.8) .. controls (4,4.2) and (6,4.2) .. (8,2.8) 
        node[pos=0.5, above, black] {Step 1: $T(k,p)$ moves};
        
    \draw[->,>=stealth,line width=1.2pt,orange,thick] (0,0.4) .. controls (4,-1.2) and (8,-1.2) .. (12,0.4)
        node[pos=0.5, below, black] {Step 2: $T(n-k,p-1)$ moves};
        
    \draw[->,>=stealth,line width=1.2pt,dashed,cB] (8,2.4) .. controls (9,3.5) and (11,3.5) .. (12,2.4)
        node[pos=0.5, above, black] {Step 3: $T(k,p)$ moves};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\paragraph{Properties of the Presumed Optimal Solution:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Optimality Status:} The algorithm was formally proven optimal for $p = 4$ pegs by Thierry Bousch in 2014. For $p > 4$, it remains an unproven mathematical conjecture, hence designated as the \textit{presumed} optimal solution.
    \item \textbf{Structure of Cost Increments:} The sequence of first differences $D(n, p) = T(n, p) - T(n-1, p)$ consists entirely of powers of 2. For a given $p$, the value $2^m$ appears exactly $\binom{m + p - 3}{p - 3}$ times.
    \item \textbf{Asymptotic Complexity:} For a fixed number of pegs $p$, the number of total moves scales as a sub-exponential function:
    \[
    T(n, p) = \Theta\left(2^{n^{1/(p-2)}}\right)
    \]
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Dynamic Programming Formulation}

To implement and evaluate the Frame-Stewart recurrence optimally, a bottom-up Dynamic Programming approach is utilized to eliminate redundant recursive calculations.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Frame-Stewart Dynamic Programming Solver}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Max disks $n$, total pegs $p$
\State \textbf{Output:} Exact minimum moves $T[n][p]$
\State Initialize a 2D array $T[n+1][p+1]$ with $0$
\For{$i \gets 1$ \textbf{to} $n$}
    \State $T[i][3] \gets 2^i - 1$ \Comment{Base Case: Standard 3-peg problem}
\EndFor
\For{$j \gets 3$ \textbf{to} $p$}
    \State $T[1][j] \gets 1$ \Comment{Base Case: 1 disk requires 1 move}
\EndFor
\For{$j \gets 4$ \textbf{to} $p$}
    \For{$i \gets 2$ \textbf{to} $n$}
        \State $min\_moves \gets \infty$
        \For{$k \gets 1$ \textbf{to} $i-1$}
            \State $current\_moves \gets 2 \cdot T[k][j] + T[i-k][j-1]$
            \If{$current\_moves < min\_moves$}
                \State $min\_moves \gets current\_moves$
            \EndIf
        \EndFor
        \State $T[i][j] \gets min\_moves$
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\State \Return $T[n][p]$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\paragraph{Complexity Analysis:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(p \cdot n^2)$. The nested loops evaluate $n$ disk configurations across $p$ pegs, iterating through $i-1$ possible split points at each step.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(p \cdot n)$ to maintain the look-up table for intermediate results.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Analytical Solution for $(n, p) = (395, 8)$}

Using the combinatorial property of the cost increments, we can systematically isolate the contribution of each power of 2 to compute the exact value of $T(395, 8)$. For $p = 8$, the frequency of each move increment $2^m$ corresponds to the binomial coefficient:
\[
\text{Frequency}(m) = \binom{m + 8 - 3}{8 - 3} = \binom{m + 5}{5}
\]

We evaluate this sequentially for each integer block $m \ge 0$ until the cumulative count of allocated disks reaches $n = 395$:

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3} 
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Exponent ($m$)} & \textbf{Increment ($2^m$)} & \textbf{Frequency $\binom{m+5}{5}$} & \textbf{Cumulative Disks} & \textbf{Moves Contributed} \\ \hline
0 & $2^0 = 1$   & $\binom{5}{5} = 1$    & 1   & $1 \times 1 = 1$ \\ \hline
1 & $2^1 = 2$   & $\binom{6}{5} = 6$    & 7   & $6 \times 2 = 12$ \\ \hline
2 & $2^2 = 4$   & $\binom{7}{5} = 21$   & 28  & $21 \times 4 = 84$ \\ \hline
3 & $2^3 = 8$   & $\binom{8}{5} = 56$   & 84  & $56 \times 8 = 448$ \\ \hline
4 & $2^4 = 16$  & $\binom{9}{5} = 126$  & 210 & $126 \times 16 = 2016$ \\ \hline
5 & $2^5 = 32$  & $395 - 210 = 185$ (Partial) & 395 & $185 \times 32 = 5920$ \\ \hline
\hline
\textbf{Total} & -- & \textbf{395} & -- & \textbf{8481} \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\paragraph{Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation:}
\begin{align*}
    \text{Disks remaining for block } m=5 &= 395 - \sum_{m=0}^{4} \binom{m+5}{5} \\
    &= 395 - (1 + 6 + 21 + 56 + 126) \\
    &= 395 - 210 = 185
\end{align*}

Because the full structural capacity of block $m=5$ is $\binom{5+5}{5} = \binom{10}{5} = 252$, and $185 \le 252$, the final 185 disks fall completely within this block and generate an increment value of exactly $32$ moves per disk.

Summing up the total operations:
\begin{align*}
T(395, 8) &= \sum_{m=0}^{4} \left[ 2^m \cdot \binom{m+5}{5} \right] + \left[ 185 \cdot 2^5 \right] \\
&= (1 \cdot 1) + (2 \cdot 6) + (4 \cdot 21) + (8 \cdot 56) + (16 \cdot 126) + (185 \cdot 32) \\
&= 1 + 12 + 84 + 448 + 2016 + 5920 \\
&= 8481
\end{align*}

\paragraph{Conclusion:}
The minimum number of moves required to solve the Multipeg Tower of Hanoi problem for 395 disks using 8 pegs under the Frame-Stewart strategy is exactly \textbf{8481} moves.

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Consider the following Boolean formula: $(x' \vee y)(x \vee y' \vee z)(y \vee y')(x \vee y)(x \vee y \vee z')(x \vee y \vee z)$. Use a backtracking algorithm to decide whether it is satisfiable. Show the backtracking tree. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2016--17}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Problem Setup and Clause Analysis}

We are given the following Boolean formula $F$ in Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) with three variables ($x, y, z$) and six clauses ($C_1$ to $C_6$):
\[
F = C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge C_3 \wedge C_4 \wedge C_5 \wedge C_6
\]
where the individual clauses are defined as:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $C_1 = (x' \vee y)$
    \item $C_2 = (x \vee y' \vee z)$
    \item $C_3 = (y \vee y')$
    \item $C_4 = (x \vee y)$
    \item $C_5 = (x \vee y \vee z')$
    \item $C_6 = (x \vee y \vee z)$
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Initial Observation (Tautology Elimination):} 
Clause $C_3 = (y \vee y')$ is a tautology because it evaluates to True ($1$) for any truth assignment of $y$. Therefore, it does not constrain the satisfiability of the formula and can be effectively ignored during evaluation, simplifying our formula to:
\[
F \equiv (x' \vee y) \wedge (x \vee y' \vee z) \wedge (x \vee y) \wedge (x \vee y \vee z') \wedge (x \vee y \vee z)
\]

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Step-by-Step Backtracking Exploration}

We apply the chronological backtracking algorithm, assigning truth values to variables in lexicographical order ($x \to y \to z$). We use $0$ for False and $1$ for True.

\paragraph{Branch 1: $x = 0$}
Substituting $x = 0$ into the simplified formula:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $C_1 = (0' \vee y) = (1 \vee y) = 1$
    \item $C_2 = (0 \vee y' \vee z) = (y' \vee z)$
    \item $C_4 = (0 \vee y) = y$
    \item $C_5 = (0 \vee y \vee z') = (y \vee z')$
    \item $C_6 = (0 \vee y \vee z) = (y \vee z)$
\end{itemize}
The formula simplifies to $F|_{x=0} = (y' \vee z) \wedge y \wedge (y \vee z') \wedge (y \vee z)$.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Sub-branch $y = 0$:}
    Substituting $y = 0$ makes clause $C_4 = 0$. Since a clause evaluates to False, the entire conjunction becomes False. 
    $$\text{\textbf{Conflict detected! Backtrack immediately.}}$$
    
    \item \textbf{Sub-branch $y = 1$:}
    Substituting $y = 1$ into $F|_{x=0}$:
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $C_2 = (1' \vee z) = (0 \vee z) = z$
        \item $C_4 = 1$
        \item $C_5 = (1 \vee z') = 1$
        \item $C_6 = (1 \vee z) = 1$
    \end{itemize}
    The remaining formula simplifies to $F|_{x=0, y=1} = z$.
    
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Try $z = 0$:} This yields $C_2 = 0$. $\to$ \textbf{Conflict!}
        \item \textbf{Try $z = 1$:} This yields $C_2 = 1$. All clauses are simultaneously satisfied!
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

At this point, the assignment $\mathbf{\{x = 0, y = 1, z = 1\}}$ satisfies the formula. A standard backtracking algorithm terminates here upon finding the first satisfying assignment. For completeness, the full search space tree below also shows the alternative choices.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Backtracking Search Tree}

The state-space tree below visualizes the execution. Nodes represent variables, edge labels ($0$ or $1$) represent truth assignments, red \textbf{F} blocks represent unsatisfiable leaves (conflicts), and green \textbf{T} blocks indicate satisfying assignments.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=6cm, level distance=1.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=3cm, level distance=1.5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.5cm},
    varNode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\bfseries},
    leafF/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=cE!10, inner sep=4pt, font=\small\bfseries, minimum size=0.5cm},
    leafS/.style={rectangle, draw=green!60!black, thick, fill=green!10, inner sep=4pt, font=\small\bfseries, minimum size=0.5cm},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth},
    edgeLabel/.style={pos=0.45, font=\scriptsize\bfseries, fill=white, inner sep=1.5pt}
]

\node[varNode] {x}
    child { node[varNode] {y}
        child { node[leafF] {F} 
            edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0} node[left=3pt, font=\tiny, text=cE] {($C_4$ fails)}
        }
        child { node[varNode] {z}
            child { node[leafF] {F} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0} node[left=2pt, font=\tiny, text=cE] {($C_2$ fails)} }
            child { node[leafS] {T} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1} node[right=2pt, font=\tiny, text=green!60!black] {(Satisfied)} }
            edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1}
        }
        edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0}
    }
    child { node[varNode] {y}
        child { node[leafF] {F} 
            edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0} node[left=3pt, font=\tiny, text=cE] {($C_1$ fails)}
        }
        child { node[varNode] {z}
            child { node[leafS] {T} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {0} }
            child { node[leafS] {T} edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1} }
            edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1}
        }
        edge from parent node[edgeLabel] {1}
    };

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Conclusion}

Since the backtracking algorithm successfully discovered at least one terminal leaf node evaluating to True (\textbf{T}), we conclude that the Boolean formula is \textbf{Satisfiable}. 

One satisfying assignment is:
\[
x = \text{False } (0), \quad y = \text{True } (1), \quad z = \text{True } (1)
\]

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{5. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(2^n \cdot m)$ in the worst case. \\
    Where $n = 3$ is the number of variables and $m = 6$ is the number of clauses. The algorithm searches a binary tree structure of depth at most $n$. At every internal node, evaluating the truth values against the clauses requires $\mathcal{O}(m)$ time. Though backtracking prunes mismatched branches early, the theoretical upper bound for full exploration remains exponential.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(n)$ \\
    The storage overhead is strictly dominated by the execution stack frame during recursion, which corresponds directly to the maximum height of the decision path tree ($n$).
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}


\item Compare the techniques of brute-force algorithms, heuristics, and approximation algorithms for coping with hard problems. Write a backtracking algorithm to color a map with no more than four colors. \mk{5+5=10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2015--16}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Comparison of Techniques for Coping with Hard Problems}

When dealing with NP-Hard problems where polynomial-time optimal solutions are unlikely to exist, we often rely on three primary strategies. The table below compares their fundamental characteristics:

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.4}
\begin{tabular}{|p{3cm}|p{4.2cm}|p{4.2cm}|p{3.2cm}|}
\hline
\rowcolor{cE!20}
\textbf{Feature} & \textbf{Brute-Force (Exact)} & \textbf{Approximation Algorithms} & \textbf{Heuristics} \\ \hline
\textbf{Objective} & Find the absolute optimal solution. & Find a near-optimal solution with proven bounds. & Find a "good enough" solution quickly in practice. \\ \hline
\textbf{Time Complexity} & Usually exponential $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ or factorial $\mathcal{O}(n!)$. & Polynomial time $\mathcal{O}(n^k)$. & Usually polynomial time, often very fast. \\ \hline
\textbf{Solution Quality} & Guaranteed $100\%$ optimal. & Sub-optimal, but guaranteed to be within a specific ratio $\rho$ of the optimum. & No worst-case guarantees. May be optimal or arbitrarily bad. \\ \hline
\textbf{Mathematical Guarantees} & Yes (Completeness and Optimality). & Yes (Rigorous approximation bounds). & None (Rely on empirical testing). \\ \hline
\textbf{Applicability} & Only feasible for very small problem instances. & Used when theoretically bounded error is strictly required. & Used for large real-world instances where speed is critical. \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Backtracking Algorithm for 4-Coloring a Map}

The problem of coloring a map such that no two adjacent regions share the same color can be modeled as the \textbf{Graph Coloring Problem}. Each region is a vertex, and adjacent regions are connected by edges. We are restricted to at most $m = 4$ colors.

We solve this using a recursive backtracking approach. We try assigning colors $1$ to $4$ to each vertex one by one. If a color assignment violates the adjacency constraint, we backtrack.

\paragraph{Algorithm Definition}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Graph Coloring Backtracking}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Adjacency Matrix $G[1 \dots n][1 \dots n]$, number of colors $m=4$
\Ensure A valid color assignment array $color[1 \dots n]$ or failure.
\State Initialize $color[1 \dots n] \gets 0$ \Comment{$0$ means uncolored}
\State
\Function{IsSafe}{$v, G, color, c$}
    \For{$u \gets 1$ \textbf{to} $n$}
        \If{$G[v][u] == 1$ \textbf{and} $color[u] == c$}
            \State \Return \text{False} \Comment{Adjacent vertex has the same color}
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
    \State \Return \text{True}
\EndFunction
\State
\Function{ColorMapUtil}{$v, G, m, color$}
    \If{$v > n$}
        \State \Return \text{True} \Comment{All vertices are successfully colored}
    \EndIf
    \For{$c \gets 1$ \textbf{to} $m$}
        \If{\Call{IsSafe}{$v, G, color, c$}}
            \State $color[v] \gets c$ \Comment{Assign color $c$ to vertex $v$}
            \If{\Call{ColorMapUtil}{$v + 1, G, m, color$}}
                \State \Return \text{True} \Comment{Proceed to next vertex}
            \EndIf
            \State $color[v] \gets 0$ \Comment{Backtrack: Remove color assignment}
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
    \State \Return \text{False} \Comment{No valid color found for vertex $v$}
\EndFunction
\State
\Function{GraphColoring}{$G, m$}
    \If{\Call{ColorMapUtil}{$1, G, m, color$}}
        \State \Return $color$
    \Else
        \State \Return \text{"No solution exists"}
    \EndIf
\EndFunction
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(m^V)$} \\
    Here, $m = 4$ is the maximum number of colors and $V = n$ is the number of vertices (regions). In the worst-case scenario, the algorithm attempts every possible color combination for every vertex. The search tree has a branching factor of $m$ and a maximum depth of $V$, leading to an exponential time bound of $\mathcal{O}(4^n)$.
