    \item \textbf{Space Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(V)$} \\
    The space complexity is determined by the maximum depth of the recursive call stack, which is exactly $V$ (one frame for each vertex). Additionally, an array of size $V$ is required to store the current color assignments. Thus, the overall space complexity is $\mathcal{O}(n)$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Solve the Multi-peg Tower of Hanoi problem with $(n, p) = (377, 8)$. Find $k_{\max}$, $N_0$, and $n_1$ for at least two subproblems. \mk{10}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2013--14}

\item Solve the Multi-peg Tower of Hanoi problem with $(n, p) = (270, 7)$, showing the values of $k$, $\max$, $N_0$, $n_1$. Present the solution by drawing a binary tree. \mk{15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2008--09}


\item Discuss the Multi-peg Hanoi problem. Argue in favour of the recurrence relation to be satisfied by the presumed optimal solution. Prove the properties of the presumed optimal solution. \mk{5+5+10=20}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2008--09}

\item Write down the backtracking technique for solving the 4-Queen problem. Give the state tree also. \mk{10+5=15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2008--09}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Problem Definition and Backtracking Formulation}

The \textbf{4-Queen Problem} requires placing 4 queens on a $4 \times 4$ chessboard such that no two queens threaten each other. This means no two queens can share the same row, column, or diagonal.

\paragraph{State Representation:}
We represent the board using a 1-dimensional array $Q[1 \dots 4]$, where the index $i$ represents the row number, and the value $Q[i]$ represents the column position of the queen in row $i$. This formulation automatically ensures that no two queens are in the same row.

\paragraph{Bounding/Pruning Constraints:}
When attempting to place a new queen in row $k$ and column $c$, we must verify it against all previously placed queens in rows $1 \dots (k-1)$. A placement is \textbf{invalid} if:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Same Column:} $Q[i] == c$
    \item \textbf{Same Diagonal:} $|Q[i] - c| == |i - k|$ \\
    \textit{(The slope of the line connecting two squares on the same diagonal is always $\pm 1$.)}
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Backtracking Algorithm}

The algorithm incrementally builds the solution. If a placement leads to a state where subsequent queens cannot be placed, it \textit{backtracks} by removing the last placed queen and trying the next available column.

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{4-Queens Backtracking Algorithm}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require $N = 4$ (Board size and number of queens)
\Ensure A valid configuration array $Q[1 \dots N]$ or exhaust all possibilities.
\State \textbf{Initialize} $Q[1 \dots N] \gets [0, 0, 0, 0]$
\Function{IsSafe}{$k, c$}
    \For{$i \gets 1$ \textbf{to} $k-1$}
        \If{$Q[i] == c$ \textbf{or} $|Q[i] - c| == |i - k|$}
            \State \Return \text{False} \Comment{Conflict detected}
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
    \State \Return \text{True} \Comment{Placement is safe}
\EndFunction
\State
\Function{SolveNQueens}{$k$}
    \For{$c \gets 1$ \textbf{to} $N$}
        \If{\Call{IsSafe}{$k, c$}}
            \State $Q[k] \gets c$ \Comment{Place Queen $k$ in column $c$}
            \If{$k == N$}
                \State \textbf{Print} $Q[1 \dots N]$ \Comment{Valid solution found}
            \Else
                \State \Call{SolveNQueens}{$k + 1$} \Comment{Recurse for next row}
            \EndIf
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndFunction
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. State-Space Tree}

The state-space tree below illustrates the backtracking process. The root represents the empty board. The levels $1$ to $4$ represent the rows. The values inside the nodes are the \textbf{column numbers} chosen for that row. 

Branches leading to immediate conflicts (column or diagonal) are pruned early (marked with \textbf{X}). We show the full trace leading to the first valid solution: $Q = [2, 4, 1, 3]$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=6cm, level distance=1.4cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=3cm, level distance=1.4cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.4cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.5cm, level distance=1.4cm},
    node/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, fill=white, inner sep=1pt, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\bfseries},
    fail/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=cE!10, inner sep=3pt, font=\scriptsize\bfseries, rounded corners},
    sol/.style={rectangle, draw=green!60!black, thick, fill=green!10, inner sep=4pt, font=\small\bfseries, rounded corners},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth}
]

\node[node] {R}
    child { node[node] {1}
        child { node[fail] {X (1, 2)} edge from parent node[left, font=\scriptsize] {Col 1,2} }
        child { node[node] {3}
            child { node[fail] {X (1..4)} edge from parent node[right, font=\scriptsize] {Col 1..4 fail} }
            edge from parent node[left, font=\scriptsize] {Col 3}
        }
        child { node[node] {4}
            child { node[node] {2}
                child { node[fail] {X (1..4)} edge from parent node[right, font=\scriptsize] {Col 1..4 fail} }
                edge from parent node[right, font=\scriptsize] {Col 2}
            }
            edge from parent node[right, font=\scriptsize] {Col 4}
        }
        edge from parent node[above left, font=\small\bfseries] {$Q_1=1$}
    }
    child { node[node] {2}
        child { node[fail] {X (1,2,3)} edge from parent node[left, font=\scriptsize] {Col 1,2,3} }
        child { node[node] {4}
            child { node[node] {1}
                child { node[sol] {3 (Sol!)} edge from parent node[right, font=\scriptsize] {Col 3} }
                edge from parent node[right, font=\scriptsize] {Col 1}
            }
            edge from parent node[right, font=\scriptsize] {Col 4}
        }
        edge from parent node[above right, font=\small\bfseries] {$Q_1=2$}
    };
    
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(N!)$} \\
    For the first row, there are $N$ choices. For the second row, there are at most $N-1$ choices (due to column constraints), and so on. Without pruning, this is bounded by $N!$. Backtracking significantly reduces the number of generated states in practice, but the worst-case upper bound remains $\mathcal{O}(N!)$.
    
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(N)$} \\
    The space complexity is defined by the maximum depth of the recursive call stack, which corresponds to the number of rows $N$. Additionally, the array $Q[1 \dots N]$ takes $\mathcal{O}(N)$ space. Thus, overall space required is $\mathcal{O}(N)$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2007--08}

\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2006--07}


%% Q — 2020-21
\end{enumerate}

%%──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
\subtopic{cE}{Branch and Bound}
\label{sub:s5-branchbound}

\begin{enumerate}

%% Q — 2019-20
\item Consider the following instance of the 0-1 knapsack problem. Solve this instance using the branch and bound algorithm. Show the branch-and-bound tree. Knapsack capacity $= 10$.
 
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Item} & \textbf{Weight} & \textbf{Value} \\
\hline
1 & 4 & 40 \\
2 & 3 & 50 \\
3 & 5 & 70 \\
4 & 3 & 45 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2024--25}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Preprocessing: Item Sorting}

The optimal strategy for the Branch and Bound approach to the 0-1 Knapsack problem is to first compute the value-to-weight ratio ($v_i/w_i$) for each item and sort them in \textbf{descending order}. This allows the algorithm to greedily find the highest theoretical upper bound at each node.

Given the knapsack capacity $W = 10$, we calculate and sort:

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\rowcolor{cE!20}
\textbf{Sorted Label} & \textbf{Original Item} & \textbf{Value ($v_i$)} & \textbf{Weight ($w_i$)} & \textbf{Ratio ($v_i/w_i$)} \\
\hline
\textbf{A} & 2 & 50 & 3 & 16.67 \\
\textbf{B} & 4 & 45 & 3 & 15.00 \\
\textbf{C} & 3 & 70 & 5 & 14.00 \\
\textbf{D} & 1 & 40 & 4 & 10.00 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Bounding Function Definition}

At any node in our state-space tree, the \textbf{Upper Bound (UB)} is calculated by taking the accumulated value $v$ of the already included items, and then greedily adding the remaining items in sorted order. If an item fits entirely, its full value is added. If it exceeds the remaining capacity, we add a \textit{fraction} of its value to fill the knapsack exactly to $W$. 
\[
UB = v + \sum_{\text{full items}} v_i + \left( \frac{\text{remaining capacity}}{\text{weight of first item that doesn't fit}} \right) \times \text{value of that item}
\]
A node is \textbf{pruned} if:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Its total weight exceeds the capacity $W$ (\textit{Infeasible}).
    \item Its $UB$ is less than or equal to the best integer solution found so far ($MaxProfit$).
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Step-by-Step State Evaluation (Depth-First Search)}

We initialize $MaxProfit = 0$ and explore the state-space tree using DFS.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Node 0 (Root):} $w=0, v=0$. 
    We fill with A($w=3$), B($w=3$), leaving capacity $4$. We take $\frac{4}{5}$ of C($v=70$). 
    $UB = 0 + 50 + 45 + (\frac{4}{5} \times 70) = 151$.
    
    \item \textbf{Node 1 (+A):} $w=3, v=50$. $UB = 151$. Explore further.
    
    \item \textbf{Node 2 (+B):} $w=6, v=95$. $UB = 151$. Explore further.
    
    \item \textbf{Node 3 (+C):} $w=11 > 10$. \textbf{\color{red} Pruned (Infeasible)}.
    
    \item \textbf{Node 4 (-C):} $w=6, v=95$. Remaining capacity $4$. Next is D($w=4, v=40$), which fits perfectly. 
    $UB = 95 + 40 = 135$. Explore further.
    
    \item \textbf{Node 5 (+D):} $w=10, v=135$. We reached a valid full assignment. 
    \textbf{Update $MaxProfit = 135$}.
    
    \item \textbf{Node 6 (-D):} $w=6, v=95$. $UB = 95$. Since $95 \le 135$, \textbf{\color{orange} Pruned}.
    
    \item \textbf{Node 7 (-B from Node 1):} $w=3, v=50$. Remaining capacity $7$. We fill with C($w=5$), leaving $2$. We take $\frac{2}{4}$ of D($v=40$). 
    $UB = 50 + 70 + (\frac{2}{4} \times 40) = 140$. Since $140 > 135$, explore further.
    
    \item \textbf{Node 8 (+C):} $w=8, v=120$. Remaining capacity $2$. We take $\frac{2}{4}$ of D($v=40$). 
    $UB = 120 + 20 = 140$. Explore.
    
    \item \textbf{Node 9 (+D):} $w=12 > 10$. \textbf{\color{red} Pruned (Infeasible)}.
    
    \item \textbf{Node 10 (-D):} $w=8, v=120$. $UB = 120 \le 135$. \textbf{\color{orange} Pruned}.
    
    \item \textbf{Node 11 (-C from Node 7):} $w=3, v=50$. Remaining capacity $7$. Next is D($w=4$). 
    $UB = 50 + 40 = 90 \le 135$. \textbf{\color{orange} Pruned}.
    
    \item \textbf{Node 12 (-A from Root):} $w=0, v=0$. Fill with B($w=3$), C($w=5$), leaving $2$. Take $\frac{2}{4}$ of D. 
    $UB = 0 + 45 + 70 + (\frac{2}{4} \times 40) = 135$. Since $UB \le MaxProfit$, \textbf{\color{orange} Pruned}.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Branch and Bound State-Space Tree}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=7.5cm, level distance=1.8cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=3.8cm, level distance=1.8cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=2.1cm, level distance=1.8cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.6cm, level distance=1.8cm},
    node/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=white, inner sep=3pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    infeasible/.style={rectangle, draw=red!80, thick, fill=red!10, inner sep=3pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    pruned/.style={rectangle, draw=orange!80, thick, fill=orange!10, inner sep=3pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    optimal/.style={rectangle, draw=green!80!black, thick, fill=green!10, inner sep=3pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth},
    el/.style={font=\tiny\bfseries, draw=none, fill=white, inner sep=1.5pt}
]

\node[node] {\textbf{Node 0}\\$v=0, w=0$\\$UB=151$}
    child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 1}\\$v=50, w=3$\\$UB=151$}
        child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 2}\\$v=95, w=6$\\$UB=151$}
            child { node[infeasible] {\textbf{Node 3}\\$w=11 > 10$\\Infeasible}
                edge from parent node[el] {$+C$}
            }
            child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 4}\\$v=95, w=6$\\$UB=135$}
                child { node[optimal] {\textbf{Node 5}\\$v=135, w=10$\\$UB=135$\\\textbf{Optimum!}}
                    edge from parent node[el] {$+D$}
                }
                child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 6}\\$v=95, w=6$\\$UB=95$\\Pruned}
                    edge from parent node[el] {$-D$}
                }
                edge from parent node[el] {$-C$}
            }
            edge from parent node[el] {$+B$}
        }
        child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 7}\\$v=50, w=3$\\$UB=140$}
            child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 8}\\$v=120, w=8$\\$UB=140$}
                child { node[infeasible] {\textbf{Node 9}\\$w=12 > 10$\\Infeasible}
                    edge from parent node[el] {$+D$}
                }
                child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 10}\\$v=120, w=8$\\$UB=120$\\Pruned}
                    edge from parent node[el] {$-D$}
                }
                edge from parent node[el] {$+C$}
            }
            child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 11}\\$v=50, w=3$\\$UB=90$\\Pruned}
                edge from parent node[el] {$-C$}
            }
            edge from parent node[el] {$-B$}
        }
        edge from parent node[el] {$+A$}
    }
    child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 12}\\$v=0, w=0$\\$UB=135$\\Pruned}
        edge from parent node[el] {$-A$}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{5. Final Solution \& Complexity Analysis}

By successfully traversing the Branch-and-Bound tree, we find the maximum profit.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Optimal Selection:} Items A, B, and D.
    \item \textbf{Original Item Mapping:} Item 2, Item 4, and Item 1.
    \item \textbf{Maximum Value:} $135$
    \item \textbf{Total Weight:} $3 + 3 + 4 = 10 \le W$
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Complexity Analysis:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ worst case. Sorting takes $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$. Although the bounding function prunes large subtrees, preventing exponential exploration in many practical scenarios, the worst-case bound remains exponential if bounds are weak.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(n)$ to maintain the DFS recursion stack height and store the current assignments, making depth-first search much more space-efficient than breadth-first (BFS) branching strategies.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Explain branch-and-bound algorithms with an illustrative example.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2023--24}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Introduction to Branch and Bound}

\textbf{Branch and Bound (B\&B)} is an algorithmic paradigm used primarily for solving combinatorial optimization problems, typically those that are NP-Hard. It systematically enumerates candidate solutions by exploring branches of a state-space tree. 

To avoid exhaustive brute-force search, B\&B employs a \textit{bounding} function to calculate the best possible solution in a given subtree. If this bound is worse than the best solution found so far, the entire subtree is discarded (\textit{pruned}). The three core operations are:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Branching:} Splitting a problem instance into mutually exclusive subproblems (e.g., "include item $i$" vs. "exclude item $i$"), creating a state-space tree.
    \item \textbf{Bounding:} Calculating a guaranteed upper bound (for maximization) or lower bound (for minimization) on the objective function for any solution in a subproblem.
    \item \textbf{Pruning:} Discarding a subproblem if its bound indicates it cannot possibly yield a better solution than the current optimal candidate.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. General B\&B Framework (Maximization Problem)}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Best-First Branch and Bound}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Problem instance $P$
\Ensure The optimal solution value and configuration.
\State $MaxProfit \gets -\infty$
\State $PQ \gets \text{Priority Queue ordered by bound (descending)}$
\State $root \gets \text{initial state}$
\State $root.bound \gets \Call{ComputeBound}{root}$
\State \Call{Enqueue}{$PQ, root$}
\While{$PQ$ is not empty}
    \State $node \gets \Call{DequeueMax}{PQ}$
    \If{$node.bound \le MaxProfit$}
        \State \textbf{continue} \Comment{Prune node since it cannot improve the solution}
    \EndIf
    \For{\textbf{each} $child$ \textbf{of} $node$}
        \If{$child$ is a valid full solution}
            \State $MaxProfit \gets \max(MaxProfit, child.value)$
        \ElsIf{$child$ is feasible}
            \State $child.bound \gets \Call{ComputeBound}{child}$
            \If{$child.bound > MaxProfit$}
                \State \Call{Enqueue}{$PQ, child$}
            \EndIf
        \EndIf
    \EndFor
\EndWhile
\State \Return $MaxProfit$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Illustrative Example: 0-1 Knapsack Problem}

Consider a 0-1 Knapsack Problem with Capacity $W = 10$. Items are sorted by value-to-weight ratio in descending order:
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\rowcolor{cE!20}
\textbf{Item} & \textbf{Value ($v_i$)} & \textbf{Weight ($w_i$)} & \textbf{Ratio ($v_i/w_i$)} \\ \hline
1 & 40 & 4 & 10 \\ \hline
2 & 42 & 7 & 6 \\ \hline
3 & 25 & 5 & 5 \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\textbf{Bounding Function (Upper Bound - UB):}
To compute the UB for any node, we take the current profit, add the profit of remaining items that fit entirely, and finally add a fractional amount of the first item that does not fit.

\paragraph{Step-by-Step Execution:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Node 0 (Root):} Current $w=0, v=0$. Add Item 1 ($w=4, v=40$). Remaining cap $6$. Item 2 ($w=7$) does not fit. Take fraction $\frac{6}{7} \times 42 = 36$. $\mathbf{UB} = 40 + 36 = \mathbf{76}$.
    \item \textbf{Node 1 (Include Item 1):} $w=4, v=40$. $\mathbf{UB = 76}$.
    \item \textbf{Node 2 (Exclude Item 1):} $w=0, v=0$. Add Item 2 ($w=7, v=42$). Remaining cap $3$. Take fraction $\frac{3}{5} \times 25 = 15$. $\mathbf{UB} = 42 + 15 = \mathbf{57}$.
    \item \textbf{Node 3 (Include Item 1 \& 2):} $w = 4 + 7 = 11 > 10$. \textbf{Infeasible}.
    \item \textbf{Node 4 (Include Item 1, Exclude Item 2):} $w=4, v=40$. Add Item 3 entirely ($w=5$). $\mathbf{UB} = 40 + 25 = \mathbf{65}$.
    \item \textbf{Node 5 (Include Item 1, 3; Exclude 2):} $w=9 \le 10, v=65$. Valid leaf. Update $\mathbf{MaxProfit = 65}$.
    \item \textbf{Pruning Node 2:} We go back to unexplored Node 2. Its $UB = 57$. Since $57 \le MaxProfit (65)$, we \textbf{prune} the entire right half of the tree.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. State-Space Tree Diagram}

The diagram below maps the execution. Node 2 is pruned mathematically, proving we do not need to evaluate Item 2 and Item 3 combinations when Item 1 is excluded.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=6cm, level distance=2cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=4cm, level distance=2.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm, level distance=2cm},
    node/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=white, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\footnotesize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    infeasible/.style={rectangle, draw=red!80, thick, fill=red!10, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\footnotesize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    pruned/.style={rectangle, draw=orange!80, thick, fill=orange!10, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\footnotesize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    optimal/.style={rectangle, draw=green!80!black, thick, fill=green!10, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\footnotesize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth},
    el/.style={font=\scriptsize\bfseries, draw=none, fill=white, inner sep=1.5pt}
]

\node[node] {\textbf{Node 0 (Root)}\\$v=0, w=0$\\$UB=76$}
    child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 1}\\$v=40, w=4$\\$UB=76$}
        child { node[infeasible] {\textbf{Node 3}\\$w=11 > 10$\\\textbf{Infeasible}}
            edge from parent node[el] {Include 2}
        }
        child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 4}\\$v=40, w=4$\\$UB=65$}
            child { node[optimal] {\textbf{Node 5}\\$v=65, w=9$\\$UB=65$\\\textbf{New Max: 65}}
                edge from parent node[el] {Include 3}
            }
            child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 6}\\$v=40, w=4$\\$UB=40$\\\textbf{Pruned}}
                edge from parent node[el] {Exclude 3}
            }
            edge from parent node[el] {Exclude 2}
        }
        edge from parent node[el] {Include 1}
    }
    child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 2}\\$v=0, w=0$\\$UB=57$\\\textbf{Pruned} ($57 \le 65$)}
        edge from parent node[el] {Exclude 1}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{5. Complexity Analysis}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ in the worst case (where $n$ is the number of items/variables). Although B\&B significantly prunes the search space reducing the average-case runtime drastically, pathological instances exist where bounds are extremely weak, forcing the algorithm to explore almost the entire exponential tree.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ in the worst case for Best-First Search because the Priority Queue must store all unexpanded nodes across the breadth of the tree. If Depth-First Branch and Bound is used, the space complexity drops to $\mathcal{O}(n)$, which is the maximum depth of the recursion stack.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2024-25
\item You want to fill up your luggage with required items for an upcoming tour. The luggage has a capacity of 60~kg. The items have the following values and weights:
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Item} & \textbf{Value} & \textbf{Weight (kg)} \\
\hline
1 & 120 & 15 \\
2 & 110 & 10 \\
3 & 95  & 25 \\
4 & 135 & 35 \\
5 & 80  & 20 \\
6 & 150 & 30 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
Solve this 0-1 knapsack problem using the branch and bound technique to maximize total value.
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Preprocessing: Item Sorting}

In the Branch and Bound technique for the 0-1 Knapsack problem, we first compute the value-to-weight ratio ($v_i/w_i$) for each item and sort them in \textbf{descending order}. This ordering allows the algorithm to greedily compute the tightest possible theoretical upper bound at each node.

Given the knapsack capacity $W = 60$, the sorted items are:

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\rowcolor{cE!20}
\textbf{Sorted Label} & \textbf{Original Item} & \textbf{Value ($v_i$)} & \textbf{Weight ($w_i$)} & \textbf{Ratio ($v_i/w_i$)} \\
\hline
\textbf{A} & 2 & 110 & 10 & 11.00 \\
\textbf{B} & 1 & 120 & 15 & 8.00 \\
\textbf{C} & 6 & 150 & 30 & 5.00 \\
\textbf{D} & 5 & 80 & 20 & 4.00 \\
\textbf{E} & 4 & 135 & 35 & 3.86 \\
\textbf{F} & 3 & 95 & 25 & 3.80 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Bounding Function}

At any node in the state-space tree, the \textbf{Upper Bound (UB)} is calculated by taking the accumulated value $v$ of the already included items, adding the full values of the remaining sorted items that fit, and then adding a \textit{fraction} of the first item that does not fit.

\[
UB = v + \sum_{\text{fitting items}} v_i + \left( \frac{\text{remaining capacity}}{\text{weight of first item that doesn't fit}} \right) \times \text{value of that item}
\]

We traverse the state-space tree using \textbf{Depth-First Search (DFS)}. A node is \textbf{pruned} if:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Its total weight exceeds the capacity $W = 60$ (\textit{Infeasible}).
    \item Its $UB \le MaxProfit$ (the best valid integer solution found so far).
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Step-by-Step Execution (DFS Trace)}

We initialize $MaxProfit = 0$ and explore by deciding whether to include ($+$) or exclude ($-$) each item in order.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Node 0 (Root):} $w=0, v=0$. Fill greedily with A($w=10$), B($w=15$), C($w=30$). Total $w=55, v=380$. Remaining capacity $= 5$. Take $\frac{5}{20}$ of D($v=80$). \\
    $UB = 380 + (\frac{5}{20} \times 80) = 400$.
    
    \item \textbf{Include A (+A):} $w=10, v=110$. $UB = 400$. (Proceed to B)
    \item \textbf{Include B (+B):} $w=25, v=230$. $UB = 400$. (Proceed to C)
    \item \textbf{Include C (+C):} $w=55, v=380$. Remaining cap 5. Next item D is 20kg. $UB = 380 + 20 = 400$.
    
    \item \textbf{Include D (+D):} $w = 55 + 20 = 75 > 60$. \textbf{\color{red} Pruned (Infeasible)}.
    \item \textbf{Exclude D (-D):} $w=55, v=380$. Next E($w=35$). $UB = 380 + (\frac{5}{35} \times 135) = 399.28$.
    
    \item \textbf{Include E (+E):} $w = 55 + 35 = 90 > 60$. \textbf{\color{red} Pruned (Infeasible)}.
    \item \textbf{Exclude E (-E):} $w=55, v=380$. Next F($w=25$). $UB = 380 + (\frac{5}{25} \times 95) = 399$.
    
    \item \textbf{Include F (+F):} $w = 55 + 25 = 80 > 60$. \textbf{\color{red} Pruned (Infeasible)}.
    \item \textbf{Exclude F (-F):} $w=55, v=380$. All items considered. This is a valid leaf! \\
    \textbf{Update $MaxProfit = 380$}.
    
    \item \textbf{Backtrack to (-C):} We excluded C. Current $w=25, v=230$. Greedily fill with D($w=20$), remaining cap $15$. Take fraction of E($w=35$). $UB = 230 + 80 + (\frac{15}{35} \times 135) = 367.85$. \\
    Since $UB = 367.85 \le 380$, \textbf{\color{orange} Pruned}.
    
    \item \textbf{Backtrack to (-B):} Excluded B. Current $w=10, v=110$. Fill with C($w=30$), D($w=20$). Capacity perfectly full. $UB = 110 + 150 + 80 = 340$. \\
    Since $UB = 340 \le 380$, \textbf{\color{orange} Pruned}.
    
    \item \textbf{Backtrack to (-A):} Excluded A. Current $w=0, v=0$. Fill with B($w=15$), C($w=30$). Remaining cap $15$. Take fraction of D($w=20$). $UB = 120 + 150 + (\frac{15}{20} \times 80) = 330$. \\
    Since $UB = 330 \le 380$, \textbf{\color{orange} Pruned}.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. State-Space Tree Diagram}

The tree demonstrates the pruning power of the Branch and Bound algorithm. Once the optimum ($380$) is found deep in the left branch, large right-hand subtrees are discarded without exploration.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.6cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=6.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=3.8cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=3.2cm},
    level 5/.style={sibling distance=2.8cm},
    level 6/.style={sibling distance=2.4cm},
    node/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=white, inner sep=3pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    infeasible/.style={rectangle, draw=red!80, thick, fill=red!10, inner sep=3pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    pruned/.style={rectangle, draw=orange!80, thick, fill=orange!10, inner sep=3pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    optimal/.style={rectangle, draw=green!80!black, thick, fill=green!10, inner sep=3pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth},
    el/.style={font=\tiny\bfseries, draw=none, fill=white, inner sep=1.5pt}
]

\node[node] {\textbf{Root}\\$v=0, w=0$\\UB=400}
    child { node[node] {\textbf{+A}\\$v=110, w=10$\\UB=400}
        child { node[node] {\textbf{+B}\\$v=230, w=25$\\UB=400}
            child { node[node] {\textbf{+C}\\$v=380, w=55$\\UB=400}
                child { node[infeasible] {\textbf{+D}\\$w=75>60$\\\textbf{Inf}}
                    edge from parent node[el] {Inc D}
                }
                child { node[node] {\textbf{-D}\\$v=380, w=55$\\UB=399.3}
                    child { node[infeasible] {\textbf{+E}\\$w=90>60$\\\textbf{Inf}}
                        edge from parent node[el] {Inc E}
                    }
                    child { node[node] {\textbf{-E}\\$v=380, w=55$\\UB=399}
                        child { node[infeasible] {\textbf{+F}\\$w=80>60$\\\textbf{Inf}}
                            edge from parent node[el] {Inc F}
                        }
                        child { node[optimal] {\textbf{-F}\\$v=380, w=55$\\\textbf{Optimum!}}
                            edge from parent node[el] {Exc F}
                        }
                        edge from parent node[el] {Exc E}
                    }
                    edge from parent node[el] {Exc D}
                }
                edge from parent node[el] {Inc C}
            }
            child { node[pruned] {\textbf{-C}\\$v=230, w=25$\\UB=367.8\\\textbf{Pruned}}
                edge from parent node[el] {Exc C}
            }
            edge from parent node[el] {Inc B}
        }
        child { node[pruned] {\textbf{-B}\\$v=110, w=10$\\UB=340\\\textbf{Pruned}}
            edge from parent node[el] {Exc B}
        }
        edge from parent node[el] {Inc A}
    }
    child { node[pruned] {\textbf{-A}\\$v=0, w=0$\\UB=330\\\textbf{Pruned}}
        edge from parent node[el] {Exc A}
    };

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{5. Final Solution \& Complexity Analysis}

\textbf{Optimal Selection:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Included Items: \textbf{A, B, C} $\implies$ Original \textbf{Item 2, Item 1, Item 6}.
    \item Total Weight: $10 + 15 + 30 = \textbf{55 kg} \le 60 \text{ kg}$.
    \item Maximum Value: $110 + 120 + 150 = \textbf{380}$.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Complexity Analysis:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ worst case. Sorting takes $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$. While Branch and Bound extensively prunes unpromising configurations (making it computationally feasible for moderate $n$), the theoretical upper bound of expanding the entire tree remains $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ in pathological cases.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(n)$ using Depth-First Branch and Bound, as we only need to store the active path recursion stack and the best solution found so far. (If Best-First Search were used instead, space complexity would be exponential due to the priority queue storing frontier nodes).
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2022-23
\item There are 4 places in your village. You want to start from a place, then visit all other places and return to the first place without visiting the same place twice (other than the starting place), minimizing cost. The costs are given below:
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{From/To} & \textbf{1} & \textbf{2} & \textbf{3} & \textbf{4} \\
\hline
1 & 0  & 10 & 15 & 20 \\
2 & 5  & 0  & 12 & 18 \\
3 & 8  & 9  & 0  & 17 \\
4 & 11 & 14 & 16 & 0  \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
Give an exponential time algorithm to solve this problem with running time $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$, and find the optimal tour using your algorithm.
\mk{20} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-1 | 2022--23}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cA!4,colframe=cA,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cA]

\subsubsection*{1. Problem Identification and Dynamic Programming Approach}

The problem described is the \textbf{Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP)} on a directed graph. A brute-force search would examine all $(n-1)!$ permutations of the vertices, which takes $\mathcal{O}(n!)$ time. To solve it in $\mathcal{O}(n^2 2^n)$ time, we use the \textbf{Bellman-Held-Karp algorithm}, a dynamic programming (DP) technique.

\paragraph{State Definition:}
Let $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ be the set of places, with node $1$ as the starting point.
Let $S$ be a subset of vertices excluding the start node $1$ ($S \subseteq \{2, 3, 4\}$), and let $j \in S$.
We define $C(S, j)$ as the minimum cost of a path starting at node $1$, visiting every node in the subset $S$ exactly once, and ending at node $j$.

\paragraph{Recurrence Relation:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Base Case:} When $S$ contains only a single node $j$ ($S = \{j\}$), the cost is simply the direct edge from node $1$ to node $j$.
    \[ C(\{j\}, j) = d_{1,j} \]
    \item \textbf{Recursive Step:} To find the shortest path visiting all nodes in $S$ and ending at $j$, we consider all possible penultimate nodes $i \in S$ (where $i \neq j$). 
    \[ C(S, j) = \min_{i \in S, i \neq j} \left[ C(S \setminus \{j\}, i) + d_{i,j} \right] \]
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Final Answer:}
Once $C(V \setminus \{1\}, j)$ is computed for all $j \in \{2, 3, 4\}$, the cost of the optimal tour is:
\[ \text{Optimal Cost} = \min_{j \in \{2,3,4\}} \left[ C(\{2,3,4\}, j) + d_{j,1} \right] \]

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. The Held-Karp Algorithm}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Held-Karp Algorithm for TSP}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\Require Distance matrix $d$ of size $n \times n$
\Ensure The cost of the optimal TSP tour
\State Initialize $C(\{j\}, j) = d_{1,j}$ for all $j \in \{2, \dots, n\}$
\For{subset size $s = 2$ \textbf{to} $n-1$}
    \For{\textbf{each} subset $S \subseteq \{2, \dots, n\}$ of size $s$}
        \For{\textbf{each} $j \in S$}
            \State $C(S, j) \gets \min_{i \in S, i \neq j} \left[ C(S \setminus \{j\}, i) + d_{i,j} \right]$
        \EndFor
    \EndFor
\EndFor
\State $MinCost \gets \infty$
\For{\textbf{each} $j \in \{2, \dots, n\}$}
    \State $MinCost \gets \min(MinCost, C(\{2, \dots, n\}, j) + d_{j,1})$
\EndFor
\State \Return $MinCost$
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Step-by-Step Execution}

We apply the DP approach to our given distance matrix.

\paragraph{Step 3.1: Subsets of Size 1 ($|S| = 1$)}
\begin{align*}
    C(\{2\}, 2) &= d_{1,2} = 10 \\
    C(\{3\}, 3) &= d_{1,3} = 15 \\
    C(\{4\}, 4) &= d_{1,4} = 20
\end{align*}

\paragraph{Step 3.2: Subsets of Size 2 ($|S| = 2$)}
For $S = \{2, 3\}$:
\begin{align*}
    C(\{2,3\}, 2) &= C(\{3\}, 3) + d_{3,2} = 15 + 9 = 24 \quad \text{(Path: 1 $\to$ 3 $\to$ 2)} \\
    C(\{2,3\}, 3) &= C(\{2\}, 2) + d_{2,3} = 10 + 12 = 22 \quad \text{(Path: 1 $\to$ 2 $\to$ 3)}
\end{align*}

For $S = \{2, 4\}$:
\begin{align*}
    C(\{2,4\}, 2) &= C(\{4\}, 4) + d_{4,2} = 20 + 14 = 34 \quad \text{(Path: 1 $\to$ 4 $\to$ 2)} \\
    C(\{2,4\}, 4) &= C(\{2\}, 2) + d_{2,4} = 10 + 18 = 28 \quad \text{(Path: 1 $\to$ 2 $\to$ 4)}
\end{align*}

For $S = \{3, 4\}$:
\begin{align*}
    C(\{3,4\}, 3) &= C(\{4\}, 4) + d_{4,3} = 20 + 16 = 36 \quad \text{(Path: 1 $\to$ 4 $\to$ 3)} \\
    C(\{3,4\}, 4) &= C(\{3\}, 3) + d_{3,4} = 15 + 17 = 32 \quad \text{(Path: 1 $\to$ 3 $\to$ 4)}
\end{align*}

\paragraph{Step 3.3: Subset of Size 3 ($|S| = 3$, $S = \{2, 3, 4\}$)}
We evaluate the minimum cost to reach each node $j \in \{2,3,4\}$ after visiting all others.
\begin{align*}
    C(\{2,3,4\}, 2) &= \min \begin{cases} 
        C(\{3,4\}, 3) + d_{3,2} = 36 + 9 = 45 \\ 
        C(\{3,4\}, 4) + d_{4,2} = 32 + 14 = 46 
    \end{cases} = \mathbf{45} \quad \text{(via node 3)} \\[6pt]
    C(\{2,3,4\}, 3) &= \min \begin{cases} 
        C(\{2,4\}, 2) + d_{2,3} = 34 + 12 = 46 \\ 
        C(\{2,4\}, 4) + d_{4,3} = 28 + 16 = 44 
    \end{cases} = \mathbf{44} \quad \text{(via node 4)} \\[6pt]
    C(\{2,3,4\}, 4) &= \min \begin{cases} 
        C(\{2,3\}, 2) + d_{2,4} = 24 + 18 = 42 \\ 
        C(\{2,3\}, 3) + d_{3,4} = 22 + 17 = \mathbf{39}
    \end{cases} = \mathbf{39} \quad \text{(via node 3)}
\end{align*}

\paragraph{Step 3.4: Final Cost to Return to Node 1}
We add the return edge $d_{j,1}$ from the last visited node back to the start:
\begin{align*}
    \text{Return from 2: } & C(\{2,3,4\}, 2) + d_{2,1} = 45 + 5 = 50 \\
    \text{Return from 3: } & C(\{2,3,4\}, 3) + d_{3,1} = 44 + 8 = 52 \\
    \text{Return from 4: } & C(\{2,3,4\}, 4) + d_{4,1} = 39 + 11 = 50 
\end{align*}
The optimal tour cost is the minimum of these values, which is \textbf{50}. 

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Constructing the Optimal Tour}
Tracing back the parent pointers that yielded the minimum values:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Path ending in 4: Optimal cost $50$ comes from returning from node 4. \\
    $4 \leftarrow 3 \leftarrow 2 \leftarrow 1$. Full Tour: $\mathbf{1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1}$.
    \item Path ending in 2: Optimal cost $50$ comes from returning from node 2. \\
    $2 \leftarrow 3 \leftarrow 4 \leftarrow 1$. Full Tour: $\mathbf{1 \to 4 \to 3 \to 2 \to 1}$.
\end{itemize}
Both paths yield the minimum cost of 50. Let's visualize one of the optimal tours ($\mathbf{1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1}$).

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    node/.style={circle, draw=cA, very thick, fill=cA!10, minimum size=0.8cm, font=\bfseries},
    edge/.style={->, >=stealth, thick, color=gray},
    optedge/.style={->, >=stealth, line width=1.5pt, color=cA},
    label/.style={font=\scriptsize, fill=white, inner sep=1pt}
]

% Nodes
\node[node] (1) at (0, 2) {1};
\node[node] (2) at (3, 2) {2};
\node[node] (3) at (3, -1) {3};
\node[node] (4) at (0, -1) {4};

% Cross edges
\draw[edge] (1) -- (3) node[midway, label] {15};
\draw[edge] (3) -- (1) node[near start, label] {8};
\draw[edge] (2) -- (4) node[midway, label] {18};
\draw[edge] (4) -- (2) node[near start, label] {14};

% Cycle 1->4->3->2->1 (gray edges)
\draw[edge] (1) -- (4) node[midway, left, label] {20};
\draw[edge] (4) -- (3) node[midway, below, label] {16};
\draw[edge] (3) -- (2) node[midway, right, label] {9};
\draw[edge] (2) -- (1) node[midway, above, label] {5};

% Cycle 1->2->3->4->1 (Optimal Path highlighted in color)
\draw[optedge] (1) to[bend left=15] node[midway, above, label] {\textbf{10}} (2);
\draw[optedge] (2) to[bend left=15] node[midway, right, label] {\textbf{12}} (3);
\draw[optedge] (3) to[bend left=15] node[midway, below, label] {\textbf{17}} (4);
\draw[optedge] (4) to[bend left=15] node[midway, left, label] {\textbf{11}} (1);

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{5. Complexity Analysis}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(n^2 2^n)$. There are $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ subproblems (the number of subsets $S \subseteq V \setminus \{1\}$). For each subset, there are at most $n$ choices for the destination node $j$, and to calculate the state $C(S, j)$, we take the minimum over at most $n$ intermediate nodes $i$. Thus, computing all table entries takes $2^n \times n \times n = \mathcal{O}(n^2 2^n)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(n 2^n)$. The DP table must store a value for every combination of subset $S$ and ending node $j$. The number of combinations is roughly $n \cdot 2^{n-1}$.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2023-24
\item There are 6 places in your village. You want to visit all the places and return to 
    the first place that you will visit. Also, you don't want to visit the same place twice 
    other than the starting place. You want to finish the visits with minimum cost. The 
    cost of the roads between these places are given below:
    
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
    \hline
    \textbf{From/To} & \textbf{1} & \textbf{2} & \textbf{3} & \textbf{4} & \textbf{5} & \textbf{6} \\ \hline
    \textbf{1}       & 0          & 10         & 15         & 20         & 25         & 30         \\ \hline
    \textbf{2}       & 5          & 0          & 12         & 18         & 24         & 29         \\ \hline
    \textbf{3}       & 8          & 9          & 0          & 17         & 22         & 28         \\ \hline
    \textbf{4}       & 11         & 14         & 16         & 0          & 21         & 27         \\ \hline
    \textbf{5}       & 13         & 19         & 23         & 26         & 0          & 31         \\ \hline
    \textbf{6}       & 28         & 27         & 25         & 24         & 22         & 0          \\ \hline
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}

Find the optimal tour using branch and bound, and pruning techniques combined.
\mk{20} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2021--22}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Problem Setup and Initial Matrix Reduction}

We are given an Asymmetric Traveling Salesperson Problem (ATSP) with 6 nodes. To apply the Branch and Bound technique, we first represent the distances in a cost matrix $C$. Since we cannot travel from a place to itself, we set all diagonal elements to $\infty$:
\[
C = \begin{pmatrix}
\infty & 10 & 15 & 20 & 25 & 30 \\
5 & \infty & 12 & 18 & 24 & 29 \\
8 & 9 & \infty & 17 & 22 & 28 \\
11 & 14 & 16 & \infty & 21 & 27 \\
13 & 19 & 23 & 26 & \infty & 31 \\
28 & 27 & 25 & 24 & 22 & \infty
\end{pmatrix}
\]

To find the lower bound (LB) for the root node, we perform matrix reduction:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Row Reduction:} Subtract the minimum value of each row from all elements in that row.
    \item \textbf{Column Reduction:} Subtract the minimum value of each column from all elements in that column.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Step 1: Row Reduction}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{ll}
Row 1 minimum: 10 & $\to ( \infty, 0, 5, 10, 15, 20 )$ \\
Row 2 minimum: 5 & $\to ( 0, \infty, 7, 13, 19, 24 )$ \\
Row 3 minimum: 8 & $\to ( 0, 1, \infty, 9, 14, 20 )$ \\
Row 4 minimum: 11 & $\to ( 0, 3, 5, \infty, 10, 16 )$ \\
Row 5 minimum: 13 & $\to ( 0, 6, 10, 13, \infty, 18 )$ \\
Row 6 minimum: 22 & $\to ( 6, 5, 3, 2, 0, \infty )$ \\
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\textit{Row Reduction Sum} $= 10 + 5 + 8 + 11 + 13 + 22 = 69$.

\textbf{Step 2: Column Reduction} (on the row-reduced matrix)
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ll}
Column 1 minimum: 0 & Column 4 minimum: 2 (from Row 6) \\
Column 2 minimum: 0 & Column 5 minimum: 0 \\
Column 3 minimum: 3 (from Row 6) & Column 6 minimum: 16 (from Row 4) \\
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\textit{Column Reduction Sum} $= 0 + 0 + 3 + 2 + 0 + 16 = 21$.

The **Root Lower Bound** is $69 + 21 = \mathbf{90}$. The fully reduced matrix $M_0$ becomes:
\[
M_0 = \begin{pmatrix}
\infty & 0 & 2 & 8 & 15 & 4 \\
0 & \infty & 4 & 11 & 19 & 8 \\
0 & 1 & \infty & 7 & 14 & 4 \\
0 & 3 & 2 & \infty & 10 & 0 \\
0 & 6 & 7 & 11 & \infty & 2 \\
6 & 5 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \infty
\end{pmatrix}
\]

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Branch and Bound Execution (Best-First Search)}

We maintain a Priority Queue of active states, always expanding the node with the lowest LB. 

\paragraph{Level 1: Branching from Node 1}
For a path $1 \to j$, the new lower bound is calculated as:
\[ LB_{new} = LB_{old} + M_{old}[1, j] + \text{Cost of reducing the new matrix} \]
We form the new matrix by setting Row 1 and Column $j$ to $\infty$ (since we leave 1 and arrive at $j$), and setting $M[j, 1] = \infty$ to prevent premature cycling.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path $1 \to 2$:} $M_0[1, 2] = 0$. After crossing out R1, C2, and setting $M[2,1]=\infty$, the new matrix requires reducing R2 by $4$. $LB = 90 + 0 + 4 = \mathbf{94}$.
    \item \textbf{Path $1 \to 3$:} $M_0[1, 3] = 2$. Requires reducing R3 by $1$. $LB = 90 + 2 + 1 = \mathbf{93}$.
    \item \textbf{Path $1 \to 4$:} $M_0[1, 4] = 8$. Matrix is already fully reduced. $LB = 90 + 8 + 0 = \mathbf{98}$.
    \item \textbf{Path $1 \to 6$:} $M_0[1, 6] = 4$. Matrix is already fully reduced. $LB = 90 + 4 + 0 = \mathbf{94}$.
\end{itemize}
\textit{Frontier:} $1 \to 3$ (93) is explored first, generating children with $LB \ge 99$. We then explore $1 \to 2$ (94).

\paragraph{Tracing the Optimal Path ($1 \to 2 \to 3 \to \dots$)}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Path $1 \to 2 \to 3$:} From matrix $M_{1,2}$, $M_{1,2}[2, 3] = 0$. Crossing out R2, C3 and setting $M[3,1]=\infty$ requires reducing R3 by $4$. \\
    $LB = 94 + 0 + 4 = \mathbf{98}$.
    
    \item \textbf{Path $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4$:} From matrix $M_{1,2,3}$, $M_{1,2,3}[3, 4] = 3$. Crossing out R3, C4 and setting $M[4,1]=\infty$ yields a fully reduced matrix. \\
    $LB = 98 + 3 + 0 = \mathbf{101}$.
    
    \item \textbf{Path $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 6$:} From matrix $M_{1,2,3,4}$, $M[4, 6] = 0$. Crossing out R4, C6 yields a fully reduced matrix. \\
    $LB = 101 + 0 + 0 = \mathbf{101}$.
    
    \item \textbf{Path $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 6 \to 5 \to 1$:} The final forced edge to the last remaining node (5) and back to the start has an incremental reduced cost of $0$. \\
    $LB = 101 + 0 + 0 = \mathbf{101}$.
\end{itemize}
Since $101$ is a valid complete tour and is $\le$ the $LB$ of all other unexplored branches in the frontier, we safely **prune** the rest of the tree. The optimal tour is found.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. State-Space Tree Diagram}

The diagram visually depicts how the state space is pruned, naturally guiding the algorithm towards the exact optimal path via bounding calculations.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=1.6cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm},
    node/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=white, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\footnotesize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    opt/.style={rectangle, draw=green!80!black, very thick, fill=green!10, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\footnotesize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth},
    el/.style={font=\scriptsize\bfseries, draw=none, fill=white, inner sep=1.5pt}
]

\node[node] {\textbf{Root}\\$LB=90$}
    child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 2}\\$LB=94$}
        child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 3}\\$LB=98$}
            child { node[opt] {\textbf{Node 4}\\$LB=101$}
                child { node[opt] {\textbf{Node 6}\\$LB=101$}
                    child { node[opt] {\textbf{Node 5}\\Tour=101}
                        edge from parent node[el] {go 5}
                    }
                    edge from parent node[el] {go 6}
                }
                edge from parent node[el] {go 4}
            }
            child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 6}\\$LB=98$}
                edge from parent node[el] {go 6}
            }
            edge from parent node[el] {go 3}
        }
        edge from parent node[el] {go 2}
    }
    child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 3}\\$LB=93$} 
        edge from parent node[el] {go 3}
    }
    child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 6}\\$LB=94$} 
        edge from parent node[el] {go 6}
    }
    child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 4}\\$LB=98$} 
        edge from parent node[el] {go 4}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Final Answer \& Complexity}

The exact route traced by the lowest bounds yields the optimal solution:
\[
\textbf{Optimal Tour: } 1 \xrightarrow{\;10\;} 2 \xrightarrow{\;12\;} 3 \xrightarrow{\;17\;} 4 \xrightarrow{\;27\;} 6 \xrightarrow{\;22\;} 5 \xrightarrow{\;13\;} 1
\]
\[
\textbf{Minimum Cost} = 10 + 12 + 17 + 27 + 22 + 13 = \mathbf{101}
\]

\paragraph{Complexity Analysis:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(n!)$ in the worst case (pathological instances). However, with effective bounding and pruning, the average time complexity drops dramatically, allowing algorithms to process real-world TSP sizes efficiently. Computing the lower bound at each node takes $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(n! \cdot n^2)$ theoretical worst-case for the Priority Queue holding $n \times n$ matrices across frontier nodes (Best-First strategy). Practical space complexity is drastically smaller due to heavy pruning of unpromising subtrees.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2022-23
\item In one approach to solve the Traveling Salesman Problem using branch-and-bound technique, the lower bound of the cost of completing a partial tour has been calculated from the cost of the minimum spanning tree spanning the remaining nodes of the graph. Decide whether this bound function will give correct answer all the time, with proper explanation. If there is any better bound function for this problem, mention it.
\mk{5} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2020--21}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Correctness of the MST Lower Bound}

\textbf{Decision:} Yes, this bound function will give the correct optimal answer all the time. 

\textbf{Explanation:} 
In the Branch-and-Bound (B\&B) algorithm, a heuristic or bounding function guarantees finding the correct optimal solution if and only if it is \textbf{admissible}. A bounding function is admissible if it \textbf{never overestimates} the true minimum cost to complete the solution. This ensures that the algorithm never accidentally prunes a branch that could lead to the optimal solution.

Let us formally prove that the cost of the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) on the remaining nodes is a valid lower bound for completing the TSP tour:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Let $V$ be the set of all vertices in the graph.
    \item Let $P$ be the sequence of nodes in the current partial tour, starting at node $S$ and ending at node $E$.
    \item Let $R = V \setminus P$ be the set of unvisited (remaining) nodes.
    \item To complete the tour, the optimal solution must construct a path that leaves $E$, visits every node in $R$ exactly once, and returns to $S$.
    \item Let this true optimal completion path through $R$ be denoted as $C_{R}$. Because $C_{R}$ is a simple path connecting all vertices in $R$, it is by definition a \textbf{spanning tree} of $R$ (specifically, a spanning tree with a maximum degree of 2).
    \item By definition, the Minimum Spanning Tree is the spanning tree with the absolute minimum possible weight over the set $R$. Therefore, the cost of the MST on $R$ will always be less than or equal to the cost of any path passing through $R$:
    \[ \text{Cost}(\text{MST}(R)) \leq \text{Cost}(C_{R}) \]
\end{itemize}

Because the MST cost is guaranteed to be $\leq$ the actual cost of visiting the remaining nodes, the lower bound is admissible. Thus, the B\&B algorithm will always yield the mathematically correct optimal tour.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Visualizing the Bound Admissibility}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    node/.style={circle, draw=cE, very thick, fill=white, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\footnotesize\bfseries},
    pnode/.style={circle, draw=black, thick, fill=gray!20, minimum size=0.6cm, font=\footnotesize\bfseries},
    edge/.style={-, thick},
    tour/.style={->, >=stealth, very thick, color=red},
    mst/.style={-, very thick, color=blue, dashed},
    label/.style={font=\scriptsize, fill=white, inner sep=1pt}
]

% Partial Tour Nodes
\node[pnode] (S) at (0, 2) {S};
\node[pnode] (P1) at (0, 0) {$P_1$};
\node[pnode] (E) at (2, -1) {E};

% Remaining Nodes (R)
\node[node] (R1) at (5, 2) {$R_1$};
\node[node] (R2) at (7, 1) {$R_2$};
\node[node] (R3) at (5, 0) {$R_3$};
\node[node] (R4) at (4, -1.5) {$R_4$};

% Partial Tour Edges
\draw[tour] (S) -- (P1);
\draw[tour] (P1) -- (E);

% MST Edges
\draw[mst] (R1) -- (R3);
\draw[mst] (R3) -- (R2);
\draw[mst] (R3) -- (R4);

% Completion Path (hypothetical)
\draw[->, >=stealth, thick, dotted, color=gray!80] (E) to[bend right=15] (R4);
\draw[->, >=stealth, thick, dotted, color=gray!80] (R4) to[bend right=15] (R2);
\draw[->, >=stealth, thick, dotted, color=gray!80] (R2) to[bend right=15] (R1);
\draw[->, >=stealth, thick, dotted, color=gray!80] (R1) to[bend left=15] (R3);
\draw[->, >=stealth, thick, dotted, color=gray!80] (R3) to[bend right=15] (S);

% Labels
\node[align=center, font=\footnotesize] at (0.5, 3) {\textbf{Partial Tour ($P$)}};
\node[align=center, font=\footnotesize, color=blue] at (5.5, 3) {\textbf{Remaining Nodes ($R$)}\\ MST shown in dashed blue};

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\textit{Figure 1: The hypothetical completion path (gray dotted) must connect all nodes in $R$. The MST of $R$ (blue dashed) connects the same nodes but without the constraints of being a path, naturally resulting in a lower or equal total weight.}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Better Bounding Functions}

While the MST of $R$ is valid, it is a \textbf{weak (loose)} lower bound because it entirely ignores the cost of the edges required to connect the remaining nodes $R$ back to the partial tour ends $E$ and $S$. B\&B efficiency heavily relies on having the tightest possible bounds to prune the search space early. 

Better bounding functions include:

\paragraph{A. The Augmented MST Bound}
A strict improvement over the simple MST bound is adding the minimum edges bridging the partial tour to the remaining nodes.
\[ LB = \text{Cost}(\text{Partial Tour}) + \text{Cost}(\text{MST}(R)) + \min_{u \in R} c(E, u) + \min_{v \in R} c(v, S) \]
This forces the bound to account for entering the set $R$ from $E$ and exiting $R$ back to $S$, remaining strictly admissible while providing a noticeably tighter estimate.

\paragraph{B. The 1-Tree Bound (Held-Karp Bound)}
For Symmetric TSP, the most widely taught optimal bound is the 1-tree bound. A 1-tree on a graph with node $1$ is defined as a spanning tree on the vertices $V \setminus \{1\}$, plus exactly two edges incident to node $1$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item Every valid TSP tour is a 1-tree (specifically, a 1-tree where every node has degree 2).
    \item Therefore, the minimum weight 1-tree provides a very tight lower bound for the TSP.
    \item Combined with Lagrangian relaxation (iteratively adjusting edge weights to penalize nodes with degree $\neq 2$), the Held-Karp bound often equals the exact optimal TSP cost, completely pruning the search tree.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{C. Matrix Reduction / Assignment Problem Bound}
For Asymmetric TSP (where $c(u,v) \neq c(v,u)$), an MST is undefined or problematic since paths are directed. Instead, the standard bound is based on \textbf{Matrix Reduction}.
\begin{itemize}
    \item Subtract the minimum from each row and column. The sum of these reductions yields a lower bound.
    \item Equivalently, the problem can be relaxed to the \textbf{Assignment Problem} (AP), solved efficiently in $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ using the Hungarian Algorithm. The AP finds a set of disjoint cycles covering all nodes; its cost is a rigorous and very tight lower bound for the ATSP.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

%% Q — 2021-22
\item Solve the following instance of a 0-1 knapsack problem by a branch-and-bound algorithm using a state-space tree. You must show the branch-and-bound tree.\\[4pt]
Knapsack capacity $= 10$.
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Item} & \textbf{Weight} & \textbf{Value} \\
\hline
1 & 2 & 10 \\
2 & 3 & 9  \\
3 & 2 & 20 \\
4 & 5 & 30 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\mk{15} \yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2019--20}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Pre-processing: Sorting by Value-to-Weight Ratio}
To apply the Branch-and-Bound strategy effectively, we first calculate the value-to-weight ratio ($v_i / w_i$) for each item and sort them in \textbf{descending order}. This allows our bounding function (which relies on the fractional knapsack greedy choice) to be as tight as possible.

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Original Item} & \textbf{Sorted Index} & \textbf{Weight ($w_i$)} & \textbf{Value ($v_i$)} & \textbf{Ratio ($v_i/w_i$)} \\
\hline
3 & $I_1$ & 2 & 20 & 10 \\
4 & $I_2$ & 5 & 30 & 6 \\
1 & $I_3$ & 2 & 10 & 5 \\
2 & $I_4$ & 3 & 9  & 3 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
The knapsack capacity is $W = 10$. We process the items in the order: $I_1 \to I_2 \to I_3 \to I_4$.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. The Bounding Function}
For a maximization problem, we need an \textbf{Upper Bound (UB)} to prune suboptimal branches. At any node representing a partial solution, the upper bound is calculated by taking the current value, adding the values of remaining items that fit entirely, and adding a \textit{fraction} of the first item that does not fit (Fractional Knapsack logic). 
Let $max\_value$ represent the maximum integer profit found so far. We prune a node if its $UB \le max\_value$.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Branch-and-Bound Execution (Step-by-Step)}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Node 0 (Root):} Level 0. Current $w=0$, $v=0$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Add $I_1$ ($w=2, v=20$): capacity remaining = 8.
        \item Add $I_2$ ($w=5, v=30$): capacity remaining = 3.
        \item Add $I_3$ ($w=2, v=10$): capacity remaining = 1.
        \item Add fraction of $I_4$: $1 \times (9/3) = 3$.
        \item $UB_0 = 20 + 30 + 10 + 3 = 63$. $\quad \text{Best value known} = 0$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Node 1 (Include $I_1$):} Level 1. $w=2$, $v=20$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item $UB_1 = 20 + 30 + 10 + (1 \times 3) = 63$. Best value updated to $20$.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Node 2 (Exclude $I_1$):} Level 1. $w=0$, $v=0$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Add $I_2, I_3, I_4$: weights $5+2+3=10 \le 10$.
        \item $UB_2 = 0 + 30 + 10 + 9 = 49$. 
        \item Since $UB_1 (63) > UB_2 (49)$, we explore Node 1 first (Best-First Search).
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Node 3 (Include $I_2$ from Node 1):} Level 2. $w=2+5=7$, $v=20+30=50$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Add $I_3$: rem=1. Add fraction of $I_4$: $1 \times 3 = 3$. 
        \item $UB_3 = 50 + 10 + 3 = 63$. Best value updated to $50$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Node 4 (Exclude $I_2$ from Node 1):} Level 2. $w=2$, $v=20$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Add $I_3, I_4$: total weight = $2 + (2+3) = 7 \le 10$.
        \item $UB_4 = 20 + 10 + 9 = 39$. 
        \item Since $39 \le \text{Best value } (50)$, \textbf{PRUNE Node 4}.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Node 5 (Include $I_3$ from Node 3):} Level 3. $w=7+2=9$, $v=50+10=60$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Add fraction of $I_4$: $1 \times 3 = 3$. 
        \item $UB_5 = 60 + 3 = 63$. Best value updated to $60$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Node 6 (Exclude $I_3$ from Node 3):} Level 3. $w=7$, $v=50$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Add $I_4$: total weight $7+3=10$. 
        \item $UB_6 = 50 + 9 = 59$. 
        \item Since $59 \le \text{Best value } (60)$, \textbf{PRUNE Node 6}.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Node 7 (Include $I_4$ from Node 5):} Level 4. $w=9+3=12 > 10$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Infeasible. \textbf{PRUNE Node 7}.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Node 8 (Exclude $I_4$ from Node 5):} Level 4. $w=9$, $v=60$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Reached a valid leaf. $UB_8 = 60$. Best value remains $60$.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textit{Final check:} Return to unexplored Node 2. $UB_2 = 49 \le 60$. \textbf{PRUNE Node 2}. Algorithm terminates.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. State-Space Tree Diagram}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=2.2cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=6cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=4cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=4cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=3cm},
    node/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=white, inner sep=6pt, align=center, font=\footnotesize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    leaf/.style={rectangle, draw=green!60!black, very thick, fill=green!5, inner sep=6pt, align=center, font=\footnotesize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    pruned/.style={rectangle, draw=red!80, thick, fill=red!5, inner sep=6pt, align=center, font=\footnotesize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth},
    el/.style={font=\scriptsize\bfseries, draw=none, fill=white, inner sep=2pt}
]

\node[node] {\textbf{Node 0}\\$w=0, v=0$\\$UB=63$}
    child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 1}\\$w=2, v=20$\\$UB=63$}
        child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 3}\\$w=7, v=50$\\$UB=63$}
            child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 5}\\$w=9, v=60$\\$UB=63$}
                child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 7}\\$w=12 > 10$\\Infeasible}
                    edge from parent node[el] {With $I_4$}
                }
                child { node[leaf] {\textbf{Node 8}\\$w=9, v=60$\\Valid Leaf}
                    edge from parent node[el] {W/O $I_4$}
                }
                edge from parent node[el] {With $I_3$}
            }
            child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 6}\\$w=7, v=50$\\$UB=59 \le 60$\\Pruned}
                edge from parent node[el] {W/O $I_3$}
            }
            edge from parent node[el] {With $I_2$}
        }
        child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 4}\\$w=2, v=20$\\$UB=39 \le 50$\\Pruned}
            edge from parent node[el] {W/O $I_2$}
        }
        edge from parent node[el] {With $I_1$}
    }
    child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 2}\\$w=0, v=0$\\$UB=49 \le 60$\\Pruned}
        edge from parent node[el] {W/O $I_1$}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{5. Final Optimal Solution \& Complexity}

Based on the leaf node that provided the maximum value without being pruned, the optimal choice encompasses $I_1, I_2$, and $I_3$. 

Translating back to the original items:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Included Items:} Original Item 3, Item 4, and Item 1.
    \item \textbf{Total Weight:} $2 + 5 + 2 = 9 \le 10$
    \item \textbf{Maximum Total Value:} $20 + 30 + 10 = \mathbf{60}$
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Complexity Analysis:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ in the worst case (where $n$ is the number of items), as Branch-and-Bound might theoretically explore all combinations if bounds are loose. However, on average and practically, the upper bounding logic heavily prunes the search space, resulting in substantially faster execution than exhaustive search.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(n)$ if utilizing Depth-First Search for branch exploration (maintaining the recursion stack/path). If full Best-First Search with a priority queue is used, space could be exponential in worst-case, $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$, though pruned practically.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

 \item Solve the following instance of the 0-1 knapsack problem using a branch-and-bound algorithm with a state-space tree (capacity = 10\,kg):
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{cccc}
\toprule
Item & Weight (kg) & Profit (Taka) \\
\midrule
1 & 3 & 18 \\
2 & 5 & 35 \\
3 & 4 & 44 \\
4 & 7 & 36 \\
5 & 2 & 18 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\mk{8}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2017--18}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Pre-processing: Sorting by Profit-to-Weight Ratio}
The Branch-and-Bound strategy for the 0-1 Knapsack problem is most efficient when we consider items in descending order of their profit-to-weight ratio ($p_i / w_i$). This allows the fractional knapsack greedy algorithm to compute the tightest possible Upper Bound (UB). 

Let us calculate the ratios and reorder the items. The knapsack capacity is $W = 10$.

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Original Item} & \textbf{Sorted Item} & \textbf{Weight ($w_i$)} & \textbf{Profit ($p_i$)} & \textbf{Ratio ($p_i/w_i$)} & \textbf{Order} \\
\hline
3 & $I_1$ & 4 & 44 & 11.00 & 1 \\
5 & $I_2$ & 2 & 18 & 9.00  & 2 \\
2 & $I_3$ & 5 & 35 & 7.00  & 3 \\
1 & $I_4$ & 3 & 18 & 6.00  & 4 \\
4 & $I_5$ & 7 & 36 & 5.14  & 5 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. The Bounding Function}
At any node in the state-space tree, let $w$ be the current weight and $p$ be the current profit. The upper bound on the maximum profit achievable from this node is calculated by taking the remaining capacity $W_{rem} = W - w$, adding the profit of all remaining items that can completely fit, and adding a fractional profit of the first item $k$ that does not fit.

Since all profits and weights are integers, the optimal profit must be an integer. Thus, we can take the floor of the calculated bound:
\[ UB = \left\lfloor p + \sum_{i=j}^{k-1} p_i + \left( \frac{W_{rem} - \sum_{i=j}^{k-1} w_i}{w_k} \right) \cdot p_k \right\rfloor \]
where $j$ is the index of the next item to be considered. We maintain a \textbf{Max Profit} variable (initially $0$) and \textbf{prune} any node where $UB \le \text{Max Profit}$.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Branch-and-Bound Execution (Depth-First Search)}

We traverse the state-space tree using Depth-First Search (left child = include item, right child = exclude item), keeping track of the current best complete solution.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Node 0 (Root):} $w=0, p=0, W_{rem}=10$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Fill with $I_1 (4)$ and $I_2 (2)$. $W_{rem} = 4$.
        \item Next is $I_3 (w=5, p=35)$. We take a fraction: $(4/5) \times 35 = 28$.
        \item $UB_0 = \lfloor 0 + 44 + 18 + 28 \rfloor = 90$. Max Profit = $0$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Node 1 (Include $I_1$):} $w=4, p=44$. $W_{rem}=6$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Fill with $I_2 (2)$. $W_{rem}=4$. Fraction of $I_3$: $(4/5) \times 35 = 28$. 
        \item $UB_1 = \lfloor 44 + 18 + 28 \rfloor = 90$.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Node 2 (Include $I_2$ from N1):} $w=6, p=62$. $W_{rem}=4$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Fraction of $I_3$: $UB_2 = \lfloor 62 + 28 \rfloor = 90$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Node 3 (Include $I_3$ from N2):} $w=6+5=11 > 10$. Infeasible. Pruned.
    
    \item \textbf{Node 4 (Exclude $I_3$ from N2):} $w=6, p=62$. $W_{rem}=4$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Fill with $I_4 (3)$. $W_{rem}=1$. Fraction of $I_5 (7)$: $(1/7) \times 36 \approx 5.14$.
        \item $UB_4 = \lfloor 62 + 18 + 5.14 \rfloor = 85$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Node 5 (Include $I_4$ from N4):} $w=9, p=80$. $W_{rem}=1$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Fraction of $I_5$: $(1/7) \times 36 \approx 5.14 \implies UB_5 = 85$.
    \end{itemize}
    
    \item \textbf{Node 6 (Include $I_5$ from N5):} $w=16 > 10$. Infeasible. Pruned.
    
    \item \textbf{Node 7 (Exclude $I_5$ from N5):} $w=9, p=80$. Valid Leaf! 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Max Profit updated to 80.}
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Node 8 (Exclude $I_4$ from N4):} $w=6, p=62$. $W_{rem}=4$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Fraction of $I_5$: $(4/7) \times 36 \approx 20.57$. $UB_8 = \lfloor 62 + 20.57 \rfloor = 82$.
        \item Children: Node 9 (Include $I_5 \to w=13$, Infeasible), Node 10 (Exclude $I_5 \to p=62 \le 80$). Both pruned.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Node 11 (Exclude $I_2$ from N1):} $w=4, p=44$. $W_{rem}=6$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Fill with $I_3 (5)$. $W_{rem}=1$. Fraction of $I_4$: $(1/3) \times 18 = 6$.
        \item $UB_{11} = 44 + 35 + 6 = 85$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Node 12 (Include $I_3$ from N11):} $w=9, p=79$. $W_{rem}=1$. $UB_{12} = 85$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Node 13 (Inc $I_4 \to w=12$, Infeasible).
        \item Node 14 (Exc $I_4 \to w=9, p=79, UB=84$). Children are Node 15 (Inc $I_5 \to w=16$) and Node 16 (Exc $I_5 \to p=79 \le 80$). Pruned.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Remaining Nodes to Backtrack:} 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 17 (Exclude $I_3$ from N11):} $w=4, p=44, W_{rem}=6$. Fill $I_4 (3)$, rem=3. Frac $I_5$: $(3/7)\times36 \approx 15.42$. $UB_{17} = \lfloor 44+18+15.42 \rfloor = 77 \le 80$. \textbf{Pruned}.
        \item \textbf{Node 18 (Exclude $I_1$ from N0):} $w=0, p=0, W_{rem}=10$. Fill $I_2(2), I_3(5), I_4(3)$, rem=0. $UB_{18} = 18+35+18 = 71 \le 80$. \textbf{Pruned}.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. State-Space Tree Diagram}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=2.2cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=7cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=4cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=2.6cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=2cm},
    level 5/.style={sibling distance=1.6cm},
    node/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=white, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    leaf/.style={rectangle, draw=green!60!black, very thick, fill=green!5, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    pruned/.style={rectangle, draw=red!80, thick, fill=red!5, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth},
    el/.style={font=\tiny\bfseries, draw=none, fill=white, inner sep=1pt}
]

\node[node] {N0\\w=0, p=0\\UB=90}
  child { node[node] {N1\\w=4, p=44\\UB=90}
    child { node[node] {N2\\w=6, p=62\\UB=90}
      child { node[pruned] {N3\\w=11\\Infeas.} edge from parent node[el] {$I_3=1$} }
      child { node[node] {N4\\w=6, p=62\\UB=85}
        child { node[node] {N5\\w=9, p=80\\UB=85}
           child { node[pruned] {N6\\w=16\\Infeas.} edge from parent node[el] {$I_5=1$} }
           child { node[leaf] {N7\\w=9, p=80\\P=80} edge from parent node[el] {$I_5=0$} }
           edge from parent node[el] {$I_4=1$}
        }
        child { node[node] {N8\\w=6, p=62\\UB=82}
           child { node[pruned] {N9\\w=13\\Infeas.} edge from parent node[el] {$I_5=1$} }
           child { node[pruned] {N10\\P=62$\le$80} edge from parent node[el] {$I_5=0$} }
           edge from parent node[el] {$I_4=0$}
        }
        edge from parent node[el] {$I_3=0$}
      }
      edge from parent node[el] {$I_2=1$}
    }
    child { node[node] {N11\\w=4, p=44\\UB=85}
      child { node[node] {N12\\w=9, p=79\\UB=85}
         child { node[pruned] {N13\\w=12\\Infeas.} edge from parent node[el] {$I_4=1$} }
         child { node[node] {N14\\w=9, p=79\\UB=84} 
            child { node[pruned] {N15\\Infeas.} edge from parent node[el] {$I_5=1$} }
            child { node[pruned] {N16\\P=79$\le$80} edge from parent node[el] {$I_5=0$} }
            edge from parent node[el] {$I_4=0$}
         }
         edge from parent node[el] {$I_3=1$}
      }
      child { node[pruned] {N17\\UB=77$\le$80\\Pruned} edge from parent node[el] {$I_3=0$} }
      edge from parent node[el] {$I_2=0$}
    }
    edge from parent node[el] {$I_1=1$}
  }
  child { node[pruned] {N18\\w=0, p=0\\UB=71$\le$80\\Pruned}
    edge from parent node[el] {$I_1=0$}
  };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{5. Final Result \& Complexity Analysis}

Following the path that yielded the maximum valid leaf node (Node 7), we trace the item inclusions: $I_1=1, I_2=1, I_3=0, I_4=1, I_5=0$.
Mapping these back to the original items:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Included Items:} Original Item 3 ($4$\,kg, $44$\,Tk), Original Item 5 ($2$\,kg, $18$\,Tk), Original Item 1 ($3$\,kg, $18$\,Tk).
    \item \textbf{Total Weight:} $4 + 2 + 3 = 9 \text{ kg} \le 10 \text{ kg}$
    \item \textbf{Maximum Profit:} $44 + 18 + 18 = \mathbf{80 \text{ Taka}}$
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Complexity:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ in the worst-case scenario where bounds are weak and all subsets must be generated. However, due to highly efficient upper-bound pruning, average-case runtime is significantly faster than exhaustive search. Sorting initially takes $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(n)$ utilizing Depth-First Search exploration, as we only need to maintain the recursive call stack proportional to the maximum height of the tree (number of items). 
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Solve the following 0-1 knapsack instance (capacity = 10) using the branch-and-bound algorithm. Show the branch-and-bound tree.
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\toprule
Item & Weight & Value \\
\midrule
1 & 2 & 10 \\
2 & 3 & 9 \\
3 & 2 & 20 \\
4 & 5 & 30 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\mk{20}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2016--17}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Pre-processing: Item Sorting by Profit-to-Weight Ratio}
To solve the 0-1 Knapsack problem efficiently using the Branch-and-Bound (B\&B) technique, we first sort the items in descending order of their value-to-weight ratio ($\frac{v_i}{w_i}$). This reordering provides a tighter upper bound at each node by maximizing the efficiency of the greedy fractional relaxation choice.

The given knapsack capacity is $W = 10$. Let us compute the ratios and define our processing order:

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Original Item} & \textbf{Sorted ID} & \textbf{Weight ($w_i$)} & \textbf{Value ($v_i$)} & \textbf{Ratio ($\frac{v_i}{w_i}$)} & \textbf{Processing Order} \\
\hline
3 & $I_1$ & 2 & 20 & 10.0 & 1 \\
4 & $I_2$ & 5 & 30 & 6.0  & 2 \\
1 & $I_3$ & 2 & 10 & 5.0  & 3 \\
2 & $I_4$ & 3 & 9  & 3.0  & 4 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

We will formulate the state-space tree by making binary decisions on items in the order: $I_1 \rightarrow I_2 \rightarrow I_3 \rightarrow I_4$.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Bounding Function Definition}
For a maximization problem solved via Branch-and-Bound, each node requires an \textbf{Upper Bound (UB)} to prune suboptimal search paths. The bound is calculated using the greedy strategy of the Fractional Knapsack problem:
\[ UB = V_{\text{curr}} + \sum_{i \in \text{Full}} v_i + \left( \frac{W - W_{\text{curr}} - \sum_{i \in \text{Full}} w_i}{w_{\text{fraction}}} \right) \times v_{\text{fraction}} \]
where $V_{\text{curr}}$ and $W_{\text{curr}}$ represent the accumulated value and weight of the items fixed along the path to the current node. 

We maintain a global variable $\text{\textit{Max\_Profit}}$ representing the best feasible solution found so far (initialized to $0$). A node is active and explored if its $UB > \text{\textit{Max\_Profit}}$; otherwise, it is \textbf{pruned}.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Step-by-Step Branch-and-Bound Execution}
We explore the state-space tree using a Best-First Search strategy determined by the maximum upper bound.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Node 0 (Root Node):} No decisions made yet. $W_{\text{curr}} = 0, V_{\text{curr}} = 0$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Greedily fill remaining capacity ($10$): Add $I_1$ ($w=2$), $I_2$ ($w=5$), $I_3$ ($w=2$). Remaining capacity = $10 - (2+5+2) = 1$.
        \item Take a fraction of $I_4$: $\frac{1}{3} \times 9 = 3$.
        \item $UB_0 = 20 + 30 + 10 + 3 = 63$. $\text{\textit{Max\_Profit}} = 0$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching from Node 0 on $I_1$:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 1 (Include $I_1$):} $W_{\text{curr}} = 2, V_{\text{curr}} = 20$. 
        \\ Remaining capacity = 8. Greedily adding $I_2, I_3$ leaves capacity 1. Add fraction of $I_4$: $\frac{1}{3} \times 9 = 3$. 
        \\ $UB_1 = 20 + 30 + 10 + 3 = 63$.
        \item \textbf{Node 2 (Exclude $I_1$):} $W_{\text{curr}} = 0, V_{\text{curr}} = 0$. 
        \\ Remaining capacity = 10. Greedily add $I_2, I_3, I_4$ completely ($5+2+3=10$).
        \\ $UB_2 = 30 + 10 + 9 = 49$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching from Node 1 on $I_2$ (Highest UB = 63):}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 3 (Include $I_2$):} $W_{\text{curr}} = 2+5 = 7, V_{\text{curr}} = 20+30 = 50$.
        \\ Remaining capacity = 3. Add $I_3$ completely ($w=2$). Remaining capacity = 1. Add fraction of $I_4$: $\frac{1}{3} \times 9 = 3$.
        \\ $UB_3 = 50 + 10 + 3 = 63$.
        \item \textbf{Node 4 (Exclude $I_2$):} $W_{\text{curr}} = 2, V_{\text{curr}} = 20$.
        \\ Remaining capacity = 8. Add $I_3, I_4$ completely ($2+3=5 \le 8$).
        \\ $UB_4 = 20 + 10 + 9 = 39$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching from Node 3 on $I_3$ (Highest UB = 63):}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 5 (Include $I_3$):} $W_{\text{curr}} = 7+2 = 9, V_{\text{curr}} = 50+10 = 60$.
        \\ Remaining capacity = 1. Add fraction of $I_4$: $\frac{1}{3} \times 9 = 3$.
        \\ $UB_5 = 60 + 3 = 63$.
        \item \textbf{Node 6 (Exclude $I_3$):} $W_{\text{curr}} = 7, V_{\text{curr}} = 50$.
        \\ Remaining capacity = 3. Add $I_4$ completely ($w=3$). Remaining capacity = 0.
        \\ $UB_6 = 50 + 9 = 59$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching from Node 5 on $I_4$ (Highest UB = 63):}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 7 (Include $I_4$):} $W_{\text{curr}} = 9+3 = 12$. 
        \\ Since $W_{\text{curr}} = 12 > W = 10$, this node is \textbf{Infeasible} and immediately pruned.
        \item \textbf{Node 8 (Exclude $I_4$):} $W_{\text{curr}} = 9, V_{\text{curr}} = 60$.
        \\ This is a valid, fully completed leaf node. 
        \\ Update global bounds: $\text{\textit{Max\_Profit}} = \max(0, 60) = 60$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Pruning Remaining Subtrees:}
    The priority queue/active set contains Node 6 ($UB=59$), Node 2 ($UB=49$), and Node 4 ($UB=39$).
    \begin{itemize}
        \item For Node 6: $UB_6 = 59 \le \text{\textit{Max\_Profit}} \ (60) \implies \textbf{Prune}$.
        \item For Node 2: $UB_2 = 49 \le \text{\textit{Max\_Profit}} \ (60) \implies \textbf{Prune}$.
        \item For Node 4: $UB_4 = 39 \le \text{\textit{Max\_Profit}} \ (60) \implies \textbf{Prune}$.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

All active branches have been exhausted or pruned. The algorithm terminates.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. State-Space Tree Diagram}
Below is the complete branch-and-bound state-space tree layout. Green highlights the optimal solution path, while red nodes are pruned.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=2.4cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=6.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm},
