    level 3/.style={sibling distance=3.5cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=3.0cm},
    node/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=white, inner sep=5pt, align=center, font=\footnotesize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    leaf/.style={rectangle, draw=green!60!black, very thick, fill=green!5, inner sep=5pt, align=center, font=\footnotesize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    pruned/.style={rectangle, draw=red!70!black, thick, fill=red!5, inner sep=5pt, align=center, font=\footnotesize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth},
    el/.style={font=\scriptsize\bfseries, draw=none, fill=white, inner sep=2pt}
]

\node[node] {\textbf{Node 0 (Root)}\\$w=0, v=0$\\$UB=63$}
    child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 1}\\$w=2, v=20$\\ $UB=63$}
        child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 3}\\$w=7, v=50$\\ $UB=63$}
            child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 5}\\$w=9, v=60$\\ $UB=63$}
                child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 7}\\$w=12 > 10$\\\textbf{Infeasible}}
                    edge from parent node[el] {$I_4 = 1$}
                }
                child { node[leaf] {\textbf{Node 8}\\$w=9, v=60$\\\textbf{Optimal Leaf}}
                    edge from parent node[el] {$I_4 = 0$}
                }
                edge from parent node[el] {$I_3 = 1$}
            }
            child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 6}\\$w=7, v=50$\\$UB=59 \le 60$\\\textbf{Pruned}}
                edge from parent node[el] {$I_3 = 0$}
            }
            edge from parent node[el] {$I_2 = 1$}
        }
        child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 4}\\$w=2, v=20$\\$UB=39 \le 60$\\\textbf{Pruned}}
            edge from parent node[el] {$I_2 = 0$}
        }
        edge from parent node[el] {$I_1 = 1$}
    }
    child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 2}\\$w=0, v=0$\\$UB=49 \le 60$\\\textbf{Pruned}}
        edge from parent node[el] {$I_1 = 0$}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{5. Final Solution Summary}
Tracing the path to the optimal leaf node (\textbf{Node 8}), the item selection matrix is:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $I_1 = 1$ (Include original Item 3)
    \item $I_2 = 1$ (Include original Item 4)
    \item $I_3 = 1$ (Include original Item 1)
    \item $I_4 = 0$ (Exclude original Item 2)
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Optimal Composition:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Selected Items: \textbf{\{Item 1, Item 3, Item 4\}}
    \item Total Weight calculation: $2 + 2 + 5 = \mathbf{9} \le 10$
    \item Maximum Value achieved: $10 + 20 + 30 = \mathbf{60}$
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.1cm}
\paragraph{Complexity Analysis:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ in the worst case, where $n$ is the number of items. This occurs if the bounds fail to prune any subtrees and the algorithm must explore all subsets. However, with an optimized bounding rule (like fractional knapsack), the average-case execution explores significantly fewer states. Sorting the items initially requires $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ time.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ in the worst case for a Best-First Search strategy because the priority queue may retain entries for an exponential number of open nodes. (If implemented via Depth-First Search with bounding, the space complexity decreases to $\mathcal{O}(n)$ due to the call stack height limit).
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Solve the following 0/1 knapsack instance (capacity = 15) using the branch-and-bound approach with a state-space tree:
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\toprule
Item & Weight & Value \\
\midrule
1 & 7 & 140 \\
2 & 6 & 132 \\
3 & 4 & 140 \\
4 & 3 & 90 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
Compare the backtracking and branch-and-bound techniques. \mk{3+12=15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2015--16}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Pre-processing: Item Sorting by Profit-to-Weight Ratio}
To solve the 0-1 Knapsack problem efficiently using the Branch-and-Bound (B\&B) algorithm, we sort the given items in descending order of their value-to-weight ratio ($\frac{v_i}{w_i}$). This maximizes the efficiency of the greedy selection strategy when computing the upper bound of any node.

The knapsack capacity is given as $W = 15$. Let us compute the individual ratios:

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Original ID} & \textbf{Sorted ID} & \textbf{Weight ($w_i$)} & \textbf{Value ($v_i$)} & \textbf{Ratio ($\frac{v_i}{w_i}$)} & \textbf{Rank Order} \\
\hline
3 & $I_1$ & 4 & 140 & 35.0 & 1 \\
4 & $I_2$ & 3 & 90  & 30.0 & 2 \\
2 & $I_3$ & 6 & 132 & 22.0 & 3 \\
1 & $I_4$ & 7 & 140 & 20.0 & 4 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

We will construct the state-space tree by making binary branching decisions ($1 = \text{include}$, $0 = \text{exclude}$) sequentially on items $I_1 \rightarrow I_2 \rightarrow I_3 \rightarrow I_4$.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Bounding Function Formulation}
For each node, we compute a relaxed upper bound ($UB$) using the Fractional Knapsack greedy approach. If a node's $UB$ is less than or equal to the current maximum profit ($\textit{Max\_Profit}$) found globally among feasible solutions, the node is pruned.

Let $w$ be the current weight, and $v$ be the current value accumulated at a node. The bound formula is defined as:
\[ UB = v + \sum_{i \in \text{Full}} v_i + \left( \frac{W - w - \sum_{i \in \text{Full}} w_i}{w_{\text{fraction}}} \right) \times v_{\text{fraction}} \]
We maintain a global variable $\textit{Max\_Profit}$ (initially $0$). 

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Step-by-Step Branch-and-Bound Execution}
We explore the tree using Best-First Search via a priority queue ordered by maximum $UB$:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Node 0 (Root Node):} $w=0, v=0$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Greedily fill remaining capacity ($15$): Take $I_1$ ($w=4$) and $I_2$ ($w=3$). Remaining capacity = $15 - 7 = 8$.
        \item Take $I_3$ ($w=6$). Remaining capacity = $8 - 6 = 2$.
        \item Take fraction of $I_4$: $\frac{2}{7} \times 140 = 40$.
        \item $UB_0 = 140 + 90 + 132 + 40 = 402$. $\textit{Max\_Profit} = 0$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching Node 0 ($I_1$):}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 1 ($I_1 = 1$):} $w=4, v=140$. Remaining capacity = $11$. Greedily adding $I_2, I_3$ leaves capacity $2$. Add fraction of $I_4 \rightarrow \frac{2}{7} \times 140 = 40$. $UB_1 = 140 + 90 + 132 + 40 = 402$.
        \item \textbf{Node 2 ($I_1 = 0$):} $w=0, v=0$. Remaining capacity = $15$. Greedily adding $I_2(3), I_3(6)$ leaves capacity $6$. Add fraction of $I_4 \rightarrow \frac{6}{7} \times 140 = 120$. $UB_2 = 90 + 132 + 120 = 342$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching Node 1 ($I_2$) [Highest $UB = 402$]:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 3 ($I_2 = 1$):} $w=4+3=7, v=140+90=230$. Capacity remaining = $8$. Add $I_3(6)$, fraction of $I_4(2)$. $UB_3 = 230 + 132 + 40 = 402$.
        \item \textbf{Node 4 ($I_2 = 0$):} $w=4, v=140$. Capacity remaining = $11$. Add $I_3(6)$, fraction of $I_4(5)$. $UB_4 = 140 + 132 + (\frac{5}{7} \times 140) = 372$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching Node 3 ($I_3$) [Highest $UB = 402$]:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 5 ($I_3 = 1$):} $w=7+6=13, v=230+132=362$. Capacity remaining = $2$. Add fraction of $I_4 \rightarrow \frac{2}{7} \times 140 = 40$. $UB_5 = 362 + 40 = 402$.
        \item \textbf{Node 6 ($I_3 = 0$):} $w=7, v=230$. Capacity remaining = $8$. Add full $I_4(7)$, remaining capacity = 1. $UB_6 = 230 + 140 = 370$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching Node 5 ($I_4$) [Highest $UB = 402$]:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 7 ($I_4 = 1$):} $w=13+7=20 > 15 \implies$ \textbf{Infeasible} (Pruned).
        \item \textbf{Node 8 ($I_4 = 0$):} $w=13, v=362$. Leaf reached! Update $\textit{Max\_Profit} = \max(0, 362) = 362$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching Node 4 ($I_3$) [Highest remaining $UB = 372 > 362$]:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 9 ($I_3 = 1$):} $w=4+6=10, v=140+132=272$. Remaining capacity = $5$. Add fraction of $I_4 \rightarrow \frac{5}{7} \times 140 = 100$. $UB_9 = 272 + 100 = 372$.
        \item \textbf{Node 10 ($I_3 = 0$):} $w=4, v=140$. Remaining capacity = $11$. Add full $I_4(7)$, remaining capacity = $4$. $UB_{10} = 140 + 140 = 280$. Since $280 \le 362$, \textbf{Pruned}.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching Node 9 ($I_4$) [Highest remaining $UB = 372 > 362$]:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 13 ($I_4 = 1$):} $w=10+7=17 > 15 \implies$ \textbf{Infeasible} (Pruned).
        \item \textbf{Node 14 ($I_4 = 0$):} $w=10, v=272$. Leaf reached! $272 \le 362 \implies$ \textbf{Pruned}.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching Node 6 ($I_4$) [Highest remaining $UB = 370 > 362$]:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 11 ($I_4 = 1$):} $w=7+7=14, v=230+140=370$. Leaf reached! Update $\textit{Max\_Profit} = \max(362, 370) = \mathbf{370}$.
        \item \textbf{Node 12 ($I_4 = 0$):} $w=7, v=230$. Leaf reached! $230 \le 370 \implies$ \textbf{Pruned}.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}
All other remaining open branches (e.g., Node 2 with $UB=342$) have $UB \le 370$ and are explicitly pruned.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. State-Space Tree Diagram}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=2.2cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=9cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=2.4cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.6cm},
    node/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=white, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    leaf/.style={rectangle, draw=green!60!black, very thick, fill=green!5, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    pruned/.style={rectangle, draw=red!70!black, thick, fill=red!5, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth},
    el/.style={font=\tiny\bfseries, draw=none, fill=white, inner sep=2pt}
]

\node[node] {\textbf{Node 0 (Root)}\\$w=0, v=0$\\$UB=402$}
    child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 1}\\$w=4, v=140$\\$UB=402$}
        child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 3}\\$w=7, v=230$\\$UB=402$}
            child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 5}\\$w=13, v=362$\\$UB=402$}
                child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 7}\\$w=20 > 15$\\\textbf{Infeasible}} edge from parent node[el] {$I_4=1$} }
                child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 8}\\$w=13, v=362$\\\textbf{Pruned by N11}} edge from parent node[el] {$I_4=0$} }
                edge from parent node[el] {$I_3=1$}
            }
            child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 6}\\$w=7, v=230$\\$UB=370$}
                child { node[leaf] {\textbf{Node 11}\\[2pt]$w=14, v=\mathbf{370}$\\[2pt]\textbf{Optimal Leaf}} edge from parent node[el] {$I_4=1$} }
                child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 12}\\$w=7, v=230$\\\textbf{Pruned}} edge from parent node[el] {$I_4=0$} }
                edge from parent node[el] {$I_3=0$}
            }
            edge from parent node[el] {$I_2=1$}
        }
        child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 4}\\$w=4, v=140$\\$UB=372$}
            child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 9}\\$w=10, v=272$\\$UB=372$}
                child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 13}\\$w=17 > 15$\\\textbf{Infeasible}} edge from parent node[el] {$I_4=1$} }
                child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 14}\\$w=10, v=272$\\\textbf{Pruned}} edge from parent node[el] {$I_4=0$} }
                edge from parent node[el] {$I_3=1$}
            }
            child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 10}\\$w=4, v=140$\\$UB=280 \le 362$\\\textbf{Pruned}} edge from parent node[el] {$I_3=0$} }
            edge from parent node[el] {$I_2=0$}
        }
        edge from parent node[el] {$I_1=1$}
    }
    child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 2}\\$w=0, v=0$\\$UB=342 \le 370$\\\textbf{Pruned}}
        edge from parent node[el] {$I_1=0$}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{5. Final Solution Vector \& Complexity Analysis}
Tracing the path leading to the absolute optimal node (\textbf{Node 11}):
\begin{itemize}
    \item $I_1 = 1$, $I_2 = 1$, $I_3 = 0$, $I_4 = 1$
\end{itemize}
Mapping these back to the original items:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Selected Items:} Original Item 3 ($I_1$), Original Item 4 ($I_2$), and Original Item 1 ($I_4$). (Original Item 2 is excluded).
    \item \textbf{Optimal Total Weight:} $4 + 3 + 7 = \mathbf{14 \le 15}$
    \item \textbf{Optimal Maximum Value:} $140 + 90 + 140 = \mathbf{370}$
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Complexity Analysis:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ in the worst case (where $n$ is the number of items), as the algorithm might expand all paths if bounds fail to restrict branching. However, the average case requires exploring far fewer states due to bounding prunes. Presorting takes $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ for Best-First Search because the priority queue can simultaneously store multiple open configurations at the fringe.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{6. Comparison: Backtracking vs. Branch-and-Bound}
Both methodologies target hard combinatorial problems by searching state-space graphs, but they differ fundamentally in design strategies:

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\small
\begin{tabular}{|p{3cm}|p{5.5cm}|p{5.5cm}|}
\hline
\textbf{Feature} & \textbf{Backtracking} & \textbf{Branch-and-Bound} \\
\hline
\textbf{Search Strategy} & Primarily Depth-First Search (DFS). & Breadth-First Search (BFS) or Best-First Search using a priority queue. \\
\hline
\textbf{Problem Applicability} & Best suited for decision problems, constraint satisfaction, or finding all solutions (e.g., $N$-Queens). & Expressly optimized for discrete optimization problems (maximizing or minimizing objective equations). \\
\hline
\textbf{Pruning Mechanism} & Prunes paths using a bounding/feasibility function as soon as constraints are violated. & Employs a continuous mathematical estimation function ($UB$ or $LB$) to check if a path can beat the current best global score. \\
\hline
\textbf{Memory Footprint} & Very light memory overhead, bounded linearly by tree depth: $\mathcal{O}(n)$. & Can have heavy memory consumption: $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ due to keeping active exploration frontiers inside queues. \\
\hline
\textbf{Traversal Direction} & Explores deep down a lineage before exploring siblings. & Evaluates nodes dynamically across levels based on maximum statistical promise. \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item Consider the following instance of the maximum satisfiability (MAX-SAT) problem.

\begin{itemize}
    \item[] Set of Boolean variables, $V = \{w, x, y, z\}$
    \item[] Set of clauses, $C = \{C1, C2, C3, C4, C5, C6\}$
    \item[] where $C1 = (w \vee \bar{x} \vee \bar{y} \vee z)$, \quad
            $C2 = (w \vee \bar{y})$, \quad
            $C3 = (x \vee \bar{y})$, \quad
            $C4 = (y \vee \bar{z})$
    \item[] $C5 = (z \vee \bar{w})$, \quad
            $C6 = (\bar{w} \vee \bar{x})$
\end{itemize}

\noindent
Now find an assignment of variables such that maximum number of clauses is satisfied.
Compute your solution using branch and bound algorithm. Write down a suitable bound
function for the problem. Show detailed calculation steps through a branch and bound
search tree.\mk{12}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2014--15}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Problem Formulation \& Bounding Function}

The Maximum Satisfiability (MAX-SAT) problem asks for an assignment of Boolean variables that maximizes the number of satisfied clauses. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Variables:} $V = \{w, x, y, z\}$
    \item \textbf{Total Clauses:} $m = 6$
    \item \textbf{Clauses:} 
    $C_1 = (w \lor \bar{x} \lor \bar{y} \lor z)$, 
    $C_2 = (w \lor \bar{y})$, 
    $C_3 = (x \lor \bar{y})$, 
    $C_4 = (y \lor \bar{z})$, 
    $C_5 = (z \lor \bar{w})$, 
    $C_6 = (\bar{w} \lor \bar{x})$
\end{itemize}

To apply the Branch and Bound algorithm efficiently, we need a bounding function. Since this is a maximization problem, we define an \textbf{Upper Bound (UB)} for any partial assignment. 
Let $F$ be the number of clauses that evaluate to strictly \textbf{False} (i.e., all literals in the clause are evaluated to $0$) under the current partial assignment. A falsified clause can never become true. Therefore, the maximum possible number of satisfied clauses achievable from this node is:
\[ UB = m - F = 6 - F \]
We maintain a global variable $\textbf{Best}$, representing the maximum number of satisfied clauses found so far (initialized to $0$). If at any node, $UB \le \textbf{Best}$, we \textbf{prune} the branch because it cannot strictly improve our best known solution.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Branch and Bound Algorithm}

\begin{algorithm}[H]
\caption{Branch and Bound DFS for MAX-SAT}
\begin{algorithmic}[1]
\State \textbf{Input:} Set of Clauses $C$, Variables $V$
\State \textbf{Global:} $\text{Best} \gets 0$, $m \gets |C|$
\State \textbf{Procedure} \Call{Solve}{current\_assignment, unassigned\_vars}
    \State $F \gets \text{number of falsified clauses in } C \text{ under current\_assignment}$
    \State $UB \gets m - F$
    \If{$UB \le \text{Best}$}
        \State \Return \Comment{Prune branch}
    \EndIf
    \If{unassigned\_vars is empty}
        \State $\text{Best} \gets \max(\text{Best}, UB)$ \Comment{Update optimal score}
        \State \Return
    \EndIf
    \State $v \gets \text{select next variable from unassigned\_vars}$
    \State \Call{Solve}{current\_assignment $\cup \{v = 1\}$, unassigned\_vars $\setminus \{v\}$}
    \State \Call{Solve}{current\_assignment $\cup \{v = 0\}$, unassigned\_vars $\setminus \{v\}$}
\end{algorithmic}
\end{algorithm}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Step-by-Step Execution Trace}

We explore the variable search space in a Depth-First Search manner, considering variables in lexicographical order: $w, x, y, z$.

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{N0 (Root):} All unassigned. $F=0 \implies UB=6$.
    \item \textbf{N1 ($w=1$):} $F=0 \implies UB=6$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{N2 ($w=1, x=1$):} $C_6 = (0 \lor 0) = \text{False}$. $F=1 \implies UB=5$.
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \textbf{N3 ($y=1$):} $F=1 \implies UB=5$.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{N4 ($z=1$):} Leaf. $C_6$ False. Sat = 5. $\textbf{Best} \gets 5$.
                \item \textbf{N5 ($z=0$):} Leaf. $C_5 = (0 \lor 0) = \text{False}$, $C_6$ False. Sat = 4.
            \end{itemize}
            \item \textbf{N6 ($y=0$):} $F=1 \implies UB=5$. Since $UB \le \textbf{Best}$ ($5 \le 5$), \textbf{Prune}.
        \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{N7 ($w=1, x=0$):} $F=0 \implies UB=6$.
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \textbf{N8 ($y=1$):} $C_3 = (0 \lor 0) = \text{False}$. $F=1 \implies UB=5 \le \textbf{Best}$. \textbf{Prune}.
            \item \textbf{N9 ($y=0$):} $F=0 \implies UB=6$.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{N10 ($z=1$):} Leaf. $C_4 = (0 \lor 0) = \text{False}$. Sat = 5.
                \item \textbf{N11 ($z=0$):} Leaf. $C_5 = (0 \lor 0) = \text{False}$. Sat = 5.
            \end{itemize}
        \end{itemize}
    \end{itemize}
    \item \textbf{N12 ($w=0$):} $F=0 \implies UB=6$.
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{N13 ($w=0, x=1$):} $F=0 \implies UB=6$.
        \begin{itemize}
            \item \textbf{N14 ($y=1$):} $C_2 = (0 \lor 0) = \text{False}$. $F=1 \implies UB=5 \le \textbf{Best}$. \textbf{Prune}.
            \item \textbf{N15 ($y=0$):} $F=0 \implies UB=6$.
            \begin{itemize}
                \item \textbf{N16 ($z=1$):} Leaf. $C_4 = (0 \lor 0) = \text{False}$. Sat = 5.
                \item \textbf{N17 ($z=0$):} Leaf. All clauses satisfied! Sat = 6. $\textbf{Best} \gets 6$.
            \end{itemize}
        \end{itemize}
        \item \textbf{N18 ($w=0, x=0$):} $F=0 \implies UB=6$. Since $UB \le \textbf{Best}$ ($6 \le 6$), \textbf{Prune}. (We have reached the maximum possible theoretical score).
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. State-Space Search Tree}

The diagram below tracks the tree generated by the execution trace. Nodes in \textcolor{green!60!black}{\textbf{green}} are fully evaluated valid leaves, and nodes in \textcolor{red!80!black}{\textbf{red}} are bounded and pruned without further expansion.

\begin{center}
\resizebox{\linewidth}{!}{
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=8cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=4.5cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=2.2cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.4cm},
    active/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=white, rounded corners, align=center, inner sep=4pt, font=\small},
    pruned/.style={rectangle, draw=red!80!black, thick, fill=red!10, rounded corners, align=center, inner sep=4pt, font=\small},
    leaf/.style={rectangle, draw=green!60!black, thick, fill=green!10, rounded corners, align=center, inner sep=4pt, font=\small},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth},
    lbl/.style={font=\footnotesize, fill=white, inner sep=1pt}
]

\node[active] {\textbf{N0}\\UB=6}
  child { node[active] {\textbf{N1}\\UB=6}
    child { node[active] {\textbf{N2}\\UB=5}
      child { node[active] {\textbf{N3}\\UB=5}
        child { node[leaf] {\textbf{N4}\\Sat=5} edge from parent node[lbl, left] {$z=1$} }
        child { node[leaf] {\textbf{N5}\\Sat=4} edge from parent node[lbl, right] {$z=0$} }
        edge from parent node[lbl, left] {$y=1$}
      }
      child { node[pruned] {\textbf{N6}\\UB=5\\(Prune)} edge from parent node[lbl, right] {$y=0$} }
      edge from parent node[lbl, left] {$x=1$}
    }
    child { node[active] {\textbf{N7}\\UB=6}
      child { node[pruned] {\textbf{N8}\\UB=5\\(Prune)} edge from parent node[lbl, left] {$y=1$} }
      child { node[active] {\textbf{N9}\\UB=6}
        child { node[leaf] {\textbf{N10}\\Sat=5} edge from parent node[lbl, left] {$z=1$} }
        child { node[leaf] {\textbf{N11}\\Sat=5} edge from parent node[lbl, right] {$z=0$} }
        edge from parent node[lbl, right] {$y=0$}
      }
      edge from parent node[lbl, right] {$x=0$}
    }
    edge from parent node[lbl, left, xshift=-10pt] {$w=1$}
  }
  child { node[active] {\textbf{N12}\\UB=6}
    child { node[active] {\textbf{N13}\\UB=6}
      child { node[pruned] {\textbf{N14}\\UB=5\\(Prune)} edge from parent node[lbl, left] {$y=1$} }
      child { node[active] {\textbf{N15}\\UB=6}
        child { node[leaf] {\textbf{N16}\\Sat=5} edge from parent node[lbl, left] {$z=1$} }
        child { node[leaf] {\textbf{N17}\\Sat=\textbf{6}\\Optimum} edge from parent node[lbl, right] {$z=0$} }
        edge from parent node[lbl, right] {$y=0$}
      }
      edge from parent node[lbl, left] {$x=1$}
    }
    child { node[pruned] {\textbf{N18}\\UB=6\\(Prune)} edge from parent node[lbl, right] {$x=0$} }
    edge from parent node[lbl, right, xshift=10pt] {$w=0$}
  };
\end{tikzpicture}
}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{5. Final Result \& Complexity Analysis}

\textbf{Optimal Assignment:}
From Leaf \textbf{N17}, the highest recorded score reaches the theoretical maximum of $6$. The assignment corresponding to this node is:
\[ w = 0, \quad x = 1, \quad y = 0, \quad z = 0 \]
Under this assignment, all $6$ clauses are strictly satisfied. 

\textbf{Complexity Assessment:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} In the worst-case scenario (where bounds fail to prune any states), the Branch and Bound explores $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ nodes. At each node, computing the bound costs $\mathcal{O}(m)$, giving an overall worst-case time complexity of $\mathcal{O}(m \cdot 2^n)$, where $n$ is the number of variables and $m$ is the number of clauses.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} Since we use a Depth-First Search approach, the space complexity is strictly limited by the height of the state-space tree. Thus, the space complexity is highly efficient at $\mathcal{O}(n)$. 
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\item What is a state-space tree? Solve the following instance of the 0/1 knapsack problem (capacity = 10) using the branch-and-bound approach with a state-space tree:
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccc}
\toprule
Item & Weight & Value \\
\midrule
1 & 7 & 42 \\
2 & 4 & 40 \\
3 & 3 & 12 \\
4 & 5 & 25 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\mk{15}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2013--14}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Concept: State-Space Tree}
A \textbf{state-space tree} is a rooted tree data structure that represents the search space of a combinatorial optimization or decision problem. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Nodes} represent the states (either partial or complete assignments) of the problem.
    \item \textbf{Edges} represent the branching decisions made to transition from one state to another (e.g., including or excluding a specific item in the knapsack).
    \item The \textbf{Root} represents the initial state (empty assignment), and the \textbf{Leaves} represent complete assignments or terminal configurations.
    \item Algorithms like Branch-and-Bound and Backtracking systematically traverse this tree, computing mathematical bounds or feasibility constraints at each node to prune non-promising subtrees and avoid exhaustive enumeration.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Pre-processing: Item Sorting}
To solve the 0/1 Knapsack problem efficiently using Branch-and-Bound, we first evaluate the items and sort them in descending order of their \textbf{value-to-weight ratio} ($\frac{v_i}{w_i}$). This ensures the greedy fractional upper bound is as tight and realistic as possible.

Given Knapsack Capacity $W = 10$:
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Original ID} & \textbf{Sorted ID} & \textbf{Weight ($w_i$)} & \textbf{Value ($v_i$)} & \textbf{Ratio ($v_i/w_i$)} & \textbf{Rank Order} \\
\hline
2 & $I_1$ & 4 & 40 & 10.0 & 1 \\
1 & $I_2$ & 7 & 42 & 6.0 & 2 \\
4 & $I_3$ & 5 & 25 & 5.0 & 3 \\
3 & $I_4$ & 3 & 12 & 4.0 & 4 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Bounding Function}
At any node with accumulated weight $w$ and value $v$, the optimistic Upper Bound ($UB$) is calculated by filling the remaining capacity $(W - w)$ greedily. We take whole items as long as they fit, and then a \textit{fraction} of the next available item.

\[ UB = v + \sum_{i \in \text{Full}} v_i + \left( \frac{W - w - \sum w_{\text{Full}}}{w_{\text{next}}} \right) \times v_{\text{next}} \]
We maintain a global $\textit{Max\_Profit}$ initialized to 0. A node is pruned if $UB \le \textit{Max\_Profit}$ or if its weight $w > W$.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Step-by-Step Branch-and-Bound Execution}
We process the tree by branching on variables $I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4$ in sequence. We use Best-First Search, prioritizing nodes with the highest $UB$.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Node 0 (Root):} $w=0, v=0$. 
    \begin{itemize}
        \item Add $I_1(4)$. Rem: 6. Next is $I_2(7, 42)$. Add fraction: $\frac{6}{7} \times 42 = 36$.
        \item $UB_0 = 40 + 36 = \mathbf{76}$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching Node 0 ($I_1$):}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 1 ($I_1=1$):} $w=4, v=40$. Rem: 6. Add fraction of $I_2 \implies \frac{6}{7} \times 42 = 36$. $UB_1 = 40 + 36 = \mathbf{76}$.
        \item \textbf{Node 2 ($I_1=0$):} $w=0, v=0$. Rem: 10. Add $I_2(7,42)$. Rem: 3. Add fraction of $I_3 \implies \frac{3}{5} \times 25 = 15$. $UB_2 = 42 + 15 = \mathbf{57}$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching Node 1 ($I_2$) [Highest open $UB=76$]:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 3 ($I_2=1$):} $w=4+7=11 > 10$. \textbf{Infeasible} (Pruned).
        \item \textbf{Node 4 ($I_2=0$):} $w=4, v=40$. Rem: 6. Add $I_3(5,25)$. Rem: 1. Add fraction of $I_4 \implies \frac{1}{3} \times 12 = 4$. $UB_4 = 40 + 25 + 4 = \mathbf{69}$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching Node 4 ($I_3$) [Highest open $UB=69$]:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 5 ($I_3=1$):} $w=4+5=9, v=40+25=65$. Rem: 1. Add fraction of $I_4 \implies \frac{1}{3} \times 12 = 4$. $UB_5 = 65 + 4 = \mathbf{69}$.
        \item \textbf{Node 6 ($I_3=0$):} $w=4, v=40$. Rem: 6. Add $I_4(3,12)$. Rem: 3. No more items. $UB_6 = 40 + 12 = \mathbf{52}$.
    \end{itemize}

    \item \textbf{Branching Node 5 ($I_4$) [Highest open $UB=69$]:}
    \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Node 7 ($I_4=1$):} $w=9+3=12 > 10$. \textbf{Infeasible} (Pruned).
        \item \textbf{Node 8 ($I_4=0$):} $w=9, v=65$. Leaf reached! We update $\textit{Max\_Profit} = \max(0, 65) = \mathbf{65}$.
    \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

Remaining active nodes are Node 6 ($UB=52$) and Node 2 ($UB=57$). Since both have $UB < 65$, they are safely \textbf{pruned} without further expansion.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{5. State-Space Tree Diagram}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level distance=2.2cm,
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=7.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=4cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=2.5cm},
    level 4/.style={sibling distance=1.8cm},
    node/.style={rectangle, draw=cE, thick, fill=white, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    leaf/.style={rectangle, draw=green!60!black, very thick, fill=green!5, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    pruned/.style={rectangle, draw=red!70!black, thick, fill=red!5, inner sep=4pt, align=center, font=\scriptsize\sffamily, rounded corners},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth},
    el/.style={font=\tiny\bfseries, draw=none, fill=white, inner sep=2pt}
]

\node[node] {\textbf{Node 0 (Root)}\\$w=0, v=0$\\$UB=76$}
    child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 1}\\$w=4, v=40$\\$UB=76$}
        child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 3}\\$w=11 > 10$\\\textbf{Infeasible}} 
            edge from parent node[el] {$I_2=1$} 
        }
        child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 4}\\$w=4, v=40$\\$UB=69$}
            child { node[node] {\textbf{Node 5}\\$w=9, v=65$\\$UB=69$}
                child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 7}\\$w=12 > 10$\\\textbf{Infeasible}} edge from parent node[el] {$I_4=1$} }
                child { node[leaf] {\textbf{Node 8}\\[2pt]$w=9, v=\mathbf{65}$\\[2pt]\textbf{Optimal Leaf}} edge from parent node[el] {$I_4=0$} }
                edge from parent node[el] {$I_3=1$}
            }
            child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 6}\\$w=4, v=40$\\$UB=52 < 65$\\\textbf{Pruned}} 
                edge from parent node[el] {$I_3=0$} 
            }
            edge from parent node[el] {$I_2=0$}
        }
        edge from parent node[el] {$I_1=1$}
    }
    child { node[pruned] {\textbf{Node 2}\\$w=0, v=0$\\$UB=57 < 65$\\\textbf{Pruned}}
        edge from parent node[el] {$I_1=0$}
    };
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{6. Final Solution Vector \& Complexity}
Tracing the path leading to the optimal leaf (\textbf{Node 8}):
\begin{itemize}
    \item $I_1 = 1$, $I_2 = 0$, $I_3 = 1$, $I_4 = 0$
\end{itemize}
Mapping these back to the original items:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Selected Items:} Original Item 2 ($I_1$) and Original Item 4 ($I_3$).
    \item \textbf{Optimal Total Weight:} $4 + 5 = \mathbf{9 \le 10}$
    \item \textbf{Optimal Maximum Value:} $40 + 25 = \mathbf{65}$
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Complexity Analysis:}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Time Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ in the worst case (where bounding fails to prune the search space). However, due to tight bounding via the greedy fractional technique, it performs significantly better on average. Pre-processing (sorting) takes $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$.
    \item \textbf{Space Complexity:} $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ for the Best-First Search because the priority queue may have to store an exponential number of active nodes at the fringe.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

  \item Applying the FIFO branch-and-bound technique on the 4-Queen problem, it is found that the technique is not efficient. Why? \mk{6}
\yr{CSE 207 | L-2/T-2 | 2008--09}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced,breakable,ansbox,colback=cE!4,colframe=cE,boxrule=0.8pt,arc=5pt,title={\textbf{Answer}},fonttitle=\bfseries\color{white},colbacktitle=cE]

\subsubsection*{1. Introduction to the Approach}
The 4-Queen problem requires placing 4 queens on a $4 \times 4$ chessboard such that no two queens attack each other (i.e., no two queens share the same row, column, or diagonal). When applying a \textbf{Branch-and-Bound} technique to solve this, a state-space tree is constructed where each level $i$ represents the placement of a queen in the $i$-th row. 

A \textbf{FIFO (First-In, First-Out)} branch-and-bound operates identically to a \textbf{Breadth-First Search (BFS)}. It uses a queue to explore the state-space tree level by level. It fully expands all nodes at depth $d$ before moving to depth $d+1$.

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{2. Why FIFO Branch-and-Bound is Inefficient}

Applying the FIFO strategy to the 4-Queen problem (and $N$-Queen problems in general) is highly inefficient due to several core structural reasons:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Exponential Space Complexity (Queue Size):}
    In FIFO search, all unpruned nodes at the current level must be stored in memory (the queue) before generating the next level. Because the state-space tree branches heavily at the top, the number of valid partial configurations grows rapidly. Instead of storing just $O(N)$ nodes (as in Depth-First Search), FIFO must store $O(b^d)$ nodes in the worst case, leading to massive memory overhead even for small $N$.

    \item \textbf{Solutions Reside Exclusively at Maximum Depth:}
    The 4-Queen problem is a \textit{Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)}, not a typical optimization problem. A valid solution requires all 4 queens to be placed, meaning all valid goal states exist strictly at the \textbf{leaf nodes} (depth $N=4$). Because FIFO explores level-by-level, it spends maximum time generating and enqueuing partial states at depths 1, 2, and 3 before it ever evaluates a single complete leaf node at depth 4. 

    \item \textbf{Ineffective "Bounding" (No Cost Heuristic):}
    In traditional branch-and-bound optimization (like the Knapsack problem), bounding functions use an objective cost/profit to prune suboptimal paths early. In the 4-Queen problem, the "bound" is merely a feasibility check (do the current queens attack each other?). There is no notion of "cost," meaning FIFO cannot prioritize promising branches (Best-First) or aggressively prune valid-but-suboptimal paths. Every feasible partial state must be strictly queued and explored.

    \item \textbf{Delayed Discovery of First Solution:}
    If the goal is to find \textit{any} single valid arrangement of the 4 queens, FIFO is uniquely disadvantageous. A Depth-First approach (LIFO/Backtracking) immediately dives to depth 4, frequently finding a valid assignment in a fraction of the time. FIFO forces the systematic expansion of the entire upper tree first.
\end{enumerate}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{3. Visualizing the FIFO State-Space Explosion}

The diagram below contrasts how FIFO (BFS) processes the state-space tree compared to LIFO (DFS/Backtracking). Notice how FIFO forces horizontal expansion, causing the queue to swell.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    level 1/.style={sibling distance=3.5cm},
    level 2/.style={sibling distance=1.2cm},
    level 3/.style={sibling distance=0.8cm},
    node/.style={circle, draw=cE, thick, fill=white, inner sep=2pt, font=\footnotesize, minimum size=0.6cm},
    edge from parent/.style={draw, thick, ->, >=stealth},
    txt/.style={font=\small, text=cE!70!black}
]

% FIFO Representation
\begin{scope}[xshift=-4cm]
\node[node, fill=cE!20] (R) {0}
    child { node[node, fill=cE!20] {1} 
        child { node[node] {5} }
        child { node[node] {6} }
        child { node[node] {7} }
    }
    child { node[node, fill=cE!20] {2} 
        child { node[node] {8} }
        child { node[node] {9} }
    }
    child { node[node, fill=cE!20] {3} 
        child { node[node] {10} }
    }
    child { node[node, fill=cE!20] {4} 
        child { node[node] {11} }
    };
\node[txt, above=0.5cm of R] {\textbf{FIFO (BFS) Search}};
\node[txt, align=center, below=3.5cm of R] {Queue stores entire levels.\\High Space: $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$};

% Draw horizontal dashed lines to show level completion
\draw[dashed, red, thick, ->] (-3, -0.8) -- (3, -0.8) node[right, font=\footnotesize] {Level 1 Processed};
\end{scope}

% LIFO / Backtracking Representation
\begin{scope}[xshift=4cm]
\node[node, fill=green!20] (R2) {0}
    child { node[node, fill=green!20] {1} 
        child { node[node, fill=green!20] {2} 
            child { node[node, fill=green!20] {3} }
            child { node[node] {} }
        }
        child { node[node] {} }
        child { node[node] {} }
    }
    child { node[node] {} }
    child { node[node] {} }
    child { node[node] {} };
\node[txt, above=0.5cm of R2] {\textbf{LIFO (DFS/Backtracking)}};
\node[txt, align=center, below=3.5cm of R2] {Dives directly to leaves.\\Low Space: $\mathcal{O}(N)$};

% Draw vertical dashed line to show depth completion
\draw[dashed, blue, thick, ->] (-1.5, -0.5) -- (-1.5, -3) node[below, font=\footnotesize] {Immediate Depth};
\end{scope}

\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.2cm}
\hrule
\vspace{0.3cm}

\subsubsection*{4. Summary and Complexity Comparison}

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{FIFO (Breadth-First Search):} The space complexity is proportional to the maximum width of the state space tree, which is $\mathcal{O}(N^N)$ asymptotically before pruning. For memory-constrained environments, the queue will overflow quickly even for moderate $N$, making it strictly inefficient.
    \item \textbf{LIFO (Depth-First / Backtracking):} Only the current path from the root to the working node is stored. The space complexity is $\mathcal{O}(N)$, representing the maximum depth of the recursive stack. It is the universally preferred standard for solving the $N$-Queen problem.
\end{itemize}

\end{tcolorbox}

\end{enumerate}
